western_lowlands_the_riverset_blade.title: The Riverset Blade
western_lowlands_the_riverset_blade.summary: Voica's grandfather forged one blade the old way, quenched in the river at the fork, and lost it to a debtor who paid a levy with it. She's traced it to a scarecrow on the strips. Bring it home before the field keeps it forever.
western_lowlands_the_riverset_blade.offer: My grandfather made one true blade -- riverset, quenched at the fork the old way, the kind of steel that outlasts the smith and the smith's grandchildren both. A man paid a levy with it in a bad year, having nothing else, and it's been lost since. I've heard where it went. It's hanging on a scarecrow now, out on the strips, a debtor's blade on a debtor's post. Bring it back. Some things shouldn't stay where the ledger put them.
western_lowlands_the_riverset_blade.progress: The riverset blade hangs on a scarecrow-wight among the unsown strips. Cut it down and take the blade.
western_lowlands_the_riverset_blade.complete: That's it. Grandfather's mark, right at the ricasso -- I'd know it blind. Still keeps its edge, after all these years on a post in the weather. Riverset steel doesn't forget what it was made to be, even when the field tries to make it a scarecrow's crutch. It's yours now. A man paid too much for it once. Carry it like it cost something, because it did.

objectives.text: Cut down the wight bearing the blade
Recover the riverset blade

western_lowlands_the_riverset_blade.note: A man paid a levy with the last true blade his family had, in a year that left him nothing else. It hung on a debtor's post until Voica sent for it. Riverset steel does not forget what it was made to be. The field had tried to make it a crutch. It is a blade again, and it remembers.

western_lowlands_the_binding_of_mira.title: The Binding of Mira
western_lowlands_the_binding_of_mira.summary: Mira means to keep the two acres her grandmother died to save -- but a scarecrow-wight has risen on them, and if she can't work the land, the ledger takes it after all. Break the wight and buy her the harvest her Gran paid for.
western_lowlands_the_binding_of_mira.offer: Gran went to the post so we'd keep these two acres, and I mean to keep them if it kills me the same way. But something's risen on the near strip -- another of the straw things -- and I can't sow with it standing there, and if I don't sow, the ledger says the land goes idle, and idle land gets re-filed. Do you see the trap? She died to save it and the same machine's coming for it again. Break the wight. Let me plant. Let her death mean the harvest it was supposed to.
western_lowlands_the_binding_of_mira.progress: The wight stands on Mira's near strip, blocking the sowing. Break it so she can work the acres.
western_lowlands_the_binding_of_mira.complete: It's down. I can sow. The furrows are Gran's furrows and the seed's in the ground and by autumn there'll be a harvest off the land she bought with herself. That's what I can give her -- not a grave, we never found enough. A crop. Take the bound keepsake; it's the last of her weaving, and it should go with someone who'll walk further than these two acres. Someone should. She never got to.

objectives.text: Break the wight on Mira's near strip
See Mira begin the sowing

western_lowlands_the_binding_of_mira.note: The wight was broken and Mira sowed her grandmother's furrows. By autumn there will be a harvest off the two acres a woman died to keep -- a crop for a grave they never found enough of a body to fill. The same machine that killed her still circles the land, patient, waiting for one idle season to file it away.

western_lowlands_the_uncounted_chimneys.title: The Uncounted Chimneys
western_lowlands_the_uncounted_chimneys.summary: Sanda says you can measure the west's health by counting the chimneys with smoke going up at dusk -- and she wants a real count this year, for stubbornness more than anything. Walk the hamlet rise and count the living fires.
western_lowlands_the_uncounted_chimneys.offer: My grandmother had a way of measuring the west: count the chimneys smoking at dusk. More smoke, more suppers, more folk still here to eat them. Clerks count coin; we counted fires. I want a true count this year -- for spite, mostly, since certain folk keep telling me the west is dying. Walk up to the hamlet rise at dusk and count the chimneys with smoke in them. Come tell me the number's still good.
western_lowlands_the_uncounted_chimneys.progress: The hamlet rise looks over the western chimneys. Reach it at dusk and count the smoking fires.
western_lowlands_the_uncounted_chimneys.complete: That many still smoking? Then we're not dead yet, whatever the reeves' rolls say. Every one of those is a supper and a family and a reason to keep the road warm. Fewer than my grandmother counted, aye -- but not few. Not yet. Here, an orange reading-still; distills a bit of comfort from plain water, my mother swore. Count your own fires, dear, wherever you end up.

objectives.label: the hamlet rise at dusk
objectives.text: Count the smoking chimneys from the rise

western_lowlands_the_uncounted_chimneys.note: Fewer chimneys smoked than Sanda's grandmother once counted, but not few -- not yet. The west measures its own life in suppers, not coin. Every fire is a family the ledger has not managed to re-file. Sanda counts them each dusk now, quietly, and does not say why the number matters so much this year.

western_lowlands_wardens_walk_daily.title: The Warden's Walk
western_lowlands_wardens_walk_daily.summary: The fallow strips fill with gnawers faster than one patrol can thin them. Stanca sets a standing bounty: keep the digging-ground and the diggers clear, one walk at a time.
western_lowlands_wardens_walk_daily.offer: The strips never stay quiet long -- thin them today and they're thick again by the week's end. So it's a standing thing now, this walk. Cull the gnawers off the digging-ground, keep the diggers able to dig, and there's coin for it each time you make the round. It's not glory. It's a fence that needs mending every single day. Most of keeping the west is exactly that.
western_lowlands_wardens_walk_daily.progress: The field-gnawers press the digging-ground on the fallow strips. Cull ten and walk the round.
western_lowlands_wardens_walk_daily.complete: Round's walked, ground's clear, diggers can work another day. That's the whole job -- not winning, just holding. Come back tomorrow and it'll need doing again, and the day after that. Here's your coin. The west runs on people who show up to mend the same fence every morning without being thanked. You're one of them now, saints help you.

objectives.text: Cull gnawers on the digging-ground

western_lowlands_wardens_walk_daily.note: The strips are clear again, for a day. Tomorrow they will not be. Keeping the west is mending the same fence every morning, unthanked -- and lately the fence keeps giving way in the same three places, as if something on the far side were leaning on it, patient, to see where it breaks.

western_lowlands_a_bowl_for_the_road.title: A Bowl for the Road
western_lowlands_a_bowl_for_the_road.summary: Sanda keeps a pot going for anyone walking through hungry, and a pot needs feeding. Bring her a day's meat off the fields and she'll keep one more traveler warm before the road takes them east.
western_lowlands_a_bowl_for_the_road.offer: There's always a pot on my fire for the walking-through -- costs me little and it's the last warm bowl before the land gets unkind east of here. But a pot eats meat faster than a fair does. Bring me a day's worth off the strips, and there's a bowl for you first, and a coin, and my thanks, which are worth more than the coin some days.
western_lowlands_a_bowl_for_the_road.progress: Field-gnawers on the strips will make the day's pot. Bring back four good cuts to Sanda.
western_lowlands_a_bowl_for_the_road.complete: Bless you -- that's the pot fed and a stranger warm tonight who'd have walked east cold otherwise. You eat first, mind, you earned it. A full bench and a full bowl: that's the whole of what I've got to give against the dark, and I give it every day the fire holds. Come back tomorrow. The pot's always hungry, and so, saints know, is the road.

objectives.text: Bring a day's provisions to Sanda's pot

western_lowlands_a_bowl_for_the_road.note: The pot was fed and a stranger sent east warm, the last warm bowl before the land turns unkind. Sanda does this every day the fire holds, unasked. It is the whole of the west's argument against the dark: feed the walking-through, and set a place, and count the ones who do not come back for supper.

western_lowlands_the_drowned_fence.title: The Drowned Fence
western_lowlands_the_drowned_fence.summary: The old fence that once marked the river's edge now stands a hundred paces out in dry field, as if the water walked inland and left it. Mira asks you to go and read the fence, because the reeves are using it to prove the boundary moved.
western_lowlands_the_drowned_fence.offer: You know the drowned fence, out past my strip? It marked the river's edge when Gran was small. It's a hundred paces into dry field now -- not because the river shrank, mind, but because the reeves say the boundary was always there and we've all misremembered the water. They're using a fence to steal a furlong. Go and look at it. Read what's carved on the posts. Tell me the fence remembers where it stood, even if we're not allowed to.
western_lowlands_the_drowned_fence.progress: The drowned fence stands out in the dry field where the river once ran. Reach it and read the posts.
western_lowlands_the_drowned_fence.complete: Water-marks halfway up the posts, dead reeds still tangled at the base -- the fence remembers the river fine, whatever the reeves' map says. It stood at the water's edge and the water's edge is where it stood, and no smudged seal changes a tide-line cut in old oak. I can't fight them with that. But I know now, and knowing is a kind of standing your ground. Take the veil-charm. It thins the seeing a little, when the seeing gets to be too much.

objectives.label: the drowned fence
objectives.text: Read the water-marks on the drowned fence

western_lowlands_the_drowned_fence.note: The fence stood at the river's edge, and the water-marks on its posts still say so, whatever the reeves' map claims. A furlong stolen by insisting everyone misremembered the tide. Mira cannot fight a smudged seal with a tide-line. But the fence remembers, and so does she, and remembering is a way of standing ground.

western_lowlands_east_of_supper.title: East of Supper
western_lowlands_east_of_supper.summary: The neighbors who walked east and never came back left a trail Ruxandra can't follow past her patrol's bounds. She asks you to carry the west's dossier to the Grey Marches road and learn what waits at the treeline that keeps taking the valley's people.
western_lowlands_east_of_supper.offer: You've heard it by now -- folk walk east and don't come back for supper, and their families set a place anyway. I've filled a dossier on every one: names, days, the direction they took. It always ends at the Grey Marches treeline, where my bounds stop and I'm not permitted past. Carry it to the road-edge and see for yourself what's out there drawing them. Then come back and tell me if I should keep setting those places, or start writing names in Ecaterina's book.
western_lowlands_east_of_supper.progress: The east road runs to the Grey Marches treeline where the missing were last seen. Reach it and learn what waits.
western_lowlands_east_of_supper.complete: Grey trees that don't drop their needles, ground warm underfoot the closer you go, and a cult-fire lit among them that isn't what warms the soil -- you saw it and you came back, which is more than the neighbors managed. So it's not east that takes them. It's what east has become. I'll keep setting the places a while yet; a set place is a kind of refusal. But I've given Ecaterina the dossier. Some of those names belong in the book now. Take it -- the marked map, the way through. Somebody after you will need to know the road bends toward that fire.

objectives.label: the Grey Marches treeline road
objectives.text: Reach the eastern treeline and look
Report to Ruxandra what waits east

western_lowlands_east_of_supper.note: The missing did not vanish east; east had become the thing that took them -- grey trees that keep their needles, warm ground, a cult-fire that is not what warms the soil. Ruxandra keeps setting the empty places, a set place being a kind of refusal. But some of the names have gone into Ecaterina's book, and the road out of the west bends now toward that fire.

iron_vein_bent_oar_flat_bread.title: Flat Bread, Grey Bread
iron_vein_bent_oar_flat_bread.summary: The Bent Oar's dough has refused to rise for a fortnight, and Dragan has run out of things to blame. He wants river-water from further up, above the bank where the yeast keeps dying.
iron_vein_bent_oar_flat_bread.offer: Sit, traveler, and mind you don't chip a tooth on my loaf. Bread's come out flat a fortnight now. Bad flour, I said. Bad water, I said. I've stopped saying. Walk up the bank past the fording-stones and fill a jar where the river still runs cold and honest. Maybe the dough'll like it better than mine.
iron_vein_bent_oar_flat_bread.progress: The good water runs above the fording-stones, up the north bank where the fog thins. Fill the jar there.
iron_vein_bent_oar_flat_bread.complete: Cold and clean, thank the saints. We'll knead it in tonight and see. ...You know, I don't mind flat bread. I mind not knowing why. A man likes to blame his flour and be right about it. Here, first bowl of the stew's on me. Eat while it's warm.

objectives.label: the cold spring above the fording-stones
objectives.text: Fill the jar above the fording-stones

iron_vein_bent_oar_flat_bread.note: Dragan kneaded in the clean water and the dough rose no better. He served it flat and grey and stopped meeting eyes, the way the trade goes when the water learns a new taste.

iron_vein_bent_oar_board.title: The Board at the Bent Oar
iron_vein_bent_oar_board.summary: The quest-board by the Bent Oar's door has three years of nails and no one to answer them. Dragan asks a stranger to clear the boldest chore: the mire-things fouling the low fording-place where children still cross.
iron_vein_bent_oar_board.offer: You read? Good. The board's the town's memory of everything nobody got to. Bottom notice, three seasons old: something's denned in the low ford where the little ones cross to the mill. Mire-crawlers, wet as the bog and twice as patient. Clear four of them and I'll strike the nail myself. Been wanting to strike that nail a long while.
iron_vein_bent_oar_board.progress: The mire-horrors haunt the low fording-place, downriver where the bank goes soft. Put four of them down.
iron_vein_bent_oar_board.complete: Four fewer, and the nail comes off the board. First notice I've cleared in a season that wasn't a burial. The children can cross to the mill again — for now, which is all any of us ever gets. Take a striker off the smith's bench; you've a knack for the bold work.

objectives.text: Clear the mire-horrors from the low ford

iron_vein_bent_oar_board.note: The nail came off the board and the low ford stood open. Dragan kept the notice anyway, folded in his apron, the way a man keeps the name of a thing that took three years to answer.

iron_vein_bent_oar_ledger.title: What the Board Remembers
iron_vein_bent_oar_ledger.summary: Clearing one old notice made Dragan look at the rest. Too many name the same three farmsteads upriver, in the same order, season after season. He wants the chronicler's eye on a pattern he can't unsee.
iron_vein_bent_oar_ledger.offer: Since you cleared that nail I can't stop reading the others. Look — same three holdings, upriver, named over and over, always the same order. Kellek's, then the Torn place, then the mill. Like a man reading down a list, and the list knows which door's next. Take it to Grigore. He keeps the years straight when the rest of us can't.
iron_vein_bent_oar_ledger.progress: Grigore Corbeanu keeps the town's chronicle by the old boundary-post. Bring him the board's ledger.
iron_vein_bent_oar_ledger.complete: He went quiet, Grigore did. Said the order matched the wells going copper, one farmstead at a time, north to south. I wanted a coincidence out of him. He's too honest for one. Here — the folk-warding my mother pinned over our door. It never kept anything out. But she slept better under it, and lately so will you.

objectives.text: Show the ledger to Grigore Corbeanu

iron_vein_bent_oar_ledger.note: Three farmsteads, always the same order, north to south — the order the wells took their copper. Dragan wanted a coincidence. The chronicle would not give him one.

iron_vein_smith_bogiron.title: Iron the River Keeps
iron_vein_smith_bogiron.summary: Mircea's ore hasn't come up the road in weeks, so he works the old way — dredging bog-iron from the slack water. His back won't stoop for it anymore. He'll pay in steel for a good haul.
iron_vein_smith_bogiron.offer: Ore comes up the road when the road feels like a road, and it hasn't felt like one since the fair. So it's back to the bog for me — or would be, if my spine agreed. Bog-iron gathers where the water goes still and rusty, and this whole valley's rusty. Bring me eight lumps and I'll hammer you something that outlasts the both of us.
iron_vein_smith_bogiron.progress: Bog-iron settles in the slack pools along the Iron Vein. Gather eight good lumps from the still water.
iron_vein_smith_bogiron.complete: Now that's honest iron — the river made it slow, over years, the way the good things get made. I'll fold it cold before I forge it hot; that's the trick my father wouldn't tell his neighbors. Here. Bog-iron in, a pot-hook out. It'll hang a kettle over any hearth you're lucky enough to find warm.

objectives.text: Dredge bog-iron from the slack pools

iron_vein_smith_bogiron.note: The road stopped bringing ore and Mircea did not ask why. He dredged the river instead, and the river gave up iron it had been making since before the town had a name.

iron_vein_smith_rustmaw.title: The Thing That Eats the Iron
iron_vein_smith_rustmaw.summary: Something has been taking the bog-iron before Mircea can dredge it — rustmaws, the smith says, drawn to metal the way flies find meat. He needs the beds cleared or the forge goes cold for good.
iron_vein_smith_rustmaw.offer: My iron's going missing from the beds, and it isn't thieves — thieves leave footprints. Rustmaws. Great slow jaws that live on metal and grow a hide of it. They came in off the salt-marsh reach, my grandfather's stories say, when the river last ran this wrong. Cull five along the beds. A forge with no iron is just a cold room a man used to be proud of.
iron_vein_smith_rustmaw.progress: The rustmaws work the bog-iron beds downriver, where the metal gathers. Cull five of them.
iron_vein_smith_rustmaw.complete: Five, and the beds are mine again. You see the hide on them? Plate over plate, all our lost iron worn like armor. My grandfather said they come when the river's about to do something. He never said what. He died before it finished. Take the striker — it throws a truer spark than the last one.

objectives.text: Cull the rustmaws at the iron beds

iron_vein_smith_rustmaw.note: The rustmaws wore the town's lost iron for a hide. Mircea's grandfather said they come when the river is about to do something. He died before the river finished the sentence.

iron_vein_smith_wardplate.title: Plate for the Fording-Stones
iron_vein_smith_wardplate.summary: Mircea means to plate the fording-stones so the crossing can be defended by one guard instead of three. He needs bog-iron enough to face the ford — and a rustmaw hide worked cold, before it wakes.
iron_vein_smith_wardplate.offer: I've a mind to armor the fording-stones. One warden could hold that crossing if the stones were faced with iron, and we're short of wardens the way we're short of everything. Bring me a dozen lumps and a rustmaw's hide off the beds. I'll fold them together the cold way. What the river takes slow, I'll give back slower and harder.
iron_vein_smith_wardplate.progress: Twelve bog-iron lumps and a rustmaw hide, both from the beds downriver. The ford wants facing before winter.
iron_vein_smith_wardplate.complete: Cold-folded, every plate, so the metal remembers being river before it was ever a wall. The stones are faced now — a warden and a spear could hold that ford against most things that walk. Against the things that don't walk... well. We do what iron can do. This riverplate's the offcut, sized for you. Wear it where the fog's thick.

objectives.text: Dredge twelve bog-iron lumps for the plating
Take one rustmaw hide, cold-worked

iron_vein_smith_wardplate.note: The fording-stones stand faced in cold iron now, and one warden can hold them against most things that walk. Mircea did not plate them against the things that do not walk. Iron has its limits, and he has always known them.

iron_vein_merchant_stranded.title: The Cart That Waits
iron_vein_merchant_stranded.summary: Vasile's supply cart threw a wheel two bends up the bank and his boy is guarding it alone after dark. He'll trade good coin for an escort's eyes on the road until he can bring a fresh axle.
iron_vein_merchant_stranded.offer: A trader's whole life is in a cart, and mine's sitting axle-broke two bends up the north bank with my sister's boy minding it in the dark. I can't leave my stall and I won't leave the lad. Walk up and stand with him a while, would you? The road's been strange after dusk. Coin says so, and coin never lies to me — I'd know.
iron_vein_merchant_stranded.progress: The broken cart sits two bends up the north bank. Reach the boy and keep the watch until the axle comes.
iron_vein_merchant_stranded.complete: You found him and you stayed — that's twice the man most would've been. He says the fog came right up to the cart-wheels and sat there, like a dog waiting to be let in, then went off upriver. Fog doesn't wait, traveler. Fog drifts. But the boy's safe and the cart's saved, and I pay what I promise. Take the charm. It's meant to keep a mind its own.

objectives.label: the broken cart on the north bank
objectives.text: Reach and guard the stranded cart

iron_vein_merchant_stranded.note: The boy was safe and the cart was saved. He swore the fog came up to the wheels and sat waiting to be let in, then went off upriver. Vasile paid what he promised and did not ask which way upriver.

iron_vein_merchant_salt.title: A Line of Salt
iron_vein_merchant_salt.summary: Every doorstep in the Iron Vein wants salt this season, and not for the pot — they lay it in lines across the threshold. Vasile's stock is bought out by noon. He'll cut a share for whoever can dredge more from the salt-marsh downriver.
iron_vein_merchant_salt.offer: Strangest run of trade I've had in thirty years. Salt. Nobody's salting meat — they're pouring it across their doorsills in a line and won't say why when I ask. Bought out by noon, every day. There's salt to be had off the marsh reach downriver if a body's willing to wade for it. Bring me a pouch's worth and I'll cut you a fair half. Don't step over the line going in, folk say. I've stopped laughing at the folk.
iron_vein_merchant_salt.progress: Marsh-salt can be gathered along the downriver reach where the water turns brackish. Fill the pouch.
iron_vein_merchant_salt.complete: Half's yours, as promised, and the rest'll be gone by dusk. Old Sorin bought a fist of it and laid it clean around his bed, not just the door. I asked him what he keeps out with salt he can step over any morning. He said: 'It's not for keeping out. It's so I'll notice if the line's been crossed in the night.' Keep the pouch, traveler. You'll want a line of your own before long.

objectives.text: Dredge marsh-salt from the brackish reach

iron_vein_merchant_salt.note: They pour the salt so they will notice if the line is crossed in the night. Not to keep a thing out — to keep count of it. Vasile sold every grain and stopped laughing at the folk.

iron_vein_guard_night_road.title: The Road After Dusk
iron_vein_guard_night_road.summary: Bogdan mans the river-road gate and swears the mire-things no longer wander — they gather at one bend after dark, always the same bend, and face upriver. He wants them scattered before the pattern finishes.
iron_vein_guard_night_road.offer: I've watched this road eleven years and I know how a thing that hunts moves. These don't hunt. Every night the mire-horrors pull to the third bend upriver and stand facing the same way, like they're waiting for a bell nobody's rung yet. It's not natural and I won't get used to it. Break them up. Six should scatter the notion out of the rest.
iron_vein_guard_night_road.progress: The mire-horrors gather at the third bend upriver after dusk, all facing one way. Break the gathering.
iron_vein_guard_night_road.complete: Six down and the bend's empty tonight. You saw how they stood, though. Not a circle, not a hunting spread — a row. Facing upriver, toward the fording-stones, toward wherever this river's carrying its rust. My grandmother would've salted the whole bank. I just watch and count. It's all a gate-guard's good for. Take the ward-sign — my post's got two, and you've got none.

objectives.text: Scatter the mire-horrors at the third bend

iron_vein_guard_night_road.note: They stood in a row, facing upriver, waiting for a bell no one had rung. Bogdan broke the gathering and it formed again the next night. A gate-guard watches and counts. It is all he is good for, and he knows it.

iron_vein_guard_lost_warden.title: Stelian's Brother
iron_vein_guard_lost_warden.summary: Stelian's brother walked the upriver patrol four nights ago and hasn't come back. Stelian can't leave his own post to look. He asks a stranger to reach the far patrol-marker and learn what the fog kept.
iron_vein_guard_lost_warden.offer: My brother had the upriver watch four nights back. He didn't come in at dawn and he hasn't since. I can't leave the gate — leave the gate and the whole town's open, and then what was his watch worth? Walk the patrol to the far marker for me. If he's hurt, help him. If he's... just tell me true. I'd rather a true grief than a kind lie. We're past kind lies in this valley.
iron_vein_guard_lost_warden.progress: The far patrol-marker stands upriver where the birches lean thickest. Walk the watch and learn what became of Stelian's brother.
iron_vein_guard_lost_warden.complete: You found his spear at the marker, planted upright, and him standing past it, facing the water, not moving. Not hurt. Just... resting, you said. Since the third night. I'll go up and bring him home if he'll come. He won't. I know he won't. But a man walks his brother home even when there's no brother left in the walking. Thank you for the truth. Take the counteragent. He'd have wanted it kept for someone.

objectives.label: the far patrol-marker upriver
objectives.text: Reach the far patrol-marker

iron_vein_guard_lost_warden.note: His spear stood planted at the marker and he stood past it, facing the water, resting since the third night. Stelian went to walk his brother home. There was no brother left in the walking, and he went anyway.

iron_vein_chronicler_names.title: The Years the River Kept
iron_vein_chronicler_names.summary: Grigore keeps the Iron Vein's chronicle and has noticed the boundary-stones upriver have started shedding their carved names, moss and all. He wants them read and recorded before the river takes the last of them.
iron_vein_chronicler_names.offer: I keep the years so the valley doesn't forget itself, but the valley's started forgetting on its own. The boundary-stones upriver — six hundred years of cut names, farmsteads, ford-tolls — they're going smooth. Not weathered. Wiped, like a hand across a slate. Reach the old boundary-post and read me what names are left, before the river keeps those too. Read them, mind. Don't copy them. There's a difference lately, and it's the difference that matters.
iron_vein_chronicler_names.progress: The oldest boundary-post stands where the river bends north. Reach it and read the names still cut there.
iron_vein_chronicler_names.complete: Four names left where my father recorded forty. And the fresh-cut mark below them — you were right not to copy it. I've filled a chronicle with things I read and lived to write down. That mark, I'll leave to the moss, and pray the moss is slower than whatever's cutting. Here. The charm keeps a mind incurious. In this valley that's not cowardice. It's the whole art of staying alive.

objectives.label: the oldest boundary-post
objectives.text: Read the names on the old boundary-post

iron_vein_chronicler_names.note: Forty names cut in the boundary-stones, and four left standing. Below them, a mark freshly cut, and the good sense not to copy it. Grigore left it to the moss and prayed the moss was slower than whatever wielded the chisel.

iron_vein_chronicler_vigil.title: A Watch on the Warm Stone
iron_vein_chronicler_vigil.summary: Grigore wants a thing witnessed, not fought. A flat stone above the north bank has gone warm to the touch in the cold, and the dust around it lies in fine parallel lines. Stand a watch and record what changes — nothing more.
iron_vein_chronicler_vigil.offer: I've a stone I need watched, not fought. Up the north bank, a flat grey slab that's gone warm as a loaf in this cold, and the dust around it — look at it and you'll see — lies in fine straight lines, all pointing one way. I'm too old to sit the night out on wet ground. Reach it near dark and keep still a while. Don't touch the stone. Don't speak near it. Watch, count, come tell me. Witnessing costs nothing. It's the reading that bills you.
iron_vein_chronicler_vigil.progress: The warm slab lies above the north bank. Reach it near dusk and keep a still watch — touch nothing, speak nothing.
iron_vein_chronicler_vigil.complete: You stood the watch. Good. The dust re-laid itself while you sat, you say — the lines swung a quarter-turn, all together, like iron filings when a lodestone's moved beneath the table. And the warmth came up through your boots. I'll record it: the stone above the north bank is live. Not what it did. Just that it is. That's all a chronicler can safely keep. Take the counteragent — a swallow of it quiets the listening, if it ever finds your ear.

objectives.label: the warm slab above the north bank
objectives.text: Keep a still watch over the warm stone

iron_vein_chronicler_vigil.note: The dust re-laid itself a quarter-turn while the watch held, all the lines swinging together, and the warmth came up through the boot-soles. Grigore recorded only that the stone was live. Not what it did. It is all a chronicler can safely keep.

iron_vein_lupu_lost_sheep.title: Lupu's Wandered Ewe
iron_vein_lupu_lost_sheep.summary: Lupu's best ewe slipped her pen and wandered toward the birch-flats, and the mire-things are thick there this year. He can't chase her on his bad foot. Thin the crawlers so a man can walk the flats safe.
iron_vein_lupu_lost_sheep.offer: My good ewe's off again — jumped the hurdle and made for the birch-flats like she's got an appointment. Trouble is the mire-crawlers are thick as fleas out there this spring, and my foot's no good for chasing anything faster than a thought. Knock a few of the wet brutes back so I can fetch her without ending up in a crawler's belly. She's worth three of the others, that ewe. Don't tell the rest.
iron_vein_lupu_lost_sheep.progress: The mire-horrors are thick on the birch-flats where the ewe wandered. Thin four so Lupu can fetch her.
iron_vein_lupu_lost_sheep.complete: Four fewer and there's my girl, chewing away like she never gave me a grey hair. Bless you, traveler. A man's whole fortune, some days, is one stubborn ewe and the legs to catch her — and I've only got the ewe. Here, take the ward my wife hexed over the pen. It kept the sheep in and worse things out, or so we tell ourselves, and telling ourselves is half of keeping warm.

objectives.text: Thin the crawlers on the birch-flats

iron_vein_lupu_lost_sheep.note: Lupu got his ewe back, worth three of the others, and said so to no one but the traveler. The hex over the pen kept the sheep in and worse things out, or so they told themselves. Telling themselves is half of keeping warm.

iron_vein_lupu_ghoul_pyre.title: The Ones Who Won't Stay Down
iron_vein_lupu_ghoul_pyre.summary: The folk who went 'resting' up the bank have started walking again — but wrong, throat-marked, upriver-facing. Lupu buried three neighbors this month and can't bury them twice. Put the walking ones down before they reach the town gate.
iron_vein_lupu_ghoul_pyre.offer: I buried Torn and both his boys this month, traveler. Dug the holes myself, said the words myself. And now they're up and walking the bank — throats marked with one clean upward cut, every one, and always shuffling upriver like they've somewhere to be. Iron-vein ghouls, the old word is. I can't bury a neighbor twice; it breaks something in a man. Put five of them down for me. I'll not watch, but I'll thank you.
iron_vein_lupu_ghoul_pyre.progress: The iron-vein ghouls walk the north bank, throat-marked, drifting upriver. Put five back down.
iron_vein_lupu_ghoul_pyre.complete: Five down, and I'll not ask which faces they wore. You saw the marks on their throats — one stroke, upward, clean as a signature. That's not a wound a wolf gives, nor a man. Something signed them. Marked the place as taken and moved on. I've stopped digging graves deep. No sense, if they won't stay in them. Take the warding. It's blessed thrice. It works about as well as anything does now, which is to say I don't know.

objectives.text: Put down the throat-marked ghouls on the bank

iron_vein_lupu_ghoul_pyre.note: Each ghoul bore one clean upward stroke across the throat, like a signature — a place marked taken, and the marker moved on. Lupu stopped digging graves deep. No sense in it, if the dead will not stay in them.

iron_vein_sorin_charm.title: Sorin's Sleepless House
iron_vein_sorin_charm.summary: Old Sorin lays salt around his whole bed, not just the door, and still can't sleep — he says the fog leans at his window and he catches himself listening back. He wants the herb-warding an apothecary might make from marsh-reagents.
iron_vein_sorin_charm.offer: You'll think me an old fool, and you'd be in good company — I think it too, most mornings. Nights are the trouble. The fog comes and leans on my window-glass, and worse, I catch myself leaning toward it. Listening. For what, I couldn't tell you, and that's the fright of it. Gather me the makings for a proper warding — marsh-herbs, the sort my mother knew — and I'll rest easier, or at least I'll rest less curious. Curious is the thing to lose out here.
iron_vein_sorin_charm.progress: The warding-herbs grow along the marsh reach downriver. Gather the makings Sorin needs.
iron_vein_sorin_charm.complete: You brought the lot, bless you. I'll bruise the leaves and hang them at the sill and lay my salt and, saints willing, keep my own ears to myself tonight. That's all a body wants at my age — to hear nothing, and to want to hear nothing. My mother lived to eighty in this valley by being the least curious woman in it. I mean to match her. Take the counteragent. If ever you catch yourself leaning toward a whisper — drink, and stop listening.

objectives.text: Gather marsh warding-herbs for Sorin

iron_vein_sorin_charm.note: Sorin hung the bruised herbs and laid his salt and hoped to hear nothing, and to want to hear nothing. His mother lived to eighty by being the least curious woman in the valley. He means to match her, if the fog will let him.

iron_vein_sorin_mill.title: The Mill That Grinds Grey
iron_vein_sorin_mill.summary: The river-mill across the ford grinds true, but every loaf from its flour comes out flat and grey — like the Bent Oar's bread, like the whole valley's. Sorin asks a stranger to reach the mill and see whether it's the stone, the water, or something the flour has learned.
iron_vein_sorin_mill.offer: The mill still turns, the stone's still true, and yet every sack it grinds makes bread that won't rise. Same as Dragan's loaf, same as everyone's. My people milled at that wheel four generations. I'd go myself but my legs are as flat and grey as the bread these days. Reach the mill across the low ford and tell me what you see. Is it the water fouling the grain? The stone worn wrong? Or has the wheat itself forgotten how to be bread?
iron_vein_sorin_mill.progress: The river-mill stands across the low ford. Reach it and see why its flour will not rise.
iron_vein_sorin_mill.complete: You saw the grain, then — plump and sound in the hopper, and dead as dust the moment it's ground, no yeast in the world can wake it. It's not the stone and it's not the water. The bread stopped rising when the wells went copper and the ground went warm, and no miller alive can fix a thing the ground decided. Four generations we ground good bread there. I'll not be the fifth to pretend. Take the crock — my mother's recipe, still on the lid. Someone should keep it who might use it somewhere the yeast still lives.

objectives.label: the river-mill across the low ford
objectives.text: Reach the grey-grinding mill

iron_vein_sorin_mill.note: The grain was sound in the hopper and dead the moment it was ground. Four generations milled good bread there; Sorin would not be the fifth to pretend. The yeast died when the ground made up its mind, and no miller can mend what the ground decides.

iron_vein_daily_board_chores.title: The Bent Oar's Standing Work
iron_vein_daily_board_chores.summary: The board never empties. Every dawn Dragan pins fresh notices — a crawler in the reeds, a beast at the beds — and pays a coin and a bowl for a day's honest clearing along the river.
iron_vein_daily_board_chores.offer: Board's full again — it always is by morning, like the valley grows chores in the night. Today's the usual: mire-things thick in the near reeds again. Clear a few, come back before the stew's cold, and there's a coin and a bowl in it. Steady work's the one warm thing left a man can count on out here. That, and the stew. Mostly the stew.
iron_vein_daily_board_chores.progress: The mire-horrors crowd the near reeds by the Bent Oar. Clear a day's worth and come back before the stew cools.
iron_vein_daily_board_chores.complete: Cleared, and before the stew went cold — that's a good day's work by any reckoning we've got left. Coin's yours, bowl's poured, and the board'll be full again tomorrow, saints preserve us. Steady's the word. In a valley like this, steady's nearly the same as safe. Nearly.

objectives.text: Clear the day's crawlers from the near reeds

iron_vein_daily_board_chores.note: The board fills every night, as if the valley grows its chores in the dark. Dragan pays a coin and a bowl for the clearing. Steady is nearly the same as safe out here. Nearly.

iron_vein_daily_iron_beds.title: A Day at the Iron Beds
iron_vein_daily_iron_beds.summary: The forge eats iron and the rustmaws eat the beds, so Mircea's work is never done. Each day he pays in coin for whoever'll dredge a fresh haul of bog-iron and knock back the beasts guarding it.
iron_vein_daily_iron_beds.offer: Forge doesn't rest, so neither does the iron-hunt. Rustmaws will have crept back onto the beds overnight — they always do, drawn to the metal like a debt to a poor man. Dredge me a fresh haul and knock the beasts off it. Coin for the iron, coin for the courage, and a forge that stays lit another day. In this valley a lit forge is nearly good news.
iron_vein_daily_iron_beds.progress: Dredge bog-iron from the beds and drive back the rustmaws that gathered overnight.
iron_vein_daily_iron_beds.complete: Fresh iron and a clear bed — that keeps the forge honest one more day, and one more day is the only length of time worth planning in, out here. Coin's yours. Come back tomorrow; the rustmaws will, and so will the iron, and so, saints willing, will we.

objectives.text: Dredge a fresh haul of bog-iron
Drive the rustmaws off the beds

iron_vein_daily_iron_beds.note: The rustmaws creep back onto the iron beds every night, drawn to metal like a debt to a poor man. Mircea pays for the dredging and the courage both. A lit forge is nearly good news in the Iron Vein.

iron_vein_main_courier_satchel.title: The Sealed Satchel
iron_vein_main_courier_satchel.summary: A courier was found dead at the upriver marker — no wound, clean hands, his satchel still sealed. Bogdan won't touch it and won't let it sit. He asks a steady stranger to carry it down to the chronicler, sealed, and not read a word of what killed its bearer.
iron_vein_main_courier_satchel.offer: We found a courier at the upriver marker this dawn. No wound on him, traveler. Hands clean, boots dry, face... I won't describe the face. His satchel's still sealed — wax whole, string knotted — so he never delivered whatever he carried. He stopped to read it. That's what I think. That's what killed him, near as any of us can say. I can't leave the gate and I won't carry it and I won't burn it unread, that's not my right. Take it to Grigore, sealed. Don't open it. Whatever it whispers at the rest-stops — and it will — you look at your boots and keep walking.
iron_vein_main_courier_satchel.progress: Carry the sealed satchel down the bank to Grigore the chronicler. Do not break the seal, whatever it offers you along the way.
iron_vein_main_courier_satchel.complete: You brought it sealed. Grigore's face when he took it — he knew the weight of it before the wax was in his hand. He'll file it unopened in the lead-lined box, he says, where a thing can be kept without being heard. The courier had a name, once. We'll cut it in the boundary-stone, small, while there's stone left to cut. You carried a killing thing a whole day and never let it speak. Not many could. Take the crown — old iron-vein work, cold-forged. It keeps a head clear. You'll want the head more than the crown, but keep both.

objectives.label: the upriver marker where the courier fell
objectives.text: Recover the courier's sealed satchel
Deliver the sealed satchel to Grigore

iron_vein_main_courier_satchel.note: No wound, clean hands, the satchel still sealed — the courier stopped to read what he carried, and the reading was the wound. Grigore filed it unopened in the lead-lined box, where a thing may be kept without being heard. The bearer had a name. They cut it small in the boundary-stone, while there was stone left to cut.

iron_vein_main_warm_ground.title: The Ground Goes Warm
iron_vein_main_warm_ground.summary: The satchel filed, Grigore turns to its source. The warm slab on the north bank has spread its heat, and a fresh inscription cut there has drawn a herald up from below — an iron-vein ghoul marking the site. The carving is the anchor; Grigore wants it buried, not read, and the herald sent back down.
iron_vein_main_warm_ground.offer: The satchel came from upriver, and upriver is where the ground's gone warm — you felt it, on the watch. Since the courier fell, the heat's spread three furrows out, the wells below it run copper, and something's cut a fresh mark in the warm slab. Deep. New. A herald's come up to stand over it — one of the throat-markers, drawn like the rustmaws to iron. Here's the whole cruelty of it: kill the herald and it comes back, because the mark summons it. The mark is the anchor. Reach the slab, put the herald down long enough to work, and bury the carving — earth over it, no reading, no copying. Efface it and the thing loses its hold and goes back under. Read it, and you go where the courier went.
iron_vein_main_warm_ground.progress: Reach the warm slab on the north bank. Put down the herald long enough to bury the fresh inscription — earth over it, no reading. The mark is the anchor; efface it and the herald loses its hold.
iron_vein_main_warm_ground.complete: Buried. You buried it. Earth over the mark, no eyes on the carving, and the herald sank back through the warm ground the moment its anchor went dark — you saw it fold down into the soil like water into sand. The wells will clear in a week, maybe. The ground will cool, maybe. I've written it in the chronicle the only safe way: a mark was cut, and covered, and the covering held. Not what it said. Never what it said. You did the harder thing, traveler — you stood over a whisper you could have understood, and you shoveled dirt instead. That restraint is the rarest strength this valley breeds. Sabira's people up the road collect that kind of strength. They'll want your name. Take the crown of the deep-bed — cold-forged over the very iron this river's been making since before we had a word for river. Wear it east.

objectives.label: the warm slab on the north bank
objectives.text: Reach the warm slab and its fresh inscription
Put down the herald standing over the mark

objectives.label: the buried inscription
objectives.text: Bury the inscription unread

iron_vein_main_warm_ground.note: The mark went under the earth unread and the herald folded back into the warm ground the moment its anchor darkened. Grigore wrote it the only safe way: a mark was cut, and covered, and the covering held. Not what it said. Never what it said. The traveler stood over a thing they could have understood and shoveled dirt instead — the rarest strength the valley breeds, and someone up the road is already collecting names like it.

iron_vein_side_pilgrims_rest.title: The Camp With No Tracks Out
iron_vein_side_pilgrims_rest.summary: Pilgrims camped on the old ford-road and never struck the tents. Stelian counted the footprints himself: many going in, none coming out. He wants the site reached and the truth of it brought back, before the fog decides the road was always empty.
iron_vein_side_pilgrims_rest.offer: Pilgrims stopped on the old ford-road three days back — I saw their fires from the gate. The fires are out now, but the tents are still up. So I walked out to the treeline and counted the ground, the way my brother taught me. Footprints going in, traveler. A dozen, more. Coming out? None. Not one. A camp doesn't unmake itself. Reach it and tell me where a dozen souls walk to and leave no track. I need to know. And I'm afraid to need to know — that's this valley, isn't it. Afraid of the answer and unable to stop asking.
iron_vein_side_pilgrims_rest.progress: The pilgrims' camp stands on the old ford-road, tents up, fires cold. Reach it and learn where a dozen souls went with no tracks out.
iron_vein_side_pilgrims_rest.complete: Bedrolls still warm, you said. Stew cold in the pots. And a letter, pinned to the center tent-pole, addressed to no name — just 'to whoever reads this.' You left it pinned. Good. You've learned the valley's one rule faster than most who're born to it. A dozen went in and none came out and the only thing that stayed was an invitation. I'll post a warning at the ford: camp elsewhere. It won't be read. Nobody reads warnings out here anymore — they're too busy reading the things that kill them. Take the ward. Keep your own name unread.

objectives.label: the pilgrims' abandoned camp
objectives.text: Reach the camp with no tracks out

iron_vein_side_pilgrims_rest.note: A dozen sets of prints went into the camp and none came out. The bedrolls were warm, the stew cold, and a letter pinned to the center pole addressed to whoever would read it. The traveler left it pinned — the valley's one rule, learned faster than most who are born to it.

iron_vein_side_torn_widow.title: A Place Set at the Table
iron_vein_side_torn_widow.summary: Sorin's neighbor still sets her husband's place at supper though he's stood in the birch-flats 'resting' since Tuesday. She won't hear he's gone. Sorin asks a stranger to bring her one true, gentle thing — a keepsake from the field — so she can begin to know.
iron_vein_side_torn_widow.offer: My neighbor lost her Torn to the resting-sickness — you know the kind, stood in the flats facing upriver, won't move, won't come home, warm dust round his boots. She sets his place at supper still. Ladles his bowl. I can't tell her; it's not my grief to end. But maybe... maybe if someone brought her one small true thing off him — his whittling-knife, his cord, something a living man would've come back for — she could start to know it in her own time. Gently, mind. Grief's the last warm thing some folk have. Don't put it out all at once.
iron_vein_side_torn_widow.progress: Torn stands 'resting' in the birch-flats. Recover one small keepsake a living man would have come back for, and bring it gently to Sorin.
iron_vein_side_torn_widow.complete: His keepsake-cord. She'll know it the moment she sees it — he wore it forty years, wouldn't sleep without it. You brought it gentle, and that's the whole of the kindness. She won't take his place away tonight, nor tomorrow. But she'll stop ladling two bowls one of these suppers, and the empty place'll say what none of us dared. That's how grief starts out here — not with a burial. With a bowl you finally don't fill. Take the cord for now; give it when she's ready. You'll know the day. Everyone always knows the day and pretends they don't.

objectives.text: Recover Torn's keepsake from the birch-flats
Bring the keepsake gently to Sorin

iron_vein_side_torn_widow.note: Torn stood resting in the flats and his widow set his place at supper still. Sorin sent his keepsake-cord home so she could begin to know it in her own time. Grief starts out here not with a burial but with a bowl you finally do not fill.

iron_vein_side_merchant_toll.title: The Toll at the Narrow Place
iron_vein_side_merchant_toll.summary: Someone has been collecting a toll at the river-narrows with a forged writ, squeezing traders who can't read it. Vasile can't prove the forgery without the writ in hand. He asks a stranger to reach the toll-post, get a look, and bring back what the collector's really holding.
iron_vein_side_merchant_toll.offer: There's a new toll at the narrows, traveler, and no lord I know of set it. A fellow with a writ and a strongbox, taking a cut off every cart through the narrow place — and half the traders can't read the writ to know it's false. I've a nose for a forged seal; thirty years of trade sharpens it. Reach the toll-post, get me a good look at that writ or a copy of it, and I'll have the collector off my river by the next fog. Careful how you copy it, mind. Copy the wrong thing in this valley and it's you who gets collected.
iron_vein_side_merchant_toll.progress: The false toll-post stands at the river-narrows. Reach it and get a look at the collector's writ.
iron_vein_side_merchant_toll.complete: A forgery, plain as a flat loaf — wrong seal, wrong hand, and the wax the cheap grey kind no true clerk would stamp with. You copied only the seal, not the body of it. Wise. I'll take this to the wardens and the toll comes down by dusk. Funny thing, though — the collector didn't run when you flashed the wardens' mark. Smiled. Said the river collects its own tolls now and he was only keeping the books tidy. Then he was gone, strongbox and all. I don't like a man who smiles at a warrant. Take the forgery; it's evidence, and it's a lesson in what a false writ can do if the reader can't read.

objectives.label: the false toll-post at the narrows
objectives.text: Reach the toll-post and read the writ

iron_vein_side_merchant_toll.note: The toll-writ was a forgery, wrong seal and cheap grey wax, and the traveler copied only the seal and not the body. The collector did not run from the warrant. He smiled, said the river collects its own tolls now, and was gone with his strongbox. Vasile does not like a man who smiles at a warrant.

iron_vein_side_ghoul_reach.title: The Herald's Ground
iron_vein_side_ghoul_reach.summary: A second warm patch has opened south of the ford, and the ghouls that gather there guard something buried. Bogdan wants the site reached and the herald-things thinned so the wardens can mark the ground unclean before it copper-poisons the last clean well.
iron_vein_side_ghoul_reach.offer: There's a second warm patch now, south of the ford, and I like it even less than the first. The throat-markers cluster there thick — standing guard over a patch of bare warm earth, and no carving I can see, which somehow frightens me worse than a carving would. Something's under it. Reach the ground, thin the herald-things enough for the wardens to come mark it unclean, and we'll rope it off before it coppers the last clean well below. I won't dig it up. I've learned that much. Some ground you mark and leave and pray the marking holds.
iron_vein_side_ghoul_reach.progress: Reach the second warm patch south of the ford and thin the iron-vein ghouls guarding it so the wardens can mark it unclean.
iron_vein_side_ghoul_reach.complete: Thinned, and the wardens have their ground to rope. You said the bare earth breathed warm the whole while, and the ghouls didn't fight so much as stand between you and the soil, like servants keeping a door. Servants keep doors for a reason. There's something behind this one and I'd rather rope it shut than learn what. The last clean well's still clean tonight. That's the whole victory, and I'll take it. Take the razor — blackglass, off the old marsh-knappers. It cuts what iron won't, and out here that's most of what wants cutting.

objectives.label: the second warm patch south of the ford
objectives.text: Reach the second warm ground
Thin the herald-things guarding the ground

iron_vein_side_ghoul_reach.note: The bare earth breathed warm and the ghouls stood between the traveler and the soil like servants keeping a door. The wardens roped it off unclean rather than learn what waited behind it. The last clean well ran clean that night — the whole of the victory, and Bogdan took it.

iron_vein_side_chronicle_close.title: The Iron Vein's Reckoning
iron_vein_side_chronicle_close.summary: With the mark buried and the satchel filed, Grigore closes the season's chronicle. He asks a stranger to carry the finished account to the Bent Oar's board — a public reckoning of what the valley lost and held — and to read it aloud once, for the ones who no longer can.
iron_vein_side_chronicle_close.offer: The season's done, or done enough to write. Marks cut and covered, wells gone copper north to south, a courier filed unheard, and the count of the resting we don't say aloud. I've set it all in the chronicle, honest as I can make it. Carry it to Dragan's board and pin it where the notices go, and — this is the asking — read it aloud once, at the hearth, for the ones who've stopped being able to. Not the whole valley needs to hear. Just the reading needs to happen. A thing witnessed is a thing not yet finished being true.
iron_vein_side_chronicle_close.progress: Carry the closed chronicle to the Bent Oar's board and read the season's reckoning aloud once, at the hearth.
iron_vein_side_chronicle_close.complete: You read it and the taproom went quiet the good way, the listening-to-a-neighbor way, not the other. Dragan pinned it high. The names are up there now — the courier's, and Torn's, and the four the boundary-stone still holds — where a passing eye might catch them and a passing soul might carry one on. That's all a chronicle is, in the end: a place to leave a name so the river doesn't get all of it. The Iron Vein won its season the only way this valley ever wins. It lost less than it might have, and it kept the count. Take the crown. Wear the cold of it east, where the next season's already writing itself.

objectives.label: the Bent Oar's quest-board
objectives.text: Pin the chronicle at the board
Read the season's reckoning aloud to Dragan's hearth

iron_vein_side_chronicle_close.note: The chronicle was read once at the hearth, and the taproom went quiet the listening-to-a-neighbor way. The names went up on the board where a passing eye might catch them and carry one on. The Iron Vein won its season the only way the valley ever wins: it lost less than it might have, and it kept the count.

stonepath_coach_the_last_seat.title: The Last Seat on the Vetka Coach
stonepath_coach_the_last_seat.summary: Pavel keeps the coach post where all four roads meet, and every seat but one is spoken for. He needs the crossroads walked and its posted milestones counted before the barge folk arrive to argue over places.
stonepath_coach_the_last_seat.offer: Four roads come in and one coach goes out, dear, and the arithmetic never once agreed with the passengers. Walk me the crossroads and count the standing milestones -- the real ones, cut ones, not the leaning stones nobody set. I sell no seat till I know how far a body's truly going.
stonepath_coach_the_last_seat.progress: The milestones stand along the four spurs off the crossroads yard. Count them, and mind which are milestones and which are the old stones.
stonepath_coach_the_last_seat.complete: Nine milestones, and you're sure of the ninth. Good. The tenth road's got no milestone -- north's never had one, and I've stopped asking who takes it down. Here's your fare back, and a seat kept warm. Warm's the only comfort left here, so I hoard it.

objectives.label: the crossroads coach yard
objectives.text: Count the milestones at the crossroads

stonepath_coach_the_last_seat.note: Nine milestones stood true, and the north road had none, as it never has. Pavel kept the tenth seat empty. He always does. He has stopped saying why.

stonepath_wolf_survey_line.title: The Line the Wolves Walk
stonepath_wolf_survey_line.summary: Tudor patrols the west spur, and the wolves there no longer hunt in a ring -- they walk a straight line, dusk to dusk, along the old road. He wants the pack broken before it finishes whatever it is pacing off.
stonepath_wolf_survey_line.offer: Beyond the western milestone the pack's gone strange -- not circling, not scattering, walking a line. Even spacing, dawn and dusk, same line, like men laying out a fence they mean to build tomorrow. Break it. I've patrolled this spur eleven years and I have never wanted less to watch it finish.
stonepath_wolf_survey_line.progress: The wolves keep to the straight line along the west spur, out past the leaning milestone. Cut the pack down before it walks the line twice.
stonepath_wolf_survey_line.complete: Scattered, and the line's broken with them. You saw how they held it, though -- shoulder to shoulder, even as fence-posts. That's not a pack. That's a survey crew that grew fur. I'll write it up. Nobody up the road reads my writing, but somebody ought to have written it.

objectives.text: Break the pack pacing the west spur

stonepath_wolf_survey_line.note: The wolves walked a straight line, evenly spaced, twice a day. Tudor called it a survey crew. He was not entirely joking, and that is the part that keeps him up.

stonepath_scholar_first_reading.title: A Reasonable Man and His Thesis
stonepath_scholar_first_reading.summary: Vintila has spent four years proving the crossroads stones are a calendar. He needs a witness to check his measurements at three stones -- and is too proud to admit he keeps forgetting to bring the fourth reading home.
stonepath_scholar_first_reading.offer: You have a steady hand and no opinion, which makes you the ideal instrument. My thesis is nearly complete: these stones are a calendar, six hundred years of one, and the four kingdoms cross here because their ancestors kept the same feast-days. Walk to three of the stones and read me the angle of the top cut. Only the angle. I do the interpreting.
stonepath_scholar_first_reading.progress: Three of the crossroads stones want measuring. Read the angle of the top cut at each -- the angle only, Vintila was firm -- and bring the numbers back.
stonepath_scholar_first_reading.complete: Twelve degrees, twelve, and twelve. ...You are certain. Of course you are. That is not a calendar, that is a repetition, and repetitions are not made by ancestors keeping feast-days, they are made by -- no. No. Four years of work. Give me the numbers again and let me find where you erred. You must have erred.

objectives.label: the western stone
objectives.text: Read the western stone's top cut

objectives.label: the southern stone
objectives.text: Read the southern stone's top cut

objectives.label: the eastern stone
objectives.text: Read the eastern stone's top cut

stonepath_scholar_first_reading.note: Three stones, one angle, repeated: twelve degrees, twelve, twelve. Vintila called it his error, not the stones'. He is a reasonable man. That is exactly how they get the reasonable ones.

stonepath_boar_fair_provisions.title: Provisions for a Crossing
stonepath_boar_fair_provisions.summary: Aurel feeds every coach that stops at the crossroads, and the pot is thin this season. The thicket boars have gone fat on the good ground -- he wants six of them for the road-kitchen before the north coach comes through cold and hungry.
stonepath_boar_fair_provisions.offer: A body should not cross four kingdoms on an empty stomach, and I'll not let one leave my yard that way. The thickets are thick with boar this year -- the ground's been generous, folk say, which I choose not to think too hard on. Bring me six good beasts and I'll have stew enough for every seat, north road included. Especially north.
stonepath_boar_fair_provisions.progress: The thicket boars root the brush off the crossroads yard. Six of them will fill Aurel's road-kitchen for the season.
stonepath_boar_fair_provisions.complete: Now that is a pot worth stopping for. The east coach will be loud and warm tonight, and the west, and even the poor souls bound north will go off with something hot in them. A full belly's a small mercy, and small mercies are the only size they come in here. Bless you -- take a bowl yourself.

objectives.text: Hunt thicket boar for the road-kitchen

stonepath_boar_fair_provisions.note: Aurel's pot fed all four roads, north included. He salts his stew heavy. He says the crossroads folk have always liked salt. He does not say salt keeps nothing out but reminds a thing there's a door.

stonepath_hornet_coach_road.title: A Nuisance With Wings
stonepath_hornet_coach_road.summary: Sava's mule bolted at a wilds-hornet nest and scattered half a cart of good cloth into the ditch. He wants the swarms cleared off the south spur so the next merchant keeps his mules -- and his temper.
stonepath_hornet_coach_road.offer: Look at me -- selling cloth out of a ditch, because my mule met a hornet and reconsidered its career. The swarms have nested all along the south spur and every carter this side of the crossroads is one sting from ruin. Clear four nests for me. Do it and I'll not only pay you, I'll tell every trader on four roads your name kindly, which in my trade is worth more than the coin.
stonepath_hornet_coach_road.progress: The wilds-hornet swarms nest along the south spur where the carters pass. Four nests cleared will settle the mules and the merchants both.
stonepath_hornet_coach_road.complete: Cleared, and the mules are calm as monks again. You've saved a dozen carters a bad afternoon and me a worse one. Here -- coin, and a word: I say your name kindly from here to Sangeroasa's gate. A merchant's kind word travels further than he does. Mine's already three roads ahead of you.

objectives.text: Clear hornet swarms from the south spur

stonepath_hornet_coach_road.note: Sava got his cloth out of the ditch and the carters got their afternoon back. He kept his word and spread the traveler's name kindly down the south road. Kind words travel that road easily. Little else comes back up it.

stonepath_forge_road_iron.title: The Ore That Stopped Coming
stonepath_forge_road_iron.summary: Radomir shoes every coach-horse at the crossroads, and the ore-carts from the Iron Vein have not come in a month. Until they do, he tempers what steel he has with boar-tusk -- and needs a good pile of it.
stonepath_forge_road_iron.offer: Four roads and not one of them brought me ore this month. The Vein carts used to come like clockwork; now the clock's stopped and nobody'll say who wound it down. I can keep the coaches shod with what I've got if I temper hard, and for that I need boar-tusk -- a season's worth. Bring me eight beasts off the thickets and I'll keep the horses walking, which keeps everybody walking.
stonepath_forge_road_iron.progress: Boar-tusk tempers the steel. Eight thicket boars will give Radomir tusk enough to shoe the coaches through the season.
stonepath_forge_road_iron.complete: Good tusk. That'll harden a season of shoes, and a shod horse is a road that still works, whatever else has stopped working on it. I don't ask why the ore quit coming. On the crossroads a man learns which questions the roads answer, and that's not one. Take this -- I hammered it before the carts stopped. Honest iron, from when iron was honest.

objectives.text: Hunt boar for tempering-tusk

stonepath_forge_road_iron.note: The Iron Vein ore stopped coming up the road, and Radomir did not ask why. He tempered with tusk and kept the coaches shod. On the crossroads, not asking is a trade skill, and he is a master of it.

stonepath_warm_stone_ward.title: The Stone That Warms Under a Hand
stonepath_warm_stone_ward.summary: Zaharia swept the crossroads yard his whole life and one stone has begun to warm under his palm -- only his, only that one. He wants it warded the old way, and he wants someone to touch it and tell him he is imagining things.
stonepath_warm_stone_ward.offer: Sixty years I've swept this yard, and I know every stone in it the way you know your own teeth. One's gone warm. Only under my hand. Marta's kin taught me a salt line for such, but my knees won't kneel to lay it right anymore. Lay it for me -- and put your own bare hand to the stone first, and tell me an old man's fancying it. Tell me it's just the sun. Please tell me it's the sun.
stonepath_warm_stone_ward.progress: The warm stone stands at the north edge of the crossroads yard. Lay the salt line the old way -- but touch it first, if you dare, and settle Zaharia's mind.
stonepath_warm_stone_ward.complete: ...You felt it too. There's no sun in the north edge before noon and you felt it warm as bread. Well. The salt's laid, for what salt's worth, and my mind's not settled -- it's the opposite. But thank you. It's a mercy to be believed, even about a thing you'd rather nobody believed. I'll sweep round it from now. I'll sweep round it careful.

objectives.label: the warm stone at the yard's north edge
objectives.text: Lay the salt line at the warm stone

stonepath_warm_stone_ward.note: The stone warmed under a bare hand where no sun reached it. The salt was laid; the salt keeps nothing out. Zaharia sweeps round it careful now, and does not put his palm to it again, and calls that wisdom, and is right.

stonepath_east_officer_dispatch.title: The Officer From the East Road
stonepath_east_officer_dispatch.summary: Ursu is Blestem's man at the crossroads, and he trades in one currency: what he knows. He will not let the player east up the Whisper Passes yet, but he will pay for the wolf-count on the east spur -- and file it somewhere the player cannot follow.
stonepath_east_officer_dispatch.offer: You want the east road. No. You are not ready for Blestem and Blestem is more than ready for you, which is worse. But you have use. There are wolves on the east spur pacing something off, and I need a count -- five, alive or dead, watched close. What they're measuring is filed above my rank. Bring me the count and I'll owe you a favor, which in my city is the only wealth that spends.
stonepath_east_officer_dispatch.progress: Ursu wants five wolves on the east spur watched and their pacing noted. Cull them and mark how they stand.
stonepath_east_officer_dispatch.complete: Five, and you saw the spacing -- twelve paces, always twelve. I'll file that. No, you may not ask where. In Blestem we do not tell a man what his information becomes; that would make it his. You've a favor from me now, and a name in a ledger you'll never read. Both are worth more than you think, and cost more, which is the same sentence said honestly.

objectives.text: Cull and count the wolves on the east spur
Report the count to Ursu

stonepath_east_officer_dispatch.note: Ursu took the count -- twelve paces, always twelve -- and filed it above his rank. The traveler's name went into a Blestem ledger, unread and unpayable. Ursu called that a favor. In his city, that is what favors are.

stonepath_the_leaning_stone.title: The Stone Nobody Set
stonepath_the_leaning_stone.summary: Among the old crossroads stones leans a new one nobody remembers raising. Vintila -- shaken since his calendar failed -- wants it charted before he admits to himself it is getting shorter, not taller, the way the shortening stone at the crossroads' heart is said to.
stonepath_the_leaning_stone.offer: There is a stone in the eastern rows that I did not chart four years ago, and I chart everything. It leans where no stone leaned. And I will say the next part quietly, once: I measured it in spring, and I measured it last week, and the second number is smaller. Stones do not shrink. Go to it. Read it. Come back and be my second measurement, so that when I am wrong I am wrong with a witness.
stonepath_the_leaning_stone.progress: The leaning stone stands in the eastern rows, off the charted paths. Read what marks it carries and mark its height for Vintila.
stonepath_the_leaning_stone.complete: Shorter. You confirm it. A line of it is simply... gone, since spring, the way a word goes off a page that's being un-written. There is one other stone in Draconia that does this, they say, at the crossroads' very heart, under a dead man's handprint. I have spent my life wanting to read these. For the first time I understand that wanting to is the whole trap, and I set it myself, and I sprang it slow.

objectives.label: the leaning stone in the eastern rows
objectives.text: Read and measure the leaning stone

stonepath_the_leaning_stone.note: A stone nobody set was shorter than in spring, a line un-written. Vintila understood at last that the wanting-to-read was the whole trap. He understood it too late to stop wanting. Understanding, here, is always too late by exactly one measurement.

stonepath_night_watch_fords.title: Night-Watch at the Four Fords
stonepath_night_watch_fords.summary: Coaches have arrived at dawn with their water-barrels tasting of copper, though the fords ran clean at dusk. Pavel wants the crossings watched through the dark hours -- and whatever comes down to the water dealt with.
stonepath_night_watch_fords.offer: My coaches leave with sweet water and arrive with copper in the barrel, every dawn this week, and the fords test clean at dusk. Something visits the crossings in the dark. Stand the watch at the north ford -- the copper's worst there -- and put down whatever comes to the water. I'd go myself, but a man my age at a ford at night is just a story the next coach tells.
stonepath_night_watch_fords.progress: The north ford waters coppery by dawn. Keep the night-watch there, and break whatever wolves come down to the crossing in the dark.
stonepath_night_watch_fords.complete: Copper by dawn again -- but fewer of them came, and you saw them come. Down to the water in a line, and drink, and the ford turns the color of our wells the moment they touch it. That's the sickness the whole border's got, and it's found the crossroads. It would. All roads lead here, and lately so does the sickness. Take your rest. Rest's the only clean thing I've left to give.

objectives.label: the north ford
objectives.text: Keep the night-watch at the north ford
Break what comes to the water in the dark

stonepath_night_watch_fords.note: The wolves came to the north ford in a line, drank, and the water went copper as they touched it. The sickness of the border wells had reached the crossroads. All roads lead here. Pavel had noticed that. He wished he had not.

stonepath_west_officer_grey_marches.title: Word for the Grey Marches
stonepath_west_officer_grey_marches.summary: Tudor holds the west road for Angel Wings, and the Grey Marches beyond have sent no forester's report in a fortnight. He wants the west overlook reached and the treeline read for smoke -- the first thread of the western arm, and the first proof the dying wood is being kept alight on purpose.
stonepath_west_officer_grey_marches.offer: The Queen's roads are my charge, and the westernmost of them's gone quiet -- no forester's word out of the Grey Marches in a fortnight, and foresters are chatty as rooks. Walk to the west overlook where the land drops toward the grey wood. Look for smoke. If there's a fire lit in a forest that's dying needle by needle, someone's keeping it lit, and I want to know it before the Marches teach me the hard way.
stonepath_west_officer_grey_marches.progress: The west overlook sits at the far west edge, where the road falls away toward the Grey Marches. Reach it and read the treeline for smoke.
stonepath_west_officer_grey_marches.complete: Smoke. Standing straight where there's wind to bend it, which means it's fed and tended, which means a cult keeps a fire in a wood that's asking permission to die. That's the west road's trouble and now it's mine to send word on. Take this dispatch to the Marches when you go -- and you will go; the road west wants walking and you've the walking look. Just know the west road doesn't get easier. It gets hungrier.

objectives.label: the west overlook toward the Grey Marches
objectives.text: Read the treeline for smoke from the west overlook

stonepath_west_officer_grey_marches.note: Smoke stood straight against the wind over the Grey Marches -- fed, tended, deliberate. A fire kept alight in a dying wood. Tudor sent his word west and warned the traveler the road only grows hungrier. He was not exaggerating. He never does.

stonepath_scholars_burning.title: What the Scholar Would Not Burn
stonepath_scholars_burning.summary: Zaharia the sweeper found Vintila's copied rubbings blowing loose in the yard and, remembering old Vasile's rule about such things, wants them gathered and burned unread -- before the scholar copies one more, or the wind carries one down a road that reads.
stonepath_scholars_burning.offer: The scholar drops his papers, and the wind takes them, and I chase them like a fool because I know what they've got scratched on them. Angular marks. Copies of the stones. Old Vasile down the Emberfall road burns such unread -- ash tells nobody anything, he says. Gather the loose ones from the yard for me and we'll burn them together, before one blows east to a city that would rather read than burn.
stonepath_scholars_burning.progress: Vintila's loose rubbings have scattered across the crossroads yard on the wind. Gather them to Zaharia for burning -- unread, mind, always unread.
stonepath_scholars_burning.complete: Face-down in the fire, every one, and neither of us looked. Good. The scholar will make more, God help him, a reasonable man can't help himself, that's the whole tragedy of reasonable men. But these won't reach a road that reads. It's a small burning. Small's the size that helps. Warm your hands -- honest warmth, this, from ash, not from the ground. There's a difference, and out here you learn it quick.

objectives.text: Gather the scattered rubbings, face-down
Burn the rubbings with Zaharia, unread

stonepath_scholars_burning.note: Five rubbings burned face-down, unread, and the ash told no one anything. Zaharia knew Vintila would copy more; a reasonable man can't help himself. He warmed his hands at the honest fire and named the difference between it and the ground.

stonepath_south_officer_bloodroad.title: The Officer Who Says Little
stonepath_south_officer_bloodroad.summary: A silent Varcolaci liaison keeps the south spur for Sangeroasa's forges, and Ursu brokers between them. He needs a war-caravan's leavings scouted at the south ford before the toll-fort up the Bloodroad learns something the crossroads would rather sell first.
stonepath_south_officer_bloodroad.offer: The south road is not mine, but the man it belongs to speaks even less than I do, so I speak for both of us, badly. A forge-caravan camped at the south ford and moved on fast -- too fast, leaving too much. What a caravan leaves behind is worth more than what it carries, if you reach it before the toll-fort does. Scout the south ford. Note what they abandoned. Sell it to me, not to Krunn's sergeants. I pay in favors; they pay in questions.
stonepath_south_officer_bloodroad.progress: A war-caravan's camp lies abandoned at the south ford on the Bloodroad spur. Scout what they left behind before the toll-fort's men arrive.
stonepath_south_officer_bloodroad.complete: Ledgers. They left ledgers, half-burned, and a man doesn't burn a ledger for the numbers in it -- he burns it for a name. I'll not tell you which name; that's the favor working. The south road runs to the forges and the forges run on debt, and now you've seen the first page of it. Go south when you're strong enough, and go knowing the Bloodroad has already got a line for you. Everyone gets a line eventually.

objectives.label: the abandoned camp at the south ford
objectives.text: Scout the caravan's leavings at the south ford

stonepath_south_officer_bloodroad.note: A forge-caravan burned its ledgers at the south ford -- for a name, not the numbers. Ursu bought the ashes with a favor and kept the name. The Bloodroad already had a line waiting, he said. Everyone gets a line. He said that part like weather.

stonepath_hornet_apiary_mercy.title: The Bee-Keeper's Argument
stonepath_hornet_apiary_mercy.summary: Aurel's kept hives make the road-kitchen's honey, but the wild hornets are raiding them out. He wants the raiders driven off the good hives -- and, being Aurel, insists the tame bees be left an untouched skep so the crossroads keeps something sweet.
stonepath_hornet_apiary_mercy.offer: My tame bees make the only honey between four cities, and the wild hornets are robbing them blind and mean. Drive the raiders off -- clear the swarms nearest my skeps -- but mind you leave the tame hive by the yard-wall be. A crossroads with no sweetness left in it is just a place four roads happen to touch, and I'll not let it come to that on my watch. Not while I've a jar to hand a traveler.
stonepath_hornet_apiary_mercy.progress: The wild hornet swarms are raiding Aurel's hives by the yard-wall. Clear four raiding swarms, and leave the tame skep untouched.
stonepath_hornet_apiary_mercy.complete: The tame hive's humming and the raiders are seen off, and there'll be honey for the coach-bread yet. You'd laugh how much it matters to me -- a jar of honey against all the strangeness out here. But that's the point, isn't it. The strangeness is free and everywhere. The sweetness you have to fight for. So I fight for it. Here's a jar. Don't argue.

objectives.text: Drive the raiders off Aurel's hives

stonepath_hornet_apiary_mercy.note: Aurel saved his tame hive and the crossroads kept its honey. The strangeness is free and everywhere, he said; the sweetness you fight for. He fights for it, jar by jar, on a crossroads the world is slowly reading. It is not nothing. It is just small.

stonepath_merchants_missing_coach.title: The Coach That Went North Full
stonepath_merchants_missing_coach.summary: A coach of pilgrims took the north road a week ago and Sava, who sold them their provisions, cannot stop noticing the road brought back the coach empty, cleaned, and parked exactly where it started.
