He wants the north spur searched as far as a sane man dares.
stonepath_merchants_missing_coach.offer: I sold six pilgrims a week of bread and a north-road seat, and the coach came back on its own -- empty, swept clean, standing in my yard this morning like it never left. No horses. No pilgrims. No blood, no sign, nothing to grieve over, which is the worst kind of nothing. Walk the north spur for me, far as your nerve holds. Find me a boot-print. Find me anything a man can put in a report that isn't just 'they went north.'
stonepath_merchants_missing_coach.progress: The north spur climbs toward the Listening Steppe. Search it as far as you dare for sign of Sava's lost pilgrims.
stonepath_merchants_missing_coach.complete: Footprints, you say -- six sets, walking north, evenly spaced, none of them scuffed or hurried or turned back. Six people walked north in step like they were marching to a count. That's what the north road does. It doesn't take you. It invites you, and you accept, and your feet learn a rhythm they weren't taught. I'll write 'they went north.' It's the only true thing, and the truest things are the ones you can't do anything with.

objectives.label: the north spur toward the Listening Steppe
objectives.text: Search the north spur for the lost pilgrims

stonepath_merchants_missing_coach.note: Six pilgrims walked north in step, evenly spaced, none turning back, and the empty coach came home swept clean. Sava wrote 'they went north.' It was the only true thing, and true things at the crossroads are the ones no one can act on.

stonepath_blacksmith_bent_nails.title: The Nails That Bend One Way
stonepath_blacksmith_bent_nails.summary: Radomir's coach-nails have started coming out of the fire already bent -- all bent the same way, all pointing the same way when he sets them down. He wants a fresh boar-hide bellows to reheat the forge hot enough to work honest iron, and he wants a witness to the nails.
stonepath_blacksmith_bent_nails.offer: Watch this. I hammer a nail straight, set it down, and by the time I've hammered the next it's leaned -- and they all lean the same way, north-and-a-bit, like filings to a lodestone that isn't there. My bellows has gone soft and the fire won't come hot enough to beat the lean out. Bring me boar-hide for a new bellows -- four beasts' worth -- and stand here and tell me you see it too. The leaning. Tell me I'm not the only one it's happening to.
stonepath_blacksmith_bent_nails.progress: Radomir needs boar-hide for a new bellows -- four thicket boars' worth -- to fire the forge hot enough to work the leaning out of his iron.
stonepath_blacksmith_bent_nails.complete: New bellows, good fire, and still -- look -- they lean. North-and-a-bit, every one, the moment my hand's off them. Hot iron shouldn't care which way's north. Nothing should care which way's north the way this crossroads has started to. You saw it. That's all I wanted -- to not be the only one. A shared madness is only half a madness. That's the closest thing to comfort I've got, so I'll take it.

objectives.text: Hunt boar for a new forge-bellows
Witness the leaning nails at Radomir's forge

stonepath_blacksmith_bent_nails.note: Radomir's nails leaned north-and-a-bit the moment his hand left them, hot iron caring which way was north. He wanted only not to be the only one who saw. A shared madness is half a madness, he said, and called it comfort, because it was the nearest thing.

stonepath_scholar_last_measurement.title: The Last Honest Measurement
stonepath_scholar_last_measurement.summary: Vintila has understood his stones at last, and it has aged him a decade in a season. Before he burns four years of work himself, he asks one thing: reach the shortening stone at the crossroads' heart, confirm its marks have faded again since spring -- and do not, whatever the pull, put a hand to it.
stonepath_scholar_last_measurement.offer: I called it a calendar because a calendar is a thing a man can keep. It is not a calendar. It is a message, and it is being sent, and every reader who bends to it is another hand on the rope. I mean to burn my work tonight -- yes, burn it, don't look so surprised, Vasile was right for sixty years and I was proud for four. One thing first. Go to the heart-stone, the one under the dead man's print. Confirm it's shorter than spring. And do not touch it. I need you to come back. I have got very few people left who come back.
stonepath_scholar_last_measurement.progress: The shortening stone stands at the crossroads' heart, under a dead man's handprint. Confirm its marks have faded since spring -- and keep your hand in your coat.
stonepath_scholar_last_measurement.complete: Shorter. Of course. It's always shorter. Thank you for coming back with your hand still your own -- you'd be amazed how the print fits, how reasonable it feels to check. That's the whole art of the thing: it makes the trap feel like your own good idea. I'm burning the papers now. I'll be a fool who wasted four years, and I'll be alive, and out here that's the only scholarship that pays. Take my measuring-cord. I've measured my last honest thing.

objectives.label: the shortening stone at the crossroads' heart
objectives.text: Confirm the shortening stone's marks -- do not touch it
Return to Vintila with your hand your own

stonepath_scholar_last_measurement.note: The heart-stone was shorter than spring, as it always is. Vintila burned four years of work and kept his life, and called that the only scholarship that pays. The print fit a living hand too well. That, he understood at last, was the whole art: the trap that feels like your own good idea.

stonepath_full_pot_arrangement.title: The Coach That Was Always Going to Stop
stonepath_full_pot_arrangement.summary: A stranded coach party was saved when the pack that stalked them scattered at exactly the right moment -- a clean rescue Pavel can't stop turning over, because the wolves broke the instant the party reached his warm yard, as if the whole ordeal had been arranged to end there. He wants the stalking-ground checked.
stonepath_full_pot_arrangement.offer: A coach limped in last night, half-mad with fear -- wolves had walked them the length of the west spur, close as shadows, and then broke off the moment they hit my lantern-light. Every soul saved. A clean rescue, and I can't sleep for it. Wolves that patient don't lose their nerve at a lantern. It's as if they were only ever herding those folk here -- to me, to my warm yard, to be saved. Go back up the spur. Tell me I'm an old fool seeing shapes.
stonepath_full_pot_arrangement.progress: The west spur is where the coach was stalked and then, too neatly, spared. Cull what wolves remain and read the ground for how the ordeal was arranged.
stonepath_full_pot_arrangement.complete: Herded. You saw the tracks -- the pack never once closed, never meant to. They walked those people to my door and let them arrive grateful. A rescue nobody needed to earn, arranged so the saved would remember being saved, here, warm, at the crossroads. I don't know what's served by a coach of grateful travelers. But something is, and it went to trouble, and troubled things want paying back. Take your rest. I'll be counting seats till dawn.

objectives.text: Cull the herding-pack on the west spur

objectives.label: the stalking-ground on the west spur
objectives.text: Read how the rescue was arranged

stonepath_full_pot_arrangement.note: The pack had herded the coach to Pavel's warm door and let it arrive grateful -- a rescue nobody earned, arranged so the saved would remember being saved, here. Something was served by grateful travelers. It went to trouble. Troubled things want paying back.

stonepath_the_kept_name.title: A Name to Keep
stonepath_the_kept_name.summary: One of Sava's lost north-bound pilgrims left a child behind at the crossroads, and Aurel has quietly taken the boy in. He asks the traveler to carve the mother's name into the yard's memory-post while it's still remembered -- because the north road takes the memory too, in time.
stonepath_the_kept_name.offer: The pilgrim coach that went north and didn't come back -- one of them left a boy. Sava won't say it, so I will: she's not coming back, and the north road takes more than bodies. It takes the remembering. Folk here are already forgetting her face, and it's been a week. There's a post in the yard where we cut the names of the gone. Cut hers deep, while the boy still knows it, so that when the road takes his remembering too, the post keeps it for him.
stonepath_the_kept_name.progress: The memory-post stands in the crossroads yard. Cut the lost pilgrim's name into it deep, while she is still remembered.
stonepath_the_kept_name.complete: Deep and clean. Good. Now when the boy grows and the road's worn her out of his head -- and it will, that's what it does, it's patient and it's thorough -- there'll be a name in oak he can put his hand to and know she was real, and his, and loved. It's a small thing against the north road. But the north road only wins the big fights. The small ones we can still take, one carved name at a time.

objectives.label: the memory-post in the crossroads yard
objectives.text: Carve the lost pilgrim's name deep

stonepath_the_kept_name.note: A pilgrim's name was cut deep in the memory-post, kept in oak for a boy the north road would one day empty of her. The road wins the big fights; the small ones -- a carved name, a face held one more year -- are still ours. Aurel keeps taking them. One at a time.

stonepath_scholars_apology.title: The Argument Vasile Won
stonepath_scholars_apology.summary: His life's work burned, Vintila has one indulgence left: he wants to send old Gravekeeper Vasile of Raven Hollow -- who burned rubbings unread for sixty years while Vintila copied them -- a formal admission of error, carried by hand, phrased to sting as little as pride allows.
stonepath_scholars_apology.offer: I have burned four years of work and gained a fact I'd have paid four more to un-know. Which leaves me one scholarly duty: a man down the Emberfall road, a gravekeeper, spent sixty years burning what I spent four copying, and he was right, and I was published. I have written him an admission. It is honest and it is worded to cost me exactly as much as I can bear, which is not much, I find. Carry it. Read it if you like -- it's only a man being wrong out loud, the rarest inscription there is.
stonepath_scholars_apology.progress: Vintila's admission of error is written and waiting. Carry it -- and the humility in it -- toward the Emberfall road, and mark the crossroads' eastern gate on your way as bound for Raven Hollow.
stonepath_scholars_apology.complete: You'll carry it. Good. Tell the old gravekeeper -- no, tell him nothing, the letter says it, and a messenger who improves the message is a scholar in the making, and we've established how that ends. Just deliver it. It's the first honest thing I've published. No stone got shorter for it, no ground warmed, nobody listened who shouldn't. It only cost me my pride, and pride was the one thing out here that was always going to be free to lose.

objectives.label: the eastern gate, road to Raven Hollow
objectives.text: Bear Vintila's admission toward the Emberfall road

stonepath_scholars_apology.note: Vintila published his first honest thing: a man being wrong out loud. No stone shortened for it, no ground warmed, no one listened who shouldn't. It cost only his pride -- the one thing the crossroads was always going to take for free.

stonepath_daily_coach_escort.title: The Crossroads Escort
stonepath_daily_coach_escort.summary: There is always a coach leaving, always a spur that isn't safe today, always wolves testing the fords. Pavel keeps a standing bounty for whoever walks the day's coach clear of the pack.
stonepath_daily_coach_escort.offer: Coach leaves at noon and the wolves have opinions about that. Walk it clear -- thin the pack on whichever spur it's taking -- and there's coin and a warm seat back. I keep this offer standing because the trouble keeps standing. If the day ever comes I don't need to post it, I'll frame it. That day's not today. Today the west spur's thick with them.
stonepath_daily_coach_escort.progress: The day's coach wants escorting. Thin the wolf-pack on the departure spur before noon.
stonepath_daily_coach_escort.complete: Clear enough for a coach, which is all clear ever means out here. Same coin, same seat, same offer tomorrow -- the wolves are nothing if not reliable, and reliability's gone rare enough that I'll almost miss them. Almost. Off you go. There's always another noon.

objectives.text: Thin the pack on the coach's departure spur

stonepath_daily_coach_escort.note: Another coach walked clear, another noon survived. The wolves are reliable, Pavel says, and reliability has gone rare enough that he'll almost miss them. Almost. There is always another noon.

stonepath_daily_wolf_line_survey.title: The Wolf-Line Survey
stonepath_daily_wolf_line_survey.summary: Tudor keeps a running count of how the packs pace the spurs -- the spacing, the direction, whether it's gotten worse. He needs the day's line broken and its measure noted, over and over, because the day it stops changing is the day to worry.
stonepath_daily_wolf_line_survey.offer: Every day the packs walk their line a little tighter, a little more even. I keep the count. Not because anyone reads it -- I've established nobody up the road does -- but because a record's a record, and one day the number stops climbing, and when it does I want to know the exact hour. Break today's line and mark its spacing for me. Twelve paces yesterday. Let's hope it's not eleven.
stonepath_daily_wolf_line_survey.progress: The day's wolf-line wants breaking and its spacing noted. Cull the pacing pack and bring Tudor the measure.
stonepath_daily_wolf_line_survey.complete: Twelve again. Not eleven, thank whatever's left to thank. Steady's the best news the crossroads offers -- not good, just not worse. I'll add it to the record no one reads. Come back tomorrow. The line always reforms by dawn. That's its own kind of measure, isn't it. Persistence. It has more of it than I do, most mornings.

objectives.text: Break the day's wolf-line and note its spacing

stonepath_daily_wolf_line_survey.note: Twelve paces again, not eleven. Steady is the best news the crossroads gives -- not good, just not worse. Tudor adds it to a record no one reads, against the day the number stops climbing. The line reforms by dawn. Its persistence outlasts his.

stonepath_thicket_bounty.title: Thicket Bounty
stonepath_thicket_bounty.summary: The thicket boars and hornets both plague the carters, and Sava -- ever the merchant -- pays a rolling bounty on whichever is worst today, because a road a carter fears is a road that stops paying tolls.
stonepath_thicket_bounty.offer: Simple trade, friend. The thickets breed faster than I can complain about them, and a carter who fears the brush is a carter who takes a longer road and pays me nothing. So: clear today's worst -- boar in the south brush, the way they've been -- and I'll pay a fair bounty and mean it. Repeat business is the only honest kind, and out here I'll take my honesty where the market offers it.
stonepath_thicket_bounty.progress: The thicket boars are thick in the south brush today. Cull them for Sava's rolling bounty.
stonepath_thicket_bounty.complete: Cleared, counted, paid -- see how easy honest trade can be when nothing's whispering under it? That's why I like this bounty. It's just boar and coin, no stones, no leaning, no roads that eat coaches. A man could almost forget where he is. Almost. Come by tomorrow; there's always more brush, and I've always more coin than sense about paying for it.

objectives.text: Clear the day's worst of the thicket boar

stonepath_thicket_bounty.note: Boar and coin, no stones under it -- Sava's favorite trade, the one that lets a man almost forget where he stands. Almost. There is always more brush tomorrow, and he has always more coin than sense about it. It is the closest thing here to peace.

copper_wells_the_one_clean_well.title: The One Clean Well
copper_wells_the_one_clean_well.summary: Every well on the moor has gone copper but one. Barbu keeps the last clean water under lock, and rations it by the cup. He wants a tally kept -- go read the poisoned wells, and count what the moor has learned.
copper_wells_the_one_clean_well.offer: Herdsmen sank these wells in a kinder age and cut their names in the stone. Now the water's turned in every one of them -- coppery, metal on the tongue -- save the deep well behind the inn, and I ration that by the cup. My grandmother had a saying: when the well goes copper, count your candles. I've candles enough. It's the wells I want counted. Walk the moor. Read them for me. Tell me how far it's come.
copper_wells_the_one_clean_well.progress: The old wells stand scattered across the moor east of the inn, each with a name cut in the stone. Read three, and mark what the water tastes of now.
copper_wells_the_one_clean_well.complete: Three read, three copper. And the dust round every wellstone lying in those little parallel lines, you said. My grandmother had that saying, and she never once laughed when she said it. Drink at ours while it lasts. Then help me understand why ours lasts.

objectives.label: the Herdsman's Well
objectives.text: Read the Herdsman's Well

objectives.label: the Twinned Well
objectives.text: Read the Twinned Well

objectives.label: the Farthest Well
objectives.text: Read the Farthest Well

copper_wells_the_one_clean_well.note: Every well but one runs copper now, and the dust round each lies in fine parallel lines pointing the one way. Barbu rations the clean well by the cup. He did not say what he will do when the cup runs out.

copper_wells_reading_the_land.title: Reading the Land
copper_wells_reading_the_land.summary: A dead alchemist left a battered primer behind -- Hessik's method, the trick of reading the moor by the colors a wash turns in wrong ground. Florea has the reagents but not the nerve. Learn the colors, and read the ground where the wells sit warm.
copper_wells_reading_the_land.offer: Fellow came through last spring selling washes and vials -- said the world's legible if you're willing to be poisoned learning to read it. He didn't come back through. Left me his primer and a satchel of reagent. I've had it under the counter a year, afraid to open it. You've a steadier hand. Take Hessik's method, take the blue wash, and go read the warm ground for me. Blue for the dead's work, they say. Orange for something worse.
copper_wells_reading_the_land.progress: Carry the blue reading-wash to the warm ground behind the wells and read what colors the moor gives back. Mind the dose -- every reading is a small poisoning.
copper_wells_reading_the_land.complete: Blue where you'd expect the dead, you said -- and then that hairline of shifting orange under the deepest well, the color no reagent's supposed to find this far south. My tongue would taste metal for a week after a reading like that. Keep the primer. I've decided I'd rather not learn to read after all.

objectives.text: Take Hessik's method from Florea

objectives.label: the warm ground behind the wells
objectives.text: Read the warm ground with the wash

copper_wells_reading_the_land.note: Under Hessik's method the moor reads blue for the dead's slow work -- and one hairline of shifting orange beneath the deepest well, a signal writing itself into the water. Florea gave the primer away. Some things are safer unread.

copper_wells_bog_iron_harvest.title: Iron Out of the Wet
copper_wells_bog_iron_harvest.summary: The ore road hasn't cooperated in a month, but the moor grows its own iron in the bog if a body's willing to wade for it. Dorin needs a good pile of bog-iron to keep the forge honest.
copper_wells_bog_iron_harvest.offer: Ore comes up the road when the road feels like a road, and it hasn't felt like one since the thaw. No matter -- the wet grows its own iron out here, rusty little nodules down in the peat, and my knees don't like the wading anymore. Bring me a good sack of bog-iron and I'll have you a nail worth more than any wish-coin. Six lumps of it, out of the sour ground. Mind your footing.
copper_wells_bog_iron_harvest.progress: Bog-iron nodules sit in the peat-hollows across the moor, rustiest where the ground runs warm. Gather six good lumps for Dorin's forge.
copper_wells_bog_iron_harvest.complete: Now that's honest iron -- sour-smelling, but it'll temper. You waded for that, I can tell; you've got the moor to the knee. Here, take this striker. Struck from bog-iron myself, so it knows the ground it came from. A well-hammered nail beats a wish every time out here.

objectives.text: Gather bog-iron from the peat

copper_wells_bog_iron_harvest.note: Dorin got his iron out of the wet, sour and rust-red, and kept the forge burning another season. He never asks why the road stopped bringing ore. Out here, not asking is its own kind of wisdom.

copper_wells_leeches_in_the_water.title: What the Water Grew
copper_wells_leeches_in_the_water.summary: The coppered wells have grown things -- pale leeches thick as thumbs, clinging to the shafts, that come out when a bucket goes down. Gavril lost a herdboy to a wellful of them. Thin the leeches before another child hauls up a rope of them.
copper_wells_leeches_in_the_water.offer: You know what a copper well grows, once it's turned? Leeches. Pale as tallow, thick as your thumb, hanging off the shaft where the water used to be sweet. A herdboy dropped his bucket for them last week -- came up with his arm roped in the things and hasn't spoken since. I can't watch every well. Go down the ladders where the copper's worst and burn them out. Before it's another child's arm.
copper_wells_leeches_in_the_water.progress: The copper-well leeches cling thickest in the shafts where the water has fully turned. Clear the moor's poisoned wells of them.
copper_wells_leeches_in_the_water.complete: Eight of them, and the herdboy still won't speak. His mother says he keeps tilting his head like he's listening down a well. I told her it's shock. I didn't tell her the leeches only grew after the water turned -- and the water only turned after the ground went warm. Take the poultice. Give it to the boy. It won't help. But she'll see you tried.

objectives.text: Clear leeches from the poisoned wells

copper_wells_leeches_in_the_water.note: The leeches came after the water turned, and the water turned after the ground went warm -- the moor learning its lesson in order. The herdboy tilts his head toward the wells now. His mother calls it shock.

copper_wells_the_widow_in_the_reeds.title: The Widow in the Reeds
copper_wells_the_widow_in_the_reeds.summary: Something four-clawed and patient dens in the deep reeds where the moor pulls at the boot. It has taken two herdsmen by the throat and left them where they fell, unrobbed. Horia will send you after it -- once.
copper_wells_the_widow_in_the_reeds.offer: There's a thing out in the deep reeds, past where the ground stops holding your weight. Bog-widow, the old folk call it -- four claws, and it goes for the throat and nothing else. Took Petru the herdsman a fortnight back. Took his brother when he went looking. Left the both of them lying, purses full, boots on. It didn't want their coin. I've buried enough. Go end it, and mind -- it dens in the wet where a running man drowns.
copper_wells_the_widow_in_the_reeds.progress: The bog-widow dens in the deep reeds south of the wells, where the moor turns to open water. Hunt it through the reeds -- and keep to firm ground.
copper_wells_the_widow_in_the_reeds.complete: Dead, then. Four claws on it, just like the old folk said, and a mouth built for the throat and nothing else. They say it comes up where the ground's read too much -- where some fool copied the wrong marks. I don't know about that. I know Petru and his brother are in the yard and it's quieter tonight. Take the skinner. You earned a sharp edge.

objectives.text: End the bog-widow in the deep reeds

copper_wells_the_widow_in_the_reeds.note: The bog-widow is dead, four-clawed and throat-hungry as the old folk warned. They say such things rise where the ground has been read too much. Horia buried the brothers side by side and did not repeat the saying.

copper_wells_names_in_the_stone.title: Names in the Stone
copper_wells_names_in_the_stone.summary: The herdsmen who sank the wells cut their names in the wellstones, and the moss has been taking those names, letter by letter, for six hundred years. Lazar wants them copied down before the moss finishes -- and before anyone reads them the wrong way.
copper_wells_names_in_the_stone.offer: Every wellstone out there carries a name -- the herdsman who sank it, cut deep in a kinder age. The moss has been eating those names since before the kingdoms had theirs, slow and without malice, the way moss takes everything. I'd have them copied into my book before they're gone. Walk to the three eldest wells and read me their names off the stone. Read the names, mind -- only the names. Not the newer marks cut beneath them.
copper_wells_names_in_the_stone.progress: The three eldest wellstones stand at the moor's edges, their carved names half-eaten by moss. Read each name for Lazar's book -- and leave the newer marks alone.
copper_wells_names_in_the_stone.complete: Vintu. Cheresh. And the third stone's name gone entirely to the moss, you said -- but a newer mark cut fresh beneath the empty place, angular, not our alphabet. You didn't copy it. Good. You looked at your boots and read me the old names and left the new one to the moss. That's the only wisdom this moor rewards. The names go in the book. The other thing goes nowhere.

objectives.label: Vintu's Well
objectives.text: Read the name on Vintu's Well

objectives.label: Cheresh's Well
objectives.text: Read the name on Cheresh's Well

objectives.label: the Nameless Well
objectives.text: Read the eldest, nameless well

copper_wells_names_in_the_stone.note: Two herdsmen's names went into Lazar's book; the third had gone wholly to the moss, and a newer, angular mark cut fresh in the empty place. He copied the old names. The new one he left where it lay, unread. Some marks are shorter for being read.

copper_wells_the_open_farmsteads.title: The Open Farmsteads
copper_wells_the_open_farmsteads.summary: Three farmsteads within sight of the warm ground stand open and unlooted -- doors wide, tables set, nobody home. Matei wants someone to walk them and bring back word. Not of who took the folk. Of why nobody took the silver.
copper_wells_the_open_farmsteads.offer: My cousin's holding sits empty out past the second well. Door wide open, been that way a month. And here's the thing keeps me up -- the silver's still on the table. Bowls, a good candlestick, her mother's ring on the sill. Open and unlooted, all three holdings within sight of the warm patch. That's worse than burned. A burned house you can understand. Walk them for me. Tell me the tables are still set. Tell me I'm imagining it.
copper_wells_the_open_farmsteads.progress: Three abandoned holdings stand open near the warm ground -- doors wide, nothing taken. Walk each and see what was left behind.
copper_wells_the_open_farmsteads.complete: Tables set, silver where it sat, and the dust indoors lying in those same thin lines pointing east, you said. Toward the Chamber, if a body walked far enough. The folk didn't flee and they weren't robbed. They just... stopped being at home. Set a place for them anyway, my cousin's neighbors do. Every night. In case somebody comes back to eat it.

objectives.label: the Cousin's Holding
objectives.text: Walk the cousin's open holding

objectives.label: the Reeve's Croft
objectives.text: Walk the reeve's open croft

objectives.label: the Shepherd's House
objectives.text: Walk the shepherd's open house

copper_wells_the_open_farmsteads.note: Three holdings stand open and unlooted, tables still set, the indoor dust drawn in fine lines pointing east toward the Chamber. The folk did not flee and were not robbed. Their neighbors set a place for them each night, in case.

copper_wells_pilgrims_who_stopped.title: The Pilgrims Who Stopped Walking
copper_wells_pilgrims_who_stopped.summary: The old pilgrim paths cross the moor, and lately the pilgrims on them have stopped going anywhere in particular. They stand in the wells' shadow, heads tilted, listening. Oprea's daughter is among them. Go to her -- and do not understand what she is hearing.
copper_wells_pilgrims_who_stopped.offer: The pilgrim road's crossed this moor since before the wells were sunk, and the faithful always kept walking, north to the shrines, footsore and singing. Now they stop. Out by the warm ground they stand and tilt their heads and stop finishing their sentences. My girl went to bring the walkers water. She's out there with them now, listening. Go to her. Speak her name. And whatever she's hearing -- for the love of every saint, don't you try to make it out.
copper_wells_pilgrims_who_stopped.progress: The stilled pilgrims stand in the shadow of the warm wells, Oprea's daughter among them. Reach her -- and hold to not understanding.
copper_wells_pilgrims_who_stopped.complete: You spoke her name and she turned her head, you said. Slow. Like a door on a wet hinge. And then she went back to listening. You didn't stay to hear what. Good. The man who understands them dies of it -- clean fingernails, no wound, a face like he finally got the joke. You came back not knowing. That's the whole of what I asked. That's the whole of what keeps you alive out here. Take the charm. It's kept me incurious forty years.

objectives.label: the stilled pilgrims
objectives.text: Reach Oprea's daughter among the pilgrims
Bring word back to Oprea

copper_wells_pilgrims_who_stopped.note: The pilgrims stand in the wells' shadow, heads tilted, no longer walking anywhere. Oprea's daughter turned to her name and turned away again. He asked only that you come back not understanding what she hears. That is the whole art of surviving this moor.

copper_wells_the_flat_grey_bread.title: Flat and Grey
copper_wells_the_flat_grey_bread.summary: The inn's bread won't rise. Barbu has blamed the flour, then the water, then stopped blaming. He wants a live yeast culture carried out from a hearth beyond the warm ground -- somewhere the dough still remembers how to lift.
copper_wells_the_flat_grey_bread.offer: You'll have noticed the bread. Flat, grey, sits in your stomach like a grievance. I blamed the flour. Then the water. Then I stopped blaming, because there's no flour on earth that dies in the bowl overnight. The yeast won't take, not near the warm ground. There's a shepherd's hearth out west, past where the moor goes cold again -- his dough still rises. Fetch me a live culture from a place that hasn't stopped. I'd like to bake one honest loaf before I forget what one tastes like.
copper_wells_the_flat_grey_bread.progress: A live culture waits at the shepherd's cold hearth, west past the warm ground where dough still rises. Carry it back to the inn before it dies on the moor.
copper_wells_the_flat_grey_bread.complete: It rose. Saints, it rose -- you should've heard the folk in the taproom go quiet when the smell hit. One good loaf, tall and brown and honest, and half the room near wept over it. It'll go flat again by the week's end, near the warm ground. But we had the one. Everything good is temporary. That's what makes it good. Sit. Eat while it's warm the right way.

objectives.text: Carry a live culture from the cold hearth
Bring the culture back to Barbu

copper_wells_the_flat_grey_bread.note: For one evening the inn's bread rose tall and brown, and the taproom went quiet at the smell of it. It will go flat again by week's end near the warm ground. Barbu baked the one good loaf. He knows how the moor takes such things back.

copper_wells_hex_signs_for_the_lintels.title: Signs for the Lintels
copper_wells_hex_signs_for_the_lintels.summary: The moor-folk paint hearth-hex signs over their doors to keep the listening out -- folk nonsense, mostly, but it lets them sleep. Matei's run short of the good ochre. Gather what he needs so the whole hamlet can mark its lintels before the dark.
copper_wells_hex_signs_for_the_lintels.offer: Old custom out here -- you paint a hex-sign over the door, a wheel and three dots, keeps the listening from crossing your threshold. Does it work? My grandmother swore by it, and she died in her bed with her wits, so who's to say. Point is it lets the little ones sleep. I've run dry of the red ochre and marsh-gum to mix it. Gather me the makings and I'll paint every lintel in the hamlet before nightfall. Can't hurt. Might help. Mostly it helps.
copper_wells_hex_signs_for_the_lintels.progress: Red ochre and marsh-gum for the hex-paint lie along the peat cuttings and the ochre bank. Gather enough for Matei to mark the hamlet's doors.
copper_wells_hex_signs_for_the_lintels.complete: That'll do the whole row and the byres besides. Come and watch -- there's a knack to the wheel, my grandmother's knack. Every door marked before the sun's down, and every child under a marked lintel sleeps easier for it. Does it stop the listening? I don't know, friend. But nobody in a hexed house has tilted their head yet. Take this sign. I painted it myself. Put it over wherever you rest.

objectives.text: Gather ochre and marsh-gum for the hex-paint

copper_wells_hex_signs_for_the_lintels.note: Matei painted a hex-sign over every lintel in the hamlet before dark, a wheel and three dots in red ochre. Whether it keeps the listening out, no one can say. But no one in a hexed house has yet tilted their head. That is enough to sleep on.

copper_wells_the_lead_the_peasant_poured.title: The Lead the Peasant Poured
copper_wells_the_lead_the_peasant_poured.summary: A herdsman tried to copy the marks off a wellstone into a cheap lead box, to keep them safe, to sell them, no one knows. The box is empty now and he is one of the listeners. Lazar wants the box brought in and read by the wash -- carefully -- to learn what a copied inscription does.
copper_wells_the_lead_the_peasant_poured.offer: There's a lead box in the reeve's burned outbuilding, cheaply cast, and it's empty -- but it wasn't always. A herdsman named Grozea poured that box himself and copied a wellstone's new marks inside it. To keep them safe, he told his wife. To sell them north, some say. Doesn't matter which. He's out with the listeners now, tilting his head. The box seeded something. I want it read with the orange wash and then I want it sunk in the deep water where nobody copies it again. Do it carefully. Curiosity is how this moor kills.
copper_wells_the_lead_the_peasant_poured.progress: The lead box lies in the reeve's burned outbuilding. Read it with the wash to see what a copied inscription grew -- then it goes to the deep water.
copper_wells_the_lead_the_peasant_poured.complete: Shifting orange, all round the empty box -- the signal writing itself into the lead the way it wrote itself into Grozea. He copied the marks to keep them, and the copying made a new mouth for the thing to speak from. That's the whole horror of it: reading is how it spreads, and Grozea read it into being. We sink the box. We don't copy the box. And we don't say his name near the warm ground -- names carry, out here. Take the primer's counteragent. You'll want it, next time you're tempted to read.

objectives.label: the reeve's burned outbuilding
objectives.text: Recover the lead box and read it with the wash

copper_wells_the_lead_the_peasant_poured.note: Grozea copied a wellstone's marks into a cheap lead box, to keep them or to sell them; the copying made a new mouth for the signal, and now he stands with the listeners. The box read shifting orange. Lazar sank it in the deep water. Reading is how the moor spreads. He asked you not to say the herdsman's name aloud again.

copper_wells_salt_the_thresholds.title: Salt the Thresholds
copper_wells_salt_the_thresholds.summary: Every morning the moor-folk salt their thresholds fresh -- a line of it across the door-stone to keep the night's listening from creeping in. Oprea's too stiff to do the far cottages. Carry the pouch and lay the lines while the salt's still holding.
copper_wells_salt_the_thresholds.offer: Do me the daily kindness, will you. There's a pouch of salt by the door -- we lay a fresh line across every threshold each morning, keeps out what walks in the night. Folk custom. My mother's mother did it. My knees won't take me to the far cottages anymore, the widow's and the byre-keeper's, and they can't stoop to it themselves. Lay the lines for them. Fresh salt each day -- yesterday's has always gone grey by dawn, and nobody likes to ask why.
copper_wells_salt_the_thresholds.progress: Fresh salt lines want laying at the far cottages each morning. Carry Oprea's pouch and mark their thresholds while the day's salt still holds.
copper_wells_salt_the_thresholds.complete: Bless you. They'll rest easier for a clean line at the door. You saw how the old salt had gone -- grey, and drawn out in little parallel streaks by morning, like something combed it? We don't talk about that. We just lay it fresh. Every day. That's the whole trick of living out here: do the small warm thing again, and don't look too hard at why it needs doing.

objectives.label: the widow's cottage
objectives.text: Salt the widow's threshold

objectives.label: the byre-keeper's door
objectives.text: Salt the byre-keeper's door

copper_wells_salt_the_thresholds.note: The thresholds are salted fresh again, the widow's and the byre-keeper's both. By dawn the old salt lies grey and combed into parallel streaks, and no one asks why. They just lay it fresh each day. The small warm thing, done again.

copper_wells_the_deepest_well.title: The Bottom of the Deepest Well
copper_wells_the_deepest_well.summary: The clean well behind the inn is starting to taste of copper at the bottom of the cup. Before the last water turns, Barbu wants the deepest well sounded -- descended, read, and understood as far as it is safe to understand. It is not far.
copper_wells_the_deepest_well.offer: Our well's turning. Sweet at the lip, copper at the bottom of the cup, and getting worse by the day -- the last clean water on the moor, going the way of all the rest. I've candles enough, my grandmother would say, but the counting's near done. Before it turns entire, I want the deepest well sounded. Go down the shaft. Read what's at the bottom with the wash. And then come up -- come up while you still want to. That's the whole of the asking. Come up.
copper_wells_the_deepest_well.progress: The deepest well stands behind the inn, its water turning copper from the bottom. Descend, read the shaft with the wash, and climb out before understanding takes hold.
copper_wells_the_deepest_well.complete: Green in the depths, you said -- thermal, the primer's color for a stone waking under the water, warming it from below. And that hairline of orange threading up through the green. You read it and you climbed. You climbed out. That's more than Grozea managed, more than my own girl. The well will turn by month's end and we'll drink copper like everyone else and count our candles down. But you climbed out knowing, and you're standing here. Sit. There's clean water left for one more cup. It's yours.

objectives.label: the bottom of the deepest well
objectives.text: Descend and read the deepest well

copper_wells_the_deepest_well.note: The deepest well read green in its depths -- a stone waking under the water, warming it from below -- with orange threading up through the green. You climbed out before understanding took you. Barbu poured the last clean cup for you. The well will turn by month's end, and the moor will count its candles down like everyone else.

copper_wells_the_reagent_hunt.title: Every Reagent Costs a Ruined Place
copper_wells_the_reagent_hunt.summary: Hessik's alchemy runs on grave-lichen scraped from threaded ground -- toxic to gather, worse to use, and only found where the moor has already gone wrong. Florea will trade fair for a good scraping. She just won't tell you where the best of it grows.
copper_wells_the_reagent_hunt.offer: The primer's no use without reagent, and the reagent's the ugly part. Grave-lichen, blue-base -- you scrape it off stones standing in threaded ground, and the best of it grows where the ground's most ruined. There's a rule in Hessik: everything's a reagent, nothing's free, and gathering is complicity. Fetch me a good scraping off the warm-ground stones and I'll pay proper. I won't pretend it's a clean errand. Nothing you scrape off a ruined place is clean. But I'll pay.
copper_wells_the_reagent_hunt.progress: Grave-lichen grows bluest on the stones standing in the warm, threaded ground. Scrape a good measure for Florea -- and mind, the harvest is a small poisoning.
copper_wells_the_reagent_hunt.complete: That's fine lichen -- blue as a drowned man's lips, and it came off ruined stone, which is the only place it grows. You've a taste of metal in your mouth now, I'll wager. That's the complicity Hessik warns of: you gathered it, so you own a little of the ruin it came from. Here's your pay, honest weight. And a jar of the cultured stuff I keep for readers with the shakes. You'll have the shakes by tomorrow.

objectives.text: Scrape grave-lichen from the warm-ground stones

copper_wells_the_reagent_hunt.note: Florea got her grave-lichen, blue as a drowned man's lips, scraped from stones standing in ruined ground. Every dose is a small poisoning and every gathering a small complicity -- you own a little of the ruin now. She paid honest weight and warned of the shakes to come.

copper_wells_a_keepsake_for_the_widow.title: A Keepsake for the Widow
copper_wells_a_keepsake_for_the_widow.summary: One of the herdsmen the bog-widow took left a wife who won't believe him dead. Horia has his keepsake-cord to give her -- but can't make himself do it. He asks you to carry it, and to let her keep believing a little longer, if she needs to.
copper_wells_a_keepsake_for_the_widow.offer: Petru's wife still sets his bowl at supper. Won't have it that he's gone -- says he's just out late with the flock, been out late before. I've his keepsake-cord here, the braided one he wore, and it ought to go to her. I've buried a dozen men and I can't walk this cord across a threshold. Would you carry it? And if she needs to believe he's coming home a while yet -- let her. There's no kindness in taking that from her before she's ready. Just leave the cord. She'll understand it when she's able.
copper_wells_a_keepsake_for_the_widow.progress: Petru's wife keeps his croft near the second well, still setting his bowl at supper. Carry the keepsake-cord to her, and be gentle with what she chooses to believe.
copper_wells_a_keepsake_for_the_widow.complete: You left it on the sill and didn't press her. That was right. She'll come to it. Maybe tonight, maybe next month -- when she stops setting the bowl. There's no hurrying grief and no kindness in trying. Take this cord of his brother's; the both of them are gone and no one left to keep it. Better on a living wrist than in my box. Thank you for doing the thing I couldn't.

objectives.label: Petru's croft
objectives.text: Bring the keepsake-cord to Petru's wife

copper_wells_a_keepsake_for_the_widow.note: Petru's keepsake-cord lies on his widow's windowsill. She still sets his bowl at supper, and you did not take that from her. Horia gave you the brother's cord too -- both men gone, no one left to keep it. Grief keeps its own hours out here, and no one hurries it.

copper_wells_measuring_the_warm_ground.title: Measuring the Warm Ground
copper_wells_measuring_the_warm_ground.summary: The patch of ground that never freezes has been growing. Gavril wants its edges walked and marked on a border-map, so the folk know how far to keep their children -- and so someone, somewhere, has a record of how fast the warmth spreads.
copper_wells_measuring_the_warm_ground.offer: The warm patch is bigger this year than last. I know it the way you know a bruise is spreading -- by where it wasn't yesterday. I want its edges walked and set down on a map, proper, so I can tell a mother exactly how far from the wells to graze her flock. And so there's a record. Somebody up the road ought to have it written down: this much moor went warm, this fast. Walk the boundary for me. Where the frost stops holding, that's the line. Mark it.
copper_wells_measuring_the_warm_ground.progress: Walk the boundary of the warm ground where the frost stops lying, and mark its edges on the border-map for Gavril's record.
copper_wells_measuring_the_warm_ground.complete: Wider than I feared, and the eastern edge pushed a good forty paces past last winter's mark, all of it pointing the one way -- toward the Chamber, if a body drew the line straight. I'll keep the map. I'll show it to the next patrol that crosses and pretends not to see us. Maybe someone with more roads than me can use it. Maybe nobody can. But it's written down now. That's worth something. It has to be worth something.

objectives.label: the northern edge of the warmth
objectives.text: Mark the warm ground's northern edge

objectives.label: the eastern edge of the warmth
objectives.text: Mark the warm ground's eastern edge
Bring the marked map to Gavril

copper_wells_measuring_the_warm_ground.note: The warm ground has grown forty paces past last winter's mark, its far edge pushed east toward the Chamber. Gavril set it down on a border-map and will show it to a patrol that pretends not to see the folk here. He needs it to be worth something. He is not sure it is.

copper_wells_the_incurious_charm.title: The Charm That Keeps You Dull
copper_wells_the_incurious_charm.summary: Old Marta's craft is remembered here: charms that keep a mind pleasantly incurious, unable to wonder at the marks, unwilling to lean toward the listening. Oprea knows the making. He needs the makings gathered -- and a warning heeded about the cost.
copper_wells_the_incurious_charm.offer: There was a herb-woman kept the whole border alive once, they say, not with cures but by keeping folk dull -- incurious, unwilling to wonder at a strange mark. Her craft came down to me, a little of it. I can make a charm that stops you leaning toward the listening. But it costs -- wear it too long and you go dull all through, no wonder left in you for anything, a stew, a sunrise, a grandchild. I'll make you one if you gather the makings. And I'll tell you when to take it off. You'll want to be told.
copper_wells_the_incurious_charm.progress: The makings for the incurious charm -- knotted cord and warded reeds -- lie by the salt-flats and the ochre bank. Gather them, and hear Oprea's warning on the cost.
copper_wells_the_incurious_charm.complete: There. Wear it near the warm ground and the marks won't pull at you, won't make you want to read. But take it off at the hearth, at the table, wherever there's a small good thing to feel -- because the charm can't tell holy curiosity from the killing kind. It dulls the both. The herb-woman knew that. Kept folk alive and half-asleep, and I don't know to this day if that was mercy. Take it. Take it off when it's safe to wonder.

objectives.text: Gather the makings for the charm
Hear Oprea's warning on the cost

copper_wells_the_incurious_charm.note: Oprea made the incurious charm the old herb-woman's way -- it stops the mind leaning toward the listening, and dulls all wonder along with it, a sunrise as much as a strange mark. He could not say whether keeping folk half-asleep was ever mercy. He told you when to take it off. He knew you'd want telling.

copper_wells_the_pot_hook_forge.title: A Hook for Every Hearth
copper_wells_the_pot_hook_forge.summary: With the ore road dead, Dorin forges what the moor needs from its own bog-iron -- pot-hooks, mostly, honest things for honest kitchens. He wants iron enough to hook every hearth in the hamlet before winter. Bring him the metal and he'll do the rest.
copper_wells_the_pot_hook_forge.offer: You want to know what keeps a place alive when the world goes strange? A working kitchen. A pot on a good hook over a fire that catches. Grand, no. But you can't tilt your head and listen to nothing if you're stirring a stew. I'm forging a pot-hook for every hearth in the hamlet from bog-iron, keeping folk's hands busy and their bellies full. Bring me the iron and I'll bend it true. Six lumps ought to hook the whole row. Warm work, this. The good kind.
copper_wells_the_pot_hook_forge.progress: Bog-iron from the peat will forge into pot-hooks for the hamlet's hearths. Gather six lumps for Dorin's honest work.
copper_wells_the_pot_hook_forge.complete: Listen to that -- the whole row's forges ringing, and by nightfall a pot swinging over every fire in the hamlet. You can't hear the moor over a good rolling boil. That's my whole philosophy, friend: keep the hooks strong and the pots full and the hands busy, and the listening's got no room to get in. Take this one. Best I've bent. Hang it wherever you make your suppers, and think of us stirring ours.

objectives.text: Gather bog-iron for the hamlet's pot-hooks

copper_wells_the_pot_hook_forge.note: By nightfall a pot swung over every hearth in the hamlet, hung on bog-iron Dorin bent himself. You can't hear the moor over a good rolling boil, he says -- keep the hands busy and the listening finds no room to get in. It is a small philosophy. Out here it is the whole of the defense.

copper_wells_the_burned_lead_pourer.title: Who Burned the Outbuilding
copper_wells_the_burned_lead_pourer.summary: The reeve's outbuilding didn't burn by accident -- someone torched it after Grozea poured his lead box there, and Horia wants to know who, and whether they were trying to hide the marks or destroy them. Two neighbors have two stories. Hear them both.
copper_wells_the_burned_lead_pourer.offer: That outbuilding where Grozea cast his box -- it burned the same night he went to the listeners, and I don't hold with coincidence out here. Somebody put a torch to it. Question is whether they were hiding what he copied or trying to kill it. Two of the reeve's neighbors were near that night. They've each got a story, and the stories don't fit. Go and hear the both of them. I want to know if we've a frightened man among us -- or a curious one. The curious kind is how this all spreads.
copper_wells_the_burned_lead_pourer.progress: Two neighbors saw the outbuilding burn and tell it differently. Hear both accounts, then bring your read back to Horia.
copper_wells_the_burned_lead_pourer.complete: So one of them saw a man run from the fire with a rubbing in his fist, and the other swears he saw the same man throw a page into the flames. Hiding it or burning it -- and no way to know which, because the only witness who'd settle it is out tilting his head at the wells. Here's the ugly truth of this moor: everyone's story is half-true and the half that matters is always the half nobody'll say. Keep the lens. Watch that man. Say nothing yet.

objectives.text: Hear Matei's account of the fire
Hear Oprea's account of the fire

copper_wells_the_burned_lead_pourer.note: The reeve's outbuilding was torched the night Grozea joined the listeners, but the two witnesses tell it opposite -- a man fleeing with a rubbing, or a man burning a page. Hiding it or killing it, no one can say, and the one who could is at the wells now. On this moor, the half of the story that matters is always the half nobody will speak.

copper_wells_the_shepherds_daily_rounds.title: The Far Flock
copper_wells_the_shepherds_daily_rounds.summary: The hamlet's sheep still graze the cold western moor, and someone has to walk them off the warm ground each day before the flock drifts toward it -- because sheep, unlike folk, have no charm to keep them incurious. Walk the daily round.
copper_wells_the_shepherds_daily_rounds.offer: Do the round with me today, would you -- or for me, my hip's no good before noon. The flock grazes the cold moor to the west, and every day a few drift east toward the warm ground, and I don't like where that leads. A sheep's got no hex-sign, no salt line, no charm to keep it dull. It just wanders toward warmth like anything cold does. Walk the western pasture, turn the strays back before they reach the warm patch. Do it every day and we keep the flock. Miss a day and we start losing them the way we lose folk.
copper_wells_the_shepherds_daily_rounds.progress: The flock grazes the cold western moor and drifts daily toward the warm ground. Walk the western pasture and turn the strays back.
copper_wells_the_shepherds_daily_rounds.complete: All turned back, the whole flock accounted for. Good. You'd think it was nothing, walking sheep off a warm patch. But it's the same work as everything out here -- notice the drift early, turn it gently, do it again tomorrow. Folk are just sheep that can talk themselves into the warmth. Here, off the day's milking. Come back tomorrow; the drift never stops, so neither do we.

objectives.label: the western pasture
objectives.text: Turn the flock back from the warm ground

copper_wells_the_shepherds_daily_rounds.note: The flock was turned back off the warm ground again, the whole day's drift undone. Folk are just sheep that can talk themselves into the warmth, Matei says. The drift never stops, so the round never ends. It is done again tomorrow, and the day after, without complaint.

copper_wells_binding_the_keepsakes.title: What the Listeners Left
copper_wells_binding_the_keepsakes.summary: Each of the folk who went to the listening left something behind -- a ring, a comb, a child's cord. Lazar gathers them into a bound keepsake, a record of who the moor took, so that when they are gone entirely, someone remembers they were here.
copper_wells_binding_the_keepsakes.offer: When a body goes to the listening, they leave a thing behind -- always. A ring on a sill, a comb in the reeds, a cord some mother braided. Nobody's coming back for them, and I can't abide them scattered, forgotten, as if the folk never were. I'm binding them together -- a keepsake of the taken, one book, so there's a record they lived here and were loved. Gather what the listeners left, out by the warm ground where they drift. Bring the small things in. Let them be remembered somewhere that isn't the moor.
copper_wells_binding_the_keepsakes.progress: The listeners left small keepsakes scattered near the warm ground -- rings, combs, braided cords. Gather them for Lazar's bound record of the taken.
copper_wells_binding_the_keepsakes.complete: A comb, two rings, a child's cord, a bent spoon somebody's grandmother left. I'll bind them in and write each name I know. It changes nothing -- they'll go entire soon, the last of them, understood to death or simply gone. But a thousand years on, someone might open this book and read that Draga lived, and Torn, and a girl whose name I never learned who liked the copper light on the reeds. That's not nothing. It's the opposite of the listening. It's keeping. Take the bound keepsake. Keep something too.

objectives.text: Gather the keepsakes the listeners left
Bring the keepsakes to Lazar

copper_wells_binding_the_keepsakes.note: Lazar bound the keepsakes of the taken into one book and wrote each name he knew -- Draga, Torn, a girl who liked the copper light on the reeds. The listeners will go entire soon. But a thousand years on, someone might open the book and read that they lived and were loved. Keeping is the opposite of the listening.

copper_wells_the_patrol_that_pretends.title: The Patrol That Pretends Not to See
copper_wells_the_patrol_that_pretends.summary: Four factions' patrols cross the moor and pretend not to see each other or the folk who live here. Gavril has a forged toll-receipt to slip a passing rider, to buy the hamlet a season's grace. He needs it carried to the crossing and left where the patrol will find it.
copper_wells_the_patrol_that_pretends.offer: Nobody owns this moor, which means everybody crosses it and nobody helps. Four kingdoms' patrols, and they pretend not to see us, and worse, they pretend not to see each other. I've a forged toll-receipt -- says the hamlet's paid its dues to a lord who doesn't know we exist -- and if I slip it where the next patrol finds it, they'll ride on and leave us a season's grace. It's a lie. It's a cheap forgery. It's also the only wall this hamlet's got. Carry it to the old crossing and leave it under the marker-stone. Don't be seen doing it.
copper_wells_the_patrol_that_pretends.progress: The forged toll-receipt wants leaving under the marker-stone at the old crossing, where the next patrol will find it and ride on. Carry it there unseen.
copper_wells_the_patrol_that_pretends.complete: Left it clean, and no one the wiser. Good. They'll find it, read it, believe it -- because a patrol out here wants an excuse not to stop as much as we want them not to. A lie both sides are glad to swallow. That's diplomacy on the border: everybody pretending, everybody safer for the pretense. It'll hold a season. Then I'll forge another. A decent man, a cheap forgery, a hamlet that gets to see the spring. I've made peace with all three.

objectives.label: the old crossing marker-stone
objectives.text: Leave the forged receipt at the crossing

copper_wells_the_patrol_that_pretends.note: Gavril's forged toll-receipt lies under the crossing marker-stone, and the next patrol will find it, believe it, and ride on -- a lie both sides are glad to swallow. It buys the hamlet a season's grace. Then he will forge another. A decent man, a cheap forgery, a hamlet that gets to see the spring.

copper_wells_the_thread_touched_dead.title: Down From the North
copper_wells_the_thread_touched_dead.summary: The dead have begun wandering down from the north onto the moor -- thread-touched things, not risen by any local grief, walking south toward the warm ground as if summoned. Horia wants them put down before the folk see what's coming for them next.
copper_wells_the_thread_touched_dead.offer: New thing on the moor, and I hate it worse than the leeches, worse than the widow. Dead men, walking down from the north -- not our dead, not the yard's, nothing anyone here mourned. Thread-touched, the word is; risen up in the tundra and walking south, toward the warm ground, slow and certain like they've an appointment. The folk haven't seen them yet. I'd keep it that way. Go out to the north edge and put them down before they reach the wells. And -- don't look at what's stitched into them. Just cut them and be done.
copper_wells_the_thread_touched_dead.progress: Thread-touched dead wander onto the moor's north edge, walking south toward the warm ground. Put them down before the folk see them, and do not study what moves them.
copper_wells_the_thread_touched_dead.complete: Down, all of them, and the folk none the wiser. But you saw it too, didn't you -- how they weren't shambling, weren't hungry, just walking, purposeful, toward the wells like the warm ground was calling them home. The north's coming to us. Whatever's woke up in the tundra, it's reaching this far south now, and it'll reach further. I'll not tell the hamlet. Let them have their pot-hooks and their salt lines a while yet. Take the wardplate. You'll be walking north yourself one day, and it's worse up there.

objectives.text: Put down the thread-touched wanderers

copper_wells_the_thread_touched_dead.note: Thread-touched dead walked down from the northern tundra onto the moor, purposeful and unhungry, drawn toward the warm ground as if called home. Horia put them down and told no one. Whatever woke in the north is reaching this far south now. It will reach further. The folk keep their salt lines a while yet.

copper_wells_the_chalk_handprint.title: The Copper Handprint
copper_wells_the_chalk_handprint.summary: On a byre wall the hamlet's children pressed their hands in chalk, a game, a keeping. One print has gone faintly copper, warm to the touch, though the child who left it is well. Matei is afraid of it. He wants it warded before it does whatever a coppered handprint does.
copper_wells_the_chalk_handprint.offer: The children play a game on the byre wall -- press their hands in chalk, leave a print, so the wall remembers them. Sweet. Except one print's gone copper. Faint, coppery, warm under your palm like the wall's got a fever just there, just that one small hand. And the child who left it -- little Draga's girl -- she's well, she's playing, she's fine. It's only the print that's turned. I don't know what a coppered handprint does. I don't want to learn it the way we learn everything out here. Ward it. Please. Before it teaches us.
copper_wells_the_chalk_handprint.progress: The coppered handprint is on the byre wall by the eastern pasture, warm to the touch though the child is well. Ward it with the chalk-handprint ward before it spreads.
copper_wells_the_chalk_handprint.complete: You pressed the ward over it -- the counter-print, chalk on chalk, the old handprint-ward my grandmother swore could cover a wrong mark. It's cool now. Cool under the palm, both prints, the coppered one sleeping under the white. The girl's still well. Maybe it holds. Maybe I bought her a year, same as the salt buys a night. That's all any of us do out here -- cover the warm mark with a cool hand and hope the ground forgets it made one. Take the spare ward. There'll be another print. There's always another print.

objectives.label: the coppered handprint on the byre wall
objectives.text: Ward the coppered handprint
Tell Matei the ward is set

copper_wells_the_chalk_handprint.note: A child's chalk handprint on the byre wall had gone coppery and warm, though the girl who left it stays well. Matei covered it with the old handprint-ward, chalk on chalk, and it went cool -- the coppered print sleeping under the white. He bought her a year, maybe, the way salt buys a night. There is always another print.

copper_wells_last_watch_at_the_clean_wel.title: Last Watch at the Clean Well
copper_wells_last_watch_at_the_clean_wel.summary: The clean well is nearly gone. Barbu wants one last watch kept over it through the night -- not to guard it from thieves, but to sit with it, the way you sit with the dying, so the last clean water on the moor does not turn alone.
copper_wells_last_watch_at_the_clean_wel.offer: It's near done, our well. A cup or two of sweet left, and copper coming up behind it. I'll not lose it in my sleep like I lost everything else out here. I want a watch kept tonight -- not against thieves, there's nothing to steal but water going bad. A watch the way you sit with the dying. Somebody by the well when it turns, so it doesn't turn alone in the dark with no one to mark the hour. Will you keep it with me? Bring the deepbed lantern. We'll want a good light for a long night.
copper_wells_last_watch_at_the_clean_wel.progress: Keep the last watch over the clean well through the night, so it does not turn alone. Sit with Barbu by the wellstone until the hour comes.
copper_wells_last_watch_at_the_clean_wel.complete: It turned near dawn. I tasted it -- copper, faint, then sure, the last sweet water on the whole moor gone the way of the rest. And we were here. We marked the hour. It didn't turn alone. That's the only thing I had left to give it, and I gave it. Everything good is temporary, my grandmother would say. That's what makes it good. We had good water in a warming land for longer than we'd any right to. Now we count our candles like the rest. Take the lantern. You kept the watch. You kept it well.

objectives.label: the clean well behind the inn
objectives.text: Keep the night watch over the clean well

copper_wells_last_watch_at_the_clean_wel.note: The last clean well on the moor turned copper near dawn, and Barbu was there to mark the hour, so it did not turn alone. He tasted the water going and named it what his grandmother would: everything good is temporary, and that is what made it good. The moor counts its candles now, all its wells the same color at last.

copper_wells_the_road_south.title: Word Down the Iron Vein
copper_wells_the_road_south.summary: With every well turned, Lazar wants his record carried on -- his book of names and readings sent south along the Iron Vein to the Bent Oar, where the road still runs and someone might act on it. He asks you to bear it, and to breadcrumb the way toward the Stonepath beyond.
copper_wells_the_road_south.offer: The wells are all one color now and my book's as full as it's going to get -- names of the taken, the readings, the map of the warm ground and how fast it grew. It's no use to me sitting on a moor that's already lost. But south, along the Iron Vein where the road still holds, there's the Bent Oar, and folk there who log such things and pass them on. Carry my record down the Vein for me. Leave it with the keeper. And past there the Stonepath, where all four kingdoms' roads cross -- if this reaches anyone who matters, that's the way it goes. Bear it faithfully. It's the last thing this moor made that wasn't copper.
copper_wells_the_road_south.progress: Carry Lazar's record south along the Iron Vein toward the Bent Oar, where the road still runs. Bear it to the marker at the moor's south edge and set out.
copper_wells_the_road_south.complete: Off it goes, then -- south down the Vein, out of my keeping and into the road's. Maybe it reaches the Bent Oar. Maybe past that to the Stonepath, where the roads cross and the stones are older than all four crowns. Maybe someone reads it and shuts a gate before the warm ground reaches them. Maybe it just sits in a keeper's box gathering the same moss that ate our names. But it's moving. It's out of the dying place and moving. That's more than the folk out at the wells can say. Go with it a way, if the road'll have you. It's kept better than we have.

objectives.label: the south road down the Iron Vein
objectives.text: Bear Lazar's record to the south road

copper_wells_the_road_south.note: Lazar's record went south down the Iron Vein -- the names of the taken, the readings, the map of the spreading warmth -- bound for the Bent Oar and perhaps the Stonepath beyond, where all four kingdoms' roads cross. Maybe someone reads it and shuts a gate in time. Maybe it gathers the same moss that ate the wells' names. But it is moving, out of the dying place. That is more than the listeners can say.

grey_marches_forester_law_01.title: Take the Grey, Leave the Green
grey_marches_forester_law_01.summary: The forester's law is older than the crowns: take the grey trees, leave the green. Marta cannot swing an axe anymore, but she can point you at the deadfall the grey wood has fed.
grey_marches_forester_law_01.offer: My grandfather taught me the law before he taught me my letters -- take the grey, leave the green, and the march outlasts your grandchildren's grandchildren. Trouble is, the grey wood's stopped keeping to itself. Bark-things walk out of the deadwood now. Put six of them down for me. And mind you don't stand still while you're at it.
grey_marches_forester_law_01.progress: The bark-revenants haunt the deadfall along the old forester's cut. Six should quiet the treeline a while.
grey_marches_forester_law_01.complete: Six down. Good. You'll have noticed they don't bleed proper -- more like a tree does, slow and clear. My grandfather never had to fight the wood he pruned. He just pruned it. Take his skinning-knife. It's kept an edge sixty winters. It'll keep yours.

objectives.text: Cull the bark-revenants at the forester's cut

grey_marches_forester_law_01.note: The bark-things do not bleed. They weep the way a cut pine weeps, slow and clear. Marta pruned this wood for sixty years and never once had to kill it. Now she cannot tell the pruning from the war.

grey_marches_forester_law_02.title: No Green Past the Second Ridge
grey_marches_forester_law_02.summary: Marta wants you to walk to the second ridge and count the green trees there. She remembers a rampart of them. She wants to be told her memory is only old.
grey_marches_forester_law_02.offer: There's a thing I can't ask my own knees to do, and I'll not send Dorica -- she talks to the trees and I'll not have her talking to those. Walk to the second ridge. Count me the green trees. When I was a girl you couldn't see sky for them. Come back and tell me my eyes have just gone soft with age. Please tell me that.
grey_marches_forester_law_02.progress: The second ridge lies deep in the march, past the forester's cut. Reach it and count what still grows green.
grey_marches_forester_law_02.complete: ...None. You counted none. I hoped you'd say a hundred and I'd call you a poor counter and we'd both laugh. There are no green ones left past the second ridge. We stopped logging Tuesday -- nobody ordered it. You just don't swing an axe in a room where something's dying. Sit. I'll pour you something warm. It's the least the march can still give.

objectives.label: the second ridge
objectives.text: Count the green trees on the second ridge
Tell Marta what still grows there

grey_marches_forester_law_02.note: Past the second ridge there was no green thing left to count. Marta had hoped to be told her eyes were old. They stopped logging on a Tuesday, she said -- nobody gave the order. You just do not swing an axe in a room where something is dying.

grey_marches_needle_count_01.title: Needle by Needle
grey_marches_needle_count_01.summary: Ileana keeps a ledger of the greying: which trees turned, and when. She needs fresh grey-moths from the fading canopy -- the moths only breed where the wood has already stopped agreeing to live.
grey_marches_needle_count_01.offer: I keep count of the dying the way the grave-women keep count of the dead -- because someone must, and because a thing counted is a thing not yet forgotten. The grey grave-moths breed only in the wood that's already given up. Bring me eight from the canopy. I'll pin them by the date each tree they hatched from went grey. It's a small science. It's the only one I have.
grey_marches_needle_count_01.progress: The grey grave-moths cloud the fading canopy where the needles no longer fall. Take eight for Ileana's ledger.
grey_marches_needle_count_01.complete: Eight, and every one hatched from a different quarter of the wood -- which means the greying isn't spreading from a place. It's arriving everywhere at once, like a decision. Trees don't decide. Something decides for them. There, I've written it down. Now it's real, and now I can't unknow it. Take the charm. It's supposed to keep a mind incurious. Mine came too late.

objectives.text: Take grey grave-moths from the fading canopy

grey_marches_needle_count_01.note: The moths hatched from every quarter of the wood at once. The greying does not spread from a place, Ileana wrote. It arrives everywhere together, like a decision. Trees do not decide. She underlined that twice, and then stopped writing for the day.

grey_marches_the_lit_fire_01.title: The Fire in the Grey Wood
grey_marches_the_lit_fire_01.summary: A cult keeps a fire lit deep in the march. Costel has watched its smoke for a month. He wants you to find the clearing where it burns -- and to notice, when you stand there, that the fire is not what warms the ground.
grey_marches_the_lit_fire_01.offer: There's a fire in the grey wood that never goes out. I've watched its smoke a month of nights, always the same thread, always straight up whatever the wind's doing. Folk go to it. Hungry folk, mostly -- the kind that's started walking east and won't say why. I need eyes at that clearing before I take spears there. Reach it. Stand in it a moment. And come tell me true: is it the fire that's warming the ground, or is the fire just where they gather because the ground's warm already.
grey_marches_the_lit_fire_01.progress: The cult's fire burns in a clearing deep in the march, its smoke rising straight against the wind. Reach it, and read the ground.
grey_marches_the_lit_fire_01.complete: Straight up against the wind. Warm ground under a cold sky. And the fire small -- too small to warm a clearing that size. You felt it, then. The fire isn't heating the earth. The earth is heating, and they lit a fire over it so they'd have a reason to kneel there that they could say out loud. Saints. I'll not take spears to a warm spot in the ground. That's not a war you win with spears. Take the striker -- and if you go back, don't you dare read anything you find carved.

objectives.label: the cult's clearing
objectives.text: Reach the clearing where the fire burns

grey_marches_the_lit_fire_01.note: The smoke rose straight against the wind, and the fire was too small for the warmth underfoot. The fire is not what warms the ground. They kneel there, Costel said, because the ground is warm -- and a fire gives them a reason to kneel that they can say out loud.

grey_marches_the_lit_fire_02.title: What the Kneelers Carry
grey_marches_the_lit_fire_02.summary: Costel will not war on warm ground -- but he will know what the kneelers pass hand to hand. He asks you to take the rubbings the hungering carry, without reading them, and bring them out sealed.
grey_marches_the_lit_fire_02.offer: I've thought on it, and I've slept none. We can't fight the warm ground, but I'll be cursed if I let the reading spread unwatched. The kneelers pass rubbings hand to hand -- charcoal on bark, angular marks, the kind that pull your eye like a hook pulls a lip. Take six off them. Don't look at them. Roll them face-in and bring them to me sealed, and I'll burn the lot unread. Ash tells no one anything. That's the only clean thing I know to do.
grey_marches_the_lit_fire_02.progress: The hungering carry bark-rubbings among the bark-revenants at the clearing's edge. Take six -- rolled face-in, unread.
grey_marches_the_lit_fire_02.complete: Six, and all sealed, and your eyes still your own. Good. You'd know if you'd read one -- you'd have gone quiet and pleased, like the kneelers. Into the fire they go, and no, I'll not watch them curl. Some things you burn with your back turned. Here's a warding-sign, hearth-blessed. Hang it where you sleep. It won't stop the reading. But it'll remind you not to.

objectives.text: Take the kneelers' rubbings, unread
Bring the sealed rubbings to Costel

grey_marches_the_lit_fire_02.note: Six rubbings, taken face-in and never read, burned with a turned back. Costel would not watch them curl. You would know, he said, if you had read one -- you would have gone quiet and pleased, like the kneelers. You did not go quiet. Not yet.

grey_marches_hollow_crown_01.title: The Hollow Crown
grey_marches_hollow_crown_01.summary: A great stag walks the deep march, its antlers hung with grey needles, and where it treads the ground goes warm. Ana believes it is not leading the greying but following it -- grazing the warm lanes like a beast that has found where the good grass grows.
grey_marches_hollow_crown_01.offer: There's a stag in the deep wood, big as a plough-horse, its rack gone grey and dripping needles. The kneelers won't touch it -- they say it walks the warm ground, and where it walks, the warm ground spreads. I don't think it's spreading anything. I think it's just a beast that found where the earth stays warm and grazes the lanes like they were spring grass. Which is worse, somehow. End it. Not because it's guilty. Because I can't watch it graze the death of my wood one more dusk.
grey_marches_hollow_crown_01.progress: The hollow stag grazes the warm lanes of the deep march. Find it and put it down.
grey_marches_hollow_crown_01.complete: Down. And look -- its belly's full of grey needles it can't have eaten for hunger. There's no hunger in a beast this fat. It ate them because the warm ground told it to, the only way ground tells a beast anything: by making one place feel like home. It wasn't guilty. Nothing here is guilty. That's the part I can't say to the others. Take the keepsake. It was my brother's. He walked east last spring. I keep meaning to stop keeping it.

objectives.text: Put down the hollow stag in the deep march

grey_marches_hollow_crown_01.note: The stag's belly was full of grey needles no hunger could explain. It grazed the warm lanes because warmth is the one language ground speaks to a beast -- it makes a place feel like home. Nothing in the march is guilty, Ana said. That is the part she cannot say to the others.

grey_marches_charcoal_run_01.title: Charcoal for the Failing Forge
grey_marches_charcoal_run_01.summary: Radu's forge runs cold without charcoal, and the char-burners have all walked off toward the fire in the wood. He asks you to burn him a batch the old honest way, from grey deadfall that will never grow again.
grey_marches_charcoal_run_01.offer: Every char-burner I had has drifted off toward that fool fire in the deep wood, and a forge without char is just a cold anvil and a sad man. Here's a mercy in it, though -- the grey deadfall makes the best char I've ever burned. It takes the flame like it's been waiting for it. Gather me a good load off the dead stands and I'll show you what a warm forge sounds like. It's a fine sound. We don't get many, lately.
grey_marches_charcoal_run_01.progress: The grey deadfall lies thick along the dead stands. Gather bog-iron-grade fuel enough for a burn.
grey_marches_charcoal_run_01.complete: Now that's a sound -- steel ringing and a fire that means it. The grey wood burns hotter than the green ever did, which ought to trouble me more than it does. Here, take this striker's cousin, a proper hearth-tool. A warm forge and a full belly, friend. Two good things in a bad month. Take them while they're offered.

objectives.text: Gather grey deadfall for the char-burn

grey_marches_charcoal_run_01.note: The grey wood burns hotter than the green ever did. Radu's forge rang for an afternoon and the smell of hot iron pushed the fog back a little. He knows the burning should trouble him more than it does. For one afternoon he let it not.

grey_marches_crows_reading_01.title: What the Crows Are Reading
grey_marches_crows_reading_01.summary: The carrion crows have stopped scattering. They gather on the grey boughs in rows and face the same way at dusk, all of them, like a congregation. Ileana wants the flocks broken before the whole march learns whatever they are listening to.
grey_marches_crows_reading_01.offer: The crows have gone wrong, and a chronicler notices when the birds go wrong before anyone else, because I count them. There were flocks that wheeled at nothing, now they perch in rows and face one way at dusk and don't caw. Rows. Facing. You've seen the boot-prints they talk about in the eastern cities -- twelve, all facing the wall. It's the same grammar. Break the flocks before the whole wood picks up the habit. Four should be enough to spoil the sermon.
grey_marches_crows_reading_01.progress: The carrion crow flocks gather in facing rows on the grey boughs at dusk. Break four flocks.
grey_marches_crows_reading_01.complete: Scattered, and screaming proper crow-screams again, thank the saints. You saw how they were set, though -- even spaces, all one heading, like something had lined them up with a ruler. Birds don't line up. Dust lines up, in the warm places. Filings line up, near a lodestone. And now crows. I'll write it down. It's all I do -- I write it down, and it doesn't stop, and I write it down again.

objectives.text: Break the facing crow flocks at dusk
