grey_marches_crows_reading_01.note: The crows perched in even rows, all facing one way, silent -- the grammar of the aligned dust and the twelve boot-prints, learned now by birds. Ileana broke the sermon and wrote it down. It does not stop, she said. So she writes it down again.

grey_marches_the_hungering_01.title: The Ones Who Walk East
grey_marches_the_hungering_01.summary: Ozun's regulars have begun leaving mid-meal to walk east into the wood, stew half-eaten, coin on the table. He cannot follow them all. He asks you to reach the eastward path and turn back the ones you can before the treeline takes them.
grey_marches_the_hungering_01.offer: I've kept this hearth twenty years and I know the sound of a man deciding to walk out on his own supper. It's not a sound. That's the thing. They just stand -- Gren did it yesterday, stew half in the bowl, coin already down like he'd planned to leave and only just remembered -- and they walk east. Toward the grey wood. I can't leave the pots. Go to the east path. Turn back who you can. Tell them their soup's getting cold. Sometimes that's enough. Lately it's less enough.
grey_marches_the_hungering_01.progress: The east path leads out of the village toward the grey wood. Reach it and turn back the ones who walk.
grey_marches_the_hungering_01.complete: You turned three, and lost sight of the fourth past the treeline. Three's good. Three's more than I could've done from behind a stew-pot. They didn't fight you, did they -- they never fight, they just... prefer the other direction, politely, like you're the one being strange. Sit. Eat. On the house. And if you ever feel your own feet wanting east, you come sit at my bar instead. I'll keep a stool warm. A warm stool's an honest warm, at least.

objectives.label: the east path
objectives.text: Turn back the hungering on the east path

grey_marches_the_hungering_01.note: They do not fight when you turn them back. They simply prefer the eastward direction, politely, as if you were the strange one. Ozun turned three and lost the fourth past the treeline. He keeps a stool warm at his bar for anyone who feels their own feet wanting east.

grey_marches_moths_and_meal_01.title: Moths in the Meal
grey_marches_moths_and_meal_01.summary: Grey grave-moths have gotten into Ozun's flour-store and the bread's coming out worse than flat -- it's coming out grey. He needs the canopy thinned near the mill so his last good sack stays good.
grey_marches_moths_and_meal_01.offer: A moth's a small enemy but it's an enemy with numbers, and mine have found my flour. Bread's coming out grey -- greyer than the water-troubles ever made it -- and a grey loaf sits in the belly like a grudge. Thin the moths by the old mill for me. Bring me a handful of the culture off the good grain-sacks while you're at it, so I know which store's still clean. Do this and there's a hot loaf in it -- a proper brown one, I swear it on my mother's oven.
grey_marches_moths_and_meal_01.progress: The grey grave-moths cloud the canopy above the old mill. Thin them and gather clean grain-culture.
grey_marches_moths_and_meal_01.complete: A brown loaf. An honest, risen, brown loaf -- I'd near forgotten the color. The good sack's clean, the moths are thinned, and for one evening the Wayhouse smells like a bakery and not like a wake. Break the crust while it's warm. Warm bread's a warmth I'll never mistrust, whatever the ground's up to. Some warmths you just get to keep.

objectives.text: Thin the moths above the old mill
Gather clean grain-culture from the good sacks

grey_marches_moths_and_meal_01.note: For one evening the Wayhouse smelled like a bakery and not like a wake. Ozun pulled a brown loaf, risen and honest, and would not mistrust that warmth whatever the ground was doing. Some warmths, he said, you just get to keep.

grey_marches_dorica_trees_01.title: Dorica Talks to the Trees
grey_marches_dorica_trees_01.summary: Dorica talks to the trees, and lately, she says, they have begun to answer -- not in words, but by leaning. She wants a keepsake-cord hung on the last green tree she knows, to remind it whose side it is on.
grey_marches_dorica_trees_01.offer: Everyone says I'm simple for talking to the trees. My mother said it, my sister says it now. But the trees never minded, and a thing that never minds you is a thing worth talking to. Only -- they've started answering. Not with words. They lean. When I talk, the grey ones lean toward me, all together, slow. The green ones don't. There's one green tree left that I know of, out past the burnt snag. Hang this cord on it for me. So it remembers there's someone on its side. So it doesn't lean.
grey_marches_dorica_trees_01.progress: The last green tree Dorica knows stands past the burnt snag in the west of the march. Reach it and hang the keepsake-cord.
grey_marches_dorica_trees_01.complete: You hung it? On the green one? And it didn't lean? ...Thank you. That's -- you don't know what that is, to me. One tree that still knows which way is up on its own. I'll go sit with it Sundays. And when it goes grey too -- because it will, they all do -- I'll take the cord down before it can lean, and I'll not talk to that spot again. You have to know when to stop talking to a thing. My mother never learned it. That's how they got her.

objectives.label: the last green tree
objectives.text: Hang the keepsake-cord on the last green tree

grey_marches_dorica_trees_01.note: The last green tree Dorica knows did not lean when you hung the cord. She will sit with it Sundays, and take the cord down before it can lean when it too goes grey. You have to know when to stop talking to a thing, she said. Her mother never learned it. That is how they got her.

grey_marches_merchant_rubbings_01.title: A Buyer for the Bark
grey_marches_merchant_rubbings_01.summary: Anton has learned that the eastern cities pay dearly for bark-rubbings out of the greying wood -- scholars, he's told, collectors of curiosities. He wants a batch gathered. He does not know, or will not say, what the eastern collectors do with them.
grey_marches_merchant_rubbings_01.offer: There's coin moving on a strange commodity, friend, and I move where coin moves -- that's the whole of my trade and I'll not apologize for it. Blestem scholars, so my factor says, pay a Vein's weight in silver for bark-rubbings out of a greying wood. Curiosities, he calls them. Angular marks, charcoal on bark. I don't ask what a Strigoi wants with a marked-up map-border -- asking's how a merchant ends up owing. Gather me six clean rubbings off the deadwood. There's a fat cut in it for you, and I don't ask questions of my carriers either.
grey_marches_merchant_rubbings_01.progress: The marked rubbings can be gathered from the bark-revenants of the deadwood. Bring Anton six.
grey_marches_merchant_rubbings_01.complete: Six, and clean. My factor'll be pleased and I'll be paid, and that's a merchant's whole prayer answered. ...Though I'll tell you what I'd never tell him: I looked at one. Just one. Just to price it fair. And I've not slept right since -- keep wanting to face east when I close my eyes, like there's a window there that isn't. So I've sold you the safe half of this job and kept the poison. Take your cut. And do yourself a mercy the merchant couldn't: don't look at the goods.

objectives.text: Gather bark-rubbings for the eastern buyer

grey_marches_merchant_rubbings_01.note: Anton sold the safe half of the trade and kept the poison. He looked at one rubbing, just to price it fair, and has not slept facing away from east since. The eastern collectors pay a Vein's weight in silver for the greying wood's marks. He does not ask what they do with them. Asking, he says, is how a merchant ends up owing.

grey_marches_chronicle_ledger_01.title: The Ledger of the Greying
grey_marches_chronicle_ledger_01.summary: Ileana has filled a book with the greying's dates and directions, and the pattern in it frightens her more than the wood does. She asks you to carry a copy to the deep march's oldest marker-stone and leave it there -- a record for whoever comes after, if anyone does.
grey_marches_chronicle_ledger_01.offer: I've filled the book. Every tree, every date, every crow-row and warm lane. And there's a shape in it -- the greying doesn't crawl, it doesn't spread from a wound. It arrives. In order. Like a man reading down a list, the same neatness they say the famine had, west of here. I can't unwrite it and I'll not carry it to the cities -- the cities are where the reading goes to breed. But someone after us should know we saw it. Take this copy to the old marker-stone in the deep wood. Leave it in the hollow of the base. If no one ever reads it, good. It'll have done its work by existing.
grey_marches_chronicle_ledger_01.progress: The oldest marker-stone stands in the deep march. Reach it and leave Ileana's ledger in the hollow of its base.
grey_marches_chronicle_ledger_01.complete: It's done, then. It's out of my hands and into the wood's, and the wood keeps things better than people do -- it kept the greying's schedule for a year before any of us noticed. Maybe it'll keep our witness the same way. I'll not chronicle the march anymore. I've written the true sentence about it, and once you've written the true one, the rest are just you refusing to stop. Take the folk-warding. And thank you -- for carrying a thing you weren't tempted to read.

objectives.label: the old marker-stone
objectives.text: Leave the ledger at the old marker-stone

grey_marches_chronicle_ledger_01.note: The greying does not crawl, Ileana's ledger says -- it arrives, in order, like a man reading down a list. She left the true sentence in the hollow of a marker-stone and stopped chronicling the march. Once you have written the true one, she said, the rest are just you refusing to stop.

grey_marches_smith_greentemper_01.title: Green Wood, Grey Wood
grey_marches_smith_greentemper_01.summary: Radu wants to temper a blade the old way, in a quench of green-wood ash -- but there is no green wood left to burn. He asks you to bring bog-iron from the fen instead, and settles for honest metal over old superstition.
grey_marches_smith_greentemper_01.offer: My grandfather quenched his best steel in green-wood ash -- said the living wood put something into the blade that the grey couldn't. Superstition, likely. But there's no green wood left to test it, so now I'll never know, and that's its own small grief. I'll make do with good bog-iron and an honest hand. Bring me eight from the fen edge. A blade's a blade whatever you quench it in. I'll believe that as long as I can.
grey_marches_smith_greentemper_01.progress: Bog-iron can be dug from the fen edge at the march's low ground. Bring Radu eight.
grey_marches_smith_greentemper_01.complete: Eight, good and rusted-red -- that's the iron that wants to be steel. I'll temper it plain, grey-ash quench, and it'll be a fine blade and my grandfather would call it half a blade and we'd argue about it happily for an hour. I miss arguing with him. The grey wood took a lot of things quiet like that -- not the big ones you'd mourn out loud, just the arguments you'd have had. Take the blade. It'll cut what needs cutting. That's all a blade owes you.

objectives.text: Dig bog-iron from the fen edge

grey_marches_smith_greentemper_01.note: There is no green wood left to test the old quench, so Radu will never know if his grandfather was right. The grey wood took the small things quiet -- not the losses you mourn aloud, just the arguments you would have had. He tempered the blade plain, and it cuts what needs cutting. That is all a blade owes you.

grey_marches_guard_patrol_01.title: The Deadfall Patrol
grey_marches_guard_patrol_01.summary: The bark-revenants press closer to the village every night the wood grows quieter. Ana keeps a standing bounty: cull the deadfall walkers, and keep the treeline a spear's length further off.
grey_marches_guard_patrol_01.offer: Simple work and steady, if you've the stomach for a bark-thing that keeps coming after you've opened it. They press in from the deadfall a little more each night the wood goes quiet -- and it goes quieter every night. Cull me five. It won't fix anything. Nothing here gets fixed. But it keeps the treeline a spear's length off, and a spear's length is a night's sleep for the folk behind the wall. That's a fair trade for a day's arm.
grey_marches_guard_patrol_01.progress: The bark-revenants press in from the deadfall each night. Cull five to hold the treeline.
grey_marches_guard_patrol_01.complete: Five, and the treeline's honest again for a night. Come back tomorrow -- they'll have made up the difference by dusk, they always do. It's not a war you win. It's a war you keep losing slower. Here's your coin. Sleep while the sleeping's good.

objectives.text: Cull the deadfall walkers pressing the treeline

grey_marches_guard_patrol_01.note: It is not a war you win, Ana says. It is a war you keep losing slower. The bark-revenants make up the culled distance by every dusk. A spear's length of held treeline buys the folk behind the wall a night's sleep. She calls that a fair trade.

grey_marches_grave_moth_bounty_01.title: By the Moth-Count
grey_marches_grave_moth_bounty_01.summary: Marta pays a small bounty by the moth: the more grey grave-moths thinned from the canopy, the fewer eggs in next season's failing trees. It is the one chore in the march that feels like it might add up to something.
grey_marches_grave_moth_bounty_01.offer: Here's honest piece-work, dear, and the only chore left in this wood that a body can feel good about. Every grey grave-moth you thin is a clutch of eggs that won't hatch in a tree that's still trying. Won't save the wood. But it might buy one green tree one more spring, and one more spring is a thing worth a few coins. Bring me the count -- six'll do for today's purse. And mind, the good work is the killing, not the counting. Don't you start counting like Ileana. It breaks a person.
grey_marches_grave_moth_bounty_01.progress: The grey grave-moths breed in the failing canopy. Thin six for today's bounty.
grey_marches_grave_moth_bounty_01.complete: Six. That's six clutches that won't hatch, and somewhere a tree that gets to try one more year without a moth eating its heart out. Small good. But I've buried the big goods, most of them, and I've a fondness for the small ones now. They stay. Here's your purse. Come back tomorrow and we'll buy another tree another spring.

objectives.text: Thin grey grave-moths for the day's bounty

grey_marches_grave_moth_bounty_01.note: The moth-bounty will not save the wood. But every clutch thinned buys one trying tree one more spring, and Marta has grown fond of small goods -- the big ones she has mostly buried. The killing is the good work, she warns. Not the counting. The counting is what broke Ileana.

grey_marches_burnt_farmstead_01.title: The Lead Box in the Ashes
grey_marches_burnt_farmstead_01.summary: A farmstead on the march's edge burned in the night, no one hurt, nothing looted. Costel wants you to reach it and find what the family was so afraid of that they cooked their own house down around it.
grey_marches_burnt_farmstead_01.offer: The Grenar place burned Tuesday last -- whole family got out, walked west to Angel Wings, wouldn't say a word to anyone. And here's the wrong of it: nothing was looted. Nothing burned but the house. You don't fire your own home over a bad harvest. You fire it over a thing you're afraid to leave standing. Go to the ashes. Find what they were trying to burn. And if it's a lead box -- one of the cheap-made ones, like the peasants copy off the Queen's discipline west of here -- you leave it lie. You hear me? You leave it lie.
grey_marches_burnt_farmstead_01.progress: The burnt Grenar farmstead sits on the western edge of the march. Reach the ashes and find what the family fled.
grey_marches_burnt_farmstead_01.complete: A lead box. Cheap-cast, badly sealed, and empty -- with scratch-marks on the inside of the lid. Someone tried to keep a thing shut in there the way the Queen keeps her fragment shut in hers, and they hadn't the craft, and it got out, and they burned the house it got out in and walked west and left their whole lives standing. You left it lie. Good. That's the whole lesson of the west in three words: leave it lie. Take this. It's a warding a mother pressed on me once. It didn't work for her. Maybe you'll do better.

objectives.label: the burnt farmstead
objectives.text: Search the burnt Grenar farmstead

grey_marches_burnt_farmstead_01.note: The lead box in the ashes was cheap-cast and empty, with scratch-marks inside the lid. A family tried to copy the Queen's discipline -- keep the thing shut, never touch it -- and hadn't the craft. It got out. They burned the house around it and walked west, leaving their whole lives standing. Leave it lie, Costel said. The whole lesson of the west, in three words.

grey_marches_crow_feast_01.title: A Feast Nobody Cooked
grey_marches_crow_feast_01.summary: Anton's supply-mule went missing on the deadwood road and turned up picked clean by crows -- but the crows left the coin-purse untouched on the carcass, which no honest scavenger would. He wants the flock thinned and the strangeness explained.
grey_marches_crow_feast_01.offer: Lost a good mule on the deadwood road, and I'd chalk it to wolves except for one thing that's kept me up doing sums that won't add. The crows had it to the bone -- but they left the coin-purse sitting on the ribs, buckle up, like a tip left on a table. Crows take shiny things. It's the one law of a crow. These didn't. Thin the flock on that road and tell me what a crow's doing that isn't crow anymore. There's coin in it -- the mule's purse, since the crows didn't want it, and clearly nobody did.
grey_marches_crow_feast_01.progress: The changed crow flocks work the deadwood road. Break three and read what the carrion tells.
grey_marches_crow_feast_01.complete: Three flocks scattered, and I've had a look at the mule myself now. Picked clean, purse untouched, and the bones -- the bones are laid out. Not scattered like a kill. Laid. In a line, even-spaced, heading east. A crow doesn't arrange its dinner. Something arranged the crows. I'm a merchant, not a priest, so I'll do the merchant thing: take the purse, take the hint, and move my routes off that road for good. Coin can't spend you if you're not on the road it's counting.

objectives.text: Break the crow flocks on the deadwood road

grey_marches_crow_feast_01.note: The mule's bones were laid out, not scattered -- even-spaced, heading east, and the coin-purse left buckle-up on the ribs like a tip. A crow does not arrange its dinner. Something arranged the crows. Anton moved his routes off that road for good: coin cannot spend you if you are not on the road it is counting.

grey_marches_soothe_dorica_01.title: A Knot for the Quiet Hours
grey_marches_soothe_dorica_01.summary: Dorica ties soothing-knots for the village children so they sleep through the wood's night-noises. She's run out of the good marshlight to fill the last few and asks you to fetch some from the fen where it still glows honest.
grey_marches_soothe_dorica_01.offer: The little ones can't sleep for the wood at night -- it creaks strange now, and creaks aren't scary till they start sounding like they mean it. So I tie them knots. A soothing-knot with marshlight in the middle glows soft and honest, and a child who can see a friendly light doesn't listen so hard to the dark. But my marshlight's run low. There's a jar's worth in the fen where it still glows the right way -- gold, not that new copper glow. Fetch me some? I've six knots left to fill and six children counting on them.
grey_marches_soothe_dorica_01.progress: Honest gold marshlight can be gathered from the fen at the march's low ground. Bring Dorica a jar.
grey_marches_soothe_dorica_01.complete: Gold. Proper gold, not copper. Oh, good -- the copper glow, that's the wrong kind, that's the glow that means the fen's gone warm, and a knot filled with that would do the opposite of soothe. Six knots, six sleeping children. That's a whole night's worth of not-listening, for six little ones who've too much time to listen already. Take a knot yourself. You look like a body who listens too hard. It won't stop the noises. It'll just remind you you're allowed to sleep through them.

objectives.text: Gather honest gold marshlight from the fen

grey_marches_soothe_dorica_01.note: Dorica fills the children's soothing-knots with gold marshlight -- never the copper glow, which means the fen has gone warm. Six knots, six sleeping children, a whole night of not-listening for little ones who have too much time to listen. You look like a body who listens too hard, she said, and pressed a knot on you too.

grey_marches_stag_trail_01.title: Where the Warm Lanes Run
grey_marches_stag_trail_01.summary: With the great stag dead, Ana wants to map the warm lanes it grazed before others learn to follow them. She asks you to walk the stag's trail to its farthest reach and mark where the warm ground ends -- if it ends.
grey_marches_stag_trail_01.offer: The stag's down, but the stag was only following a road that's still there -- the warm lanes, running through the wood like veins under skin. Now that it's gone, the kneelers will start walking the lanes themselves; they always follow the beasts, the beasts follow the warmth. I want the lanes mapped before that. Walk the stag's old trail east to its far end. Find where the warm ground stops. Mark it. If it stops. I'd like very much for it to stop somewhere I can put a fence.
grey_marches_stag_trail_01.progress: The stag's trail runs east along the warm lanes. Follow it to its farthest reach and mark where the warm ground ends.
grey_marches_stag_trail_01.complete: It doesn't stop. You walked it to the far treeline and the ground was warm the whole way and warmer at the end, not cooler -- which means the warmth isn't leaking from a place behind us. It's coming from ahead. From the east. From under the whole wood, running toward something we can't see yet. There's no fence for that. You can't wall off a direction. Take the ward. And Ana's thanks -- for walking a road I couldn't make my own feet take.

objectives.label: the far end of the warm lanes
objectives.text: Follow the stag's trail to where the warm ground ends

grey_marches_stag_trail_01.note: The warm lanes do not end. They ran warmer at the eastern edge, not cooler -- the warmth comes from ahead, from under the whole wood, running toward something unseen. You cannot wall off a direction, Ana said. She could not make her own feet walk that road.

grey_marches_innkeeper_vigil_01.title: The Empty Chair
grey_marches_innkeeper_vigil_01.summary: Ozun keeps one chair at the hearth for Gren, the regular who walked east and did not come back. He asks you to reach the treeline where Gren was last seen and leave his cup there -- a small rite, so the man is grieved and not just gone.
grey_marches_innkeeper_vigil_01.offer: There's a chair at my hearth nobody sits in. It was Gren's -- twenty years he sat there, same seat, same grumble about the ale, and one Tuesday he stood up and walked east and that was that. No body to grieve, so nobody grieves him, so he's not dead, he's just... gone, which is worse, because gone doesn't end. I want to end it. Take his cup to the treeline where he was last seen. Leave it. Say something or don't. Just let the man be grieved. A body deserves grieving even when there's no body. Especially then.
grey_marches_innkeeper_vigil_01.progress: Gren was last seen at the north treeline. Reach it and leave his cup, so he is grieved and not just gone.
grey_marches_innkeeper_vigil_01.complete: You left it there. On a root, you said, upright, where he'd have seen it. ...That's done, then. He's grieved. He's not gone anymore -- he's dead, in my heart at least, and dead's a thing with an edge you can hold. Gone had no edges. I'll put the chair to a stranger tomorrow. Gren wouldn't mind -- he complained about company but he never once sat alone by choice. Here. It's a keepsake-token. Kept names last longer than kept chairs.

objectives.label: the north treeline
objectives.text: Leave Gren's cup at the treeline

grey_marches_innkeeper_vigil_01.note: Gren walked east on a Tuesday and left an empty chair twenty years warm. With no body, no one grieved him -- so he was not dead, only gone, and gone does not end. Ozun sent his cup to the treeline to give the man an edge his heart could hold. Kept names, he said, last longer than kept chairs.

grey_marches_chronicler_last_green_01.title: A True Accounting of the Last Green
grey_marches_chronicler_last_green_01.summary: Before she stops chronicling, Ileana wants one honest census of the green that remains. She asks you to find the deepest surviving green stand and bring back its exact count -- a number to set against the day it, too, goes grey.
grey_marches_chronicler_last_green_01.offer: One last count, and then I'm done with counting -- I've promised myself. There's a green stand in the deep wood, the last real one, a dozen trees or so still holding their color where the fog's thinnest. I want the true number. Not 'some,' not 'a few' -- the number. Because when it goes grey, and it will, I want to be able to say exactly what we lost, down to the trunk. Vague grief is lazy grief. Go count them. Bring me the number. Let it be an honest one, even if it's small. Especially if it's small.
grey_marches_chronicler_last_green_01.progress: The last true green stand grows in the deep wood where the fog thins. Reach it and count the surviving trees.
grey_marches_chronicler_last_green_01.complete: Nine. Nine green trees, in a wood that was a green rampart in my grandmother's telling -- you couldn't see the sky for it, she said, and now the whole sky's visible and it's the wrong trade. Nine. I've written it: 'On this day, nine.' When it's zero I'll write that too, and then I'll close the book, because a book that only counts down isn't a chronicle, it's a clock. Take the reading-draught. Blue, for the honest colors -- it shows a thing as it is, not as the warm ground would have it. Use it to know a green tree from a grey one when your own eyes start to lie.

objectives.label: the last green stand
objectives.text: Count the trees of the last green stand

grey_marches_chronicler_last_green_01.note: Nine. In a wood that was a green rampart in living memory, nine green trees remain. Ileana wrote 'On this day, nine,' and will write the zero when it comes, and then close the book -- a book that only counts down is not a chronicle but a clock. Vague grief, she said, is lazy grief.

grey_marches_blacksmith_bell_01.title: A Bell for the Treeline
grey_marches_blacksmith_bell_01.summary: Radu is casting a warning-bell for the north wall so the folk have something loud and human to ring against the wood's quiet. He needs bog-iron and a strong pair of hands to hang it -- and a clear head, because a bell is only a comfort if someone's there to pull it.
grey_marches_blacksmith_bell_01.offer: The wood's gone too quiet, and quiet's the worst of it -- worse than the bark-things, worse than the warm ground. So I'm casting a bell. A good loud human noise for the north wall, that a body can ring when the quiet gets to leaning on them. Won't scare off a single revenant. Doesn't need to. It's not for them. It's for us -- so we've something to answer the silence with that's ours. Bring me iron for the casting, and then help me hang the fool thing. A bell's no good in a forge. It wants to be up where the quiet is.
grey_marches_blacksmith_bell_01.progress: Radu needs bog-iron to cast the bell, then a hand to hang it at the north wall.
grey_marches_blacksmith_bell_01.complete: Listen to that. -- Go on, pull it again, don't be shy. That's the best sound this village has made in a year: a loud, stupid, human clang that means nothing at all except we're still here and we're still noisy about it. The quiet hates a bell. I don't know that for a fact. I choose to believe it, and choosing to believe a good thing is half of keeping it. Take the striker. Every hearth should have one, and yours should have a good one.

objectives.text: Gather bog-iron for the warning-bell

objectives.label: the north wall
objectives.text: Hang the bell at the north wall

grey_marches_blacksmith_bell_01.note: The bell will not scare a single revenant, and does not need to -- it is not for them. It is a loud, stupid, human noise to answer the wood's quiet with, and choosing to believe the quiet hates it is, Radu says, half of keeping the village whole. The best sound the march has made in a year.

grey_marches_kneeler_talk_01.title: The One Who Came Back
grey_marches_kneeler_talk_01.summary: One of the hungering walked back out of the wood -- Dorica's own cousin, quiet and pleasant and wrong. She's too frightened to speak to him. She asks you to talk to him for her, and to tell her, gently, whether her cousin is still in there.
grey_marches_kneeler_talk_01.offer: My cousin Fen walked east three days back, with the others. And this morning he walked out again -- and he's home, and he's smiling, and he asks after my health, and it's him, it's his voice and his face and his manners, and I've never been so frightened of anyone in my life. Because he came back. They don't come back. Please -- I can't. Talk to him. Ask him what he saw. And tell me, kindly, whether it's Fen in there, or whether the wood sent something home wearing him to see how we'd take it.
grey_marches_kneeler_talk_01.progress: Fen sits by Dorica's door, home from the east and smiling. Speak with him, and read what came back.
grey_marches_kneeler_talk_01.complete: You spoke to him. And he answered everything -- warm, and polite, and pleased to be home -- and there wasn't one wrong word in it, you said. That's what's wrong. Nobody's that untroubled coming out of that wood. He's not lying, he's not raving. He's content. He came back content, from a place that eats content people. I think... I think the wood didn't take him. I think it read him, and decided he wasn't ready yet, and set him back down. Like a bookmark. Oh, Fen. Take the token. And thank you for being braver at my own cousin than I could be.

objectives.text: Speak with Fen, home from the east

grey_marches_kneeler_talk_01.note: Fen walked east and walked back, warm and polite and content -- and that is the wrong of it, for no one comes out of that wood untroubled. Dorica believes the wood did not take him. It read him, found him not ready, and set him down again like a bookmark. Oh, Fen, she said, and could not say more.

grey_marches_accord_survey_01.title: A Survey for the Accord
grey_marches_accord_survey_01.summary: An Accord rider came asking, quietly, for a map of the march's warm ground -- where it warms, how fast, how far east it runs. Costel distrusts giving the four powers anything, but distrusts hiding worse. He asks you to survey the deep march's warmest reach so the border, at least, holds honest numbers.
grey_marches_accord_survey_01.offer: A rider came from the Accord -- soft-spoken, well-fed, and asking for our warm ground like a man asking the price of grain. They want it mapped: where it warms, how fast, how far the lanes run east. Now, I don't hand the four crowns anything for free; a thing you give the powers becomes a thing they use on you. But a lie handed up the road becomes their surprise, and their surprise becomes our grave. So we give them the truth, and we keep a copy. Survey the deep march's warmest reach for me. Let the border be the one place on this continent that told no one a comfortable number.
grey_marches_accord_survey_01.progress: The march's warmest reach lies deep to the east. Survey it for the Accord's map -- and Costel's copy.
grey_marches_accord_survey_01.complete: You've walked it, then, and I can read the count in how you're standing. Warm the whole reach, warmest at the eastern seam, spreading a hand's breadth a week toward the deep wood -- toward the grave under Black Night, if you draw the line straight, and lines here always draw straight. I'll send the Accord their honest map and keep our honest copy, and neither of us will feel one bit safer for the honesty. But we'll not be the liars. In a country of comfortable numbers, that's a thing to be. Take the counteragent. It quiets the pull, a little. You've earned a little quiet.

objectives.label: the warmest reach
objectives.text: Survey the deep march's warmest ground
Bring Costel the survey

grey_marches_accord_survey_01.note: The warm ground spreads a hand's breadth a week toward the deep wood -- toward the grave under Black Night, if the line is drawn straight, and the lines here always draw straight. Costel sent the Accord an honest map and kept an honest copy, and felt no safer for it. But the border, he said, would not be the liars. In a country of comfortable numbers, that is a thing to be.

grey_marches_merchant_daily_01.title: Safe Passage, Priced Fair
grey_marches_merchant_daily_01.summary: Anton still runs a caravan or two through the march, and the crows still work the roads. He pays a standing rate to any hand willing to clear the flocks ahead of his mules -- coin being the one warmth he trusts to stay warm.
grey_marches_merchant_daily_01.offer: I'll be honest with you, which costs me nothing since you already know: I keep trading through a dying wood because a merchant who stops trading is just a man who used to have money. My mules go through, the crows work the road, and I'd rather pay you to scatter them than pay a factor to explain a lost load. Break three flocks ahead of tomorrow's run. Standing rate, coin on delivery. It's not brave work. It's paid work. In this wood, paid and alive is the whole of ambition.
grey_marches_merchant_daily_01.progress: The crow flocks work Anton's caravan road. Break three ahead of the run.
grey_marches_merchant_daily_01.complete: Three flocks, road's clear, mules'll thank you in the only language a mule has, which is by not dying. Here's your rate -- counted twice, because I count everything twice now, ever since I learned what happens to a man who reads a thing once. Come back tomorrow. The road'll need clearing again, and I'll need clearing again, and coin's the one warm thing in this wood I still trust to stay itself.

objectives.text: Clear the crow flocks ahead of the caravan

grey_marches_merchant_daily_01.note: Anton keeps trading through a dying wood because a merchant who stops is just a man who used to have money. Paid and alive, he says, is the whole of ambition here. He counts his coin twice now -- ever since he learned what happens to a man who reads a thing once.

famine_fields_full_granary_01.title: House by House, In Order
famine_fields_full_granary_01.summary: The hunger came to good soil and moved through the villages with a strange neatness, house by house in order, like a hand reading down a list. Zenobia keeps the count. She would like a second pair of eyes on the burned farmstead before the ground finishes reading.
famine_fields_full_granary_01.offer: I keep the grave-rows, traveller, and I keep them honest -- so hear me plain. The famine did not fall on us. It walked. House by house, in order, the way a clerk reads down a list and ticks each name before the next. Nobody starves in a straight line. Go to the burned farmstead at the field's edge and look at what a hungry man left behind. Then come tell me I imagined the order of it.
famine_fields_full_granary_01.progress: The burned farmstead sits north of the grave-rows, at the field's ragged edge. Look, and count as you go.
famine_fields_full_granary_01.complete: So you saw it too. The empty box, cheap lead, scratch-marks on the inside where a man tried to hold something in and it got out anyway. And the rows -- you counted the rows. Good. A thing that starves a village in order is a thing that can be answered in order. Take the pouch. Salt outlasts yeast, and it will outlast this.

objectives.label: the burned farmstead
objectives.text: Walk the burned farmstead
Put down the walking-hungry on the rows

famine_fields_full_granary_01.note: A cheap lead box, scratched from within. Grave-rows counted and kept in order. The famine did not fall on the fields. Something read them, name by name, and the ground did the ticking.

famine_fields_full_granary_02.title: The Granary Was Full
famine_fields_full_granary_02.summary: A cult keeps a fire lit at the old granary and tells the starving there is nothing left to eat. Zenobia wants the fire out and the doors opened. She does not yet know the cruelest part -- that the granary has been full the whole time.
famine_fields_full_granary_02.offer: There's folk out by the great granary who've made a religion of our hunger. Hooded, patient, feeding a fire that never warms anyone. They stand at the door and tell the starving the bins are empty and the only bread left is the kind you have to walk east for. Break them off the door, traveller. Open it. Let people see what a granary looks like when it's doing its honest work.
famine_fields_full_granary_02.progress: The cult keeps its watch at the great granary, east of the burned farmstead. Scatter them, then open the doors.
famine_fields_full_granary_02.complete: ...It was full. Every bin, to the rafters, dry and sound and a year's bread for the whole west. Full the entire time we buried our own. The cult wasn't starving us -- there was no need. They were just staffing it. Keeping the door shut and the story going while the ground did the rest. Take the sign. Hang it on a door you mean to keep open.

objectives.text: Break the watch off the granary door

objectives.label: the great granary doors
objectives.text: Open the granary and look inside

famine_fields_full_granary_02.note: The granary was full. Every bin, to the rafters. The cult never caused the hunger -- there was bread enough all along. They only kept the door shut and the story going. The famine was engineered on ground that had already agreed to be hungry.

famine_fields_full_granary_03.title: Who Reads the List
famine_fields_full_granary_03.summary: The granary stood full while the fields starved in order. Someone wrote that order down. Zenobia sends you to the reading-stone at the field's heart to see the list with your own alchemical sight -- and to look at your boots the whole way out.
famine_fields_full_granary_03.offer: A full granary and a starving field in order means somebody is keeping a list, and lists are written somewhere. There's a stone at the field's heart under the lone dead oak, and the dust around it lies in lines a broom couldn't make. Go and read the color of it -- just the color, mind. Not the marks. Read the color and come straight back and do not, whatever pulls at you, learn what it says.
famine_fields_full_granary_03.progress: The reading-stone stands under the lone dead oak at the field's heart. Read the color of the signal. Only the color.
famine_fields_full_granary_03.complete: Shifting orange. You said orange and I felt my supper turn. That's not a dead thing and it's not a sleeping one -- that's a live hand, reading down the list right now, this season, us. You looked at your boots on the way out. Good. The ones who don't look at their boots end up in my rows with clean fingernails. Keep the still. Read the world, never the word.

objectives.label: the reading-stone
objectives.text: Read the signal's color, and only that

famine_fields_full_granary_03.note: The stone under the dead oak read shifting orange -- the color of a hand still working. The famine was never scarcity. It was a list, being read aloud to the ground, this season, aimed at the west. Zenobia counts on. Someone else counts too.

famine_fields_open_door_01.title: A Full Table Drives Them Off
famine_fields_open_door_01.summary: Casandra keeps the last inn with a lit hearth for leagues, and she has learned the trick her grandmother swore by: the walking-hungry cannot bear the sight of a truly full table. She wants provisions carried to the crossroads to set one.
famine_fields_open_door_01.offer: You'll have heard the husks shamble toward warmth and can't be filled, poor damned things. What folk forget is the old cure: a table so full it shames them. They come for a lit hearth, see a real spread, and can't stand the reminder -- off they go. I'm setting one at the crossroads for the ones still fleeing. Carry the pots and hooks out for me, and bring me back four of the salt-crocks from the burned holding while you're at it.
famine_fields_open_door_01.progress: Casandra needs the salt-crocks from the burned holding for her crossroads table. Four should fill the boards.
famine_fields_open_door_01.complete: There -- boards groaning, hearth roaring, salt in every dish. Watch them turn at the treeline and shuffle back into the dark. It's a mean sort of mercy, feeding a thing by showing it food it can't take. But it clears the road, and cleared roads let living folk walk home. Take the hook. It's hung over a warm pot its whole life and means to keep at it.

objectives.text: Fetch salt-crocks from the burned holding

famine_fields_open_door_01.note: Casandra set a table so full the walking-hungry turned at the treeline and shuffled back. A mean mercy, feeding a thing by showing it what it can never take. But the road cleared, and the living walked home by it.

famine_fields_open_door_02.title: Bread That Will Not Rise
famine_fields_open_door_02.summary: Casandra's dough has not risen in a fortnight, and it is not the flour. She has heard the old wisdom-women speak of a culture that outlasts the killing quiet, and wants you to gather what still ferments from the deep cellars.
famine_fields_open_door_02.offer: My mother's crock has kept a living dough for fifty years -- her mother's before that -- and a fortnight ago it went flat and grey and stayed that way. It's not the flour, love. The small warm life in it just... stopped. Same day the ground by the well went warm. There's a culture the herb-women kept that outlasts the quiet, cellared deep where the cold can't reach the reading. Bring me what still bubbles. I'll not let this hearth go bread-dead.
famine_fields_open_door_02.progress: The living cultures cellared deep beneath the field still ferment against the quiet. Bring back three.
famine_fields_open_door_02.complete: It's breathing again -- feel the crock, warm the right way, the honest way, sour and alive. Bread tomorrow, and the day after. Small thing to fight a world-machine with, a bowl of live dough. But my grandmother said a village that keeps its yeast keeps its nerve, and she outlived three famines saying it. Here, for your trouble -- a poultice grown from the same good rot. It mends what the quiet frays.

objectives.text: Gather living cultures from the deep cellars
Clear the cellar vermin

famine_fields_open_door_02.note: The dough went flat the day the well-ground warmed. Casandra fed her crock a culture cellared against the quiet, and by morning it breathed again. A village that keeps its yeast keeps its nerve. The quiet takes the small warm things first, so no one names it a war.

famine_fields_open_door_03.title: The Long Bread
famine_fields_open_door_03.summary: With her dough breathing again, Casandra means to do the one defiant thing a baker can: feed everyone. She needs bog-iron for a new oven-door and the whole village's hands, and she wants it loud enough that the reading-stone hears it.
famine_fields_open_door_03.offer: The crock's alive, so we bake. Not a loaf -- a hundred. A long bread, the harvest kind, the kind that fills a table so full no husk on this field will come near it and no neighbour will think east is the only road to a full stomach. I need a good iron door for the great oven and I need the smith to hang it. Fetch the bog-iron off the ruined range, and go tell Despina to bring her hammer. We're going to be loud about being fed.
famine_fields_open_door_03.progress: Fetch bog-iron from the ruined range for the oven-door, then tell Despina the smith to come and hang it.
famine_fields_open_door_03.complete: Smell that -- the whole field smells of proper bread for the first time since the ground turned. Folk are crying over loaves, which is a foolish thing and the best thing I've seen in a season. Let the stone under the oak read that, if it reads. It won't understand a village choosing to be full on purpose. Take the warding -- a hearth-hex, blessed on the first loaf. It knows which side of the door you're on.

objectives.text: Fetch bog-iron for the oven-door
Ask Despina to hang the oven-door

famine_fields_open_door_03.note: Casandra baked a hundred loaves and made the whole field smell of bread on purpose. Folk wept over it. The stone reads scarcity and desperation; it has no entry for a village that chose, out loud, to be full. Everything good is temporary. That is what made the smell of it unbearable and holy.

famine_fields_counted_rows_01.title: The Rows Are Counted
famine_fields_counted_rows_01.summary: Smaranda has stood watch over the grave-rows since the hunger began, and lately the scarecrow-wights come to stand among the dead at dusk, tilting toward the reading-stone. She will not have the rows disturbed. Clear the field of them.
famine_fields_counted_rows_01.offer: I've buried more of my own than I've words for, and I laid every one in a straight row and counted them and I keep the count. It matters that someone counts. At dusk the straw-things come and stand among my rows -- just stand, heads tilted the wrong way, like they're waiting their turn on a list. I'll not have my dead stood over by whatever's writing that list. Clear them off the rows, and mind you don't tread on a marker.
famine_fields_counted_rows_01.progress: The scarecrow-wights gather among the grave-rows at dusk. Clear them without disturbing the markers.
famine_fields_counted_rows_01.complete: Good. My rows are my rows again. You stepped careful -- I saw. Most don't. Most see a field of the dead as a shortcut. You saw it as a place where somebody's mother is. That's the whole difference between us and the thing under the oak: it counts to file, we count to remember. Take the cord. I knotted it for every row. It's heavier than it looks.

objectives.text: Clear the straw-things off the grave-rows

famine_fields_counted_rows_01.note: Smaranda counts her dead to remember them; the thing under the oak counts to file them. She keeps the rows straight and the markers true. The scarecrows tilted toward the stone, waiting their turn on a list. She will not have her mother waiting on anyone's list.

famine_fields_counted_rows_02.title: A Marker With No Name
famine_fields_counted_rows_02.summary: One grave in Smaranda's careful rows has no name -- a courier found dead on the east road, no wound, clean fingernails, his satchel still sealed. She wants a proper stone cut for him and the truth of how he died, if the truth can be had cheaply.
famine_fields_counted_rows_02.offer: There's one in my rows with no name, and it gnaws at me. A courier, they found him on the east road -- no wound on him, fingernails clean as a christening, and his satchel still sealed and buckled. Died of nothing, walking. I'll not leave a man nameless in my count. Have the smith cut a marker, and ask the merchant what he carried, if she'll say. A man ought to have more on his stone than 'found'.
famine_fields_counted_rows_02.progress: Have Despina cut a marker for the nameless courier, and ask the merchant Ilinca what he was carrying.
famine_fields_counted_rows_02.complete: Ilinca says he carried nothing anyone paid for. Sealed satchel, empty writ inside. Died of the reading, then -- got too close to understanding something and the understanding took him, clean. No wound. That's the courier's death, the one the old ones warn of. I cut him a marker anyway. 'A man who carried something, and did not open it.' That's an honest epitaph out here. Take his cord. He'd want it walking.

objectives.text: Have Despina cut a courier's marker
Ask Ilinca what the courier carried

famine_fields_counted_rows_02.note: A courier found dead of nothing, satchel sealed, fingernails clean. He died of coming too close to understanding. Smaranda cut him a marker: a man who carried something and did not open it. Out here, restraint is the only epitaph worth cutting.

famine_fields_counted_rows_03.title: Salt for the Keeping
famine_fields_counted_rows_03.summary: The old preservations -- salt and fire -- keep the dead in their rows against the quiet. Smaranda needs a fresh salt-line laid along the grave-field each dawn, and the walking-hungry driven back before they can stand among her count.
famine_fields_counted_rows_03.offer: Salt keeps. It kept before yeast, it'll keep after. Every dawn I lay a fresh line of it along my rows, and every dusk the quiet tries to wear it thin. Lay the line for me today -- reach the far marker at the field's foot and salt the ground as you go -- and put down any husk that's wandered in overnight. It's dull work. Dull work is how you keep a field of the dead from getting up. I'll thank you the same tomorrow.
famine_fields_counted_rows_03.progress: Lay the salt-line to the far marker at the field's foot, and drive back any husks that wandered in.
famine_fields_counted_rows_03.complete: Line's laid, husks are down. My count holds another day. There's no glory in it and I've stopped wanting any. A field kept is a field kept, dawn after dawn, and one morning I'll be a row myself and I'd like whoever salts the line then to be as dull and stubborn about it as I am. Take your coin and a pinch of the salt. It's the same salt. That's the point of salt.

objectives.label: the far marker
objectives.text: Lay the salt-line to the far marker
Drive back the overnight husks

famine_fields_counted_rows_03.note: Salt keeps -- before yeast, after yeast. Smaranda lays her line each dawn against the quiet that would raise her count. Dull, stubborn, endless work. One morning she will be a row herself, and she means the field to be kept just as dully then.

famine_fields_smith_salt_01.title: Fire and Salt
famine_fields_smith_salt_01.summary: Despina's ore stopped coming when the road turned bad, but she has learned the two things the walking-hungry cannot abide: fire and salt. She wants to forge striker-flints for the village and needs veinflint knapped from the old quarry.
famine_fields_smith_salt_01.offer: Blade's no good against a husk -- you can't cut a hunger. But fire and salt, those they flee, being the old keepings that outlast yeast. So I'm not making swords this season, I'm making strikers. A striker in every hand means a lit brand in every hand, and a lit brand turns a husk at the door. Bring me veinflint off the old quarry face -- enough to knap a dozen strikers -- and I'll arm this whole field with fire it can hold.
famine_fields_smith_salt_01.progress: Veinflint waits at the old quarry face, east of the forge. Bring back enough for a dozen strikers.
famine_fields_smith_salt_01.complete: Look at that spark. A dozen strikers, a dozen brands, a dozen doors the quiet won't come through tonight. My father made blades for a war you could see coming. I make fire for one you can't. Different craft, same honest iron. Take one for yourself -- a veinflint striker, tempered slow. When the road's dark and the hearth's far, you'll not curse the weight of it.

objectives.text: Knap veinflint from the old quarry

famine_fields_smith_salt_01.note: You cannot cut a hunger. Despina forges strikers instead of swords -- fire and salt, the old keepings the walking-hungry flee. A dozen brands, a dozen doors held for one more night. Her father made blades for a war you could see. She makes fire for one you cannot.

famine_fields_smith_salt_02.title: The Empty Box
famine_fields_smith_salt_02.summary: Despina found the cheap lead box in the burned farmstead and cannot stop thinking about it -- a peasant tried to seal something the way the western queen seals hers, and failed. She wants to understand the failure before someone in this village tries the same.
famine_fields_smith_salt_02.offer: There's a box in the burned holding out east, and it's been eating at me. Lead, cheap-cast, my trade -- I can see it was poured in a hurry by a man who'd heard the Queen in the west keeps her fragment in lead and thought he'd do the same. Save himself. Except he cast it thin and sealed it wrong and the scratch-marks inside tell how that ended. Go and bring me the box, and the reading-still, so I can see what he saw. I'll not have a neighbour try it next and worse.
famine_fields_smith_salt_02.progress: The cheap lead box lies in the burned farmstead. Bring it back with a reading-still so Despina can examine it.
famine_fields_smith_salt_02.complete: Thin lead, sealed wrong, scratched to ribbons from the inside. He held it maybe a night before he had to open it -- and once it's open, once you're reading, you're the road's already. The Queen's discipline isn't the lead, you see. It's never opening it. It's the not-looking, which a starving man can't afford and she can. That's the whole cruelty of it -- restraint is a rich woman's virtue out here. Take the box. Keep it shut. Truly.

objectives.label: the burned holding
objectives.text: Recover the empty lead box
Drive off the blightflies nesting in the ruin

famine_fields_smith_salt_02.note: A poor man copied a queen's discipline in cheap lead and failed at the one part that mattered: the not-looking. The scratch-marks tell the rest. Restraint is a rich woman's virtue out here -- and the stone knows exactly which villages cannot afford it.

famine_fields_merchant_seals_01.title: Last Year's Seal
famine_fields_merchant_seals_01.summary: Ilinca trades what the field can spare, and lately grain-sacks turn up in the ruins stamped with last year's seal -- grain that was written off as lost, sitting whole. She wants them counted before she decides who profited from a famine that had bread in it.
famine_fields_merchant_seals_01.offer: I keep an honest ledger in a place where honesty is a luxury, so hear this quietly. Sacks are turning up in the burned holdings -- good grain, sound, stamped with last year's seal. Grain that was written down as lost. Lost, and here the whole time. Somebody moved it out of the count and let the villages starve around a supply that existed. Go tally the sacks in the ruins for me. I want a number before I decide whose name that number spells.
famine_fields_merchant_seals_01.progress: Sealed grain-sacks lie in the burned holdings across the field. Tally them, and mind the vermin at them.
famine_fields_merchant_seals_01.complete: Forty sacks, last year's seal, all sound. Forty sacks is a village fed through winter. Somebody knew that and shut them in a burned house and called them lost. That's not a thief -- a thief takes to have. This one took to make us hungry. I don't have the name yet. But I have the number, and out here a number is a knife you can hold onto. Take the dossier. Names go on it as I get them.

objectives.text: Tally the sealed grain-sacks in the ruins
Clear the rats gnawing the stores

famine_fields_merchant_seals_01.note: Forty sound sacks under last year's seal, written off as lost and shut in a burned house. Enough to feed a village through winter. A thief takes to have; this one took to make hunger. Ilinca has the number now. The name will come. Numbers are the only knives the west can hold.

famine_fields_merchant_seals_02.title: The Buyer From the East
famine_fields_merchant_seals_02.summary: Ilinca has traced the vanished grain to a buyer who paid in eastern coin for a famine, and the deal was struck on a forged toll-receipt. She wants the forgery recovered before it can be used to sell the next village's hunger.
famine_fields_merchant_seals_02.offer: I found the shape of it. The lost grain wasn't stolen for profit -- it was bought, by eastern coin, to make us desperate enough to sell. Blestem pays best for a people who'll trade their neighbours' names for bread, and a starving field sells cheap. The deal moved on a forged toll-receipt through the crossroads. Get me that forgery. With it I can prove a famine was purchased -- and a purchased famine has a purchaser, even one who thinks the west too poor to keep a ledger.
famine_fields_merchant_seals_02.progress: The forged toll-receipt changed hands at the crossroads store, now husk-haunted. Recover it and bring it back.
famine_fields_merchant_seals_02.complete: There it is -- a forgery good enough to fool a toll-post, careless enough to keep. Eastern coin, western grain, a famine bought wholesale so the buyer could pick our informants off the desperate. The stone doesn't need to starve us itself, see. It just makes desperation valuable and lets clever men do the starving for a wage. I'll file this. Take the forgery's twin -- a clean writ. Out here, a paper that can unfile a lie is worth more than a sword.

objectives.label: the crossroads store
objectives.text: Reach the husk-haunted crossroads store
Recover the forged toll-receipt
Clear the husks from the store

famine_fields_merchant_seals_02.note: The famine was bought, not born -- eastern coin for western grain, so the desperate would sell their neighbours' names cheap. The stone need not starve anyone; it makes desperation valuable and lets clever men do the starving for a wage. Ilinca filed the proof. A purchased famine has a purchaser.

famine_fields_scarecrow_watch_01.title: What the Scarecrows Face
famine_fields_scarecrow_watch_01.summary: Varvara walks the field-edge at night and swears the scarecrows have started turning -- every straw-figure now faces the lone oak, no matter how the farmers set them. She wants a witness before she trusts her own eyes.
famine_fields_scarecrow_watch_01.offer: You'll think me touched, but stand the field-edge with me and see. The scarecrows -- we set them facing the crows, out and away, every one, the way you always do. Come back at night and they've turned. All of them, toward the dead oak at the field's heart, straw heads tilted like they're listening for a whistle only they can hear. I want another pair of eyes before I say a word to the others. Walk the field-line at dark and count which way they face.
famine_fields_scarecrow_watch_01.progress: Walk the field-line after dark and see which way the scarecrows face. Cut down any that have gone worse than wooden.
famine_fields_scarecrow_watch_01.complete: Every one. Facing the oak. You saw it too, so I'm not touched -- I'm just the first to look. A scarecrow's a dead man's coat on a cross of sticks, and something's teaching them to listen the way it's teaching the living. When the coats start listening, the wearers aren't far behind. Take the ward. Chalk it on your door. It won't stop the turning. But it'll tell you which way to run.

objectives.label: the western field-line
objectives.text: Walk the field-line after dark
Cut down the turned scarecrows

famine_fields_scarecrow_watch_01.note: Every scarecrow had turned to face the dead oak, straw heads tilted, listening. A scarecrow is a dead man's coat on sticks. Something teaches the coats to listen the way it teaches the living. When the coats start, the wearers are not far behind.

famine_fields_scarecrow_watch_02.title: The Vigil at the Oak
famine_fields_scarecrow_watch_02.summary: The scarecrows face the oak; Varvara means to know why. She asks you to stand a silent watch within sight of the reading-stone at dusk -- to witness the one thing that happens there, and to leave the moment it happens.
famine_fields_scarecrow_watch_02.offer: The straw-things face the oak, so I want to know what the oak does. Not read it -- watch it. There's a difference and it's the difference between us and the dead courier. Stand a watch within sight of the stone at dusk, quiet as you can, and mark the one thing that happens. Then leave. The instant you understand it, leave. If you feel yourself starting to make sense of the marks, you look at your boots and you walk. Promise me the walking part. That's the only part that keeps you.
famine_fields_scarecrow_watch_02.progress: Keep a silent vigil in sight of the reading-stone at dusk. Witness the one thing -- then walk away.
famine_fields_scarecrow_watch_02.complete: You came back. Most who watch the oak too long don't -- or come back wrong, quiet, unfinishing their sentences. So. At dusk the dust around it lifts and lies down again in lines, and every scarecrow bows an inch, all together, once. A field taking a breath it didn't ask for. You witnessed and you walked, and the walking is why you're telling me instead of joining them. Take the charm. It dulls the pull. Nothing stops it.

objectives.label: the oak-watch
objectives.text: Keep vigil in sight of the reading-stone

famine_fields_scarecrow_watch_02.note: At dusk the dust rose and lay down in lines, and every scarecrow bowed an inch, together, once -- a field taking a breath it never asked for. You witnessed and walked away. The walking is the only part that keeps you. Those who understand the oak come back quiet, unfinishing their sentences.

famine_fields_neighbor_01.title: A Neighbour Started Listening
famine_fields_neighbor_01.summary: Catrina's neighbour has stopped eating and started walking to the field-edge at dusk to 'feel clearer.' She has heard where that road ends and wants him fed and turned back before the hunger blooms into a will to walk east.
famine_fields_neighbor_01.offer: It's Old Nedelcu next door. He's stopped eating -- says food muddies him, says he thinks clearer empty. And come dusk he stands at the field-edge facing east, listening to nothing, and last night he took a step toward it before I called him back. I know that road. It starts with skipping supper to feel clear and it ends walking east to touch a thing you were never meant to touch. Bring me a proper meal-tin and stand with me while I feed him. A full stomach's the only argument that works on a hungry ghost.
famine_fields_neighbor_01.progress: Bring a full meal-tin from Casandra's hearth, then reach the field-edge where Nedelcu keeps his dusk-watch.
famine_fields_neighbor_01.complete: He ate. Grumbling, but he ate, and the eating pulled him back an inch from the edge. For tonight. That's how it goes out here -- you don't cure the listening, you just out-feed it, meal by meal, and hope the road forgets his face before his stomach forgets ours. The stone doesn't take people. It just makes empty feel like clarity, and lets them walk to it themselves. Take the token. It's his -- he gave it me for the tin. Keep his name walking.

objectives.text: Bring a full meal-tin from the hearth

objectives.label: the eastern field-edge
objectives.text: Reach Nedelcu at his dusk-watch

famine_fields_neighbor_01.note: Nedelcu stopped eating to think clearer, and started walking east to feel clean. You out-fed the hunger for one night. The stone takes no one -- it makes empty feel like clarity and lets them walk to it. You cannot cure the listening. You can only feed it, meal by meal, and hope.

famine_fields_neighbor_02.title: The Chalk Handprint
famine_fields_neighbor_02.summary: Catrina's children press chalk handprints on the wall for luck, the way the western orphanage does. One print has gone faintly copper and warm to the touch, and no one has noticed but her. She needs it warded before a child leans a hand on it again.
famine_fields_neighbor_02.offer: My little ones chalk their handprints on the wall for luck -- everyone does, it's the orphanage way, Maren started it in the west and it spread like all good small things do. But there's one print gone wrong. Faintly copper, and warm -- warm, on a cold wall, where a child's hand was. You know what warm means out here. I've not told them. I can't have them frightened of their own wall. Fetch me a proper ward from the smith and stand by while I chalk over it. Quiet, so the little ones don't see me shake.
famine_fields_neighbor_02.progress: Fetch a chalk-handprint ward from Despina, then reach Catrina's wall to seal the copper print.
famine_fields_neighbor_02.complete: It's covered. Fresh chalk, a good ward under it, and warm no more -- for now. A child's hand, and the ground reached up through the wall to hold it. That's the cruelty of this thing, that it comes for luck-charms and children's games, the smallest warmest things, because those are the ones we don't guard. Thank you. The little ones will chalk again tomorrow and never know. Take the cord -- it's the same string I mark their heights with. Keep them all walking.

objectives.text: Fetch a chalk-handprint ward from Despina

objectives.label: Catrina's wall
objectives.text: Seal the copper handprint

famine_fields_neighbor_02.note: A child's chalk handprint had gone copper and warm on a cold wall -- the ground reaching up to hold a small hand. Catrina warded it and told no one. The stone comes for luck-charms and children's games, the smallest warmest things, because those are the ones no one thinks to guard.

famine_fields_blightfly_01.title: The Flies That Won't Feed the Crows
famine_fields_blightfly_01.summary: Marioara's late husband kept bees and she keeps his grudge against the blightflies that boil up off the warm ground and foul what little grows. She wants them thinned, and she wants a marshlight jar of them so the chronicler can settle an argument.
famine_fields_blightfly_01.offer: Blightflies, dear. Great oily clouds of them, boiling up off the warm patches where no fly should breed in this cold. My Gherghe kept bees and he'd turn in his row to see these things where his girls used to work. They foul the little that grows and the crows won't even eat them -- crows! who'll eat anything! Thin the clouds for me. And catch me a jar of the wretches. Zenobia says they only swarm over live signal, and I say she's clever and I want to prove her right and buy her a drink about it.
famine_fields_blightfly_01.progress: Thin the blightfly clouds over the warm ground, and trap a jarful for Zenobia's argument.
famine_fields_blightfly_01.complete: Ha -- a full jar, and Zenobia's right as usual, drat her: they only rose over the warm patches, the live ones. So the flies are a map, if you're grim enough to read a map made of flies. My Gherghe would've laughed and then gone quiet, which was his way. Thank you, dear. The crows still won't touch them, but the air's breathable again. Take the jar-light. It burns marsh-gas and asks no questions, which is a rare virtue in a lamp or a friend.

objectives.text: Thin the blightfly clouds over the warm ground
Trap a jar of blightflies

famine_fields_blightfly_01.note: The blightflies breed only over the warm patches -- the live signal. A map made of flies, for anyone grim enough to read one. Marioara's husband kept bees where these things now boil. She laughed about it, then went quiet, which was his way and has become hers.

famine_fields_blightfly_02.title: Marsh-Gas and Old Grudges
famine_fields_blightfly_02.summary: Marioara has turned mapping the warm ground into a daily habit and a small revenge -- the more warm patches she can name, the less room the quiet has to hide. She sends you out each day to burn back the flies and mark the fresh warm ground.
famine_fields_blightfly_02.offer: Turns out a widow with a grudge and a jar of flies is more use than a whole council of clerks. Every day I send someone to burn the blightflies off the warm patches and mark where they were, and every day the map of the wrong ground gets truer. Won't stop it, mind. But a thing you can name and put on a map is a thing that's stopped sneaking. Go burn today's clouds and mark the new warm ground at the field's low end. Come back and I'll stand you tea and complaints.
famine_fields_blightfly_02.progress: Burn back today's blightfly clouds and reach the fresh warm ground at the field's low end to mark it.
famine_fields_blightfly_02.complete: Marked and mapped. The wrong ground grows, but slower now that we're watching it grow, or so I tell myself over the tea. Small revenge, mapping a thing that means to eat you. My Gherghe would've called it stubborn. I call it Tuesday. Here's your coin and my thanks, dear, same as tomorrow. The flies don't stop coming. Neither do I.

objectives.text: Burn back today's blightfly clouds

objectives.label: the field's low end
objectives.text: Mark the fresh warm ground

famine_fields_blightfly_02.note: Marioara maps the warm ground daily, burning the flies and marking the wrong earth -- a widow's small revenge on a thing that means to eat her. It does not stop the spread. It stops the spread from sneaking. She calls it Tuesday. Her husband would have called it stubborn.

famine_fields_smith_reaver_01.title: Iron the Hunger Fears
famine_fields_smith_reaver_01.summary: Despina wants to forge one proper weapon for the field's defence -- a reaver keened with salt and fire-hardened -- but the temper needs blackglass from the quarry's deepest cut, where the husks den thickest.
famine_fields_smith_reaver_01.offer: Strikers hold the doors, but a door isn't the only thing that fails. Sooner or later something comes that a brand won't turn, and I'll want one honest edge in this village that a hunger can feel. A reaver, salt-keened, fire-drawn -- the old way, before the ore stopped. For the temper I need blackglass off the quarry's deep cut, where the husks den three-deep and hungry. Clear them and cut me the glass. I'll draw you a blade the walking-hungry will actually flinch from. That's not nothing out here.
famine_fields_smith_reaver_01.progress: Cut blackglass from the quarry's deep cut, clearing the husks that den there.
famine_fields_smith_reaver_01.complete: Feel the balance of that. Salt worked into the quench, edge drawn slow over honest fire -- the two things the hunger flees, made into one thing it fears. My father would say a blade's a poor answer to a famine and he'd be right and he'd forge it anyway, because a poor answer held in your hand beats a good one you're too starved to reach. It's yours. A blackglass razor, keened for the walking dead. Mind the edge. Mind the door more.

objectives.text: Cut blackglass from the quarry's deep cut
Clear the husks denning in the deep cut

famine_fields_smith_reaver_01.note: Despina forged a reaver quenched in salt and drawn over fire -- the two things the hunger flees, made one thing it fears. A poor answer to a famine, she admitted, and forged it anyway. A poor answer held in the hand beats a good one you are too starved to reach.

famine_fields_merchant_relief_01.title: The Relief That Never Came
famine_fields_merchant_relief_01.summary: A relief cart from the western capital was sent to the fields and never arrived. Ilinca wants its ledger recovered from the wreck on the east road -- to learn whether it was robbed, or turned back, or simply written down as delivered while the field went hungry.
famine_fields_merchant_relief_01.offer: The capital sent us relief. A cart, grain and salt-fish, off the Queen's own stores -- I saw the writ myself. It never came. And here's the thing that keeps me up: the capital's ledger says it did. Marked delivered, signed, filed. So either it was taken on the road, or it was never loaded and someone in a warm office wrote 'delivered' over an empty field. Find the wreck on the east road and bring me its cart-ledger. I want to know which kind of lie starved us -- the robber's or the clerk's.
famine_fields_merchant_relief_01.progress: The relief cart's wreck lies on the east road. Recover its ledger, past whatever haunts the wreck.
famine_fields_merchant_relief_01.complete: The clerk's lie, then. The cart was never robbed -- it was never loaded. Empty axles, a real ledger marked 'delivered' in a hand that never left the capital. Someone in a dry office signed our winter away and filed it and went to supper. That's how the west dies, not to armies but to paperwork -- a famine you can survive, a false entry you can't argue with. The stone taught nobody this. It just found people already willing. Take the ledger-craft. Learn to read the lie faster than it's written.

objectives.label: the relief-cart wreck
objectives.text: Reach the wreck on the east road
Recover the cart-ledger
Clear the wreck of straw-things

famine_fields_merchant_relief_01.note: The relief cart was never robbed -- it was never loaded, and marked delivered anyway by a hand that never left the capital. The west dies not to armies but to paperwork: a famine you can survive, a false entry you cannot argue with. The stone taught no one this cruelty. It found them already willing.

famine_fields_guard_patrol_01.title: The Straw-Things at the Well
famine_fields_guard_patrol_01.summary: The village's one clean well is the last honest water for leagues, and the scarecrow-wights gather at it nightly, tilting toward the reading-stone. Varvara needs the well kept clear each day, and the copper-tainted ones capped and watched.
famine_fields_guard_patrol_01.offer: One clean well left, and it's the difference between a village and a graveyard. The straw-things gather at it come dark -- not drinking, they don't drink, just standing round it facing the oak, and I don't like what standing round a well means when the wells are going copper one by one. Clear them off it for me, and check the old wells at the field-edge -- if another's gone copper, we need to know before someone drinks it. Do it today. Do it every day. That's the job now.
famine_fields_guard_patrol_01.progress: Clear the scarecrows off the clean well, then check the old wells at the field-edge for copper.
famine_fields_guard_patrol_01.complete: Clean well stays clean another day, and the field-edge wells are -- two more gone copper, which is bad, but knowing is better than a child drinking it. This is the whole of soldiering out here: not battles, just standing between people and the slow wrong thing, dawn to dark, and counting the wells. Take your coin. Come tomorrow. The straw-things will be back at the well and so will I, and one of us has to be stubborn about it.

objectives.text: Clear the scarecrows off the clean well

objectives.label: the field-edge wells
objectives.text: Check the old wells for copper

famine_fields_guard_patrol_01.note: Two more wells gone copper, and the one clean well held for another day. Soldiering in the fields is not battles -- it is standing between people and the slow wrong thing, dawn to dark, counting the wells. One of them has to be stubborn about it. Varvara has decided it is her.

famine_fields_chronicler_book_01.title: A True Accounting of the Want
famine_fields_chronicler_book_01.summary: Zenobia is writing the field's famine down before the survivors forget the order of it, and she needs the parish records recovered from three ruined holdings -- so that when this happens again, someone will have written that it was made, not fallen.
famine_fields_chronicler_book_01.offer: Memory is the one thing the stone can't forge, so I write. A true accounting of this want -- who starved, in what order, and the granary full the whole time -- so that when it comes for the next field, and it will, someone can hand them a book that says: this was made. You did not deserve it. Look for the granary. I need the old parish records to get the order right. They're in three ruined holdings across the field. Bring them, and mind the husks that nest in paper. Even the dead want something to read.
famine_fields_chronicler_book_01.progress: Recover the parish records from the three ruined holdings, clearing the husks nesting in them.
famine_fields_chronicler_book_01.complete: All three, water-stained but legible. Now I have the order -- and it's worse written down than remembered. House by house, eldest first, the strong left to watch the weak go, precisely the order to break a village's will to share. Nobody starves that tidily by accident. I'll finish the book. It won't feed anyone. But a people who know they were farmed are harder to farm twice. Take the method -- how to read a made thing from a fallen one. It's the only weapon I've ever forged.

objectives.text: Recover the parish records from the ruins
Clear the husks nesting in the records

famine_fields_chronicler_book_01.note: Zenobia is writing the famine down: who starved, in what order, the granary full the while. The order was worse than remembered -- eldest first, the strong left to watch, precisely the order to break a village's will to share. A people who know they were farmed are harder to farm twice. It is the only weapon she has ever forged.

famine_fields_innkeeper_road_01.title: Word North to Riverfork
famine_fields_innkeeper_road_01.summary: Casandra wants the field's hard-won lessons carried on to the next village down the water -- keep your yeast, salt your dead, feed the hungry a full table -- and a hot meal readied for whoever walks in off the Riverfork road cold and frightened.
famine_fields_innkeeper_road_01.offer: We learned this the hard way, and I'll be damned if the next field down the water learns it the same way. Salt your dead, keep your yeast, and when the hungry come, out-feed them -- that's the whole of it, and it's worth walking north to say. Take a meal-tin and my note to the folk at Riverfork, and reach the north ford where the road picks up the river. Whoever you meet coming the other way, cold and looking east -- sit them down and feed them first. Talk after. Always feed first.
famine_fields_innkeeper_road_01.progress: Carry Casandra's meal-tin and note north to the ford where the road meets the Riverfork water.
famine_fields_innkeeper_road_01.complete: You made the ford, and you fed the ones you met -- I can see it on you, that's a good tiredness. So the word goes on down the water: keep your yeast, salt your dead, feed the hungry. It's not much against a thing that engineers famines. But it's a warm hearth passed hand to hand faster than the cold can follow, and that's how the west has always won -- by outlasting, by feeding, by refusing to learn the lesson the stone keeps teaching. Go on. The road north is yours now.

objectives.label: the north ford
objectives.text: Carry the word to the Riverfork road

famine_fields_innkeeper_road_01.note: Casandra sent the field's hard lesson north down the water: keep your yeast, salt your dead, feed the hungry first. Small defences against a thing that engineers famines. But warmth passed hand to hand travels faster than the cold, and that is how the west has always won -- by refusing to learn the lesson the stone keeps teaching.

famine_fields_commoner_ratcatch_01.title: The Rats That Won't Leave
famine_fields_commoner_ratcatch_01.summary: The plague-rats have grown bold and strange in the empty holdings -- they nest in the full granary itself and will not flee fire the way rats should. Marioara wants them cleared and a nest checked, mostly so she can stop hearing them in the walls at night.
famine_fields_commoner_ratcatch_01.offer: Rats, dear, and don't tell me it's nothing -- I know rats, my Gherghe fought them off his hives for thirty years. These are wrong. Fat as cats in a starving field, which makes no sense unless they're eating what we're told isn't there. And they won't run from a brand. A rat that won't run from fire has forgotten it's a rat. Clear the nests in the ruined holdings for me. I want to sleep without the scratching, and I want to know what a rat's grown fat on in a famine. Nothing good, I'd wager.
famine_fields_commoner_ratcatch_01.progress: Clear the plague-rat nests in the ruined holdings. Bold as they've grown, mind the brand won't scare them.
