famine_fields_commoner_ratcatch_01.complete: Cleared, thank the saints, and my walls are quiet. And you saw where they nested -- in the granary stores, the ones we're told are empty. Fat rats in an empty granary. Even the vermin know it's a lie. There's a bleak comfort in that, dear -- when the rats are better informed than the parish, at least somebody's keeping honest books, even if it's the rats. Take the culture-jar. Grew it off the good rot in the cellar. It mends more than it should.

objectives.text: Clear the plague-rat nests in the ruins

famine_fields_commoner_ratcatch_01.note: The rats grew fat in a famine because they nested in the granary stores the parish swears are empty. Even the vermin know the lie. A bleak comfort, Marioara said, when the rats keep more honest books than the clerks. She sleeps better for the quiet walls, and worse for the reason they went quiet.

famine_fields_guard_husk_01.title: The Ones Who Fled East
famine_fields_guard_husk_01.summary: The famine-husks that shamble the east road were villagers once -- the fleeing who did not make it. Smaranda cannot bury them all, but she can lay to rest the ones close enough to know, and wants their keepsakes recovered so a name can go on a marker.
famine_fields_guard_husk_01.offer: The husks on the east road were ours. The ones who fled when the hunger came, who walked east looking for bread and got hollowed on the way -- the fleeing who didn't make it. I knew some of their faces before they went straw and shadow. I can't bury them all. But the ones near enough, I can lay down proper and put a name on. Go out on the east road and put the poor damned things to rest, and bring back any keepsake still on them. A cord, a token, anything. A name is the last thing I can give them.
famine_fields_guard_husk_01.progress: Lay the famine-husks to rest on the east road, and recover any keepsakes still on them.
famine_fields_guard_husk_01.complete: You brought back cords, a token, a child's cord -- gods, a child's. So they carried something to the end, even hollowed, even walking east with nothing left in them but the stone's borrowed hunger. That's the mercy in it, if there is one: the keepsake outlasts the person, and a keepsake with a name on it is a person the stone didn't fully collect. I'll put them in my rows tonight, named. Take the keepsake-cord. The living should carry what the dead couldn't keep.

objectives.text: Lay the famine-husks to rest on the east road
Recover keepsakes from the fallen

famine_fields_guard_husk_01.note: The husks on the east road were the fleeing who did not make it -- villagers hollowed to the stone's borrowed hunger. Smaranda laid the near ones down and named them from their keepsakes. A keepsake with a name is a person the stone did not fully collect. Even a child carried something to the end.

angel_wings_full_granary_01.title: The Full Granary
angel_wings_full_granary_01.summary: A western village starves within a day's walk of the capital, and its own granary stands full. Quartermaster Paraschiva counts grain the way other people count breaths, and her count does not balance. Go and see what a full barn beside empty tables can mean.
angel_wings_full_granary_01.offer: I keep the ledger of every sack that moves through this city, and there is a village east of here — Two-Furrow — that has buried nine this month for want of bread. And its granary is full. I checked the tallies thrice. Nobody stole the grain. Nobody rationed it. They simply... stopped eating it, house by house, in order. Walk out there. Stand in the barn. Tell me I am wrong to be afraid of a full barn.
angel_wings_full_granary_01.progress: Two-Furrow lies east along the river road, past the mill-weir. The granary is the tall roof you cannot miss. Go and stand in it.
angel_wings_full_granary_01.complete: Full. You saw it full. Grain to the rafters and a graveyard fresh-turned and no thief between them. That is not famine, that is a man reading down a list and skipping the ones he means to keep. Somebody engineered this hunger where there was bread — so that walking east and touching would feel like the only door left open. Take the warding. I will not sleep, but I would rather you didn't either.

objectives.label: the Two-Furrow granary
objectives.text: Stand in the full granary at Two-Furrow
Ask a Two-Furrow survivor what happened

angel_wings_full_granary_01.note: The granary was full the whole time. The hunger came anyway, house by house, in order — the way a hand moves down a page. Paraschiva has stopped calling it famine. She has not yet decided what to call it instead.

angel_wings_lead_vault_01.title: The Room Built Around Not
angel_wings_lead_vault_01.summary: The Queen keeps one room in the northwest that the city does not name aloud, built stone by stone around a power it refuses to use. The clerk who guards it has begun skipping meals to feel clearer. Envoy Tinca needs a stranger's eyes on the thing before the court's eyes find it first.
angel_wings_lead_vault_01.offer: I speak for the Queen, and the Queen speaks little, so hear this once. There is a room in the northwest of the city. You will feel it is important and you will want to know why. Refuse that wanting — it is the whole discipline of the place. The boy who guards the door has lately stopped eating. Says the fasting makes him 'clearer.' I need someone with no stake in the court to walk to that door, look at the boy, and come tell me plainly whether he is still a boy. Do not ask what is behind the door. That question has cost cleverer people than you.
angel_wings_lead_vault_01.progress: The unnamed room sits in the far northwest of the capital, past the granary quarter. Go to the door. Look at the warden. Ask him nothing about the room.
angel_wings_lead_vault_01.complete: Skipping meals to feel clearer. Yes. That is the word, isn't it — clearer. It is not fasting; it is listening, and the thing he is listening to is the thing this whole room was built to keep quiet. The Queen keeps a stone in a lead box the way you keep a hand over a candle — not to hold the fire, only to stop it lighting the drapes. Now the box has begun to hum, and the boy hears it. Tell no one. I will move him myself, gently, before the court smells that the fragment is awake.

objectives.label: the unnamed room
objectives.text: Go to the door in the northwest
Speak with the clerk who guards the door
Report plainly to Envoy Tinca

angel_wings_lead_vault_01.note: The fragment has begun to hum a single note, and the boy at the door has begun to hear it. Tinca will move him gently, and quickly, and never tell him why. The one leader who can resist can only resist by keeping the thing that corrupts. This is the rarest agenda on the board, and it is losing.

angel_wings_clerk_canary_02.title: The Canary or the Cage
angel_wings_clerk_canary_02.summary: The clerk who hears the box is the only warning the West has that the fragment has woken. Moving him saves a boy and blinds a queen; leaving him listening keeps the watch and slowly unmakes him. Tinca cannot choose. She asks a stranger to carry the counteragent, and the choosing, to the door.
angel_wings_clerk_canary_02.offer: I told you I would move the boy gently. I lied — not to you, to myself. If I move him, we lose our only ear on the fragment, and the day it fully wakes we will learn too late, in the ordinary way, by the wells going copper and the court going east. If I leave him, he gets clearer and clearer until there is nothing left to be clear. The Queen builds rooms around not using power. She never told me how to look at a boy and use him for a bell. Take this counteragent to him. It will quiet the note in his ears — if he will drink it. He may not want to. That, saints help me, is the choice I am handing you.
angel_wings_clerk_canary_02.progress: Return to the clerk at the northwest door with the counteragent. What he becomes after is between the two of you.
angel_wings_clerk_canary_02.complete: He drank it. Or he didn't — you'll carry which, not me, and I am coward enough to be grateful for that. Either the West has a boy again and no warning bell, or it has a bell that used to be a boy. There is no third door; the Queen would tell you the same, and she would not soften it. Keep the token. It is a name someone chose to keep. In this city that is the only kind of victory we sell.

objectives.label: the northwest door
objectives.text: Bring the counteragent to the clerk
Offer the clerk the counteragent — and the choice

angel_wings_clerk_canary_02.note: Either a boy was saved and a watch was blinded, or a watch was kept and a boy was spent. Tinca did not want to know which, and the stranger did not tell her. Somewhere a lead box hums its single note into an ear that may or may not still be listening. The choice is logged. It always is.

angel_wings_maren_handprints_01.title: Small Hands on the Wall
angel_wings_maren_handprints_01.summary: At Maren's orphanage the children press their hands in chalk along the east wall — a wall of small proofs that they were here, and warm, and fed. Zoe is out of chalk before the new ones arrive. Fetch her a fresh block from the merchant, and let the wall grow.
angel_wings_maren_handprints_01.offer: Every child who comes to us gets their hand on the wall the first day — chalk, pressed flat, name underneath if they've got their letters. It's the first thing that's ever been theirs, some of them. I've run dry, and three new little ones came in off the Two-Furrow road last night with eyes like winter. I want their hands up before they think they're not staying. Anca at the market keeps good chalk. Would you? It's a small thing. Small things are the whole trade here.
angel_wings_maren_handprints_01.progress: Anca the merchant keeps chalk among her wares near the market cross. Bring a block back to the orphanage wall.
angel_wings_maren_handprints_01.complete: There. Three new prints, still wet, names underneath in my worst hand. They stood on their toes to reach where the older ones are. That's the trick of it — you put your hand up high so next year you can see how you've grown, so you know you had a next year at all. Bless you. Take the cord — one of ours plaited it. It holds a keepsake close. Everything good here is small and holds close.

objectives.text: Buy a block of chalk from Anca

objectives.label: the orphanage east wall
objectives.text: Bring the chalk to the orphanage wall

angel_wings_maren_handprints_01.note: Three new handprints went up on the east wall, small and high and proud. On that same wall, lower down and older, one print is faintly copper and warm to the touch. No one has noticed yet. Zoe pressed her palm beside it once, for luck, and drew it back without knowing why.

angel_wings_maren_trout_02.title: Every Fish But One
angel_wings_maren_trout_02.summary: The orphanage serves every fish the river gives — perch, bream, eel — and never once a trout, and no one still living will say why. Old Cott by the weir has a good haul and bad knees. Carry his catch up to Zoe's kitchen, and mind which fish you leave out.
angel_wings_maren_trout_02.offer: Cott's pulled a fine haul off the weir and can't carry it up the hill on those knees of his — go fetch it for the pot, will you? The little ones eat tonight regardless of whose war is winning. One thing, and don't laugh: leave the trout in the river. We serve every fish God made but that one. It's an old rule. The woman who made it isn't here to explain it, and the man who could is Cott, and Cott won't. But we keep it. Some rules you keep because keeping them is the point.
angel_wings_maren_trout_02.progress: Old Cott fishes the weir at the river's bend, southeast of the market. Carry his catch up to the orphanage kitchen — every fish but the trout.
angel_wings_maren_trout_02.complete: You left the trout. Good. I watched to see if you'd ask why, and you didn't, and that's the second kindness. The stew's on, and it's loud in there, and for one supper nobody's listening to anything but the ladle. Cott says tell you the river's running clean this week. He always says that. It's how he says he's still alive. Take the poultice — a full belly and a whole skin, that's the whole of what we can give.

objectives.text: Take Old Cott's catch at the weir

objectives.label: the orphanage kitchen
objectives.text: Carry the catch up — every fish but the trout

angel_wings_maren_trout_02.note: The pot was full and loud and warm, and there was no trout in it, and no one asked. Somewhere a woman named Maren still can't cook the fish that was the last kind thing an old man ever gave her. The rule outlived the reason. In Angel Wings, that is how the small warm things are kept — carried past the point where anyone remembers the weight.

angel_wings_pall_fogline_01.title: What the Widow Smells
angel_wings_pall_fogline_01.summary: Safta lost her husband to the marks and gained, in the trade, a nose for wrongness — she can smell a well go copper from a mile off, and wishes she couldn't. Three farmsteads on the fog-line have stopped answering. Walk her early-warning line and read the wells with the blue draught.
angel_wings_pall_fogline_01.offer: I run the human watch along the fog-line — the wet edge west of the city where the wrongness always arrives first. I lost Petru to the marks and got this in exchange: I can smell a well turn copper before the bucket's up. It's not a gift. Three holdings out on the line have gone quiet. Take this reading-draught — a drop on your tongue and your eyes will read what water's carrying. Blue is old death, we can bury that. Anything that shifts toward orange, you come back at a run and you don't look at it twice.
angel_wings_pall_fogline_01.progress: The fog-line farmsteads lie west of the capital, past the last chimneys. Use the blue draught to read the three silent wells.
angel_wings_pall_fogline_01.complete: Blue at the first two — old death, drowned kin, grief we know how to bury. The third. You saw it. It didn't hold a color; it slid, warm-edged, toward the orange, and slid back, the way a thing does when it's not sure yet whether it's awake. That's a well learning a new taste. I'll mark the map and pull the families in tonight. Petru used to say the land tells you everything if you'll pay its price. I've paid. Here — a jar of marshlight. Carry your own small honest fire out there. Don't trust the ground's.

objectives.label: the fog-line farmsteads
objectives.text: Read the three silent wells
Report the colors to Safta

angel_wings_pall_fogline_01.note: Two wells read blue and honest. The third would not hold a color — it slid toward orange and back, warm-edged, a well not yet certain it was awake. Safta pulled the families in and marked her map. She has smelled this before. She wishes, every hour of her watch, that she could not.

angel_wings_pall_map_02.title: The Border of the Map
angel_wings_pall_map_02.summary: Safta's watch is only as good as her map, and her map is going wrong at the edges — margins she swears she drew clean now carry marks in a hand that isn't hers. She needs a second walker to check three border-stones against the parchment, and tell her whether the land is moving or she is.
angel_wings_pall_map_02.offer: A watch is a map and a nose, and I trust the nose. The map's the trouble. Look here — the margin. I ruled that border myself, forty years a scout's widow, and there are marks in it now I did not make, small and even and growing. Either someone's editing my map in the night, or the land's edge is editing itself and the parchment's only catching up. Walk to the three border-stones I've circled. Read what's cut on them against what's drawn here. Then tell me which of us has gone strange — the map, the land, or me.
angel_wings_pall_map_02.progress: Three border-stones sit at the western margin of the fog-line, each circled on Safta's map. Check the cut stone against the drawn border.
angel_wings_pall_map_02.complete: The stones match the map. The map does not match the map I drew. So it's not the land moving and it's not me — it's the margin filling in, line by even line, the way that crossroads stone up north is said to be getting shorter. Something is writing at the edges of what we've counted. I'll keep the map. A wrong map you can read is worth more than a right one you can't. Take the border — the marked one. When yours starts filling in, you'll know to run.

objectives.label: the western border-stones
objectives.text: Check the three border-stones against the map
Tell Safta which one has gone strange

angel_wings_pall_map_02.note: The stones matched the map; the map no longer matched the hand that drew it. Something writes at the edges of the counted world, line by patient line. Safta keeps the wrong map anyway. A wrong map you can read, she says, beats a right one you can't — and she has stopped drawing new margins altogether.

angel_wings_rat_cellars_01.title: Under the Grain-Floors
angel_wings_rat_cellars_01.summary: A capital of grain is a capital of rats, and the storehouse cellars are boiling with them before the winter tally. Paraschiva can lose grain to a hundred honest causes, but not to vermin on her own watch. Thin the swarms under the grain-floors.
angel_wings_rat_cellars_01.offer: Every sack I save is a child through the winter — that's the arithmetic, and it's the only war I'm winning, so I fight it sack by sack. And I am losing sacks to rats. The cellars under the west storehouses are alive with them, whole grey rivers of the things, and my clerks won't go down with a broom, the cowards, bless them. You look like you've stepped on worse. Clear the swarms before the tally. Grain saved is the whole point of me.
angel_wings_rat_cellars_01.progress: The swarms breed in the storehouse cellars beneath the western grain-floors. Go down and thin them out.
angel_wings_rat_cellars_01.complete: Listen to that quiet. That's the sound of a winter's grain not being eaten by anything with a tail. You've fed a village and you'll never meet them — that's the best kind of good deed, the kind nobody thanks you for. I'll thank you. Here's coin, and a warding for the road. A quartermaster's blessing is a full sack and a dry roof. I've precious few of either to spare, so take it as meant.

objectives.text: Thin the swarms in the grain cellars

angel_wings_rat_cellars_01.note: The cellars fell quiet and a winter's grain went uneaten by anything with a tail. Paraschiva marked the sacks saved in her private ledger, beside the count of children fed. It is the only war she is winning, she says. She fights it a sack at a time, and she means to keep winning it, whatever the wells decide to taste like.

angel_wings_rat_plague_02.title: The Grey Rivers
angel_wings_rat_plague_02.summary: The rats are not just many this year; they run wrong — east, all of them, in the same direction, like water tilted. Safta wants the swarms culled and their strangeness confirmed. She has learned to distrust anything in Angel Wings that moves as if it were being called.
angel_wings_rat_plague_02.offer: I'll tell you what my nose tells me and you can tell me I'm an old fool. The rats run east. Not scatter — east, every swarm, dawn and dusk, like the whole cellar-floor's been tilted toward the sunrise. Vermin don't have a heading. These have a heading. Cull them along the east storehouses and watch which way the last ones bolt when there's nothing left to bolt from. I need to know if even the rats have started listening. Saints, listen to me. But go, and watch.
angel_wings_rat_plague_02.progress: The east-running swarms gather by the storehouses along the eastern grain-floors. Cull them and watch which way the survivors run.
angel_wings_rat_plague_02.complete: East. You watched, and even with the swarm broken and nothing chasing, the last of them turned east and ran, toward the touch, the way the hungering do, the way the pilgrims do. It's in the rats now. When the small crawling things start walking toward the same thing your neighbors do, you don't need a scholar to read it for you. Take the poultice, and take the warning under it: whatever's calling east isn't particular about what answers. That's the frightening part. It'll take a rat.

objectives.text: Cull the east-running swarms
Confirm the strangeness with Safta

angel_wings_rat_plague_02.note: The swarms broke and the survivors ran east, toward nothing, toward the touch — the way the hungering walk and the pilgrims stand. The compulsion is not particular; it will take a rat. Safta wrote it in her watch-book and underlined it once. When the crawling things heed the call, she says, the reading is done and only the running is left.

angel_wings_hungering_neighbor_01.title: The Neighbor Who Listens
angel_wings_hungering_neighbor_01.summary: A man named Borys stopped at his own field-gate three days ago, turned east, and has not moved since — not eaten, not spoken, only tilted toward the treeline, listening. His wife will not have him called mad. Draga needs someone gentle to go to him, and to understand there may be no bringing him back.
angel_wings_hungering_neighbor_01.offer: I've buried men and I've jailed men and I never learned the trick of this. Borys — good farmer, three girls, never a wrong word — stopped at his gate three days back, turned to face the east wood, and stood. Won't eat. Won't hear his wife. Just... tilts, like a dog toward a whistle none of us can hear. She won't let me call it madness and she's right, it isn't. It's the other thing. Go and stand with him. Speak his name. See if there's a Borys still in there to answer. Be gentle. He was a gentle man.
angel_wings_hungering_neighbor_01.progress: Borys stands at his field-gate on the eastern edge of the city, facing the treeline. Go to him. Speak his name.
angel_wings_hungering_neighbor_01.complete: You said his name and he didn't turn. Course he didn't. They don't, once they've gone that far — they're not deaf, they're just listening to something that's louder than a name. His wife will sit with him till the end because that's what love does when it can't do anything else. We can't jail a symptom and we can't cure a listening. All we can do is not join him. Take the charm. It's meant to keep a mind incurious. Wear it when you walk the east gate. Especially then.

objectives.label: Borys's field-gate
objectives.text: Go to Borys at the eastern gate
Sit with Borys's wife

angel_wings_hungering_neighbor_01.note: Borys did not turn when his name was spoken. He is not deaf; he is only listening to something louder than a name. His wife will sit with him to the end, because that is what love does when it can do nothing else. The scariest enemy in Angel Wings, they say, is a neighbor who has started listening. His gate is still open. No one has closed it.

angel_wings_empty_leadbox_01.title: The Empty Box
angel_wings_empty_leadbox_01.summary: In a burned farmstead south of the city, someone found a lead box — cheaply made, badly sealed, and empty, with scratch-marks on the inside. A peasant tried to copy the Queen's discipline and failed at it. Ioana keeps the record of the West's small tragedies; she wants this one seen and set down true.
angel_wings_empty_leadbox_01.offer: I keep the chronicle, which in this city means I keep the failures nobody else can bear to write down. There's a burned holding south of here — the Achard place, gone last spring. In the ashes they found a lead box. Cheap lead, poured wrong, hinge like a child made it. Empty. And scratched, on the inside. Someone out there heard the Queen keeps her fragment in lead and thought: I could do that. I could hold my own bad luck in a box and study it and be strong. Go and look at what that costs a poor man. Then come and help me write it true. Someone should.
angel_wings_empty_leadbox_01.progress: The burned Achard farmstead lies south of the capital. Find the empty lead box in the ashes and read the scratches inside.
angel_wings_empty_leadbox_01.complete: Scratch-marks on the inside. On the inside. You don't scratch the inside of a box unless you're holding what's in it up against your own skin to feel it, night after night, thinking discipline is a thing a hungry man can just decide to have. The Queen's box is a gag on a stone. His was a poor man's copy of a queen's terrible patience, and it ate him. I'll set it down plain — no moral, no blame, just: he tried her trick and had none of her walls. That's the whole tragedy of the West in one cheap box.

objectives.label: the burned Achard farmstead
objectives.text: Find the empty lead box in the ashes
Help Ioana set the record down true

angel_wings_empty_leadbox_01.note: The box was cheap lead, poured wrong, and scratched on the inside — a poor man's copy of a queen's terrible patience, held bare against a skin that had no walls behind it. He tried her discipline and it ate him. Ioana set it down with no moral and no blame, only the fact of it, which was worse. Lead is a gag, she wrote. It only works on the disciplined. The rest it simply muffles while it works.

angel_wings_helva_ledger_01.title: The Only War She's Winning
angel_wings_helva_ledger_01.summary: Paraschiva keeps a private ledger apart from the grain-tallies: every child fed at the orphanage, by name. A page has gone missing and she is quietly frantic. Recover it from the market district before the wind or a careless boot finishes what a gust started.
angel_wings_helva_ledger_01.offer: There's a ledger I keep that isn't the city's business — every child fed at Maren's house, by name, the day they came, the day they left fed. I call it the only war I'm winning, and I mean it, it's the one page of my work that's all profit and no cost. A leaf of it blew off my table and out the market window this morning and I have been sick over it since. Forty names. Find it before it's mud or kindling. I can lose grain to a hundred honest thieves. I cannot lose forty children to a gust of wind.
angel_wings_helva_ledger_01.progress: The missing ledger-page blew loose somewhere in the market district. Recover it before it's lost.
angel_wings_helva_ledger_01.complete: Forty names, mud on the corner, and all of them still legible. Thank the saints and thank you. You'll think it's foolish, a grown woman frantic over one page. But grain feeds a child once. The page means someone wrote the child down — meant them, kept them, counted them as worth the ink. In a world that's learning to file people cold, keeping a warm list is its own small mutiny. Take the coin. And don't tell Tinca I cried over stationery.

objectives.label: the market district
objectives.text: Recover the missing ledger-page
Return the page to Paraschiva

angel_wings_helva_ledger_01.note: Forty names came home muddy and whole. Grain feeds a child once, Paraschiva said; the page means someone wrote the child down, meant them, counted them worth the ink. In a world learning to file people cold, a warm list is a small mutiny. She keeps hers in a drawer now, weighted with a stone. Not that kind of stone. She was very clear about that.

angel_wings_blacksmith_leadgloves_01.title: Gloves of Dead Lead
angel_wings_blacksmith_leadgloves_01.summary: Zamfira has an order she doesn't understand and doesn't like: lead-lined gloves, a queen's commission, no questions attached. She needs good bog-lead she can't get through the choked river road. Fetch it from the merchant, and don't ask her what hands they're for.
angel_wings_blacksmith_leadgloves_01.offer: I've a commission I'd rather not have. Lead-lined gloves — heavy, dead, useless for any honest work I can think of. Comes down from the court with coin enough that I don't get to ask why, and a note that says handle only closed. Handle what closed? I don't want to know. Anca had bog-lead in from the fens before the road choked; go buy it off her before it's spoken for. You bring the lead, I'll pour the gloves, and neither of us will think too hard about whose hands go dead-quiet inside them.
angel_wings_blacksmith_leadgloves_01.progress: Anca the merchant holds bog-lead from the fens. Bring it to Zamfira's forge for the pour.
angel_wings_blacksmith_leadgloves_01.complete: Good lead. Dead as it ought to be — you can feel it eat the ring of the anvil when I set it down. I'll pour the gloves tonight and send them up to the court and never learn whose hands they're gagging. Lead's honest that way; it doesn't listen and it won't let a thing be heard through it. That's the whole of what it's good for. Take this striker — proper steel, sings when you strike it. After pouring the dead metal I needed to make something that still rang.

objectives.text: Buy bog-lead from Anca

objectives.label: Zamfira's forge
objectives.text: Bring the lead to the forge

angel_wings_blacksmith_leadgloves_01.note: The gloves were poured, dead-quiet, and sent up to a court that had not explained them. Lead eats the ring of the anvil, Zamfira said; it does not listen and it lets nothing be heard through it. She did not ask whose hands they gagged. After the pour she forged herself a striker that still rang — a small loud honest thing, to remember the difference by.

angel_wings_inn_lastwarm_01.title: The Last Warm Table
angel_wings_inn_lastwarm_01.summary: Otilia keeps the biggest hearth in the West and a rule to match it: no one goes east from her door on an empty stomach. Refugees are drifting in off the Two-Furrow road with nothing, and her stew-pot needs meat. Bring her something for the pot and fill the last warm table before the road takes another one.
angel_wings_inn_lastwarm_01.offer: I run the loudest room in Angel Wings and I mean to keep it loud. There's folk coming in off the eastern roads with the winter already in their faces, and I've a rule older than my grey hairs: nobody leaves my door hungry, and God help me, nobody leaves it east. My pot's thin, though — all these mouths. Clear the vermin out of my stores so I've something to cook, and we'll fill every bench tonight. A full room is a room that isn't listening to the dark. That's the whole of my trade.
angel_wings_inn_lastwarm_01.progress: Otilia needs her stores cleared before she can fill the pot. Deal with the vermin, then bring it to a boil.
angel_wings_inn_lastwarm_01.complete: Now THAT is a pot. Smell it — that's the smell of a room that's going to be too warm and too loud and too full to hear a single thing on the road tonight. That's the war I fight, traveler, and it's the same one Paraschiva fights with her grain and Zoe fights with her chalk. We keep the room warm. We keep them looking at each other and not at the east. Sit. Eat. You've earned a bench. The saints know the road hasn't earned you.

objectives.text: Clear vermin from Otilia's stores
Bring the pot to a boil with Otilia

angel_wings_inn_lastwarm_01.note: The room was too warm and too loud and too full to hear anything on the road, and that was the point of it. Otilia keeps the West's biggest hearth and its oldest rule: no one leaves hungry, and no one leaves east. She fights the same war Paraschiva fights with grain and Zoe with chalk — the war to keep people looking at each other, and not at the dark past the door.

angel_wings_merchant_debt_01.title: Sold to Blestem
angel_wings_merchant_debt_01.summary: A starving hamlet on the fog-line has begun selling what it knows to Blestem's ledger-men to eat through winter — small truths, harmless-seeming, that add up in an eastern file. Anca trades honestly and hates to watch it. She wants a stranger to carry a better offer out before the hamlet sells the one thing it shouldn't.
angel_wings_merchant_debt_01.offer: I'll speak plain, being a merchant and knowing the price of everything. There's a hamlet west of here — Reedy Cross — so hungry they've started selling to Blestem. Not grain; they've no grain. They're selling what they know. Who guards what. Which road's watched. Small coins of truth, and every one of them ends up filed in the East against the day it's worth spending. I'll not have decent folk priced into betraying their own gate. Carry them my offer — honest goods for honest work — before some clerk's coin buys the one word that gets them all killed.
angel_wings_merchant_debt_01.progress: Reedy Cross sits on the fog-line, west of the market. Carry Anca's counter-offer before Blestem's price finds the wrong tongue.
angel_wings_merchant_debt_01.complete: They took the deal. Barely. A man that hungry does the sum in front of you — my honest coin against Blestem's, and mine's smaller, always is, because I'm not buying anything worth killing over. That's the whole cruelty of it: the East pays more because the East is buying the thing that gets you sacked. Decent people, priced into indecency, one winter at a time. That's the lever, traveler. Not swords. Hunger and arithmetic. Take the warding. Keep your own gate's secrets close.

objectives.label: Reedy Cross
objectives.text: Carry Anca's offer to Reedy Cross
Make the offer to the hamlet's elder

angel_wings_merchant_debt_01.note: Reedy Cross took the honest coin, barely — a hungry man does the sum in front of you, and the East always pays more, because the East is buying the word that gets you sacked. Decent people priced into indecency, one winter at a time. Anca has seen the lever now, and it is not swords. It is hunger and arithmetic, and it never runs short of either.

angel_wings_chronicler_notes_01.title: The Present, Kept Warm
angel_wings_chronicler_notes_01.summary: Ioana copies the small true things of the West — a stew recipe, a lullaby, the name of a good dog — against the day something colder tries to keep the record instead. She has three testimonies to gather from three neighbors. Go and let people tell you something worth keeping warm.
angel_wings_chronicler_notes_01.offer: There's an argument coming, though it's a long way off yet, about who gets to remember the world. And there's a cold way to do it — file the fact, seal the truth, keep the perfect dead record. I do it the warm way, the human way: I write down the mess. Old Neaga's lullaby that her mother made up wrong. The name of Draga's dog. How Otilia salts her stew. Go let three of them tell you something small and true, and bring it back, and I'll keep it. The specific imperfect thing is the only thing the cold record can't copy right.
angel_wings_chronicler_notes_01.progress: Three neighbors have something small and true to tell. Gather Neaga's, Zoe's, and the guard Floarea's testimonies for the chronicle.
angel_wings_chronicler_notes_01.complete: A lullaby sung wrong, a chalk-wall rule, and how a watchwoman knows rain by her knee. Perfect — no, imperfect, gloriously imperfect, and that's the whole point. The cold way of keeping records wants everything true and flat and final. This is true and warm and wrong in all the human ways, and it will outlast the marble. Preserve life by living it, not by filing it. That's the West's whole answer to what's coming. Take this — it's plaited to hold a keepsake. Keep something small and true of your own.

objectives.text: Gather Neaga's lullaby
Gather Zoe's wall-rule
Gather Floarea's weather-knee

angel_wings_chronicler_notes_01.note: A lullaby sung wrong, a rule about chalk, a watchwoman's aching knee — true and warm and imperfect in all the human ways. The cold record cannot copy them right, and that is the West's whole answer to what is coming: preserve life by living it, not by filing it. Ioana keeps the warm way. Somewhere far off and far ahead, an Archive keeps the other.

angel_wings_guard_gate_daily_01.title: Watch the East Gate
angel_wings_guard_gate_daily_01.summary: Floarea keeps the east gate, and the east gate is where the West's people go when they've begun to listen. Each dusk she counts who leaves and does not come back. Walk the gate-line with her, turn the gentle ones home, and mark the ones already too far gone.
angel_wings_guard_gate_daily_01.offer: It's dusk again, so it's the count again. I keep the east gate — the one the listening ones drift toward when the pull gets into them. Most I can turn: a hand on the shoulder, a word, walk them back to a warm room and they forget they meant to go. Some are already too far, and those I only mark, and pray their families come before the dark does. Walk the gate-line with me tonight. Turn who can be turned. It's the same work every dusk. It's the only work that matters.
angel_wings_guard_gate_daily_01.progress: The listening ones drift toward the east gate at dusk. Walk the gate-line, turn the gentle ones home.
angel_wings_guard_gate_daily_01.complete: Two turned, one marked. That's a good night — most nights it's the other way. The two I turned won't remember they meant to go; they'll wake tomorrow and think they had a bad dream about the treeline. The one I marked... his sister's coming at first light. I'll sit with him till she does. Same time tomorrow, if you've the stomach. The gate doesn't close and the dusk doesn't stop coming and neither, saints keep us, does the pull.

objectives.label: the east gate-line
objectives.text: Walk the gate-line at dusk
Turn the gentle ones home with Floarea

angel_wings_guard_gate_daily_01.note: Two turned, one marked — a good night, by the gate's arithmetic. The turned ones will wake and call it a bad dream about the treeline. The marked one's sister came at first light. Floarea keeps the count every dusk, because the gate does not close and the pull does not stop, and someone has to stand where the West walks out of itself.

angel_wings_market_daily_01.title: Grain to the Small Doors
angel_wings_market_daily_01.summary: Every morning Paraschiva parcels out grain to the households too proud or too far to come to the storehouse — the widows, the shut-ins, the orphanage. Run her rounds before the market fills. It is the daily labor of a faction that outlasts by feeding.
angel_wings_market_daily_01.offer: Morning, and the rounds want running. There's houses that won't come to the storehouse — pride, or bad legs, or three little ones they can't leave. So the grain goes to them. Old Neaga on the market lane, Zoe's house of children, and the widow's row by the west wall. Take the sacks round before the market crowds the lanes. It's dull work and it's the whole of us — the West doesn't win, traveler, it feeds, and feeding is a thing you do again every single morning or it stops meaning anything.
angel_wings_market_daily_01.progress: Run the grain-round: Neaga's door, the orphanage, and the widow's row by the west wall.
angel_wings_market_daily_01.complete: All delivered, all fed, all before the crowds. Dull as dishwater and I'd not trade it for a crown. You know what winning looks like in Angel Wings? It looks like a boring morning where everybody ate. No glory in it. That's how you know it's real — the stone can't make a boring morning look like the only option. Come round tomorrow. There's always a tomorrow's round. That's the promise and the burden both.

objectives.text: Deliver grain to Neaga

objectives.label: the orphanage
objectives.text: Deliver grain to the orphanage

objectives.label: the widow's row
objectives.text: Deliver grain to the widow's row

angel_wings_market_daily_01.note: The grain went round and everyone ate, before the crowds, dull as dishwater. Winning in the West looks like a boring morning where nobody starved — and the boredom is how you know it is real, because the stone cannot make a boring morning feel like the only door. There is always a tomorrow's round. That is the promise and the burden both.

angel_wings_dignitary_court_01.title: The Queen Who Flinched Right
angel_wings_dignitary_court_01.summary: Word has leaked that the West holds a Bloodstone fragment, and three larger powers now covet the poorest throne on the map. Tinca must know who is talking. Trace the leak through the market and the court, and learn what it costs to be the one crown that would rather understand a thing than own it.
angel_wings_dignitary_court_01.offer: You've earned enough of the Queen's trust to hear the shape of the trouble. Word is out — the West holds a fragment. We denied nothing and confirmed nothing and it didn't matter; the wanting is loose now. Blestem wants to buy it, the South wants to seize it, the North wants it sealed under their Thread, and every one of them thinks Fielderine's a fool for only wanting to understand it. Someone here is talking. Trace it — the market, the court, whoever's coin looks too new. I need the leak before the leak needs us.
angel_wings_dignitary_court_01.progress: The leak runs through the market and the court. Trace it — the merchant's whispers, the chronicler's records, the too-new coin.
angel_wings_dignitary_court_01.complete: A hungry clerk and a Blestem coin — of course. It's always hunger and arithmetic in the end. We'll close it quietly; the Queen doesn't punish desperation, she prices it and moves on. Here's the thing you should carry away, traveler: the whole world is racing to own the stone, and the one crown that would rather leave it alone and understand it is the poorest and the weakest and the most likely to be sacked for its restraint. If there's a right answer on this board, it's hers. And it's losing. Take the veil. Some nights it helps to not be seen thinking.

objectives.text: Trace the whisper through the market
Cross-check the court records with Ioana
Bring the leak's name to Tinca

angel_wings_dignitary_court_01.note: The leak was a hungry clerk and a coin too new — hunger and arithmetic, as it always is. The Queen priced the desperation and closed it quietly, because she does not punish the starving for being starved. The whole world races to own the stone; the one crown that would rather leave it alone is the poorest, the weakest, the likeliest to be sacked for its restraint. If there is a right answer on the board, it is hers, and it is losing.

angel_wings_cott_river_01.title: The River Runs Clean
angel_wings_cott_river_01.summary: Old Cott has fished the weir since before the queens were queens, and he judges the whole world by whether the river runs clean. His nets have gone missing off the far bank. Fetch them back, and let an old man keep the one measure of health he still trusts.
angel_wings_cott_river_01.offer: Sixty years I've read this river, and it's read me back — the one honest ledger left in the West, that water. Runs clean, we're all right. Runs otherwise, count your candles. Some fool's boat dragged my nets off downstream and I can't wade for them anymore, these knees. Fetch them, would you? An old man needs his nets the way he needs his mornings. And while you're down there — look at the water. Tell me it's still running clean. I'll believe it more from young eyes than my own.
angel_wings_cott_river_01.progress: Cott's nets drifted downstream from the weir, caught on the far bank. Fetch them back and look at the water.
angel_wings_cott_river_01.complete: Clean, you say. Good. GOOD. Some days that's the whole of the news I need — clean water and my nets back and knees that got me through another morning. I taught a hungry girl to fish on this bank once, before I knew she was stealing, and again after, on purpose. Best thing I ever did and I can't talk about it. Take this — it's a small thing, but so's a trout, and so's a kindness, and the small things are what the river keeps. Go on. Mind the current.

objectives.label: the far bank
objectives.text: Fetch Cott's nets from the far bank
Tell Cott the river runs clean

angel_wings_cott_river_01.note: The nets came home and the water ran clean, and for Old Cott that was the whole of the good news. He taught a hungry girl to fish on that bank once, before he knew, and again after, on purpose — the best thing he ever did, and the one thing he cannot say. The river keeps the small things. It is the one ledger in the West he still trusts, and he reads it every morning he wakes.

angel_wings_pilgrim_road_01.title: Three Bedrolls, No Footprints Out
angel_wings_pilgrim_road_01.summary: A pilgrim camp on the east road has gone silent — three bedrolls, a cold stew still in the pot, footprints going in and none coming out. Draga needs it walked and understood before he reports it, because the last report of its kind got a good scout laughed out of the barracks.
angel_wings_pilgrim_road_01.offer: There's a camp on the east road I need walked, and I need it walked by someone who won't lose their nerve and won't make it up. Pilgrims — three of them, headed east like they all do lately. Camp's still there. Bedrolls laid out, stew gone cold in the pot, boots by the fire. Footprints in the mud going toward the camp. None going away from it. Three came, none left, and nothing's disturbed. Go and read it. Come back and tell me plain what makes three people leave without their boots or their feet.
angel_wings_pilgrim_road_01.progress: The silent pilgrim camp lies on the east road. Read the ground — the bedrolls, the cold stew, the footprints in.
angel_wings_pilgrim_road_01.complete: None going out. You're sure. Three walked in, sat, cooled their supper, and then just... weren't, and the mud never felt them leave. That's the listening taken all the way — they didn't walk east, the east came the last little way and collected them where they sat. And there was a letter, you said. Addressed to whoever reads this. Don't tell me what it said. Don't tell me you read it. Burn it, and forget it, and take this warding, and let's neither of us be a fourth bedroll.

objectives.label: the silent pilgrim camp
objectives.text: Read the abandoned pilgrim camp
Report the camp plainly to Draga

angel_wings_pilgrim_road_01.note: Three walked in and none walked out, and the mud never felt them leave — the listening taken all the way, the east come the last little distance to collect them where they sat. There was a letter, addressed to whoever reads this. It was burned unread. Draga filed the camp as pilgrims lost to weather. He knows it wasn't weather. He would rather be laughed at than believed.

angel_wings_widow_row_01.title: The Widow's Row
angel_wings_widow_row_01.summary: The widow's row by the west wall has lost three of its own to winter and want, and the graves need digging while the ground is still soft enough to take them. Old Neaga is too old to dig and too proud to ask twice. Turn the earth, and sit with her while she names who's going in.
angel_wings_widow_row_01.offer: Three of us gone since the frost — Ilka, and the Marek sisters who came as a pair and left as one. The row wants three graves and there's not a young back left on it to dig them; we're all widows here, that's the joke of the place, the widow's row with no one to widow anymore. I'll not beg. But I'll say it once, plainly, the way we say things here: the ground's soft now and it won't be for long. Dig for us. And sit after, and let me tell you who they were. Somebody living ought to hear it.
angel_wings_widow_row_01.progress: The widow's row lies by the west wall. Turn the three graves while the ground is soft, then sit with Neaga.
angel_wings_widow_row_01.complete: There. Three, deep enough, decent. Ilka sang flat and never knew it and we loved her for it. The Marek sisters finished each other's sentences for sixty years and now there's half a sentence loose in the world with no one to finish it. That's what a grave is, traveler — a sentence with no one left to finish it. You dug them proper and you sat and you heard the names. That's all the dead of the West ask: to be missed by name and not counted in a row. Take Ilka's shawl-knot. She'd want it walking.

objectives.label: the widow's row
objectives.text: Dig the three graves by the west wall
Sit with Neaga while she names the dead

angel_wings_widow_row_01.note: Three graves, dug decent while the ground was soft, for three the winter took. Ilka sang flat and was loved for it; the Marek sisters left half a sentence loose in the world with no one to finish it. A grave, Neaga said, is a sentence with no one left to finish it. The dead of the West ask only to be missed by name, and not counted in a row. Somewhere, someone is learning to count them in rows instead.

angel_wings_guard3_relief_01.title: Standing the Boy's Post
angel_wings_guard3_relief_01.summary: Aglaia is one of the rotating handful trusted to stand the door of the room no one names — and she has never once opened the box, and lies awake certain it has opened itself. Her relief has not come. Stand the post beside her a while, and learn what it costs to guard a thing you're forbidden to understand.
angel_wings_guard3_relief_01.offer: You're the one Tinca sent about the other warden, so you know more than most who stand this door, which is to say you know it exists. I'm on it now. My relief's late and I won't leave the post untended, not this post. Stand with me a turn? I'll be honest — I've never opened the box, none of us are told what's in it, that's the mercy of the duty. But I lie awake certain it's opened itself. Certain the note's louder than yesterday. Keep me talking about anything else. That's the whole job some nights: not listening.
angel_wings_guard3_relief_01.progress: Aglaia stands the unnamed door in the northwest, waiting on a relief who hasn't come. Stand the post beside her.
angel_wings_guard3_relief_01.complete: Your company got me through the turn, and the relief's finally come, and I can go be a person somewhere warm for a few hours before it's my door again. You want to know the secret of standing this post? You don't guard the box. You guard yourself. You keep your mind full of small dull things — your sister's name, the price of bread, a song sung wrong — so there's no quiet room inside you for the note to move into. Take the knot. It's for keeping calm. On this door, calm is the whole of the armor.

objectives.label: the unnamed door
objectives.text: Stand the post with Aglaia
Keep Aglaia talking through the turn

angel_wings_guard3_relief_01.note: The turn passed and the relief came. You do not guard the box, Aglaia said — you guard yourself, you keep your mind so full of small dull things that there is no quiet room inside for the note to move into. None of the wardens are told what they guard. It is the mercy of the duty. She has never opened the box, and she lies awake certain, every night, that it has opened itself.

angel_wings_smith_bells_01.title: Bells for the Warm Rooms
angel_wings_smith_bells_01.summary: After a week of pouring dead lead, Zamfira wants to make something that rings. She's casting hand-bells for the orphanage and the widow's row — small loud things to fill the quiet the listening leaves behind. She needs good bronze and a clear place to test the tone.
angel_wings_smith_bells_01.offer: I've had a week of dead metal and I'm sick to my teeth of quiet. So I'm casting bells — little hand-bells, a dozen of them, for Zoe's children and Neaga's row and anyone with a room gone too still. You laugh, but there's sense in it: the listening's a silence that gets into people, and a bell's the opposite of a silence, isn't it. Bring me clean bronze from Anca and I'll pour a sound you can't not hear. Then help me test the tone somewhere open. I want to know they carry.
angel_wings_smith_bells_01.progress: Buy clean bronze from Anca, then help Zamfira test the bells' tone in the open square.
angel_wings_smith_bells_01.complete: Hear that? That carries. That gets into a room and fills it and leaves no corner for the quiet to sit in. Zoe's little ones will ring these raw for a week and drive everyone mad and it'll be the best noise in the West. A bell's an honest thing — it only ever says here, here, here, I'm still here. After all that lead, I needed to make a thing that insists on being heard. Take one. Ring it when the road gets too quiet. It won't stop anything. But it'll remind you you're still here to be stopped.

objectives.text: Buy clean bronze from Anca

objectives.label: the open square
objectives.text: Test the bells' tone with Zamfira

angel_wings_smith_bells_01.note: The bells carried, and filled the rooms, and left no corner for the quiet to sit in. A bell is an honest thing, Zamfira said — it only ever says here, here, I'm still here. After a week of pouring dead lead she needed to make a thing that insisted on being heard. The children rang them raw for a week and drove everyone mad. It was, by common agreement, the best noise in the West.

angel_wings_two_furrow_return_01.title: The Ground Grows a Story
angel_wings_two_furrow_return_01.summary: The famine broke at Two-Furrow when the granary was finally opened — a clean win, a village saved. Ioana asks a returning stranger to walk it once more and read the after: the dust in the emptied barn, the color of the well now that the story is over. The West has learned to distrust a clean win.
angel_wings_two_furrow_return_01.offer: They opened the Two-Furrow granary and the village ate and the hunger lifted, and everyone called it a mercy and a victory and I wrote it down as both, because it was. And then I couldn't sleep. So I'll ask you, since you stood in that barn when it was full and terrible: go back now that it's empty and saved. Walk the floor. Look at the dust. Read the well. Tell me a victory's supposed to leave a mark like the trouble did. Tell me the ground doesn't keep the shape of what used it. I'll believe you. I want to believe you.
angel_wings_two_furrow_return_01.progress: Return to the emptied Two-Furrow granary. Read the after — the dust on the floor, the color of the well.
angel_wings_two_furrow_return_01.complete: The dust lies in lines. Even, parallel, all one way, across a barn floor that a hundred grateful feet have crossed since the saving. And the well shows a color that slides when you look at it and settles when you don't. So it wasn't a victory. It was a door held open until we walked through it grateful. The famine was the staffing, not the cause — engineered hunger to make one desperate village a live and warm and useful thing. We didn't win at Two-Furrow. We were used there, and thanked for it, and I have to write that down too. Take the veil. Some knowledge you'd rather wear than carry bare.

objectives.label: the emptied granary
objectives.text: Read the after at Two-Furrow
Bring Ioana the shape the ground kept

angel_wings_two_furrow_return_01.note: The village was saved and the dust on the emptied floor lay in even parallel lines, and the well showed a color that slid when watched and settled when not. The famine was the staffing, not the cause — a door held open until a grateful village walked through. They did not win at Two-Furrow. They were used there, and thanked for it. Ioana wrote that down too. The chronicler is honest the way the ground is: it keeps the shape of what used it.

gravemark_tundra_the_counted_rows.title: The Counted Rows
gravemark_tundra_the_counted_rows.summary: The graves of the tundra are cut in rows of twelve and have been since before the North had a name to bury under. Radu keeps the tally. Last night a row grew a thirteenth stone no hand of his set.
gravemark_tundra_the_counted_rows.offer: I keep the rows. Twelve to a row, cut so since the grave took its first soldier, and I have counted them every dawn for thirty winters. This dawn one row held thirteen. I did not cut the extra. Walk the kerb-field and read me the numbers off every row, so I know how far the wrongness has spread before I have to write it down.
gravemark_tundra_the_counted_rows.progress: The rows lie north of the meltwater ditch, where the blue-black stone breaks the snow. Read each row and hold your count.
gravemark_tundra_the_counted_rows.complete: Thirteen in one row, eleven in another, and a row that will not stay the same number twice. Somebody is keeping a different tally than mine, and theirs is winning. Take the token. A kept name is worth more here than coin.

objectives.label: the kerb-field
objectives.text: Read the rows in the kerb-field
Gather the loose kept-name tokens

gravemark_tundra_the_counted_rows.note: The rows did not agree with Radu's ledger, and the ledger is thirty winters old. Something counts the dead here on its own schedule. His numbers were right once. They are only history now.

gravemark_tundra_shallow_graves.title: So They Have Not Far to Come
gravemark_tundra_shallow_graves.summary: Costel digs the tundra's graves shallow now — a courtesy, he calls it, so the dead have not far to climb when they climb. Lately they climb. The frost keeps them, and the frost keeps them moving.
gravemark_tundra_shallow_graves.offer: I dig them shallow now. Two spades deep, no more. Folk think it's my back, and I let them. Truth is a deep grave here is just a longer walk for what won't stay in it — kinder on everyone to keep the door low. But three came up last night and did not lie back down. Put them down proper, before the children see how they walk.
gravemark_tundra_shallow_graves.progress: The frost-wights walk the low ground east of the burial rows, where the graves are shallowest. Lay them down.
gravemark_tundra_shallow_graves.complete: Down again, and thank you. Fear dressed up as courtesy, that's all my shallow graves ever were. I dig them low so I don't have to watch them come far. Doesn't stop them coming. Take the ward. Nail it over your own door, wherever that turns out to be.

objectives.text: Lay the risen frost-wights down

gravemark_tundra_shallow_graves.note: Costel digs his graves two spades deep and calls it kindness. The dead came up anyway. In the tundra the ground does not keep what you give it — it only holds it, the way a hand holds a thing it means to use.

gravemark_tundra_cedar_on_stone.title: Cedar on the Stone
gravemark_tundra_cedar_on_stone.summary: Every dawn Anton leaves cedar shavings on the old grave-stone at the field's heart, as his mother did, and hers. None of them could say why. The stone is warm to the hand. It should not be, in this country of ice.
gravemark_tundra_cedar_on_stone.offer: I leave cedar on the big stone each morning. My mother did it, and hers, and not one of us could ever tell you why — you just do it, the way you close a dead man's eyes. My knee's gone this week. Will you carry the shavings up and lay them proper? And put your hand flat on the stone while you're there. Tell me true whether it's cold like everything else. I've stopped being able to tell.
gravemark_tundra_cedar_on_stone.progress: The warm stone sits at the heart of the kerb-field. Lay the cedar and lay your hand on the stone.
gravemark_tundra_cedar_on_stone.complete: Warm, you say. In this cold, warm. My mother knew a reason once and wore it smooth telling nobody. Cedar doesn't rot, is all I know — it outlasts the thing it was cut from. Maybe that's the whole of it. Maybe we're feeding something that outlasts us. Keep the charm. It quiets the wanting-to-know.

objectives.label: the warm grave-stone
objectives.text: Lay cedar on the warm stone
Take the offered charm

gravemark_tundra_cedar_on_stone.note: The stone is warm, and no one alive remembers why the cedar is laid — only that it must be. A ritual outlives its reason the way cedar outlives the tree. Something under the field is being fed, gently, one morning at a time.

gravemark_tundra_the_one_warm_well.title: The One Warm Well
gravemark_tundra_the_one_warm_well.summary: Every well in the tundra freezes by the Long Dark. One never has, and folk blessed it for years. This winter its water came up copper and warm, and Gherghe has learned enough of this country to stop counting warmth a blessing.
gravemark_tundra_the_one_warm_well.offer: One well in this whole frozen march never ices over. My grandfather called it a mercy and drank from it proud. This week it came up warm — warm, and the taste of a coin held too long. I've been north long enough to know a warm thing is a wrong thing here. Carry a jar of the counteragent down to it and treat the water, before the whole village decides warm is worth the copper.
gravemark_tundra_the_one_warm_well.progress: The unfreezing well stands below the merchant row. Bring the counteragent to its warm and coppered water.
gravemark_tundra_the_one_warm_well.complete: Treated, and the taste is cutting already. My grandfather thanked God for that warmth. I've learned to thank nothing that warms the ground in the North. Copper first, then the yeast dies, then folk go quiet and stand in fields. I know the order now. We all learn it up here, in order.

objectives.text: Gather doses of the counteragent

objectives.label: the unfreezing well
objectives.text: Treat the warm well

gravemark_tundra_the_one_warm_well.note: The well that never froze runs copper now, and warm. Gherghe knows the symptom order by heart, the way the whole North does — recited flat, like a prayer that stopped being one. He treated the water. The order does not run backward.

gravemark_tundra_the_still_market.title: The Still Market
gravemark_tundra_the_still_market.summary: The old market ground stands full of the standing dead — shells arranged in rows of twelve, patient, harmless, until they are not. Nica walked past yesterday and one of them turned its head to follow him. Only one. Only once.
gravemark_tundra_the_still_market.offer: There's a market ground east of here that hasn't traded in a lifetime, and it's not empty. They stand in it — the dead, in rows, twelve to a rank, quiet as posts. You can walk right through. I've done it a hundred times. Yesterday one turned its head as I passed. Just one. Just the once. I need to know if the rest have started to notice us. Walk the rows. If they move, put down what moves.
gravemark_tundra_the_still_market.progress: The still market lies east, past the meltwater ditch. Walk among the ranks. Cut down any shell that turns.
gravemark_tundra_the_still_market.complete: So more than one turns now. That's the thread waking them by degrees, the Corbeanu chronicler would say, if you could get him this far north. A shell notices nothing until it's told to. Somebody's begun telling them. Take the ward-plate. It won't stop a rank of them. It might buy you the turning of your own head.

objectives.label: the still market
objectives.text: Walk the rows of the still market
Cut down the shells that turn

gravemark_tundra_the_still_market.note: The market's dead stood in rows of twelve, harmless as fence-posts, until they began to notice. A shell is only meat with a line run through it. Someone, somewhere below, has started pulling the line. The rows turned their heads together, once, and Nica walks the long way round the market now.

gravemark_tundra_thread_that_reaches.title: The Thread That Reaches Up
gravemark_tundra_thread_that_reaches.summary: Under the alchemist's light the tundra floor threads blue — filaments of the buried will that moves the dead, reaching up from the grave a thousand feet below. Radu has traced one filament to where it surfaces. A tomb-golem stands guard over the place it breaks the ground.
gravemark_tundra_thread_that_reaches.offer: Hold the reading-draught to your eye and look at the floor. See it? The blue — threads of it, fine as spider-silk, running up through the frozen dirt from something a thousand feet down. That's the will that walks our dead. I've followed one strand to where it surfaces, and there's a golem of stacked grave-tablets standing over the spot, guarding the seam. Break the golem. Let me see where the thread comes up. Do not touch the seam itself. Look at your boots and break the guard.
gravemark_tundra_thread_that_reaches.progress: The surfacing thread lies in the deep hollow north-east, guarded by a tomb-golem. Break the guard. Do not attend the seam.
gravemark_tundra_thread_that_reaches.complete: It came up right where I marked it — a wound in the floor threaded blue, and under the blue, when the light shifts, a deeper violet. Sub-terrestrial. The roots of the thing itself. We are living on the lid of it, and the lid is thinning. I logged it and I looked away. You did too. That's the only wisdom this country has to teach. Take the lantern; its light shows the seams before you step on one.

objectives.text: Break the tomb-golem guarding the seam

objectives.label: the surfacing thread
objectives.text: Mark where the thread breaks the ground

gravemark_tundra_thread_that_reaches.note: The thread that moves the dead runs up from the grave, and under it, deeper, a violet older than the North. The village lives on the lid of the thing. Radu logged the seam and looked away, and taught the player to do the same. In the tundra, restraint is not virtue. It is the only survival anyone has found.

gravemark_tundra_iron_for_the_markers.title: Iron for the Markers
gravemark_tundra_iron_for_the_markers.summary: Iosif shoes the grave-markers in cold iron, a smith's small defiance — folk say iron pins the dead the way a nail pins a plank. He has run through his bog-iron. The stuff lies frozen in the old cuttings, and he is too broad now to crawl for it.
gravemark_tundra_iron_for_the_markers.offer: I cap the markers in iron. Old smith's trick — iron in the ground over a grave, and the grave minds its manners a while longer. Superstition, maybe. Maybe not, in a place where the graves have opinions. I'm out of bog-iron and the cuttings where it lies are a crawl I've grown too wide for. Bring me six good lumps and I'll cap another season of markers. It's not much. Up here, not-much is the whole trade.
gravemark_tundra_iron_for_the_markers.progress: The bog-iron lies frozen in the old cuttings west of the forge. Bring back six good lumps.
gravemark_tundra_iron_for_the_markers.complete: Good iron, cold-dug. Six markers more will keep their tenants a while, or keep me lying to myself a while, and I've stopped being able to tell those apart. My father capped these same graves. Half his are open now. But he bought us the years, and I'll buy the next fellow his. That's the work. Take a striker off my bench — flint's honest, whatever else fails.

objectives.text: Dig bog-iron from the old cuttings

gravemark_tundra_iron_for_the_markers.note: Iosif caps the graves in iron and does not know if it holds them or only holds his nerve. His father did the same, and half his father's graves stand open. The work buys years, not victories. In the North that is the only thing anyone sells.

gravemark_tundra_the_listening_field.title: The Listening Field
gravemark_tundra_the_listening_field.summary: Four villagers walked out to the frozen threshing-field and stopped, heads tilted, listening to a ground that hums. Ozun has hauled two home already. The other two he was too late for — they stand out there still, and they are no longer alone in the standing.
gravemark_tundra_the_listening_field.offer: Four of ours walked out to the old threshing-field at dusk and just — stopped. Heads cocked, listening at nothing. I dragged two back by their collars, screaming like I'd stolen the only good thing they'd ever had. The other two I reached too late. They've gone still all the way through. Frost took what the listening left. Go out and put those two down gentle — they're not in there anymore — and haul back anyone still soft enough to save.
gravemark_tundra_the_listening_field.progress: The listening-field lies south, past the fuel-cuttings. Put down the finished. Bring back any still living.
gravemark_tundra_the_listening_field.complete: The two I saved won't speak yet, but they flinch when I say their names, so there's someone still in there to flinch. Give it a week, the fresh ones stop flinching, and then the name's just a noise the wall made once. The finished ones you put down — they died the moment they understood, not the moment you swung. Kinder that way. Nothing about this is kind. Take the shroud. It's for the two I couldn't reach.

objectives.label: the listening-field
objectives.text: Reach the villagers in the field
Put down those who finished listening

gravemark_tundra_the_listening_field.note: Two were pulled back and still flinch at their own names. Two finished listening and became the frost's, and the swing that felled them was mercy for a death already done. Comprehension cannot be un-happened. Ozun learned the difference between the ones you reach in time and the ones you only bury standing.

gravemark_tundra_kept_and_pending.title: Kept and Pending
gravemark_tundra_kept_and_pending.summary: The tundra's kerbs read like ledger lines: a name, a count of winters, and one word — KEPT, or PENDING. Radu's own daughter's stone reads KEPT and has for six years. This morning a second hand recut it. It reads PENDING now.
gravemark_tundra_kept_and_pending.offer: Every kerb here carries one word under the name. Kept, meaning the ground has them and means to hold them. Pending, meaning — nobody says what pending means. We just re-carve them to Kept when we can and sleep better. My girl's stone has read Kept for six years. I read it clean each dawn. This dawn a second chisel had been at it, deeper than mine, and it reads Pending. Find who's re-cutting our word, and bring me back the tokens they've been leaving in the rows. I'll not lose her twice.
gravemark_tundra_kept_and_pending.progress: The re-cut kerbs stand through the northern rows. Gather the pending-tokens left among them and read who wrote the word.
gravemark_tundra_kept_and_pending.complete: No hand did this. No living one. The cuts go too deep and too even, and the chips lie in aligned lines, like the dust does. The word isn't being changed. It's being corrected — to what the grave already knows and my chisel keeps arguing with. Kept is my word for her. Pending is its. I'll go on cutting Kept every dawn regardless. Take the token that was hers. It kept her name a while. That was never nothing.

objectives.text: Gather the tokens from the re-cut rows
Ask Costel who tends the northern rows

gravemark_tundra_kept_and_pending.note: Radu carves KEPT into his daughter's stone each dawn, and each dawn a deeper hand corrects it to PENDING. The living keep their dead by insisting. The grave keeps a ledger, and the ledger is patient. He will go on insisting. The chips lay in aligned lines, and he did not mention that to anyone.

gravemark_tundra_a_warm_patch.title: A Warm Patch in the Snow
gravemark_tundra_a_warm_patch.summary: A trader's mule refused a stretch of the north track and would not be beaten past it. The snow there had melted to bare black ground in a country that has not thawed in living memory. Manea wants to know what warms it before his caravans have to go around for good.
gravemark_tundra_a_warm_patch.offer: My mule planted its feet on the north track and would not pass, and a beaten mule that still won't go is a mule that knows something. Went and looked. A patch of the track's gone bare — snow melted clean off, black earth steaming, in a march that hasn't thawed since my grandfather's grandfather. Trade can't run a road that thaws in winter; folk'll think it cursed and they'll be right. Go stand on it, tell me how far the warmth runs, and gather up whatever's left steaming there. I'll pay for knowing what I'm routing around.
gravemark_tundra_a_warm_patch.progress: The bare warm patch lies on the north track past the last marker. Walk its edge and gather what steams there.
gravemark_tundra_a_warm_patch.complete: Steaming dirt, in the Long Dark. That's the fifth I've had to route around this season, and they make a line if you mark them on a map — a straight line, pointing north, toward the grave. The road's not cursed. The road's being drawn on, and my caravans are the last honest thing still following the old marks. Here. A jar of marshlight — burns cold, so it won't add to whatever's warming that ground.

objectives.label: the warm patch
objectives.text: Walk the edge of the thawed ground
Gather the cold-burning marshlight

gravemark_tundra_a_warm_patch.note: Five patches of the frozen march have thawed this season, and marked on Manea's map they draw a straight line pointing north, to the grave. The ground warms first. It is always first. His caravans route around the thaw and call it trade. The line does not care what it is called.

gravemark_tundra_the_colossus_stirs.title: The Colossus Stirs
gravemark_tundra_the_colossus_stirs.summary: At the field's far edge stands the Gravemark Colossus — a mountain of stacked grave-tablets, a thousand contradicting records fused into one shape, inert for as long as the village has stood. It has taken a step. The step aligns with a debt no one here remembers owing.
gravemark_tundra_the_colossus_stirs.offer: You'll have seen the big shape at the field's edge — the tablet-mountain, all our old grave-stones grown together into something taller than the forge. It's stood dead still my whole life. A thing to plough around. Last week it took one step. One. And where it set its foot, the row it crushed held the marker of a debt this village paid off before I was born — paid, sealed, done. It's come to collect a thing already settled. Put it down before it takes the second step, and mind: it recites as it comes. Don't listen to the numbers.
gravemark_tundra_the_colossus_stirs.progress: The Colossus stands at the field's far edge, a step out of its ancient place. Break it before it takes the second step. Do not hear the count it recites.
gravemark_tundra_the_colossus_stirs.complete: Down, and the ground shook when it fell like the whole march exhaled. It was reciting a tally the entire time — our debts, our dead, our names, in a flat voice with no anger in it, only arithmetic. It came because a record and the world stopped agreeing, and it meant to make the world agree. We paid that debt. It came anyway. That's the lesson of this country: settled is a word the living use. Take the crown. Cold-forged. It's the closest thing to a reward a grave-field can offer.

objectives.text: Break the Gravemark Colossus

objectives.label: the Colossus's place
objectives.text: Reach the Colossus at the field's edge

gravemark_tundra_the_colossus_stirs.note: The Colossus of stacked tablets took one step, toward a debt the village settled a lifetime ago, and recited the arithmetic of the dead as it came. It was broken, not reasoned with — it is the reasoning. Settled is a word the living use. The grave keeps its own accounts, and does not accept that a thing can be paid.

gravemark_tundra_grandmothers_cord.title: Grandmother's Cord
gravemark_tundra_grandmothers_cord.summary: Anton's grandmother knotted a keepsake-cord for every child born in the cold months, one knot a wish, and the wishes came true often enough that no one questioned it. She is gone now, and there is a new child, and the last of her cords lies lost somewhere in the snow between here and the well.
gravemark_tundra_grandmothers_cord.offer: There's a new one born to us — first cry the village has heard since autumn, and up here that's a festival all by itself. My grandmother knotted a cord for every winter-child, a knot to a wish, and folk swore by them. She's under the frost now with the rest, but she finished this last cord before she went, and I've gone and lost the fool thing in the snow between the house and the well. Find it for me? A winter-child ought to have its cord. It's a small thing. Small things are the whole point of them.
gravemark_tundra_grandmothers_cord.progress: The lost cord lies somewhere in the trodden snow between the houses and the well. Find it before the child is named.
gravemark_tundra_grandmothers_cord.complete: You found it! Look — every knot still tight, still hers. My grandmother's hands, on a child she'll never hold. That's not nothing, that a dead woman's wish gets to sit at a living child's throat. That's about the whole of what we've got up here — small warm things, passed down, kept tight against the cold. Take this one. She'd knotted a spare. She always did knot a spare.

objectives.label: the trodden snow by the well
objectives.text: Search the snow for the lost cord
Recover the grandmother's cord

gravemark_tundra_grandmothers_cord.note: A dead woman's last cord found its way to a living child's throat, every knot still tight. In the burial country the small warm passed-down things are not sentiment — they are the whole of the resistance. Anton kept his grandmother's spare. She always knotted a spare, against exactly this cold.

gravemark_tundra_the_grave_watch.title: The Grave-Watch
gravemark_tundra_the_grave_watch.summary: The oldest kerb in the tundra stands over a soldier the grave has held six hundred years without complaint. Twice this month the dawn watch has found the dust around it lying in fresh parallel lines. Nica needs someone to stand the night and see what does the aligning.
gravemark_tundra_the_grave_watch.offer: There's a kerb older than the North's name, over a soldier the ground's held six hundred years and never once argued. Reliable, that grave. My grandfather stood watch on it for the peace of standing somewhere the dead behaved. Now twice this month the morning finds the dust round it combed into little straight lines, even as furrows, and no boot-print near. I can't watch it — I've the market rows to walk. Stand the grave through the dark. Don't touch anything. Just see what does the combing.
gravemark_tundra_the_grave_watch.progress: The oldest kerb stands at the field's cold heart. Keep the watch through the night. Only watch.
gravemark_tundra_the_grave_watch.complete: Lines in the dust, you say, forming while you stood there and no hand near. That's the ground listening on the grave's behalf, arranging itself to a voice too low for us. Six hundred years that soldier lay quiet. Something's begun speaking to him at last, and he's begun tidying up to answer. You watched and you didn't touch. That's the whole of the job, and the hardest part of it. Take the ward. You've earned an easy night, if there's such a thing left here.

objectives.label: the oldest kerb
objectives.text: Keep watch at the oldest kerb
Take the watch-ward

gravemark_tundra_the_grave_watch.note: The dust combed itself into parallel lines on a grave that lay quiet six hundred years, while the watcher stood and did nothing, which was the whole of the task. The ground is beginning to align. It arranges itself to answer a voice too low to hear. The soldier has started, after six centuries, to tidy up for a caller.

gravemark_tundra_shells_in_ranks.title: Shells in Ranks
gravemark_tundra_shells_in_ranks.summary: A column of the threaded dead has formed on the west approach, marching nowhere in ranks of twelve, tireless and exact. They do not attack the village. They only march. Ozun wants the column broken before whatever aims it decides where it should go.
gravemark_tundra_shells_in_ranks.offer: There's a column of them on the west approach — the marching dead, in ranks of twelve, going nowhere at a soldier's pace, round and round the same frozen circuit. They've not raised a hand to us. That's what turns my stomach. A thing that could and doesn't is a thing that's waiting to be told. Break the column while it's still just marching. Cut the front ranks down and the rest may lose the shape. And if you see the blue line that runs through them, you leave it be and you tell Radu. The line's above my pay.
gravemark_tundra_shells_in_ranks.progress: The threaded column marches the west approach in ranks of twelve. Break the front ranks. Report any thread to Radu.
gravemark_tundra_shells_in_ranks.complete: Ranks broken, and the rest wandered off the shape like a sentence with the verb cut out. They'll re-form given a night — the line's still down there pulling them into order — but you bought us the night. Rows of twelve. Same count the Strigoi use for their killings, they say, far south.
