The thread and the ledger speak the one tongue, and it counts in twelves. Take the plate. It's turned worse than these.

objectives.label: the west approach
objectives.text: Reach the marching column
Break the front ranks of twelve

gravemark_tundra_shells_in_ranks.note: The column marched in ranks of twelve — the grammar of the ledger and the thread alike, the same count a Strigoi execution uses far to the south. Broken, it will re-form; the line beneath still pulls the shape. Ozun bought a night, and does not say aloud what the twelves mean, though he has begun to count them in his sleep.

gravemark_tundra_the_sextons_doubt.title: The Sexton's Doubt
gravemark_tundra_the_sextons_doubt.summary: Costel has dug the tundra long enough to know its floor, and lately his spade strikes hollow where it should strike stone. Something has been under the burial ground the whole time, he thinks, and only now is close enough to sound. He wants a second opinion before he says so out loud.
gravemark_tundra_the_sextons_doubt.offer: I know this floor the way you know your own teeth with your tongue. Every spadeful, thirty years. And lately I strike a place and it answers hollow — a drum, not a stone, right under where the dead lie. There's a room down there, or a want of one, and I think there always was, and I think it's rising to meet my spade. I'll not say that in the taproom and be the madman of the march. Take these soundings up to Radu. If a chronicler tells me I'm mad, I'll believe him gladly.
gravemark_tundra_the_sextons_doubt.progress: Costel's soundings need a chronicler's ear. Gather the cuttings and carry them to Radu.
gravemark_tundra_the_sextons_doubt.complete: He didn't call me mad. Worse — he wrote it down. Said the deep is close to the surface here, closer each year, and the grave we tend is only the lid of a deeper one. Thirty years I've buried folk two spades down and thought I was giving them to the ground. Turns out I've been laying them on a roof. Keep the poultice. Digging on a roof is hard on the back, and you've a fair bit of it left ahead.

objectives.text: Gather the hollow-struck cuttings
Carry the soundings to Radu

gravemark_tundra_the_sextons_doubt.note: The tundra's floor rings hollow where it should ring stone. Radu named it plainly: the deep rises to meet the spade, and the burial ground is only the lid of a deeper grave. Costel has spent thirty years laying the dead on a roof and calling it the earth. He believed the chronicler gladly. Belief was easier than the drum-sound underfoot.

gravemark_tundra_salt_for_the_thresholds.title: Salt for the Thresholds
gravemark_tundra_salt_for_the_thresholds.summary: A line of salt across a threshold, folk hold, will stop the walking dead from crossing — one of the small wardings the tundra keeps out of stubbornness more than proof. The village has run low, and the Long Dark is the season the doors most need it. Gherghe needs the pouches filled and set.
gravemark_tundra_salt_for_the_thresholds.offer: We line the doors in salt here. A dead thing won't cross a salt-line, they say, and up here you keep every they-say you're given, because the ones we threw out turned out true. My stores are down to a dusting, and the Long Dark's the season the doors earn their salt. Take these pouches to the old salt-cut west of the well, fill them, and lay a fresh line at the thresholds nobody's warded. I sleep better with the doors dressed. We all do. It's not much sleep. Take what you can get.
gravemark_tundra_salt_for_the_thresholds.progress: The salt-cut lies west of the well. Fill the pouches and set fresh lines at the bare thresholds.
gravemark_tundra_salt_for_the_thresholds.complete: Doors dressed, all down the row. Does salt stop the dead? I've never seen one try to cross, and I'd rather not run the experiment. My mother swore by it, and my mother saw a winter I only heard about, so I'll swear by it too. The small wardings are what we've got when the big ones fail, and up here the big ones always fail. Keep a pouch for your own door. You'll want one, whatever door that turns out to be.

objectives.label: the old salt-cut
objectives.text: Fill the pouches at the salt-cut
Set salt-lines at the bare thresholds

gravemark_tundra_salt_for_the_thresholds.note: The doors of the tundra are lined in salt against the walking dead — a small warding kept out of stubbornness, since the they-says thrown out here have a way of proving true. Gherghe would rather not test whether it works. His mother swore by it, and his mother saw a winter he only heard about. That was proof enough for the door, and for him.

gravemark_tundra_what_the_frost_keeps.title: What the Frost Keeps
gravemark_tundra_what_the_frost_keeps.summary: The tundra's cold keeps a body perfect — a fact traders once turned to profit, moving the honored dead south for burial where the ground is soft. Manea's last consignment arrived warm and moving. He needs to know which of his frozen cargo is truly keeping and which is only waiting.
gravemark_tundra_what_the_frost_keeps.offer: There's a trade folk don't speak of politely — the cold keeps a body perfect, so the great houses of the south pay to have their dead hauled up here to wait out the thaw, whole and unspoiled, till the home ground softens enough to take them. Honest work, mostly. My last wagon south arrived warm. The receiving clerk opened it and — well. He's not writing me letters anymore. I need to know which of my stored cargo is keeping clean and which has started keeping itself. Go through the ice-house. Put down anything that's stopped waiting. Gather what's still only frozen.
gravemark_tundra_what_the_frost_keeps.progress: The ice-house stores the frozen consignments north of the merchant row. Put down what moves. Gather what still keeps.
gravemark_tundra_what_the_frost_keeps.complete: So four of them had started keeping themselves. Four out of forty — that's a ruined season and a reputation, and it's worse than that, isn't it. The frost doesn't rot them, so the thread's got a clean instrument to work with — a body perfect and preserved and only wanting a line run through it. I've been hauling the south's dead into the one country that can wake them. I'm done with the trade. Take the dossier; it's my routes and my buyers. Somebody honest ought to have it, and I've run out of honest uses for it myself.

objectives.text: Put down the cargo that stopped waiting
Take Manea's routes and buyers

gravemark_tundra_what_the_frost_keeps.note: The frost keeps a body perfect, which the trade prized and the thread prizes more — a clean instrument, preserved, wanting only a line. Manea hauled the south's dead into the one country able to wake them, and has ended the trade too late for four of them. He gave away his ledger. He had run out of honest uses for it, and would not say what dishonest ones remained.

gravemark_tundra_the_locked_horizon.title: The Locked Horizon
gravemark_tundra_the_locked_horizon.summary: From the tundra's northern rise, on a clear day, you can see it: the Grave, and the Pit at its heart, black against the snow, a day's walk that no one walks. Radu has charted its edge from a distance his whole life. He wants the newest reading taken from the overlook, and he wants a witness who will look and come back.
gravemark_tundra_the_locked_horizon.offer: There's a rise at the north edge where, on a clear hard day, you can see the whole of it — the Grave, and the black wound of the Pit at its centre, a day's walk off and locked to every living foot. I've charted its rim from that rise for forty years with a borrowed lens, and every year the rim is a little wider and the black a little deeper. Go up. Take the reading. Look at it once, truly, so there's a witness younger than me who's seen the thing we all pretend is only weather. Then come back down. That last part's the whole of the ask. Come back down.
gravemark_tundra_the_locked_horizon.progress: The northern rise overlooks the Grave and the locked Pit. Take the reading from the overlook. Then come down.
gravemark_tundra_the_locked_horizon.complete: You saw it. Good. Now you carry it too, and I'm sorry for that, and I'm not — a thing that's watched should be watched by more than one failing old man. Under the lens the Pit reads shifting orange, did you catch it? Orange is the signal writing itself. It's not sleeping out there. It's working, patient as a clerk, a day's walk from our doors, and the road to it runs on north through the Whisper Passes. You've the making of someone the Accord could use. Whether that's a blessing I've long since stopped being able to say. Take the lens. See clearly. It's the cruelest gift I own.

objectives.label: the northern overlook
objectives.text: Take the reading from the overlook
Read the Pit through the borrowed lens

gravemark_tundra_the_locked_horizon.note: From the northern rise the Grave and its Pit stand black against the snow, a day's walk off and locked to every living foot. Under the lens the Pit reads shifting orange — the signal writing itself, working patient as a clerk. It is not sleeping. Radu handed over the lens and called clear sight the cruelest gift he owned. The road to the thing runs north, and the player has been marked, gently, as someone who might walk it.

gravemark_tundra_bone_in_the_permafrost.title: Bone in the Permafrost
gravemark_tundra_bone_in_the_permafrost.summary: The thaw-patches spreading across the march have begun to give up what the frost swallowed — old iron, old bone, and older things. Iosif wants the good iron pulled before the wrong hands pull it, and one thing in particular that the frost should never have been made to keep.
gravemark_tundra_bone_in_the_permafrost.offer: The warm patches have a use, grim as they are — the frost gives things back where it thaws. Iron, mostly, old war-iron worth the reforging. But there's a thing out at the largest thaw I want brought to me before some fool of a scavenger takes it south to sell — a length of worked bone, carved along its shaft, and I don't like the carving. Bring it to my bench with whatever iron you turn up. If the marks on it start to pull your eye, you look at the snow instead. Every fool up here learned that trick or stopped being up here.
gravemark_tundra_bone_in_the_permafrost.progress: The largest thaw-patch has given up old iron and a carved bone. Gather the iron. Bring the bone, eyes down.
gravemark_tundra_bone_in_the_permafrost.complete: Good iron, and — ah. Yes. That's the thing. Carved the whole length in the angular script, the kind you feel in your teeth before you read it. I'll not have it above ground. Into the forge with it, and we'll see if the fire cares more for the marks than we do. You kept your eyes off it, I can tell — you're still you. That's not nothing to be able to say of a man, up here. Take the striker back, and the warding too. Frost-digging splits the knuckles.

objectives.label: the largest thaw-patch
objectives.text: Search the thaw for iron and the bone
Gather the thawed war-iron

gravemark_tundra_bone_in_the_permafrost.note: The thawing march gives back what the frost swallowed — old iron, and a carved bone Iosif fed to the forge unread. He judged the player still themselves by their kept-down eyes, and named that no small thing to be able to say of a man in the burial country. The fire took the marks. Whether it cared about them, the forge did not say, and Iosif did not ask twice.

gravemark_tundra_the_names_she_named.title: The Names She Named
gravemark_tundra_the_names_she_named.summary: Someone has begun naming the standing dead — chalking a name at the foot of each shell in the still market, kindly, under her breath. Anton's own sister has taken it up, and the shells she names have started, he swears, to answer to the names. He needs it stopped, and he cannot be the one to stop his sister.
gravemark_tundra_the_names_she_named.offer: My sister's begun a habit that'll damn her. She goes to the still market at dusk and names the dead — chalks a name at each one's feet, soft, like tucking in children. Says it's forbidden, which is a strange thing for a village girl to say, since we've no law up here, only fear. And the ones she's named — Anton, they turn toward her now. They know their names. I can't be the one to drag her home; she'd never forgive me, and I need her to have a home to be dragged to. Go. Talk to Ozun first, he's watched her. Then put down the ones that have started answering. Not her. Never her.
gravemark_tundra_the_names_she_named.progress: Speak with Ozun, who has watched the naming. Then put down the shells that have learned to answer.
gravemark_tundra_the_names_she_named.complete: It's done, and my sister's home, and she won't speak to me, and that's a price I'll pay a hundred times over. Naming them — she thought she was being kind. Give a dead thing a name and it's a person again, that was her whole heart in it. But a named shell is a shell that can be called, and something's begun calling back through the names she gave. Kindness turned into a door. That's the cruelest trick this country plays: it takes the warmest thing you do and makes a handle of it. Take the token. It was one of the names she chalked. It's just chalk now. See it stays that way.

objectives.text: Ask Ozun what he saw of the naming
Put down the shells that answer to names

gravemark_tundra_the_names_she_named.note: A girl named the standing dead out of kindness, to make them persons again, and the naming became a door something called back through. The warmest instinct made a handle. Anton put down the shells and brought his sister home to a silence between them he called a price worth paying. In the tundra even mercy is an opening. Especially mercy.

gravemark_tundra_counter_the_cold.title: A Counter to the Cold
gravemark_tundra_counter_the_cold.summary: An Accord courier died three days out from the tundra, and the counteragent doses he carried — the quiet-draughts that dull the signal's pull — froze solid in his pack. Manea has the contract to replace them. The trouble is finding the doses, since the treating of a live field is not work anyone advertises.
gravemark_tundra_counter_the_cold.offer: I've a contract, and a distasteful one, which is how you know it pays. An Accord courier went down three days north of here and the cold froze his cargo — counteragent doses, the quiet-draughts they use to dull a field before their readers go in. The Accord wants them replaced, discreet, and won't say where they're going or why a village merchant is trusted with the errand. I don't ask. Gather the doses from the treating-cache west of the rows and I'll see them handed off. Quiet work. The quiet kind's the kind that pays up here, and the kind you don't sleep well on.
gravemark_tundra_counter_the_cold.progress: The treating-cache lies west of the burial rows. Gather the counteragent doses for the Accord handoff.
gravemark_tundra_counter_the_cold.complete: Handed off, and I've a receipt I can't read and a purse I can. The Accord's moving quiet-draughts north in quantity — that's not the behavior of folk who mean to leave a thing sleeping. They're preparing to go in somewhere, dose in hand, and read something they oughtn't. My trade's carried grain and dead men and now this, and every cargo's heavier than the last. Take a dose for yourself. If you ever stand too long in the wrong field, you'll want it. Pray you never know you wanted it.

objectives.label: the treating-cache
objectives.text: Reach the treating-cache
Gather the counteragent doses

gravemark_tundra_counter_the_cold.note: The Accord moves quiet-draughts north in quantity — not the habit of those content to leave a thing sleeping. Manea ran the errand without asking where the doses went, and drew his own answer anyway: someone means to walk into a live field, dose in hand, and read what should be left. His cargoes grow heavier each season. He no longer names what he suspects, only carries it.

gravemark_tundra_kerb_rounds.title: The Dawn Rounds
gravemark_tundra_kerb_rounds.summary: Every dawn the watch walks the burial rows and puts back down whatever rose in the night. It is never finished, and it is never the same dead twice. Nica shares the round with any hand willing, because a round walked alone is a round walked afraid.
gravemark_tundra_kerb_rounds.offer: Same ask as every dawn, and it'll be the same tomorrow. Walk the rows with me — or ahead of me, my knee's slow — and put down what came up in the dark. Never the same ones twice, never the last of them. It's not a thing you finish. It's a thing you keep doing so the children can cross the field to the well without seeing their grandmother upright. That's the whole of the wage: a clear crossing, one more day. Go on. The frost holds them slow; you've time, if you don't dawdle in the middle rows.
gravemark_tundra_kerb_rounds.progress: The risen dead stand through the burial rows. Put down tonight's, before the crossing wakes.
gravemark_tundra_kerb_rounds.complete: Cleared, and the crossing's safe till dusk. Till dusk. I've stopped promising further than that; the march taught me not to. My grandfather walked this round, and his before, and I'll walk it till my knee gives and then you'll walk it, or someone will. The rows don't empty. We just keep them walkable. Here's your due. It's daily coin for daily dead. Come back tomorrow. There'll be tomorrow's.

objectives.text: Put down what rose in the burial rows

gravemark_tundra_kerb_rounds.note: The dawn round is never finished and never the same dead twice — only a clear crossing bought for one more day, so the children reach the well without meeting their grandmother upright. Nica walks it as his grandfather did, and does not promise past dusk. The rows do not empty. The living only keep them walkable, dawn after dawn, against a patience deeper than their own.

gravemark_tundra_thread_cutting.title: Thread-Cutting
gravemark_tundra_thread_cutting.summary: The tomb-golems build themselves anew each night from the loose grave-tablets of the field, gathering the scattered records into walking shape. Ozun sends a hand out each day to break them back to rubble before they finish standing, and to scatter the tablets so the next night's building starts from nothing.
gravemark_tundra_thread_cutting.offer: The golems put themselves together overnight — grave-tablets, our own dead's records, loose in the field, and something below drags them into a shape and stands it up by dawn. Break them while they're new-stood and stupid, before the shape settles and starts reciting. Scatter the tablets after, wide as you can. Makes the next night's building start from scratch. It's stone-rolling work, the chronicler tells me there's an old tale for it — roll the stone up, it rolls down, you roll it up again. That's the whole of holding a line up here.
gravemark_tundra_thread_cutting.progress: The night's tomb-golems stand new-built across the field. Break them and scatter their tablets.
gravemark_tundra_thread_cutting.complete: Broken and scattered, and tomorrow they'll be up again — the below never runs short of will, only patience, and it's got more of that than the whole village put together. But an unbuilt golem is a night the field stays ours. We don't win this. We tie it, one day at a time, and a tie against a thing that can't die is the closest to a victory the North allows. Your coin. Come cut them again tomorrow. The below keeps regular hours; so do we.

objectives.text: Break the night's new-built golems

gravemark_tundra_thread_cutting.note: The tomb-golems rebuild themselves nightly from the field's own grave-tablets, dragged into shape by the patient will below. Ozun's hands break them by dawn and scatter the records wide, and call it a tie — a tie against a thing that cannot die being the nearest the North comes to a win. The below keeps regular hours. So, now, does the village.

gravemark_tundra_the_cold_hearth.title: The Cold Hearth
gravemark_tundra_the_cold_hearth.summary: The inn's fire is the last warm thing for a day's travel in any direction, and folk walk from the far crofts just to sit near it through the Long Dark. Gherghe's fuel is low and the cold is deepening. A cold hearth here is not discomfort. It is how the tundra takes a village.
gravemark_tundra_the_cold_hearth.offer: The fire's my whole trade and better than my trade — it's the last warm room for a day's walk any way you point, and come the Long Dark folk trudge in from crofts you'd not believe are inhabited, just to sit and thaw and hear each other talk. I'll not let it go low. A cold hearth up here isn't a chilly night; it's the thin end of how this country empties a place. Fetch me fuel from the cuttings south — the dead timber the frost's dried hard. Enough to keep the room warm and full and loud a while longer. That's a fight worth the fetching.
gravemark_tundra_the_cold_hearth.progress: The frost-dried timber lies in the southern cuttings. Bring back fuel enough to hold the hearth.
gravemark_tundra_the_cold_hearth.complete: Now that's a blaze. Listen to them — full room, warm and loud, old Costel telling the shallow-grave joke for the hundredth time and everyone laughing like it's new. That noise is the fight, friend. That's us telling the cold there's still a village here that wants to sit up talking. Every night we keep the room full is a night we don't lose one. Take a hot meal and the tonic both. And take the seat by the fire an hour. You've bought it honest.

objectives.text: Gather frost-dried fuel from the cuttings

gravemark_tundra_the_cold_hearth.note: The inn's fire is the last warm room for a day's walk, and a full loud hearth is how the village tells the cold it is still inhabited. Gherghe calls keeping it the fight, and he is not wrong — in the tundra a hearth gone cold is the thin end of how a place empties. The room stayed warm and loud one more night. That was the whole of the victory, and it was real.

gravemark_tundra_reader_in_the_snow.title: The Reader in the Snow
gravemark_tundra_reader_in_the_snow.summary: A scholar came north to study the kerb-inscriptions and did not come back. Trappers found her frozen at a stone with her notebook open and her pen still in her stiff hand, no wound on her, the page half-copied. Radu wants the notebook recovered before anyone reads what she was reading.
gravemark_tundra_reader_in_the_snow.offer: A scholar came up from the south to copy the kerb-script — the Accord sent her, or she said the Accord did, and I let her, God forgive me, because I wanted to know what she'd find too. That's the sin of it, wanting to know. The trappers found her at the far stone, frozen, no mark on her, pen still in her hand, notebook open, the inscription half-copied onto the page. Bring me the notebook. Do not read the page. She read it, or near enough, and look what reading bought her. Bring it sealed and eyes-down and we'll bury the thing where curiosity can't dig it up.
gravemark_tundra_reader_in_the_snow.progress: The frozen scholar lies at the far kerb, notebook open. Recover it. Do not read the copied page.
gravemark_tundra_reader_in_the_snow.complete: You brought it sealed. Good. Braver than brave, that — not the taking, the not-looking. She died of understanding, no wound, clean hands, the way the Courier did in the old tales, the way they all do. And here's the thing that keeps me up: her copy's spreading. The marks she wrote are surfacing on the blank facing page, unbidden, in a hand that isn't hers. It's copying itself now. Curiosity was the door and she left it open. We'll burn the book, and it still won't be the last one somebody opens. Take the dossier of her habits. Learn how a careful person dies careless, so you don't.

objectives.label: the far kerb
objectives.text: Reach the frozen scholar
Recover the sealed notebook

gravemark_tundra_reader_in_the_snow.note: The scholar died at the stone with no wound and clean hands, killed by understanding as the Courier was in the old tales. Her half-copied inscription now surfaces on the blank facing page in a hand not hers — it is copying itself. Curiosity was the door; she left it open. Radu, who let her come because he too wanted to know, will burn the book, and knows another will always open the next one.

gravemark_tundra_the_twelfth_bed.title: The Twelfth Bed
gravemark_tundra_the_twelfth_bed.summary: A pilgrim band bound north for the Grave made camp at the tundra's edge and never broke it. Costel found the camp untouched — bedrolls laid out, a pot of stew gone to ice, footprints leading in and none leading out — and twelve beds where he can only account for eleven travelers. He wants it seen to and prayed over.
gravemark_tundra_the_twelfth_bed.offer: Pilgrims come through, bound north for the Grave, drawn to the thing the way moths take a candle, and mostly we feed them and warn them and they go on and we never hear. This band made camp at the march-edge and never struck it. I found it whole — bedrolls neat, a stew-pot gone to solid ice, packs still packed. Footprints leading in. None leading out. And twelve beds laid, friend, twelve, and the traders' roll I found in a pack names eleven of them. Go out. See it. Gather what should go to their families, and say a word over the ground, because I hadn't the voice for it and someone ought.
gravemark_tundra_the_twelfth_bed.progress: The pilgrims' camp stands unbroken at the march-edge. See it. Gather what should go home. Say the word.
gravemark_tundra_the_twelfth_bed.complete: You said the word. Thank you. I couldn't. Twelve beds, eleven names — I've stopped trying to make that come out even, and I'd counsel you to stop too. Footprints in, none out, and a cold stew nobody rose to eat because nothing there was hungry anymore. They came to stand near the Grave, and they got their wish, closer than they meant. We'll not follow their road, whatever draws down it. Take the keepsake — it was in the twelfth pack, the one with no name to it. Send it nowhere. There's no address for the twelfth bed.

objectives.label: the pilgrims' camp
objectives.text: See the unbroken camp
Gather what should go home

gravemark_tundra_the_twelfth_bed.note: The pilgrims made camp bound north for the Grave and never struck it — footprints in, none out, a stew gone to ice because nothing there was hungry anymore, and twelve beds laid where eleven names are known. They came to stand near the thing and stood nearer than they meant. Costel stopped trying to make the count come out even. There is no address for the twelfth bed, and the keepsake from it goes nowhere at all.

threadlands_cold_camp_bedrolls.title: Three Bedrolls, Cold Stew
threadlands_cold_camp_bedrolls.summary: A pilgrim family made camp on the north road and never broke it. Three bedrolls, a pot gone cold, footprints going in and none coming out. Matei found the child. Go see the camp he cannot walk back to.
threadlands_cold_camp_bedrolls.offer: There's a family camp up the pilgrim road, and I want you to look at it, because I looked at it and now I can't stop. Three bedrolls laid neat. Stew in the pot, cold, uneaten. Footprints in the snow going TO the fire, mind you — and not one coming away. The little one's still there. Sanda, her name is. She won't say a word about where the others went. Go up. Count the tracks yourself. Tell me I miscounted.
threadlands_cold_camp_bedrolls.progress: The camp sits off the pilgrim road north, where the snow lies flat. Go and count the tracks in and the tracks out.
threadlands_cold_camp_bedrolls.complete: You counted the same as me. In, and never out. And the girl standing there the whole while like she was waiting for the pot to boil. ...I brought her down to the inn. Somebody has to feed a child, even a quiet one. Take this — Ozana knots them for luck. I'll need mine for other things now.

objectives.label: the abandoned family camp
objectives.text: Find the family's camp on the north road
Ask Lazar what the pilgrims were walking toward

threadlands_cold_camp_bedrolls.note: Three bedrolls, laid neat. Footprints in, none out. The child Sanda was brought down warm and fed, and she has not once said where her family went. The snow kept their coming and lost their going.

threadlands_warm_taproom.title: The Last Warm Room
threadlands_warm_taproom.summary: Marcu keeps the one heated taproom for living visitors, and he keeps the fire like an argument he means to win. His wood is buried under new snow. Cut through to the woodpile and see the hearth stays lit.
threadlands_warm_taproom.offer: Everybody who comes off that road comes in shaking, and I am NOT the man who lets a fire go low on such a night — I'd sooner burn the chairs, and don't think I haven't looked at them. Trouble is my woodpile's gone under a foot of fresh, out back by the north fence. Dig me a path to it, and there's a bowl and the good chair by the flame for you. A body ought to be warm somewhere, even up here.
threadlands_warm_taproom.progress: The buried woodpile sits behind the inn, along the north fence. Reach it and clear the way.
threadlands_warm_taproom.complete: There. Listen to it catch — that's the best sound in the whole north, a fire that means it. Sit. You've earned the good chair and I've earned the pleasure of a full room. Take this, too. Reads by lamplight fine, and up here a jar that still burns honest is worth more than the coin.

objectives.label: the buried woodpile
objectives.text: Clear a path to the woodpile

threadlands_warm_taproom.note: The taproom stayed lit and full for a night, and Marcu burned no chairs. Warm is the wrong word for what this land has been learning. Warm is also the only word left worth defending.

threadlands_pale_road_stalkers.title: What Walks the Pilgrim Road
threadlands_pale_road_stalkers.summary: Something pale keeps pace with the pilgrims just past the treeline, never closing, never leaving. Florea has lost two travelers to whatever it patiently escorts. Break the pale-stalkers off the north road.
threadlands_pale_road_stalkers.offer: I've walked this road nine years and I know its dangers by their manners. These have none I recognize. Pale things, out past the tree-shadow — they don't rush a body and they don't quit one either. They pace you. Like the wolves used to, before the wolves learned worse. I've buried two this season who walked too far watching them. Thin them. Give the road back its plain honest cold.
threadlands_pale_road_stalkers.progress: The pale-stalkers keep to the treeline along the pilgrim road. Break them where they pace.
threadlands_pale_road_stalkers.complete: Six down, and the road breathes easier for a night. You saw how they moved, though. Not hunting. Herding — spread even, keeping a distance, like drovers who've all the time there is. I don't say that word aloud to the pilgrims. They've enough to carry. Take the striker. Flint's honest, whatever else has stopped being.

objectives.text: Break the pale-stalkers along the road

threadlands_pale_road_stalkers.note: Six pale-stalkers broken, and Florea watched them the way you watch a thing that is watching back. They did not hunt the pilgrims. They kept them — spaced even, unhurried, herding a road toward somewhere.

threadlands_surveyor_charts.title: The Charts All Agree
threadlands_surveyor_charts.summary: Thread-Surveyor Vlad charts the filaments' slow drift and sells the maps to whoever pays. Every fresh chart points at the same buried place. Dorin wants a second, independent reading before he trusts what the first ones say.
threadlands_surveyor_charts.offer: Vlad the surveyor draws the filaments as they drift — good work, sober work, and it's made him rich and made him quiet, which is a strange pairing in a merchant. Every chart he's drawn this winter arrows at one spot. Same spot. He says it's the pens drifting. I say pens don't conspire. Walk out to where the lines knot thickest and stand there. Read the drift with your own eyes. I'll trust two accounts before I trust none.
threadlands_surveyor_charts.progress: The filaments knot at a rise north of the tending-post. Reach it and read which way they lean.
threadlands_surveyor_charts.complete: You saw it lean the same as his charts. North and down, north and down, every strand. So it isn't the pens. It's the ground telling all of them one thing and hoping nobody compares notes. Vlad already knows. That's why he's rich AND quiet — knowledge you can sell twice if you never say it plain. Here. Read the drift yourself, next time. Trust no one map.

objectives.label: where the filaments knot
objectives.text: Read the filament drift where the lines knot
Compare the drift against Horia's old survey

threadlands_surveyor_charts.note: Every chart arrows at one buried place, and the ground drew them all. Vlad knew, and stayed rich, and stayed quiet. A thing you can sell twice is a thing you never say once.

threadlands_dead_filaments.title: The Thread That Went Dark
threadlands_dead_filaments.summary: A stretch of blue filament near the smithy has gone dark and cold — which sounds like mercy, until you notice frost-wights gather only where the thread has died. Clear the wights from the dead line so Barbu can work.
threadlands_dead_filaments.offer: You'd think a dead thread was good news, aye? Blue goes out, one less cursed leash in the world. But the wights don't gather on the LIVE lines. They gather where the blue's gone dark, like mourners, like the cold itself came to sit up with the corpse. Two of them by my forge now and my hammer won't ring true with them watching. Clear the dark stretch. I'll pay in steel a man can trust the temper of.
threadlands_dead_filaments.progress: Frost-wights gather along the dead filament past the smithy. Clear them from the dark line.
threadlands_dead_filaments.complete: Quiet again, and the anvil rings clean. But I'll tell you the thing that's kept me at the bellows past dark: a live thread has a use, awful as it is. A DEAD one has none — and still the cold comes to keep it company. What mourns a leash? Take the blade. I quenched it cold, the northern way. It keeps that.

objectives.text: Clear frost-wights from the dead filament

threadlands_dead_filaments.note: The dead thread draws the cold the way a wake draws mourners. A live filament has a cruel use; a dead one has none, and still the frost sits up with it. Barbu did not ask what grieves a leash.

threadlands_shepherd_turned_back.title: Iov's Losing Errand
threadlands_shepherd_turned_back.summary: Pilgrim-Shepherd Iov walks the road every day turning travelers back, and every day he fails a little. His voice is going. Lazar asks you to help Iov turn one more group before the road takes them all north.
threadlands_shepherd_turned_back.offer: Old Iov walks that road dawn to dark, planting himself in front of pilgrims and begging them to go home. Most days he turns back one or two. Most days that many go past him anyway, smiling, patient, like he's a fence-post that learned to talk. His throat's near gone from it. Go stand with him at the fork today. Two voices might turn a group his one can't. It's a losing errand. He knows. He walks it anyway. That's the whole of him.
threadlands_shepherd_turned_back.progress: Iov keeps his vigil at the road-fork north. Reach him, then turn back the pilgrims with him.
threadlands_shepherd_turned_back.complete: You turned three. THREE — I saw his face when the last one sat down and wept instead of walking. That's a good day up here; that's the best day he's had in a month of losing. He'll walk it again tomorrow and lose again. But three went home tonight who wouldn't have. Take the ward-sign. It means nothing — and that, Iov says, is exactly why it holds.

objectives.label: Iov's vigil at the fork
objectives.text: Join Iov at the road-fork
Walk the turned-back pilgrims down to Matei

threadlands_shepherd_turned_back.note: Three pilgrims turned home; the rest smiled and walked past a man begging in the snow. Iov will lose again tomorrow, and walk it again, and lose. Some vigils are kept not to win but so that losing has a witness.

threadlands_snow_that_wont_settle.title: Where the Snow Won't Lie
threadlands_snow_that_wont_settle.summary: Snow-Hunter Ozana sells furs and one rule: never camp where the snow won't settle. There are three such bare patches near the tending-post now, and pale-stalkers den in all three. Clear the dens on the ground the snow refuses.
threadlands_snow_that_wont_settle.offer: Ozana's got one rule she'll say for free and she says it like scripture: never camp where the snow won't settle. Warm ground. You know what warm ground means up here, or you should. Well, there's three bald patches gone bare by the post now, steam coming off them like breath, and the pale things have made dens of all three. Clear them out. And traveler — don't stand on the warm too long. The rule's for you too.
threadlands_snow_that_wont_settle.progress: Three bare, warm patches lie near the tending-post. Clear the pale-stalker dens on the ground the snow won't cover.
threadlands_snow_that_wont_settle.complete: Cleared, and you didn't linger — good. Ozana asked was your color right when you came back, and it was, so she'll trade with you. Warm ground and pale dens together, though. She's stopped selling furs for the far camps entirely. Says the money's not worth learning why the snow's ashamed to fall there. Take the skinner. First edge she ever trusted, she says.

objectives.text: Clear the dens on the ground that stays bare

threadlands_snow_that_wont_settle.note: The snow will not settle on warm ground, and pale things den where it won't. Ozana stopped selling furs to the far camps. Some knowledge costs more than the fur is worth, and she has always known the price of a thing.

threadlands_ilka_shells.title: The Shells Ilka Names
threadlands_ilka_shells.summary: Threadwarden Ilka tends the standing thread-shells and ties a knot for each one, naming them though regulations forbid it. Some shells have gone violent, thread-driven and wrong. Gavril needs the broken ones put down before Ilka tries to soothe one that can't be.
threadlands_ilka_shells.offer: Ilka tends the shells — the drawn, the standing dead, whatever you want to call the poor threaded things. She ties a little knot for every one and whispers it a name, which the Council says she mustn't, so she does it quiet. Trouble is a few have turned. Thread's got into them wrong and they lash at anything warm. She'll walk right up to one and try to name it calm. Put the turned ones down before she reaches the next. She'll grieve them. Let her grieve them whole.
threadlands_ilka_shells.progress: The turned thread-shells stand among Ilka's rows. Put down the broken ones before she reaches them.
threadlands_ilka_shells.complete: Done, and gently as such a thing can be done. She watched, and she tied a knot for each after — regulations be damned, she names the dead too. She's shaking, did you see? Her hands. She traced a live thread last week and swears it hummed back at her. Two notes. Of three. She won't say the third out loud. Take a cord of hers. She ties them for the ones she couldn't keep.

objectives.text: Put down the turned thread-shells
Report the turnings to Florea

threadlands_ilka_shells.note: Ilka named every shell she could not save, against every regulation, her hands shaking the whole while. She traced a live thread once and it hummed back — two notes of three. She has not said the third aloud. Saying it is the danger.

threadlands_courier_of_rolls.title: Moasa's Rolls-Courier
threadlands_courier_of_rolls.summary: The census rolls from the Listening Steppe are owed to Threadwarden Ilka, carried by a courier who has not arrived. Horia asks you to walk the pilgrim road and find where the rolls — and their carrier — stopped.
threadlands_courier_of_rolls.offer: There's a courier owed to come up from the Steppe, carrying Moasa's census rolls for Ilka to countersign — dry work, the driest, the kind nobody dies over. Except she's three days late and the Steppe swears she left on time. I've learned that up here 'left on time and didn't arrive' is a whole sentence with a grave at the end of it. Walk the road south. Find the rolls. Don't read them past the tally — a name's just a name until you say it.
threadlands_courier_of_rolls.progress: The courier's last camp lies south along the pilgrim road, toward the Steppe. Find where she stopped.
threadlands_courier_of_rolls.complete: Rolls intact, seal unbroken, and the courier sat beside them in the snow, unmarked, patient, cold. No wound. Nothing taken. She stopped to rest, they'll say, and the rest went on longer than a body allows. You brought the tally up sealed — that was right. Ilka will sign what she must and grieve what she can't. Take the dossier. Horia keeps facts. He does not keep assumptions.

objectives.label: the courier's last camp
objectives.text: Find the courier's last camp on the south road
Deliver the sealed rolls to Gavril for Ilka

threadlands_courier_of_rolls.note: The rolls came up sealed; the courier did not come up at all. No wound, nothing taken — she rested, and the rest outlasted her. On this road, 'left on time and never arrived' is one whole sentence, and it ends in the snow.

threadlands_incurious_traveler.title: The Man Who Stopped Wondering
threadlands_incurious_traveler.summary: A guest at the inn has gone strange — placid, incurious, unbothered by cold or company, forever tilting his head toward the north road. Marcu wants him spoken to before the other guests start tilting theirs.
threadlands_incurious_traveler.offer: I've a guest gone wrong in the quiet way, and I'd rather he unsettled you than the whole taproom. Came in shivering like a proper man three nights back. Now he doesn't shiver. Doesn't eat much. Doesn't ask a single thing — not the weather, not the road, not the price of his bed. Just sits, head cocked north, calm as a pond. Talk to him. See is there a man still in there. And if he asks you to walk north with him — you don't.
threadlands_incurious_traveler.progress: The changed guest sits by the north window of the inn. Speak with him and take his measure.
threadlands_incurious_traveler.complete: So he answered you plain and pleasant and told you nothing, and then asked would you walk him north to hear it better. I heard. You said no — good. That 'no' is a muscle up here and most folk never learn to flex it. He'll drift off up that road on his own soon, I expect, calm as you like. I can't stop a man from resting. Take the charm. It means nothing, and today that's the strongest thing I've got.

objectives.text: Ask Lazar when the guest first stopped shivering

objectives.label: the guest by the north window
objectives.text: Take the changed guest's measure

threadlands_incurious_traveler.note: The guest stopped shivering, stopped wondering, stopped everything but tilting north. He asked, pleasantly, to be walked closer so he could hear it better. Saying no was the whole test. Curiosity is the door; contentment is what waits on the far side of it.

threadlands_hunter_furs.title: Furs for the Cold Nights
threadlands_hunter_furs.summary: The pilgrims come up underdressed and shivering, and Ozana can't hunt fast enough to fur them all. Dorin asks you to bring frost-wight rime and pale hides so the inn can send no one back onto the road cold.
threadlands_hunter_furs.offer: Honest work for once, and I'll not pretend it's anything grander: folk come up that road dressed for a kinder country and they freeze for it. Ozana hunts what she can but she's one woman with one bow. Bring me hides off the pale things and rime off the wights — the rime cures the leather stiff against the wind, don't ask me why, it just does — and we'll fur every pilgrim who comes through. A warm traveler's a traveler who might still turn back.
threadlands_hunter_furs.progress: Pale hides and frost-rime come off the beasts near the tending-post. Bring enough to fur the pilgrims.
threadlands_hunter_furs.complete: That's a winter's furs right there, and a dozen backs that'll be warm tonight. Small thing, keeping folk warm. Smallest thing there is. But a body that's warm can still be talked into going home, and a body that's frozen only wants to lie down — and lying down is exactly what this land keeps whispering. So it's not small at all, is it. Here. The poultice is grown, not mixed. It'll mend what the cold's already bitten.

objectives.text: Gather frost-rime from the wights
Take pale hides for the leather

threadlands_hunter_furs.note: A winter's furs, and a dozen warm backs on the road. A warm traveler can still be argued home; a frozen one only wants to lie down. And lying down is the very thing the land keeps whispering. So the smallest kindness was the largest defense.

threadlands_weavers_loom.title: The Weaver at the Loom
threadlands_weavers_loom.summary: Deep off the road, thread-weavers have gathered at a knot of dead filament and work it like a loom, drawing more line out of nothing. Florea needs the weavers scattered before the pattern they're making finishes itself.
threadlands_weavers_loom.offer: There's a place off the road my patrols won't walk twice, and I've stopped ordering them to. The weavers gather there — around a dead thread, of all things — and they WORK it. Draw new blue line out of the empty air and lace it into the old, patient as women at a loom. I don't know what they're making. I know I don't want to see it done. Go break them off it. Scatter the loom before the loom finishes the cloth.
threadlands_weavers_loom.progress: The thread-weavers work the dead knot deep off the north road. Scatter them before the pattern completes.
threadlands_weavers_loom.complete: Scattered — and the line they'd drawn went slack and grey the moment the last of them fell, thank whatever's left to thank. But you saw the pattern half-made, didn't you. It wasn't random. It was a MAP. Lines and a mark where the lines all met, and the mark sat right where Vlad's charts keep pointing. They weren't weaving cloth. They were weaving directions. Take the ward. It refuses to mean anything, loudly, and that's its whole armor.

objectives.text: Scatter the weavers at the loom

threadlands_weavers_loom.note: The weavers drew new thread from empty air and laced a pattern half-done — not cloth but a map, with a mark where every line converged, over the same buried place the charts keep naming. They were weaving directions. Someone, somewhere, meant to follow them.

threadlands_hum_along_finger.title: A Thread That Answers
threadlands_hum_along_finger.summary: Ilka traced a live filament and it hummed back — two notes of a three-note thing. The Council wants it confirmed by someone deniable. Gavril hands you the errand nobody senior will sign their name to: go trace the thread, and listen.
threadlands_hum_along_finger.offer: This one didn't come down through me and I'll thank you to forget my face after. Ilka traced a live filament last week and it hummed. Back. At her. Two notes, she says, of three, and she won't speak the third and I won't make her. Word's got up the road to the Council, and the Council wants it CONFIRMED — but confirmed by nobody, you understand. By a pair of hands they can disown. So. Walk out, find a live blue line, lay one finger on it, and tell me what it does. One finger. Not two. Not long.
threadlands_hum_along_finger.progress: A live filament runs bright near the northern knot. Trace it — one finger, briefly — and listen for what answers.
threadlands_hum_along_finger.complete: ...It hummed for you too. Two notes. And you took your finger off before the third, which is why you're standing here telling me and Ilka's still up nights. That's what the Council wanted proven, and proven deniably: the threads have started answering. Radovan's been waiting for exactly this, carried by exactly someone like you. Take the binding. Every legal gram comes with paperwork. This gram has none, and that's the point.

objectives.label: the live filament
objectives.text: Trace the live thread and listen
Have Horia record what the thread answered

threadlands_hum_along_finger.note: The live thread hummed two notes of three, and answered a living finger the way a leash tests a hand. The Council wanted it proven by someone they could disown. Radovan has waited all winter for this carried up by exactly such a someone. The confirming was the recruitment.

threadlands_sanda_will_not_speak.title: The Girl Who Will Not Say
threadlands_sanda_will_not_speak.summary: Sanda of the cold camp eats, sleeps, and will not say one word about where her family went. Matei can't reach her. He asks you to sit the long watch with the child and let her speak, or not, in her own time.
threadlands_sanda_will_not_speak.offer: The girl's mending in the body and not at all in the rest of her. She'll take bread. She'll sleep by the fire. But ask her one gentle thing about that camp and she goes still as the snow itself and looks past you at the door. I can't sit with her the way it needs — I've the road and the woodpile and my own bad dreams. Sit with her a while. Don't press. Just be a warm body in the room that isn't going anywhere. Sometimes that's the whole of it.
threadlands_sanda_will_not_speak.progress: Sanda keeps to the corner by the inn fire. Sit the watch with her and let her speak in her own time.
threadlands_sanda_will_not_speak.complete: She spoke. Not much — you'd hardly call it speaking. She said her mother told them all to be very quiet and listen, because someone was going to explain everything, and it was going to be so simple, and then it would stop being cold. And her mother stood up to listen closer. And Sanda... didn't. That's why she's here. She was the only one who kept her hands over her ears. Take this. It's a knot for a name kept. She's the name.

objectives.label: Sanda's corner by the fire
objectives.text: Sit the long watch with Sanda
Tell Marcu what the child finally said

threadlands_sanda_will_not_speak.note: Sanda's mother told them to be quiet and listen, because someone would explain everything, and it would be simple, and then it would stop being cold. She stood to hear it closer. Sanda kept her hands over her ears. That is the whole difference between a survivor and a bedroll.

threadlands_buried_place.title: Where All the Lines Point
threadlands_buried_place.summary: The charts, the weavers' map, and the filament drift all name one buried place north of the post. Dorin has stopped selling the maps and started fearing them. He asks you to walk to the spot itself and read what the ground is broadcasting.
threadlands_buried_place.offer: I sold those charts all winter and told myself they were curiosities. They're not. They're a finger, and I finally followed where it's pointing, on paper, and I didn't like the address. Everything agrees now — Vlad's drift, the weavers' cloth, Ilka's humming thread — one spot, north and down, under the snow that won't settle. I'll not sell another map. But someone with a steadier stomach than mine should stand there and read it, once, so we know what we're all pretending not to. Read it. Don't dig. Come back and tell me it's nothing.
threadlands_buried_place.progress: The buried place lies north of the post, where the snow steams and the lines converge. Reach it and read what it broadcasts — but do not dig.
threadlands_buried_place.complete: You stood on it, and the ground was warm through your boots, and the blue ran INTO it like water down a drain, and the frost-wights kept their distance the way a dog keeps clear of its master's chair. And you didn't dig. Good. Digging is what it wants and patience is the only thing it hasn't got a use for yet. I'm burning the charts tonight. Won't help. Take the lens — ground so fine it corrects for the truth. You'll want to see clearly, where you're going next.

objectives.label: the buried place
objectives.text: Read the buried place the lines all name
Clear the wights ringing the warm ground

threadlands_buried_place.note: The ground was warm through the boots, and every blue thread drained into it, and the wights kept their distance the way a dog clears its master's chair. The lines all pointed here because here is where something is listening. Dorin burned his charts. It will not help. Patience is the only tool it lacks.

threadlands_pot_hook_repair.title: A Pot That Won't Hang
threadlands_pot_hook_repair.summary: The inn's great stew-pot has lost its hook and the whole taproom eats cold until it hangs again. Barbu needs good bog-iron from a wight-haunted seep to forge a new one. Fetch the iron and get the pot back over the fire.
threadlands_pot_hook_repair.offer: Petty little job and I love it for being petty — a job with no doom in it at all, imagine. Marcu's stew-pot dropped its hook clean through and now the whole inn eats cold, and a cold supper up here is a small cruelty I can actually FIX. I need bog-iron for the new hook, from the seep past the fence, only the wights have taken a liking to it. Chase them off, bring me a good lump, and I'll have that pot swinging over the flame by dark. Warm supper. That's the whole ambition.
threadlands_pot_hook_repair.progress: Bog-iron lies in the seep past the north fence, where the wights gather. Bring Barbu a good lump.
threadlands_pot_hook_repair.complete: Ha — hear that? That's the sound of a pot hung right, and a room that'll eat hot tonight. Bog-iron's ugly work and honest metal, and it's the one craft the stone's got no clever use for, which is exactly why I love the making of a plain pot hook. No doom in a hook. Just supper. Take one for your own fire, wherever you hang it. A body should eat warm somewhere.

objectives.text: Chase the wights off the bog-iron seep

threadlands_pot_hook_repair.note: The pot hangs, the room eats hot, and Barbu forged a thing the stone has no use for — a plain hook, no doom in it, only supper. In a land learning to listen, a job with no doom in it is nearly a prayer.

threadlands_chalk_prints.title: The Prints on the Shelter Wall
threadlands_chalk_prints.summary: The pilgrims chalk their handprints on the road-shelter wall for luck before walking on. One print has gone warm to the touch and faintly copper. Lazar wants it found and understood before someone presses their palm to it.
threadlands_chalk_prints.offer: There's an old road-shelter up north, and pilgrims have pressed chalk handprints on its wall for years — a little luck before the last cold stretch, harmless as a child's game. Except one of them's gone WARM. I put my palm near it and pulled back like it was a stove. Faint copper to it, too, in the chalk. I don't know whose hand made it or when. Go find it. Don't press yours to it, whatever the fool part of you says. Just find it and tell me it can be scrubbed off.
threadlands_chalk_prints.progress: The chalk prints cover the road-shelter wall to the north. Find the warm one — but do not press your palm to it.
threadlands_chalk_prints.complete: You found it. Warm, coppered, and here's the part that's kept me from sleeping: it's a CHILD's hand. Small. And there's no child among the pilgrims who came through these last weeks — none living, anyhow. I'll not scrub it. Scrubbing a thing sometimes tells the thing you noticed, and I'd rather it thought us blind. Take the hex-sign. Paint it slow, in the wet, so the wet can teach it patience. We'll need patience more than luck.

objectives.label: the road-shelter wall
objectives.text: Find the warm chalk print
Ask Matei if any child came through the shelter

threadlands_chalk_prints.note: One chalk print has gone warm and coppered — a child's small hand, and no living child came through the shelter. Lazar would not scrub it clean, lest scrubbing tell the thing it had been seen. In the north you learn to notice without letting the noticing show.

threadlands_deep_bed_light.title: Light That Does Not Break the Dark
threadlands_deep_bed_light.summary: Horia is copying the old survey-books before the cold ruins them, but no lantern reads true near the live threads — flame lies about the ink. He needs cultured light from a lichen-bed grown in true dark, harvested where pale-stalkers guard it.
threadlands_deep_bed_light.offer: I'm copying the survey-books before the damp takes them, and every honest lantern I own goes strange near a live thread — the flame bends, the ink reads wrong, a six becomes a five and a warden's name becomes a stranger's. There's a lichen-bed off the road that grows a cold light, cultured in the true dark, and THAT light doesn't lie. The pale things guard it, naturally. Nothing worth having up here comes unguarded. Bring me a bed of it, unbroken, and I'll copy the rolls true.
threadlands_deep_bed_light.progress: The cultured lichen-bed grows in the dark hollow the pale-stalkers guard. Bring it to Horia unbroken.
threadlands_deep_bed_light.complete: Cold light with no flame to bend — and look, the ink reads true under it, every six a six, every name its own. Flame-light flatters and lies; this stuff just... belongs to the dark, so the dark's got no quarrel with it. I'll copy the rolls right now, while the reading's honest. Take the lantern for yourself. It doesn't break the true dark. It keeps it company. Up here, that's the only kind of light you can trust.

objectives.text: Clear the pale-stalkers from the lichen-bed

objectives.label: the cultured lichen-bed
objectives.text: Harvest the cold-light bed unbroken

threadlands_deep_bed_light.note: Flame-light bends near the live threads and lies about the ink — a six for a five, a warden's name for a stranger's. Cultured light does not break the dark; it keeps the dark company, and so the dark lets it read true. Horia copied the rolls honest by a light that belongs to the cold.

threadlands_daily_tending_rounds.title: The Tending Rounds
threadlands_daily_tending_rounds.summary: Ilka's rows must be walked daily — the turned shells put down, the frost driven off the standing ones before it settles into them. It never ends and it never can. Walk today's round for the warden whose hands have started to shake.
threadlands_daily_tending_rounds.offer: Same round as ever, and it wants doing every single day the sun bothers to rise. Ilka can't walk the whole of it anymore — her hands, you've seen. So the turned shells need putting down before they lash a pilgrim, and the frost needs driving off the standing rows before it beds in and turns more. It's not a task you finish. It's a task you keep. Walk it today. Tomorrow it'll want walking again. That's the north for you: the work is the vigil.
threadlands_daily_tending_rounds.progress: The turned shells rise among the rows, and the frost gathers on the standing. Walk today's tending round.
threadlands_daily_tending_rounds.complete: Walked, and the rows are quiet for a night. Ilka tied her knots and named her dead and thanked you in that cracked voice of hers. It'll all want doing again tomorrow — the frost doesn't rest, so neither can the round. Here's your day's due. Come back when the sun does. The vigil isn't kept once. It's kept daily, or it isn't kept at all.

objectives.text: Put down the turned shells on the round
Drive the frost off the standing rows

threadlands_daily_tending_rounds.note: The turned put down, the frost driven off, the rows quiet for one more night. Ilka named her dead and thanked you in her cracked voice. Tomorrow it wants walking again. In the north the vigil is not a deed but a habit, kept daily or not kept at all.

threadlands_daily_road_watch.title: The Road-Fork Watch
threadlands_daily_road_watch.summary: Every day the pale-stalkers drift back to the pilgrim road, and every day they must be broken off it before dusk brings the pilgrims. Stand the day's watch at the fork with Florea and keep the road plain.
threadlands_daily_road_watch.offer: The road needs watching, and it needs it fresh each morning, because the pale things drift back overnight like they've nowhere else they're wanted — and something's called them here, so I suppose they haven't. Break them off the fork before dusk. That's when the pilgrims come, and a pilgrim who sees a pale thing pacing him decides it's a guide instead of a warning, nine times of ten. Keep the road plain today. It won't stay plain. Nothing here does. But today, keep it.
threadlands_daily_road_watch.progress: The pale-stalkers drift to the fork by dusk. Break them off the road before the pilgrims come.
threadlands_daily_road_watch.complete: Broken off, and the dusk pilgrims walked a plain road for once, none the wiser what walked it before them — which is how I like them: unwise and alive. They'll be back tomorrow, the pale things. So will you, I hope. It's a job you can't win, only keep. But a road kept plain one more day is a handful of folk who reach the far side still themselves. Take your due.

objectives.text: Break the pale-stalkers off the fork

objectives.label: the road-fork watch
objectives.text: Stand the dusk watch at the fork

threadlands_daily_road_watch.note: The road kept plain one more day, the dusk pilgrims none the wiser what paced it before them. They will drift back by morning. Some roads can only be won by keeping, dusk after dusk, a handful of strangers still themselves at the far side.

threadlands_surveyor_missing.title: Vlad Went to Check His Own Charts
threadlands_surveyor_missing.summary: Thread-Surveyor Vlad, rich and quiet all winter, has walked out to verify his own charts at the buried place and not come back. Horia asks you to find him before curiosity finishes what caution long postponed.
threadlands_surveyor_missing.offer: Vlad's gone. The one man who KNEW better — who got rich precisely by never following his own finger — walked out three dawns ago to stand on the spot his charts keep naming, and hasn't come back. I think the wondering finally beat the wisdom. It does, up here, given time; a man can only draw the same arrow so many winters before he needs to see the target. Go after him. If he's reading the ground, you stop him reading. If he's past stopping... bring back the charts, at least. They shouldn't be left lying where the thing can use them.
threadlands_surveyor_missing.progress: Vlad walked north to the buried place his charts name. Find him before the reading finishes.
threadlands_surveyor_missing.complete: You found him standing on it, you say — calm, warm, his own chart open in his hands, tracing the lines with his finger and MOUTHING the mark at the center like he'd nearly got the word. And you closed the chart and turned him round and walked him back before he said it aloud. He'll not survey again; something's gone quiet in him that used to be sharp. But he's breathing, and he isn't a bedroll, and up here that's the win. Take the still-run. Brewed in the one cold place it left you. Drink it angry.

objectives.label: Vlad at the buried place
objectives.text: Find Vlad before the reading finishes
Return Vlad's charts to Dorin for burning

threadlands_surveyor_missing.note: Vlad, who grew rich never following his own finger, stood on the spot his charts named and mouthed the word at its center, nearly. He was turned round in time. Something sharp in him has gone quiet since. On this road, breathing and turned back is the whole of victory — and it still costs.

threadlands_pilgrim_ledger.title: The Names That Didn't Arrive
threadlands_pilgrim_ledger.summary: Ilka's countersigned rolls list every pilgrim who entered the Threadlands, and Black Night's rolls list every one who arrived. The two counts no longer match. Gavril needs the gap walked and confirmed before the discrepancy climbs to the Council.
threadlands_pilgrim_ledger.offer: Here's a clerk's nightmare with a grave under it. Ilka signs off every pilgrim who ENTERS the Threadlands. Odeta down in Black Night signs off every one who ARRIVES. For years the two numbers kissed. This season Ilka's is bigger — folk who came in and never came out the far side, and no camp, no cairn, no body to account for a single one. Walk the road between the two tallies and find where the names fall out of the count. Quiet, mind. A discrepancy that reaches the Council reaches Radovan, and Radovan's been fishing for exactly this.
threadlands_pilgrim_ledger.progress: The count falls short somewhere between the entering-roll and the arriving-roll. Walk the road and find where the names drop out.
threadlands_pilgrim_ledger.complete: So they don't fall out at any one place. They thin the whole way — a few fewer at every mile-post, steady, like water losing itself to dry ground. No violence, no scene. Just a road that keeps a little less of everyone who walks it. That's the discrepancy, and now it's confirmed, and now I have to decide who I let it reach. Radovan wants it. The Council wants not to know it. Take the writ — stamped, un-stamped, no third impression. Some counts are safer left uncounted.

objectives.label: the southern mile-posts
objectives.text: Walk the road where the count falls short
Have Horia reconcile the two rolls

threadlands_pilgrim_ledger.note: The names do not vanish at one place; the road keeps a little less of everyone the whole way north, like water lost to dry ground. The discrepancy is real and now Gavril must choose who learns it. Radovan is fishing for exactly this count. Some tallies are safer left un-tallied.

threadlands_hunters_last_rule.title: Ozana's Last Rule
threadlands_hunters_last_rule.summary: Snow-Hunter Ozana has stopped selling furs and started giving them away, which from Ozana means she's decided she won't need the money. Dorin fears she means to walk north herself. Find her camp and learn her intention.
threadlands_hunters_last_rule.offer: Ozana's giving her furs away. Ozana. The woman who'd charge her own sister for kindling and call it a life-lesson. When a hoarder starts giving, it's not generosity — it's a person who's decided she won't be needing things much longer. I think she means to walk north. To the warm ground. To rest. Go to her camp. Don't argue her out of it, that never works on the ones who've already decided. Just... be there. Sometimes a witness turns a body round when an argument only sets their heels.
threadlands_hunters_last_rule.progress: Ozana's camp lies at the edge of the far snow, where she has always camped alone. Find her, and be there.
threadlands_hunters_last_rule.complete: She was sitting where the snow wouldn't settle — HER rule, the one she says like scripture, and she'd walked out to break it on purpose. She'd have gone, too. But you came, and you didn't lecture, you just sat down in the warm beside her, in the danger with her, and after a while she said 'you shouldn't be here' and you said 'neither should you' and she laughed — LAUGHED — and got up and walked back to the cold with you. That laugh saved her. Take the token. She named it herself. Choose whose, she said. Choose out loud.

objectives.label: Ozana's camp on the warm ground
objectives.text: Find Ozana at the far snow
Bring Ozana down to Marcu's fire

threadlands_hunters_last_rule.note: Ozana walked out to break her own last rule and sit on the warm ground until it took her. A witness sat down beside her in the danger, argued nothing, and after a while she laughed and rose and walked back to the cold. The land offers rest. Only company outbids it.

threadlands_section_report.title: Ilka's Section Report
threadlands_section_report.summary: Ilka's report on the thinned threads is written, sealed, and owed to the Council at Black Night — and it is exactly the deniable evidence Radovan has waited all winter to receive. Florea asks you to carry it south, knowing what carrying it makes you.
threadlands_section_report.offer: It's done. Ilka's written the whole of it — the humming threads, the thinning count, the buried place the lines all name — sealed it, and it has to go down to the Council. And I'll be honest with you the way this road taught me to be honest: carrying this makes you a piece. Radovan wants exactly this report, brought by exactly a deniable hand, and delivering it moves his game and not ours. But not delivering it leaves us up here alone with the thing, unheard. There's no clean way to carry it. There's only carrying it, or not. Will you?
threadlands_section_report.progress: Ilka's sealed report is owed to the Council at Black Night. Carry it south to the border, and choose whether it stays sealed.
threadlands_section_report.complete: Carried, and carried sealed — you didn't read it, which is the only mercy left in a job with none. It's gone south now, into Radovan's hands, and it'll do exactly what he needs and something for us too, maybe, if we're lucky, which we're not. You've stopped being a traveler on this road and started being a courier on someone's board. That's how it works up here. That's how it's always worked. Take the veil. It wards the attention of the dead. You'll want that where the report is taking your name.

objectives.label: the southern border-post
objectives.text: Carry Ilka's report to the Black Night road
Sign the report over to Gavril at the post

threadlands_section_report.note: Ilka's sealed report went south — the humming threads, the thinning count, the buried place — into exactly the hands that waited all winter for a deniable someone to bring it. Carried sealed, unread, which was the only mercy in it. The road stopped handing the traveler errands and started handing them a role.

threadlands_first_edition.title: The Book the Later Copies Forgot
threadlands_first_edition.summary: Copying the survey-rolls by cultured light, Horia found an entry the newer editions all omit — a first-hand account of the Threadlands before the filaments, deliberately un-copied. He wants the original page confirmed against the stone it describes.
threadlands_first_edition.offer: I told you the flame-light lies. Well, by the cultured light I found something the flame was helping to hide: the FIRST edition of the north-survey has a page that every later copy leaves out. On purpose — the omission's too clean to be an accident. It describes this land BEFORE the threads. Says there was a marker-stone here, a warding-stone, that someone raised to keep a buried thing quiet — and that the thread only started after the stone was thrown down. Go find where the page says the stone stood. Confirm it's gone. I need to know if we forgot a defense or lost one.
threadlands_first_edition.progress: The first-edition page names where the warding-stone once stood, north of the post. Reach it and confirm the stone is gone.
threadlands_first_edition.complete: Gone, root and socket, just as you say — nothing but a scar in the ground and the blue running thick right through where it stood. So it wasn't ALWAYS like this. Someone, long before us, knew enough to raise a stone against the buried thing, and someone after knew enough to throw it down. The later editions were paid to forget both. We didn't lose a battle up here. We lost the memory that there was one to fight. Take the draught — everything the later editions were paid to forget, tasted at once. Drink it knowing.

objectives.label: where the warding-stone stood
objectives.text: Confirm the warding-stone is thrown down
Ask Dorin who profited from forgetting the stone

threadlands_first_edition.note: The first edition remembered a warding-stone raised against the buried thing, and every later copy was paid to forget it. The stone is thrown down, the blue runs thick through its scar, and the thread began the day it fell. The north did not lose a war. It lost the memory that one was ever fought.

listening_steppe_a_place_that_turns_you_.title: A Place That Turns You Back
listening_steppe_a_place_that_turns_you_.summary: Nedelcu holds the last hearth on the north road, where the grass takes over and the wind carries a note. Walk the pilgrim track to the marker stone and see how many are standing in the field this week.
listening_steppe_a_place_that_turns_you_.offer: This post used to be where I turned drunks back toward Vetka. Now I turn back the sober ones -- families, mostly, walking north with their good boots on and no reason they can name. Go up the track to the Kneeling Stone and count the ones already stopped in the grass. Then come tell me I'm getting old and seeing things.
listening_steppe_a_place_that_turns_you_.progress: The Kneeling Stone sits up the north pilgrim track, where the road stops pretending to be a road. Count the standing folk.
listening_steppe_a_place_that_turns_you_.complete: Nineteen. You counted nineteen standing in a field with no crop in it, all faced the same way, none of them cold enough to shiver. My father held this post forty years and turned back thieves. I hold it and turn back the wind. Take the cord -- it's meant to keep a name knotted to a body. Keep yours knotted.

objectives.label: the Kneeling Stone
objectives.text: Reach the Kneeling Stone up the track
Bring Nedelcu your count

listening_steppe_a_place_that_turns_you_.note: Nineteen stood in the grass, faced one way, and did not shiver. Nedelcu turns back the ones he can. The wind hands nothing back.

listening_steppe_the_grass_that_learned_.title: The Grass That Learned to Wait
listening_steppe_the_grass_that_learned_.summary: Toma runs sheep on the open steppe, and something long-legged and quiet has learned to walk the flock's edge without spooking it. Thin the prowlers off the grazing before the ewes lamb.
listening_steppe_the_grass_that_learned_.offer: A wolf you can shout at. These -- prowlers, my grandfather called them, though his were shyer -- they pace the flock's edge and the sheep don't even lift their heads. That's the part I can't sleep on. The dogs won't bark. The ewes won't run. Something's telling them not to. Thin the prowlers on the west grazing before lambing, and I'll cut you a haunch off the fattest.
listening_steppe_the_grass_that_learned_.progress: Steppe-Prowlers work the west grazing where the grass grows long. Cull them before the ewes lamb.
listening_steppe_the_grass_that_learned_.complete: That's honest work, and my thanks. The dogs found their voices again once the grass was clear -- barked all night at nothing, which is how a dog ought to behave. Take the skinner. It knapped clean through the last one's hide, and that hide came off like it wanted to.

objectives.text: Cull Steppe-Prowlers on the west grazing

listening_steppe_the_grass_that_learned_.note: The prowlers paced the flock and the flock did not run. Toma's dogs bark again -- at nothing, all night, the way frightened things should.

listening_steppe_the_courier_who_arrived.title: The Courier Who Arrived Early
listening_steppe_the_courier_who_arrived.summary: A rider came down the north road with a sealed satchel and no memory of setting out. Serban wants the satchel carried on to the Accord's agent, unopened, before whoever it was routed to decides to read it.
listening_steppe_the_courier_who_arrived.offer: A rider came in at dawn -- good horse, Accord seal, a satchel sealed twice over. Only she can't tell me where she started, nor when, and her fingernails are clean as a christening though the road's a fortnight of mud. She keeps saying the letter's for whoever reads it. That's the part that closes my throat. I'll not have it opened here. Carry it up to the merchant -- he keeps the quiet channel -- and give it sealed. Sealed, you hear me. Do not read it.
listening_steppe_the_courier_who_arrived.progress: Carry the twice-sealed satchel up to Iancu at the trade-post. Give it sealed. The temptation to look is the whole trap.
listening_steppe_the_courier_who_arrived.complete: Sealed. Good. You brought it sealed. I watched your hands the whole way in and your hands stayed honest, and that's rarer up here than you'd think. The rider's asleep in the loft and won't wake for a day, and when she does she'll have lost the fortnight the way you lose a dream. Take the charm. It's called incurious for a reason. Wear it in the grass.

objectives.text: Carry the twice-sealed satchel, unopened
Give the sealed satchel to Iancu
Return to Serban

listening_steppe_the_courier_who_arrived.note: The courier arrived without setting out, her nails clean, the seal unbroken. It was carried on sealed. The letter is still addressed to whoever reads it. Someone, somewhere, will.

listening_steppe_what_the_merchant_wont_.title: What the Merchant Won't Carry
listening_steppe_what_the_merchant_wont_.summary: Iancu's north-road trade has dried up -- not from bandits, but because the pilgrims buy nothing and the traders won't cross the standing fields. Gather his ruined stock and hear why the Gale-Swarm follows the still ones.
listening_steppe_what_the_merchant_wont_.offer: I've sold on this road since I was a boy holding my father's ledger, and I have never once failed to move a cart of salt. Now the pilgrims walk past my stall like it's furniture, and the swarm -- the gale, the biting cloud -- it doesn't come for me. It comes for the still ones, settles on them like they're already meat, and they don't so much as blink. Bring me back what the swarm spoiled off my north cart. I want to see how a thing decides what's already dead.
listening_steppe_what_the_merchant_wont_.progress: Gather Iancu's swarm-spoiled goods scattered along the north cart-track, and bring what the Gale-Swarm marked.
listening_steppe_what_the_merchant_wont_.complete: Look at it. Everything the swarm touched is the stock I'd have sold to a living man -- bread, cloth, candle-tallow. Everything it left is what you'd bury someone with. It's not spoiling my goods. It's sorting them. A merchant knows a sorting when he sees one, and this is the coldest inventory I ever took. Here -- the striker. Light your own fire out here. Don't trust the warm ground to do it.

objectives.text: Recover swarm-spoiled goods from the north track
Drive off the Gale-Swarm that settled on the still ones

listening_steppe_what_the_merchant_wont_.note: The swarm took only what the living use, and left the shrouds. Iancu called it a sorting. He has never in his life been wrong about an inventory.

listening_steppe_the_smith_who_hears_the.title: The Smith Who Hears the Ground
listening_steppe_the_smith_who_hears_the.summary: Petru's forge floor has gone warm from beneath, and his hammer-rhythm keeps slipping a beat he never strikes. He needs bog-iron from the old sink to re-lay a cold hearthstone before the warmth reaches his anvil.
listening_steppe_the_smith_who_hears_the.offer: Forty years at this anvil and I know my own hammer like my own heartbeat. Lately the beat slips -- one strike goes missing, always the same place in the round, like the ground's keeping time with me and getting it wrong on purpose. And the floor's warm now, warm from under, where a forge floor's got no business being anything but scorched-cold at dawn. Fetch me bog-iron off the old sink so I can re-lay a stone that doesn't listen. I'll not stand on a hot floor and call it nothing.
listening_steppe_the_smith_who_hears_the.progress: Bog-iron lies in the old sink east of the forge. Bring enough to re-lay a hearthstone that stays cold.
listening_steppe_the_smith_who_hears_the.complete: Good iron, river-fed, cold to the core -- that's the stuff. I'll set it deep and maybe the floor forgets whatever it's started remembering. The slipped beat, though. That I can't re-lay. My father used to say a forge that keeps its own time is a forge with a guest. Take the pot-hook. Bog-iron, hung over an honest fire. Some old shapes still work.

objectives.text: Gather bog-iron from the old sink
