listening_steppe_the_smith_who_hears_the.note: Petru re-laid his hearthstone cold. The floor is warm again by evening. His hammer still drops the same beat, in the same place, as if something is filling the silence.

listening_steppe_count_the_standing_folk.title: Count the Standing Folk
listening_steppe_count_the_standing_folk.summary: Serban keeps the parish rolls, and the standing pilgrims are still on them -- alive, taxed, expected home. He asks you to walk the field and read them, one by one, without letting any of them read you back.
listening_steppe_count_the_standing_folk.offer: I keep the rolls. Births, deaths, the tithe. And I've a page now I don't know how to close -- fourteen souls standing in the north field who are neither at home nor in my burial book. They breathe. They pay no tithe. They will not answer to their names. Walk out and read them for me -- who they are, what they face -- and mark it in the roll. But hear me: read them, do not answer them. If one turns and you feel you almost understand it, look at your boots and walk away. That almost is the whole of the danger.
listening_steppe_count_the_standing_folk.progress: Walk the north field and read the standing folk. Read them; do not answer them. The almost-understanding is the killing part.
listening_steppe_count_the_standing_folk.complete: Fourteen names, and now I can at least write beside each one where it stood and which way it faced. They all face a little east of north -- toward Black Night, toward the grave the maps won't draw. My grandfather closed pages with a death-date. I close this one with a compass-bearing. Take the token. It keeps a name spoken over you. Out here, a spoken name is a rope home.

objectives.label: the standing field
objectives.text: Read the standing folk in the north field
Put down the ones already gone past reading
Report the names to Serban

listening_steppe_count_the_standing_folk.note: Fourteen still stand, faced east of north, toward a grave no map draws. Serban closed the page with a bearing instead of a date. Some of them, he did not write down. Those, you had to put down yourself.

listening_steppe_the_reader_of_hessik.title: The Reader of Hessik
listening_steppe_the_reader_of_hessik.summary: A scholar bought a strange primer off Iancu's cart last spring and walked north with it, unpaid. Iancu wants the debt collected -- or the man found -- before the book finishes teaching him to read the wrong things.
listening_steppe_the_reader_of_hessik.offer: A scholar -- soft hands, city coat -- bought a book off my cart last spring and promised to pay on his way back down. Practical Foundations, the alchemy primer, Hessik's. He said it was for reading colors in the wells. Well. He never came back down. And I hear there's a man near the far waystone who reads the grass now, out loud, with his coat still buttoned wrong from the day he stopped changing it. Go collect my debt, or what's left to collect it from. Bring me the primer if the man's past caring.
listening_steppe_the_reader_of_hessik.progress: Find the scholar near the far waystone, collect the debt or the primer. He reads the grass aloud now.
listening_steppe_the_reader_of_hessik.complete: The book, and no man to pay it. That's a loss I'll wear. He read too well, you say -- read the colors, then read past them, and the reading kept him the way the grass keeps everything up here. Hessik's primer teaches you to see the signal. It never once teaches you to look away. Keep the method-notes. Learn the colors. Then, for the love of your own name, stop reading.

objectives.label: the far waystone
objectives.text: Find the scholar at the far waystone
Recover the Hessik primer
Bring the primer back to Iancu

listening_steppe_the_reader_of_hessik.note: The scholar read the colors, then read past them, and the grass kept him. Hessik's book teaches the eye to see the signal. It teaches nothing about closing that eye. Iancu shelved it unsold.

listening_steppe_a_warm_lane_in_cold_cou.title: A Warm Lane in Cold Country
listening_steppe_a_warm_lane_in_cold_cou.summary: Vlaicu found a lane of steppe where the frost never sets and the dust lies in perfect parallel lines. He needs it walked and scanned before the wardens are ordered to march men down it in the cold.
listening_steppe_a_warm_lane_in_cold_cou.offer: There's a lane out east where it doesn't frost. Grass green as spring in a field white as a shroud all around it, and the dust down the middle lies in lines so straight you'd swear a clerk ruled them. The captain wants to bivouac the cold-marches there because it's warm. I want someone with alchemist's eyes to walk it first and tell me what's making it warm, because warm out here is the one thing that's never once meant kind. Walk the warm lane. Read the ground. Then we'll argue with the captain.
listening_steppe_a_warm_lane_in_cold_cou.progress: Walk the warm lane east of the post. Read the ground where the dust runs in ruled lines.
listening_steppe_a_warm_lane_in_cold_cou.complete: Green under a shifting light, you say -- not fire-green, not thermal, but a green that slides toward orange when you don't look straight at it. That settles the bivouac. No man of mine sleeps warm on a floor that's writing itself. I'll tell the captain the lane's cursed and let him think it's superstition. Sometimes the true word and the useful word aren't the same word. Take the ward -- chalk-drawn, but it's held a night before.

objectives.label: the warm lane
objectives.text: Walk and read the frost-free warm lane
Clear the shades drawn to the warm lane

listening_steppe_a_warm_lane_in_cold_cou.note: The lane stays warm, the dust stays ruled, and the green slides orange when unwatched. Vlaicu called it cursed to the captain and true to himself. The bivouac went elsewhere. The lane keeps writing.

listening_steppe_the_innkeepers_empty_be.title: The Innkeeper's Empty Beds
listening_steppe_the_innkeepers_empty_be.summary: Sorin's hearth is the last full table before the open steppe, but his rooms sit empty now that no one comes back down the road. He asks you to fetch marsh-tallow so at least the lamp in his window keeps burning for anyone who does.
listening_steppe_the_innkeepers_empty_be.offer: Come in, sit, the stew's honest and the fire's real -- both of which are getting scarce north of here. I'll be plain: my beds are empty and my window-lamp's the last light a body sees before the grass. I keep it lit on principle. But my tallow's near out, and the chandler's gone quiet like everyone else. Fetch me marsh-tallow off the old bog-jars and I'll keep the window burning. Somebody might yet come back down wanting a bed. I'd like the light on when they do.
listening_steppe_the_innkeepers_empty_be.progress: Bring Sorin marsh-tallow from the bog-jars so the window-lamp stays lit for anyone returning.
listening_steppe_the_innkeepers_empty_be.complete: Bless you. That's a winter of window-light, easy. You know, most nights nobody comes -- but three weeks back a girl walked down out of the dark, saw my lamp, and cried like she'd forgotten light was a thing. Slept two days. Left south, the right way. One in three months. That's why you keep the lamp on. Here -- a proper poultice, cultured the old way. Roads are hard on a body that walks back.

objectives.text: Gather marsh-tallow from the bog-jars

listening_steppe_the_innkeepers_empty_be.note: Sorin keeps his window-lamp lit for the one in three months who walks back down. He has no better reason than that. It is reason enough. The stew stays honest.

listening_steppe_where_the_dust_runs_str.title: Where the Dust Runs Straight
listening_steppe_where_the_dust_runs_str.summary: The old pilgrim waystones have started to point. Vlaicu wants their bearings walked and marked on his patrol-map before the wardens trust the road they carve.
listening_steppe_where_the_dust_runs_str.offer: The waystones on the pilgrim road have always leaned every-which-way -- frost heaves them, carts clip them, nobody minds. Now they lean the same way. All of them. And the dust between them runs in one straight line, stone to stone, like a chalk-snap on a carpenter's board. Walk the line, mark each stone's bearing on this map-border, and let's see where a road nobody built is trying to go. I'd rather know the destination before we're ordered to march it.
listening_steppe_where_the_dust_runs_str.progress: Walk the leaning waystones, mark each bearing on the map-border. Learn where the unbuilt road points.
listening_steppe_where_the_dust_runs_str.complete: Every stone, the same bearing, and your line runs true off the edge of my map -- northeast, past the fold, toward Black Night and the grave under it. A road that surveys itself. My grandfather chained ground for the crown and even he needed pegs and a boy holding the far end. This laid itself while we slept. Keep the map-border. You marked it honest. I'll not be the warden who leads men down a road that drew its own straight line.

objectives.label: the leaning waystones
objectives.text: Mark the bearings of the leaning waystones
Scatter the Gale-Swarm nesting along the line

listening_steppe_where_the_dust_runs_str.note: The waystones lean one way now, the dust ruled straight between them, the bearing running off the map toward the grave. A road that surveyed itself while men slept. Vlaicu will not march it.

listening_steppe_the_accords_first_ask.title: The Accord's First Ask
listening_steppe_the_accords_first_ask.summary: Iancu keeps the Accord's quiet channel, and the Accord has noticed you keep walking back. Their first ask is small, deniable, and a test: watch a man near the waystones and bring back only what he does, not what he says.
listening_steppe_the_accords_first_ask.offer: You brought the satchel sealed. That got noticed by people who notice for a living. I run more than salt down this road -- I run the Accord's quiet ear, and they've an ask, small and deniable, the way all their first asks are. There's a man camped by the near waystones who talks to three different couriers and pays them all in the same clipped coin. Watch him. Bring me a dossier of what he does -- his habits, his hours, his hands. Not a word he says. Words up here have started saying themselves. It's the doing that tells true.
listening_steppe_the_accords_first_ask.progress: Watch the man at the near waystones. Bring Iancu a dossier of his habits -- what he does, never what he says.
listening_steppe_the_accords_first_ask.complete: Habits, hours, hands -- good. He empties one boot at the same bell each night and buries something under the third stone; that's a signal-drop, plain as a printed sign, and now the Accord owns it without a word crossing. You did this clean. You'll be asked again, and the asks won't stay small -- that's how the Accord uses a useful person, gently, until useful is all you are. Here's your fee, and a lens for reading what's clipped and what's whole. Watch your own habits, now. Somebody's keeping your dossier too.

objectives.label: the near waystones
objectives.text: Observe the courier-man at the near waystones
Compile the dossier of his habits
Deliver the dossier to Iancu

listening_steppe_the_accords_first_ask.note: The dossier bought the Accord a signal-drop without a word crossing. Iancu was honest about the price: the asks grow, and useful becomes the only thing you are. Somewhere, a dossier of your own habits thickens.

listening_steppe_the_swarm_that_sorts.title: The Swarm That Sorts
listening_steppe_the_swarm_that_sorts.summary: Ilie's brother stood too long in the grass and now stands there still. Ilie can't approach him for the Gale-Swarm that guards the stillness. Clear the swarm so a family can carry one of its own home.
listening_steppe_the_swarm_that_sorts.offer: My brother. He's out there, in the grass, standing where he stopped. Three days now. I've tried to reach him and the swarm won't let me near -- rises off him in a cloud when I close within twenty paces, and settles back when I retreat, patient as a dog on a porch. I don't ask you to bring him round. I've watched enough of these to know that's not a thing anyone brings round. I ask you to clear the swarm so I can carry him off that field and bury him on ground with a name. Let him be somewhere his mother can find.
listening_steppe_the_swarm_that_sorts.progress: Drive off the Gale-Swarm guarding Ilie's brother so his family can carry him from the field.
listening_steppe_the_swarm_that_sorts.complete: It's done. I felt his hand -- warm, still warm, God help me -- and we carried him to the low churchyard where the stones have names. He didn't know us. But he was ours to carry, and now he's ours to visit, and that's more than the field would ever have given back. Take the knot. My mother tied it. It's supposed to soothe. It doesn't, much. But she tied it, and that counts for the both of us.

objectives.text: Drive the Gale-Swarm off Ilie's brother
Tell Ilie the field is clear

listening_steppe_the_swarm_that_sorts.note: Ilie carried his brother to ground with a name. The man's hand was warm and he knew no one. To be carried and buried and visited is more than the field returns. His mother's knot soothes no one. She tied it anyway.

listening_steppe_the_last_letters_south.title: The Last Letters South
listening_steppe_the_last_letters_south.summary: The pilgrims leave letters on Sorin's mantel meaning to post them south, then walk north and forget. Carry the year's stack down to the guard-post so at least the families get word, however late.
listening_steppe_the_last_letters_south.offer: People leave letters here. Set them on my mantel meaning to hand them to a south-bound cart, then they sleep, and in the morning the north's got hold of them and off they go with the letter still on my shelf. I've a year of them now -- 'dear mother,' 'dear wife,' 'we're well, we'll be home by the fair.' Homes that are still waiting. Carry the stack down to Nedelcu at the post; he sends a runner to Vetka weekly. Let the families have the word. Late word beats a silence that never ends.
listening_steppe_the_last_letters_south.progress: Carry the mantel's stack of forgotten letters down to Nedelcu at the guard-post for the Vetka runner.
listening_steppe_the_last_letters_south.complete: You gave them to Nedelcu? Good man. They'll reach Vetka by week's end, and some mother reads 'home by the fair' a year too late and knows, at least, that the ink was hopeful when it dried. That's not nothing. A hopeful letter is a kind of grave-marker, when there's no body to mark. Sit. The stew's on. You've earned a warm bowl and a dry bed, both of which I've got too many of.

objectives.text: Gather the mantel's stack of forgotten letters
Give the letters to Nedelcu for the runner

listening_steppe_the_last_letters_south.note: A year of letters reached Vetka a year late -- 'home by the fair,' all of them, from people the fair never saw again. A hopeful letter is a grave-marker where there is no body. Sorin's beds stay empty. The stew stays on.

listening_steppe_the_prowler_that_leads.title: The Prowler That Leads
listening_steppe_the_prowler_that_leads.summary: Toma has watched a lone prowler herd the standing pilgrims, nudging them a step northward each night. He wants the herd-beast killed before it walks a whole field of them over the fold.
listening_steppe_the_prowler_that_leads.offer: I keep telling myself they're just beasts. Then I watched one work. There's a big prowler, pale round the muzzle, and it doesn't hunt -- it herds. Walks the standing folk. Comes up behind one in the night and gives it a nudge, gentle as a dog moving sheep, and by morning that soul's a step further north than it was. It's walking the whole field over the fold, one nudge a night, patient as weather. Kill it. Kill the pale one before it moves them all past where anyone can carry them back.
listening_steppe_the_prowler_that_leads.progress: Hunt the pale herd-prowler moving the standing folk northward, and thin its pack.
listening_steppe_the_prowler_that_leads.complete: You got the pale one? Then the field's stopped drifting, for now. I watched at dawn -- they stood exactly where they stood yesterday, not a step gained. Small mercy. The kind we count up here. Something's still teaching the beasts to herd us, mind -- the pale one didn't invent it. But the pupil's dead, and the lesson's paused. Take the razor. Blackglass. It parts a prowler's hide like the hide's glad of it.

objectives.text: Hunt the pale herd-prowler and its pack

listening_steppe_the_prowler_that_leads.note: The pale prowler herded the standing folk north, one nudge a night. It is dead; the field holds where it stands. Something taught the beasts to herd. Toma killed the pupil. The lesson waits for another.

listening_steppe_a_bell_against_the_quie.title: A Bell Against the Quiet
listening_steppe_a_bell_against_the_quie.summary: Petru means to cast a warning-bell for the north road so a living sound rings against the silence. He needs blackglass from the shade-haunts to line the mold, and the shades cleared so he can pour.
listening_steppe_a_bell_against_the_quie.offer: The steppe's too quiet. You notice? No birdsong past the post, no wind-in-wire, nothing but that one note nobody can quite hum back. I mean to cast a bell for the road. A big honest clang, rung on the hour, so a body walking north hears a living sound and maybe remembers there's a living world behind him. But I need blackglass to line the mold -- it holds a true tone -- and the stuff only forms where the shades stand thickest. Bring me blackglass, and clear the shades so I can pour in peace.
listening_steppe_a_bell_against_the_quie.progress: Gather blackglass from the shade-haunts and clear the Listening-Shades so Petru can pour the bell.
listening_steppe_a_bell_against_the_quie.complete: Enough to line the mold twice over. I'll pour at dawn and hang it by week's end, and then the road gets an hour-bell whether the quiet likes it or not. Will it save anyone? I don't know. But a man walked past my forge yesterday, half-gone, and when I dropped my hammer he flinched -- flinched, turned, looked at me like he'd heard a voice from a country he'd left. A sound can reach where a word can't. That's worth a bell. Take the pot-hook's cousin -- I'd nothing finer, and you've earned finer.

objectives.text: Gather blackglass from the shade-haunts
Clear the Listening-Shades from the casting-ground

listening_steppe_a_bell_against_the_quie.note: Petru cast his hour-bell. A half-gone man flinched when the hammer fell -- turned, looked, like a voice from a country he'd left. A sound reaches where a word cannot. It is not much. It is a bell against the quiet.

listening_steppe_the_incurious_charm.title: The Incurious Charm
listening_steppe_the_incurious_charm.summary: An old herb-wife's recipe kept the border folk incurious and alive; Sorin has the words but not the makings. Gather lichen-culture and marsh-things so he can brew charms for the pilgrims who still pass through.
listening_steppe_the_incurious_charm.offer: My grandmother learned herb-craft off Old Marta down in Vetka -- the one who kept whole villages alive by keeping them bored, if you can credit it. 'Don't wonder at the wells, dear. Wondering's what does you.' I've got the words of her incurious-charm but not the makings. Gather me lichen-culture off the north stones and a jar of marshlight, and I'll brew a batch for every pilgrim who sits at my fire. Can't stop them walking north. Can maybe stop them looking too hard on the way.
listening_steppe_the_incurious_charm.progress: Gather lichen-culture and a marshlight jar so Sorin can brew Old Marta's incurious-charms.
listening_steppe_the_incurious_charm.complete: Look at that -- good culture, live and green. That's a season of charms. I'll press one on every guest and tell them it's for luck, because 'this keeps you from understanding things that kill you' does put folk off their stew. Old Marta had it right: the safest mind on this road is a dull one. Take a folk-warding for yourself. Hang it, don't read it, and when the grass tries to make you curious -- think about supper instead.

objectives.text: Gather lichen-culture from the north stones
Gather a marshlight jar

listening_steppe_the_incurious_charm.note: Sorin brews Old Marta's incurious-charm now, and presses one on every guest as a luck-token. The safest mind on the road is a dull one. He tells no one why. Old Marta would have approved of that, too.

listening_steppe_the_thing_at_the_kneeli.title: The Thing at the Kneeling Stone
listening_steppe_the_thing_at_the_kneeli.summary: Something has taken to standing at the Kneeling Stone each dusk, in a warden's old coat, facing the post. Nedelcu is too shaken to walk out himself. Go and see what wears his dead partner's coat.
listening_steppe_the_thing_at_the_kneeli.offer: There's a thing stands at the Kneeling Stone now, come dusk. Wears a warden's coat -- grey, brass buttons, torn at the left cuff. I know that tear. I know that coat. It's Aurel's, who held this post with me and walked north two winters back and did not come down. And now something wears it and faces my window every night at dusk and I can't -- I can't be the one to walk out there and see whether it's got his face. I've turned back a hundred strangers. I can't turn to this one. Go. Tell me what it is. Put it down if it needs putting down.
listening_steppe_the_thing_at_the_kneeli.progress: Walk to the Kneeling Stone at dusk and face the thing in the warden's coat. Put it down if you must.
listening_steppe_the_thing_at_the_kneeli.complete: His face. It had his face, and it stood the way he stood, weight on the bad knee -- and it was empty as a hung coat, Aurel long gone out of it. You did right. You did what I couldn't, and I'll carry that I couldn't. We buried the coat with the brass buttons up by the low church. A grave for the coat, since we'd nothing else of him. Take the ward. It's stronger than the chalk kind. I should have carried one the day I let him walk north.

objectives.label: the Kneeling Stone at dusk
objectives.text: Face the thing in the warden's coat
Put down what wears Aurel's coat
Tell Nedelcu what stood there

listening_steppe_the_thing_at_the_kneeli.note: It wore Aurel's coat and Aurel's face and stood on Aurel's bad knee, and was empty as a hung coat inside. They buried the coat, having nothing else. Nedelcu carries that he could not walk out himself. So now does the one who could.

listening_steppe_the_wind_in_the_wire.title: The Wind in the Wire
listening_steppe_the_wind_in_the_wire.summary: Serban has noticed the note the wind carries is not random -- it repeats, three tones settling toward one. He wants the fence-wire waystation reached and its resonance read before the tones fully converge.
listening_steppe_the_wind_in_the_wire.offer: I'm a clerk, not a musician, but I keep records, and I've been recording the wind. That note everyone half-hears -- I've marked it a hundred dusks now, and it is not the wind. It's three tones, and they're closing. Each week they sit a hair nearer one another, like three singers walking toward the same note from far apart. When they meet -- I don't know. Nobody's written that page yet. Go out to the old fence-line station, where the wire hums clearest, and read the resonance for me. I want to know how long until the three become one.
listening_steppe_the_wind_in_the_wire.progress: Reach the fence-line station where the wire hums, and read the resonance of the three converging tones.
listening_steppe_the_wind_in_the_wire.complete: Nearer than I feared. Your reading puts the three tones a season, maybe two, from meeting -- and when a thing this patient starts to hurry, the hurrying is the frightening part. I've set it in the roll, dated, witnessed. If I'm right, someone reading this book in a better year will know exactly when the quiet stopped being quiet. Take the still. It's for reading the orange signal safe, at a distance. Read it at a distance, always. The near reading is the one that keeps you.

objectives.label: the fence-line station
objectives.text: Read the resonance at the humming wire
Clear the swarm nesting in the wire

listening_steppe_the_wind_in_the_wire.note: Three tones close toward one, a season or two from meeting; Serban dated the page for a reader in a better year. When the patient thing begins to hurry, the hurry is the fear. He wrote it down. The wind kept singing.

listening_steppe_bad_iron_bad_bread.title: Bad Iron, Bad Bread
listening_steppe_bad_iron_bad_bread.summary: Sorin's yeast has died three batches running and his well-water runs faintly copper. He wants clean water hauled from the one honest spring, and the symptoms noted before he admits what the pattern means.
listening_steppe_bad_iron_bad_bread.offer: I'll say it plain because pretending's beneath us both: my yeast's died three risings running, and this morning the well came up with a taste of copper in it. Faint. But I've tended a hearth long enough to know the order these things come in, and I know what usually stands at the end of that order. There's one spring east that still runs sweet -- I've tasted it. Haul me clean water from it while I've a kitchen worth the name. And keep the count of it with me. A thing shared is a thing half-carried.
listening_steppe_bad_iron_bad_bread.progress: Haul clean water from the sweet spring east of the inn, past the copper wells.
listening_steppe_bad_iron_bad_bread.complete: Sweet water, thank God -- I'll bake a proper loaf tonight and the road'll smell of bread instead of whatever it's starting to smell of. Copper well, dead yeast. You know the list too, I can see it in your face. Warm ground next, then the dust goes straight, then folk stop finishing their -- well. You know. Let's not say the last of it aloud in a warm kitchen. Take the tonic. Salt and brine. Old bodies, old wards. Some of them still work.

objectives.text: Haul clean water from the sweet spring
Share the count with Sorin

listening_steppe_bad_iron_bad_bread.note: Dead yeast, copper well -- Sorin knows the order and would not say its end aloud in a warm kitchen. He baked a proper loaf on the sweet spring's water. The road smelled of bread for one night. He knows what it smells of after.

listening_steppe_the_veil_of_the_thinnin.title: The Veil of the Thinning
listening_steppe_the_veil_of_the_thinnin.summary: A dead trader's pack held a veil said to dull the signal to whoever wears it -- a thing worth a fortune to three buyers. Iancu wants it recovered from the shades that took him, and a decision made about who learns it exists.
listening_steppe_the_veil_of_the_thinnin.offer: A trader died in the grass east of here -- one of mine, or near enough, I'd fronted him goods. In his pack was a thing I've only heard of: a veil, woven against the thinning, that dulls the signal to whoever wears it. The Accord would pay a kingdom for it. Blestem would pay two and kill you for the receipt. And Fielderine's people would want it buried where no one's tempted. Recover it off the shades that took him. Then we decide, you and I, who's ever to know it surfaced. Some things are safest unfiled.
listening_steppe_the_veil_of_the_thinnin.progress: Recover the veil from the shades east of the post, where the dead trader fell.
listening_steppe_the_veil_of_the_thinnin.complete: You've got it. Real, and it dulls the note -- I felt the quiet come down over me when you held it near, like a hand over a candle. Now. Three buyers, and each one worse to hand it to than the last. I'll tell you what I think: we file it nowhere. I keep it in the false bottom of the salt-cart and it protects the folk on this road, quietly, and no crown ever learns a warding exists that it might learn to break. Agreed? Good. Take the veil's twin -- lesser, but yours. Wear it in the deep grass. And Iancu Vulpescu never sold you a thing.

objectives.text: Clear the shades where the trader fell
Recover the veil of the thinning
Decide the veil's fate with Iancu

listening_steppe_the_veil_of_the_thinnin.note: Three crowns would kill for the veil against the thinning. Iancu filed it nowhere -- a false-bottomed salt-cart, a warding no throne learns to break. Some things are safest unfiled. He never sold you a thing.

listening_steppe_the_daily_watch.title: The Warden's Standing Watch
listening_steppe_the_daily_watch.summary: The north field never stays clear. Nedelcu asks any able hand to walk it each day, put down what has gone past reading, and turn back what can still be turned.
listening_steppe_the_daily_watch.offer: Same ask as yesterday, because it's the same field, and it never stays clear. Walk the north grass, put down the shades that have gone all the way over, and if you find one still soft enough to turn, turn it south and pray it keeps walking. I'll pay the day-rate out of the post's purse. It's not a war you win. It's a field you sweep, and sweep again tomorrow. That's the whole of the job up here. Some days that's enough. Most days it has to be.
listening_steppe_the_daily_watch.progress: Sweep the north field: put down the far-gone shades, thin the prowlers working the edge.
listening_steppe_the_daily_watch.complete: Cleared for the day. Tomorrow it fills again -- there's always another walking up the north road with their good boots on -- but today the field's honest grass, and a family got to carry one home who'd have been past carrying by dusk. Day-rate's in the purse. Come back tomorrow. I'll have the same ask and the same thanks, and both'll be meant.

objectives.text: Put down the far-gone shades in the north field
Thin the prowlers working the field's edge

listening_steppe_the_daily_watch.note: The field never stays clear. You sweep it, and sweep it tomorrow. It is not a war you win. Nedelcu means his thanks each time, which is its own small stubbornness against the quiet.

listening_steppe_the_daily_lamp.title: Tallow for the Window
listening_steppe_the_daily_lamp.summary: Sorin's window-lamp burns through tallow fast, and the light matters more than the cost. Bring a day's marsh-tallow so the last light before the grass keeps burning for anyone walking back.
listening_steppe_the_daily_lamp.offer: Lamp's low again -- it drinks tallow like the road drinks travelers, and I mean to keep it lit longer than the road keeps them. Bring me a day's worth of marsh-tallow from the bog-jars and I'll keep the window burning through the dark. It's foolish, keeping a light for people who mostly don't come. But 'mostly' isn't 'never,' and the night one walks back and there's no light -- I won't be the reason. Bring the tallow. Have a bowl while you're at it.
listening_steppe_the_daily_lamp.progress: Bring Sorin a day's marsh-tallow from the bog-jars to keep the window-lamp burning.
listening_steppe_the_daily_lamp.complete: That'll see the window through the night, and maybe the next. You're good to keep a foolish old man's foolish light going. It's not foolish, though, is it -- you've walked that dark road yourself. You know what a lit window's worth from out there. Warm bowl's on the hob. Sit a while before you go back out into it.

objectives.text: Gather a day's marsh-tallow from the bog-jars

listening_steppe_the_daily_lamp.note: The lamp drinks tallow like the road drinks travelers. Sorin keeps it lit for the 'mostly don't' who might. It is not foolish. Anyone who has walked that dark road knows exactly what a lit window is worth.

listening_steppe_the_map_that_marks_itse.title: The Map That Marks Itself
listening_steppe_the_map_that_marks_itse.summary: The bearings you marked for Vlaicu all point one place, and the Accord wants the route walked to the fold and the far marker read -- the last honest waypoint before Black Night and the grave the maps refuse.
listening_steppe_the_map_that_marks_itse.offer: You marked my waystones true, and every line you drew runs to one place past the fold -- northeast, toward the grave under Black Night that no honest map will draw. The Accord's seen your marks now. They want the road walked to its last waypoint, the far marker at the fold's lip, and the marker read before anyone commits men to what's beyond it. I'll not lie to you: this is the edge of my country and the start of a worse one. Walk to the fold. Read the far marker. Come back and tell me it's still just grass out there. I'd like to believe you.
listening_steppe_the_map_that_marks_itse.progress: Walk the surveyed route to the fold's lip, read the far marker, and clear the shades that guard the edge.
listening_steppe_the_map_that_marks_itse.complete: You walked to the edge of the map and came back, which is more than the far marker's other readers managed. And you read it at a distance, the way a body ought. What it says -- no. Don't tell me the words. I saw your face when you came over the rise and that's report enough. The road goes to the grave, the grave is waking, and my country is the last warm mile before it. Take the plate. Wardsmith's work, river-forged. If you're walking north past here -- and you are, I can see that too -- walk it in good iron. This is where I turn back. You never learned how.

objectives.label: the far marker at the fold
objectives.text: Walk to the fold and read the far marker
Clear the shades guarding the fold's edge
Bring Vlaicu word of the far marker

listening_steppe_the_map_that_marks_itse.note: The road runs to the grave and the grave is waking; the Listening Steppe is the last warm mile before it. Vlaicu turns back here, as a warden should. The one who read the far marker did not learn how to turn back. That is noted, somewhere, in a ledger not his.

listening_steppe_the_chroniclers_witness.title: The Chronicler's Witness
listening_steppe_the_chroniclers_witness.summary: Serban is closing the parish for the season and wants a witness to the standing field before he seals the roll -- someone to stand a quiet watch with him and confirm, for the record, what the steppe has become.
listening_steppe_the_chroniclers_witness.offer: I'm closing the roll for the season. Sealing it. And a sealed roll needs a witness beside the clerk, by old parish law -- two hands to say this is true, this is what was, on this day in this place. I've no one left steady enough of eye. Stand the watch with me at the field's edge, one hour, quiet as we can keep it. Watch the standing folk. Don't call to them, don't count aloud, just see -- so that when I sign, I sign that two of us saw, and neither of us understood, and both of us walked away. That last part's the important part. Both of us walk away.
listening_steppe_the_chroniclers_witness.progress: Stand the sealing-watch with Serban at the field's edge. Watch, do not call out, and walk away.
listening_steppe_the_chroniclers_witness.complete: There. Signed, sealed, witnessed -- two hands, one truth, both walking away from it upright. Thank you for the eye and the restraint. Most who watch that field an hour start to lean toward it without knowing they've moved. You didn't. I marked that too, in the margin, small: 'the witness kept their feet.' It's a good thing to have written down about a person. It may be the only true thing anyone writes about most of us. Take the token. A name, kept. It's what I deal in, really -- keeping the names after the bodies stop keeping them.

objectives.label: the field's edge
objectives.text: Stand the sealing-watch with Serban
Sign the sealed roll as witness

listening_steppe_the_chroniclers_witness.note: Two hands sealed the roll: this is true, this is what was, both walked away upright. In the margin Serban wrote, small, 'the witness kept their feet.' It may be the truest thing ever written of most of us.

listening_steppe_the_smiths_last_commiss.title: The Smith's Last Commission
listening_steppe_the_smiths_last_commiss.summary: Petru is old, the floor stays warm, and he means to leave one good blade behind for whoever holds this road after him. He needs bog-iron and blackglass for the last thing his hands will make well.
listening_steppe_the_smiths_last_commiss.offer: I'm too old for the road to bother turning me north -- small mercy of a bad back. But the floor's warm and getting warmer, and a man knows when he's tending his last few fires. I'd like to leave one good blade behind. Not for a lord. For whoever holds this post after me, walking out to a field that won't stop filling. Bring me bog-iron for the spine and blackglass for the edge, and I'll pour the best of myself into it while my hands still answer. Let me make one last honest thing before the ground gets its way.
listening_steppe_the_smiths_last_commiss.progress: Bring Petru bog-iron and blackglass for the last blade he means to forge for this road's next keeper.
listening_steppe_the_smiths_last_commiss.complete: Feel that balance. Forty years in one swing. I'll not see who carries it -- the floor tells me that plain enough -- but it'll be here, oiled, hung on the cold nail by the door, for the hand that comes after mine. That's a kind of living-on the Archive never thought of: not a name filed cold, but a good blade left warm on a nail for a stranger. Here. This one's for you. I made two. A man's allowed one vanity at the end, and mine was making sure the road you walk out on carried better iron than the road that took Aurel.

objectives.text: Gather bog-iron for the blade's spine
Gather blackglass for the blade's edge

listening_steppe_the_smiths_last_commiss.note: Petru forged one last blade for the road's next keeper and hung it on the cold nail by the door. Not a name filed cold, but good iron left warm for a stranger's hand. The floor keeps warming. He keeps hammering, one beat short.

listening_steppe_the_one_who_walks_back.title: The One Who Walks Back
listening_steppe_the_one_who_walks_back.summary: Ilie swears a woman walked out of the north grass fully herself -- rare past counting -- and Sorin's window brought her in. Find her before she drifts north again, and learn how one mind walked back from the listening.
listening_steppe_the_one_who_walks_back.offer: I need you to find her before she's gone again. A woman walked down out of the north grass three nights back -- down, mind, against the pull -- and she was herself. Herself. Do you know how rare that is? They go north and they stay. She came back. Sorin's window caught her eye and she wept and slept and now she's wandering the near grass, half-here, and I'm afraid she'll turn round and go back up before anyone learns how she did it. Find her. Sit with her. Learn what let one mind walk back. It might be the only useful thing this whole cursed field ever taught anyone.
listening_steppe_the_one_who_walks_back.progress: Find the woman who walked back, wandering the near grass, and sit with her before she drifts north again.
listening_steppe_the_one_who_walks_back.complete: You found her. And she told you -- she was reading the marks, like the rest, leaning in, almost through -- and then she thought of her daughter's name. Just the name. Held it, wouldn't let it resolve into the marks, kept it warm and separate and stubborn in her mouth. And it pulled her back out. A name against the meaning. That's the whole secret, and it's the smallest thing, and it might save a hundred souls if we can teach it before the grass teaches them otherwise. Take the token. A name, kept apart. Now you know why it matters. Go teach someone.

objectives.label: the near grass
objectives.text: Find the woman who walked back
Tell Ilie how one mind walked back

listening_steppe_the_one_who_walks_back.note: She read the marks, leaned almost through, then held her daughter's name and would not let it resolve. A name against the meaning pulled her back. The smallest thing. It may save a hundred souls, if it can be taught before the grass teaches otherwise.

vetka_flat_grey_bread.title: Flat, Grey, and No Fault of Hers
vetka_flat_grey_bread.summary: Petru's bread has come out flat and grey for a fortnight and he has stopped apologizing for it. He blames the flour, then the water. Ask the herb-woman's stock and the miller's boy before he blames himself.
vetka_flat_grey_bread.offer: Sit, traveller, and don't look at the loaf. Fortnight now it comes out flat and grey, and I've blamed the flour and blamed the water and I'm running out of things that aren't me. Ask Marcu at the mill and Ilie who keeps old Magda's stock -- one of them will tell me it's the season. Then I can sleep.
vetka_flat_grey_bread.progress: Marcu keeps the mill; Ilie keeps what the herb-woman left. Ask them both what kills a yeast.
vetka_flat_grey_bread.complete: The season, they both said. Bad damp, bad air, the season. That's twice now a thing gets called the season by everyone at once. Eat -- it's flat but it's warm, and warm is the part I can still manage. There's a coin in it for the walk.

objectives.text: Ask Marcu the miller about the bread
Ask Ilie about the herb-woman's yeast

vetka_flat_grey_bread.note: Everyone called it the season, in the same breath, as if reading it off a wall. Petru serves the bread flat and warm and no longer meets a stranger's eye over it.

vetka_dorica_well.title: The Taste in Dorica's Well
vetka_dorica_well.summary: The well by the low houses has gone the color of a coin, and it is the only one Dorica's family can reach. She boils it and blames her bucket. Go and look at the wellstone before she drinks another morning.
vetka_dorica_well.offer: You'll have heard the bread's gone wrong. The water's gone worse, and nobody wants that said aloud. Dorica draws from the low well -- gone copper, taste of a held coin -- and there's no second well for her, so she boils it and blames the bucket. Walk down and read the wellstone. Tell me it's the pipe. Tell me it's anything with a name.
vetka_dorica_well.progress: The low well sits south among the sunken houses, past the thatch that leans. Read what's cut in the wellstone.
vetka_dorica_well.complete: Fresh marks in the stone, you say, under the moss that's been there since before my father. Angles that pull the eye. Good that you looked at your boots coming back -- Ilie says that's the trick, don't read them twice. Here. It's not much against a well, but it's what a grandmother would've given you.

objectives.label: the low well
objectives.text: Read the wellstone at the low well
Tell Ilie what you saw

vetka_dorica_well.note: The well runs copper still, and Dorica boils it still, and blames her bucket. Fresh angles were cut under moss older than her father. Somebody drew them where a woman with no other well would have to look.

vetka_resting_since_tuesday.title: He's Just Resting
vetka_resting_since_tuesday.summary: A man stands at his fence past the east furrows, faced the same way since Tuesday, and does not answer his name twice. His wife says he's resting. Go out to him, and bring back the one thing that will tell Ilie whether to be afraid.
vetka_resting_since_tuesday.offer: There's a man at the fence past the east furrows. Been there since Tuesday, faced the one way, and his wife brings him bread he doesn't take and calls it resting. I'd go myself but my knees and, honest, my nerve. Go stand by him. Say his name -- once is enough, he won't answer the second. And look at the ground round his boots. The dust, mind. Look at the dust.
vetka_resting_since_tuesday.progress: The still man stands at the eastern fence-line, past the furrows where the frost hasn't taken. Reach him and read the ground.
vetka_resting_since_tuesday.complete: Dry, and warm, and the dust in lines, you say. Lines like filings to a stone you couldn't see. That's the third symptom in a week and I'm out of words that aren't the true one. His wife'll go on saying resting. Let her. Take this -- old Magda made a dozen, said they keep a curious head pointed at its own feet.

objectives.label: the eastern fence
objectives.text: Reach the man who has not moved since Tuesday

vetka_resting_since_tuesday.note: The ground at his boots was dry and warm, and the dust lay in fine parallel lines, aligned to a lodestone no one could point at. His wife still calls it resting. He has been resting since Tuesday.

vetka_borek_cellar.title: Honest Work
vetka_borek_cellar.summary: Something has denned in the old grain-house cellar and fouls the air below. Nedelcu offers coin to clear it -- and a bowl of stew he knows the traveller may not want. The work is honest. What waits under the grain-house is not what he thinks.
vetka_borek_cellar.offer: Coin first, then I'll tell you it's worse than you'd like. The cellar under the old grain-house has gone foul -- something's widened the way down and I've lost a dog and near lost a boy to it. Clear it. I'll pay fair and leave a bowl out, and I won't ask why a body doesn't touch it. A chair pulled to a table is a chair pulled to a table.
vetka_borek_cellar.progress: The grain-house stands at the north edge of the low town. Go down into its cellar and clear what has widened the way.
vetka_borek_cellar.complete: Quiet down there now, they tell me. You saw the wall, then. The marks worming into the foundation, the faint wrong shine of them. I don't want the word for it. I want the cellar clear and the boy safe and the bowl I leave out to be a kindness, not a wake. Take your coin, and take this -- bog-iron, river-smelted. The one honest thing the ground can't turn.

objectives.label: the grain-house cellar
objectives.text: Descend into the fouled cellar
Clear what widened the way down

vetka_borek_cellar.note: The fouling was no rot. Angular script wormed into the foundation stone, and under the alchemist's blue it shone a faint, shifting orange -- a signal writing itself into the wall. Nedelcu left the stew out anyway. Some kindnesses are chairs, not meals.

vetka_the_chamber.title: The Chamber and the Courier
vetka_the_chamber.summary: Beyond the cleared cellar the way opens into a cold vault where the script is dense and old. A courier lies there unwounded, his satchel still sealed. He never delivered the letter. He stopped to read the wall.
vetka_the_chamber.offer: You cleared the near dark. There's a far dark past it -- a vault, cold, the walls thick with the marks. A runner went down before you, weeks back, and never came up. His people are still waiting on the letter he carried. Go and find him. Read nothing on those walls aloud, and don't read the courier's notes twice, whatever they seem to promise. Bring me the sealed letter. Sealed, mind.
vetka_the_chamber.progress: The far vault opens past the cellar you cleared. Find the courier, and take up only what is still sealed.
vetka_the_chamber.complete: No wound, you say. Clean fingernails. The quill still in his hand and the last mark half-drawn. He didn't die of the dark, then -- he died of finishing a sentence. The letter's still sealed. Good. It stays sealed. Whoever's waiting on it can go on waiting; waiting's the safe half. Here -- Magda's own, blue as her book. It shows you a color you'll wish you couldn't name.

objectives.label: the cold vault
objectives.text: Find the courier in the chamber
Face what keeps the vault
Recover the sealed letter

vetka_the_chamber.note: The courier had no wound. Clean fingernails, satchel sealed, the last glyph half-copied under his still hand. He died the instant he understood. The letter came up sealed and stays sealed. To read the wall is to be read by it.

vetka_torn_three_notes.title: The Three Notes Torn Can't Place
vetka_torn_three_notes.summary: Ilie's neighbor has taken to humming three notes he swears he heard somewhere, and grows short when asked where. He didn't hear them anywhere. Sit with him a while and learn what a man looks like just before he stops answering.
vetka_torn_three_notes.offer: You'll know the fellow -- hums three notes over and over at the taproom, gets prickly if you ask where from. Says a fair, says a wedding, can't land on it. I've a bad guess and I'd like you to prove it wrong. Go sit with Ilie who drinks beside him, then keep watch at the treeline where he wanders at dusk. Just watch. Don't hum it back to him. Whatever you do, don't learn the third note.
vetka_torn_three_notes.progress: Ask Ilie about his humming neighbor, then keep the dusk watch at the western treeline where the man drifts.
vetka_torn_three_notes.complete: Two notes, you say, and he reaches for the third and can't -- and is glad he can't, some animal part of him. The third isn't lost. It hasn't arrived. He didn't hear them at a fair. They're coming to him, one at a time, the way the copper came to the well. Sit by the fire. You've earned the warm and I've little else to give but that and this old sign for your door.

objectives.text: Ask Ilie about his humming neighbor

objectives.label: the western treeline
objectives.text: Keep the dusk watch at the treeline

vetka_torn_three_notes.note: Torn hums two notes and cannot find the third, and is glad, in the part of him that is still his. The third has not been lost. It has not yet arrived. Petru had guessed as much, and hoped to be wrong.

vetka_moor_wail_nights.title: The Wailing Off the Moor
vetka_moor_wail_nights.summary: Something wails off the moor at night, close enough now to keep the low houses from sleep. Vlaicu can't watch two ends of town alone. Thin the wailing things before folk stop coming in from the fields at dusk.
vetka_moor_wail_nights.offer: I've one good knee between two gates, and the wailing's come off the moor these three nights closer than the last. Folk are keeping their children in and their doors barred, which is sense, but a barred door doesn't cut hay. Go out to the wet ground west and thin the wailers. Won't stop the fog making that sound. Might buy a week of sleep. A week's a lot, out here.
vetka_moor_wail_nights.progress: The moor-wails drift the wet ground west of the sunken houses after dark. Thin them.
vetka_moor_wail_nights.complete: Quieter tonight. I heard my own bootsteps for the first time in a week -- didn't know how loud a man's own feet were. It'll come back, the wailing. Always does. But a week of sleep is a week of sense, and sense is thin out here. Take the striker. Bog-iron and vein-flint. It'll light a fire in the wet, which is more than most things can say.

objectives.text: Thin the wailers on the western moor

vetka_moor_wail_nights.note: The wailing eased for a week, and Vlaicu heard his own footsteps again, and marveled at them. The fog will find the sound again. Out here a week of sleep is counted like coin.

vetka_bog_iron_haul.title: The Metal the Stone Can't Turn
vetka_bog_iron_haul.summary: Iancu's ore comes up the road when the road feels like being a road, which is seldom now. Until it does, he smelts the ugly honest metal out of the bog itself. Bring him a good haul of bog-iron from the wet ground.
vetka_bog_iron_haul.offer: Ore comes up the old road when the road's minded to, and it hasn't been minded in weeks. No matter. There's iron in the bog if you don't mind ugly work -- river-smelted, brown as a bad tooth, and honest. It's the one metal the ground can't turn against me, my father said, because it's already been in the ground and come back cross about it. Fetch me a good haul off the wet west and I'll ring you something worth the walk.
vetka_bog_iron_haul.progress: Bog-iron nodules lie in the wet ground west of town, near where the moor takes over. Dig a good haul.
vetka_bog_iron_haul.complete: Now that's honest metal -- brown and ugly and mine. Ore that thinks it's precious gets ideas; bog-iron's got no ideas at all, and in this town that's a virtue. Here. I hammered it while you dug. It'll never be pretty. It'll never let you down either, which is the trade I'd make every day out here.

objectives.text: Dig bog-iron from the wet ground

vetka_bog_iron_haul.note: Iancu smelts the bog's own ugly iron while the road sulks and refuses to be a road. His father called it the one honest metal, already in the ground once and cross about it. In Vetka, having no ideas is a virtue in a thing.

vetka_magdas_grave.title: What Keeps at Magda's Grave
vetka_magdas_grave.summary: Old Magda fed six winters of strangers and asked one question. Now her grave will not keep, and Toma is too old to swing a spade at a woman he loved. Lay her back down, and read the one thing carved on her kerb.
vetka_magdas_grave.offer: I buried Magda myself, sixty-one winters of her, and cut her kerb with my own bad hand. Now she walks the yard at night and I can't -- I won't -- put a spade to that. But she'd not want to be up there, listening, whatever's got hold of her. You do it. Gently, if a thing like that can be done gentle. And read her kerb before you leave. I want to know if the words still say what I cut.
vetka_magdas_grave.progress: Magda's grave lies in the old yard at the high edge of town. Lay her shade back down, and read the kerb.
vetka_magdas_grave.complete: Down again. Thank you -- I'd not have slept knowing she wandered. And the kerb reads true, you say. Sixty-one winters, she fed six winters of strangers, she asked one question. Good. That's the whole of her worth keeping, and it's more than most kingdoms manage. They'll file her stick in some archive one day and lose the woman. Not while I've a chisel.

objectives.text: Lay Grave-Magda's shade to rest

objectives.label: Magda's kerb
objectives.text: Read the words on Magda's kerb

vetka_magdas_grave.note: Magda Vetkan, sixty-one winters, fed six winters of strangers and asked one question. Her kerb still reads true, and Toma keeps a chisel against the day someone files the woman away and keeps only the stick.

vetka_gren_grave.title: A Grave Nobody Asked For
vetka_gren_grave.summary: Gren is out past the yard digging a grave nobody died to fill. He won't say who it's for. Marcu wants someone gentler than himself to go and find out whether it's fear or foresight in that hole.
vetka_gren_grave.offer: My brother Gren's out past the yard with a spade, digging a grave nobody's died to need. Won't say who. Just says someone might want putting to rest proper, and someone ought to be ready. He's not cruel, my Gren -- he's scared, and scared men reach for the shovel. Go and stand with him a while. Take his measure. Tell me it's foresight and not a man digging his own good sense a hole to lie in.
vetka_gren_grave.progress: Gren digs past the old yard, at the eastern edge where the ground runs warm. Go and stand with him.
vetka_gren_grave.complete: Foresight, then. Or fear that's learned to look like it. He's right, is the worst of it -- someone will need putting down proper before this is through, and better a grave dug calm than in a panic. Same hands dig it that'd swing a spade at a stranger. Cruelty and care, one shovel. That's my brother. That's the whole town. Here -- he said give you this. It's Magda's cord. He kept it.

objectives.text: Ask Ilie where Gren has been digging

objectives.label: Gren's dig
objectives.text: Stand with Gren at the grave he's digging

vetka_gren_grave.note: Gren digs a grave for no one yet, past the yard where the ground runs warm. Cruelty and care from the one pair of hands, the one shovel. He is not wrong that someone will need it. That is the worst of Vetka.

vetka_dust_in_lines.title: Filings to a Stone Unseen
vetka_dust_in_lines.summary: The dust in the mill storeroom lies in fine parallel lines, aligned like iron filings to a lodestone no one can see. Marcu has swept it three times. It comes back the same. He wants a second pair of eyes before he starts believing it.
vetka_dust_in_lines.offer: Come to the mill, and mind you don't laugh. The dust in my store lies in lines -- fine, even, parallel, like filings pulled to a lodestone I can't find. Swept it three times. Comes back the same by morning, pointed the same way each time. I need someone to see it who isn't me, or I'll talk myself into calling it the season like everyone else. Go stand in the store. Tell me which way the lines point.
vetka_dust_in_lines.progress: The mill storeroom sits below Marcu's wheel at the water's edge. Stand in it and read the way the dust lies.
vetka_dust_in_lines.complete: East, you say. Toward the grain-house. Toward the dark under it. Three sweeps and it points the same way each dawn, and now I've a second pair of eyes says the same, so I can't call it the season anymore, can I. That's a debt you've done me, seeing it. A cruel gift, seeing. Take the jar -- it holds a marsh-light that won't lead you wrong. Everything else here will.

objectives.label: the mill storeroom
objectives.text: Read the aligned dust in the storeroom

vetka_dust_in_lines.note: Three times swept, the dust returns by dawn in fine parallel lines, pointed east -- toward the grain-house, toward the dark below it. Marcu can no longer call it the season. Seeing is the cruel gift Vetka gives.

vetka_bittterroot_stock.title: Magda's Bitterroot
vetka_bittterroot_stock.summary: Ilie keeps the herb-woman's stock now, and her bitterroot is what keeps a fever from turning. The bog patch she gathered from is thick this year. Bring him a good armful before the damp rots it in the ground.
vetka_bittterroot_stock.offer: I kept old Magda's cottage after -- somebody had to, and I'd sat at her table longest. Her bitterroot's what turns a fever before it turns you, and the patch she worked is thick this year, thick as I've seen. My back won't bend to it, though. Bring me a good armful off the wet ground before the damp takes it, and half the town'll owe you a winter they didn't cough through.
vetka_bittterroot_stock.progress: Magda's bitterroot grows thick in the bog patch west of town. Gather a good armful before the damp rots it.
vetka_bittterroot_stock.complete: That's the stuff -- bitter as a bad debt, and it'll break a fever the same as it did in her hands. Funny, the plant doesn't care she's gone. Comes up thick as ever, asks after no one. Maybe that's the kindness in it. Here, from her shelf -- a poultice she cultured herself. Last of it. Use it on someone worth the last of Magda.

objectives.text: Gather Magda's bitterroot from the bog

vetka_bittterroot_stock.note: Magda's bitterroot comes up thick as ever and asks after no one. Ilie keeps her cottage and her stock and the last poultice she ever cultured. The plant does not grieve her. Perhaps that is its kindness.

vetka_bog_widow_nest.title: Under the Sunken Houses
vetka_bog_widow_nest.summary: The bog-widows have nested under the sunken houses at the south edge, and folk are abandoning the lowest lanes to them house by house. Serban wants the nest broken before the whole low quarter goes open and unlooted -- which is worse than burned.
vetka_bog_widow_nest.offer: The widows have got in under the sunken houses -- the low lanes, where the ground's wettest and the rent's cheapest and the folk have nowhere else. Family's left already, doors open behind them. You know what open doors mean here. Not burned. Worse than burned. Go down and break the nest before the whole low quarter stands empty and waiting. I'd go, but somebody's got to hold a gate that's still worth holding.
vetka_bog_widow_nest.progress: The bog-widows nest beneath the sunken houses at the low south edge. Break the nest.
vetka_bog_widow_nest.complete: Broken, and one lane held. One. There were three when we started this season. I know how that arithmetic ends and so do you, so I'll not pretend the count. But a family sleeps behind a barred door tonight instead of an open one, and out here that's the whole of a victory. Take the skinner. Knapped, not forged -- older way, holds an edge the damp can't argue with.

objectives.label: the sunken houses
objectives.text: Reach the nest under the sunken houses
Break the bog-widow nest

vetka_bog_widow_nest.note: One lane held, of three that stood this season. Serban does not pretend the arithmetic. A family sleeps behind a barred door tonight instead of an open one. Out here that is the whole of a victory.

vetka_undelivered_letter.title: Someone Is Still Waiting
vetka_undelivered_letter.summary: The courier's letter came up sealed, and somewhere a person is still waiting on an answer a wall ate. Toma keeps the town's records; he means to learn who sent the runner, without ever breaking the seal that killed him.
vetka_undelivered_letter.offer: You brought up a sealed letter off a dead man. That letter's got a sender, and a sender's still waiting, months on, for word that isn't coming. I keep the town's book -- I can trace a runner by the toll-marks on his boots and the road-dust in his cuffs, and never once lift that seal. Help me put a name to who's waiting. We won't read it. We'll just stop a stranger listening at a door for a knock that a wall swallowed.
vetka_undelivered_letter.progress: Ask Nedelcu what road the courier came in on, then bring Toma the sealed letter to read its outside only.
vetka_undelivered_letter.complete: A merchant's hand on the outside, bound east, and a woman's name half-worn on the fold -- that's enough. She's owed the truth that he died on the road, not the truth that killed him. I'll send that much up the line: he never faltered, never opened it, carried it sealed to the last. It's near enough true to be a mercy, and mercy's the only lie I'll keep in my book.

objectives.text: Ask Nedelcu which road the courier walked
Bring Toma the sealed letter to read its outside

vetka_undelivered_letter.note: The seal stayed shut. Toma read only the letter's outside -- a merchant's hand, bound east, a woman's half-worn name. He will send word that the courier carried it faithfully to the last. It is near enough true to be a mercy, and the only lie he keeps in his book.

vetka_warm_lane.title: Where the Frost Won't Take
vetka_warm_lane.summary: One lane through town stays warm when the frost takes everywhere else. Dogs won't walk it. Children play there because children play where dogs won't. Vlaicu wants it walked end to end and read before a child is standing in it too still.
vetka_warm_lane.offer: There's a lane runs warm. Frost takes the whole town but skips that one stretch, and the dogs know it -- won't set a paw down it, hackles up, tails under. Children play there for exactly that, the little fools, because a place the dogs fear is a place with no grown folk to shoo them off. Walk it end to end for me. Read the ground. And if a child's standing in it too still, you carry that child out before you do one other thing.
vetka_warm_lane.progress: The warm lane runs between the low houses where the frost has skipped. Walk it end to end and read the ground.
vetka_warm_lane.complete: No child in it, thank the ground for one small thing. But warm, you felt it -- warm from below, in a winter month, and the dust starting its lines already. It's spreading. The copper reached the second well this week; now the warm's found a new lane. It moves like something reading down a list. Take this salt-line -- lay it at your own door before you sleep. It's folk nonsense. Do it anyway.

objectives.label: the warm lane
objectives.text: Walk the warm lane and read the ground

vetka_warm_lane.note: The lane runs warm from below in a winter month, and the dust has begun its lines, and the copper has reached the second well. It moves like something reading down a list. The children play there still. Dogs know better.

vetka_boys_dare.title: A Dare at the Treeline
vetka_boys_dare.summary: The miller's youngest dared the other children to touch the far treeline at dusk and run back, and now they'll do it whether or not a grown soul is watching. Ilie would rather one was. Walk the dusk boundary and turn the little fools homeward before the fog leans in.
vetka_boys_dare.offer: The children have a game now -- run to the far treeline at dusk, touch a certain tree, run back before the fog leans in. The miller's youngest started it and won't be talked out of it, and where one goes the pack follows. I can't run them down, not with these knees. Walk the boundary at dusk. Steer the little fools home. Make it a game you win, not one the woods do. There'll be no harm in it if someone's watching. There's harm in it if no one is.
vetka_boys_dare.progress: The children dare the far treeline at dusk, at the wood's edge west of the last houses. Walk the boundary and turn them home.
vetka_boys_dare.complete: All home, all counted, all cross with you for spoiling the game -- which is exactly how a good evening ends out here, a child cross that nothing happened. Nothing happened. Say it with me. Nothing happened, and that took someone walking the line at dusk to be true. Here, off Magda's shelf, a knot she tied for fretful sleepers. Give it to a child who dreams of that treeline. They will.

objectives.label: the dusk boundary
objectives.text: Walk the children home from the treeline

vetka_boys_dare.note: All the children home and counted, and cross that nothing happened. Nothing happened. It took a grown soul walking the boundary at dusk to keep it so. Ilie counts that a good evening: a child annoyed, and safe.

vetka_road_wont_road.title: When the Road Won't Be a Road
vetka_road_wont_road.summary: Nedelcu's goods rot on the wagon because the old road out of Vetka has stopped delivering carts to the far end. Folk who walk it come back turned around, or don't come back for supper. Scout the road to the overlook and bring word of where it goes now.
vetka_road_wont_road.offer: A merchant lives and dies by roads, and mine's gone strange. The old road east used to hand a cart to the Iron Vein by dusk. Now carts go in and come out turned around, drivers swearing they walked straight the whole while. Two didn't come back for supper; their families set a place anyway. I can't send another driver blind. You've a steadier head. Walk it to the overlook. Tell me the road still ends somewhere a man can sell to.
vetka_road_wont_road.progress: The old east road climbs to the overlook past the last furrows. Scout where it goes and bring word.
vetka_road_wont_road.complete: It ends where it always did, you say -- the Vein, the Bent Oar, the wide world past. Good. Then it's not the road that's wrong, it's the walking of it. Something makes a straight road feel like a straight road while it bends you soft as a stream bends a leaf. I'll send carts by day, in twos, and lay a marked map by every driver's seat. Take one yourself. It won't stop the bending. It'll tell you when you've been bent.

objectives.label: the eastern overlook
objectives.text: Scout the east road to the overlook

vetka_road_wont_road.note: The road still ends where it always did. It is not the road that is wrong but the walking of it -- a straight way that bends the walker soft as a stream bends a leaf. Two drivers never came back to supper. Their families set the place anyway.

vetka_widows_teeth.title: Teeth for the Tempering
vetka_widows_teeth.summary: Iancu can temper an edge harder with bog-widow fang than with any file he owns, and the widows are one thing Vetka is not short of this season. Bring him a good count of fangs and he'll ring out a blade worth the wet walk.
vetka_widows_teeth.offer: Here's a black joke for you: the one thing this town's got in plenty is bog-widow, and their fang tempers an edge harder than any file I own. So the rot gives me my best steel. I'd laugh if I could remember how. Bring me a good count of widow-fang off the wet ground and I'll ring you a river-set blade that'll outlast the both of us -- which, out here, is a promise a smith can nearly keep.
vetka_widows_teeth.progress: Bog-widow fang comes off the widows in the wet ground west and under the sunken houses. Bring Iancu a good count.
vetka_widows_teeth.complete: Look at that edge. Rot's own tooth, and it holds truer than good ore ever did. There's a lesson in that I'd rather not have learned. River-set, this one -- bog-iron spine, widow-fang edge, quenched in the Vein where it runs slow. It'll never rust and it'll never pretty up. Same as the town. Same as me. Take it and mind the wet.

objectives.text: Take widow-fang from the bog-widows

vetka_widows_teeth.note: The rot gives Iancu his best steel -- widow-fang holds an edge truer than honest ore. He would rather not have learned the lesson. The river-set blade will never rust and never pretty up. Same as the town.

vetka_chronicle_the_rot.title: A True Accounting of a Bad Season
vetka_chronicle_the_rot.summary: Toma keeps the town's book and means to write this bad season down plainly, before Vetka agrees to forget it. He asks a clear-eyed witness to walk the symptoms with him, so the record says a true thing when no one else will.
vetka_chronicle_the_rot.offer: Every town that dies quiet does it the same way: by agreeing, one soul at a time, that nothing's wrong. I keep the book. I mean this bad season written down plain, so a stranger in a hundred years reads what happened and not what we told ourselves. But a book needs a witness who saw with clear eyes. Walk the symptoms with me -- talk to Dorica of her well, and stand once more in the warm lane -- and I'll set it down true, copper and all.
vetka_chronicle_the_rot.progress: Speak with Ilie of what the town won't say, then stand once more in the warm lane so the record reads true.
vetka_chronicle_the_rot.complete: It's written, then. The wells went copper on a market day. The bread came flat. A lane runs warm and the dust points east. A man rests at a fence since a Tuesday nobody will name. I've put no comfort in it and no cause I can't prove. That's the kindest thing I know how to do -- to keep the true version, so that when we've all agreed to forget, the book won't have. Take the record's twin. Keep it somewhere we can't reach to soften it.

objectives.text: Gather what the town won't say from Ilie

objectives.label: the warm lane
objectives.text: Witness the warm lane for the record

vetka_chronicle_the_rot.note: Toma set the season down plain: the wells copper on a market day, the flat bread, the warm lane, the man resting since a Tuesday no one names. No comfort, no unproven cause. When the town has agreed to forget, the book will not have.

vetka_last_dog.title: The Dog That Wouldn't Walk the Lane
vetka_last_dog.summary: Marcu's old dog has gone missing, and the mill is quieter for it in a way he doesn't like. The animal never would set foot in the warm lane. Find the dog, or find where a creature goes when it finally refuses the one path home.
vetka_last_dog.offer: My dog's gone. Old thing, half-deaf, wouldn't hurt a rat if the rat asked nicely. But she had sense the rest of us lost -- wouldn't cross the warm lane, not for meat, not for me. Now she's not come home two nights, and the mill's quiet in a way I don't care for. Go look where a sensible creature goes. Not the warm lane -- she'd never. The wet ground, maybe, or the treeline. Find her, or find what a good dog runs from.
vetka_last_dog.progress: Look for the dog where a sensible creature would run -- the wet ground west, away from the warm lane.
vetka_last_dog.complete: By the wet ground, curled up and cold, and no mark on her -- ran her old heart out getting as far from the warm as she could, and made it, near enough. That's a better death than the town's like to get. She knew. Dumb beast knew more than the lot of us and couldn't say it but with her four feet. I'll bury her where she stopped. Take her collar-charm. Magda blessed it. For all the good.

objectives.label: the wet ground
objectives.text: Find Marcu's dog by the wet ground

vetka_last_dog.note: The old dog ran her heart out getting clear of the warm, and near made it. No mark on her. She knew more than the town and could say it only with her four feet. Marcu buried her where she stopped running.

vetka_petrus_thumb.title: A Thumb Still in the Page
vetka_petrus_thumb.summary: Petru has kept the Bent Oar's warmth going by not asking questions, and he knows it. Now he asks one. He wants a plain reckoning of who in Vetka still answers their own name twice -- a quiet census of the town's remaining selves.
vetka_petrus_thumb.offer: I've kept this hearth warm forty years by minding my own and pouring the next. I know what that's cost. So here's my one question, and I hate that I'm asking it: how many of us are left. Not bodies -- selves. Folk who answer their own name the second time you say it. Go quiet through the town. Speak to Ilie, who watches the listeners, and to Serban at the gate, who counts who comes and goes. Bring me the number. I'll pour whatever it is.
vetka_petrus_thumb.progress: Ask Ilie who still answers twice, and Serban who still comes and goes, then bring Petru the town's true count.
vetka_petrus_thumb.complete: Fewer than last month. More than I feared, at the black hour I feared it. That's the reckoning, then -- Vetka's not lost, only lessening, one curious glance at a time. The Pause is a bookmark, they say up the river, and lately I hear the pages turning. Well. Long as I can pour, there's a hearth. Long as there's a hearth, there's a town. Sit. First one's mine.

objectives.text: Ask Ilie who still answers twice
Ask Serban who still comes and goes

vetka_petrus_thumb.note: Petru took the town's true census -- selves, not bodies, the ones who answer twice. Fewer than last month; more than the black hour feared. Vetka is not lost, only lessening, one curious glance at a time. As long as he can pour, there is a hearth.

vetka_shade_of_the_kind.title: The Shade That Fed Six Winters
vetka_shade_of_the_kind.summary: Magda's shade will not stay down. Toma believes it is not the stone that keeps her walking but the town's own refusal to let her rest. Lay her down a final time, and give Vetka leave to grieve her clean.
vetka_shade_of_the_kind.offer: She's up again, Magda. I laid her three times now and she rises, and I've stopped believing it's the marks that do it. It's us. Six winters she fed strangers, and every soul she fed still reaches for her at the black hour, still calls her name like a child in the dark. Love can keep a body walking near as cruel as the stone can. Lay her down once more -- and then let me ring the bell for her, proper, so the town lets go the hand it's been holding.
vetka_shade_of_the_kind.progress: Lay Magda's shade to rest a final time in the old yard, then stand for the bell at her kerb.
vetka_shade_of_the_kind.complete: She's down. And the bell's rung, and folk wept who'd not let themselves before, and that -- that is what keeps her now. Not us clutching, but us letting go clean. The stone can raise a body. It's got no entry for a town that finally grieved right. Here. This was hers -- a token with her kept name on it. Carry it. The archive that comes after us will lose the woman. You won't.

objectives.text: Lay Magda's shade to a final rest

objectives.label: Magda's kerb
objectives.text: Stand for the bell at Magda's kerb

vetka_shade_of_the_kind.note: Magda rests, and the bell rang, and a town that would not weep finally did. Love had kept her walking near as cruel as the stone. Letting go clean is what the stone has no entry for. The archive to come will lose the woman; the token keeps her name.

vetka_moor_at_the_gate.title: The Moor Comes to the Gate
vetka_moor_at_the_gate.summary: The moor-wails no longer keep to the wet ground; they've drifted to the town gate itself, and Serban has watched two nights alone. He needs the wailing broken at the threshold before Vetka's last held edge stops being held.
vetka_moor_at_the_gate.offer: It's at the gate now. Two nights I've stood and listened to the moor wail from the wrong side of the wall -- our side. The wet ground was one thing; a man could tell himself the town was the town and the moor was the moor. That line's gone. If the gate stops being a gate, Vetka stops being a place and starts being ground the fog walks through. Break the wailers at the threshold. I'll hold the bar. Go, before I've stood here long enough to stop answering my own name.
vetka_moor_at_the_gate.progress: The moor-wails have drifted to the town gate itself. Break them at the threshold before the last edge fails.
vetka_moor_at_the_gate.complete: Held. The gate's a gate one more night. You heard it though -- how the wailing's got a shape now, almost a shape, two notes of it near enough to hum. I put my thumbs in my ears like a child and I'm not ashamed. A man holds what he can and covers his ears for the rest. Take the ward. Folk-made, means nothing, works because it means nothing. Hang it on the gate. It's the only kind of nothing that's ever helped out here.

objectives.label: the town gate
objectives.text: Reach the gate the moor has come to
Break the wailers at the threshold

vetka_moor_at_the_gate.note: The moor came to the gate, and Serban held it one more night. The wailing has near a shape now -- two notes of it, close enough to hum. He put his thumbs in his ears like a child. A man holds what he can and covers his ears for the rest.

vetka_bent_oar_daily.title: Word for the Bent Oar
vetka_bent_oar_daily.summary: The Bent Oar downriver runs on rumor, and rumor runs dry when the road won't cooperate. Petru pays a coin a day for anyone willing to walk the boundary and bring back a true account of what the night left behind.
vetka_bent_oar_daily.offer: The Oar keeps a board of what's true this week, and lately my share of true things has thinned with the road. So here's standing coin: walk the town's edge once, wherever the night's left a mark -- a fresh symptom, a moved thing, a quiet house -- and bring it back plain. No embroidery. A rumor sells better dressed up, but out here a dressed-up rumor gets somebody standing too still at a fence. Bring me the bare of it. Daily, if you like. The coin's daily too.
vetka_bent_oar_daily.progress: Walk the town's edge and read whatever the night has left, then bring Petru the bare account.
vetka_bent_oar_daily.complete: Bare and true, just as asked. I'll chalk it on the board and the Oar downriver will know what Vetka knows by supper. That's a small machine that still works, that is -- word for coin, coin for bread, bread for another day of both. Small machines are the ones I trust. Here's your coin. Come again tomorrow; the night always leaves something.

objectives.label: the town's edge
objectives.text: Read what the night left at the town's edge

vetka_bent_oar_daily.note: Word for coin, coin for bread, bread for another day of both. Petru trusts small machines, being one himself. The night always leaves something to chalk on the board, and the Bent Oar downriver knows what Vetka knows by supper.

vetka_widow_bounty_daily.title: Standing Bounty: the Wet Ground
vetka_widow_bounty_daily.summary: The town keeps a standing bounty on whatever crawls up out of the wet ground, paid by the head, no questions on the count. Vlaicu would rather empty the small purse nightly than dig one more grave. Thin the wet ground and collect.
vetka_widow_bounty_daily.offer: We keep a purse for the wet ground -- widows, wailers, whatever the bog's decided to grow this week. Paid by the head, and I trust your count because a man who'd cheat the widow-bounty deserves the widows. I'd rather empty this purse every night than fill one more grave, and those are honestly the only two options left me. So. Go thin the wet. Come back. I'll pay you out of the small coin and sleep the better for it.
vetka_widow_bounty_daily.progress: Thin whatever the wet ground has grown -- widows in the bog west of town -- and return for the bounty.
vetka_widow_bounty_daily.complete: Good count, and I'll not check it. The purse holds another night and my spade stays clean another day, and between those two facts is the whole of what passes for peace out here. Take your coin.
