eastern_ridges_ledger_road_01.offer: I run blackglass up to the listening city, and I'll tell you a thing they don't teach traders: in Blestem, the price is set before you speak, because they already know what you paid for it. Fine. That's their game. But now the game's followed me west, onto my own ridges. Carry a load of blackglass to the cleft-marker for me. I'll hang back and watch who steps out of the fog to price you. Let's see whose ears these are.
eastern_ridges_ledger_road_01.progress: Carry the blackglass to the cleft-marker on the east road while Maricica watches from the fog. Reach the marker.
eastern_ridges_ledger_road_01.complete: Somebody stepped out to price you before you'd stopped walking. Grey coat, no face I could keep -- I looked right at him and lost him twice. That's a Blestem trick, the managed blankness. They've put a reader on my road, this far from their walls. They want us used to being watched before they ever ask for anything. Take the blackglass razor. If the corners have ears, at least you'll have an edge.

objectives.label: the cleft-marker
objectives.text: Carry blackglass to the cleft-marker

eastern_ridges_ledger_road_01.note: A grey-coated reader stepped from the fog to price the traveler before a word was spoken -- the listening city's managed blankness, set to work a day's walk from its own walls. Blestem wants the ridges used to being watched. Maricica understood the lesson. She wishes she did not.

eastern_ridges_ledger_road_02.title: The Dossier
eastern_ridges_ledger_road_02.summary: The grey reader left something behind on the cleft road: a slim dossier, un-addressed, its pages full of a stranger's habits written in a clerk's clean hand. Maricica wants to know whose life it prices -- and asks the innkeeper, who knows every traveler's face.
eastern_ridges_ledger_road_02.offer: Your grey-coat dropped this, or wanted it found -- with that lot you never know which. It's a dossier. Someone's habits, written down cold: when they sleep, which well they use, the hour they let their guard down. No name on it. Take it to Floarea at the inn. She's watched a thousand travelers cross her threshold. If anyone can put a face to these habits, it's her. And if she can't -- burn it, and be glad.
eastern_ridges_ledger_road_02.progress: Carry the un-addressed dossier to Floarea at the inn and let her read the habits it prices.
eastern_ridges_ledger_road_02.complete: Floarea read one page and went white and handed it straight back. The habits are hers. Every one. When she banks the fire, which stair she trusts, the well she draws from at dawn -- a stranger wrote her own evening down and left it on my road to be found. That's the whole Blestem cruelty in one page: they don't need to hurt you. They just need you to know they could, and hand you the proof yourself. Keep the dossier. Learn from it. Then never sleep the same way twice.

objectives.text: Take the dossier to Floarea at the inn

eastern_ridges_ledger_road_02.note: The un-addressed dossier priced Floarea's own evening -- when she banks her fire, which stair she trusts, the well she draws at dawn -- written cold by a hand she never saw. Blestem does not need to strike. It needs only to hand you the proof that it could, and let you carry it home.

eastern_ridges_ledger_road_03.title: The Reader in the Grey
eastern_ridges_ledger_road_03.summary: Maricica has had enough of being priced on her own road. She has found where the grey reader shelters when the fog lifts -- a hollow under the cleft-stones -- and she wants him met. Not killed. Met. So he knows the ridges read back.
eastern_ridges_ledger_road_03.offer: I'm done being counted like stock at a fair. The reader shelters in a hollow under the cleft-stones when the fog thins -- I followed the one trail he couldn't hide, his own habit of shelter. Go to the hollow. Don't draw on him. Just stand where he can't lose you, and let him understand the road he's been reading has learned to read him. That's the only language his masters respect: being seen back.
eastern_ridges_ledger_road_03.progress: The grey reader shelters in the hollow beneath the cleft-stones when the fog lifts. Reach the hollow and let yourself be seen.
eastern_ridges_ledger_road_03.complete: You stood in his doorway and he didn't run this time. Left you something instead -- a lens, Blestem-ground, the kind their readers use to hold a face they mean to keep. A gift or a threat, and with them it's always both. But he moved his shelter that same night. So we're even, for a season: he knows the ridges read back, and we know he can be made to blink. Take the lens. Now you can hold a face too. Choose whose.

objectives.label: the reader's hollow
objectives.text: Confront the reader at his hollow

eastern_ridges_ledger_road_03.note: The grey reader met the traveler's eyes and, for once, was the one to move his shelter by dark. He left a Blestem lens behind -- a gift, a threat, with the listening city always both. The ridges read back now. It buys a season. Nothing on this road is bought for longer.

eastern_ridges_fog_reads_01.title: The Fog Learns Names
eastern_ridges_fog_reads_01.summary: Tinca's boy went up to the spring in the fog and came back saying the grey called him by name. She does not believe it. She needs someone to walk to the spring and listen, and come back saying it was only the wind in the cleft.
eastern_ridges_fog_reads_01.offer: My boy came down from the spring white as milk, saying the fog said his name. Wind, I told him. Wind in the cleft-stones sounds like near enough anything if you're small and it's grey. But he won't go back up, and we need the water. Walk to the spring for me. Stand a moment in the grey. Come tell me it was wind. That's all I want -- somebody grown to say it was wind.
eastern_ridges_fog_reads_01.progress: The spring sits in a fold of the ridge where the fog gathers. Reach it, stand in the grey, and listen.
eastern_ridges_fog_reads_01.complete: ...You stood there a while before you answered. That's not the answer of somebody who heard wind. But you're saying wind, and I'll take wind, and I'll draw the water at midday from now on when the grey's thin. He's a good boy. He doesn't lie. I know that. That's the part I'm trying not to think about. Here -- for the walk, and for saying it kindly.

objectives.label: the fogged spring
objectives.text: Listen at the fogged spring

eastern_ridges_fog_reads_01.note: The traveler stood a long moment in the grey before answering that it was only wind. Tinca chose to believe it. Her boy does not lie -- that is the part she is trying not to think about. She draws the water at midday now, when the fog is thin.

eastern_ridges_fog_reads_02.title: The Water at Midday
eastern_ridges_fog_reads_02.summary: Drawing at midday is not enough. The near spring has warmed and gone copper -- the ground's oldest wrongness, come to the ridges. Tinca asks for water hauled from the far clean spring, and for the truth about her own.
eastern_ridges_fog_reads_02.offer: The spring's gone warm. In this cold, in these stones, warm. And it's taken a taste I know from my grandmother's stories and hoped I'd die not knowing. Copper. I can't give my boy copper water and midday won't fix that. There's a clean spring on the west shoulder, out past the harpy crags, where the fog doesn't reach yet. Haul me what you can. And don't drink from the warm one. Not a drop. Promise me.
eastern_ridges_fog_reads_02.progress: The clean spring lies on the west shoulder, past the harpy crags, where the fog has not yet reached. Reach it and draw clean water.
eastern_ridges_fog_reads_02.complete: Cold. Cold and it tastes of nothing but stone and rain, thank the old wind. That's water. The warm spring's finished -- I've laid a salt-line round it and a hex-sign my grandmother taught me, not because it'll help but because doing nothing is worse. The copper's come to the ridges. It came the way it always comes: quiet, and to the smallest well first. Take the tonic. Bitter, but it settles what the fog unsettles.

objectives.label: the clean west spring
objectives.text: Haul clean water from the west spring

eastern_ridges_fog_reads_02.note: The near spring went warm and copper -- the ground's oldest wrongness, arrived in the ridges, come as it always comes: quiet, and to the smallest well first. Tinca laid salt and a hex-sign around it, knowing they will not help, because doing nothing is worse.

eastern_ridges_forge_stone_01.title: Stone That Rings Wrong
eastern_ridges_forge_stone_01.summary: Ioana forges the wardens' blades from ridge-basalt, and it has always rung true under her hammer. Lately a seam of it rings flat and dead. She needs good stone quarried from the old outcrops, where the rock still remembers how to sing.
eastern_ridges_forge_stone_01.offer: Come here, put your ear to this. Hear that? Dead. Basalt should ring like a struck bell -- it's what makes the wardens' blades hold an edge in the wet. But there's a seam gone flat, no note in it at all, like the stone's forgotten it was ever a mountain. I won't forge dead metal. There's an old outcrop northwest that still sings true. Quarry me a good load, and I'll keep the wardens armed another season.
eastern_ridges_forge_stone_01.progress: The singing outcrops lie northwest of the forge, where the basalt still rings true. Reach them and quarry sound stone.
eastern_ridges_forge_stone_01.complete: Now listen -- THAT'S a note. Strike it and it answers, every time, the way stone's answered a smith since the first fool hit a rock and liked the sound. I'll have a dozen good blades out of this. Funny thing -- the dead seam runs east, toward the cleft, and the singing stone all lies west. As if something's been going down the mountain, note by note, from that direction. Ah, listen to me. Take the striker. Best I've made this year.

objectives.label: the singing outcrops
objectives.text: Quarry sound stone from the outcrops

eastern_ridges_forge_stone_01.note: The dead seam of basalt runs east toward the cleft; the stone that still sings all lies west. Something has been going down the mountain note by note, silencing it from the direction of Blestem. Ioana laughed the thought off. She quarried west anyway.

eastern_ridges_forge_stone_02.title: The Golem's Heart
eastern_ridges_forge_stone_02.summary: Ioana has worked out where the dead note comes from: the waking basalt carries a worked core, cut smooth, that will not ring at all. She wants one such heart brought to her whole -- not to study, she says. To weigh how heavy the wrong has grown.
eastern_ridges_forge_stone_02.offer: I've been thinking on the dead stone, and I don't like where the thinking leads. The waking golems -- when the wardens crack one, there's a heart inside, smooth as poured glass and dead as a stopped clock. That's the flat note. That's what's going down my mountain. Bring me one whole. Not to keep -- old wind, no. Just to hold it once, and know the exact weight of the thing that's coming for the ridges. A smith likes to know what she's forging against.
eastern_ridges_forge_stone_02.progress: The waking basalt carries a smooth, worked heart. Break one open and bring the golem's core to Ioana whole.
eastern_ridges_forge_stone_02.complete: Heavier than it's any right to be. And warm -- warm, this stone, when the mountain it grew in is cold enough to crack your teeth. There's angle-marks on it my hammer couldn't cut in a year, and it doesn't ring, won't ring, can't. This is older than my forge and it's newer than yesterday, both at once, and I don't have the words. I'm sinking it in the deep quench and never looking again. Take the razor. And if you find where these are cut -- don't. Just don't.

objectives.text: Break a golem and take its heart whole

eastern_ridges_forge_stone_02.note: The golem's heart came out warm in a mountain cold enough to crack teeth, cut with angles no hammer could match, ringing no note at all. Ioana sank it in the deep quench and swore never to look again. Old as her forge, new as yesterday, and no words for it.

eastern_ridges_cliff_watch_01.title: The Level They Keep
eastern_ridges_cliff_watch_01.summary: Neaga has noticed the Cliff-Stalkers no longer den where prey is thickest -- they den in an even ring along the ridge, spaced like fence-posts. She wants the ring broken and the pattern proven, before she has to write it in a report no one will believe.
eastern_ridges_cliff_watch_01.offer: A beast dens where the food is. Basic as breathing. But the Stalkers on the south face have denned in a ring -- even-spaced, all one height off the valley floor, like somebody paced it off with a measuring-cord. I've counted the gaps twice. They're identical. Clear the south-face dens for me. I need the ring broken, and I need to have seen it broken, before I can make myself write it down.
eastern_ridges_cliff_watch_01.progress: The Cliff-Stalkers have denned in an even ring along the south face. Clear the dens and break the pattern.
eastern_ridges_cliff_watch_01.complete: Broken, and I watched the last of them fall, and I still don't believe it though I saw it with my own two eyes. Even spacing, one height, gaps all matched -- that's not hunting. That's a survey line. Something's using my beasts to measure my mountain, den by den, the way you'd mark out ground before you build. I'll write the report. Nobody up the road will believe it. Somebody, someday, ought to have written it anyway. Take the coin, and take care.

objectives.text: Clear the ring of Stalker dens

eastern_ridges_cliff_watch_01.note: The Cliff-Stalkers had denned in an even ring, one height, gaps all matched -- a survey line worn into the mountain by living beasts that do not survey. Neaga wrote the report knowing no one up the road would believe it. Somebody, someday, ought to have written it anyway.

eastern_ridges_cliff_watch_02.title: The Dust in the Dens
eastern_ridges_cliff_watch_02.summary: In the cleared dens Neaga found something worse than the ring: the floor-dust lies in fine parallel lines, all pointing east. She sends you back to the deepest den to confirm it -- and to stand there long enough to be sure the lines are not pointing at you.
eastern_ridges_cliff_watch_02.offer: I went back to the dens after you cleared them. Wish I hadn't. The dust on the den floors lies in lines -- fine, even, parallel, like filings pulled toward a lodestone -- and every line points east. I need a second pair of eyes before I can trust my own. Go to the deepest den. Look at the floor. Stand a while. Tell me the lines point east and not at whoever's standing in the room.
eastern_ridges_cliff_watch_02.progress: The deepest Stalker den lies at the base of the south face. Reach it and read the dust on the floor.
eastern_ridges_cliff_watch_02.complete: East. You're certain. Not at you -- east, at the cleft, every line. That's some comfort, if a cold sort: it means the thing isn't looking at us yet. It's looking THROUGH us, past us, at the city in the fog. We're just the ground it's pacing on its way there. I've salted every den. Won't help. Take the lantern -- it burns steady even where the dust wants to lie in lines. Small mercy. Out here we count the small ones.

objectives.label: the deepest den
objectives.text: Read the dust in the deepest den

eastern_ridges_cliff_watch_02.note: The den-dust lay in fine parallel lines, all aimed east at the cleft -- the ground's fingerprint, the Underlanguage running beneath the skin of the ridge. The lines do not point at the ridge-folk yet. They point through them, at the city in the fog. Neaga takes what comfort the small mercy allows.

eastern_ridges_lantern_line_01.title: The Lanterns Won't Stay Lit
eastern_ridges_lantern_line_01.summary: Floarea's inn is the last warm door on the ridge road, and she keeps a lantern-line burning out to the cleft for travelers caught in the fog. Lately the far lanterns keep going dark. She needs them re-lit and re-oiled, and the travelers home.
eastern_ridges_lantern_line_01.offer: This inn's kept a lantern-line out toward the cleft since my great-grandmother's day -- so nobody caught in the grey has to guess which way is warm. Lately the far three keep dying, and I've had two travelers wander half the night who ought to have been by my fire in an hour. Go out and light them proper, oil and wick and all. A lit road home is the whole reason a border keeps a hearth.
eastern_ridges_lantern_line_01.progress: The far lanterns of Floarea's line stand dark along the road to the cleft. Reach them and light the road home.
eastern_ridges_lantern_line_01.complete: Lit, all three, and burning steady -- bless you. Do you know, my great-grandmother lit that far one the night the border was quiet enough to hear a fiddle from Blestem, and she said as long as it burned, the ridges belonged to the folk who kept the fire and not to the city that kept the ledger. Silly old thing to remember. Warm, though. Here -- a bowl and a bed, and the keepsake she left. It's yours as long as the line's yours to keep.

objectives.label: the far lantern-line
objectives.text: Light the far lanterns of the road home

eastern_ridges_lantern_line_01.note: Floarea's lantern-line burns again to the cleft, the last warm road home on the eastern border. Her great-grandmother lit the far lamp saying that as long as it burned, the ridges belonged to the folk who kept the fire and not the city that kept the ledger. It is a small light. It is kept anyway.

eastern_ridges_lantern_line_02.title: Who Puts the Lanterns Out
eastern_ridges_lantern_line_02.summary: The far lanterns did not fail on their own. Floarea has found them capped -- snuffed deliberately, hoods set neat over the flames by a careful hand. She wants to know who walks her road at night putting out the light, and why.
eastern_ridges_lantern_line_02.offer: The lanterns didn't die. They were put out. I found the hoods set over them, neat as you please, snug, the way you'd cap a candle you meant to keep the wick for. Somebody walks my road in the dark, unhooding as they go west, capping again on the way back. They want the cleft road dark. A dark road's a road nobody watches you walk. Find their track. I'll not have my great-grandmother's line snuffed to hide somebody's errand.
eastern_ridges_lantern_line_02.progress: Whoever snuffs the lanterns leaves a track along the road at the cleft-marker. Reach the marker and find their trail.
eastern_ridges_lantern_line_02.complete: You found the cap-marks and the boot-prints going both ways, and a coin dropped in the mud -- Blestem-struck, the listening city's own mint. So that's the errand. They're not smuggling goods. They're smuggling quiet. A dark road, a snuffed line, and folk taught not to notice who crosses at night -- that's how a border stops being a border and starts being a corridor. I'll relight the line and post a watch. Take the pouch. And thank you for looking. Most folk have learned not to.

objectives.label: the snuffed lantern track
objectives.text: Track who snuffs the lantern-line

eastern_ridges_lantern_line_02.note: The lanterns were snuffed by a careful hand, hooded neat, and the mud held a Blestem-struck coin. The errand was not goods but quiet -- a dark road, folk taught not to notice who crosses at night. That is how a border stops being a border and becomes a corridor. Floarea relit the line and posted a watch.

eastern_ridges_grey_fair_01.title: The Fair Under the Teeth
eastern_ridges_grey_fair_01.summary: The ridge-folk hold a small autumn fair where the four roads meet, and Zoe runs the sweets-stall. The harpies scattered her flour-sacks off the crag-path. She needs the good stuff hauled back before the dancing starts.
eastern_ridges_grey_fair_01.offer: It's the fair tomorrow -- proper fair, fiddles and dancing and my grandmother's honey-cakes, the one day these grim teeth let us be a village and not a watch-post. But the harpies knocked my flour off the crag-path swooping for their roost, and there's no cakes without flour. Haul my sacks back down before the dancing? I'll save you the first cake, and it's a cake worth saving, I promise you that.
eastern_ridges_grey_fair_01.progress: Zoe's flour-sacks lie scattered on the crag-path below the harpy roost. Reach the path and haul them back for the fair.
eastern_ridges_grey_fair_01.complete: You got them all, and only one split -- I'll bake round it. Come to the fair tonight, will you? Stand in the light and eat a cake and dance one dance and let the ridges be ordinary for the length of a fiddle-tune. It doesn't last. Nothing good does out here -- that's what makes it good, my grandmother says. So come and have your share of it. Here's your cake. And here's a coin the fair brought in that actually smiled at me.

objectives.label: the crag-path
objectives.text: Haul Zoe's flour back for the fair

eastern_ridges_grey_fair_01.note: For the length of a fiddle-tune the ridge-folk danced at the four roads and were a village, not a watch-post. It did not last -- nothing good does out here, which Zoe's grandmother says is what makes it good. The traveler had a cake and a dance and their share of it.

eastern_ridges_grey_fair_02.title: The Empty Chair
eastern_ridges_grey_fair_02.summary: A trader who came to the fair every year did not come this year. Zoe kept his chair and his cake. She wants someone to walk to his last camp on the cleft road and learn whether he is merely late, or whether the road east has kept another one.
eastern_ridges_grey_fair_02.offer: There's a chair I keep at the fair for old Barnabe -- the tin-trader, came every autumn since I was a girl, always late, always loud, always ate three cakes and paid for one. He didn't come. First year in thirty. I kept his chair. I kept his cake. It's gone stale and I can't make myself throw it out. His last camp's on the cleft road. Go and see. If he's just late I'll laugh at myself. If he's not... somebody should know his name, and I do.
eastern_ridges_grey_fair_02.progress: Old Barnabe's last camp lies on the cleft road east. Reach it and learn what became of him.
eastern_ridges_grey_fair_02.complete: Cold ashes, and his cart, and his tin all still in it -- nobody robbed him, he just... stopped. Sat down by his own fire and didn't get up, they say it takes them like that near the cleft, no wound, no struggle, like the road asked them a question they answered too well. I'll throw the cake out now. I'll keep the chair. Thirty years he ate three and paid for one, and I'd give a season's honey to shout at him for it once more. Take the knot. It won't help. Neither will crying. I'm doing both.

objectives.label: Barnabe's last camp
objectives.text: Find what became of old Barnabe

eastern_ridges_grey_fair_02.note: Old Barnabe sat down by his own fire on the cleft road and did not get up -- no wound, no struggle, his cart unrobbed, the way the road takes them near the cleft, as if it asked a question they answered too well. Zoe threw out his stale cake and kept his chair. Thirty years he ate three and paid for one.

eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_01.title: The Colour of the Cleft
eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_01.summary: Safta has kept the ridge's register long enough to read its every symptom. The warm ground, the copper spring, the aligned dust -- all point east, all sharpen toward the cleft-stones above the road to Blestem. She asks you to read that ground with alchemical sight, and to come back curious about nothing.
eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_01.offer: I've written down every wrong thing in these teeth for forty years, and I can read the ladder now the way a farmer reads weather. Warm ground, copper wells, the dust in lines -- it's the oldest sickness in Draconia and it always climbs the same rungs. It's climbing here, and every rung points at the cleft-stones. I want a reading with the sight -- the proper alchemical sight -- taken right at the stones. Look for the colour. And whatever colour it is: read it, write it, and want nothing more. The wanting-more is how it takes you.
eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_01.progress: The cleft-stones stand above the road to Blestem, where the ridge's symptoms sharpen to a point. Reach them and read the ground with alchemical sight.
eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_01.complete: You saw it, then. Not blue -- that's the dead, we could bear the dead. Not violet, not green. Shifting. Orange. The colour of a thing writing itself into the stone as you watch it. That's the worst reading in the book and it's the one I feared. The signal isn't near the cleft. The signal is coming UP the cleft, from the city, warming the ridges rung by rung. Drink this and forget the shape of what you saw. I mean it. The reading is safe in my register. The looking is not safe in you.

objectives.label: the cleft-stones
objectives.text: Read the cleft-stones with alchemical sight

eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_01.note: The cleft-stones read shifting orange -- a signal writing itself into the rock as it was watched, climbing up from the listening city rung by rung. Not the dead. Not the deep stone. The villain's own colour. Safta kept the reading safe in her register and begged the traveler to forget the shape of what they saw. The reading is safe. The looking is not.

eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_02.title: The Sealed Satchel
eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_02.summary: A courier lies dead on the cleft road -- no wound, clean hands, satchel still sealed, the way the road takes them. Paraschiva cannot leave the message rotting on her mountain, nor read it, nor bear to. She asks you to carry the sealed satchel down to the chronicler, and not to open it on the way.
eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_02.offer: There's a dead man on the cleft road and I need you to understand how he died before you go up. No wound. Hands clean as a child's. His satchel sealed, wax unbroken, nothing taken. He didn't die of the road. He died of what he was carrying -- of understanding it, the wardens say, the way the courier in the old stories died. Bring the satchel down to Safta. Sealed. Don't break the wax. Don't read a word. The reading is what killed him, and you're worth more to me alive and ignorant.
eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_02.progress: The dead courier lies on the cleft road, his sealed satchel beside him. Carry it -- sealed, unread -- down to Safta the chronicler.
eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_02.complete: You brought it down sealed. Good. That's the hard part and you did the hard part. Safta will lock it in the deep register with the other things nobody should have read -- and it'll sit there sealed until the day someone weak or curious or brave breaks the wax, and then we'll lose them the way we lost him. That's the whole shape of this war, traveler. Not a battle. A sealed thing, and everyone who wants to know what's inside. You didn't want to know. That's rarer than courage. Take the lens. Watch the fog with it. Don't watch too long.

objectives.label: the dead courier
objectives.text: Recover the courier's sealed satchel
Carry the sealed satchel to Safta

eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_02.note: The courier died of his own message -- no wound, clean hands, the wax unbroken -- the way understanding kills on the cleft road. The traveler carried the satchel down sealed and unread. It sits in the deep register now, sealed, waiting for someone weak or curious or brave. That is the shape of the war: a sealed thing, and everyone who wants inside.

eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_03.title: The Watcher Repeats
eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_03.summary: The grey reader who watched the ridges has stopped watching -- he sits at the cleft-marker now, still, facing the fog, and repeats one sentence to no one. Safta needs it heard by someone who can walk away from the words. She asks you to reach him, hear the sentence once, and leave.
eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_03.offer: Your grey-coat has changed. He doesn't read the road anymore -- he sits at the cleft-marker, still as the stone, facing the fog, and he says one sentence over and over to nobody. The wardens who got close say it's not his voice, quite. Say it's flat. Patient. That it uses a deed instead of a name. Go and hear it -- once, at a distance, then walk away the moment you've got it. Don't answer him. Whatever it says about you, it says to hook you. Hear it. Leave. Come tell me, and we'll write it in the deep register with the rest.
eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_03.progress: The grey reader sits at the cleft-marker, repeating one sentence to the fog. Reach him, hear the words once, and walk away.
eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_03.complete: You heard it. And you're pale, and you left, and both of those are exactly right. It named a deed of yours, didn't it -- not your name, never your name, just a thing you did, said back to you flat and patient like an entry in a book. That's not the reader talking. The reader's just the mouth. The thing in the fog borrowed him the way it borrowed the harpies and the stone, to say one sentence to the one person on this ridge worth saying it to. It's noticed you. That's the last thing I wanted to write in the register, and I've written it. Take the ward. It means nothing, loudly. Out here, that's the only armour that holds.

objectives.label: the repeating watcher
objectives.text: Hear the watcher's sentence, then leave

eastern_ridges_read_the_land_main_03.note: The grey reader sat facing the fog and repeated one flat, patient sentence -- a deed of the traveler's, never their name, said back like an entry in a book. The reader was only the mouth. The thing in the fog borrowed him to speak to the one soul on the ridge worth speaking to. It has noticed. Safta wrote it in the deep register: the last line she wanted to write.

eastern_ridges_daily_patrol.title: Walk the Teeth
eastern_ridges_daily_patrol.summary: The ridge-watch never ends, because the fog never rests. Neaga sets the day's patrol: thin the Cliff-Stalkers before they can pace out another survey line across her mountain.
eastern_ridges_daily_patrol.offer: Fog's up, so the Stalkers are hunting, so the patrol goes out -- same as every day the grey allows, which lately is all of them. Walk the south face and thin them before they finish measuring whatever it is they measure. I'll mark it done in the book, same as always. The book's the only thing out here that stays honest for me. Off you go.
eastern_ridges_daily_patrol.progress: The Cliff-Stalkers work the south face under the fog. Thin them on today's patrol.
eastern_ridges_daily_patrol.complete: Marked and done. The ring's broken again -- it'll be back tomorrow, spaced just the same, because the thing that draws it doesn't tire and I do. That's the job, though: not winning, just not losing today. Today we didn't lose. Take your pay and get your rest. The fog'll want walking again come morning.

objectives.text: Thin the Stalkers on today's patrol

eastern_ridges_daily_patrol.note: The patrol is walked, the ring broken, the book marked -- and by morning the Stalkers will have paced it out again, spaced just the same. The job is not winning. It is not losing today. Today, the ridges did not lose.

eastern_ridges_daily_crag_cull.title: The Roost's Toll
eastern_ridges_daily_crag_cull.summary: Safta keeps the harpy register honest by the hardest arithmetic there is: each day she must strike out the ones that have stopped flying, before the listening pose spreads to the birds that still ride the wind.
eastern_ridges_daily_crag_cull.offer: Every dawn I climb far enough to count, and every dawn there's another few hanging on the wind facing the cleft, wings open, gone. I strike their line, I send you up, you bring them down before the healthy ones learn the pose from watching. It's a foul sum to do each morning -- subtract what you love before it teaches the rest to stop. But it's mine to do, and I'll do it till there's nothing left to count or nobody left to count it.
eastern_ridges_daily_crag_cull.progress: The changed harpies hang listening in the updraft above the crags. Bring down today's toll before the pose spreads.
eastern_ridges_daily_crag_cull.complete: Three more struck through. The living roost held one more day -- that's the whole victory and I've stopped wishing it were bigger. Wishing costs more than salt out here, and buys less. You did clean work. The birds that still fly will roost tonight not knowing what you spared them. That's how mercy usually goes: unwitnessed, and to something that can't thank you. Same time tomorrow. The wind won't wait.

objectives.text: Bring down today's changed harpies

eastern_ridges_daily_crag_cull.note: Safta does the foulest arithmetic there is each dawn: subtract what she loves before it teaches the rest to stop. Three struck through today; the living roost held one more day. Mercy usually goes like this -- unwitnessed, and to something that cannot thank you.

riverfork_the_narrow_places.title: The Narrow Places
riverfork_the_narrow_places.summary: Everything the kingdoms sell crosses the delta at the toll-bridges, and lately the field-gnawers have chewed the pilings soft. Thin them before a span goes down with a grain-cart on it.
riverfork_the_narrow_places.offer: Every plank bridge in the fork pays for itself twice a day -- and rots quicker than a toll-clerk's smile. The gnawers are in the pilings now, gnawing where I can't see. Thin them along the west span before the whole thing folds into the water with a season's grain riding it.
riverfork_the_narrow_places.progress: The field-gnawers work the pilings under the west toll-span. Cut their number before the timber goes.
riverfork_the_narrow_places.complete: That's the west span holding another month, saints willing. You'd think a thing that small couldn't take a bridge. But small things take everything here, given a season and no one watching. Take the coin. It came off an honest toll, which is rarer than you'd think.

objectives.text: Thin the gnawers in the toll-span pilings

riverfork_the_narrow_places.note: The west span held. Vasile counted the gnawn pilings after and stopped counting. Some things you keep patching because the alternative is to stop, and stopping is how a place drowns.

riverfork_tar_and_rope.title: Tar and Wet Rope
riverfork_tar_and_rope.summary: Dragan reforges the delta's nail-hooks by the barrel, and the fork eats iron faster than he can hammer it. Bring him river-bog iron from the low banks and he'll set you up proper.
riverfork_tar_and_rope.offer: You smell that? Tar, wet rope, and hot iron -- that's Riverfork breathing. Trouble is, the salt eats my nail-hooks quicker than a dockman eats his dinner. There's bog-iron in the low banks, good and heavy. Bring me a load and I'll bang you out something that bites.
riverfork_tar_and_rope.progress: River-bog iron lies in the low banks south of the wharf. Gather a good load for Dragan's forge.
riverfork_tar_and_rope.complete: Now that is iron worth the burning. Heavy, hungry stuff -- takes a temper like it's grateful for it. Here, one of my nail-hooks, riverset the old way. It'll hold a plank or a purse or a man's collar, whichever the day calls for.

objectives.text: Gather river-bog iron from the low banks

riverfork_tar_and_rope.note: The forge rang late into the night, and the whole wharf slept easier for the sound. A place is alive as long as someone's still hammering in it.

riverfork_flat_bread.title: Flat Bread, Copper Water
riverfork_flat_bread.summary: Profira's bread has stopped rising, same as it did upriver in Vetka a season back. She wants the old wisdom asked before she starts blaming her flour. Speak with the chronicler who keeps the delta's records.
riverfork_flat_bread.offer: My mother's mother proofed dough on that same hearthstone, and it never once came out flat. Now it's grey and heavy as a toll-slab, and the well by the kitchen's gone the colour of a bad penny. I heard it went just so in Vetka before things got bad. Ask Stelian -- he keeps the records. Ask him has he seen this pattern before, and don't let him soften it for me.
riverfork_flat_bread.progress: Stelian the chronicler keeps Riverfork's ledgers and its longer memory. Ask him about dead yeast and coppered water.
riverfork_flat_bread.complete: So he's seen it. Vetka, the Copper Wells, then here -- each one a little further down the river, in order, like something reading a list from the top. He said count my candles. My grandmother's people said the same, saints keep them. I'll serve the bread flat. I won't meet anyone's eye while I do it.

objectives.text: Ask Stelian about the dead yeast and coppered wells

riverfork_flat_bread.note: Dead yeast, coppered water, moving downriver in order. Profira nailed a hex-sign over the hearth the way her grandmother would have. It changed nothing. It was never meant to. It was meant to be a candle.

riverfork_the_penitents_wade.title: The Penitents Wade
riverfork_the_penitents_wade.summary: Cultists have come down out of the mudflats to kneel in the shallows and press their hands into the delta silt, all facing the same way -- downriver, toward the sea no map shows. Bogdan wants them off the crossings before the pilgrims see.
riverfork_the_penitents_wade.offer: There's mudlarks in the shallows again, on their knees in the cold with their hands pushed down into the silt, all of them turned to face the water going out. Downriver. Toward that grey nothing the dockmen won't run to. They don't fight when you come at them -- that's the worst of it. They just keep listening. Move them off the crossings.
riverfork_the_penitents_wade.progress: The mudlark penitents kneel in the delta shallows, facing downriver. Break their wade before the pilgrim-traffic sees.
riverfork_the_penitents_wade.complete: Cleared, for tonight. You saw how they were kneeling, though. Not praying to anything above. Pressing their hands down, into the silt, like they were trying to feel a heartbeat under the river. And every one of them faced the same way -- the way the water leaves. Toward that coast the ferrymen curse. I don't like a prayer I can't hear the words of.

objectives.text: Break the penitents' wade in the shallows

riverfork_the_penitents_wade.note: They knelt facing the water going out, hands pressed to the silt, listening for something under the river. Bogdan cleared them twice. By the third night he stopped saying they were praying.

riverfork_a_place_at_the_table.title: A Place at the Table
riverfork_a_place_at_the_table.summary: Grigore's brother walked down to the mudflats a week ago to 'have a listen' and hasn't come back for supper. His wife still sets his bowl. Grigore can't bear the empty chair -- go down to the flats and find what's left of him.
riverfork_a_place_at_the_table.offer: My brother Ilie went down to the flats to see what the kneeling folk were about. 'Just having a listen,' he said. That was a week Tuesday. His Dorica sets his bowl every night still, and every night the stew goes cold in it, and I can't watch it another evening. Walk out to the flats. If he's -- if there's anything of him -- I need to know.
riverfork_a_place_at_the_table.progress: Ilie was last seen at the far mudflats, west of the wharf, where the penitents kneel. Reach the flats and look for him.
riverfork_a_place_at_the_table.complete: Kneeling. With the rest of them. Hands in the mud, face to the water, and he didn't turn when you said his name. ...You did right to tell me plain. Dorica'll keep setting the bowl -- I won't take that from her. But I'll stop pretending the stew's for anyone who's coming home.

objectives.label: the far mudflats
objectives.text: Find Ilie on the far mudflats

riverfork_a_place_at_the_table.note: Ilie knelt with the rest, hands in the silt, and did not turn for his own name. His bowl is still set. Dorica knows. She sets it the way you keep a lantern lit in a window -- not because anyone is coming, but because to stop would be to agree.

riverfork_the_toll_that_walks.title: The Toll That Walks
riverfork_the_toll_that_walks.summary: Mircea's cargo is stuck at the east toll-post: the clerk has invented a new fee and won't let a barrel pass without it. Bring him a forged toll-receipt to slip past the racket -- or don't, and know you passed up easy coin.
riverfork_the_toll_that_walks.offer: The east toll-clerk's grown a new appetite -- calls it a 'delta passage surety,' which is a fine long name for robbery. My whole consignment's sitting in the wet while he invents fees. Now. There's a fellow makes receipts that look realer than the real ones. Fetch me one, and my barrels sail free, and the clerk's none the wiser till his ledger won't add up. What do you say?
riverfork_the_toll_that_walks.progress: The forger works the back-wharf shanties. Acquire a toll-receipt forgery for Mircea's stuck cargo.
riverfork_the_toll_that_walks.complete: Look at that work -- stamp, seal, the little smudge where an honest clerk's thumb would go. Beautiful. My barrels are already moving. Here's your cut, and don't lose sleep: the clerk was stealing first. In the fork, the only clean coin is the coin you take back. That's not cynicism, friend. That's the toll.

objectives.text: Obtain a forged toll-receipt from the back-wharf

riverfork_the_toll_that_walks.note: The barrels sailed free on a lie that looked more honest than the truth. Mircea called it the toll. He may be right. It is a poor country where the cleanest coin is the coin you steal back from a thief.

riverfork_blightfly_season.title: Blightfly Season
riverfork_blightfly_season.summary: The blightflies rise off the standing water every delta summer and drive the wharf half-mad, but this year they cluster where the ground runs warm. Burn out the swarms so the children can play the docks again.
riverfork_blightfly_season.offer: Every summer the flies come up off the still water and every summer we curse and swat and live with it -- that's the delta tax. But this year they're thick as smoke over the warm patches by the old dock, and my little ones can't set foot there without coming home welted. Clear the worst of the swarms and there's a jar of my wife's marsh-honey in it for you.
riverfork_blightfly_season.progress: The blightfly swarms cluster over the warm ground by the old dock. Burn them out.
riverfork_blightfly_season.complete: Bless you -- the little ones were down there splashing before the smoke cleared. Marsh-honey, as promised, and a jar of marshlight besides, for walking the banks after dark. ...Odd, though. They only swarmed thick where the mud runs warm to the hand. My father would've said don't stand where the flies won't leave. But my father said a lot of things.

objectives.text: Burn out the blightfly swarms by the old dock

riverfork_blightfly_season.note: The children got their docks back and the wharf smelled of smoke and marsh-honey for a day. The flies clustered only where the ground ran warm. Lupu let the children play there anyway. You cannot raise them afraid of warm mud. You can only wish you had a better world to be brave in.

riverfork_the_drowners_lament.title: The Drowner's Lament
riverfork_the_drowners_lament.summary: Something wears the shape of the drowned in the deep channel and drags the unwary off the low bridges after dark. Vasile has fished out three this month. Go down to the channel and put the drowners under before it's a fourth.
riverfork_the_drowners_lament.offer: I've pulled three out of the deep channel this month -- good waders, careful folk, no reason to fall. And every one of them fell where the water calls up a shape like a drowned man reaching. The drowners, the old dockwives name them. Dead that won't lie in the mud. Put them under, proper, before I'm fishing out a fourth on a hook meant for eels.
riverfork_the_drowners_lament.progress: The drowners rise in the deep channel below the low bridges. Put them under before they take another.
riverfork_the_drowners_lament.complete: Under, then. For a while. You'd have seen their faces -- some of them are folk the wharf still misses, come back wrong and wet. Old Nedelcu's boy is down there among them, or the shape the river makes of him. We don't say so to his mother. Take the reaver. It's bit deeper things than eels.

objectives.text: Put the drowners under in the deep channel

riverfork_the_drowners_lament.note: The drowners wore the faces of the wharf's own missing. Vasile puts them under and does not tell the mothers. Some mercies are only silences kept long enough. The river does not lie in the mud. Nothing here lies still that once went under.

riverfork_where_the_light_arrives_tired.title: Where the Light Arrives Tired
riverfork_where_the_light_arrives_tired.summary: Stelian keeps the delta's records, and a page has gone wrong in them: cargo manifests for the downriver grey piers, stamped and filed, for ferry-runs that never left. He wants you to walk the ferry dock and read the water with alchemical sight before he writes the pattern down.
riverfork_where_the_light_arrives_tired.offer: I keep the fork's ledgers honest, traveller, and my ledger has started lying to me. Here -- manifests for the grey piers downriver, stamped, sealed, filed in good order. Cargo received at a coast our ferry hasn't sailed to in a year. Runs that never left, closing accounts on the far side of a water no living map shows. I daren't write down what that means until someone with the alchemist's eye stands at the dock and tells me what colour the river reads. Will you go and look?
riverfork_where_the_light_arrives_tired.progress: Take the alchemical sight to the ferry dock at the delta's mouth. Read the colour the downriver water gives back.
riverfork_where_the_light_arrives_tired.complete: Orange. Shifting orange, at the waterline, where the Grey Ferry puts out. ...I have read enough to know that colour is not necromancy, and not the deep dark, and not a stone waking. It is the last colour. The live one. The manifests were true -- the runs were made -- only not yet, and not by us. Something downriver is keeping our books a thousand years early. Say nothing of the orange. I will file this where no one curious will find it. Curiosity is how it gets in.

objectives.label: the ferry dock
objectives.text: Reach the ferry dock at the delta's mouth
Report the colour of the water to Stelian

riverfork_where_the_light_arrives_tired.note: The ferry water read shifting orange -- the live colour, the last one. The manifests were true: runs closed a thousand years too early, on a coast no map shows. Stelian filed the page where no curious hand would reach it. What the water does to Riverfork, it does in the order of a man reading down a list.

riverfork_the_ferryman_takes_no_names.title: The Ferryman Takes No Names
riverfork_the_ferryman_takes_no_names.summary: Stelian has found the ferryman's passenger-book, and at the head of the coming season's crossings there is only a smudge -- a name un-written, waiting. He wants it carried sealed and unread to the guard-post before anyone reads it into being.
riverfork_the_ferryman_takes_no_names.offer: One more thing, and then I beg you rest. This is the ferryman's book -- who crosses, when, what fare. Look at the head of the coming season. There should be a name. There is a smudge, warm to the touch, a name not yet written and leaving a space for itself. I will not read it. You must not read it. Carry it sealed to Vasile at the guard-post and let him lock it in the lead-lined chest. The book will offer itself to you on the way. It offers itself to everyone. Deliver it shut, and set it down, and be the one who did not look.
riverfork_the_ferryman_takes_no_names.progress: Carry the ferryman's book, sealed and unread, to Vasile at the guard-post. It will tempt you at every rest. Do not read it.
riverfork_the_ferryman_takes_no_names.complete: Shut. You carried it shut. ...You cannot know what that costs the ones who don't -- I have seen the reading take a man between one breath and the next, no wound, clean hands, a face gone soft with understanding. The smudge at the top of the book is still a smudge. A name still un-written. Vasile has the chest. We have bought the season a little quiet, and paid for it in the one coin the fork still mints freely: the choice not to know. Rest now. You've earned the not-knowing.

objectives.label: the guard-post
objectives.text: Deliver the sealed book to Vasile at the guard-post
Give the sealed ferryman's book to Vasile

riverfork_the_ferryman_takes_no_names.note: The book crossed the wharf shut, and the un-written name at its head stayed un-written. It sits in a lead chest at the guard-post, warm through the metal. Restraint is a coin, and Riverfork spent it well. But a smudge that leaves room for a name means to be filled. The book is not closed. It is patient.

riverfork_the_last_span.title: The Last Span West
riverfork_the_last_span.summary: The road north is the only way still leading to living country, and Bogdan means to keep it open. He asks you to walk the north bridgehead, mark it on the border-map, and set your face upriver toward the Listening Steppe -- away from the coast that reads orange.
riverfork_the_last_span.offer: You've done more for the fork than most who were born here. So let me point you at the one road that still goes somewhere living. North -- over the great span, up out of the delta toward the steppe, where the fog thins and the ground hasn't started listening yet. Walk the north bridgehead. Mark it on the border-map for the ones who come after. And carry the fork's warning upriver, away from that grey water. Downriver is the ferryman's road. Nobody good is hiring for that run.
riverfork_the_last_span.progress: Walk the north bridgehead, mark it on the border-map, and set your face upriver toward the Listening Steppe.
riverfork_the_last_span.complete: There -- marked, and the road north held open one more season. You feel it too, don't you: the fork's got its back to the water and its face to the living country, and it's leaning north the way a candle leans from a draught. Take the map. It's a true one, borders and all. Follow it up to the steppe. Leave the grey ferry for the folk with nothing left to lose. There'll be plenty of those, further down the years.

objectives.label: the north bridgehead
objectives.text: Reach and mark the north bridgehead

riverfork_the_last_span.note: The north road held, and the border-map got one honest mark. Riverfork leans from the grey water toward living country, the way the last warm thing leans from the cold. The road north goes to the steppe, and past it the spine of the world. Downriver goes somewhere too. No one who read the manifests will say where.

riverfork_honest_weights.title: Honest Weights
riverfork_honest_weights.summary: A rival trader has been shaving the delta's weigh-stones to cheat the grain-tally, and Mircea wants proof, not a fight. Gather the doctored weights off the field-gnawers' burrows where the cheat has been hiding them.
riverfork_honest_weights.offer: Somebody's been trimming the weigh-stones on the grain-dock -- a hair off each, so a full sack tallies light and the difference walks into somebody's private purse. I know who. What I lack is proof. He's been stashing the shaved stones down the gnawer-burrows on the south bank, clever fool, where no honest hand would dig. Bring me the doctored weights and I'll hang him with his own arithmetic.
riverfork_honest_weights.progress: The shaved weigh-stones are hidden in gnawer-burrows on the south bank. Recover them for Mircea.
riverfork_honest_weights.complete: Every one of them shaved to the same false weight -- that's not a slip, that's a system. He'll answer for it to the toll-court, and the grain-tally comes honest again. For a while. Somebody always finds the next hair to shave. That's trade in the fork: it's not that men are wicked, it's that the wickedness pays so patiently. Here -- an honest cut of an honest recovery.

objectives.text: Clear the gnawer-burrows hiding the shaved stones
Recover the doctored weigh-stones

riverfork_honest_weights.note: The shaved stones hanged their maker in the toll-court, and the grain came honest again. For a while. Mircea does not believe in the while. He believes in the next hair, and the man patient enough to shave it.

riverfork_the_bowl_still_set.title: The Bowl Still Set
riverfork_the_bowl_still_set.summary: Dorica, whose husband knelt down in the flats, has stopped eating and started kneeling by the water at dusk herself. Her neighbour Lupu is afraid she means to follow Ilie in. Bring her the folk-warding the old women swear keeps the listening out.
riverfork_the_bowl_still_set.offer: It's Dorica -- Ilie's wife. Since he knelt down in the mud she's gone thin as a reed, and now she goes to the bank at dusk and stands looking out at the water the way he used to. I'm frightened, plainly. The old women say a folk-warding kept close to the heart stops your ears against the listening. I'd fetch it myself but I can't leave my little ones. Take it to her. Sit with her a while. Just -- don't let her be alone facing that water.
riverfork_the_bowl_still_set.progress: Carry the folk-warding to Dorica and sit with her at the dusk-bank. Speak with Lupu once she is safe indoors.
riverfork_the_bowl_still_set.complete: She took it. Held it to her chest and wept, which is more than she's done in a fortnight, and let you walk her home before the light went. It won't bring Ilie back from the mud. Nothing will. But she ate tonight, and she slept indoors, and tomorrow she'll do it again, and that's how you carry a person over the worst of it -- one supper, one indoor night, one dusk she didn't spend facing the water. Bless you for the sitting.

objectives.text: Bring the folk-warding to Dorica, then return to Lupu

riverfork_the_bowl_still_set.note: Dorica ate, and slept indoors, and did not go to the water. A folk-warding stops no listening; the sitting did that, and the walking-home. Grief is a current too. You do not fight it. You keep someone's head above it one dusk at a time, and you do not let them face the water alone.

riverfork_barge_wardens_watch.title: The Barge-Warden's Watch
riverfork_barge_wardens_watch.summary: The night barges must not run aground on the drowners' channel, and Vasile is one warden short. Keep the dusk watch at the low bridge and turn back what tries to climb the pilings.
riverfork_barge_wardens_watch.offer: Short a warden again tonight, and the barges run late and blind. Take the low-bridge watch for me -- stand where the lantern-post leans, and when the drowners try the pilings, and they will, put them back in the water. It's a long dull cold nothing of a job right up until it isn't. Those are the ones worth doing.
riverfork_barge_wardens_watch.progress: Keep the dusk watch at the low bridge. Turn back the drowners that climb the pilings.
riverfork_barge_wardens_watch.complete: Barges through, and dry, and no one the poorer. Two came up the pilings tonight -- did you mark how they climbed toward the lantern, not away? Everything in the fork's turned toward a light lately, or a sound, or that grey water. Everything but us. We turn toward the barges. Somebody has to. Same time tomorrow, if your knees'll take it.

objectives.label: the low bridge watch
objectives.text: Take up the dusk watch at the low bridge
Turn back the drowners on the pilings

riverfork_barge_wardens_watch.note: The barges got through dry. The drowners climbed toward the lantern, not away. Everything in the fork leans toward a light now. The wardens lean toward the barges instead. It is a small stubbornness. It is the only kind Riverfork has left.

riverfork_the_forgers_conscience.title: The Forger's Conscience
riverfork_the_forgers_conscience.summary: The back-wharf forger who sells false receipts has gone quiet and afraid, and left word he wants out. Grigore asks you to hear him -- there's a name in the man's ledger that shouldn't be there, and he's terrified of who will collect it.
riverfork_the_forgers_conscience.offer: You know the forger, down the back-wharf shanties? He's sold me a receipt or two, no shame in it, everyone here shaves the toll. But he's gone strange. Sent a boy to say he wants out, wants to burn his stamps, wants to talk to someone who isn't buying. There's a name in his forgery-book he never wrote, he says, and it scares him worse than the toll-court ever could. Go and hear the man. He's earned a listener.
riverfork_the_forgers_conscience.progress: Speak with the back-wharf forger who wants out. Hear the name in his book, then return to Grigore.
riverfork_the_forgers_conscience.complete: A name he never wrote, appearing on the blank facing page, a little clearer each morning -- copying itself, he swears, spreading the way ink doesn't. He's burning the whole book tonight and taking the ferry-money he saved and going north, up out of the delta, fast as the barges run. ...I told him to go. Whatever's writing itself in a forger's book, I'd rather he outran it than we learned to read it. Some things you don't want translated.

objectives.label: the back-wharf shanties
objectives.text: Reach the forger in the back-wharf shanties
Tell Grigore what the forger showed you

riverfork_the_forgers_conscience.note: A name wrote itself onto the blank page of a forger's book, clearer each morning, spreading like ink never does. He burned the book and ran north. Grigore told him to. Whatever copies itself into paper unasked, better outrun than read. The forger is gone. The blank pages, wherever paper is kept, are not.

riverfork_marsh_honey.title: Marsh-Honey and Warm Bread
riverfork_marsh_honey.summary: If the bread must come out flat, Profira means to make the wharf love it anyway -- with marsh-honey and river-fish for the crossing-eve supper. Gather the makings so Riverfork can have one good loud warm night.
riverfork_marsh_honey.offer: The bread comes out flat, fine, so I'll drown it in marsh-honey and folk'll thank me for it. Crossing-eve's coming -- the night the season's last barges put north -- and I want this taproom loud and warm and full, flat bread and all. Bring me a good store of the low-bank makings and I'll cook the fork a supper it'll talk about all winter. Everyone deserves one good night before the cold.
riverfork_marsh_honey.progress: Gather the low-bank makings for Profira's crossing-eve supper.
riverfork_marsh_honey.complete: Now that's a larder. The taproom'll be packed rail to rail, and for one night nobody in the fork will strain to hear what's out on the water -- they'll be too busy singing over flat bread and honey. That's the whole trick of an inn, you know. Not to keep the dark out. You can't. Just to make one room too warm and loud for it to matter. Here -- a striker, so you always carry a little fire.

objectives.text: Gather marsh-honey and river makings for the supper

riverfork_marsh_honey.note: The taproom ran loud and warm past midnight, and for one crossing-eve nobody strained to hear the water. An inn does not keep the dark out. It makes one room too bright for the dark to matter. That is enough. That is, some nights, everything.

riverfork_the_even_pilings.title: The Even Pilings
riverfork_the_even_pilings.summary: Dragan drove new pilings to shore up a sagging span, and by morning the gnawers had chewed them -- but chewed them even, spaced like fence-posts, like a thing measuring. He wants them cleared and the pattern seen before he tells anyone.
riverfork_the_even_pilings.offer: I set fresh pilings under the mill-span yesterday, good bog-iron-banded oak, meant to last ten years. Come dawn the gnawers had been at them. Fine -- gnawers gnaw. But look. Look how they chewed them. Even. Spaced to a hand's width, every notch the same, straight down the row like posts of a fence somebody's planning. Vermin don't measure. Clear them off, and tell me I'm an old fool seeing patterns in tooth-marks.
riverfork_the_even_pilings.progress: Clear the gnawers from the mill-span pilings and see how they have chewed them.
riverfork_the_even_pilings.complete: You saw it too. Even notches, spaced like a surveyor's marks, straight down the row -- and the mud at the foot of each piling lying in little parallel lines, fine as combed hair. That's not hunger. Hunger's messy. This was neat. Something's teaching the vermin to measure my bridge, and I don't want to know for what. Take the riverplate. Old wardsmith work. It's turned worse than teeth.

objectives.text: Clear the gnawers from the mill-span and read the notches

riverfork_the_even_pilings.note: The gnawers had chewed the pilings even, spaced like a fence a surveyor planned, the mud beneath lying in fine parallel lines. Vermin do not measure. Dragan cleared them and asked no more. In the fork you learn quickly: the tooth-marks that frighten you are the ones that come out straight.

riverfork_downriver_rumor.title: What the Dockmen Won't Say
riverfork_downriver_rumor.summary: A downriver debt-broker has offered Mircea a fortune to run cargo to the grey piers -- fares paid a year in advance, in coin already cold. Mircea wants the offer weighed before he takes it. Ask the guard and the chronicler what waits downriver.
riverfork_downriver_rumor.offer: I'll be honest, because you've been square with me. A broker downriver -- grey piers, the drowned end -- wants me running cargo to the coast. Pays a year ahead, in coin, and the coin comes cold out of the purse, cold as pond-ice in high summer. It's more money than the fork sees in a decade. And every dockman I ask goes quiet and finds somewhere else to look. Ask Bogdan at the post, ask Stelian in his ledgers. Then tell me if a man should take cold coin to a grey coast.
riverfork_downriver_rumor.progress: Ask Bogdan and Stelian what waits at the grey piers downriver, then bring your reckoning to Mircea.
riverfork_downriver_rumor.complete: Cold coin, a year ahead, from a coast that closes accounts before they're opened. ...No. You're right. No. I've made my money shaving tolls and cheating cheats, and I'll answer for that, but I'll not run a barge to a place that pays for the trip before you've decided to take it. That's not a wage. That's a debt with your name already in it. Keep the receipt-craft. I'm done with clever paper. I want to owe nobody downriver.

objectives.text: Ask Bogdan about the grey piers downriver
Ask Stelian about the cold coin and the coast

riverfork_downriver_rumor.note: Cold coin, paid a year ahead, from a coast that closes accounts before they open. Mircea refused it, and slept badly for the refusing, and worse for how nearly he had said yes. A wage you are paid before you agree is not a wage. Someone downriver already has his name. He would rather not know for what.

riverfork_the_pilgrim_road.title: The Pilgrim Road Runs Out
riverfork_the_pilgrim_road.summary: The old pilgrim road that once led west to Angel Wings now just stops at the delta water, and pilgrims arrive still walking, unable to say why they came or where they meant to go. Grigore asks you to turn them back before they wade in after the penitents.
riverfork_the_pilgrim_road.offer: The pilgrim road used to run clear west to Angel Wings, to Maren's house and the granaries. Now it just -- stops, at our water, and the pilgrims keep coming down it and stand at the edge like they've forgotten the road ended. Ask them where they're bound and they can't say. Just that they were walking, and something wanted them further on. Go up the road and turn them back before they follow the mudlarks in. Point them home. Speak plain -- sometimes plain words wake a body up.
riverfork_the_pilgrim_road.progress: Walk the pilgrim road to the delta's edge and turn the wandering pilgrims back toward living country.
riverfork_the_pilgrim_road.complete: Some of them woke when you spoke and thanked you and went home shaking. Some just looked at you, and past you, at the water, and you couldn't reach them, and you had to leave them standing there because you can't carry a grown soul home on your back. ...You did what could be done. That's the fork's whole prayer these days. Do what can be done, walk home the ones who'll come, and don't count too loud the ones who won't.

objectives.label: the pilgrim road's end
objectives.text: Turn the pilgrims back at the road's end

riverfork_the_pilgrim_road.note: The pilgrim road runs west no longer; it stops at the water, and pilgrims arrive still walking, unable to say why. Some were turned home. Some could not be reached, and stand there yet. You carry home the ones who'll come. You do not count too loud the ones who won't. Counting is how it starts.

riverfork_eel_traps.title: The Eel-Traps
riverfork_eel_traps.summary: Lupu runs eel-traps in the reed-channels, best eating in the delta, but the blightflies foul the lines. Clear the swarms off the reed-channels so a family can make its living and its supper.
riverfork_eel_traps.offer: Eel's the best eating in the fork and my traps are the best-set, if I say it, but the blightflies come up thick in the reed-channels and foul the lines something terrible. Clear the worst of the swarms so I can run my traps, and I'll send you home with a string of eel and a jar of the good marshlight. Honest work, honest eels. Some days that's the most a man can ask.
riverfork_eel_traps.progress: Clear the blightfly swarms fouling the eel-trap lines in the reed-channels.
riverfork_eel_traps.complete: Traps clear and running -- I'll have eel enough for the whole lane tonight. There, a jar of marshlight for your trouble; light it low on the bank and the flies leave you be. It's a small life, the eel-trapping. But it's mine, and the reed-channels are mine, and long as I can keep the flies off and the traps set, the fork's still a place a body can make a supper in. That's not nothing. That's the whole of it, some years.

objectives.text: Clear the blightflies off the reed-channel traps

riverfork_eel_traps.note: The eel-traps ran clear and the lane ate well. A small life, honestly made, on channels a man can call his own. It is not nothing. In a delta the world is quietly digesting, a supper you made yourself is near enough to everything.

riverfork_the_kneeling_ground.title: The Kneeling Ground
riverfork_the_kneeling_ground.summary: The penitents keep returning to one patch of mudflat no matter how often they're cleared -- and where they kneel, the ground has gone warm and the reeds have died in a perfect circle. Bogdan wants the ground itself walked and read, not just the kneelers driven off.
riverfork_the_kneeling_ground.offer: We drive the mudlarks off and they come back -- always to the one patch, the same handspan of flat, like a dog to a buried thing. I went out at noon to look at it plain. The mud's warm there. Warm to the wrist, in cold weather, and the reeds have died back in a ring around it neat as a drawn compass. Driving off the kneelers does nothing; it's the ground that calls them. Go and stand on it. Read it, if you've the eye. Tell me what's cooking under my delta.
riverfork_the_kneeling_ground.progress: Break the penitents at the warm patch, then stand on the kneeling ground and read what lies beneath.
riverfork_the_kneeling_ground.complete: Warm ground, dead reeds in a perfect ring, and the dust at the centre lying in fine parallel lines pointing downriver -- toward the grey water, always the grey water. That's the last of the old signs, the one the Vetka wisdom lists dead last: the ground goes warm, and then folk listen. It's here now, at the bottom of the river, at the end of the west. There's nowhere downstream left for it to move to but off the map. Take the counteragent. Keep it close. Don't kneel, whatever the ground promises. Whatever it says it'll ease.

objectives.text: Break the penitents at the warm patch

objectives.label: the kneeling ground
objectives.text: Stand on the kneeling ground and read it

riverfork_the_kneeling_ground.note: Warm ground, dead reeds in a perfect ring, dust aligned and pointing downriver. The last symptom on the old woman's list, arrived at the end of the west, at the bottom of the river. There is nowhere downstream left for it to spread but off the edge of every living map. It is not spreading further. It has arrived.

riverfork_a_true_border.title: A True Border
riverfork_a_true_border.summary: Stelian is drawing the first honest map of the delta in a generation, and the toll-lords have every reason to want it drawn wrong. He needs the border-stones walked and their true positions marked before the powers redraw the fork to suit their purses.
riverfork_a_true_border.offer: I am drawing a true map of the fork -- the first honest one since my grandfather's -- and you would not believe how many powerful men would prefer it drawn crooked. A border a foot to the east is a toll-post's fortune. The old boundary-stones still stand where they were set, if a body walks out and finds them. Walk the delta bounds. Mark the stones as they truly lie, not as some lord's clerk swears they do. Bring me a true border, and the fork keeps one honest thing.
riverfork_a_true_border.progress: Walk the delta bounds and mark the true positions of the old border-stones for Stelian's map.
riverfork_a_true_border.complete: True to the inch. Now I've one map in the fork that answers to the ground and not to a purse. It's a small honesty. But a place that can't say truly where its own edges are can be moved, quietly, a foot at a time, until it's somewhere else entirely -- and I'll not have Riverfork moved without its knowing. Take the map-craft. And traveller -- when you go north, carry a true map. Down here, only the ground can be trusted, and lately not even that.

objectives.label: the eastern border-stones
objectives.text: Mark the true border-stones for the map

riverfork_a_true_border.note: Stelian has one map that answers to the ground and not a purse. A small honesty. But a place that cannot say where its own edges lie can be moved a foot at a time until it is somewhere else. He will not have the fork moved without its knowing. The ground can be trusted. Lately, not even that.

riverfork_the_iron_that_bites.title: The Iron That Bites
riverfork_the_iron_that_bites.summary: Dragan means to arm the wardens against the drowners with river-set steel, but the deep channel eats an ordinary edge to rust in a week. Bring him drowner-silt and bog-iron to forge blades that hold against wet dead things.
riverfork_the_iron_that_bites.offer: The wardens are fighting drowners with plain steel and the deep channel rusts a blade to nothing in a week -- you may as well hit the dead with a wet loaf. I can forge better. River-set steel, quenched in drowner-silt off the channel bottom, banded with the heavy bog-iron. It bites the wet dead and it holds. Bring me the silt and the iron. Bring the wardens something that doesn't quit halfway through the fight.
riverfork_the_iron_that_bites.progress: Gather drowner-silt from the deep channel and bog-iron from the low banks for Dragan's river-set steel.
riverfork_the_iron_that_bites.complete: Feel the heft of that. River-set, drowner-quenched, bog-banded -- it'll take a drowner's head clean and come up dry. I'll arm the whole watch with these before crossing-eve. It's a poor trade, forging better ways to put down folk who were our neighbours last spring. But if they're coming up wet and wrong out of my channel, I'd rather Vasile met them with an edge that holds. Take a blade. You've earned steel that doesn't quit.

objectives.text: Gather drowner-silt for the quench
Gather bog-iron for the banding

riverfork_the_iron_that_bites.note: Dragan armed the watch with river-set steel that bites the wet dead and comes up dry. It is a poor trade, forging better ways to put down last spring's neighbours. He forges them anyway. When the dead come up wrong out of your own channel, mercy is an edge that does not quit halfway through the work.

riverfork_the_last_warm_run.title: The Last Warm Run
riverfork_the_last_warm_run.summary: Profira sends a warm meal down to the wardens on the night crossings -- flat bread, honey, and hot broth against the delta cold. Carry the supper-basket the length of the wharf before the barges put north.
riverfork_the_last_warm_run.offer: The wardens stand out on the cold water all night keeping the crossings safe, and the least I can do is see they stand it warm. Here -- flat bread, marsh-honey, a crock of broth still steaming. Carry the basket down the wharf to the crossing-post before the barges put north. A warm supper's a small thing. But a body can stand a great deal of cold and dark on a full warm belly, and precious little on an empty one. Off you go, and mind the honey doesn't tip.
riverfork_the_last_warm_run.progress: Carry Profira's warm supper-basket down the wharf to the wardens at the crossing-post.
riverfork_the_last_warm_run.complete: Delivered warm, and I'll wager you got a cheer for it. Good. Let them stand their watch on hot broth and honey; let the fork feed its own a little longer. That's the whole of what an innkeeper's for, at the end of the west -- to make sure that whatever's coming down the years, the folk who face it face it fed. Here's a warm bowl for you too. Sit. You look like a body that's been standing in the cold.

objectives.label: the crossing-post
objectives.text: Carry the supper-basket to the wardens

riverfork_the_last_warm_run.note: The wardens stood their watch on hot broth and honey, and cheered the basket down the wharf. An innkeeper at the end of the west has one task above all others: to see that whatever comes down the years, the folk who face it face it fed. Everything good is temporary. That is what makes a warm supper worth carrying.

riverfork_names_in_the_silt.title: Names in the Silt
riverfork_names_in_the_silt.summary: The drowners the wardens put down keep washing up clutching debt-tablets stamped in a script no living clerk can read -- their own names, Stelian thinks, closed and filed from downriver. He wants the tablets gathered and warded, not read, before the wharf learns to translate them.
riverfork_names_in_the_silt.offer: The drowners wash up holding things now. Little tablets, clay and something colder, stamped with a script that isn't ours and isn't Vetkan and isn't any hand a living clerk was taught. I've held one to the lamp too long already -- it pulls the eye, the way a hook pulls a lip. I think they're names. Closed accounts. Filed from that coast downriver, a thousand years early, and handed to the dead to carry up to us. Gather the tablets off the drowners. Do not read them. Bring them sealed, and we'll ward them, and no one on this wharf will learn what they say.
riverfork_names_in_the_silt.progress: Gather the debt-tablets off the drowners, sealed and unread, and bring them to Stelian to be warded.
riverfork_names_in_the_silt.complete: Sealed, all of them, and you didn't read them -- I can see it in your face, which is still your own. Good. We'll pack them in the lead chest with the ferryman's book and let them stay un-read till the fork itself is under water, which by the manifests is not so long. They are names, I'm sure of it now. Ours, some of them. Closed early, filed downriver, sent back up on the tongues of our own drowned. To read one is to agree to it. So we don't read. That's the whole of the wisdom. That's all the wisdom there is.

objectives.text: Put down the tablet-bearing drowners
Gather the debt-tablets, sealed and unread

riverfork_names_in_the_silt.note: The drowners washed up clutching debt-tablets stamped in no living hand -- names, Stelian is sure, closed early and filed downriver and handed back to the drowned to carry up. They lie sealed in the lead chest, unread. To read one is to agree to it. That is all the wisdom there is. Downriver, the fork's accounts are already closing.

riverfork_the_clerks_ledger.title: A Clerk's Honest Grief
riverfork_the_clerks_ledger.summary: The east toll-clerk has been found dead at his post with no mark on him and his ledger open -- and Vasile, who never liked the man, finds himself oddly grieved. Recover the ledger before a thief does, and learn what the honest column cost.
riverfork_the_clerks_ledger.offer: Now here's a thing I never thought to be sorry over. The east toll-clerk -- the fee-inventing weasel himself -- found dead at his post this dawn, no wound, hands folded, ledger open in front of him like he'd just balanced it. And I'm grieved. Me. I'd have gladly drowned the man in his own coin-box a week ago. Fetch me his ledger before some dockside magpie lifts it. Whatever killed a clerk that neat, I'd sooner it was written down than walking about.
riverfork_the_clerks_ledger.progress: Recover the toll-clerk's ledger from his post at the east crossing before a thief takes it.
riverfork_the_clerks_ledger.complete: Balanced to the last penny -- every shaved toll, every invented fee, all of it squared and confessed in his own hand, like he'd sat down at the end and made it honest before he... stopped. No mark on him. Clean hands. Face gone soft, near peaceful. I've seen that look once before, on a corpse in the Chamber they still talk about. The man cheated everyone in the fork for twenty years and died the moment his books came true. There's a lesson in that. I'll be cursed if I know which one. Take his coin. He's done counting it.

objectives.label: the east toll-post
objectives.text: Recover the clerk's ledger from his post

riverfork_the_clerks_ledger.note: The toll-clerk died the moment his crooked books came honest: no wound, clean hands, a face soft with understanding. Vasile grieved a man he'd have drowned a week before, and took the coin, for the clerk was done counting it. Even a cheat's ledger, read to its true bottom, is the last thing a man understands.

bloodroad_krunn_first_ledger.title: The Toll That Went North
bloodroad_krunn_first_ledger.summary: The war-caravans roll north out of the forge in numbers no honest war would need. Lazar keeps a private tally at Toll-Fort Krunn and wants a second pair of eyes on the ditch-road before the count stops making sense.
bloodroad_krunn_first_ledger.offer: Every convoy pays its toll and I write it down, same as my father did. But the ledger's gone strange, friend. More axeheads north this season than there are hands south to swing them. Walk the ditch-road to the counting-post and tell me what you see rolling. I'll square the numbers against yours.
bloodroad_krunn_first_ledger.progress: The counting-post sits where the ditch-road bends below the fort. Watch the convoys, then bring Lazar what your eyes made of them.
bloodroad_krunn_first_ledger.complete: Same as mine, then. He's arming for everyone at once -- which means he's arming for no war anybody's declared. Keep that between us. In Krunn, a number that doesn't add up is a thing men get buried for asking about.

objectives.label: the counting-post
objectives.text: Watch the convoys from the counting-post
Bring the count back to Lazar

bloodroad_krunn_first_ledger.note: The steel goes north faster than any war could spend it. Lazar squared the numbers twice and liked them less the second time. A caravan road that arms against everyone arms against nothing yet.

bloodroad_krunn_second_ledger.title: Whose Banner the Axes Answer
bloodroad_krunn_second_ledger.summary: The convoys carry more than iron. Lazar has heard of manifests hidden in the load-beds, sealed against the toll-clerks. Recover a set from the Ember-Sworn who guard the night runs, and learn which kingdom the forge is truly counting toward.
bloodroad_krunn_second_ledger.offer: The real ledger doesn't ride with me.
