the_gift_rim_watch_daily.note: You cannot unwrite a name off the rim ledger; the body walks its labor-days regardless. Ruxandra walks the rim each shift anyway. In the south everything comes due -- the whole religion in three flat notes.

the_gift_widows_walk.title: The Widow Who Still Weeps
the_gift_widows_walk.summary: Lenuta's mother was a Mourner of the old kind -- she wept the harvest home so the dead rested quiet. Now the changed Mourners walk and Lenuta fears her mother is among them. She asks the player to walk the deep rows and find whether the old woman still grieves like herself.
the_gift_widows_walk.offer: My mother mourned the Gift the right way, the old way -- walked the rows at reaping and truly wept, because a body ought to be grieved even when you're going to eat off it. She went out three nights back and hasn't come home, and now the changed ones walk humming that flat awful tune. I have to know. Walk the deep rows. If she's still weeping her own tears, bring her home. If she's keeping the ground's time now... then at least I'll know, and I can grieve her the right way myself.
the_gift_widows_walk.progress: The deep reaping-rows lie south of the abundant furrow. Find Lenuta's mother among the Mourners.
the_gift_widows_walk.complete: You found her weeping. Truly weeping, her own grief, not the ground's -- she'd only lost her way in the dark and the sorrow. She's home, and shaking, and herself. Thank you doesn't cover it. Most of what the Gift takes it keeps. You brought one back before it was kept. Take my mother's warding-charm; she pressed it on me and now I press it on you. Wear it in the deep rows. Not everyone gets walked home.

objectives.label: the deep reaping-rows
objectives.text: Search the deep rows for Lenuta's mother
Bring word of her mother to Lenuta

the_gift_widows_walk.note: Most of what the Gift takes, it keeps. Lenuta's mother was still weeping her own grief, and came home shaking and herself. One walked back before the ground kept her. Not everyone gets walked home.

the_gift_hammers_still.title: The Count of Three
the_gift_hammers_still.summary: Each day the forge-hammers stop at once, for a count of three, and resume -- and no one on the killing floors will meet a stranger's eye about it. Stanca, posted at the works, wants the player to stand the floor at the stilling and mark what moves in the silence.
the_gift_hammers_still.offer: You'll hear it if you stand the floor long enough. Hundreds of hammers, day and night, and then -- nothing. All at once. A count of three, dead quiet, and then they pick back up like it never happened. Nobody on the floor will look at you about it. I've stood this post two years and I still can't tell if it's a signal or the ground swallowing. Go stand the floor at the still. Watch what moves in the three counts. Then tell me I'm not mad. Or tell me I am. Either's a mercy.
the_gift_hammers_still.progress: The killing floors run east of the vents. Stand the floor and mark what moves during the count of three.
the_gift_hammers_still.complete: You saw it too. Every head on the floor turns the same way in the quiet -- toward the Pit, one beat, then back to the hammers like nothing. They don't know they do it. I asked a floor-man once and he wept and couldn't say why. Three counts, every day, the whole south nodding at the same thing. I don't want to know what it's nodding to. Take the charm. It's meant to keep you incurious. Best thing you can be, here.

objectives.label: the killing floors
objectives.text: Stand the floor through the count of three
Tell Stanca what moved in the silence

the_gift_hammers_still.note: When the hammers still for their count of three, every head on the floor turns toward the Pit and back, and none who do it can say why. The whole south nods at one thing. Stanca would rather not know what.

the_gift_anaras_page.title: A Page for the Last Reader
the_gift_anaras_page.summary: The changed Mourners' three notes match marks Ecaterina has found carved on the Pit's old thresholds. She dares not copy them -- copying is how the sickness spreads -- but the last living reader of the Underlanguage could translate the cycle into a weapon of conscience. First, the marks must be read by the eye alone, not written.
the_gift_anaras_page.offer: I told you the Mourners keep the ground's time. I've since found the same three-beat cut into the old Pit thresholds -- the marks the reaping-voice is singing. There is one living soul who could read them and turn this whole obscene cycle into something that shames the men who run it. But I will not copy the marks. Copying is the sickness; that is how it walks. Take a reading-glass instead -- look, do not write -- and carry only what your eye holds to me. Restraint is the whole art of surviving the south.
the_gift_anaras_page.progress: The old marks are cut into the Pit thresholds at the rim. Read them by glass -- do not copy them -- and bring what your eye holds to Ecaterina.
the_gift_anaras_page.complete: You looked and did not write. Good. That discipline is the only thing between us and becoming a Mourner ourselves. What you carry in your eye I'll get to Anara -- the last who reads this truly -- and she may forge a conscience out of it, or get us all collected for the trying. Politically it is a lit fuse. Morally it is the one honest thing left to do. Take the veil. It thins what the marks do to a looking mind. Wear it when you look at true things.

objectives.label: the Pit thresholds
objectives.text: Read the threshold marks by glass -- do not copy
Carry what your eye holds to Ecaterina

the_gift_anaras_page.note: The reaping-voice sings marks cut in the Pit's thresholds, and reading them shifts orange in the glass. Ecaterina looked and did not copy. Restraint is the whole art of surviving the south. Anara will get the rest.

the_gift_glass_razor_trade.title: Glass for the Gentle Cut
the_gift_glass_razor_trade.summary: Blackglass razors fetch a fine price up the trade road -- the surgeons of the north pay for an edge that parts skin without dragging. Rada wants raw glass off the vents to fill a standing order, and asks the player to gather it before a rival factor does.
the_gift_glass_razor_trade.offer: There's coin in glass if you're quick. The northern cutters -- barbers, surgeons, the ones who mend what the forges break -- pay dear for a blackglass razor, an edge so fine it parts skin before the skin knows. I've a standing order and a rival factor sniffing the same vents. Beat him to it: raw glass off the beds, and I'll cut you in on the northern price. It's clean trade, this one -- no bodies in it, only stone. Rare enough here to be worth saying out loud.
the_gift_glass_razor_trade.progress: The raw blackglass lies in the vent beds at the Gift's southern edge. Gather eight pieces before the rival factor.
the_gift_glass_razor_trade.complete: Eight, and good fracture on every one -- the cutters will weep for joy and pay through the nose. There. A trade with no body in it. Do you know how rare that is down here? I could count the clean deals I've made this year on one hand and have fingers spare. Take your cut, and take this reading-lens as a bonus -- it prices a gem true. In the south, knowing what a thing's worth is worth more than the thing.

objectives.text: Gather raw blackglass before the rival factor

the_gift_glass_razor_trade.note: A trade with no body in it -- rare enough in the south that Rada said it out loud. Eight clean pieces of glass, sold north to gentle hands. She counts her clean deals on one hand, with fingers spare.

the_gift_seed_of_the_pale.title: Seed That Remembers
the_gift_seed_of_the_pale.summary: Lenuta keeps a small plot she refuses to feed with fresh red -- barley grown honest, in soil she carted from beyond the Gift. It comes up thin and grey, but it comes up clean. She asks the player to guard the plot from the imps a single season, to prove it can be done.
the_gift_seed_of_the_pale.offer: Everyone says you can't farm the south without feeding the ground bodies. I mean to prove them wrong, or die stubborn. I carted clean soil in from beyond the skirts, dear as it was, and I'll grow barley in it that never ate a soul. It comes up grey and thin and half of nothing -- but it's clean, and it's mine, and no child's shoe will ever come up in my row. Keep the imps off it one season while it takes. Just one honest harvest. I want to know it's possible before I die not knowing.
the_gift_seed_of_the_pale.progress: Lenuta's honest plot sits apart, west of the main rows. Keep the brazier-imps off it while the clean seed takes.
the_gift_seed_of_the_pale.complete: It took. Grey and thin and hardly a loaf in the whole of it -- and clean, every grain of it clean, grown from nothing but rain and stubbornness. I know now it's possible. It's just poor. That's why nobody does it; the Gift is generous and honest is hungry, and hungry people take the generous ground. But I know. And now you know. Take the keepsake. Bind it to something you mean to keep honest. It's not much. Neither's my harvest. Both are mine.

objectives.text: Keep the imps off Lenuta's honest plot

the_gift_seed_of_the_pale.note: Lenuta grew one honest harvest -- grey, thin, and clean, from rain and stubbornness. It proved honest farming possible in the Gift, and poor. The generous ground eats bodies. The hungry take the generous ground.

the_gift_rusted_axe.title: The War-Axe in the Row
the_gift_rusted_axe.summary: A great war-axe of the Old War has surfaced in a plowed row, rusted into the red soil that its own dead made fertile. Stanca wants it recovered before a farmer breaks a share on it -- and before it becomes a shrine to a battle the Gift is still eating.
the_gift_rusted_axe.offer: A plow struck iron in the east rows this morning -- turned up a war-axe, old as the Great War itself, rusted right into the red. Fitting, in a grim way: the soil it's stuck in is made of the men that axe likely killed. Get it out before some farmer snaps a share on it, or worse, before folk start leaving offerings at it like it's holy. It's not holy. It's a murder weapon in a graveyard that grows bread. Haul it to me and we'll melt it into something that doesn't remember.
the_gift_rusted_axe.progress: The rusted war-axe lies in the plowed east rows. Reach it and haul it clear before offerings gather.
the_gift_rusted_axe.complete: Heavy old thing. Rusted into the very ground its killing made rich -- there's the whole south in one image, if you want it. We'll take it to Voica and she'll unmake it into scythes. Better it cut bread than remember cutting men. Already there were three flowers laid at it when I passed. Folk here will worship anything that fed them, and never once ask what it was fed. Take the reaver's edge. It remembers being a weapon. At least it's honest about it.

objectives.label: the plowed east rows
objectives.text: Recover the rusted war-axe from the row
Haul the axe to Stanca

the_gift_rusted_axe.note: A war-axe surfaced rusted into the soil its own killing made fertile -- the whole south in one image. Voica will beat it into scythes. Better it cut bread than remember cutting men. Three flowers already lay at it.

the_gift_apothecary_quiet.title: A Draught Against the Listening
the_gift_apothecary_quiet.summary: A regular at Sanda's inn has gone quiet-eyed and stopped eating, saying food dulls his hearing of something in the rows. Sanda knows the sign -- the warm ground got into him. She sends the player for the vent-herbs an old counteragent needs, before he stops eating for good.
the_gift_apothecary_quiet.offer: Borek's a good customer gone wrong before my eyes. Won't touch a bowl -- says eating muffles what he's trying to hear out in the rows. I've seen it before and it ends one way: they stop eating, they start walking to the warm ground, they don't come back for supper and their family sets a place anyway. There's an old draught for the early stage, before it sets. I need vent-herbs for it, the kind that only grow where the imps den. Fetch me a handful. I'll not lose a man over a soup he won't eat.
the_gift_apothecary_quiet.progress: The vent-herbs grow among the brazier-imp dens south of the works. Gather them so the draught can be brewed.
the_gift_apothecary_quiet.complete: That's the herb. I'll steep it strong and get it into him warm, whether he wants it or not -- a body listening to the ground has forgotten it's a body, and you remind it the plain way, with bread and a fire and someone sitting with you. He ate half a bowl tonight. Half's a victory here. Take the poultice; there's more of the same herb in it. If you ever go quiet-eyed on a warm row, you drink it and you come home. Promise me you'll come home.

objectives.text: Gather vent-herbs from the imp dens

the_gift_apothecary_quiet.note: Borek stopped eating to hear the rows better -- the warm ground in him, the plainest way it takes a man. Sanda got half a bowl into him. Half is a victory here. You remind a body it's a body with bread and a fire.

the_gift_marked_border.title: Where the Gift Ends
the_gift_marked_border.summary: The warm ground is spreading, and last year's boundary stones no longer mark where the good soil stops. Ruxandra wants the true edge of the Gift walked and set down on a map, before farmers plant clean plots into ground that has turned since spring.
the_gift_marked_border.offer: A border's only as good as the day it was drawn, and this one's drawn on ground that moves. The warm soil -- the Gift proper -- is creeping outward. Last year's markers sit a good stone's throw inside the true edge now. Walk me the new line: where the ground goes warm underfoot, that's the Gift, whatever the old stones say. Set it on the map. Farmers are planting clean plots into soil that turned warm since spring, and they've a right to know what they're standing on. Someone ought to keep an honest border.
the_gift_marked_border.progress: The true warm edge runs east of the old boundary stones. Walk it and mark the new line on the map.
the_gift_marked_border.complete: So it's crept a hundred paces east since last reaping. I'll redraw it and post it plain: past this line, the ground listens. It won't stop anyone planting there -- hungry people plant where the yield is, and the yield is best where the ground's warm, that's the devil of it. But they'll know. A border you can trust is a small honest thing, and I collect small honest things down here. There aren't many. Take the marked map; a copy, for the walking of it.

objectives.label: the creeping warm edge
objectives.text: Walk the true warm edge of the Gift
Mark the new border with Ruxandra

the_gift_marked_border.note: The Gift crept a hundred paces east since last reaping; the warm ground spreads. Ruxandra redrew the border true. It stops no one planting the warm rows -- the yield is best there -- but now they know what listens.

the_gift_forge_rest.title: The Day the Hammers Rest
the_gift_forge_rest.summary: Once a year the forges go cold by custom -- Forge-Rest, the one day the south lets itself be soft. Sanda throws the whole quarter a table and needs it stocked. She sends the player to gather grain and render fat so no one in the Gift goes hungry on the day made for gladness.
the_gift_forge_rest.offer: One day a year the hammers rest -- properly rest, by old custom, not the three-count that scares the wits from us. Forge-Rest. The one day the south is allowed to be gentle with itself, and I mean to feed the whole quarter at one long table. Bring me grain from the floors and fat off the vent-brood, enough for a hundred bowls, and I'll cook till dawn. Let them forget the Pit for a day. Let them be just people at a table, warm and full and loud. They earn one day. I'll not let it pass thin.
the_gift_forge_rest.progress: Gather threshed grain and render imp-fat for the Forge-Rest table -- enough to feed the quarter.
the_gift_forge_rest.complete: Look at them. A hundred souls at one table, laughing, sticky with honey-bread, not a hammer in earshot -- and for one whole day nobody in the Gift is a debt or a body or a thing that's owed. Just people. That's what I cook for. Everything good here is temporary, and that's exactly what makes today worth the aching feet. Take the hearth-sign. Hang it where you rest. Wherever you hang it, that's a hearth. That's the whole magic of it.

objectives.text: Gather grain for the Forge-Rest table
Render fat for the Forge-Rest bowls

the_gift_forge_rest.note: On Forge-Rest the hammers rested proper, and a hundred souls sat one table -- not debts, not bodies, just people, warm and loud and full. Everything good here is temporary. That is exactly what made the day worth its cost.

the_gift_pit_bounty.title: Cinders at the Rim
the_gift_pit_bounty.summary: Brazier-imps breed in the Pit's warm vents and swarm the labor-lines, burning the debt-workers who cannot leave their posts to flee. Ruxandra pays a daily bounty to any who thin the brood, since the overseers count a burned worker as merely slower, not saved.
the_gift_pit_bounty.offer: The imps breed in the Pit vents and swarm the labor-lines, and a debt-worker can't run -- run and the overseers add days to your sentence for leaving the post. So they burn where they stand. It's cheaper to the Pit to let them burn than to lose the labor-hour, and that arithmetic makes me sick daily. I can't change the arithmetic. I can pay a bounty for a thinned brood and buy those poor souls an easier shift. Clear a swarm. It's the one number in this place I get to make smaller.
the_gift_pit_bounty.progress: The imp brood swarms the Pit labor-lines from the warm vents. Thin a swarm to spare the debt-workers a burning shift.
the_gift_pit_bounty.complete: Cleared, and the lines'll breathe easier for a shift. The overseers won't thank you -- to them a burned worker's just a slow one, and slow's a thing they know how to price. But the workers know. One nodded at me today, careful, so the post-boss wouldn't see. That nod's worth more than the coin, and I'm giving you the coin too. Come back tomorrow; the vents breed faster than mercy. In the south they always do.

objectives.text: Thin the imp brood off the labor-lines

the_gift_pit_bounty.note: To the Pit, a burned worker is merely a slow one, and slow they know how to price. Ruxandra cannot change that arithmetic; she can only make one number smaller. A careful nod from the lines was worth more than the coin.

the_gift_first_furrow.title: The First Furrow's Name
the_gift_first_furrow.summary: The oldest furrow in the Gift bears a name-stone worn past reading -- the first field fed by the first war-dead, six hundred years gone. Ecaterina wants it read by alchemical light to recover the name before it fades entirely from the record she keeps.
the_gift_first_furrow.offer: There is a first furrow -- the oldest in the Gift, six hundred years turned, the field where the south first learned to feed the ground its dead. It has a name-stone, worn now past any reading by eye. But the old carvers cut deep, and under the right light the ghost of the letters answers. I keep the record of who this place is made of, and I will not let the first of them go nameless. Take the light. Read the stone. Bring me the name it's forgotten. A body deserves at least its name, even a body that became a field.
the_gift_first_furrow.progress: The first furrow's name-stone stands at the Gift's oldest corner. Read it under alchemical light to recover the name.
the_gift_first_furrow.complete: Vestala. That was her name -- the first fed to the first furrow, six hundred years back. I've written it in the book at last, six centuries late, and the book is truer for it. She is a field now, and a good one, and she has her name again. It changes nothing. The row still grows, still eats, still feeds us. But she is named. In the south that is the whole of what we can give the dead, and we so seldom give it. Take the name-token. Carry a name that was almost lost. Names want carrying.

objectives.label: the first furrow's name-stone
objectives.text: Read the worn name-stone by alchemical light
Bring the recovered name to Ecaterina

the_gift_first_furrow.note: Vestala -- the first fed to the first furrow, six hundred years gone, named again at last in Ecaterina's book. It changes nothing; the row still grows and eats and feeds. But she is named. That is the whole of the south's mercy.

the_gift_thresher_wardens.title: Wards for the Threshing Floor
the_gift_thresher_wardens.summary: The threshers work close to the warm rows and the old folk swear a hearth-charm at each corner keeps the listening away. Voica indulges the custom gladly -- it costs nothing and it lets the young ones work unafraid. She needs pouches of blessed salt to set the wards.
the_gift_thresher_wardens.offer: The old threshers won't work a floor without a corner-charm, and I'll not argue custom that keeps my crew easy. Warm rows run close to the threshing floor, and where the ground listens the workers get skittish -- fair enough, so would I. A pouch of blessed salt at each corner and they work the day whistling. Is it the salt that keeps the listening off, or the whistling? Doesn't matter. Both work. Fetch me salt-pouches, four corners' worth, and I'll set the wards myself and stand the crew a round after.
the_gift_thresher_wardens.progress: Blessed salt-pouches are wanted for the four corners of the threshing floor. Gather them and bring them to Voica.
the_gift_thresher_wardens.complete: Four corners warded and the crew whistling by noon -- listen to them. Maybe it's the salt, maybe it's just that a whistling floor is a floor that isn't listening for anything else. I think that's the real ward, if I'm honest: a room too busy being glad to hear the ground. Keep one pouch for yourself. Set it where you sleep in the Gift. It works or it doesn't, but you'll rest easier for the setting of it, and easier rest is never a waste down here.

objectives.text: Gather blessed salt for the floor wards

the_gift_thresher_wardens.note: Voica warded the threshing floor's four corners with blessed salt, and the crew whistled by noon. Maybe it was the salt; maybe a floor too busy being glad cannot hear the ground. Either way, they rested easier.

the_gift_last_honest_meal.title: The Last Honest Meal
the_gift_last_honest_meal.summary: Lenuta's clean plot failed in the end -- one honest harvest, then the thin soil gave out for good. She means to hold one last supper from it before she surrenders the plot to the Gift. She asks the player to help gather the meagre yield, and to sit at the table when it is done.
the_gift_last_honest_meal.offer: My honest plot's finished. One harvest, grey and thin, and then the clean soil just... gave up. I can't feed it what the Gift eats; that was the whole point of it. So I'm done. Come spring it goes back to warm ground like everything here. But there's one last meal in it -- one honest supper, grown from nothing but rain -- and I want it eaten right, at a full table, by people who know what it cost. Help me bring in the last of it. Then sit. I don't want to be the only one who tastes the difference. I don't want it lost that there was a difference.
the_gift_last_honest_meal.progress: Help Lenuta gather the last honest yield from her failing plot, then reach her table for the supper.
the_gift_last_honest_meal.complete: That's all of it. Every grey grain. We ate it slow tonight, and it was poor, and it was the best bread I ever tasted, because I knew what it wasn't. Come spring the plot's warm ground and I'll plant it like the rest and eat like the rest and try to forget there was ever a difference. But there was. You tasted it. Now two of us remember, and a thing two people remember isn't wholly lost. Take the soothing-knot. My mother's. For nights the remembering keeps you up. It will.

objectives.text: Gather the last honest yield

objectives.label: Lenuta's supper table
objectives.text: Sit at Lenuta's last honest supper

the_gift_last_honest_meal.note: Lenuta's honest plot gave one harvest and died; come spring it returns to warm ground. They ate the last of it slow, and it was poor, and it was the best bread. Two people remember there was a difference. That is not nothing.

the_gift_valroms_grain.title: The King's Uneasy Staple
the_gift_valroms_grain.summary: Valrom's musters march on Gift-grown bread, and a war-quartermaster has come to buy the whole reaping for the King's coming campaign of unification. Rada scents that the demand is unnatural -- the ledgers rising in a hand too flat, too regular. She sets the player to gather the requisition orders and read whose appetite is truly being fed.
the_gift_valroms_grain.offer: The King's quartermaster is here to buy the entire reaping -- every loaf of it, for the muster. Valrom means to unify Draconia under one banner, and an army marches on Gift bread, so the whole south's harvest is going to feed a war of conquest grown from war-dead. There's a poetry to it that turns my stomach. But here's the thing: the requisition's rising faster than any army eats. The orders come in a flat, regular hand, more each week, as if the appetite were the paper's, not the King's. Get me the requisition slips off the muster-posts. I want to read whose hunger this really is.
the_gift_valroms_grain.progress: The war-requisition slips are posted at the muster-stations around the Gift. Gather them and bring them to Rada.
the_gift_valroms_grain.complete: Read them together and the pattern's plain and it's wrong. The orders grow by the same increment, same flat hand, week on week -- not an army's appetite, an arrangement's. Somebody's using the King's hunger to unify the south into one supply-line, one command-path, no friction. Valrom thinks he's feeding a conquest. He's being fed into one. I'll get this to the Accord's people quiet. Take the forgery-craft; you've the eye for it now. And take care whose bread you praise. The Gift feeds the hand that's steering the King, and neither the King nor the Gift has noticed.

objectives.text: Gather the war-requisition slips
Read the pattern with Rada

the_gift_valroms_grain.note: The war-requisition grows by a flat regular increment, week on week -- not an army's appetite but an arrangement's. Valrom thinks he feeds a conquest; he is being fed into one. The Gift feeds the hand that steers the King.

greyhollow_drowned_ledger_the_warm_table.title: The Warm Tablet
greyhollow_drowned_ledger_the_warm_table.summary: A debt-tablet washed up on the Blackpier at dawn, hairline-cracked and still faintly warm. Smaranda will not touch it, and will not let it go back out with the tide. Carry it up from the water before someone reads it.
greyhollow_drowned_ledger_the_warm_table.offer: It came in on the tide before light, and it's warm. Tablets aren't warm, do you understand me. That's a whole person's account, cracked but not closed, lying in the mud like driftwood. I've buried dockhands for less curiosity than you're wearing on your face right now. Go down to the pier, lift it clean, and bring it here without looking at the marks. Face-down. I mean it.
greyhollow_drowned_ledger_the_warm_table.progress: The cracked tablet lies at the tide-line under the Blackpier, at the south water. Lift it face-down and bring it up.
greyhollow_drowned_ledger_the_warm_table.complete: You kept it face-down. Good. It stopped being warm somewhere on the walk up, and I can't tell you why that's worse. Somebody's out with the tide and not quite closed, and the sea handed them to us like a bill. Keep the ledger. Reading it is your affair now, not mine -- I've a harbor to run and a short enough account of my own.

objectives.label: the Blackpier tide-line
objectives.text: Reach the cracked tablet at the tide-line
Lift the debt-tablet face-down

greyhollow_drowned_ledger_the_warm_table.note: A whole life came in on the tide, cracked and not quite closed. It was warm when it beached and cold by the harbor rail. The sea, it seems, has learned to deliver.

greyhollow_drowned_ledger_reading_the_fo.title: Reading the Fog
greyhollow_drowned_ledger_reading_the_fo.summary: Marioara has kept Hessik's alchemy alive here, quietly, the way one keeps a candle in wet weather. She wants you to read the harbor fog through the orange-still -- and warns you what it will read, wherever you happen to be standing.
greyhollow_drowned_ledger_reading_the_fo.offer: I keep the old sight the way my grandmother kept it -- for finding rot in a wall, nothing grander. But the fog off the Pit has started to answer. Take the still to the canal steps and look through it, and pay close attention: blue is the raised dead, violet the deep stone, green a carving about to wake. If it goes shifting orange, that is the signal writing itself into the air, and you are to leave. Do not read. Look, and come back, and tell me it was somewhere else and not where you were standing.
greyhollow_drowned_ledger_reading_the_fo.progress: Take the orange reading-still to the canal steps and read the fog. Clear the drowned clerks that gather where it thickens.
greyhollow_drowned_ledger_reading_the_fo.complete: Orange. Of course it was orange. And it followed you along the steps, you say -- brightest where you stood. That is not weather. That is attention. Hessik wrote that a live stone reads a person the way a wet page reads ink, and I have spent thirty years pretending he meant it as a figure of speech. Take his method. Learn the colors cold. The only sight worse than seeing this is not seeing it coming.

objectives.label: the canal steps
objectives.text: Read the fog through the orange-still
Scatter the drowned clerks in the thick fog

greyhollow_drowned_ledger_reading_the_fo.note: The fog read shifting orange, and it was always brightest where the reader stood. Marioara named it plainly and did not soften it: not weather. Attention. Something in the water was learning the shape of whoever came to look.

greyhollow_drowned_ledger_what_keeps_wal.title: What Keeps Walking
greyhollow_drowned_ledger_what_keeps_wal.summary: Varvara keeps the Greyhollow Records, and Varvara has been collected -- closed, finalized, filed -- and yet here she is, taking receipts. The blind seer at the Pit will not stop screaming about her. She asks you to un-file one drowned account, and to watch what the mercy costs.
greyhollow_drowned_ledger_what_keeps_wal.offer: Look at me and don't be polite about it. I'm closed on these books. Collected, finalized, a settled account -- and still keeping the office, because the one thing the machine can't file is a discrepancy that keeps getting up. You and me are the same overdue notice; I just stopped arguing with the arithmetic. There's a node at the Pit rim holding a district shut. Break it, and a hundred doors reopen. Do it. Then stand at the rim and watch the fog, because the mercy has to go somewhere, and it never once goes nowhere.
greyhollow_drowned_ledger_what_keeps_wal.progress: Break the node at the Pit rim -- put down the Sink-Horror that guards it -- then stand at the rim and read what the un-filing costs.
greyhollow_drowned_ledger_what_keeps_wal.complete: Doors reopened along the drowned quarter -- faces flickering back into windows that had been shut a year. And the fog crept one canal nearer, exactly as I told you it would. That's the whole of it, traveler. You didn't cancel the debt. You moved it. Mercy here is a transfer, never a pardon. Take the writ. It un-files one name -- one -- and you'll spend the rest of your walk deciding whose. I stopped being able to decide. That's how I ended up closed.

objectives.text: Put down the Sink-Horror at the Pit node

objectives.label: the Pit rim
objectives.text: Read what the un-filing costs at the rim

greyhollow_drowned_ledger_what_keeps_wal.note: A district un-filed; a hundred doors swung open on flickering faces -- and the orange fog moved one canal closer for it. Varvara did not call it a victory. Mercy in Greyhollow is a transfer, not a pardon. Something, somewhere down the ledger, was charged for every door.

greyhollow_records_rows_stamped_thrice.title: The Line That Would Not Close
greyhollow_records_rows_stamped_thrice.summary: Every account in the annex is stamped collected in violet ink -- every account but one, near the bottom of a roll, stamped and un-stamped and stamped again, the ink smeared where a dead hand kept refusing the verdict. Varvara wants to know whose hand.
greyhollow_records_rows_stamped_thrice.offer: The rolls are tidy. That's the horror of the place -- tidy. One line isn't. Near the bottom of the seventh roll a name's been stamped collected, then scraped, then stamped again, three times over, the violet smeared like someone kept licking a thumb to rub it out. I keep books; I don't keep patience for a corpse arguing arithmetic with my inkpot. Find me who signs that scraping. There'll be a forged toll-receipt behind it -- there always is -- and forgeries have owners.
greyhollow_records_rows_stamped_thrice.progress: Search the annex rolls and the drowned account-house for the forged toll-receipt behind the thrice-stamped line.
greyhollow_records_rows_stamped_thrice.complete: A forgery, just as I said, and a clumsy one -- the receipt was written to un-close a debt, not dodge a toll. Someone loved that name enough to commit a crime against the ledger for it, three nights running, and the ink beat them every dawn. I've seen soldiers with less courage than a smeared thumbprint. File it under nothing. Some lines are meant to stay open, and I've decided this is one.

objectives.text: Ask the annex functionary about the seventh roll
Recover the forged receipt behind the line

greyhollow_records_rows_stamped_thrice.note: One line, stamped and scraped and stamped again, three nights of a dead hand refusing the verdict. Varvara filed it under nothing and let it stay open. In a city that closes people, an unclosed line is the nearest thing to a candle.

greyhollow_records_the_incurious_line.title: Keep Your Eyes on the Floor
greyhollow_records_the_incurious_line.summary: The annex holds tablets no living clerk is allowed to read, and Profira has kept forty years by keeping incurious. A new roll came in that reads orange under the sight. She needs it shelved -- by someone who will not look at the marks.
greyhollow_records_the_incurious_line.offer: I have worked these shelves since before your parents courted, and I have done it by never once wanting to know. That is the whole trade. A roll came in this morning that reads orange when you hold the sight to it -- alive, still writing itself -- and it must go up on the high shelf where the others sleep. I cannot shelve it; my hands want to turn it over, and I no longer trust them. Take this charm. It dulls the wanting. Shelve the roll. Look at the floor the whole way.
greyhollow_records_the_incurious_line.progress: Shelve the orange-reading roll on the high shelf of the annex. Keep incurious; put down whatever the live marks have already pulled from their files.
greyhollow_records_the_incurious_line.complete: It's up, and you kept your eyes down -- I watched your boots the whole way, which is the only part of a person I trust anymore. The ones that had gathered near it went quiet when the roll settled. They only ever wanted to hear it read, poor closed things. Keep the charm. Forty years I've worn its like, and I have never once been curious, and that is the entire reason I am still a person and not a shelf.

objectives.text: Put down the clerks drawn to the live roll

objectives.label: the annex high shelf
objectives.text: Shelve the orange roll without reading it

greyhollow_records_the_incurious_line.note: A live roll went up on the high shelf, unread, by someone who kept her eyes on the floor. Profira has stayed a person and not a fixture by never once wanting to know. In Greyhollow, incuriosity is not ignorance. It is armor.

greyhollow_pit_the_deposits.title: Deposits
greyhollow_pit_the_deposits.summary: The canals are the city's veins and its grave at once; bodies go in as deposits and something down there sorts them. Drowned clerks have been dragging fresh ones under before the families can claim them. Catrina wants them stopped, and does not expect thanks.
greyhollow_pit_the_deposits.offer: You'll have noticed nobody drowns by accident in Greyhollow. The canal is the disposal ledger -- a body goes in, it's a deposit, and by law that's the end of the paperwork. Except the clerks have started pulling the fresh ones under before kin can even claim them, and a corpse claimed is a corpse mourned, which is the last free thing left in this city. Go down to the loading steps and break them off the new dead. It won't fix anything. Do it anyway.
greyhollow_pit_the_deposits.progress: Break the drowned clerks off the fresh dead at the canal loading steps, before the families are turned away.
greyhollow_pit_the_deposits.complete: Six of them off the new dead, and three families got to claim their own before the water took them. Three. In this place that's a good day, and I hate that it's true. The clerks don't hunger, you understand -- they file. They were just moving deposits along, the way they did in life, the way they'll do till the fog eats the last of them. Take the coin. Spend it on someone who's still warm.

objectives.text: Break the clerks off the fresh dead

greyhollow_pit_the_deposits.note: The clerks do not hunger. They file. Catrina bought three families the right to mourn their own, and called it a good day, and hated that it was one. In Greyhollow, a claimed corpse is the last free thing a person owns.

greyhollow_pit_names_owed.title: Names Owed
greyhollow_pit_names_owed.summary: A ledger is nailed to the Pit rim listing names and the labor-days owed against them -- and a name freshly marked collected belongs to a man Smaranda hired yesterday, who is still, as far as anyone can tell, alive. She wants his token off the rim before the rim makes it true.
greyhollow_pit_names_owed.offer: There's a board nailed at the Pit rim, names and days-owed, and I read it every morning the way other people read the weather. This morning a name was marked collected in fresh violet -- Ostrov, who I paid a full day's wage yesterday and who ate his supper across from me last night. He's upstairs asleep right now. But the rim's marked him closed, and I've learned the rim is patient, not wrong. Get his name-token down off that board before the arithmetic catches up to the boy.
greyhollow_pit_names_owed.progress: Take the kept-name token off the Pit-rim ledger. Cut through the drowned clerks that keep the board.
greyhollow_pit_names_owed.complete: You got it down. He woke this morning coughing water he never swallowed, but he woke. I'll not pretend the rim gave up -- it doesn't; a scraped name is a delayed name, not a spared one. But delayed is a whole extra season of a boy eating supper across from me, and I'll take the season and not ask the price of it out loud. Keep his token. Some debts you carry so the ledger can't.

objectives.text: Clear the clerks keeping the Pit-rim board
Take the boy's name-token off the ledger

greyhollow_pit_names_owed.note: A living man's name, marked collected on the Pit rim in fresh violet. Smaranda took his token down and bought him a season. A scraped name is a delayed name, never a spared one -- but she asked the boy no questions, and he ate his supper again that night.

greyhollow_canal_the_handprint.title: The Handprint on the Wall
greyhollow_canal_the_handprint.summary: On a canal wall, a child's chalk handprint -- the same emblem an orphanage used a thousand years and a whole map away. Around it, a fresh Morven surveillance-mark, drawn carefully. Zenobia drew that mark, and cannot tell you whether she was protecting the handprint or cataloguing it.
greyhollow_canal_the_handprint.offer: There's a small hand chalked on the wall down by the sixth lock. A child's. It matches an old, old emblem -- an orphanage, a woman who kept children incurious so the stones couldn't reach them, a thousand years gone and half the world away, and I only know that because knowing is my whole profession. I drew a mark around it last week. Careful. Correct. And I have stood here every night since unable to tell you whether I ringed it to keep it safe or to file it. Go and look. Tell me which line I drew.
greyhollow_canal_the_handprint.progress: Go to the handprint at the sixth lock and read the Morven mark ringed around it. Put down the ledger-drakes roosting in the flooded lock-house.
greyhollow_canal_the_handprint.complete: You looked, and you can't say either, and neither can I, and that -- that is the whole disease in one wall. The mark I drew is exactly the mark we use to protect a thing and exactly the mark we use to catalogue it for collection. The same hand draws both. I have forgotten which one I am. Take the ward; it's copied off the handprint, and it's the only chalk in this city I'm certain was drawn out of love. I keep it to remember there was a difference, once.

objectives.text: Clear the drakes from the flooded lock-house

objectives.label: the handprint at the sixth lock
objectives.text: Read the mark ringed around the handprint

greyhollow_canal_the_handprint.note: A child's chalk hand, ringed by a Morven mark that means protect and means catalogue in the same strokes. Zenobia drew it and cannot say which she intended. The last unbroken echo of a small warm kindness, watched by a house that has forgotten the difference between guarding and filing.

greyhollow_canal_bodies_are_deposits.title: The Drowned Account-House
greyhollow_canal_bodies_are_deposits.summary: An account-house went under when the sixth lock failed, and the ledger-drakes have nested in its flooded rooms, hoarding waterlogged tablets in the rafters. Marioara needs a specific reagent from its cellar before the drakes drag the whole record down for good.
greyhollow_canal_bodies_are_deposits.offer: When the old lock let go, the Threnody account-house went under to its second floor, and the drakes moved in like they'd been waiting for it -- roosting in the rafters, hoarding tablets they can't read, dropping them in the black water one by one. There's a salt-line pouch in that cellar, cured the proper Hessik way, that I cannot make anymore for want of the recipe. Clear the drakes enough to reach it. And mind the tablets in the rafters; a dropped one has been known to read itself aloud on the way down.
greyhollow_canal_bodies_are_deposits.progress: Clear the ledger-drakes from the flooded account-house and recover the salt-line pouch from its cellar.
greyhollow_canal_bodies_are_deposits.complete: You got it, and you didn't linger under the rafters -- I can tell, because you're still yourself and not standing very still with your head tilted. Good. The drakes will nest again by winter; they always do; a drowned ledger is a warm place to a thing that eats meaning. But I've my pouch, and I can salt a doorway proper again, and a proper-salted door is a real thing in a city full of paper ones. My thanks. Keep the tonic -- your joints will learn this cold soon enough.

objectives.text: Clear the drakes from the account-house
Recover the Hessik-cured salt-line pouch

greyhollow_canal_bodies_are_deposits.note: The drakes nest in drowned ledgers the way rooks nest in dead oaks -- a warm place, to a thing that eats meaning. Marioara salted her doorway proper again and called it a real thing in a city full of paper ones. The tablets still drop from the rafters, still read themselves aloud on the way down.

greyhollow_side_the_flat_loaf.title: The Flat Loaf
greyhollow_side_the_flat_loaf.summary: The yeast will not rise in Greyhollow anymore -- it dies overnight, the way it does near a live stone -- and Paraschiva bakes flat grey loaves anyway, and sets a warm one out each dawn for whoever walks in cold. She only needs a marshlight to see by while she works the pre-dawn dough.
greyhollow_side_the_flat_loaf.offer: Don't tell me about the bread. I know it's flat. Yeast won't live here -- goes to nothing in the crock overnight, and I've stopped apologizing, same as the Vetka women did in the stories my gran told. But flat bread's still bread, and a warm flat loaf set on the sill before light has turned more than one frozen soul back from the canal, and I'll go on setting it out till I'm the one they set out. My lamp's guttering, is all. Fetch me a good marshlight from the fen-trader and I'll bake by it and call us square with a bowl.
greyhollow_side_the_flat_loaf.progress: Bring Paraschiva a marshlight jar so she can work the pre-dawn dough by its glow.
greyhollow_side_the_flat_loaf.complete: There -- steady blue, burns clean, and doesn't gutter when the fog leans on the window. Now I can see the dough at the hour nobody else is fool enough to be awake. Sit. Eat. Yes it's flat. It's also warm, and warm is the whole argument I've got against this city, and I've been winning it one loaf at a time for eleven years. Come back at dawn if the cold gets into you. There's always one on the sill.

objectives.text: Bring the marshlight jar to Paraschiva

greyhollow_side_the_flat_loaf.note: The yeast dies overnight in Greyhollow, the way it dies near anything the stones are reading. Paraschiva bakes the flat grey loaf anyway and sets a warm one on the sill each dawn. Eleven years, one loaf at a time -- the whole argument she has against the city, and she is winning it.

greyhollow_side_a_warm_bowl_downwind.title: A Warm Bowl, Downwind
greyhollow_side_a_warm_bowl_downwind.summary: Despina sets a bowl of broth out each night at the edge of the finalized district -- for the closed ones, who cannot taste it and cannot thank her and come for it anyway, out of a habit their debt did not manage to collect. She wants someone to walk the round with her, once, so she is not the only one who saw.
greyhollow_side_a_warm_bowl_downwind.offer: You'll think I'm soft in the head. Every night I leave a bowl at the district edge, downwind, where the finalized drift. The collected ones -- closed files that kept their feet. They can't taste it. Can't thank me. Can't much of anything. But they come, every night, and stand near the warm of it a while, and something in them remembers there was a warm to stand near. The Morven have a mark for me, I'm sure. I'd like one living soul to walk the round with me once and see it isn't nothing. Just once. Then you can decide if I'm mad.
greyhollow_side_a_warm_bowl_downwind.progress: Walk the evening round with Despina to the finalized district edge and set the bowl at the marked step.
greyhollow_side_a_warm_bowl_downwind.complete: You saw them come. You saw one -- the tall one, was a lamplighter I think, before -- you saw him stand in the steam a moment longer than the rest. That's all it is. That's all I've got. Whatever the machine did to close him, it didn't quite reach the part that knows warm from cold, and I feed that part, and it isn't nothing, and you can't tell me it's nothing now, because you stood there too. Take this. My mother knotted it against bad dreams. You'll want it after tonight.

objectives.text: Agree to walk the evening round with Despina

objectives.label: the finalized district edge
objectives.text: Set the bowl at the marked step

greyhollow_side_a_warm_bowl_downwind.note: Despina feeds the finalized a broth they cannot taste, and one of them -- a lamplighter, before -- stood in the steam a moment longer than the rest. The machine closed him and did not quite reach the part that knows warm from cold. She feeds that part. It is not nothing.

greyhollow_side_the_lamp_that_reaches.title: The Lamp That Reaches the Ground
greyhollow_side_the_lamp_that_reaches.summary: Greyhollow's gaslight never reaches the ground -- it hangs above the fog like a ceiling, and the drowned clerks work the dark beneath it unseen. Safta has forged a deepbed lantern that burns low enough to light the black water. She needs it proved on the loading steps, where the clerks are thickest.
greyhollow_side_the_lamp_that_reaches.offer: You'll have noticed the light here's a liar. The lamps hang up in the fog like a false ceiling and the ground stays black as the water, and that's no accident -- a city that disposes of people in the dark keeps the dark where it needs it. I've forged a lantern that burns deep and low, deepbed oil, throws its light down instead of up. It'll show you the loading steps as they truly are. I want it proved before I make more. Take it down there and light the water. Then tell me if I should keep forging them or melt the mold and forget I ever tried.
greyhollow_side_the_lamp_that_reaches.progress: Take the deepbed lantern to the loading steps, light the black water, and clear the drowned clerks it reveals.
greyhollow_side_the_lamp_that_reaches.complete: So they were down there. Rows of them, working the dark, sorting deposits by a light that was never meant to reach that far. You lit the water and they didn't like it -- a filed thing hates being looked at; being unseen is half of what closed them. Keep the lantern. I'll forge more, quietly, and hand them out to the ones who leave bowls at the district edge and bake flat bread and do the small mad decent things. A city can't be all ceiling. Somebody's got to light the ground.

objectives.label: the loading steps
objectives.text: Light the black water with the deepbed lantern
Clear the clerks the lantern reveals

greyhollow_side_the_lamp_that_reaches.note: The gaslight hangs above the fog like a false ceiling, and the clerks work the dark beneath, unseen. Safta forged a lantern that reaches the ground -- and means to hand them, quietly, to everyone in Greyhollow doing a small mad decent thing. A city cannot be all ceiling.

greyhollow_side_salvage_rights.title: Salvage Rights
greyhollow_side_salvage_rights.summary: Tinca trades in what the black water gives up -- and the water has been giving up locked strongboxes with the Archive's own seal. She wants one salvaged and its contents read before she decides who to sell the knowledge to: the annex, the Morven, or nobody.
greyhollow_side_salvage_rights.offer: Everything sinks in Greyhollow, and I make my living on the small fraction that floats back up. Lately it's strongboxes -- Archive seals, still locked, bobbing up at the third lock like the vault itself is molting. There's a border-map in the last one I pulled, marked in a hand I don't know, and I've three buyers already: the annex wants it filed, the Morven want it vanished, and a third party wants it never to have existed. Dive the third lock, bring me the marked border, and we'll decide together who gets the poorer for knowing.
greyhollow_side_salvage_rights.progress: Salvage the sealed strongbox at the third lock and bring Tinca the marked map-border sealed inside.
greyhollow_side_salvage_rights.complete: There she is -- Archive seal unbroken, and inside, exactly as I feared, a map with our own canals redrawn one district smaller than they are today. Someone's mapping Greyhollow as it will be after the fog takes another quarter. The annex would file that and do nothing; the Morven would bury it and know everything; I'm going to sell it to the third buyer, who pays best and asks that it stay a secret, which suits a thing this ugly. Your cut. And your discretion, which costs me more than your cut.

objectives.label: the third lock
objectives.text: Dive the third lock for the sealed strongbox
Recover the marked map-border

greyhollow_side_salvage_rights.note: A strongbox with the Archive's own seal, bobbing up unbroken -- and inside, Greyhollow redrawn one district smaller, mapped as it will be after the fog takes another quarter. Tinca sold the ugly thing to the buyer who wanted it kept secret. Somewhere, someone is already planning around the city's future losses.

greyhollow_side_the_tap_of_a_tablet.title: The Tap of a Tablet
greyhollow_side_the_tap_of_a_tablet.summary: Varvara keeps her own debt-tablet in her coat and taps it when she's nervous -- checking she is still, legally, allowed to exist. Lately the tapping has changed pitch. She asks you to carry the tablet to the apothecary and have it read, because she cannot bear to look at what her own account now says.
greyhollow_side_the_tap_of_a_tablet.offer: You've seen me do it -- reach into the coat, tap the tablet twice, put it back. Everyone here does it who's closed. It's how a dead clerk checks the arithmetic still lets them keep the office. The sound was always the same, dull as a coffin lid, and I found a grim comfort in that. Three days ago it changed. Rings higher now. Warmer. And I know -- I know -- what a warming tablet means, and I cannot be the one to look. Take it to Marioara. Have her read it with the sight. Bring me back a yes or a no and none of the middle.
greyhollow_side_the_tap_of_a_tablet.progress: Carry Varvara's debt-tablet to the apothecary Marioara and have it read with the alchemical sight.
greyhollow_side_the_tap_of_a_tablet.complete: ...A yes. It's warming. She's certain. Which means somewhere the machine is deciding my discrepancy has gone on long enough, and the account that let me keep walking is being made ready to close for good. Well. I've had a year of extra ledgers I was never owed, and I filed them tidy, and I un-filed one line for a name that deserved it. That's more than most closed things get to say. Keep this token -- my kept-name, while it's still mine to give. When the tapping stops, you'll know I'm properly filed. Don't leave a bowl. I never liked broth.

objectives.text: Have Marioara read Varvara's tablet with the sight

greyhollow_side_the_tap_of_a_tablet.note: Varvara's debt-tablet has begun to warm -- the machine readying to close, at last, the discrepancy that let a dead clerk keep her office. She gave away her kept-name token while it was still hers to give. When the tapping stops, she said, you will know she is properly filed.

greyhollow_side_grey_wood_never_lived.title: Grey Wood That Never Lived
greyhollow_side_grey_wood_never_lived.summary: Greyhollow's piers and pilings are cut from grey wood that never quite finished being alive -- the dying forests of the old world, dead here -- and the ledger-drakes strip and hoard the good beams before Safta can reclaim them for repairs. She needs bog-iron nails and the beams the drakes are stealing.
greyhollow_side_grey_wood_never_lived.offer: Half this city stands on grey wood -- old-world timber that stopped growing without ever agreeing to die. It doesn't rot the honest way; it just greys through and holds, cold and patient as everything else here. I keep the piers standing with what I can reclaim, but the drakes strip the sound beams for their nests faster than I can pull them, and the sixth lock's walkway is three planks from dropping a family into the black. Kill enough drakes to break the roost, and bring me bog-iron for nails. The dead wood I'll manage. I always do.
greyhollow_side_grey_wood_never_lived.progress: Break the drake roost stripping the sixth-lock walkway, and bring Safta bog-iron for nails.
greyhollow_side_grey_wood_never_lived.complete: That's the roost broken and enough iron to make the walkway honest again. It'll hold now -- grey wood does hold, that's the awful mercy of it, it just holds and holds and never lets you forget what it used to be. My grandmother worked river-birch that smelled of sap. I work this. Cold in the hand, quiet in the fire. Still, a mended walkway is a real thing, and a family crossed it this morning and didn't fall, and I'll take real things where this city hands them out. Here -- the striker's yours. It lights even the wet wood.

objectives.text: Break the drake roost on the sixth-lock walkway
Gather bog-iron for the nails

greyhollow_side_grey_wood_never_lived.note: The piers of Greyhollow stand on grey wood that never finished dying -- cold, patient, holding and holding. Safta mended the walkway and a family crossed it and did not fall. She works dead timber where her grandmother worked river-birch that smelled of sap, and takes the real things where the city hands them out.

greyhollow_side_the_forgery_market.title: The Forgery Market
greyhollow_side_the_forgery_market.summary: A ring of forgers sells un-closing receipts under the ninth lock -- papers that unbind a collected debt, worth more than most lives, and just as fake. Tinca has a client who needs one real enough to fool the annex. Three parties want the forgery; only one deserves to be lied to.
greyhollow_side_the_forgery_market.offer: There's a trade under the ninth lock in the only merchandise that matters here -- un-closing receipts. A paper that says a collected debt was collected in error, and unbinds the account. Real ones don't exist; the machine doesn't make mistakes it admits to. But a good forgery, stamped right, will fool a tired annex clerk for exactly as long as it takes a mother to walk her son out the water-gate. I've a client who needs one. So does a Morven, for reasons I don't ask, and so does the annex, who wants to catch the forgers. Buy me the receipt. We'll pick which of the three we cheat.
greyhollow_side_the_forgery_market.progress: Acquire a forged un-closing receipt from the ring under the ninth lock, and clear the drakes guarding their den.
greyhollow_side_the_forgery_market.complete: Clean work -- stamped in real annex violet, the dies must have walked out in someone's boot. Three buyers, and you know what, I've decided we cheat the annex: let them keep hunting phantom forgers while a mother walks her boy out the water-gate on this very paper tonight. The Morven will note that I chose mercy over coin, and file it, because they file everything, and one day that file will cost me. Everything here costs later. But the boy walks tonight, and later is later. Your share, and my thanks.

objectives.text: Clear the drakes guarding the forgers' den
Buy the forged un-closing receipt

greyhollow_side_the_forgery_market.note: An un-closing receipt, forged in stolen annex violet, worth more than most lives and every bit as false. Tinca chose to cheat the annex and let a mother walk her son out the water-gate. The Morven filed the mercy. Everything in Greyhollow costs later. The boy walked that night.

greyhollow_side_a_name_off_the_books.title: A Name Off the Books
greyhollow_side_a_name_off_the_books.summary: The Morven can lift a name out of the ledger entirely -- unmake the record so the machine has nothing to collect. Zenobia can do it once, for a price paid in knowledge: she needs the dossier of a target's habits, which means someone must be watched, learned, and quietly betrayed.
greyhollow_side_a_name_off_the_books.offer: You've earned enough of my trust for the ugliest favor I trade. I can take a name off the books -- not un-close it, unmake it, so the machine reaches for the record and finds only where it used to be. It works. It also requires I know a person completely first: their walk, their hours, the door they lock last. That dossier is the price, because to unmake one name I must first make another entirely knowable, and knowable is the first half of collectable. Bring me the habits of the man I'll name. Then decide, once you've watched him breathe a week, whether the trade was worth what you learned about doing it.
greyhollow_side_a_name_off_the_books.progress: Compile the dossier of the target's habits for Zenobia, and clear the drowned clerks shadowing his route.
greyhollow_side_a_name_off_the_books.complete: Complete. Down to which foot he favors on the stairs. I'll lift the other name tonight and the machine will grope after a record that isn't -- one soul saved, clean off the books. And you and I both know the cost now, don't we. To spare one, I had to render another perfectly legible, and legible is how the fog starts. That's the Morven, in one favor: we save people by learning them the way the enemy learns them. Take the dossier. Burn it, if you're wise. I never can.

objectives.text: Clear the clerks shadowing the target's route
Compile the target's dossier of habits

greyhollow_side_a_name_off_the_books.note: To lift one name off the books, the Morven must first render another perfectly legible -- and legible is how the fog begins. Zenobia saves people by learning them exactly as the enemy learns them. One soul went clean off the ledger. The dossier that bought it, she could never bring herself to burn.

greyhollow_side_the_seer_at_the_pier.title: The Seer at the Pier
greyhollow_side_the_seer_at_the_pier.summary: A blind old man stands at the end of the fourth pier with his ruined eyes to the fog, screaming the same thing at every closed thing that passes: already collected -- why is it still walking? Ilinca's grandfather. She wants someone unafraid to sit with him and learn what he keeps seeing.
greyhollow_side_the_seer_at_the_pier.offer: That's my grandfather at the pier's end -- eyes gone, been gone since I was small, and yet he's the only one in Greyhollow who sees anything true. He reads the matured stones the way we read faces, and it's driven the peace clean out of him. All day he screams at the finalized as they drift past: already collected, why is it still walking. Over and over. Nobody'll sit with him; they say his sight's catching. I say a man shouldn't scream alone into fog. Go to him. Let him take your measure. Come tell me what he calls you -- he calls everyone something.
greyhollow_side_the_seer_at_the_pier.progress: Sit with the blind seer at the end of the fourth pier and let him read you, then report to Ilinca what he named you.
greyhollow_side_the_seer_at_the_pier.complete: ...He called you that. He doesn't say things kindly, my grandfather, but he doesn't say them wrong either -- his eyes went so the rest of him could see, and what he sees, he can't unsee, which is why he screams. He knew, before anyone, that the closed things kept walking. He'll be right about you too, in time, whatever he meant by it. Sit with him again when the fog's bad. He knows he's alone out there. That's the cruelest part -- the sight took his eyes and left him that.

objectives.label: the end of the fourth pier
objectives.text: Sit with the blind seer at the pier's end
Tell Ilinca what the seer named you

greyhollow_side_the_seer_at_the_pier.note: A blind man at the pier's end, screaming already collected -- why is it still walking at every closed thing that drifts past. His eyes went so the rest of him could see, and what he sees he cannot unsee. He was the first to know the finalized kept their feet. He knows he screams alone.

greyhollow_side_cold_that_gets_in.title: The Cold That Gets In
greyhollow_side_cold_that_gets_in.summary: Greyhollow's cold gets into the joints and stays, and the old dockhands seize up before their time. Marioara brews a brine-tonic that helps, the plain honest way, but she is out of the fen-herb that cuts the ache. A simple errand, and a rare warm one.
greyhollow_side_cold_that_gets_in.offer: Not everything I do here is fog and dread and reading the future in a jar, thank the saints. Most days I'm just an old woman with a kettle, easing joints. The cold in this city gets into the bones and sets like mortar, and the dockhands seize up years before they ought -- good people, worked hard, owed a little ease. My brine-tonic helps, but I'm out of the fen-herb that gives it its bite. Fetch me a proper poultice-culture from the marsh-trader and I'll brew a batch that'll have half the harbor moving like they're forty again. That's a good day's work, that is. Rare enough here.
greyhollow_side_cold_that_gets_in.progress: Bring Marioara a cultured poultice from the marsh-trader so she can brew tonic for the seized-up dockhands.
greyhollow_side_cold_that_gets_in.complete: Perfect -- properly cultured, none of that grey mold the cheats sell. I'll have a batch by morning and old Brasov'll unbend his knees enough to dance at his granddaughter's wedding, which is Thursday, which is the whole point of anything. You laugh, but easing one old man's knees is the most good I'll do all month, and I'll not apologize for being glad of it. Take a tonic yourself. And come to the wedding -- there'll be flat bread, and it'll be warm, and Brasov will dance badly, and it will be enough.

objectives.text: Bring cultured poultice from the marsh-trader

greyhollow_side_cold_that_gets_in.note: The cold in Greyhollow sets in the joints like mortar. Marioara brewed her brine-tonic and old Brasov unbent his knees enough to dance, badly, at his granddaughter's wedding on Thursday. Flat bread, warm, an old man dancing wrong. In this city it was enough, and she would not apologize for being glad.

greyhollow_side_what_the_watcher_draws.title: What the Watcher Draws
greyhollow_side_what_the_watcher_draws.summary: Zenobia has been chalking Morven marks along the canals for twenty years without ever being told what they mean -- she copies them from a master-sheet she was handed as a girl. Now the marks have started appearing on their own, ahead of her, and she wants to know who is drawing them.
greyhollow_side_what_the_watcher_draws.offer: I've chalked the marks along these canals since I was a girl of twelve -- protection here, catalogue there, watch this door -- copied off a master-sheet I was handed and told never to read as words, only as shapes. Twenty years. And this month I've come to walls that were already marked. In my hand. Marks I hadn't drawn yet, waiting for me, correct to the last stroke. Someone -- or something -- is drawing ahead of me, and I need to know if I'm being helped or replaced. Bring me the master-sheet's border from the old station; I want to compare the hand.
greyhollow_side_what_the_watcher_draws.progress: Recover the master-sheet's marked border from the old Morven station and compare it against the marks drawing themselves.
greyhollow_side_what_the_watcher_draws.complete: It's the same hand. Mine, and not mine -- the marks appearing on the walls match my master-sheet exactly, which means whoever's drawing ahead of me has my master-sheet, or is my master-sheet, or the marks have learned to write themselves the way the tablets do. I don't know which is worse and I've stopped ranking them. I'll keep chalking. What else is there. But I've started drawing a small thing of my own in the corner of each -- a mark that means nothing, that no sheet taught me. If it starts appearing ahead of me too, I'll know the last part of me that's mine is gone.

objectives.label: the old Morven station
objectives.text: Recover the master-sheet border from the station
Take the marked master-sheet border

greyhollow_side_what_the_watcher_draws.note: The Morven marks have begun drawing themselves ahead of Zenobia, correct to the last stroke, in a hand that is hers and not hers. She has started signing each with a small meaningless mark no sheet taught her. If that too begins appearing ahead of her, she will know the last part of herself that is hers is gone.

greyhollow_side_the_dead_dont_argue.title: The Dead Don't Argue
greyhollow_side_the_dead_dont_argue.summary: The finalized move like closed files -- orderly, incurious, no want left in them -- until a work-gang of them turns down the wrong lane and begins, slowly, methodically, walking the drowned quarter's living out of their homes. Catrina needs them broken before the district is emptied and finalized in fact.
greyhollow_side_the_dead_dont_argue.offer: The closed ones don't fight, mostly. They drift, they file, they don't argue -- that's the whole horror, a person with the argument taken out. But a gang of them's turned down Cutter's Row and started walking folk out of their houses, calm as clerks reading an eviction, and the folk they walk out don't come back and the houses go quiet. Finalized in fact, not just on paper. I can't reason with a closed file; you can't argue with something that's had the argument removed. So it's the hard way. Break the gang on Cutter's Row before the row goes silent for good.
greyhollow_side_the_dead_dont_argue.progress: Break the finalized work-gang emptying the houses along Cutter's Row.
greyhollow_side_the_dead_dont_argue.complete: Down, all of them, and Cutter's Row still has folk in it tonight who'd have been fog by morning. I hate this work. Not because it's hard -- because they don't resist right. A living thing fights for its life; these just... stop, when you break them, like closing a book. I keep waiting to feel like I killed something, and instead I feel like I filed it, and that's the city getting into me the way the cold gets in the joints. Take the plate. Wear it. And go somewhere warm after this, if there is anywhere. Find the flat bread. Something.

objectives.text: Break the finalized gang on Cutter's Row

greyhollow_side_the_dead_dont_argue.note: The finalized fought like closing books, not like living things, and Catrina felt she had filed them rather than killed them. Cutter's Row kept its people that night. She went looking, after, for the flat bread and the warm -- for anything in Greyhollow that still argued for itself.

greyhollow_side_a_receipt_for_a_life.title: A Receipt for a Life
greyhollow_side_a_receipt_for_a_life.summary: When the annex collects a soul, it issues a receipt -- a small violet slip proving the erasure was lawful and complete. A widow has come to Profira begging for her husband's receipt, not to undo it, only to have one paper that says he was ever owed enough to be worth collecting. Profira cannot give it. She asks you to.
greyhollow_side_a_receipt_for_a_life.offer: There is a woman in my waiting hall who has been there three days. Her husband was collected -- lawfully, completely, gone to the black water like a settled account -- and she does not want him back; she knows better than to want that here. She wants his receipt. The slip we issue proving the collection was in order. She wants one piece of paper that says the machine thought him worth the ink to close. I am forbidden to issue it to kin. But you are not kin, and you are standing here, and the receipt is in the drowned account-house third drawer, and I am a coward who has decided to be a slightly smaller one today. Go.
greyhollow_side_a_receipt_for_a_life.progress: Recover the collection-receipt from the drowned account-house third drawer and clear the drakes nesting in the records.
greyhollow_side_a_receipt_for_a_life.complete: You brought it. Violet ink, proper stamp, his name spelled right -- they always spell them right, that's the obscenity, such care taken to erase a man correctly. I gave it to her myself; broke the rule with my own hand for once in forty years. She held it like a portrait. Said now there was proof he'd been here, been owed, been worth closing. That the machine's own paperwork could love a man back into having existed. I've filed the collected for four decades and never once thought a receipt could be a mercy. It can't. But she thinks it can, and I let her, and my hands haven't stopped shaking, and I find I don't want them to.

objectives.text: Clear the drakes from the records
Recover the husband's collection-receipt

greyhollow_side_a_receipt_for_a_life.note: The annex spells the collected names right -- such care taken to erase a man correctly. A widow wanted only the receipt, proof her husband was ever worth the ink to close. Profira broke a forty-year rule to give it, and her hands would not stop shaking, and she found she did not want them to.

greyhollow_side_the_last_unbroken_echo.title: The Last Unbroken Echo
greyhollow_side_the_last_unbroken_echo.summary: Dragan runs the nearest thing Greyhollow has to an orphanage -- a leaky room over a chandlery where a dozen children sleep. He teaches them the old handprint game, the one from a thousand years and a whole world gone, without knowing what it wards against. He only knows it keeps them from listening to the fog.
greyhollow_side_the_last_unbroken_echo.offer: I've a dozen children in a room over the chandlery, and no coin, and no calling but that somebody has to. I teach them a game my mother taught me -- press your hand in chalk, name the print yours, never wash it off. Foolish. But the ones who play it don't stand at the windows when the fog comes talking, and the ones who don't play it -- do. So I teach it. There's a woman in the old tales, a healer, kept children incurious with small games so the stones couldn't reach them, and I think maybe I'm doing her work without the wit to know it. Bring me chalk and a keepsake-cord to bind the little ones' prints. It's all the warding I've got.
greyhollow_side_the_last_unbroken_echo.progress: Bring Dragan a keepsake-cord to bind the children's chalk handprints against the listening fog.
greyhollow_side_the_last_unbroken_echo.complete: Bless you. The little ones have their prints bound now -- each one named, each one theirs, cord through the loop like a proper keepsake. Watch them tonight when the fog leans on the glass: they'll play their game and not one will drift to the window. I don't understand it. I don't need to. A thousand years ago a woman kept children safe by keeping them uncurious and asking, and here I am doing it in a leaky room, and if that's the last unbroken thread of something warm running through all this cold water -- then good. Let it run through me. Take the cord's twin. Bind something you don't want the fog to have.

objectives.text: Bring the keepsake-cord to Dragan

greyhollow_side_the_last_unbroken_echo.note: In a leaky room over a chandlery, Dragan keeps a dozen children incurious with a chalk-handprint game -- the same warding a healer used a thousand years and a whole world gone. He does not understand why the fog cannot reach the ones who play it. He does not need to. The last unbroken thread of something warm, running through the cold water.

greyhollow_daily_the_narrows_toll.title: The Toll at the Narrows
greyhollow_daily_the_narrows_toll.summary: Everything that moves through Greyhollow pays at the narrow places, and Zoe keeps the toll at the second narrows -- a thankless post the drowned clerks keep trying to collect at, badly, since they only know one kind of toll. She could use a hand thinning them each morning.
greyhollow_daily_the_narrows_toll.offer: You'd think guarding a tollgate would be dull. It was, once. Now the closed ones drift up the narrows every dawn and try to collect the toll themselves -- except the only toll a finalized clerk knows how to take is the whole account, so they reach for the boatmen's names instead of their coppers. Bad for trade, worse for the boatmen. I can hold the gate; I can't hold the gate and clear the water both. Come by any morning and thin them off the narrows, and I'll square you from the toll-box. It's honest coin. Well -- it's coin. Honest is a lot to ask down here.
greyhollow_daily_the_narrows_toll.progress: Clear the drowned clerks trying to collect at the second narrows.
greyhollow_daily_the_narrows_toll.complete: Cleared, and the morning boats got through with their names intact, which is the whole job. You'd be surprised how few things down here you can finish and have stay finished -- this one un-finishes itself by tomorrow, they always drift back, but at least it's the honest kind of pointless. Your cut from the box. Come again at dawn; the narrows never do stay clear, and I never do get tired of pretending they might.

objectives.text: Thin the clerks at the second narrows

greyhollow_daily_the_narrows_toll.note: The narrows never stay clear -- the finalized drift back by dawn, reaching for the boatmen's names instead of their coppers. Zoe clears them each morning and never tires of pretending they might, one day, stay cleared. The honest kind of pointless, she calls it.

greyhollow_daily_deposits_pulled.title: Deposits Pulled
greyhollow_daily_deposits_pulled.summary: The ledger-drakes work the harbor by night, pulling salvage and the odd unclaimed tablet up from the black water into their roosts. Smaranda runs a standing bounty: bring down enough drakes each day to keep the shipping lanes clear and the roosts from owning the harbor outright.
greyhollow_daily_deposits_pulled.offer: Standing offer, this one, so mark it well. The drakes work my harbor every night, dragging up whatever the water's swallowed -- salvage, tablets, the odd boot with a foot still in it -- and hauling it to roosts that get bolder every week. A roost over a shipping lane is a wreck waiting for a fog-night. Bring down a good number for me any day you're able and I'll pay from the harbor purse, same rate, no haggling. It's never done and it never will be. But a harbor's like a ledger: you don't close it, you just keep it from getting away from you.
greyhollow_daily_deposits_pulled.progress: Bring down ledger-drakes over the harbor shipping lanes for Smaranda's standing bounty.
greyhollow_daily_deposits_pulled.complete: That'll keep the lanes open another day, which is all a day's ever good for down here. The roosts'll be back to bold by week's end, and I'll have the bounty waiting, same as always -- a harbor's a thing you tend, not a thing you finish. Your pay from the purse. You've a good arm for it and a better sense than most to quit while the light holds. Come back when it does again.

objectives.text: Bring down drakes over the shipping lanes

greyhollow_daily_deposits_pulled.note: The drakes haul the water's swallowed things up into bolder roosts each week -- salvage, tablets, the odd boot with a foot still in it. Smaranda tends the harbor the way a clerk tends a ledger: never closed, only kept from getting away. The bounty stands. It is never done.

greyhollow_side_what_the_drakes_carry.title: What the Drakes Carry
greyhollow_side_what_the_drakes_carry.summary: The ledger-drakes hoard debt-tablets in their roosts -- meaningless shiny weight to them -- but Aglaia has noticed the tablets they steal are always live ones, orange under the sight, and always from the same three account-houses. Something is using the drakes to move active signal around the city.
greyhollow_side_what_the_drakes_carry.offer: I've watched the drakes long enough to know they're thieves without taste -- they'll haul a copper spoon and a dead man's tablet with equal joy, magpie-brained, hoarding weight for weight's sake. Except I started marking what they take, and it isn't random. The tablets they steal are the live ones -- orange under the sight, still writing themselves -- and always from three houses near the Pit. Something's using the birds as couriers, moving live signal around the city where no clerk would carry it. Break a roost and bring me the tablets. I want to see where the birds have been told to fly.
greyhollow_side_what_the_drakes_carry.progress: Break a drake roost near the Pit and recover the live tablets they have been hoarding.
greyhollow_side_what_the_drakes_carry.complete: Look at them -- every one reads orange, every one live, and laid out in the roost not scattered like a magpie's hoard but stacked. Sorted. A bird doesn't sort. Something's directing the flights, using the drakes to shuffle live signal from house to house under our noses, and the map of where they've flown is a map of where the fog means to grow. I'll take this to the watcher; she reads such maps. You did well to see it. I almost didn't, and I'm paid to look. That's how it gets you here -- not by hiding, but by looking like weather.

objectives.text: Break the drake roost near the Pit
Recover a live tablet the drakes hoarded

greyhollow_side_what_the_drakes_carry.note: The drakes hoard live tablets -- orange under the sight -- stacked and sorted, from three houses near the Pit. A bird does not sort.
