objectives.text: Break the shades on the market canal
Recover the widow's deposit slip

drowned_quarter_canal_deposits_02.note: The slip was stamped three days before the woman was seen buying bread. The paperwork was never wrong. She was the error -- collected, and walking anyway, until the ledger noticed and reconciled her. Lupu has not gone back to the fish-market since.

drowned_quarter_pells_favor_01.title: The Broker's Errand
drowned_quarter_pells_favor_01.summary: Stelian trades in living goods in a district that mostly trades in the dead, and he survives by owing the dead broker favors. The broker wants a dossier fetched from a drowned safe-hold -- guarded, of course, by clerks who never left. Fetch it, and buy Stelian another month of breathing.
drowned_quarter_pells_favor_01.offer: You want to do business in the drowned quarter and keep a pulse? Then you owe the broker favors, and you pay them promptly. He wants a dossier out of a safe-hold that went under with the last high tide -- and the clerks that guarded it went under with it, and kept guarding. Fetch it. It buys me a month of not being a line item. I'll cut you in on the month.
drowned_quarter_pells_favor_01.progress: The dossier lies in the drowned safe-hold at the west sink. The clerks that guarded it are still at their posts. Persuade them otherwise and bring it up.
drowned_quarter_pells_favor_01.complete: You beauty. That's my month bought -- his favor paid, my name off his short list of overdue notices. It's an ugly way to live, trading breath for errands to a corpse. But every merchant on this canal does it, and the honest ones admit it. Here -- your cut, and my thanks, which down here is rarer than the coin.

objectives.label: the drowned safe-hold
objectives.text: Reach the drowned safe-hold at the west sink
Clear the clerks still guarding their posts

drowned_quarter_pells_favor_01.note: Stelian bought another month of breathing with an errand to a dead man. He said every living merchant on the canal keeps a tab with the broker. The honest ones, he said, admit the tab is the rent they pay to stay a person and not an entry.

drowned_quarter_pells_favor_02.title: Same Overdue Notice
drowned_quarter_pells_favor_02.summary: The broker taps his own debt-tablet when he's nervous -- checking he's still legally allowed to exist. Stelian asks the player to carry the broker a message: a name off an old ledger the broker has been dreading. Deliver it, and watch a dead man learn he was collected years before he thought.
drowned_quarter_pells_favor_02.offer: The broker keeps his own tablet in his coat and taps it when he's afraid -- like a man patting his pocket for a paper that says he's allowed to be here. He's been dreading a name off an old roll. I found it. I can't carry it to him; I owe him too much to be the one who says it. But you're new, and the dead go easier on strangers. Take the clerk the name, and let him tell the broker.
drowned_quarter_pells_favor_02.progress: Vlaicu at the ledger-office holds the name in his files. Speak with him, then it's the broker's to hear. Some errands you only run once.
drowned_quarter_pells_favor_02.complete: You told Vlaicu, and Vlaicu will tell the broker, and the broker will tap his tablet and say what he always says: you and me, we're the same overdue notice, difference is I stopped arguing with the math. He stopped. That's the whole horror of him. A man who read his own final total and just... agreed. Don't you agree. Whatever it costs. Don't you agree.

objectives.text: Give Vlaicu the name off the old roll

drowned_quarter_pells_favor_02.note: The broker keeps his own tablet in his coat and taps it to check he is still allowed to exist. He read his final total once and simply agreed with it. Stelian begged the player never to agree -- as if agreeing were the only death down here that stuck.

drowned_quarter_chalk_hand_01.title: The Chalk Hand
drowned_quarter_chalk_hand_01.summary: On a canal wall there is a child's chalk handprint -- the same small emblem that marked an orphanage a thousand years and a whole map away, the last unbroken echo of warm kindness. A fresh surveillance-mark has been chalked carefully around it. Petru wants to know if the mark protects the handprint or catalogues it.
drowned_quarter_chalk_hand_01.offer: Come and see, before the damp takes it. On the wall by the fish-stair there's a child's handprint in chalk -- small, you know, a child's -- and my grandmother's grandmother swore the same mark warmed an orphanage wall in the old country, west and a thousand years gone. Someone's chalked a careful ring around it. A watcher's mark. I can't tell if they're keeping it safe or keeping it filed. Look with me. I've looked alone too long.
drowned_quarter_chalk_hand_01.progress: The chalk handprint is on the canal wall by the fish-stair. Go and stand with Petru before it. Sometimes the errand is only to look, and to have looked together.
drowned_quarter_chalk_hand_01.complete: A ring drawn slow and careful, right around a child's hand. Protecting it or cataloguing it -- and I'll tell you the worst of it, the watcher who drew the ring doesn't know which either. She told me so herself. In this quarter even the kindnesses come stamped, and nobody can read their own stamp. Thank you for looking. It's less heavy, looked at by two.

objectives.label: the chalk handprint
objectives.text: Stand with Petru before the chalk hand

drowned_quarter_chalk_hand_01.note: A child's chalk hand on a canal wall, and a surveillance-ring drawn slow around it -- the last warm echo of a kindness a thousand years and a whole sea away. The watcher who drew the ring cannot say if she was guarding it or filing it. Neither could Petru. They looked at it together, which was something.

drowned_quarter_chalk_hand_02.title: Neither Is the Watcher
drowned_quarter_chalk_hand_02.summary: Serban drew the ring around the chalk hand and truly does not know why. The Morven-trained watcher asks the player to walk the district's other marks with him -- every careful chalk ring he has drawn and forgotten the reason for -- and help him decide, before his hand does it again, whether he protects this quarter or indexes it.
drowned_quarter_chalk_hand_02.offer: I drew the ring. Around the child's hand. I remember drawing it and I do not remember choosing to -- my hand knew a thing my head has lost. We were an order that listened to save people, once. Now we file them, and I cannot feel the difference anymore, and that is the wound I wear where feeling used to be. Walk my marks with me. Every ring I've drawn and forgotten. Help me read my own hand.
drowned_quarter_chalk_hand_02.progress: Serban's chalk rings ring the district -- pier, sink, and the old fish-market wall. Walk them with him at the market wall. Reading his own hand is a thing he cannot do alone.
drowned_quarter_chalk_hand_02.complete: There. Three rings, three walls, and not one of them tells me which I meant. To guard or to catalogue -- the same careful line, drawn by the same tired hand. My order taught the line and lost the reason a long time before I was born. I keep drawing it. I keep hoping this is the one where I remember it was mercy. Take the charm. It means nothing, deliberately. That's the only honest ward left down here.

objectives.label: the old fish-market wall
objectives.text: Walk Serban's chalk rings to the market wall
Help Serban read his own hand

drowned_quarter_chalk_hand_02.note: Serban drew the rings and lost the reasons. His order once listened to save people; now it files them, and the line for both is the same careful line. He keeps drawing it, hoping to find the one he meant as mercy. He has not found it. He keeps drawing.

drowned_quarter_seer_screams_01.title: Already Collected
drowned_quarter_seer_screams_01.summary: A blind seer with eyeless sight sits at the drowned crossing and screams one thing at everything that passes: already collected -- why is it still walking? His screaming is cracking a debt-node open, which weakens the machine, and drawing an enforcer who will finalize him for the truth. Serban asks the player to reach the seer before the enforcer does.
drowned_quarter_seer_screams_01.offer: There's a blind man at the drowned crossing who sees more with no eyes than the whole Morven order sees with theirs. He screams one thing at every soul that passes -- already collected, why is it still walking -- and he is right, every time, and it is destabilizing a debt-node, which is good, and it is drawing an enforcer to finalize him, which is not. Reach him first. The truth that could free this whole quarter is being screamed by the one man it will get killed.
drowned_quarter_seer_screams_01.progress: The seer sits at the drowned crossing, screaming the same true thing. Reach him before the enforcer does. His screaming holds a node open; his life holds the truth. You cannot save both cheaply.
drowned_quarter_seer_screams_01.complete: You reached him. Good. He's still screaming -- already collected, why is it still walking -- and the node's still cracking, and somewhere an enforcer is filing the paperwork to close his mouth for good. I can't tell you which to spend: the truth or the man. My order spent that choice a thousand years ago and chose the paperwork. Choose worse than we did. Please. Choose the man.

objectives.label: the drowned crossing
objectives.text: Reach the blind seer at the drowned crossing
Hold the crossing against the gathering deposits

drowned_quarter_seer_screams_01.note: A blind man screams the one true thing at the drowned crossing, and it is right every time. His voice cracks the machine open and draws the machine's enforcer down on him. The truth that frees the quarter is spoken by the man it will get killed. Serban begged for the man over the truth. His whole order, once, chose the truth.

drowned_quarter_sink_horror_01.title: The Sink That Grew Teeth
drowned_quarter_sink_horror_01.summary: Where the deepest canal drains, something has assembled itself from every unpaid death in a forgotten ledger -- a shape grown grotesquely literal, come for whoever holds the tablets. Bogdan's smithy backs onto the drain, and the water has started running against its own current. End the thing in the sink before it climbs into the district.
drowned_quarter_sink_horror_01.offer: My forge backs onto the deep drain, and for a week the water's run the wrong way -- uphill, against itself, like something below is breathing in. It's the sink. Something's grown down there, out of a whole ledger of deaths that never got paid, and it's come up looking for whoever's holding the tablets. That's you now, isn't it. I saw you carrying one. Go down and end it before it decides the whole quarter owes it. Take my heaviest hammer. It's honest iron. It never meant a thing but hitting.
drowned_quarter_sink_horror_01.progress: The thing has assembled itself where the deep canal drains. Go down to the sink and end it. Honest iron; heavy swing. It is a debt with a body -- give it a reason it can't file.
drowned_quarter_sink_horror_01.complete: It's down. Saints, the shape of it -- every unpaid death in that ledger, stacked into one wanting thing. And it came for the tablet-holder. That's the price of what you carry, traveler: the debts of this drowned place can smell it on you now. My iron held, though. Bog-iron never meant anything but nails, and the Stone can't corrupt a thing that never meant. Keep the blade. It'll not betray you. It doesn't know how.

objectives.label: the deep drain
objectives.text: Descend to the deep drain
End the Sink-Horror below

drowned_quarter_sink_horror_01.note: A whole ledger of unpaid deaths assembled itself in the drain and came up hunting whoever carried the tablets. It came for the player. Bogdan's honest iron held -- metal that never meant anything but nails, which is the one craft the Stone has never learned to turn. He gave the blade freely. It does not know how to betray.

drowned_quarter_honest_iron_01.title: The One Craft It Can't Subvert
drowned_quarter_honest_iron_01.summary: Bogdan is the last river-smith in the quarter still smelting bog-iron the ugly, honest way, and he is out of good scale from the shallows. The drowned clerks hoard river-metal in the flooded counting-house for reasons no living person understands. Bring him enough to keep the forge honest another season.
drowned_quarter_honest_iron_01.offer: Everything down here means something -- every stamp, every mark, every kindness comes filed. Not my forge. Bog-iron never meant a blessed thing but nails, and that's why the Stone's teeth slide right off it. But I'm out of good scale, and the drowned clerks in the flooded counting-house have been hoarding river-metal like it's coin -- can't spend it, can't eat it, just stack it. Fetch me a load. I'll make you something that never learned to lie.
drowned_quarter_honest_iron_01.progress: The clerks hoard river-metal in the flooded counting-house off the merchant stair. Gather good scale -- twelve lumps will see the forge through the season.
drowned_quarter_honest_iron_01.complete: Now that's honest metal. Ugly as sin and true as a nail. I don't know why the dead clerks stack it -- old habit, maybe, some ledger in their heads that still says metal is worth keeping. Doesn't matter. It's mine now, and it'll be nails and hinges and blades that hold. In a quarter that files its own children, traveler, an honest hinge is nearly a prayer. Here. Warm bread and a coin. The forge eats well tonight.

objectives.text: Gather good river-metal from the flooded counting-house

drowned_quarter_honest_iron_01.note: Bog-iron never meant anything but nails, and so the Stone's teeth slide off it -- the one craft in Draconia that cannot be turned. Bogdan keeps his forge honest with hoarded river-metal and does not ask why the dead stack it. In the drowned quarter, he said, an honest hinge is nearly a prayer.

drowned_quarter_warm_bowl_01.title: A Warm Bowl in a Cold Quarter
drowned_quarter_warm_bowl_01.summary: Vasile keeps the only warm room in the drowned quarter and refuses, on principle, to serve grey flat bread like the rest of the district. He needs clean salt hauled from the tidal flats to keep the brine off his stores. Fetch it, and earn a bowl of something that was recently, defiantly, alive.
drowned_quarter_warm_bowl_01.offer: Everybody in this quarter serves their bread flat and grey and won't meet your eye about it. Not my house. I keep one warm room on the whole canal and I mean to keep it warm if it kills me, which down here it might. But the brine's in my stores and I need clean salt off the flats to draw it out. Haul me a few sacks and there's a bowl of stew waiting -- real stew, with things in it that were walking last week. Spite, mostly. It's my secret ingredient.
drowned_quarter_warm_bowl_01.progress: Clean salt lies on the tidal flats past the low pier. Haul three sacks back before the tide turns. The stew keeps. Warmth is the whole point of it.
drowned_quarter_warm_bowl_01.complete: There we are. Salt in the stores, brine out, and one warm bowl in a quarter that forgot warmth was allowed. Sit. Eat. Don't thank me -- thank the spite. A man keeps a hearth lit in a place like this out of pure contrariness, and I'll not let the drowned quarter make me flat and grey and ashamed of my own bread. Everything good is temporary. That's exactly what makes it good. Eat while it's hot.

objectives.text: Haul clean salt from the tidal flats

drowned_quarter_warm_bowl_01.note: Vasile keeps the one warm room on the whole canal out of pure spite, and serves stew with things in it that were lately alive. He will not let the drowned quarter make him flat and grey. Everything good is temporary, he said, ladling. That is exactly what makes it good.

drowned_quarter_last_verse_01.title: A Tune Nobody Taught Her
drowned_quarter_last_verse_01.summary: A girl comes to Vasile's warm room each dusk and hums a tune no one taught her -- three notes that climb and refuse to resolve. Vasile, who has heard the district's stories, is afraid of what she's begun to hum along to. He asks the player to sit the dusk watch with her and listen for what answers.
drowned_quarter_last_verse_01.offer: There's a girl comes to my room at dusk -- won't eat, just hums. Three notes, climbing, and they never land on the fourth. Nobody taught it her. And lately, traveler, when she hums it, something out on the canal hums it back, a half-beat late, like an echo learning the words. I've heard enough stories to be afraid of a tune that gets answered. Sit with her tonight. Listen. Tell me it's only the water. Tell me and I'll believe you and sleep.
drowned_quarter_last_verse_01.progress: The girl hums at dusk in the corner of the warm room. Take the dusk watch beside her and listen for what hums back. Do not hum along. Whatever you do, do not learn the fourth note.
drowned_quarter_last_verse_01.complete: It answered. You heard it too. Three notes, climbing, and the canal hums them back and neither of them ever lands -- because the day they land, something's finished converging, and I don't want to be alive on the day a tune like that resolves. She won't stop. I won't make her. A child humming is the last warm thing in this quarter and I'll not be the man who takes it from her. Just... watch her. And count the notes. Always count. It's still three.

objectives.label: the warm room corner
objectives.text: Sit the dusk watch beside the humming girl

drowned_quarter_last_verse_01.note: A child hums three notes that climb and never land, and something on the canal hums them back a half-beat late. Vasile will not take the tune from her -- a humming child is the last warm thing on the canal. He only asks that someone count the notes. It is still three. He said it as a prayer: still three.

drowned_quarter_ferry_toll_01.title: Every Crossing Has a Price
drowned_quarter_ferry_toll_01.summary: The grey ferry that crosses the widest canal takes a toll no one remembers agreeing to, and lately it collects from passengers who never disembark. Nedelcu wants the toll-ledger recovered from the ferry-house before the count comes up a body short and names his dock. Fetch it from the crossing.
drowned_quarter_ferry_toll_01.offer: The grey ferry's been on this canal since before the district drowned, and it takes a toll nobody living recalls setting. Fine. But lately the count's gone strange -- boards more than it lands, and the difference doesn't wash up. When the numbers don't balance, the annex looks for someone to name, and my dock's nearest the crossing. Fetch me the ferry's toll-ledger before I'm the name that balances it. It's in the ferry-house at the wide crossing.
drowned_quarter_ferry_toll_01.progress: The toll-ledger sits in the ferry-house at the wide crossing. Recover it before the count comes up short. Watch the ferryman. He collects, they say, in more than coin.
drowned_quarter_ferry_toll_01.complete: Here's the ledger, and here's the strange of it: every crossing balanced. Boarded and landed, coin for coin -- except the landing's marked in a hand nobody's seen sign, and the passengers it lands aren't the ones that boarded. The ferry doesn't lose people, traveler. It exchanges them. And my dock's still clean, which tonight is as much luck as this quarter allows. Take the map-work. You've earned a road that's yours alone.

objectives.label: the ferry-house
objectives.text: Recover the ferry toll-ledger at the wide crossing
Clear the shades crowding the ferry-house

drowned_quarter_ferry_toll_01.note: The grey ferry does not lose its passengers. It exchanges them -- boards one soul and lands another, the books balanced in a hand no one has seen sign. Nedelcu's dock stayed clean this time. Every crossing has a price, the ferry-ledger read. It did not say the price was ever paid by the one who crossed.

drowned_quarter_thin_fog_01.title: Where the Fog Reads Orange
drowned_quarter_thin_fog_01.summary: Sorin has been mapping where the drowned-quarter fog turns colors under a reading-draught, and one patch reads shifting orange -- the signal writing itself into the water. He needs the reading confirmed at the source before he dares record it, because recording a live stone is how the network grows. Scan the orange fog and come back changed as little as possible.
drowned_quarter_thin_fog_01.offer: I map what the fog does under a reading-draught -- blue where the dead are thick, violet where the deep stone roots. There's a patch by the drowned wharf that reads shifting orange, and orange is the one color a chronicler prays never to write down. It means the signal's writing itself, live, into the water there. I have to confirm it before I record it -- and recording it might be what wakes it. Go and read it. Come back before you understand anything. Understanding is the death down here.
drowned_quarter_thin_fog_01.progress: The orange fog gathers by the drowned wharf. Read it with the draught and no more than read it. Do not linger. Do not transcribe. Come back before comprehension finds you.
drowned_quarter_thin_fog_01.complete: Orange. Shifting. Writing itself. I'll not record where -- an unwritten stone is a stone that hasn't yet taught anyone to find it. You look at your boots, I notice. Good. That's the reflex that keeps a chronicler breathing: read the land, note the color, look away before the angles pull the eye like a hook pulls a lip. This quarter is one long temptation to understand. Everyone who gave in is filed.

objectives.label: the drowned wharf
objectives.text: Read the orange fog by the drowned wharf

drowned_quarter_thin_fog_01.note: By the drowned wharf the fog reads shifting orange -- the signal writing itself into the water, live. Sorin refused to record the place; an unwritten stone teaches no one to find it. Read the land, note the color, look away. Everyone in the quarter who gave in to understanding is filed.

drowned_quarter_finalized_kin_01.title: The Ones Who Won't Lie Down
drowned_quarter_finalized_kin_01.summary: Iancu's whole street was finalized in a single tide -- collected, closed, filed -- and now his neighbors move like shut files through the rooms they died in. He cannot bring himself to end them, but he cannot watch them keep his mother's kettle warm either. He asks the player to do what a son cannot.
drowned_quarter_finalized_kin_01.offer: My street went under in one tide. All of them collected together -- the ledger likes a round number. And now they're... still here. Moving through their own rooms like closed files that forgot they're closed. My mother still sets her kettle. Still warms it. Still waits for water that's been copper since I was a boy. I can't put her down, traveler. A son can't. But I can't hear that kettle another night either. Go to the finalized rows. Do what I haven't the heart to.
drowned_quarter_finalized_kin_01.progress: The finalized keep their old streets in the sunk rows off the east stair. Do gently what Iancu cannot. They will not fight so much as continue. That is the worst of it.
drowned_quarter_finalized_kin_01.complete: Quiet now. The kettle's cold. Thank you -- no. Not thank you. There's no thank you for this. You did the thing a son couldn't and I'll carry that I couldn't, all my days, however few this quarter grants me. They didn't even fight, did they. Closed files don't fight. They just keep going till someone insists they stop. Somebody had to insist. It shouldn't have been a stranger. But I'm glad it was kind.

objectives.text: Lay Iancu's finalized neighbors to rest

drowned_quarter_finalized_kin_01.note: Iancu's street was finalized in one tide -- a round number for the ledger -- and his mother kept warming a kettle over copper water. He could not end her. A stranger did, and was kind about it. Closed files do not fight, Iancu said. They only continue until someone insists they stop.

drowned_quarter_marrow_pell_01.title: A Discrepancy of One's Own
drowned_quarter_marrow_pell_01.summary: The dead broker owes the Archive a debt he cannot pay because he is already dead -- a discrepancy the machine cannot finalize, exactly like the cracked tablet, exactly like the seer. He begs to have his file lost. Vlaicu can do it, but losing a debt at the top lands it on someone warm at the bottom. He wants the player to find who inherits it before he decides.
drowned_quarter_marrow_pell_01.offer: The broker came to me at last. Wants his file lost -- can't pay what he owes, being dead, and the being-dead is the whole discrepancy. I can lose it. I can un-file a corpse. But a debt forgiven at the top does not vanish, traveler; it is reassigned to a heartbeat. Somewhere a living family would wake owing what a dead man walked away from. Before I lose his file, find me the heir. I'll not do a mercy blind. I did that once. I still hear the kettle.
drowned_quarter_marrow_pell_01.progress: The reassignment falls to whoever the ledger names next of the broker's line. Find the heir among the living stalls of the fish-market, then bring Vlaicu the name. A mercy done blind is a cruelty with better manners.
drowned_quarter_marrow_pell_01.complete: So that's the heir. A fish-wife with three children and no notion she stands next in a dead man's book. Lose his file and she inherits his total; keep it filed and the broker stays a closed thing that walks, forever. There's no clean page here. There never is, in a ledger. I'll hold the stamp till you've decided you can live with either weight. Take the dossier -- you've read enough of the machine now to use its own hand against it.

objectives.label: the living fish-market
objectives.text: Find the broker's living heir at the fish-market
Bring Vlaicu the heir's name

drowned_quarter_marrow_pell_01.note: The broker begged to have his file lost. But a debt forgiven at the top lands on a heartbeat below -- a fish-wife with three children, next in a dead man's book and unaware. Mercy is a transfer, not a cancellation. Vlaicu held the stamp. He has done a blind mercy before, and still hears the kettle.

drowned_quarter_gaslight_01.title: The Light That Doesn't Reach the Ground
drowned_quarter_gaslight_01.summary: The drowned quarter's gaslight hangs high and never reaches the ground -- deliberately, the old guards say, so the district can pretend it doesn't see what walks the low fog. Three lamps have gone out on Lupu's beat, and the dark that filled them is not empty. Relight them, and mind what the light kept up out of reach.
drowned_quarter_gaslight_01.offer: You'll have noticed our lamps hang high and the light dies before it hits the cobbles. That's on purpose, old hands say -- a district decides not to see its own low fog, and the lamps agree to help. Three have gone dark on my beat, though, and the dark they left isn't just dark. Something's been living in the gap the light used to hold. Relight the lamps at the low fog. Slowly. Don't run when they catch. Running teaches the dark where the door is.
drowned_quarter_gaslight_01.progress: Three lamps stand cold in the low fog off the sink stair. Relight them, and put down what moved into the dark they left. Do not run when they catch.
drowned_quarter_gaslight_01.complete: Lit again -- and you saw what the light had been keeping up off the ground all along, didn't you. That's why they hang the lamps high. Not to see by. To keep a ceiling on the fog. A whole quarter agreed, some tide long past, that it was better not to look down. I break that agreement three lamps a night, and every night it re-forms. That's the drowned quarter: an argument the dark keeps winning by patience.

objectives.label: the low fog
objectives.text: Relight the three cold lamps in the low fog
Put down what moved into the dark

drowned_quarter_gaslight_01.note: The quarter hangs its gaslight high so the light dies before the ground -- a district agreeing, long ago, not to see its own low fog. Lupu relights three lamps a night and every night the dark re-forms. The drowned quarter, he said, is an argument the dark keeps winning by patience.

drowned_quarter_grey_wood_01.title: Cut From Wood That Never Finished
drowned_quarter_grey_wood_01.summary: The quarter's piers and planking are cut from grey wood that never quite finished being alive -- the dead descendants of the old world's dying forests. Bogdan needs sound pilings pulled from the collapsed pier to shore up the fish-stair, but the grey wood remembers being a tree and does not like being disturbed. Gather what's still sound.
drowned_quarter_grey_wood_01.offer: Look at our pilings. Grey wood -- cut from forests that a thousand years back were only going grey, and here they've finished the job: dead, but never quite done dying. The fish-stair's giving way and I need sound timber to shore it, but the grey wood remembers being a tree, and it groans when you pull it, like waking something from a sleep it wanted. Gather me the sound pilings off the collapsed pier. Bring them slow. It hates to be hurried into being useful.
drowned_quarter_grey_wood_01.progress: Sound grey-wood pilings lie in the wreck of the collapsed pier by the north stair. Gather eight. Draw them slow; the wood remembers being alive and resents the reminder.
drowned_quarter_grey_wood_01.complete: Sound enough. Grey and groaning and never quite dead, but it'll hold the fish-stair up another year. Strange trade, isn't it -- shoring the living up on wood that's still half-dreaming it's a forest. My grandfather said the old west had trees older than the four thrones. This is what became of them. Cut into pilings for a drowned city, still remembering the green. I try to be gentle with it. It's owed that much.

objectives.text: Gather sound grey-wood pilings from the collapsed pier

drowned_quarter_grey_wood_01.note: The quarter's piers are cut from grey wood that never finished dying -- the last of forests older than the four thrones, now planking for a drowned city and still half-dreaming green. Bogdan draws it slow, and gentle. It is owed that much, he said, for remembering.

drowned_quarter_undelivered_01.title: Still Waiting on an Answer
drowned_quarter_undelivered_01.summary: A sealed letter has sat in the merchant's lost-post drawer for longer than anyone can date -- the carrier stopped to read a wall and never delivered it, and somewhere a name still waits on the answer inside. Stelian cannot bring himself to open a letter a man died not-reading. He asks the player to carry it, sealed, to whoever the ledger says still waits.
drowned_quarter_undelivered_01.offer: There's a letter in my lost-post drawer, sealed, buckles done up, and it's outlasted three of my ledgers. The carrier stopped to read a wall, they say, and never got up. Died of paying attention. And here's what won't let me sleep: somewhere there's a name on the recipient roll still waiting on the answer inside. Waiting a lifetime. I can't open it -- a man died not-reading it, and I'll not undo his last good sense. Carry it sealed to whoever still waits. Let the waiting end. Not the seal.
drowned_quarter_undelivered_01.progress: The recipient still waits, the roll says, at the old post-stair. Carry the letter sealed -- do not read it; the carrier's whole death is the case against reading. Deliver it and let the waiting, if not the letter, close.
drowned_quarter_undelivered_01.complete: Delivered. Sealed, as it came, as it should. The one who waited... you saw them. Waited so long the waiting became the whole of them. Now the answer's in their hands and they'll not open it either -- some seals, once you've carried them far enough, you understand are the point. The carrier knew. Died knowing. In this quarter of opened books and filed lives, one sealed letter, delivered whole, is nearly a decent thing. Nearly. Thank you.

objectives.label: the old post-stair
objectives.text: Deliver the sealed letter to the one who waits

drowned_quarter_undelivered_01.note: A letter its carrier died not-delivering -- he stopped to read a wall, and paying attention was the wound. Somewhere a name waited a lifetime on the answer inside. The player carried it sealed to the end. Some seals, delivered far enough, are understood to be the point. The waiting closed. The letter never will.

drowned_quarter_night_watch_01.title: Count the Deposits
drowned_quarter_night_watch_01.summary: The night canal patrol never ends, only rotates. Grigore posts the standing order: walk the disposal reach, thin the deposits the slow water has gathered since dusk, and log the count for the annex before they invent a number of their own.
drowned_quarter_night_watch_01.offer: Standing order, same as every night: the slow water's gathered its dead since dusk and someone has to thin them before the annex counts the district short and starts asking whose. Walk the disposal reach. Break what's crowded up. Bring me a true count -- because if I don't hand up a number, they'll write one, and their numbers always name a name. Go on. The canal doesn't rest, so neither do we.
drowned_quarter_night_watch_01.progress: The deposits gather on the disposal reach off the low stair, thicker every hour past dusk. Thin them and bring Grigore a true count before the annex writes a false one.
drowned_quarter_night_watch_01.complete: A true number. Good. I'll log it before the annex dreams up a rounder one. You'd think a man got used to counting the dead like fish in a weir. You do. That's the part that ought to frighten us both and stopped frightening me last winter. Same time tomorrow. The water gathers whether we count it or not -- but counted, at least it's ours, and not theirs to name a scapegoat by.

objectives.text: Thin the night's deposits on the disposal reach

drowned_quarter_night_watch_01.note: The night canal gathers its dead whether or not anyone counts. Grigore counts anyway -- a true number handed up before the annex invents a false one, because their numbers always name a name. Counted, he said, the dead are at least ours, and not theirs to blame someone by.

drowned_quarter_copy_desk_01.title: The Annex Wants Its Pages
drowned_quarter_copy_desk_01.summary: The copy-desk never clears. Each day the drowned annex demands its ledgers recopied before the salt-rot takes the ink, and each day the clerks that guard the wet stacks must be moved aside first. Vlaicu sets the day's quota: clear the stacks, and mark one road wrong on purpose.
drowned_quarter_copy_desk_01.offer: Day's work, same shape as yesterday's: the annex wants its ledgers recopied before the brine eats the ink, and the drowned clerks guarding the wet stacks want otherwise. Move them. Copy the board. And -- quietly -- I draw one road wrong on every fair copy I hand up. One. A small lie folded into all their true ones, so the annex never has a map that's entirely theirs. Do the day's clearing and I'll teach you the trick of it.
drowned_quarter_copy_desk_01.progress: The wet stacks sit in the drowned annex off the east canal, guarded as ever. Clear the clerks so the copying can go on. The one wrong road is Vlaicu's to place; the clearing is yours.
drowned_quarter_copy_desk_01.complete: Stacks cleared, pages saved, and one road drawn wrong for the day -- my single act of resistance, and small enough the machine's never noticed it in a hundred years of copying. That's how you survive a ledger, traveler: you don't smash it, you feed it one lie a day and keep breathing. Here's the day's map. The wrong road on it is yours to know and no one else's. Come back tomorrow. The brine never rests, and neither, more's the pity, do I.

objectives.text: Clear the clerks from the wet stacks

drowned_quarter_copy_desk_01.note: Vlaicu draws one road wrong on every fair copy the annex takes -- a single lie folded into a hundred years of true ones, so the machine never holds a map entirely its own. You survive a ledger, he said, by feeding it one lie a day and keeping your pulse. It is the smallest resistance in Draconia. It is still resistance.

drowned_quarter_pit_node_01.title: The Pit Beneath the Quarter
drowned_quarter_pit_node_01.summary: The quarter's central node is the drowned descendant of an old southern debt-pit: no longer molten labor but cold clerical finality, where accounts and account-holders are settled into the black water. The seer's screaming has cracked it ajar. Serban asks the player to descend to the pit-rim and read how far the crack has spread before the annex seals it and the truth with it.
drowned_quarter_pit_node_01.offer: Under this whole quarter there's a pit -- the drowned grandchild of a burning one they dug in the south, a thousand years and a whole cruelty ago. It doesn't melt people now. It files them, cold, into the black water: accounts and account-holders settled together. The seer's screaming has cracked it ajar, and a cracked node is the one place the machine can be hurt. Go down to the pit-rim. Read how far the crack runs. Read it -- and do not, whatever it offers, agree to rest.
drowned_quarter_pit_node_01.progress: The pit-rim lies at the drowned heart of the quarter, cracked ajar by the seer's cry. Descend and read the crack. It will offer you rest. It offers everyone rest. Decline, and come back.
drowned_quarter_pit_node_01.complete: You read it and came back. That's rarer than you know -- the pit's whole talent is making rest sound like the only door left, and it says so in the flattest, kindest voice, and it never lies, which is what makes it terrible. The crack's spreading. Good. A settled thing that comes unsettled is the closest this drowned quarter gets to hope. It won't last. Nothing good here does. But it cracked, and you saw it crack. Hold onto having seen it.

objectives.label: the drowned pit-rim
objectives.text: Descend to the pit-rim and read the crack
Face what the crack let up

drowned_quarter_pit_node_01.note: Beneath the quarter, a pit files people cold into black water -- the drowned grandchild of a burning southern one. The seer's cry cracked it ajar, and a settled thing come unsettled is the nearest thing to hope down here. The pit offers rest in the kindest, flattest voice, and never lies. The player declined, and saw it crack, and held onto having seen.

drowned_quarter_wrong_current_01.title: The Water Runs Against Itself
drowned_quarter_wrong_current_01.summary: Petru has fished the quarter's canals his whole life and knows their currents like his own pulse -- and for a fortnight the water by the drowned wharf has run uphill, against the tide, against sense. He is too frightened to look alone at what makes water climb. He asks the player to walk the wrong current with him to its source.
drowned_quarter_wrong_current_01.offer: I've fished these canals since I could hold a line, and I know which way water goes the way I know my own heartbeat. For a fortnight now, the reach by the drowned wharf runs uphill. Against the tide. Water doesn't do that, traveler -- not unless something below wants it to, something drawing breath. I can't walk to the source alone. A man my age, his heart's not up to seeing what makes a canal climb. Walk it with me. I'd sooner know the shape of it than lie awake guessing.
drowned_quarter_wrong_current_01.progress: The wrong current runs uphill toward the drowned wharf. Walk it with Petru to its source and see what draws the water against the tide. Some things a man would rather see than imagine.
drowned_quarter_wrong_current_01.complete: So that's the shape of it. Water climbing toward the thing in the sink like it's being breathed in. I've fished past that wharf ten thousand times and never once feared the water till it started disobeying itself. Now I'll not put a line in past the second bridge. Thank you for walking it. Knowing's heavy, but guessing was heavier -- I lay awake building it worse than it is. It's bad enough true. It usually is.

objectives.label: the source of the wrong current
objectives.text: Walk the uphill current to its source
Clear the shades drawn to the climbing water

drowned_quarter_wrong_current_01.note: For a fortnight the canal by the drowned wharf ran uphill, breathed in toward the thing below. Petru, who knew the currents like his pulse, feared the water only once it disobeyed itself. Knowing was heavy. Guessing, he found, was heavier -- he had built it worse than the truth. It was bad enough true. It usually is.

drowned_quarter_last_verse_02.title: Something to Set Beside the Tune
drowned_quarter_last_verse_02.summary: Vasile cannot stop the humming girl, so he means to out-warm it -- to fill his room with enough small, loud, living kindness that a child has better things to hum along to. He asks the player to gather what a real hearth-night needs from the living market, and help him throw the one warm evening the drowned quarter will allow.
drowned_quarter_last_verse_02.offer: I can't take the tune from her and I won't try. So I'll drown it -- not in the canal way, the other way. A room so warm and loud with living kindness that a child forgets to answer the water. I need what a real hearth-night wants: honest bread, a fiddle, salt for the pot, and folk brave enough to be happy out loud for one evening in a quarter that files joy as a discrepancy. Gather it from the living market. Then come sing off-key with the rest of us. It's the whole cure I've got.
drowned_quarter_last_verse_02.progress: The living market off the merchant stair has what a hearth-night needs. Gather the salt and honest bread, then bring it to the warm room. The cure is warmth, thrown loud, on purpose.
drowned_quarter_last_verse_02.complete: Listen to that. A whole room warm and loud and off-key, and the girl -- did you see -- she hummed with US for one verse. Ours, not the water's. One evening we out-shouted the drowned quarter, and tomorrow it'll be grey again, and that's all right. That's the whole of it, traveler. You don't beat a place like this. You steal one warm night from it and you call the theft a victory, because down here it is one. Everything good is temporary. Sing anyway.

objectives.text: Gather what a hearth-night needs from the living market

objectives.label: the warm room
objectives.text: Bring it to Vasile's warm room

drowned_quarter_last_verse_02.note: Vasile could not take the tune from the child, so he out-warmed it -- one room loud and off-key and defiantly alive, and for a single verse the girl hummed with them, and not with the water. You do not beat the drowned quarter, he said. You steal one warm night and call the theft a victory. Down here, he was right, it is one.

transcub_vale_ivy_priest_confession.title: The Priest Who Answers Walls
transcub_vale_ivy_priest_confession.summary: The vale keeps a church older than Blestem's first wall, roofless now under the ivy, and its last priest confesses to the stones and swears the stones confess back. The chronicler wants a witness before he writes the man down as mad.
transcub_vale_ivy_priest_confession.offer: There is a church up the vale the ivy has been eating for two hundred years, and one priest left in it who hears confession from the walls and answers them out loud. I would call him mad and be done, but lately he answers questions no living man asked him. Go up. Stand in the nave. Tell me what you hear -- and whether the wall waits for its turn to speak.
transcub_vale_ivy_priest_confession.progress: The old Transcub church sits up the northern fold of the vale, past where the lichen glows on wet stone. Brother's priest keeps its floor. Listen there.
transcub_vale_ivy_priest_confession.complete: Warm under the altar, you say. In a church with no roof, through two hundred winters of rain, and the one flat stone under the altar warm to the hand. I will not write him mad. I will write nothing at all. Some pages the vale is better off not keeping.

objectives.label: the ivy-eaten nave
objectives.text: Stand in the roofless nave

objectives.label: the warm altar-stone
objectives.text: Read the altar-stone by sight

transcub_vale_ivy_priest_confession.note: The priest confesses to the walls and the walls confess back. Under the altar the stone is warm, roof or none. Barbu wrote nothing. Some pages the vale keeps better lost.

transcub_vale_rows_of_twelve.title: Rows of Twelve
transcub_vale_rows_of_twelve.summary: Blestem buries by ledger, twelve to a row and no name cut deeper than the count. A new row opened in the vale this week that no clerk in the city will own. The gate-guard wants to know who is filling it before it fills again.
transcub_vale_rows_of_twelve.offer: You will have seen the grave-rows coming up. Twelve to a row, always twelve, that is how the city keeps its dead -- by the count, never the name. A new row opened this week and the last three plots are dug and empty, waiting. No clerk in Blestem signed the order. Walk the row. Tell me how many are filled, and whether the empty ones are meant for anyone I would know.
transcub_vale_rows_of_twelve.progress: The new grave-row runs the eastern verge of the vale, below the ridge track. Count it, and mind the diggers do not count you.
transcub_vale_rows_of_twelve.complete: Nine filled, three waiting. Nine, and not one stone cut deeper than the tally, and the three empties squared off neat as a debt not yet called. I have been a guard here eleven years. I know when a hole is dug for a stranger and when it is dug for a neighbour. These three are the second kind. Take your pay before I lose the nerve to give it.

objectives.label: the new grave-row
objectives.text: Walk the row of twelve
Break the stone wardens that guard the digging

transcub_vale_rows_of_twelve.note: Nine filled, three waiting, no name cut deeper than the count. The three empty plots were squared for neighbours, not strangers. Ilie took his pay and stopped counting aloud.

transcub_vale_lichen_harvest.title: Wet-Stone Light
transcub_vale_lichen_harvest.summary: The pale lichen that glows on the vale's wet stone is the one honest export the fog cannot listen to. Dorin scrapes it for lamp-oil that never made a soul rich and never got anyone filed either. He needs a strong back to reach the high ledges.
transcub_vale_lichen_harvest.offer: There is a light grows on the wet stone up here -- soft, blue-white, no smoke, no flame. My grandmother scraped it for lamp-light and hers before that, and in all those years it made nobody rich and got nobody filed, which up the vale is the same as being blessed. My knees have quit the high ledges. Fill this creel with good culture and there is warm bread in it and no questions asked, which is the rarer coin.
transcub_vale_lichen_harvest.progress: The best lichen glows on the north ledges where the fog wets the stone through. Scrape the pale culture, not the grey.
transcub_vale_lichen_harvest.complete: Look at it glow in the creel. Enough to light the low houses a month, and every lamp of it a thing the city cannot hear, cannot price, cannot put a row of twelve to. My grandmother called it the poor man's honesty. Eat. You have earned the loaf and the quiet both.

objectives.text: Scrape glowing lichen from the ledges

transcub_vale_lichen_harvest.note: The pale lichen glows without smoke and without a ledger. Dorin lit the low houses a month on light the city could not price. His grandmother called it the poor man's honesty.

transcub_vale_the_dossier.title: A Book of Small Habits
transcub_vale_the_dossier.summary: A dossier fell off a courier's saddle in the vale -- a book of one man's small habits, when he eats, which stair he favours, the name he says in sleep. Three buyers already want it. Toma wants you to decide who never should have it.
transcub_vale_the_dossier.offer: A courier dropped a book on the vale road and did not go back for it, which tells you the book was worth more lost than carried. It is a dossier -- one man's habits, nothing grand, when he takes his bread, which stair he trusts, the name he mutters asleep. In Blestem that is a knife with the handle already fitted to your hand. Three parties have asked me for it since morning. Read it. Then tell me who gets it -- or whether the fire does.
transcub_vale_the_dossier.progress: Bring the dossier back to Toma's stall by the vale crossing and say who is to have it: the subject, his rival, or the flame.
transcub_vale_the_dossier.complete: The fire, then. You are the first to say the fire. The rival would have paid triple and the subject would have paid double that to bury it, and you fed it to the coals for nothing. In Blestem that is not thrift, friend, that is a confession -- you have told me exactly what you value, and I will not forget it. Here. Coin for a man who cannot be bought is coin well spent.

objectives.text: Recover the fallen dossier
Decide the dossier's fate with Toma

transcub_vale_the_dossier.note: The dossier held one man's small habits, which in Blestem is a fitted knife. It went to the fire for nothing, and Toma marked that down too. In this vale, refusing to sell is the loudest thing you can say.

transcub_vale_gargoyles_of_the_spire.title: What the Spire Sheds
transcub_vale_gargoyles_of_the_spire.summary: The stone wardens that ring the old spire have begun stepping down from their perches at dusk and standing in the vale, facing the same direction. The smith wants their basalt hearts -- and a straight answer about what they are all watching.
transcub_vale_gargoyles_of_the_spire.offer: The gargoyles up on the spire's shoulder are meant to sit and shed rain, and for a hundred years that is all they did. Now at dusk they climb down. They stand in the low field, all of them, turned the same way, watching a stretch of empty ground as patient as a queue. I want their basalt for a tempering nobody in the city can match. Break six. And while you are close -- look where they look, and tell me it is empty.
transcub_vale_gargoyles_of_the_spire.progress: The gargoyles descend at dusk into the field below the spire. Break six, and mark the ground they face.
transcub_vale_gargoyles_of_the_spire.complete: Six hearts of good black basalt, and you saw it too. The ground they face is bare. Bare and warm, you said. A hundred stone things climbing down each night to keep a vigil over a warm patch of nothing. I will make you a blade of them so cold it forgets the vale ever heated. That much I can do about it. That much only.

objectives.text: Break the descended gargoyles

objectives.label: the ground they face
objectives.text: Read the ground they all face

transcub_vale_gargoyles_of_the_spire.note: The spire's gargoyles climb down at dusk to keep vigil over a bare warm patch of nothing. Serban forged their hearts into something cold. It was the only thing he could do about it, and he knew it.

transcub_vale_ridge_harpies.title: The Ones That Carry Off the Quiet
transcub_vale_ridge_harpies.summary: The harpies of the upper ridge have started taking the confessors -- the folk who climb to the roofless church to say the thing they cannot say in the city. They take them from the church steps, and the steps stay warm. Thin the flock before the confessions stop climbing.
transcub_vale_ridge_harpies.offer: People climb to the old church to say the thing the city walls would carry -- the harpies of the high ridge have learned it. They wait above the nave now, and take the confessors off the steps at dusk, and the steps stay warm a long while after. It is the one place in the vale a soul could be honest and unheard, and the flock has made a larder of it. Thin them. Ten wings, and maybe a body can climb up there again to be cruel to itself in peace, the way the old god meant.
transcub_vale_ridge_harpies.progress: The ridge-harpies roost above the roofless church on the north fold. Cut ten from the flock.
transcub_vale_ridge_harpies.complete: Ten down, and the church steps free again for whoever needs them. It is a small thing to make a place safe for confession -- the safest thing left in the vale is a roofless ruin where you can hate yourself unwatched. The old god of this church punished cruelty turned inward. The new one seems to require it. Take the striker. You have kept a door open that the city would rather see shut.

objectives.text: Thin the flock above the church

transcub_vale_ridge_harpies.note: The harpies had made a larder of the confessors' steps. Marcu's coin bought the last honest silence in the vale. The old god punished cruelty turned inward; the new one seems to require it.

transcub_vale_warm_stone_scan.title: The Ground Keeps Its Own Counsel
transcub_vale_warm_stone_scan.summary: The chronicler has been keeping a quiet record of where the vale runs warm -- under the altar, under the gargoyles' bare field, under a grave-row's third empty plot. He wants the three read by alchemical sight, together, to learn if the warmth is scattered or aimed.
transcub_vale_warm_stone_scan.offer: I keep a record no one asked for. Where the vale runs warm through the frost -- and it runs warm in more places each month. Under the church altar. Under the bare field the stone things watch. Under the third empty plot in the new grave-row. Three warm places, and I am too old to walk between them fast enough to know if they are three or if they are one. Read them by the shifting-sight. Green is a stone waking. Tell me they are only three.
transcub_vale_warm_stone_scan.progress: Read all three warm sites: the altar, the watched field, the empty grave-plot. Read them by day, when the frost shows the warmth clearest.
transcub_vale_warm_stone_scan.complete: All three green. All three, and set on a line if a man draws one between them -- a line that points off the vale toward the grave under Black Night, the way everything here eventually points. Not three warm places. One warm thing, patient, spread thin under the whole vale like a hand under a blanket. I have kept records forty years. This is the first I am afraid to file.

objectives.label: the warm altar
objectives.text: Read the warm altar-stone

objectives.label: the watched field
objectives.text: Read the watched field

objectives.label: the empty grave-plot
objectives.text: Read the empty grave-plot

transcub_vale_warm_stone_scan.note: Three warm sites, all green, all set on one line pointing off the vale toward the grave. Not three warm places -- one warm thing spread thin beneath the whole vale, like a hand under a blanket. Barbu was afraid to file it.

transcub_vale_the_walled.title: The One in the Wall
transcub_vale_the_walled.summary: Blestem does not write down its cruelest sentence: to be mortared alive into a listening-wall until you become its ear. Florea's brother went into a vale wall two winters back. She swears it still knows her voice.
transcub_vale_the_walled.offer: There is a punishment the city has no word for, because a word could be carried and the whole point is that it cannot. They put you in the wall. Alive, and mortared, until the wall hears with you and you are only its ear. My brother went into a vale wall two winters back for informing on the informers. I have not been able to go near it. But it knows my voice -- I have heard the mortar shift when I weep in that lane. Go where I cannot. Tell me if there is any of him left to say sorry to.
transcub_vale_the_walled.progress: The listening-wall runs the blind lane on the vale's south side, where no street crosses it. Stand close, and listen for what answers.
transcub_vale_the_walled.complete: It shifted for you too, then. Not words -- there are no words left in him, if he is in there at all. Only the mortar breathing, patient, the way this whole vale breathes now. I will stop going to the lane. Whatever answers is not my brother wanting comfort. It is the wall wanting more voices, and I have grieved too loud too long already. Take this. It kept a name once. Keep yours quiet.

objectives.label: the blind lane
objectives.text: Stand at the listening-wall

objectives.label: the mortared listening-wall
objectives.text: Read the wall by sub-terrestrial sight

transcub_vale_the_walled.note: Her brother was mortared into a listening-wall for informing on the informers. The mortar still shifts at a weeping voice -- not for comfort, but for more voices to hear with. Florea stopped going to the lane.

transcub_vale_market_of_ears.title: Priced by the Ear
transcub_vale_market_of_ears.summary: A fence in the lower market pays more for a kindness than a threat, because a warm story is rare enough in the vale to be worth killing for. Toma wants three overheard kindnesses gathered and sold -- and wants to see whether you understand why they are dangerous.
transcub_vale_market_of_ears.offer: Down where I trade, coin is the soft currency and what folk say when they think the wall is asleep is the hard one. Odd thing: the best fence in the market pays more for a kindness overheard than a cruelty. A threat, everyone has. A tenderness -- who tends whom, who forgives whom, who would break a rule for love -- that is the rarest leverage there is, because it tells you exactly where a careful person is soft. Gather me three. I will pay, and I will watch how it sits with you to sell them.
transcub_vale_market_of_ears.progress: Overhear three kindnesses in the vale -- who tends whom, who forgives whom -- and bring them to Toma's stall.
transcub_vale_market_of_ears.complete: Three kindnesses, priced and sold. You did it clean, and you did not like it, and both of those I noticed. That is the whole trade in one lesson: in this vale the tender thing is the killing thing, because a wall that hears you love someone has just been handed the shape of your throat. You will sleep worse tonight for the coin. Good. The ones who sleep fine are the ones the city trusts.

objectives.text: Gather three overheard kindnesses
Sell the kindnesses to Toma

transcub_vale_market_of_ears.note: In the vale the tender thing is the killing thing: a wall that hears you love someone has the shape of your throat. Toma paid well for three kindnesses, and watched to see if the buyer slept afterward.

transcub_vale_incurious_draught.title: The Draught for Not Wanting to Know
transcub_vale_incurious_draught.summary: The innkeeper brews a bitter draught that dulls curiosity -- the one appetite the vale cannot afford. His stores ran dry the same week the church stone went warm. He needs the ingredients gathered before his regulars start reading things they should not.
transcub_vale_incurious_draught.offer: I keep a bitter draught behind the bar that no other inn on the continent would stock: it dulls the wanting-to-know. Sounds a poor sort of medicine until you have watched a curious man read the wrong stone and go quiet in the wrong way. My stores ran dry the same week the church went warm, which I do not think is luck. Gather what I need -- lichen for the base, gargoyle-basalt ground fine for the bitter -- before some clever regular of mine decides to understand something. Understanding is what kills them here.
transcub_vale_incurious_draught.progress: Gather lichen culture and grind gargoyle basalt for the innkeeper's incurious draught.
transcub_vale_incurious_draught.complete: Enough to keep the bar dull-witted a season, thank the vale. You look at me like a healer ought to want folk clever. Not here. Here the clever ones read the warm stones and understand the marks and then they are found with clean fingernails and no wound and a face like they finally got the joke. My draught keeps them stupid and breathing. I will take stupid and breathing every night this place is open.

objectives.text: Gather lichen for the draught's base
Break gargoyles for grinding-basalt

transcub_vale_incurious_draught.note: The innkeeper brews a draught that dulls curiosity, the one appetite the vale cannot afford. The clever ones read the warm stones, understand, and are found unwounded with clean fingernails. He keeps his regulars stupid and breathing on purpose.

transcub_vale_the_courier_run.title: Carry It Sealed
transcub_vale_the_courier_run.summary: A courier died on the vale road with a sealed satchel and no wound, clean fingernails, the wax unbroken. The satchel must reach the church priest, still sealed. The road offers you a chance to read it at every rest. The whole test is not reading it.
transcub_vale_the_courier_run.offer: We found a courier on the vale road cold as the frost, no wound on him, fingernails clean, and the satchel still sealed on his back. He died of the carrying, not the road. Whatever is in there wants reading, and the reading is what killed him. It has to go to the church priest up the north fold -- he knows how to take a thing like this and not open it, which is a craft. Carry it sealed. It will offer itself to you at every rest. The offer is the danger. Do not accept.
transcub_vale_the_courier_run.progress: Carry the sealed satchel to the priest at the roofless church. Do not break the seal, whatever it promises at the resting-places.
transcub_vale_the_courier_run.complete: Sealed. You brought it up the whole vale and it never got its hooks in you. The priest took it the way he takes everything -- gently, unread, straight to the warm stone under his altar, where he says it will lie and hum and bother no one who is careful. You could have read it. It would have told you true things; the stone never lies. And you would have joined the courier in the frost with clean hands. You chose not to know. Up here, that is the only heroism the vale has left.

objectives.text: Carry the sealed satchel to the priest, unread

objectives.label: the roofless church
objectives.text: Deliver it to the church

transcub_vale_the_courier_run.note: The courier died of carrying, not the road -- no wound, clean fingernails, the seal unbroken. Carried sealed to the priest and never read, though it offered itself at every rest. The stone never lies. That is exactly why you do not read it.

transcub_vale_two_confessions.title: The Priest and the Clerk
transcub_vale_two_confessions.summary: The vale's priest wants a warm confession copied down and kept, as the old faith demands -- the sin admitted, the self forgiven. A city clerk wants the same confession filed as leverage. Both ask through the guard. You cannot serve both.
transcub_vale_two_confessions.offer: Two men want the same page and only one can have it. Brother priest up the church wants a confession I overheard -- wants it written and kept as the old faith says, so the man who confessed can be forgiven and go on. The clerk from the city wants it too, filed, so the man can be owned. Same words, two uses: absolution or leverage. I am only a gate-guard. I brought it to you because you have a look like someone who knows the difference costs. Choose. Whoever you refuse will remember it.
transcub_vale_two_confessions.progress: Take the confession to the priest for keeping, or to the clerk for filing. You cannot do both.
transcub_vale_two_confessions.complete: The priest, then. It is written in his gentle hand now and read back to the man who said it, and the man wept and went home lighter, and the clerk went home with nothing and a long memory. You spent a piece of leverage to buy a stranger one clean night's sleep. That is a bad trade in Blestem and a good one in the church, and up this vale those are increasingly the same place. Take the token. It is a name the clerk will not get to keep.

objectives.text: Bring the confession to the priest for keeping

transcub_vale_two_confessions.note: The same confession could absolve a man or own him. Given to the priest, it bought a stranger one clean night's sleep and cost a piece of leverage. In the vale, the church and the losing trade are increasingly the same place.

transcub_vale_blackglass_debt.title: Glass Traded Under Duress
transcub_vale_blackglass_debt.summary: Blackglass comes up from the southern forges as tribute the vale pretends is trade. This season's crates came short, and the shortfall will be paid in names, not coin, unless the smith can prove the theft was the harpies' and not a neighbour's.
transcub_vale_blackglass_debt.offer: The blackglass we work comes up from Sangeroasa's forges, and we call it trade to keep our faces, but it is tribute and everyone knows it. This season's crates came in short. Blestem does not take a shortfall in coin -- it takes it in names, one neighbour handed up per missing crate, and the clerk already has his quill wet. I say the harpies took the shortfall to line their roosts; they hoard anything that shines. Bring me blackglass from the ridge nests. Prove it was birds, not a man, before a man gets filed for it.
transcub_vale_blackglass_debt.progress: The harpies line their ridge roosts with anything that shines. Recover the stolen blackglass from the nests.
transcub_vale_blackglass_debt.complete: Blackglass, and plenty -- pulled straight from the harpy roosts, still bright. Enough to make the tally whole and put the clerk's quill back in the pot. No name goes up the road this season. You understand what you have actually done here? You have stolen a debt back from a bird before a man could be sold to cover it. That is the whole moral economy of the vale in one errand. Take the plate. It was going to be tribute too.

objectives.text: Recover stolen blackglass from harpy roosts
Clear the hoarding harpies

transcub_vale_blackglass_debt.note: A short blackglass tribute would have been paid in a neighbour's name, not coin. The shortfall was the harpies' hoard all along. Serban made the tally whole and stole a debt back from a bird before a man could be sold to cover it.

transcub_vale_chalk_confession.title: I Only Wanted to Know
transcub_vale_chalk_confession.summary: A confession is scratched into the Transcub altar in a shaking hand: 'I only wanted to know what it said.' The chronicler wants it copied for his record -- but copying an inscription is how the trouble spreads. He needs someone to read it once and refuse to keep it.
transcub_vale_chalk_confession.offer: There is a confession cut into the altar in a hand that shook cutting it: I only wanted to know what it said. Six words, and the stone under them warm, and I want them for my record the way I want every true thing -- and I have lived long enough to know the wanting is the trap. Copying an inscription is how it spreads; the ink carries what the stone carried. Go and read it once, for me, with your own eyes. Then come back and tell me you did not copy it down. I need to hear a person say that out loud.
transcub_vale_chalk_confession.progress: Read the shaking-hand confession on the church altar. Read it once. Do not copy it.
transcub_vale_chalk_confession.complete: You read it and you did not keep it. Say it again -- I need it twice. 'I only wanted to know what it said.' The man who cut that into the stone is under a grave-row now with no name deeper than his tally, and the last thing he wanted was to understand. He got his wish. Do not let me want it for you. Some records the vale is safer without, and a chronicler who knows that is a chronicler who gets to keep chronicling.

objectives.label: the shaking-hand altar
objectives.text: Read the confession once
Swear to Barbu you did not copy it

transcub_vale_chalk_confession.note: The altar confession read: I only wanted to know what it said. The man who cut it lies nameless in a grave-row, granted his wish. Read once, never copied. Barbu needed to hear a person refuse the wanting out loud.

transcub_vale_the_unswept_corner.title: The Corner Left for Himself
transcub_vale_the_unswept_corner.summary: The city's blind-spot broker sells clean corners to the desperate but keeps one un-swept for himself. He is coming up the vale to sell here, and Toma wants his one private corner found and mapped -- leverage on the man who sells escape from leverage.
transcub_vale_the_unswept_corner.offer: A man is coming up from the city who sells the one thing Blestem forbids: a blind spot. A clean corner the ears do not reach, sold to the desperate at a killing price. Clever trade. Here is the crack in it -- a man who sells blind spots keeps one un-swept for himself, always, and he is too proud not to. Find where his own private corner is in the vale before he does his rounds, and mark it on this. Leverage on the man who sells freedom from leverage is the sweetest coin the market knows.
transcub_vale_the_unswept_corner.progress: The broker's private blind-corner lies somewhere in the vale's tangle of dead-end lanes. Find it and mark the map.
transcub_vale_the_unswept_corner.complete: You found his corner. Of course it is by the church -- the one place the city already half-forgets to listen, so his blind spot costs him nothing to keep. Now I own the man who owns the market's blind spots, and I did not have to buy a single one. This is how the vale works, friend: everyone is somebody's un-swept corner, and the whole trade is learning whose you are before they learn yours.

objectives.label: the broker's private corner
objectives.text: Find the un-swept corner
Mark the corner on Toma's map

transcub_vale_the_unswept_corner.note: The broker who sells blind spots kept one un-swept for himself, by the church, where the city half-forgets to listen. Toma now owns the man who owns the market's blind spots, bought for nothing. Everyone in the vale is somebody's un-swept corner.

transcub_vale_widows_ledger.title: A Debt Passed Down
transcub_vale_widows_ledger.summary: A vale widow keeps swinging her dead husband's trade so the debt passes to her back and not her sons'. The city means to file the boys anyway, for the shortfall. Florea wants the ledger error found that could keep them unnamed one more winter.
transcub_vale_widows_ledger.offer: There is a widow up the lane keeps her dead man's stall open, doing his trade with her own bad back, so that his debt stays hers and does not roll onto the boys. The city does not care whose back pays -- it cares that the ledger balances, and this season it does not, and an unbalanced ledger in Blestem gets a name written into it. Two names, the age of her sons. Somewhere in the tally is an error; there always is, the clerks are proud and proud men miscount. Find it. Buy those boys one more winter unfiled.
transcub_vale_widows_ledger.progress: The error hides in the widow's copy of the debt-ledger. Read it against the stall's true takings and find the clerk's mistake.
transcub_vale_widows_ledger.complete: There it was -- a column carried wrong, twelve where it should read eleven, the clerk's own pride the flaw in his sums. The boys stay off the row another winter. Do not look pleased; look at what you had to become to save them. You did not overturn a cruelty. You found a clerk's typo and used it, the way this whole vale survives -- on the small honest mistakes of proud men who are wrong about exactly one number. Take her thanks. She has little else, and the little is warm.

objectives.text: Recover the widow's ledger copy
Show Florea the clerk's error

transcub_vale_widows_ledger.note: A widow paid her dead man's debt with her own back so her sons stayed unfiled. A clerk's proud miscount -- twelve for eleven -- bought the boys one more winter. The vale survives on the small honest mistakes of proud men wrong about one number.

transcub_vale_the_listeners.title: The Ones Without Faces
transcub_vale_the_listeners.summary: Informants embedded so long they have stopped having faces of their own come up to the vale to rest between postings. One has forgotten which side it reports to. The guard wants it found before it decides -- or before it forgets it was ever a person.
transcub_vale_the_listeners.offer: The city breeds a kind of person that stops being one. A listener -- planted so deep, so long, that it forgets it had a face and just becomes the ear it was made into. They come up the vale to rest between postings, and there is one here now that has forgotten which side it reports to. That is dangerous to everyone: a listener with no master listens for anyone. Find it. It will look like a neighbour you cannot quite place, because that is what it has spent its life learning to be. Tell me if there is enough person left to send home, or only ear.
transcub_vale_the_listeners.progress: The faceless listener rests somewhere among the vale's folk, wearing the shape of a neighbour you cannot place. Find it.
transcub_vale_the_listeners.complete: You found it. Sitting by the church, of all places -- drawn there, I think, the way the old faith draws the ruined ones, to the god who audits the self. There was a little person left. Enough to weep when you named it, which is more than the city ever allows itself. It does not remember which side, and I will not make it choose. Let it sit by the ruin and be no one's ear a while. That is as close to mercy as this vale gets, and it is not close.

objectives.label: the faceless listener
objectives.text: Find the resting listener
Report to Ilie what is left of it

transcub_vale_the_listeners.note: A listener embedded so long it forgot which side it reports to came to rest by the church, drawn to the god who audits the self. Enough person was left to weep when named. Ilie let it be no one's ear a while -- as close to mercy as the vale gets.

transcub_vale_gargoyle_line.title: The Line They Draw
transcub_vale_gargoyle_line.summary: The smith split a broken gargoyle and found the truth: the stone wardens are not watching the warm field but lined on it, spaced even, aligned like the dust and the graves -- one figure the villain is slowly drawing across the vale.
transcub_vale_gargoyle_line.offer: I split one of the gargoyles you broke for me, to see what makes them climb down. There is nothing inside but stone. But I laid the pieces out and I saw the thing I did not want to see: they do not stand where a hundred stone things would happen to stand. They stand spaced even. Aligned. Same as the dust in the church, same as the graves in the row, same as the warmth on Barbu's line. It is all one figure being drawn, slow, across the whole vale, and the gargoyles are just the part of it that can walk. Break the last of them off the line. Then let us both forget I said this.
transcub_vale_gargoyle_line.progress: Break the gargoyles still standing on the aligned line below the spire, and read the ground where the line runs.
transcub_vale_gargoyle_line.complete: Broken, the line scattered -- for tonight. It will re-form; a figure being drawn does not stop for a broken pen. You saw how they lay: not watching the warm ground, standing on it, part of it, like ticks on a tally too big to see the top of. Everything in this vale is being arranged into one slow mark, and the arrangement was here before us and will be here after. I made you a reaver from the pieces. Swing it at the parts that walk. It will not touch the hand that draws them.

objectives.text: Break the gargoyles standing on the line

objectives.label: the aligned line
objectives.text: Read the line the arrangement draws

transcub_vale_gargoyle_line.note: The gargoyles do not watch the warm field -- they stand on it, spaced even, part of one slow figure being drawn across the whole vale, like the dust and the graves and the warmth. Break them and it re-forms. The arrangement was here before us and will outlast us.

transcub_vale_confession_of_none.title: A Congregation of None
transcub_vale_confession_of_none.summary: The priest preaches to empty pews and the innkeeper thinks that is a sadness worth a small kindness. He wants a proper congregation gathered for one service -- warm bodies, warm bread after -- so the gentle half-mad man can once say his piece to living ears.
transcub_vale_confession_of_none.offer: The priest up at the ruin has preached to empty stone for years -- a congregation of none, and he does it anyway, gentle as rain, against cruelty-to-the-self to nobody at all. It breaks my heart in the one soft spot the vale has not yet found. I want to give him a service. Real folk in the pews for once, and my best bread and a barrel after. Walk the low houses. Bring me anyone willing to sit an hour and be preached at kindly. It is a small warm thing. Let us do one before the vale takes the taste for warm things away entirely.
transcub_vale_confession_of_none.progress: Gather willing folk from the vale's low houses to sit one service at the roofless church.
transcub_vale_confession_of_none.complete: You filled the pews. Nine of them -- a small number, but you should have seen him. He wept and forgot his sermon and just thanked us, one by one, by name, and every name was right though he had never met a soul of them. The old god of that church punished cruelty to the self. For one hour the priest was not cruel to himself, and neither was anyone. Bread is warm. Sit. This is what the vale is for, under all the rest of it. We forget, is all.

objectives.text: Gather willing folk from the low houses

objectives.label: the roofless church
objectives.text: Fill the pews for one service

transcub_vale_confession_of_none.note: The half-mad priest preached to empty stone for years. Filled for one service, he wept and thanked nine strangers by their right names, though he had never met them. For one hour no one in the vale was cruel to themselves. This is what the vale is for, under all the rest.

transcub_vale_harpy_song.title: What the Harpies Learned to Say
transcub_vale_harpy_song.summary: The ridge-harpies have always screamed. Now, once a night, the flock falls silent and one says a single sentence in a voice no bird owns -- and it is about you. The guard cannot climb up to hear it. You must, and must not understand it.
transcub_vale_harpy_song.offer: The harpies scream, always have, it is half the reason no one sleeps up the fold. But three nights running the whole flock goes dead quiet at the same hour and one of them -- one -- says a sentence. Not a screech. Words, in a voice that never came out of any bird. The lad who heard it closest will not repeat it; he says it was about him, by something he did, not his name. Climb up. Hear it once. And whatever you do -- do not work out what it means. The ones who understand up here are the ones we bury unwounded.
transcub_vale_harpy_song.progress: Climb to the harpy roost at the silent hour and hear the one sentence. Hear it. Do not try to understand it.
transcub_vale_harpy_song.complete: You heard it, then, and you came back with your fingernails dirty and your eyes still your own, which is how I know you did not solve it. It borrowed a bird's throat because it has learned we listen to the ridge now, the way it learned to borrow a wall, a mouth, a forge-skip. It said a true thing about something you did -- deeds, not your name, it never has the name. Let it stay a sound. A sound cannot bill you. Meaning can. You did the brave thing, which up here is the refusing.

objectives.label: the silent roost
objectives.text: Hear the one sentence at the silent hour
Silence the flock that carried it

transcub_vale_harpy_song.note: Once a night the harpy flock falls silent and one speaks a true sentence about a thing you did -- deeds, never the name. It borrowed a bird's throat as it borrows walls and mouths. Heard once, left un-understood. A sound cannot bill you; meaning can.

transcub_vale_the_forty_pace.title: The Forty-Pace Instinct
transcub_vale_the_forty_pace.summary: Vale children are taught before they can read never to speak in a straight line, never to finish a sentence a wall could carry. One child has started finishing her sentences -- loudly, in the open. Dorin is afraid she is being taught by something else. He wants her watched.
transcub_vale_the_forty_pace.offer: We teach the little ones the forty-pace before they can read: never speak in a straight line, never finish a sentence a wall could carry to the end.
