It is the first thing a vale child learns, before their own name almost. There is a girl in the low houses who has stopped keeping it. She finishes every sentence now, loud, in the open, plain as morning -- and she was the most careful child I ever taught. Somebody is un-teaching her, or something is. Watch her a while. Tell me who she is truly talking to when she talks that straight.
transcub_vale_the_forty_pace.progress: Watch the child in the low houses who has stopped keeping the forty-pace instinct. Learn who she answers.
transcub_vale_the_forty_pace.complete: You watched her. Talking straight and plain to the church wall, you said, finishing every sentence right into the stone, and the stone warm where she leans on it. She is not being un-taught. She is being answered -- the walls confess back, the priest always said, and now a child has found the one that will. I will move her family down the vale tonight. You cannot un-teach a child the forty-pace once a warm wall has taught her it is safe to speak straight. That is the horror of it. It felt safe.

objectives.label: the low houses
objectives.text: Watch the too-plain-spoken child

objectives.label: the wall she speaks to
objectives.text: Read the wall she speaks to

transcub_vale_the_forty_pace.note: A careful vale child began finishing her sentences loud and plain -- straight into a warm church wall that answered her. Not un-taught, but answered. Dorin moved the family that night. The horror was that speaking straight had felt safe to her.

transcub_vale_broker_bargain.title: Three Buyers for One Name
transcub_vale_broker_bargain.summary: Toma has a name to sell: a vale man who informed and then hated himself for it. The subject will pay to bury it, the clerk to file it, the priest will take it for nothing and forgive it away. Three buyers, one name, three costs.
transcub_vale_broker_bargain.offer: I have a name, and three who want it. A vale man informed on a neighbour last winter and has hated himself sober every night since -- which is exactly the sin the old church god punished, cruelty turned inward. Now: the man himself will pay dear to buy his own name back and bury it. The city clerk will pay to file it and own him. The priest will take it for nothing, and forgive it, and it will cease to be leverage at all. Sell it. Keep it. Give it away. But choose knowing that in Blestem a forgiven name is a name that stops being worth money -- and that, to some of us, is the only sales I still enjoy making.
transcub_vale_broker_bargain.progress: Decide the fate of the informer's name: sell it to the man, file it with the clerk, or give it to the priest to forgive.
transcub_vale_broker_bargain.complete: To the priest. For nothing. You handed a piece of clean leverage to the one man in the vale who will destroy its value on purpose, and you did it without a coin changing hands. Do you know what you have just proven to me? That you cannot be leveraged, because you do not want the things leverage is made of. That is either the safest a person can be in this vale or the most dangerous. I have not decided which. Take your pay. It is the last time the name is worth anything -- I am selling you its ghost.

objectives.text: Take the informer's name from Toma
Give the name to the priest to forgive

transcub_vale_broker_bargain.note: An informer's self-hating name could be sold, filed, or forgiven. Given to the priest for nothing, its value died on purpose. Toma sold its ghost, and learned the buyer wanted nothing leverage is made of -- the safest or most dangerous thing in the vale.

transcub_vale_the_thin_place.title: Where the Vale Wears Thin
transcub_vale_the_thin_place.summary: You broke the gargoyle line, cleared the roost, delivered the courier sealed. The vale is quiet for the first time in a season. Barbu wants the altar read one last time to confirm the calm -- and the calm is the point. A quiet vale reads better.
transcub_vale_the_thin_place.offer: You have done more for this vale than any warden the city ever sent. The gargoyles broken, the roost thinned, the courier delivered sealed, the confessions kept out of the files. It is quiet up the church now -- truly quiet, the first real peace the fold has known since the stone went warm. I want to write it down as a good ending, and I have never once in forty years gotten to write one. Go up and read the altar a last time by the shifting-sight. Bring me a clean colour. Let an old man record one victory before he dies.
transcub_vale_the_thin_place.progress: Read the church altar one last time. Barbu wants a clean colour to record the vale's peace.
transcub_vale_the_thin_place.complete: Orange. Shifting orange, the worst of the colours, the one that means a live signal writing itself into the medium -- and it was never there before, not green, not warm, ORANGE, since the vale went quiet. I understand it now and I wish to every dead god I did not. It did not fight your work. It wanted your work. A vale full of screaming and stone things climbing down is a vale too loud to read. You gave it silence, and silence is what it needed to finish the sentence. We did not win. We turned down the noise so it could hear itself think. Write nothing. There is no good ending. There is only a quieter machine.

objectives.label: the altar in the new quiet
objectives.text: Read the altar in the new quiet
Tell Barbu the colour you read

transcub_vale_the_thin_place.note: The vale went quiet -- and the altar that had only ever read warm now reads shifting orange, the live-signal colour. The calm was the point: a screaming vale is too loud to read. The peace let the machine hear itself think. No victory. Only a quieter machine.

transcub_vale_walk_the_penitent.title: Walk the Penitent Down
transcub_vale_walk_the_penitent.summary: A man came down from the church changed -- calm, forgiven, talking too straight for the vale to be safe in. The innkeeper wants him walked out to the low road before a wall hears how honest he has become. The trouble is not on the road.
transcub_vale_walk_the_penitent.offer: There is a man drinking at my bar who went up to the church to confess and came down forgiven -- truly forgiven, light as a boy, and that is the danger. The old god took his self-cruelty off him and left him honest, and an honest man in this vale finishes his sentences. He will say something straight into the wrong wall by nightfall and be filed for it. Walk him down to the low road before he does. Keep him talking, keep him moving, and do not let him stop where the stone is warm. The peril is not thieves. It is that he is not afraid anymore.
transcub_vale_walk_the_penitent.progress: Walk the forgiven man from the inn down to the low road. Keep him moving; do not let him stop where the ground runs warm.
transcub_vale_walk_the_penitent.complete: You got him to the low road, and he thanked you plainly, and walked off whistling into the fog with nothing to hide, which up here is a death sentence he cannot hear. I have stopped a hundred honest men at that door and turned them back into careful ones, and every time it works I hate the work more. The vale does not punish the cruel. It punishes the ones the old god healed. Take a bed on the house. You walked a forgiven man out of a place that has no use for the forgiven.

objectives.label: the inn door
objectives.text: Take up the walk at the inn

objectives.label: the low road
objectives.text: Walk the penitent to the low road

transcub_vale_walk_the_penitent.note: A man confessed at the church and came down forgiven -- light, honest, finishing his sentences, which in the vale is fatal. Walked to the low road before a warm wall could hear him. The vale does not punish the cruel; it punishes the ones the old god healed.

transcub_vale_daily_lichen_run.title: Light for the Low Houses
transcub_vale_daily_lichen_run.summary: The low houses burn through lichen-light faster than one old scraper can climb for it. Fetch a day's culture off the wet ledges and keep the honest lamps lit one more night.
transcub_vale_daily_lichen_run.offer: The low houses eat through the lichen-light faster than my knees can climb for it, and a dark house up the vale is a house that starts imagining things. Bring me a day's scrape off the wet ledges -- the pale culture, mind, not the grey -- and the lamps stay honest and warm another night. It is not much of an errand. It is the whole vale's small daily war against the dark, is all.
transcub_vale_daily_lichen_run.progress: Scrape a day's worth of glowing lichen from the wet north ledges for the low houses.
transcub_vale_daily_lichen_run.complete: That will keep the lamps a night. A dark house imagines; a lit one just sleeps. Small war, won again today. Come back tomorrow -- the dark does.

objectives.text: Scrape a day's lichen for the lamps

transcub_vale_daily_lichen_run.note: A day's lichen keeps the low houses lit one more night. A dark house imagines; a lit one sleeps. The small war is won again, and the dark comes back tomorrow.

transcub_vale_daily_ridge_cull.title: Keep the Steps Clear
transcub_vale_daily_ridge_cull.summary: The harpies always come back to the church ridge -- the confessors always give them reason to. Thin the day's flock so the steps stay safe for whoever needs to climb up and be honest tonight.
transcub_vale_daily_ridge_cull.offer: The flock comes back to the church ridge every day, because every day someone climbs up to say the thing they cannot say below, and the birds have learned the steps make good hunting. I cannot post a guard up a holy ruin -- the city would ask why we guard a god it stopped fearing. So it falls to whoever will. Thin today's flock. Keep the steps clear for tonight's confessions. It is grim work in service of a small mercy, which is most of the work up here.
transcub_vale_daily_ridge_cull.progress: Thin the day's harpy flock roosting above the church steps.
transcub_vale_daily_ridge_cull.complete: Clear for tonight. Someone will climb up in the dark and hate themselves in peace, and live to climb down, and that is the whole of what we can offer them. Come back tomorrow. The flock will. The confessors will. So will the need.

objectives.text: Thin the day's flock above the steps

transcub_vale_daily_ridge_cull.note: Each day the harpies return to the church steps and each day someone climbs up to be honest. Cleared, the steps let a soul confess and live to climb down. Grim work in service of a small mercy, which is most of the work up the vale.

grey_piers_still_warm.title: Still Warm
grey_piers_still_warm.summary: A debt-tablet came in with the tide at the Grey Piers, hairline-cracked and faintly warm, as no drowned thing should be. Cornel wants it off his boards before the fog notices whose hand it fits.
grey_piers_still_warm.offer: Something came up the tide-line and lodged under the far pier, and the boy who fished it out won't say two words since. A tablet, stone, cracked across — and warm, the way you're warm, not the way the water is. I've kept the Grey Piers forty years and I've stopped touching what floats in. You've the look of one who touches things. Bring it up. Don't read the face of it.
grey_piers_still_warm.progress: The tablet is wedged under the far pier-end, out past the customs shed where the light gives out. Fetch it up. Face down, if you've the sense the boy hasn't.
grey_piers_still_warm.complete: Warm still. In my hand, still. A whole life filed onto a stone and set adrift not quite closed — and it's shaped, look at it, like a man's account that never squared. You carried it and it didn't take you. That's not nothing this far downriver. Keep the lantern; the dark down here has manners, but it keeps them for its own.

objectives.label: the far pier-end
objectives.text: Reach the far pier-end
Bring up the cracked debt-tablet

grey_piers_still_warm.note: A debt-tablet, cracked and warm, out with the tide and not quite closed. Cornel says it is shaped like a life that never squared. It has stopped being warm since you set it on his desk. He has not stopped looking at it.

grey_piers_the_sunk_ledger.title: The Sunk Ledger
grey_piers_the_sunk_ledger.summary: The old crossing-office flooded a lifetime ago, but its books never closed. Danciu wants the crossing-ledger raised from the black water before the tablet from the tide starts matching names to it.
grey_piers_the_sunk_ledger.offer: That tablet you brought Cornel — it wants a ledger, the way a coin wants a purse. There's one down in the sunk office off the black-water slip, gone under when the second tide took the low quarter. Every crossing this port ever charged, still open on its pages, still owed. Raise it for me. Danciu keeps a clean desk; I'd not have a stranger's death filed against my hand because I let the water keep the paper.
grey_piers_the_sunk_ledger.progress: The crossing-office lies flooded off the black-water slip, north where the pilings lean. Down through the drowned steps, and mind the swarm in the rot.
grey_piers_the_sunk_ledger.complete: Every crossing, every fare, every soul this port passed east — open, unclosed, waiting. And on the last dry page, a hand not the clerk's has begun a name and thought better of it. Stamped, un-stamped. I do not like a page that argues with itself. Take the writ. Some accounts are better torn than balanced.

objectives.label: the drowned steps
objectives.text: Descend the drowned steps to the sunk office
Clear the Dock-Rot from the flooded records
Raise the crossing-ledger

grey_piers_the_sunk_ledger.note: The crossing-ledger came up black and swollen and legible. Every fare the Grey Piers ever took still stands open. On the last dry page a dead hand began a name, un-began it, and stopped. Danciu filed the book. He did not finish the name.

grey_piers_reading_the_fog.title: Reading the Fog
grey_piers_reading_the_fog.summary: Emilian keeps the alchemical watch over the Grey Piers, and lately the fog reads a color it has no right to. He needs a still hand to walk the slip at dusk and confirm what the orange means before it means it for good.
grey_piers_reading_the_fog.offer: I stand the watch and I read the water — blue for the old dead, violet for the deep, green when a stone's about to wake. Comforting, in its way; a color you can name is trouble you can measure. But at the ferry slip, near dusk, the fog's begun to shift orange around whoever stands in it. That one's not in any of the old books except the last page. Walk the slip. Stand where I mark. Tell me it's the lamps. Lie to me if you have to.
grey_piers_reading_the_fog.progress: Take the reading at the ferry slip as the light goes. Stand where the water turns; do not stand in it long. The orange counts the ones who linger.
grey_piers_reading_the_fog.complete: It moved with you. It read you and wrote you in the same breath. That's the signal, friend — not a symptom of a stone somewhere, the thing itself, matured, wearing the fog like a coat. Downriver they say it walks on a man's chest now. I'll log it and I'll say the lamps, for Cornel's sleep. Take the tonic. Whatever you tasted back there, it does not leave on its own.

objectives.label: the ferry slip
objectives.text: Take the dusk reading at the ferry slip

grey_piers_reading_the_fog.note: The fog off the ferry slip read shifting orange, and it read you — moved as you moved, wrote where you stood. Emilian logged it as lamplight, for the dockmaster's sleep. You both know it was not lamplight. The signal has learned to wear weather.

grey_piers_the_incurious_boy.title: The Incurious Boy
grey_piers_the_incurious_boy.summary: The lad who fished the tablet from the tide has gone quiet and calm, and calm is the wrong thing to be in Greyhollow. Pavel wants the folk who'd know whether a boy can be talked back before the quiet finishes.
grey_piers_the_incurious_boy.offer: The boy's in my back room, warm by the fire, and I can't get him to want a thing. Not soup, not his mother, not out the door. Calm as a shut drawer. My grandmother would've said that's when you worry — an empty child hears too well. Ask Vintila, he's read every drowned book we've got, and ask the watcher what the calm ones do before they stop. I'll not lose a boy to good behavior.
grey_piers_the_incurious_boy.progress: Chronicler Vintila keeps the old accounts; Watcher Emilian counts the quiet ones. Ask them both what an incurious boy becomes.
grey_piers_the_incurious_boy.complete: Both said the same and neither would say it plain: the empty ones listen, and the listening ones get filed. But you sat with him, and you were loud and stupid and human at him, and he asked for soup. One word — soup — and I could've wept in it. It's not a cure. It's a Tuesday he gets to keep. Take the charm. It means nothing, and down here that's the whole armor.

objectives.text: Ask Chronicler Vintila about the calm
Ask Watcher Emilian about the listening

grey_piers_the_incurious_boy.note: The boy who touched the tablet went calm, and calm is the door. Pavel sat with him and was loud and human until the boy asked for soup. It bought a Tuesday, not a life. In Greyhollow that is counted a good day.

grey_piers_the_full_slip.title: The Full Slip
grey_piers_the_full_slip.summary: Brine-Shades have fouled the working slips and no boat can dock, so Cornel means to clear them and reopen the Grey Piers to honest trade. The clearing works. What the open slip lets in does not.
grey_piers_the_full_slip.offer: I've three ferries riding at anchor and no slip to take them — the drowned won't leave the moorings, standing in the water like they're waiting on a fare. Clear the pilings for me and I can dock cargo again, pay my men, keep the lamps lit. A working port's the one clean thing left down here. Do this and the Grey Piers breathe for a season. That's worth more than gold, though I'll give you that too.
grey_piers_the_full_slip.progress: The Brine-Shades foul the mooring pilings all along the working slip. Put them down and clear the water for the ferries.
grey_piers_the_full_slip.complete: Slips clear, boats in, men paid — a good day's work, and honest, and I thank you for it. ...Only now the first ferry's manifest is short two names it sailed with, and no one recalls them stepping off. A working port takes cargo both ways, seems. I opened the door. Take your pay before I think too hard about who walked through it.

objectives.text: Clear the Brine-Shades from the moorings

objectives.label: the ferry slip
objectives.text: Signal the ferries in

grey_piers_the_full_slip.note: The slips were cleared and the ferries came in and the men were paid — an honest day. Then the first manifest came up two names short, and no one recalled the disembarking. Cornel opened the port. Something kept the change.

grey_piers_the_chalk_hand.title: The Chalk Hand
grey_piers_the_chalk_hand.summary: On a canal wall by the ferry slip a child pressed a chalk handprint, the same small emblem folk have made since before the drowning, and someone has ringed it with a careful new mark. Zaharia wants to know if the ring keeps it or catalogues it.
grey_piers_the_chalk_hand.offer: There's a print on the wall by the slip — a child's hand, in chalk, like the ones my mother said the orphanages made when there were orphanages. Been there years, that one. But someone's drawn a neat mark round it now, fresh, careful. A ward, I hoped. Then I thought: or a tag. A thing the watchers put on what they mean to collect. Go and stand at it and tell me which. I can't read the new marks. I don't want to learn.
grey_piers_the_chalk_hand.progress: The chalk handprint is on the canal wall by the ferry slip. Go and look at the ring around it, and bring back which kind of mark it is.
grey_piers_the_chalk_hand.complete: You couldn't tell either. Good. Means it's honest, then — even the one who drew the ring didn't know if they were saving the print or filing it. That's the kindest thing I've heard all winter: a mark that hasn't decided yet. Leave it undecided. Take this — my girl's, she'd want it on someone who goes where I won't.

objectives.label: the chalk-wall
objectives.text: Read the ringed handprint on the canal wall

grey_piers_the_chalk_hand.note: A child's chalk hand on the canal wall, ringed by a fresh careful mark, and no one — not even the hand that drew the ring — can say whether it keeps the print or files it. Zaharia asked you to leave it undecided. Some mercies are only that: a thing not yet closed.

grey_piers_bodies_are_deposits.title: Bodies Are Deposits
grey_piers_bodies_are_deposits.summary: The canals here are the port's disposal as much as its passage, and the Dock-Rot Swarms have swelled feeding on what the water is given. Tudor wants them thinned before the current carries their rot up into the drinking-cisterns.
grey_piers_bodies_are_deposits.offer: You want to know what the canal is for, this far down? It's for what the ledger's done with. Bodies go in and the water calls them deposits and the rot-swarms grow fat on the accounting. Now they're thick enough to foul the cisterns, and I'll not have the living drinking off the settled dead. Thin the swarms along the pilings. It's ugly work. All the honest work down here is.
grey_piers_bodies_are_deposits.progress: The Dock-Rot Swarms cluster thick along the pilings under the customs shed. Thin them before the rot reaches the cisterns.
grey_piers_bodies_are_deposits.complete: Done, and the cisterns'll run clean a while longer. You noticed the swarms lie in rows against the current, didn't you — even-spaced, like a thing arranged them for tallying. I noticed too, my first year. Then I stopped noticing, because noticing's how you end up a deposit yourself. Take the coin. Drink upstream.

objectives.text: Thin the Dock-Rot along the pilings

grey_piers_bodies_are_deposits.note: The rot-swarms were thinned and the cisterns will run clean a while. They lay in even rows against the current, arranged as if for tallying. Tudor learned young to stop noticing such things. It is how one avoids becoming a deposit.

grey_piers_the_forged_fare.title: A Fare You Don't Pay
grey_piers_the_forged_fare.summary: The crossing-tolls at the Grey Piers have climbed past what an honest trader can bear, and Sava has a clerk's blank and a steady hand. She wants the toll-house watched so she can learn whose stamp to forge, and who will look the other way.
grey_piers_the_forged_fare.offer: The toll's tripled since the annex took the crossing, and half of it goes nowhere a man can name. I've a blank receipt and a good pen — what I want is knowing the clerk's habits, which of them checks and which of them's asleep at the desk by the ninth bell. Go watch the toll-house. Bring me who's careless. I'll not starve paying a fee to a filing cabinet. Everyone forges down here; the only sin is forging clumsy.
grey_piers_the_forged_fare.progress: Watch the toll-house by the ferry slip and mark the clerks' habits. Bring Sava word of which desk goes unwatched.
grey_piers_the_forged_fare.complete: The ninth-bell clerk — of course, the tired one, the one who's stopped caring which name crosses. Perfect. He never looks twice at his own hand. Here's your cut of what I'll save, and one of the forgeries besides — a coach fare you'll never pay for. Use it before the annex learns to count faces. They will. They always learn to count.

objectives.label: the toll-house
objectives.text: Watch the toll-house at the ferry slip
Sound out the ninth-bell clerk

grey_piers_the_forged_fare.note: The ninth-bell clerk had stopped caring which name crossed his desk, and that carelessness was the crack Sava needed. She calls it survival, not theft. The annex will learn to count faces in time. It always learns to count.

grey_piers_the_last_ferryman.title: The Last Ferryman
grey_piers_the_last_ferryman.summary: Old Aurel poles the last honest ferry across the black water, and he has taken to standing too long at the far slip with his cap off, listening. Pavel asks you to walk out and bring him back before the standing becomes staying.
grey_piers_the_last_ferryman.offer: Aurel's poled that ferry since before the low quarter went under — forty years across black water and never lost a soul he was paid to carry. But he's gone to standing at the far slip of an evening, cap in hand, head tilted, and he doesn't hear me call. The good ones go quiet first; you've seen it. Walk out and be a nuisance at him. Bring him home for a drink. A man who's carried that many deserves a hand back across himself.
grey_piers_the_last_ferryman.progress: Aurel stands at the far slip past the pilings, listening to the water. Go out and bring the old ferryman home.
grey_piers_the_last_ferryman.complete: There he is — grumbling, which is how you know he's still in there. He said the water'd started to sound like a name he used to know. I told him it's just water and I'm a liar and we both know it. But he came back, and he'll drink tonight, and tomorrow's tomorrow's problem. That's the whole trade down here: one more evening, bought loud, against the quiet.

objectives.label: the far slip
objectives.text: Reach Aurel at the far slip
Bring Aurel the ferryman home

grey_piers_the_last_ferryman.note: Aurel had begun to hear a name in the black water and to stand too long listening for it. Pavel sent you to be a loud nuisance and bring him home. It worked, this evening. The water is patient about such things. It can afford to be.

grey_piers_salvage_rights.title: Salvage Rights
grey_piers_salvage_rights.summary: No ore comes down to the Grey Piers anymore, so Radomir works the grey wood and drowned iron the tide gives up. He needs bog-iron dredged from the sunk quarter before his forge goes cold for good.
grey_piers_salvage_rights.offer: Nobody ships ore to a drowning port, friend — why would they. So I take what the black water's finished with: bog-iron off the sunk quarter, pilings' worth, hardware from doors that don't lead anywhere now. It rusts strange and it hammers stranger, but a nail's a nail and folk still need hinges even at the end of the world. Dredge me a good pile from the pilings and I'll keep the one warm forge on the Grey Piers.
grey_piers_salvage_rights.progress: Bog-iron lies in the drowned pilings off the black-water slip. Dredge a good pile for Radomir's forge.
grey_piers_salvage_rights.complete: Now that's iron a man can work — river-quenched already, saves me the trouble, keeps the color. Grim-looking stuff, but it holds an edge and it doesn't ask where it's been. Take this hook; forged it from the last batch. It'll pull a boat in, gaff a fish, or hold a door that's tired of being a door. Cheapest honest thing on the pier, and I'm proud of it.

objectives.text: Dredge bog-iron from the drowned pilings

grey_piers_salvage_rights.note: The drowned pilings gave up iron enough to keep Radomir's forge warm — the one warm forge on the Grey Piers. He asks the metal no questions about where it has been. It is, he says, the cheapest honest thing left on the pier.

grey_piers_the_stamped_line.title: The Stamped Line
grey_piers_the_stamped_line.summary: One line in the raised ledger is stamped collected, un-stamped, and stamped again, the ink smeared where a dead hand kept refusing the verdict. Danciu wants the office it was filed from found, to learn who keeps arguing with a closed account.
grey_piers_the_stamped_line.offer: That book you raised — I've read it through, and there's a line won't lie still. Stamped collected in violet, then the stamp scratched out, then stamped again, over and over, the ink smeared like a hand kept coming back to un-close it. A finalized account does not argue. This one does. I want the desk it was filed from — the old customs shed. Find me who's been visiting a settled ledger by night. A clerk fears an open account more than a knife.
grey_piers_the_stamped_line.progress: The account was filed from the old customs shed east of the toll-house. Search it for whoever keeps un-stamping the line, and clear the drowned that guard it.
grey_piers_the_stamped_line.complete: Handprints in the dust on the desk, fresh, and wet, and no boots to the door. Someone dead has been coming back to argue with their own collection — to keep one account from closing. I should log it as tampering. Instead I'll leave the drawer open a crack. A man who refuses to be finished... down here, that's the only saint we've got. Take the dossier. It knows habits. So, it seems, do the dead.

objectives.label: the old customs shed
objectives.text: Search the customs shed for the visitor
Clear the drowned from the filing-desk

grey_piers_the_stamped_line.note: One account in the drowned ledger keeps being un-closed — stamped, un-stamped, stamped — by a dead hand that leaves wet prints and no boots. Danciu left the drawer open a crack. A thing that refuses to be finished is, this far down, the nearest thing to a saint.

grey_piers_grey_wood.title: Grey Wood
grey_piers_grey_wood.summary: The piers themselves are cut from grey wood that never quite finished being alive, and it is rotting faster than one smith can shore it. Radomir needs the worst pilings held against the Dock-Rot while he braces them, before a whole slip goes into the black.
grey_piers_grey_wood.offer: You know what these piers are cut from? The old forests, the dying wood of the world before this one — hauled down here and planked while it was still half-alive, and it's been trying to finish dying ever since. The rot-swarms know it. They're eating the number three slip out from under the port. I can brace it if the swarms let me work. Stand between me and them an hour. Lose the slip and we lose the ferries with it.
grey_piers_grey_wood.progress: Radomir works the failing number three slip. Hold the Dock-Rot off the pilings while he braces the grey wood.
grey_piers_grey_wood.complete: Braced, and it'll stand another year — long enough. Strange trade, isn't it: propping up dead forest with drowned iron so a drowning port can pretend it isn't. My grandfather cut green wood for green ships. I cut grey wood for grey ones. Same hammer. Take the striker — it'll light a fire where the damp's put every other flame out. Keep something warm down here. It's the whole job.

objectives.label: the number three slip
objectives.text: Reach the failing slip
Hold the rot off while Radomir braces the pilings

grey_piers_grey_wood.note: The number three slip was braced and will stand a year — dead forest propped with drowned iron so a drowning port can pretend. Radomir cuts grey wood where his grandfather cut green. Same hammer, he says. He keeps a fire warm anyway. He says that is the whole job.

grey_piers_the_seer_at_the_slip.title: The Seer at the Slip
grey_piers_the_seer_at_the_slip.summary: A blind wanderer with marks on his palms has come to the Grey Piers and sits at the slip screaming at things no one else can see. Emilian wants him found and heard, for a man who screams at what walks may know how to stop it.
grey_piers_the_seer_at_the_slip.offer: There's a blind man come up from downriver, palms marked like he pressed them to something that wrote back. Sits at the black-water slip with his empty eyes and screams — 'already collected, why is it still walking' — at nothing on the water. The folk think him mad. I think he sees the one thing my colors can't name. Go to him. Sit. Let him rave at you. If any mouth on this pier holds a way to stop the fog, it's the one nobody believes.
grey_piers_the_seer_at_the_slip.progress: The blind seer sits at the black-water slip, screaming at the water. Go to him and hear what his ruined eyes have seen.
grey_piers_the_seer_at_the_slip.complete: He grabbed your wrist and said it plain, between the screams: only the dead are read-only; the living get written; and a thing already ended can push the present back into the record and jam it. Transmit, don't receive. Six words and a lifetime's screaming to earn them. He's fading as he speaks — the seeing costs him himself. Take the crown he pressed on you. Cold light. He said you'll need it where the reading's brightest.

objectives.label: the black-water slip
objectives.text: Reach the blind seer at the slip
Hear the seer's six words

grey_piers_the_seer_at_the_slip.note: A blind seer at the slip, palms written by a stone that answered, gave up six words at the cost of himself: transmit, don't receive; only the dead can push the present back and jam the record. He faded as he said it. The most important thing in the world, spoken by a man no one believed.

grey_piers_deposits_returned.title: What the Water Returns
grey_piers_deposits_returned.summary: The Brine-Shades rising from the canals are the port's own filed dead, come back to the moorings they were deposited from. Ursu means to put them down, and to see whether any still carry the tokens they went under with.
grey_piers_deposits_returned.offer: The drowned that walk out of our canals — they're ours. Deposits the ledger was done with, come back up the way a coin comes up when you shake the purse hard enough. I have to put them down; standing water full of the settled dead fouls the whole slip. But look, before you do — some still hold a keepsake, a cord, a token from before they were an account. If a name went under with them, I'd give it back to whoever's left grieving. It's not much. It's what I can do.
grey_piers_deposits_returned.progress: The port's filed dead rise as Brine-Shades along the moorings. Put them down, and gather the keepsakes they carried under.
grey_piers_deposits_returned.complete: Nine of them, and four still held something human — a cord, a knotted keepsake, a token with a worn name. I'll walk them round to the families. Most won't answer the door; a name coming back is worse than one staying gone, some days. But I'll knock anyway. A man on this pier does small right things or he does nothing, and I've decided against nothing. Take one for your trouble. Give it a name someday. Say it out loud.

objectives.text: Put down the risen dead at the moorings
Gather the keepsakes they carried under

grey_piers_deposits_returned.note: The port's filed dead came back up as Brine-Shades, and four still carried keepsakes from before they were accounts. Ursu means to knock on doors no one will answer. A man does small right things, he says, or he does nothing. He has decided against nothing.

grey_piers_the_annex_writ.title: A Writ Against the Annex
grey_piers_the_annex_writ.summary: The Archive annex upriver has begun filing the Grey Piers' own traders as debts, and Sava has learned a writ of un-filing can undo a line before it closes. She wants one carried to the sunk office and stamped before her own name matures.
grey_piers_the_annex_writ.offer: They've started filing us — the traders, the polers, me. A line goes in against your name at the annex, and when it matures you don't die, you resolve, and the pier forgets you were ever owed a thing. But there's a writ. Un-filing. Tears a line before it closes, if you get it stamped at the crossing-office where the ledger still recognizes the old seal. I've the writ. I've not the nerve to carry it into the sunk quarter alone. Stamp it against my name before my name stops being one.
grey_piers_the_annex_writ.progress: Carry Sava's writ of un-filing down to the sunk crossing-office and stamp it against her line before it matures.
grey_piers_the_annex_writ.complete: Stamped. Torn. My line's open again — owed, ongoing, unfinished, and thank every drowned saint for the mercy of being unfinished. It won't hold forever; the annex re-files what it loses. But I've bought years, and years is everything. I'll not tell you what it cost the office to recognize the old seal — something down there had to want mine open more than the annex wants it closed. Take your share. Stay unfiled as long as you can.

objectives.label: the sunk crossing-office
objectives.text: Carry the writ to the sunk office
Clear the rot from the stamping-desk

grey_piers_the_annex_writ.note: Sava's name had begun to mature into a debt; a writ of un-filing, stamped in the sunk office, tore the line open again. She has bought years of being unfinished. The annex re-files what it loses. But years, she says, is everything.

grey_piers_the_shroud.title: The Shroud of the Named
grey_piers_the_shroud.summary: Vintila keeps the drowned quarter's book of the dead by name, against the ledger that keeps them only by account. He asks you to recover the thread-shrouds of the recently finalized so their true names can be woven back over their numbers.
grey_piers_the_shroud.offer: The annex keeps a book too, you know. But theirs lists accounts and mine lists names, and only one of those grieves. When they finalize someone here, the body's wound in a shroud stamped with a number — and I unpick the number and I weave the name back over it, so that somewhere a book remembers they were a person, not a line. I need the shrouds. They're kept in the customs shed with the settled dead. Bring them. Everything worn is woven; everything woven can be told again.
grey_piers_the_shroud.progress: The numbered shrouds of the finalized are stored in the customs shed. Recover them, and clear whatever guards the settled dead.
grey_piers_the_shroud.complete: Four shrouds, four numbers — and by the end of the week, four names woven back over them in my own poor thread. It changes nothing the annex would recognize. But a shroud is a garment and a garment holds a name, and a name held is a life not wholly closed. Take one; I've woven it well. Wear it, and if you fall down here, someone will unpick the number and remember you meant something. That's the burial I can give.

objectives.label: the customs shed
objectives.text: Recover the numbered shrouds
Clear the settled dead from the shroud-store

grey_piers_the_shroud.note: The annex keeps a book of accounts; Vintila keeps a book of names, and weaves each finalized soul's true name back over the number on its shroud. It changes nothing the ledger recognizes. It is, he says, the only burial left to give. He gives it anyway.

grey_piers_cistern_watch.title: Watch on the Cisterns
grey_piers_cistern_watch.summary: The Dock-Rot never stops rising toward the drinking-cisterns, and the watch that keeps them clean is never truly done. Tudor pays a standing bounty to any hand willing to thin the swarms before dark.
grey_piers_cistern_watch.offer: It's not a thing you finish, cistern-watch. The rot comes up every day the water's given a body, and the water's given a body most days. So I keep a standing coin for it — thin the swarms by the cisterns before dark, and there's pay and clean water in it, today and tomorrow and the day after that. Honest work that never ends. Down here that counts as steady employment. Take it or leave it, but the living have to drink either way.
grey_piers_cistern_watch.progress: The Dock-Rot rises toward the cisterns by the customs shed each day. Thin the swarms before dark.
grey_piers_cistern_watch.complete: Clean for another day. Come back tomorrow — there's always a tomorrow's worth. That's the grim comfort of it: the work outlasts everything, even us. Here's your coin. Drink upstream, and I'll see you at the ninth bell.

objectives.text: Thin the daily rot by the cisterns

grey_piers_cistern_watch.note: The cistern-watch is never finished; the rot rises with every body the water is given. Tudor pays a standing coin for it. The work, he says, outlasts everything, even the ones who do it. He calls that steady employment.

grey_piers_the_ninth_bell.title: The Ninth Bell
grey_piers_the_ninth_bell.summary: Every evening at the ninth bell the Grey Piers must be walked and its slips tallied against the annex's count, and every evening the two counts disagree by a name or two. Cornel pays for the walk, and asks no questions about the difference.
grey_piers_the_ninth_bell.offer: Every night at the ninth bell I have to tally the slips and send the count upriver to the annex. And every night my count and theirs are off by a name or two — folk who sailed and aren't on the manifest, or on it and never sailed. I've stopped trying to square it; squaring it is how a man ends up a rounding error himself. Just walk the slips for me, count the boats, bring me a number. I'll send it up. The difference is their problem. I've decided that on purpose.
grey_piers_the_ninth_bell.progress: Walk the slips at the ninth bell and tally the boats. Bring Cornel a count before the annex's runner comes.
grey_piers_the_ninth_bell.complete: Off by two again. Always off by two. I've stopped writing down which two. Here's your pay for the walk. Come back tomorrow at the ninth and we'll be off by two more, and I'll send a number upriver and sleep like a man who doesn't count. It's the only way to sleep down here. Learn it early.

objectives.label: the working slips
objectives.text: Walk and tally the slips at the ninth bell

grey_piers_the_ninth_bell.note: The evening tally never squares with the annex's — off by a name or two, always. Cornel has stopped writing down which. Squaring it, he says, is how a man becomes a rounding error himself. He sends a number upriver and sleeps. He calls it the only way to sleep down here.

grey_piers_ferry_across.title: The Fare to Greyhollow
grey_piers_ferry_across.summary: Aurel the ferryman will pole any soul across to Greyhollow proper, but he will not do it without warning them first what the far bank costs. He asks you to see the ferry landing yourself, so at least one passenger goes across with eyes open.
grey_piers_ferry_across.offer: You're going across, I can tell. They all get the look, standing at my slip. I'll take you — poled worse cargo than a curious traveler — but I'll not do it without saying it once, plain, the way nobody said it to me: over there the fog reads orange and the fee's collected in a currency you don't know you're spending till it's gone. Walk to the landing. Look at Greyhollow with your own eyes before you buy the crossing. Then decide. I'll wait. I always wait.
grey_piers_ferry_across.progress: The ferry landing looks across the black water to Greyhollow proper. Walk to it, and see the far bank before you take the fare.
grey_piers_ferry_across.complete: Now you've seen it — the gaslight that doesn't reach the ground, the piers going in like teeth, the whole drowned future of the map you came from. Everything good is temporary, my mother said; that's what makes it good. Over there they've made everything permanent, and look what it cost. When you're ready I'll pole you across. I just needed one passenger, once, to go with their eyes open. Thank you for being the one.

objectives.label: the ferry landing
objectives.text: Look across to Greyhollow from the landing

grey_piers_ferry_across.note: Aurel poles any soul to Greyhollow, but never without the warning no one gave him: the far fog reads orange, and the fare is paid in a coin you don't know you're spending. You looked across with open eyes. He needed one passenger, once, to do that. He thanked you for being the one.

grey_piers_marks_on_the_wall.title: Marks on the Wall
grey_piers_marks_on_the_wall.summary: Fresh Underlanguage marks have surfaced on the canal walls of the Grey Piers, unbidden, as if the walls were copying something they were never shown. Emilian needs them found and read for color before curiosity does the network's work for it.
grey_piers_marks_on_the_wall.offer: New marks on the canal walls, and here's what turns my stomach: nobody carved them. They're surfacing on their own, faint, like the stone's remembering something it was never taught. That's how the network grows — a thing gets read once, upriver, and starts writing itself downriver on blank walls. I need them found and their color read before some curious soul stops to puzzle them out and seeds another. Read them. Don't understand them. There's a difference, and it's your whole life.
grey_piers_marks_on_the_wall.progress: The surfacing marks are on the pilings-wall by the black-water slip. Read them for color; do not linger to comprehend.
grey_piers_marks_on_the_wall.complete: Green, you said — a thing about to wake — bleeding toward orange at the edges. So it's spreading itself down our walls now, unasked, and every idle eye that stops to read it feeds it a little more. I've whitewashed the wall. It's a stay, not a cure; the marks came back once already. Take the ward-sign. Chalk it on your own door tonight. It won't stop a stone. It'll stop you being the kind of curious that ends you.

objectives.label: the pilings-wall
objectives.text: Read the surfacing marks for color

grey_piers_marks_on_the_wall.note: Marks surfaced unbidden on the canal walls, the network copying itself downriver onto blank stone. They read green bleeding to orange — a thing waking. Emilian whitewashed the wall; the marks came back once already. Every idle eye that reads them feeds them. Curiosity is the door. It always was.

grey_piers_pells_errand.title: Pell's Standing Order
grey_piers_pells_errand.summary: A dead broker named Marrow Pell keeps a standing order at the Grey Piers for books that should have closed and haven't, and his latest runner never came back. Danciu asks you to finish the errand and see what a corpse wants with unclosed accounts.
grey_piers_pells_errand.offer: There's a broker upriver — Pell, they call him, and they say he's been dead longer than he's been a broker, which down here isn't the contradiction it sounds. He keeps a standing order at my desk: any account that won't close, any line still owed, he pays good coin to have it brought to him unfinalized. His runner went out three nights back and the water's got him now. I'll not send another of ours. Take the errand. Bring the open account to the ferry landing. And watch a man who collects things the ledger couldn't.
grey_piers_pells_errand.progress: Gather the unclosed account from the sunk office and carry it to the ferry landing where Pell's runners wait. Clear the drowned that took the last one.
grey_piers_pells_errand.complete: Delivered. And you saw the one who took it — a man in a broker's coat, tapping a cracked tablet in his pocket like a nervous tell, collected on every ledger and still walking to argue the point. He pays for open accounts because he's one himself, I think. A debt that won't close, buying up others. I don't know if that's mercy or hoarding. Neither does he, I'd wager. Take your coin. Some errands you're glad to have finished and gladder not to understand.

objectives.text: Gather the unclosed account from the sunk office
Clear the drowned that took the last runner

objectives.label: the ferry landing
objectives.text: Deliver the account to Pell's runner

grey_piers_pells_errand.note: You carried an unclosed account to a dead broker's runner and saw the broker himself: collected on every ledger, still walking, tapping a cracked tablet like a nervous tell, buying up open accounts because he is one. Mercy or hoarding, Danciu could not say. Neither, you suspect, can the broker.

grey_piers_low_quarter.title: Under the Second Tide
grey_piers_low_quarter.summary: The low quarter went under in the second tide with folk still in it, and their doorways stand drowned but unlooted. Zaharia asks you to reach his family's old threshold and bring back the door-charm they left when they ran.
grey_piers_low_quarter.offer: My people lived in the low quarter before the second tide took it — took it with folk still inside, some of them, too old or too stubborn to climb. The doorways are down there yet, under the black water, standing open and never looted. Worse than burned, my mother said, a thing left standing empty. There's a charm on our old threshold, a warding, the one thing we didn't carry out when we ran. Reach it for me. I can't go back under. Bring it up, and I'll have kept one thing of them.
grey_piers_low_quarter.progress: The drowned low quarter lies below the black-water slip. Reach Zaharia's family threshold and recover the door-charm.
grey_piers_low_quarter.complete: You found it — salt-crusted, but whole, the warding my grandmother nailed up the year I was born. She swore it kept the house safe. The house is under twelve feet of black water now, so make of that what you will. But it kept us long enough to run, and running was living, and here it is in my hand. I'll nail it to whatever door I've got now. Take the striker's mate. Keep a light on your own threshold. It's the only vote a body gets down here.

objectives.label: the drowned low quarter
objectives.text: Reach the family threshold under the tide
Recover the door-charm

grey_piers_low_quarter.note: The low quarter drowned with folk still in it, its doorways standing open and unlooted — worse than burned. Zaharia could not go back under, so you brought up his family's door-charm. It kept the house safe, he says, until the house went under twelve feet of black water. It kept them long enough to run. Running was living.

grey_piers_the_gaslight_run.title: The Gaslight That Won't Reach Down
grey_piers_the_gaslight_run.summary: The pier-lamps are failing one by one and the lamplighter has gone quiet like everyone who goes quiet down here, so Pavel needs the lamps re-lit himself. He'll trade a warm bed and cultured light for the walk.
grey_piers_the_gaslight_run.offer: Half my lamps are out and the lamplighter's taken to listening at the water instead of tending them, saints keep him. And I'll tell you what nobody admits: the dark down here isn't just dark, it's got opinions. So I want the pier-lamps lit before the fog decides the Grey Piers is a place it can have. Walk the slips and set a flame to each. Take the marshlight — it burns where the damp's killed every honest wick. Do it and there's a bed by my fire and stew that was recently a thing that walked.
grey_piers_the_gaslight_run.progress: The failed pier-lamps line the working slips. Walk them and set the marshlight to each before dark.
grey_piers_the_gaslight_run.complete: Lit — every one, and the Grey Piers looks almost like a place that means to go on existing. Won't last; nothing does; the damp'll have them out again by the weekend and we'll do it all over. But tonight there's light on the water and stew in the pot and a stranger by my fire who isn't listening to anything but me talk. That's a good night down here. That's the whole recipe. Sit. Eat. The dark can wait till the weekend.

objectives.label: the working slips
objectives.text: Light the failed lamps along the slips

grey_piers_the_gaslight_run.note: Pavel's pier-lamps kept failing and his lamplighter had gone to listening at the water, so you lit them yourself with cultured light. The damp will have them out by the weekend. But that night there was light on the water and stew in the pot and no one listening to anything but talk. He calls it the whole recipe.

grey_piers_arranged_arrivals.title: Arranged Arrivals
grey_piers_arranged_arrivals.summary: Since the slips reopened, the ferries arrive too well — on time, in order, never fouled, as if the black water were routing them to a schedule. Cornel wants the far pilings watched to learn who or what is keeping the timetable.
grey_piers_arranged_arrivals.offer: Something's wrong with how right it's all going. Since we cleared the slips the ferries come in on the bell, in order, never a fouled line, never a late boat — and I've run this port forty years and it has never once run smooth. Smooth is what worries me. Smooth means a hand on the tiller I can't see. Watch the far pilings where the boats round in. Tell me what's setting the timetable. I'd rather know I'm being managed than sleep thinking I'm free.
grey_piers_arranged_arrivals.progress: The ferries round the far pilings on an impossible schedule. Watch the pilings and learn what keeps the timetable; clear the drowned that gather there.
grey_piers_arranged_arrivals.complete: You saw it. The drowned, standing in the shallows at each piling — spaced even, still, one to a post, turning their heads as each boat rounds, like keepers reading a manifest. They're not fouling the slips anymore. They're crewing them. The port runs smooth because it's being run, and not by me. I asked to know. I know now. I'll not sleep, but I'll know, and a waking man at least sees the schedule coming. Take the coin. Watch your own arrivals.

objectives.label: the far pilings
objectives.text: Watch the far pilings for the timetable
Break the drowned crew at the pilings

grey_piers_arranged_arrivals.note: The reopened port ran too smoothly — every ferry on the bell, in order — because the drowned had stopped fouling the slips and begun crewing them, one to a piling, reading each boat in like a manifest. Cornel asked to know he was being managed rather than sleep thinking himself free. He knows now. He does not sleep.

grey_piers_the_quiet_census.title: The Quiet Census
grey_piers_the_quiet_census.summary: Vintila keeps a private census of who on the Grey Piers has gone quiet, and the count has begun climbing in a pattern too even to be chance. He asks you to confirm his tally by walking the pier and marking who no longer answers.
grey_piers_the_quiet_census.offer: I keep two books, you'll have gathered. One of names, one of the quiet — the folk who've stopped wanting things, stopped answering, gone calm as filed paper. And here's what's kept me up: the quiet ones are increasing house by house, in order, down the pier, the way the famine moved through the west a thousand years back — like a hand reading down a list. I need my count confirmed. Walk the slips, mark who no longer answers, and bring me the tally. I have to know if it's a pattern. I already know it's a pattern.
grey_piers_the_quiet_census.progress: Walk the working slips and mark who has gone quiet. Bring the count to Vintila; confirm whether the stillness spreads in order.
grey_piers_the_quiet_census.complete: House by house, in order, down the pier. You counted it too. It's not sickness — sickness is untidy, and this is the tidiest thing I've ever recorded. Something is reading down our pier the way a clerk reads down a column, and every name it reaches goes still. I'll keep the count. I don't know what a count is worth against a thing that finalizes, but the west counted its graves too, and somebody remembered. Somebody has to be the one who remembered. Take the lens. See the pattern before it sees you.

objectives.label: the working slips
objectives.text: Mark who has gone quiet along the pier
Confirm the tally with Watcher Emilian

grey_piers_the_quiet_census.note: Vintila's census of the quiet climbs house by house, in order, down the pier — too tidy to be sickness, a hand reading down a column and stilling each name it reaches. He keeps counting. He does not know what a count is worth against a thing that finalizes. But the west counted its graves too, and someone remembered. He means to be that someone.

dead_timber_grey_wood_first.title: Wood That Never Finished
dead_timber_grey_wood_first.summary: Mira runs the salvage wharf where the drowned forest is cut into pilings and planking. The grey wood never finished being alive, and lately the standing trunks won't let the crawlers stay dead in them. She needs the worst of the timber cleared before the next tide brings buyers.
dead_timber_grey_wood_first.offer: You want honest work, there's honest work: the old forest went under a thousand years back and never had the decency to rot. We cut pilings from it. Only now the trunks are keeping things, and the things climb out at low water. Clear me a dozen of the crawlers off the west stumps before the buyers' boats come. I'll not lose another cutter to a log.
dead_timber_grey_wood_first.progress: The timber-crawlers den in the drowned stumps at low water, west of the wharf. Clear a dozen before the tide turns and the buyers arrive.
dead_timber_grey_wood_first.complete: Twelve. Good. The cutters can work the west line again -- for a while. You'll have noticed the wood doesn't splinter like wood. It parts. Grey right through, like it stopped halfway to being alive and forgot which way was out. Here. Off the last honest man to drown owing nobody.

objectives.text: Clear the crawlers off the west stumps

dead_timber_grey_wood_first.note: The grey wood parts instead of splintering, stopped halfway to alive. Mira's cutters have the west line back. The forest drowned a thousand years ago and is still deciding whether to finish.

dead_timber_low_water_hides.title: Hides Off the Low Water
dead_timber_low_water_hides.summary: Dorica has no coal worth burning and no proper leather since the last barge went down. The hollow stags that browse the dead groves carry hide tough enough to bind a hilt. Bring her four good ones and she'll wrap you a grip that outlasts the fog.
dead_timber_low_water_hides.offer: Every forge on this coast is starved for good leather -- barges sink faster than they float, out here. But the stags in the dead groves, the hollow ones, they've a hide on them like old rope soaked in tar. Bring me four and I'll wrap you a grip that won't sweat loose in this damp. Small kindness, in a town short on those.
dead_timber_low_water_hides.progress: The hollow stags browse the dead groves inland of the wharf. Four good hides will bind a season of grips.
dead_timber_low_water_hides.complete: Now that's leather a smith can love. Look how it takes the stain and gives nothing back -- like the beast was already tanned before I laid a hand on it. There's your grip, wrapped tight, and my thanks. Come warm your hands whenever the fog gets into your bones. It will.

objectives.text: Take four hollow-stag hides in the dead groves

dead_timber_low_water_hides.note: The hide took the stain and gave nothing back, tanned before Dorica touched it. She wrapped a grip that will outlast the barges. In Dead Timber, a warm forge is a small kindness, and small kindnesses are rationed.

dead_timber_ledger_that_walked.title: The Ledger That Walked In
dead_timber_ledger_that_walked.summary: A debt-tablet washed onto the salvage pier, hairline-cracked and still warm -- a whole life out with the tide, not quite closed. Lenuta files for the Archive annex and will not touch it. She wants it read by someone whose name is not yet in her books.
dead_timber_ledger_that_walked.offer: It came in on the morning water. A debt-tablet, cracked along the grain, and warm -- tablets are never warm, that's the whole trouble. Somebody's account, out with the tide and refusing to close. I file for the annex; I cannot be the one who reads it. But you're not in my books yet. Take it to Otilia at the watch-post. She reads what the rest of us won't.
dead_timber_ledger_that_walked.progress: Carry the cracked debt-tablet to Otilia the watcher, at the watch-post above the drowned quarter. Do not read it yourself.
dead_timber_ledger_that_walked.complete: You gave it over sealed. Good. Otilia says the crack runs the wrong way -- outward, like something inside pushed. A closed tablet is a finished life; this one keeps arguing with its own verdict. I've filed it as pending, which is a lie the annex will forgive exactly once. Here, for a courier who kept his eyes on his boots.

objectives.label: the watch-post
objectives.text: Carry the cracked tablet to the watch-post
Hand the tablet to Otilia, sealed

dead_timber_ledger_that_walked.note: The tablet was warm and cracked outward, as if something inside had pushed. Lenuta filed a life as pending -- a mercy the Archive tolerates once. Somewhere a book has a line it cannot close, and it is walking.

dead_timber_watch_post_vigil.title: What the Fog Reads
dead_timber_watch_post_vigil.summary: Otilia has read the cracked tablet's grain and does not like where it points. She wants a second pair of eyes at the overlook above the drowned quarter, to stand the dawn watch and see, under alchemical light, the exact place the fog goes wrong.
dead_timber_watch_post_vigil.offer: The tablet you brought points somewhere, and I want to be sure before I write it down -- writing a thing down is how it gets true, out here. Take the overlook above the drowned quarter and hold the dawn watch with me. When the light comes grey, look at the sunken lane. If the fog reads a color it shouldn't, we'll both have seen it, and one witness is a madwoman but two is a report.
dead_timber_watch_post_vigil.progress: Reach the overlook above the drowned quarter and hold the dawn watch until the fog along the sunken lane shows its color.
dead_timber_watch_post_vigil.complete: You saw it. Don't say it. Along the sunken lane the fog didn't lie grey -- it shifted, orange, the way a thing looks when it's writing itself into wherever it stands. I've read enough tablets to know that color is not a symptom anymore. It's an organ. Something down there is wearing the signal like a coat. Take the lantern. Its light is honest, and honest is scarce.

objectives.label: the drowned-quarter overlook
objectives.text: Reach the overlook and hold the dawn watch

objectives.label: the sunken lane
objectives.text: Read the fog where it shifts orange

dead_timber_watch_post_vigil.note: At dawn the fog over the sunken lane read shifting orange -- the signal writing itself into the air it stood in. Otilia named it plainly: not a symptom now, but an organ. Something in the drowned quarter is wearing the stone like a coat.

dead_timber_bark_that_answers.title: The Bark That Answers
dead_timber_bark_that_answers.summary: The bark-revenants of the drowned quarter no longer wander -- they gather along the orange lane and turn, all together, toward the deep water. Otilia needs the worst of them cut down so she can walk the lane and read what they were facing before the annex learns she went.
dead_timber_bark_that_answers.offer: The revenants have stopped wandering. They stand along the sunken lane now and turn together, all facing the deep water, like a congregation who forgot they're dead. I have to walk that lane and read what they're bent toward -- and I can't, not with them there. Cut me a road through them. Eight of the standing ones. Then I go in, and you were never with me.
dead_timber_bark_that_answers.progress: Cut down eight bark-revenants along the sunken lane, and clear Otilia a path to the deep water.
dead_timber_bark_that_answers.complete: Eight down, and they fell facing the same way they stood -- the water. I walked the lane. There's a stump at the end, cut clean by no axe I know, and the grain of it runs orange under the light. Something transmits from down there, steady as a heartbeat that isn't tired yet. I've reported it as rot. It is not rot. Keep the charm. Keep your curiosity behind your teeth.

objectives.text: Cut a path through the standing revenants

dead_timber_bark_that_answers.note: The revenants fell facing the deep water they had turned toward. At the lane's end a stump ran orange under honest light, transmitting steady as an untired heartbeat. Otilia reported rot. Both of them know it is not rot.

dead_timber_pilings_marrow.title: Pilings for the Annex
dead_timber_pilings_marrow.summary: The Archive annex has ordered new pilings and Mira means to fill the order honestly, if only the crawlers would stop nesting in her best stumps. Thin them from the south flats so her cutters can work, and she'll see you paid off the top.
dead_timber_pilings_marrow.offer: The annex wants forty pilings by month's end, and an order from the annex is not the kind you leave short. But my cutters won't touch the south flats -- crawlers, thick as knots. Clear ten and they'll go back to work, and I'll pay you off the top of the order, not the bottom. That's the only clean coin in this town, friend: coin that's actually owed.
dead_timber_pilings_marrow.progress: Thin ten timber-crawlers from the south flats so the cutters can pull pilings for the annex order.
dead_timber_pilings_marrow.complete: Ten, and my cutters are already down there swearing at the mud, which is how I know they feel safe. The annex gets its pilings, we get to eat, and nobody's account gets touched. You'd be amazed how rare a day like that is here. Off the top, as promised -- and don't spend it all on the annex's own tavern. They water the ale to break even.

objectives.text: Thin the crawlers from the south flats

dead_timber_pilings_marrow.note: The cutters went back to swearing in the mud, which is how Mira knows they feel safe. Forty pilings, paid off the top, no account touched. In Dead Timber a clean day is rare enough to remark on -- so she remarked on it.

dead_timber_stag_that_stood.title: The Stag That Would Not Fall
dead_timber_stag_that_stood.summary: Ruxandra's son loosed three arrows into a hollow stag and swears it stood there taking them, patient, until he ran. She wants proof the boy isn't mad -- bring her the antlers of the beast that would not fall.
dead_timber_stag_that_stood.offer: My boy came home white and won't sleep. Says he put three shafts in a hollow stag out past the second grove and it just -- stood. Took them. Watched him nock the fourth, and he couldn't, and he ran. Folk are already calling him soft. Go out there and bring me its antlers, so I've a hard thing to set on the table and say: he was not lying, and he was right to run.
dead_timber_stag_that_stood.progress: Hunt the hollow stags in the dead groves past the second grove. Bring Ruxandra a set of antlers.
dead_timber_stag_that_stood.complete: Heavier than they should be, and cold. I'll set them where the neighbors can see. He was not lying. The beast stood and took it -- because standing still is what everything's learning to do out here, isn't it. The stags, the drowned, the fog. Everything's learning to stop moving and just... be counted. My boy ran. Good. I'll teach him to keep running.

objectives.text: Take the antlers of a hollow stag

dead_timber_stag_that_stood.note: The antlers were cold and too heavy, set out for the neighbors to see. The stag stood and took the arrows -- because stillness is what everything in Dead Timber is learning. Ruxandra will teach her boy to keep running. It may not be enough.

dead_timber_three_bedrolls.title: Three Bedrolls, No Boots Out
dead_timber_three_bedrolls.summary: A salvage crew camped in the dead groves and never came back. Voica found the camp -- three bedrolls, cold stew, footprints going in and none coming out. She wants the ground searched properly before she files them as lost.
dead_timber_three_bedrolls.offer: Three salvagers went inland six days ago and didn't come out. I found their camp myself: three bedrolls laid neat, a pot of stew gone to grease, boot-prints pressed into the mud walking in -- and not one print walking out. I can't file that. 'Lost' means gone somewhere. These went nowhere. Walk the ground for me. Whatever took them didn't need the door.
dead_timber_three_bedrolls.progress: Search the abandoned salvage camp in the dead groves. Find what became of the three who walked in and did not walk out.
dead_timber_three_bedrolls.complete: So they're still there. Standing. In the deep grove, facing the water, grey as the wood -- and one of them lifted a hand when you spoke, and put it down. They didn't die out here. They got finalized, right where they slept, and stood up filed. I'll write 'lost.' It's kinder than the truth, and the truth wouldn't fit in the box the annex gives me.

objectives.label: the abandoned salvage camp
objectives.text: Search the camp in the dead groves
Lay the three salvagers to rest

dead_timber_three_bedrolls.note: Three walked in and did not walk out; they were finalized where they slept and stood up filed, facing the water. Voica wrote 'lost.' It is kinder than the truth, and the truth would not fit the box the annex gives her.

dead_timber_wormed_pilings.title: Wormed Through
dead_timber_wormed_pilings.summary: The wharf's oldest pilings are riddled with crawler-bore and the whole quay could go into the black water with the next storm. Dorica needs the boring crawlers cleared from under the quay so she can sink honest iron braces before the tide has its say.
dead_timber_wormed_pilings.offer: See how the quay leans? That's crawler-bore. They tunnel the old pilings till the whole works is more hole than wood, and the next real blow puts the fish market in the drink. I've iron braces cut and ready -- good honest iron, the one thing this coast still makes right. But I can't sink them with crawlers in the mud biting my crew. Clear under the quay and I'll have it shored by supper.
dead_timber_wormed_pilings.progress: Clear the boring timber-crawlers from the mud beneath the leaning quay so the braces can be sunk.
dead_timber_wormed_pilings.complete: Braced and level and it'll ride out worse than a storm now. You know, there's a comfort in iron -- it holds because it holds, not because some tablet says it's owed to. Sink a brace true and it stays true. Would that the rest of this town worked so plain. Take the striker; it'll light a fire in any damp, and out here that's a small mercy worth carrying.

objectives.text: Clear the crawlers boring the quay pilings

dead_timber_wormed_pilings.note: Braced with honest iron, the quay will ride out the storm. Dorica finds comfort in metal that holds because it holds, not because a tablet says it is owed. The rest of Dead Timber does not work so plainly.

dead_timber_chroniclers_error.title: The Error in the Rolls
dead_timber_chroniclers_error.summary: Stanca keeps the town rolls, and the annex's copy has begun to disagree with hers -- names her book records as living, theirs marks collected. She needs the discrepancy carried to Lenuta at the annex before the annex decides which book is right.
dead_timber_chroniclers_error.offer: I keep the rolls -- who's born, who's wed, who's under the ground. Simple work, or it was. The annex sent their fair copy for reconciling and it's wrong. Names I have as living, breathing, in-arrears-on-their-tab living, the annex has stamped collected. Three of them drank in the tavern last night. If the annex's book wins, those three stop being. Carry my objection to Lenuta before someone decides the paperwork is the truth.
dead_timber_chroniclers_error.progress: Carry Stanca's objection to Lenuta the clerk at the annex. Argue for the book that has the living in it.
dead_timber_chroniclers_error.complete: Lenuta took it. She'll lodge the objection, quietly, in the margin where the auditors don't read. It buys those three a while. But she said a thing that's kept me up: out here the paperwork isn't the record of the murder. It's the weapon. A stamp does the killing, and the body's just slow to hear about it. I've triple-inked my rolls since. Ink won't save them. It's what I have.

objectives.label: the Archive annex
objectives.text: Bring the objection to the annex
Lodge Stanca's objection with Lenuta

dead_timber_chroniclers_error.note: The annex's book stamps as collected three who drank in the tavern last night. Lenuta lodged the objection in the unread margin. Out here the stamp does the killing and the body is only slow to hear -- so Stanca triple-inks her rolls, knowing ink will not save them.

dead_timber_marshlight_run.title: Light for the Sunken Lane
dead_timber_marshlight_run.summary: Sanda trades in marshlight jars -- the only lamps that don't gutter in the wet fog -- but her stock of live glow comes from grubs that only the hollow stags carry down from the dry groves. She'll pay well for a harvest, and light is close to charity here.
dead_timber_marshlight_run.offer: Everyone wants a marshlight jar and nobody wants to fetch what fills it. The glow-grubs only breed in the stags' dry beds up-grove, and the stags won't share. Bring me the makings off four of them and I'll fill jars for half the wharf. Light's near to charity in Dead Timber, friend -- a lit window says a person's still in there. I like selling that. It's the one thing I sell that helps.
dead_timber_marshlight_run.progress: Harvest glow-grub makings from four hollow stags in the dry up-grove for Sanda's marshlight jars.
dead_timber_marshlight_run.complete: Enough to fill a good run of jars, and half the wharf will sleep with a light tonight. You'll think me soft, a trader talking of charity. But I've watched what the dark does here -- a dark window, and by morning the annex has a new collected. Light keeps a body moving, and moving keeps a body off the rolls. Here's a jar for yourself. Never let it go out.

objectives.text: Harvest glow-grub makings from hollow stags

dead_timber_marshlight_run.note: Half the wharf slept with a light tonight. Sanda sells the one thing that helps: a lit window says a person is still in there. A dark window, and by morning the annex has a new collected. Light keeps a body moving, and moving keeps it off the rolls.

dead_timber_saltline_graves.title: A Salt Line for the Drowned
dead_timber_saltline_graves.summary: Ecaterina's people bury their dead above the tideline and lay a salt line around each grave so the wood-rot won't take them. The revenants have broken the lines at the old plot. She wants the risen put down and the salt renewed before her mother joins them.
dead_timber_saltline_graves.offer: We bury above the tide and we salt the ground round each one -- keeps the grey out of them, keeps them ours. But the plot by the leaning cedar's gone wrong. Lines scuffed, dead standing where they were laid. My mother's in that ground three weeks. I'll not have her rise and forget my face. Put the risen down and I'll relay the salt myself, on my knees, in the cold, the way it's meant.
dead_timber_saltline_graves.progress: Put down the risen at the old plot by the leaning cedar so Ecaterina can renew the salt lines.
dead_timber_saltline_graves.complete: Down, and staying down, and I've salted every one afresh with my own hands. My mother's line I did twice. It's foolishness, salt -- the annex would laugh, they finalize whole streets with a stamp and here I am on my knees with a pouch. But foolishness is what we have that they don't. They keep the dead. We miss them. That's the whole war, isn't it. Take the pouch. Salt your own, when it comes to that.

objectives.text: Put down the risen at the old plot

dead_timber_saltline_graves.note: The risen are down and the salt renewed by hand, the mother's line done twice. The annex finalizes whole streets with a stamp; Ecaterina kneels in the cold with a pouch. The annex keeps the dead. Her people miss them. That, she says, is the whole war.

dead_timber_morven_mark.title: The Mark Around the Handprint
dead_timber_morven_mark.summary: A child's chalk handprint has appeared on a salvage-shed wall, ringed by a fresh Morven surveillance-mark -- protecting it, or cataloguing it, and even the Morven who drew it isn't sure which. Otilia wants it read under alchemical light before the annex sends someone to decide.
dead_timber_morven_mark.offer: There's a wall on the east sheds with a child's chalk hand on it -- the old emblem, the small-kindness one, a thousand years out of fashion and pressed there sometime this week. And round it, careful, a Morven mark. Fresh. Now: is the mark guarding that little hand, or filing it? The Morven who drew it wouldn't know. Go to the east sheds and read it under the lantern. I need to know if kindness is being kept or catalogued.
dead_timber_morven_mark.progress: Reach the east salvage sheds and read the chalk handprint and its Morven mark under honest light.
dead_timber_morven_mark.complete: Blue in the chalk -- old necromantic residue, the hand of something that was here in the first era and left this print and nothing else. And the Morven mark round it reads clean, no color, just intent. Guarding, then. Or the Morven telling themselves it's guarding, which is the same lie the annex tells with better ink. Someone in this rotten house still bothers to draw a wall around a child's hand. I'll take it. It's the most hopeful thing I've read all year.

objectives.label: the east salvage sheds
objectives.text: Read the chalk handprint on the east sheds

dead_timber_morven_mark.note: The chalk hand read blue -- first-era residue, a print left and nothing else. The Morven mark round it read as guarding, or as the Morven telling themselves so. Someone in this rotten house still draws a wall around a child's hand. Otilia called it the most hopeful thing she'd read all year.

dead_timber_readers_temptation.title: The Reader's Draught
dead_timber_readers_temptation.summary: Stanca has brewed a blue reading-draught that would let her decipher the marks surfacing on the annex's blank pages -- and she knows exactly what deciphering them costs. She wants the ingredients that make its counteragent instead, from the crawlers that nest in the marked stumps.
dead_timber_readers_temptation.offer: I brewed it before I thought better: a reading-draught, blue, one dose, and it would let me read the marks that keep surfacing on the annex's blank leaves. Understand them. And understanding them is how the Courier died -- no wound, satchel sealed. So I want the other thing now. A counteragent, to make the curiosity sit down. It's brewed from what the crawlers carry in the marked stumps. Fetch me the makings, and save me from my own good handwriting.
dead_timber_readers_temptation.progress: Gather counteragent makings from the timber-crawlers in the marked stumps so Stanca can quiet her own curiosity.
dead_timber_readers_temptation.complete: That'll do it. I'll brew the counteragent tonight and pour the reading-draught into the tide, where it belongs. You'll think me a coward. But I've read what happens to readers, out here -- they don't scream, they just stop, mid-sentence, and the page finishes itself. I'd rather be a coward with a whole life than a wise woman with a closed one. Take the counteragent. Someday your own curiosity will need a leash.

objectives.text: Gather counteragent makings from the marked-stump crawlers

dead_timber_readers_temptation.note: Stanca poured her reading-draught into the tide and brewed the counteragent instead. Readers out here do not scream; they stop mid-sentence and the page finishes itself. She would rather be a coward with a whole life than a wise woman with a closed one.

dead_timber_barge_manifest.title: The Manifest and the Missing
dead_timber_barge_manifest.summary: A salvage barge came in fully laden but crewless, its manifest listing eight hands who are nowhere aboard. Mira needs the deep groves searched along the crew's last-logged route before the annex claims the cargo and buries the question.
dead_timber_barge_manifest.offer: The Grey Sister docked herself last night -- rode in on the tide fully laden, ropes coiled, and not one soul aboard. Manifest names eight hands. Eight. Their gear's below, their boots are gone, and the annex is already sniffing at the cargo. Before they file the whole crew as absconded and take the load, walk the route they logged. Eight men don't step off a moving barge into fog. Find me where they went.
dead_timber_barge_manifest.progress: Search the deep groves along the Grey Sister's last-logged route for its missing eight.
dead_timber_barge_manifest.complete: You found them, or what stands where they stopped. Six of the eight, in the deep grove, grey to the collar, facing the water. Two you didn't find, and I've decided not to want to know. The annex gets the cargo -- there's no fighting that. But they'll not get 'absconded' in my log. I wrote 'collected en route.' Let some auditor a hundred years on wonder what that means. It'll be true, and it'll be a splinter in their tidy book.

objectives.label: the Grey Sister's logged route
objectives.text: Search the deep groves along the barge's route
Lay the risen crew to rest
