You were scheduled to make them, so the maze could keep them. That's the cruelty of this place: it doesn't stop you doing good. It waits for the good to become a line it can carry. Take the ledger-work. Knowing it doesn't help. Not knowing it is worse.

objectives.label: Marrow Pell's annex
objectives.text: Reach the broker's open books
Show Florea what the annex keeps open

canal_maze_marrow_pells_books.note: Marrow Pell is dead and keeps books regardless. The accounts you settled up the long road were not closed -- they were scheduled, filed here, still owing. The maze does not stop you doing good. It waits for the good to become a line.

canal_maze_the_ninth_lock.title: The Ninth Lock
canal_maze_the_ninth_lock.summary: The ninth lock has not cycled in a fortnight, jammed by a standing crowd that will not step back from the gate -- the finalized, moving like closed files, waiting on a verdict that already came. Lazar needs the water running before the maze backs up into the living quarters.
canal_maze_the_ninth_lock.offer: Ninth lock's jammed. Not the gear -- the gate's clear of rot, I checked. It's a crowd. Standing in the lock chamber, tide after tide, not stepping back so the water can turn. The finalized do that -- move like a book that's already closed, wait on a verdict that came and went. I can't turn the maze with them in the throat of it, and if the water backs up it comes into the rooms where folk still breathe. Clear the lock. Gently as it'll allow, and it won't allow much.
canal_maze_the_ninth_lock.progress: The finalized crowd the ninth lock chamber and will not step back. Reach it, and clear the throat of the lock so the water can turn.
canal_maze_the_ninth_lock.complete: Water's turning. Heard the gate cycle from here -- first time in a fortnight. You did what had to be done, and I won't pretend it was clean, because there's no clean in clearing folk who were already finished. They were owed a verdict and they got it and still they stood there, waiting to be told again. Take your due. And drink something warm topside. You've earned the forgetting.

objectives.label: the ninth lock
objectives.text: Reach the jammed lock chamber
Clear the throat of the lock

canal_maze_the_ninth_lock.note: The finalized stood in the lock's throat a fortnight, waiting on a verdict already delivered. The water turns again now. There is no clean way to clear folk who were already finished, and Lazar did not pretend there was.

canal_maze_keep_her_incurious.title: Keep Her Incurious
canal_maze_keep_her_incurious.summary: Toma's niece has begun asking the maze its questions -- what the marks say, why the water is warm in one spot. In this city, curiosity is the door the wrong things come through. He wants her kept busy, kept laughing, kept from wondering aloud.
canal_maze_keep_her_incurious.offer: My sister's girl is nine and clever as a whip, saints keep her, and that's the trouble. She's started asking the walls their meaning. Why's that stone warm. What's the ring round the handprint spell. In this city a question is a door, and I want her door shut. Not shut hard -- shut warm. Sit with her, tell her the daft old stories, teach her the counting rhyme that means nothing. Make being a child louder than the maze. That's the whole ward, down here.
canal_maze_keep_her_incurious.progress: The girl waits in the taproom, asking the walls their questions. Sit with Toma's niece and make being a child louder than the maze.
canal_maze_keep_her_incurious.complete: Listen to that -- she's got the counting rhyme now, the one that's just numbers and nonsense, and she's chalking hopscotch instead of copying the marks. That's it. That's the win, small as it is. Down here you don't beat the thing. You just keep one girl laughing louder than it a while longer. Take the warding. And come back. She likes you, and I sleep better when she's laughing.

objectives.text: Fetch Gavril's old counting rhyme for the girl
Sit with the girl and keep her incurious

canal_maze_keep_her_incurious.note: The girl chalks hopscotch now, and sings a rhyme that is only numbers and nonsense. You do not beat the maze. You keep one child laughing louder than it, a while longer. Toma sleeps better for the sound.

canal_maze_under_the_boards.title: Under the Boards
canal_maze_under_the_boards.summary: When the tide runs low and the watch grows careless, the dock-rot comes up through the boards in a chittering carpet. Horia sweeps the walkway that his family has swept for longer than the family remembers why.
canal_maze_under_the_boards.offer: Low tide again, and you know what low tide brings up through the planks. My people have swept this walkway since before anyone could tell you why we started -- rot comes up, we sweep it down, that's the whole inheritance. It'll be back tomorrow, and the day after, world without end. But not tonight if you help me. Clear the boards, and I'll stand you a dry seat and a wet cup. Small mercy. They're the only size that keeps here.
canal_maze_under_the_boards.progress: The dock-rot swarms up through the low boards along the walkway. Sweep it back down before the tide turns.
canal_maze_under_the_boards.complete: Boards are clear. They'll rot up again by the next low water -- they always do, that's the arrangement -- but tonight the walkway's ours and dry and that's a whole entire victory the size it comes in. Here's your cup and your coin. Same time tomorrow, if your feet still fit the maze. Mine never leave it.

objectives.text: Sweep the rot off the low boards

canal_maze_under_the_boards.note: Rot comes up, the boards are swept, and by the next low water it is back -- an inheritance whose reason nobody can name. Tonight the walkway is dry. In the maze, a dry walkway is a whole entire victory, the only size they come in.

canal_maze_the_watchers_round.title: The Watcher's Round
canal_maze_the_watchers_round.summary: Oprea walks the Morven round, chalking marks around the maze's small warm things. He has never been told whether the marks protect them or catalogue them for collection, and he has stopped being sure he wants to know.
canal_maze_the_watchers_round.offer: I draw the marks. That's my trade -- the Morven round, wall to wall, a careful ring around anything the fog reads strange. A warm print. A stone that shouldn't be warm. A door somebody keeps opening. I was taught the marks mean guard this. My grandfather was taught they mean file this. Same marks. Same hand. Walk the round with me and tell me which we're doing, because I've drawn ten thousand rings and I no longer know if I've been minding this city or measuring it for the water.
canal_maze_the_watchers_round.progress: Oprea's round threads the maze from mark to chalked mark. Walk it to the collector's landing, then bring your read back to him.
canal_maze_the_watchers_round.complete: You saw them all -- the print, the warm stone, the door. Rings around every one, careful as a promise, and not a soul who can say if the promise is to keep them or to keep track of them. That's the Morven for you. We were the ears that saved lives, once. Now we're the ears, and nobody's checked in a thousand years what the mouth does with what we hear. Take the file-work. And if you ever learn which it is -- guard or catalogue -- don't tell me kindly. Tell me true.

objectives.label: the collector's landing
objectives.text: Walk the Morven round to the landing
Bring your read of the marks back to Oprea

canal_maze_the_watchers_round.note: The Morven were the ears that once saved lives. They are still the ears, and no one has checked in a thousand years what the mouth does with what they hear. Oprea draws rings that guard, or file. He asked to be told true, not kindly.

canal_maze_salt_the_thresholds.title: Salt the Thresholds
canal_maze_salt_the_thresholds.summary: The old folk of the maze lay salt across their doorsteps at dusk -- not to keep anything out, Gavril says, but to remind whatever comes that a door is a thing with two sides. He is short on hands and long on thresholds.
canal_maze_salt_the_thresholds.offer: Dusk's coming, and my knees have opinions about stairs. Every door in my row wants its salt-line laid before dark -- the old way, a straight white line across the sill. Doesn't keep a thing out, mind. Salt never did. It just reminds them there's a door, and a door's got an inside and an outside, and they're on the wrong one. Lay the lines for me, up the whole row, and I'll teach you the little the salt does do. It's not nothing. Down here, not-nothing is plenty.
canal_maze_salt_the_thresholds.progress: The doorsteps of Gavril's row wait for their salt at dusk. Lay the lines the old way before the dark comes down the maze.
canal_maze_salt_the_thresholds.complete: Every sill white and straight. Good. Now they'll come to the row and feel the door the way you feel a cold coin in a warm hand -- something that's got an edge, and a side you're not on. That's the whole trick. Not a wall. A reminder. Take a pouch of the salt for your own thresholds. A traveler ought to know where the inside is, wherever he sleeps.

objectives.label: the low door-row
objectives.text: Lay the salt-lines up the row at dusk

canal_maze_salt_the_thresholds.note: Salt keeps nothing out, and never did. It reminds what comes that a door has two sides, and a side it is not on. Gavril calls that not-nothing. In the maze, not-nothing is counted as plenty.

canal_maze_the_drowned_wage.title: The Drowned Wage
canal_maze_the_drowned_wage.summary: The counting-house is worked yet by hands that drowned at their desks, still filing under the water, still owed the wage they died before collecting. Matei wants one honest ledger brought up before the drowned clerks file it away forever.
canal_maze_the_drowned_wage.offer: The counting-house never stopped working. That's the thing topside folk can't hold in their heads -- the clerks that drowned at their desks are down there still, filing. Still owed the wage they died before drawing. One of them kept an honest book once -- a true count, before the ink went violet and the accounts started closing people. If I can get that book up, I can prove the maze wasn't always this. Go down among the drowned wage-hands and bring me the honest ledger before they file it under themselves.
canal_maze_the_drowned_wage.progress: The drowned clerks work the sunken desks, the honest book somewhere among them. Reach it, and take word to Lazar, who'll want it kept dry.
canal_maze_the_drowned_wage.complete: You brought it up. Look at the hand -- steady, kind, a real count of real lives, from before the Archive learned to spend the people it was built to keep. They drowned owed their wage and they file yet, waiting to be paid. This book proves there was a before. It doesn't buy a single one of them their wage. Take your due. It's more than the clerks below will ever draw, and don't I know it.

objectives.label: the sunken desks
objectives.text: Recover the honest ledger from the drowned clerks
Take the honest ledger to Lazar to keep dry

canal_maze_the_drowned_wage.note: The clerks drowned owed their wage, and file yet, waiting to be paid. The honest book proves there was a before -- a count of real lives, kept before the Archive learned to spend the people it was built to keep. It buys none of them their wage.

canal_maze_walls_that_read_back.title: The Walls That Read Back
canal_maze_walls_that_read_back.summary: A stretch of canal wall has begun to answer. Folk who read the marks aloud find the marks, next tide, changed to fit the reading -- the inscription copying itself into whoever attends it. Florea needs the reach mapped before another reader spreads it further.
canal_maze_walls_that_read_back.offer: There's a wall by the low sluice that's begun to read back. A dockhand spoke the marks on it aloud -- just sounding them out, the way you do -- and by next tide the marks had shifted to match how he'd said them. The wall's learning us. The inscription copies itself into whoever attends it, and grows a little each reading. I need the spread mapped before some poor curious soul reads it whole. Walk the reading-wall, mark where the marks are freshest, and whatever you do -- don't sound them out.
canal_maze_walls_that_read_back.progress: The reading-wall runs along the low sluice, freshest where it was last read aloud. Reach it and map the spread -- silently. Then bring the map to Florea.
canal_maze_walls_that_read_back.complete: Fresh here, here, and here -- exactly where folk stood and read. It's not the wall spreading. It's us. Every mouth that sounds it out plants it one wall further, like footprints that walk on after the foot's gone home. You kept your mouth shut and your eyes on your boots. Good. That's the only reading that doesn't cost. Take the draught -- it'll show you blue from worse, and down here the difference is your whole life.

objectives.label: the reading-wall
objectives.text: Map the reading-wall without sounding it out
Bring the spread-map to Florea

canal_maze_walls_that_read_back.note: The wall does not spread itself. Every mouth that sounds it out plants it one wall further, like footprints that walk on after the foot goes home. The only reading that costs nothing is the one you keep behind your teeth, eyes on your boots.

canal_maze_letter_to_the_hearth.title: A Letter to the Last Hearth
canal_maze_letter_to_the_hearth.summary: There is one warm place left at the far edge of the drowned country, and Toma writes it letters he can rarely send. He wants this one carried to the ferryman's box by hand, so it goes out sealed and warm and unread.
canal_maze_letter_to_the_hearth.offer: There's a hearth at the end of the world -- the Last Hearth, they call it, out past the drowned country, the one place the fog forgot to sour. My cousin keeps a room there. I write her when I can, though a letter through the counting-house comes out read, stamped, and half the warmth pressed out of it. This one I want carried by hand to the ferryman's box -- sealed, warm, and nobody's business but hers. Take it. Don't let it near a clerk's desk. Some words are only good unopened.
canal_maze_letter_to_the_hearth.progress: The ferryman's box waits at the toll pontoon. Carry Toma's sealed letter there by hand, past the clerks' desks, and leave it warm and whole.
canal_maze_letter_to_the_hearth.complete: In the box, sealed, unread? Good. Bless you. She'll open it herself, warm as I meant it, and know somebody in the drowned city still writes to the warm one. That's the whole of the mercy -- a letter that arrives the way it left. Here's a coin, and a pot-hook off Ilie's bench for your trouble. A traveler ought to have somewhere to hang his supper. Even here.

objectives.label: the ferryman's box
objectives.text: Carry the sealed letter to the ferryman's box
Tell Toma the letter went out whole

canal_maze_letter_to_the_hearth.note: A letter through the counting-house comes out read and stamped, half its warmth pressed away. This one went by hand, sealed, and will arrive the way it left. Some words are only good unopened. In the drowned city, that is the whole of the mercy.

canal_maze_the_wrong_water_pilot.title: The Wrong-Water Pilot
canal_maze_the_wrong_water_pilot.summary: A lurker has learned to steer the ferry-punts, nudging them off the marked runs into the deep dark where the walls close over. Three crews have gone wrong-water this month. Dorin wants the false pilot ended before the ferrymen stop crewing at all.
canal_maze_the_wrong_water_pilot.offer: Ferrymen won't crew the eastern runs no more, and I don't blame them. Something's learned to pilot the punts -- nudges the hull off the marked channel, tide by tide, till the crew's poling into a run where the walls close over and the light quits. Three punts gone wrong-water this month, none come back. It's the lurkers, the clever ones, herding boats like the wolves herded folk up north in the old tales. Go into the closed runs and end the pilots. A ferry has to be a ferry, or this city's got no veins left that carry the living.
canal_maze_the_wrong_water_pilot.progress: The false pilots work the eastern runs, herding punts into the closed dark. Hunt the lurkers where the walls close over the water.
canal_maze_the_wrong_water_pilot.complete: Three of them, down in the closed run where the light quits. That's the ferrymen's confidence back, or the start of it. You felt it work, didn't you -- the little wrongness in the water, the nudge you didn't make? Clever as the drowned who dug the maze, and twice as patient. Ferry's a ferry again on the eastern runs. Take the tonic. It's cold comfort, but comfort keeps poorly down here anyway.

objectives.text: End the false pilots in the closed runs
Clear the rot from the fouled hulls

canal_maze_the_wrong_water_pilot.note: The lurkers herd ferry-punts as the changed wolves once herded folk up north -- off the marked runs, into the dark where the walls close over. Three crews went wrong-water and none returned. The ferrymen crew the eastern runs again, warily.

canal_maze_the_pontoon_skim.title: The Skim at the Toll Pontoon
canal_maze_the_pontoon_skim.summary: The toll pontoon's ledger and its coffer disagree by a steady sum every tide, and the difference walks off in someone's pocket. Marcu smells a rival skimming his water. He wants the books read before he decides whose fingers to break -- gently, this being a business dispute.
canal_maze_the_pontoon_skim.offer: Somebody's skimming my pontoon. The toll-book says one number, the coffer says another, and the gap between them's been walking off every tide, steady as a wage. Now, I'm a reasonable man -- I skim a little myself, it's the industry -- but there's a courtesy to it, and whoever this is has no courtesy. Go read the pontoon books against the coffer and put a name to the gap. Then we'll discuss the name. Civilly. This is the maze -- even the throat-cutting's got paperwork.
canal_maze_the_pontoon_skim.progress: The toll pontoon keeps two numbers that disagree. Reach it, read the books against the coffer, then bring the name of the gap to Marcu.
canal_maze_the_pontoon_skim.complete: The clerk. Of course it's the clerk -- it's always the one who writes the number that steals it. He's been un-writing a fare in ten and pocketing the round of it, tide after tide. Well. Now I know, which in this city is the whole of the leverage. I won't break his fingers -- I'll own his gap. He'll skim for me now, and thank me for the wage. That's civilization, friend. Here's your cut and a forged writ for your pocket. You never know when you'll need to un-file a thing.

objectives.label: the toll pontoon
objectives.text: Read the pontoon books against the coffer
Bring the name of the gap to Marcu

canal_maze_the_pontoon_skim.note: It is always the one who writes the number who steals it. Marcu will not break the clerk's fingers -- he will own the clerk's gap, and be thanked for the wage. In the maze, even the throat-cutting keeps paperwork, and Marcu calls that civilization.

canal_maze_the_cold_landing.title: The Cold Landing
canal_maze_the_cold_landing.summary: Wherever the Collector stands, the fog off the water reads shifting orange under the alchemist's light -- the signal grown from a symptom into an organ he wears. Something has stood at the collector's landing lately. Oprea wants a witness who reads the color, and who logs choices, and who might be spoken to.
canal_maze_the_cold_landing.offer: The fog turns orange at the collector's landing. Not blue, not violet -- orange, shifting, the color that writes itself into whatever it touches. The old alchemy's plain about it: you leave, or you transmit. You do not read. Something's been standing at that landing between tides, and the color's thickened where it stood. I need a witness who knows the color and won't sound the marks -- and I'll be honest, I need one the maze already has entries for. You've closed accounts up and down the world. The landing may have a word for you. Go and let it not be said. Then walk away.
canal_maze_the_cold_landing.progress: The collector's landing lies where the fog reads orange. Reach it, endure whatever the color has to say, and carry it back to Oprea -- unread.
canal_maze_the_cold_landing.complete: You went, and you came back, which is more than the color usually allows. And it spoke -- not your name, it never troubles with names, but your deeds. The one who counted the low water. The one who kept the girl incurious. It offered you rest, didn't it. It always does. Rest is the whole of what it sells, and the price is the reading. You walked away. That's the only answer it can't file. Take the counteragent. When the quiet comes for you -- and it will, after a landing -- drink it, and stay curious, and stay loud.

objectives.label: the collector's landing
objectives.text: Stand at the orange landing and do not read
Carry the landing's word back to Oprea, unread

canal_maze_the_cold_landing.note: The fog reads shifting orange where the Collector stands -- the signal matured from symptom to organ. At the landing it spoke, not a name but deeds, and it offered rest. Rest is all it sells; the price is the reading. To walk away is the one answer it cannot file.

canal_maze_the_one_warm_stone.title: The One Warm Stone
canal_maze_the_one_warm_stone.summary: One paving stone under the black weir stays warm through every cold tide, and the drowned drift toward it in their slow closed way, orienting on a heat that should not be. Lazar has watched it a year and needs it read before the whole quarter learns to lean that way.
canal_maze_the_one_warm_stone.offer: There's a stone under the weir stays warm. Every tide in this city runs cold enough to set in your marrow, and one paving stone stays warm as a hand. I've watched a year. The drowned drift to it -- not fast, nothing's fast down here, but they turn toward it the way a plant turns to a window. Dust lays in lines pointing at it. Folk topside have started saying they sleep better facing that way, and none of them can tell you why. Go read the warm stone. And for the love of the saints, don't warm to it yourself.
canal_maze_the_one_warm_stone.progress: The warm stone lies under the black weir, the drowned leaning toward it. Reach it, read it cold-eyed, then bring word to Florea's chronicle.
canal_maze_the_one_warm_stone.complete: Warm. A year, and I hoped I'd imagined it. A live stone -- the ground warms, the drowned lean in, the dust points the way, folk face it in their sleep. It's the whole sickness in one paving stone, doing to the maze what it did to the border a thousand years ago and will do a thousand years on. We can't cool it. Nobody's ever cooled one. We can only know it's there, and salt around it, and keep the girl incurious. Take the lantern. Its light belongs to the dark. Down here, that's the only light that doesn't lie.

objectives.label: the warm stone under the weir
objectives.text: Read the warm stone without warming to it
Bring word of the live stone to Florea

canal_maze_the_one_warm_stone.note: One paving stone stays warm through every cold tide -- the ground warmed, the drowned leaning in, the dust in lines, the living facing it asleep. A live stone, the whole sickness in one flag of paving. No one has ever cooled one. You salt around it, and keep the children incurious.

canal_maze_a_widows_knot.title: A Widow's Knot
canal_maze_a_widows_knot.summary: A woman two doors down had her man collected -- finalized, filed, unmade from the record so cleanly the city insists he never was. Horia wants her given something to hold that says otherwise. A knot binds a name the ledger has un-written.
canal_maze_a_widows_knot.offer: Woman two doors down lost her man to the Pit. Collected. And here's the cruelty of the way this city kills -- once you're collected, you're not just dead, you're un-been. The books say there was never a husband. Neighbors half-believe it. She's starting to. I can't give her him back. But I can give her a thing to hold that says he was -- a knot, bound with a scrap of his and a scrap of hers, that the ledger can't un-write because it was never written down. Fetch the makings from Ilie's bench and I'll tie it. Some records you keep in your hand, not their book.
canal_maze_a_widows_knot.progress: The widow needs a knot the ledger cannot un-write. Fetch the binding-scrap from Ilie, then bring it to Horia to tie.
canal_maze_a_widows_knot.complete: There. Bound. A scrap of his shirt, a scrap of her hem, a knot that won't say which is which and won't come undone. Now when the books tell her he never was, she's got a thing in her fist that argues. It's not much against a whole city's forgetting. But it's a record they can't collect, because it never went in a ledger to be closed. Take one for yourself. Everybody down here loses somebody the city insists they never had. Best you're ready to remember out loud.

objectives.text: Fetch the binding-scrap from Ilie's bench
Bring the makings to Horia to tie the knot

canal_maze_a_widows_knot.note: To be collected is to be un-been; the books insist there was never a husband, and the widow half-believes them. The knot binds a name the ledger cannot un-write, because it was never written to be closed. A record kept in the fist, not their book.

canal_maze_the_black_weir.title: The Black Weir
canal_maze_the_black_weir.summary: The black weir is the maze's last gate before the Pit's cold water, and it has choked -- rot in the sluices, lurkers denning in the drowned machinery, the whole quarter's tide backing up behind it. Barbu means to open it before the maze floods into the living rooms. He will not send his own men. He will send the one the road cannot keep.
canal_maze_the_black_weir.offer: The weir's choked. It's the last gate before the water that runs down into the Pit, and when it jams the whole maze backs up -- and it's jamming now, rot in every sluice and the big lurkers denned in the machinery like it's a barrow built for them. If it doesn't open by the next high tide, the maze floods the rooms where folk still sleep dry. I've a squad of good men and I won't spend one of them in that dark. But you keep walking back out of places that keep people. So it's you, or it's the flood. Open the weir.
canal_maze_the_black_weir.progress: The black weir chokes on rot and denned lurkers, the tide rising behind it. Reach the drowned machinery and open the gate before high water.
canal_maze_the_black_weir.complete: It's turning. Heard the weir groan open from the wall -- the whole quarter breathing out at once. You went into a dark that's kept better folk and you came back and the water's running down where it belongs. I've buried men in that machinery. I won't bury you, it seems -- the maze can't hold you either, and I've stopped asking why. Take the plate. Ilie's pattern, one size too honest, and honest's the only armor worth a damn where everything else keeps two sets of books.

objectives.label: the black weir machinery
objectives.text: Reach the drowned weir gate
Clear the lurkers denned in the weir
Burn the rot from the sluices

canal_maze_the_black_weir.note: The weir choked on rot and denned lurkers, and the tide climbed toward the sleeping rooms. It runs again -- the quarter breathing out at once. Barbu has buried men in that machinery. He no longer asks why the maze cannot keep you, and no longer wants to know.

canal_maze_the_forged_writ.title: The Forged Writ
canal_maze_the_forged_writ.summary: A writ of un-filing can lift an account off the books -- unbind a debt, unmake a collection -- if it is stamped just so. The genuine ones are the Archive's alone. Matei has taught himself the stamp, and needs a hand steadier than his to lift a blank while the annex sleeps.
canal_maze_the_forged_writ.offer: There's a document -- a writ of un-filing. Stamp it right and it lifts an account clean off the books. Unbinds a debt. Un-collects a soul, if you're bold and quick. The real ones are the Archive's alone, and the Archive doesn't un-file, it only closes. But I've watched the stamp thirty tides and I've got the impression by heart. What I haven't got is a steady hand and a strong nerve. Lift me a blank from the annex while Pell's books are dreaming, and I'll teach you the forgery. One un-filed name won't tip the world. But it might tip one.
canal_maze_the_forged_writ.progress: The un-filing blanks lie in Marrow Pell's annex. Lift one while the books sleep, then bring it to Matei for the stamp.
canal_maze_the_forged_writ.complete: Perfect blank -- true watermark, true weave. Now watch the stamp: press once, and there's no un-pressing, no third impression like the cursed line in the standing ledger. Just once, clean, and a name comes off the books that the Pit was owed. It's a small crime against a great cruelty. I'll not pretend it's more. But a small crime against a great cruelty is about the biggest good this city leaves a clerk to do. Take one. Use it on a name you'd die to keep.

objectives.label: the annex blank-desk
objectives.text: Lift an un-filing blank from the annex
Bring the blank to Matei for the stamp

canal_maze_the_forged_writ.note: A writ of un-filing lifts an account clean off the books -- unbinds a debt, un-collects a soul. The Archive owns the true ones and never un-files, only closes. A small crime against a great cruelty, Matei calls it, and the biggest good the city leaves a clerk to do.

canal_maze_the_low_tide_bell.title: The Low-Tide Bell
canal_maze_the_low_tide_bell.summary: At the bottom of every tide Toma rings the old bell over the taproom -- a plain kindness, a sound to say the living are still here. Lately the rot answers it, boiling up out of the boards the moment it stops. He rings it anyway, and would ring it easier with the walkway clear.
canal_maze_the_low_tide_bell.offer: Every low tide I ring the old bell over the door. My grandmother's practice -- a plain sound at the bottom of the dark to say the living are still keeping hours down here. Trouble is, lately the rot answers. Comes boiling up through the boards the second the last ring fades, like it heard and took it personal. I'll ring it regardless -- some kindnesses you don't quit just because the dark's got opinions. But clear the walkway first, would you, so the folk coming for a warm cup don't wade through a carpet of the things.
canal_maze_the_low_tide_bell.progress: The rot rises to answer the low-tide bell. Clear it off the taproom walkway before Toma rings the hour.
canal_maze_the_low_tide_bell.complete: Walkway's clear, and I've rung the hour -- there, hear it fade? And nothing answering but the drip. That's a good tide, by the maze's reckoning: the bell rang, the living heard it, and the dark kept its carpet to itself a while. Come back next low water. The bell wants ringing whether the rot likes it or no, and the company makes the ringing lighter. Here's your cup. Warm, and no charge for a bell-keeper's friend.

objectives.text: Clear the walkway before the bell rings

canal_maze_the_low_tide_bell.note: A plain sound at the bottom of the dark, to say the living still keep hours here. The rot answers it now, boiling up the moment the last ring fades. Toma rings the hour regardless. Some kindnesses you do not quit because the dark took them personal.

canal_maze_the_sinking_names.title: The Sinking Names
canal_maze_the_sinking_names.summary: The maze's grave-kerbs were carved with names once, the way the living country carves its dead. Here the names are sinking -- letter by letter the stone gives them up, until a kerb reads only a row, a count, and one word: pending. Florea copies them down before the last letter goes under.
canal_maze_the_sinking_names.offer: Come see the kerbs before the water finishes them. Up in the living country they carve a grave with a name and a year and one true thing the dead person did -- a man, missed, particular. Down here the names are sinking. Letter by letter the stone lets them go, and what's left when a name's gone all the way under is a row, a count, and one word. Pending. I'm copying them while there's a letter left to copy. Walk the kerb-run with me and read them aloud to me as I write -- softly, mind, only the names, never the marks around them.
canal_maze_the_sinking_names.progress: The sinking kerbs line the low run, their names half-gone into the stone. Reach them, read the names for Florea's copy, then bring the last legible name back to her.
canal_maze_the_sinking_names.complete: You caught three names before the water had them -- three people who were particular and missed, near enough un-sunk. That's the chronicle's whole fight down here: not to save them, we can't, but to keep a name legible one season longer than the stone means to. Up north a grave says who and what they did. Down here it counts toward pending, and pending is the maze saying not yet, not the maze saying no. Take the map-work. It's the same fight on paper -- one road drawn true, against a city that files them wrong.

objectives.label: the sinking kerbs
objectives.text: Read the sinking names for Florea's copy
Bring the last legible name back to Florea

canal_maze_the_sinking_names.note: Up in the living country a grave keeps a name, a year, one true thing the dead did. Here the names sink letter by letter until only a row, a count, and one word remain -- pending. The chronicle cannot save them. It fights to keep one name legible a season longer than the stone intends.

canal_maze_the_accord_dispatch.title: The Accord Dispatch
canal_maze_the_accord_dispatch.summary: A sealed dispatch must reach the Accord's contact at the toll pontoon, carried across the maze unopened. The seal is the whole safety of it: the carrier who reads a thing like this learns what the maze does to readers. Dorin trusts a hand that has learned to look at its boots.
canal_maze_the_accord_dispatch.offer: I've a dispatch for the Accord -- their quiet contact takes it at the toll pontoon at the tide-turn. Sealed. And it stays sealed, that's the whole of the job and the whole of your safety. You'll want to read it. Everybody wants to read the sealed thing -- it's the maze's oldest hook, curiosity, and it's caught better carriers than you. But a message down here costs whoever carries it, and it costs double whoever opens it. Carry it across, hand it over whole, and look at your boots the entire way. I picked you because I hear you know how to not look. Prove me right.
canal_maze_the_accord_dispatch.progress: The Accord's contact waits at the toll pontoon. Carry the sealed dispatch across the maze unopened, and hand it over at the tide-turn.
canal_maze_the_accord_dispatch.complete: Sealed still. Good hand, good nerve. The contact's got it, and it went across the maze the way a message ought to -- unread, uncopied, un-seeded. You didn't crack it, and I can tell, because you're standing here with your own name still your own. Every carrier that reads one of these comes back a little less themselves, or doesn't come back. You came back whole. That's rarer down here than gold, and here's gold besides. The Accord remembers a clean carrier. So do I.

objectives.label: the toll pontoon
objectives.text: Carry the sealed dispatch to the Accord contact
Report the dispatch delivered whole to Dorin

canal_maze_the_accord_dispatch.note: Curiosity is the maze's oldest hook, and it has caught better carriers. The sealed dispatch crossed unread -- un-seeded, uncopied. Every carrier who cracks one comes back a little less themselves, or does not come back. This one came back whole, which the Accord counts rarer than gold.

canal_maze_and_kept_the_change.title: And Kept the Change
canal_maze_and_kept_the_change.summary: A debt-tablet washed up at the toll pontoon, hairline-cracked and still faintly warm -- a whole existence out with the tide, not quite closed. Crack it the rest of the way and a name comes back to the maze. Marcu smells profit. He does not yet smell the price the whole quarter will pay for one reopened door.
canal_maze_and_kept_the_change.offer: Something's come up on my pontoon worth more than a season of tolls. A debt-tablet -- one of the anchors, a whole life bound into stone -- washed up hairline-cracked and still warm to the hand. Not quite closed, this one. Now: crack it the rest of the way, and the account it holds comes un-finalized. A door reopens. A face flickers back. And a man who owns that trick owns something the Archive itself can't make. Take it to the low sluice where the water's stillest, work the crack open, and let's find out whose life just came back to market. Every crossing has a price, friend. This one, we collect.
canal_maze_and_kept_the_change.progress: The warm cracked tablet came up at the pontoon. Carry it to the low sluice and work the crack open -- then bring Marcu whatever comes back.
canal_maze_and_kept_the_change.complete: It worked. The crack ran, the tablet went cold, and somewhere in the maze a door that was finalized swung open -- a shuttered room lit again, a name back on a lintel that the books swore was never there. Marcu's rich, or thinks he is. But you scanned the fog on the way out, didn't you. Orange, and thicker than yesterday, and creeping one channel nearer the living rooms. That's the price. Un-finalizing a soul doesn't cancel the debt. It moves it -- widens the signal, feeds the fog. Marcu kept the change. The maze kept the account. It always does. Take the ledger. You've earned the knowing, and the knowing is the whole of the wage.

objectives.label: the still low sluice
objectives.text: Work open the warm cracked tablet
Bring what came back to Marcu

canal_maze_and_kept_the_change.note: The cracked tablet ran cold and a finalized door swung open -- a room lit again, a name back on its lintel. But the fog off the water read orange, thicker, one channel nearer the living. Un-finalizing a soul does not cancel the debt. It moves it, and widens the signal. Marcu kept the change. The maze kept the account.

salt_fens_causeway_short.title: The Causeway Shortens
salt_fens_causeway_short.summary: The fen-guide has walked the same safe causeway since he was a boy, and every year it grows shorter by a stride. He wants it walked to the last dry stone before the brack takes another.
salt_fens_causeway_short.offer: My father walked this causeway, and his father cut the stones. It was longer then. Every spring I lose a pace of it to the water, and no spring gives one back. Walk it to the last dry stone for me. Count it with your own feet. Tell me I'm an old fool and it hasn't moved.
salt_fens_causeway_short.progress: The causeway runs out east from my hut. Walk it to the last dry stone, where the reeds stand in. You'll feel where safe stops.
salt_fens_causeway_short.complete: Nineteen paces. It was twenty last year and one-and-thirty when my father counted. You're no fool and neither am I. The brack isn't rising, friend. The dry is leaving. Here -- a pouch of salt-line. Draw it at your sill and sleep the better for it.

objectives.label: the last dry stone of the causeway
objectives.text: Walk the causeway to its last dry stone

salt_fens_causeway_short.note: The causeway shortens by a pace a year, and the water does not rise to meet it. The dry ground is leaving, quietly, the way everything here leaves. Mâl keeps counting anyway. Someone should.

salt_fens_brine_lurkers.title: By the Notch
salt_fens_brine_lurkers.summary: The lurker-hunter keeps his tally in notches on a spear-haft, and he is out of spear before he is out of lurkers. Thin the salt-stingers off the low pans while he cuts a new one.
salt_fens_brine_lurkers.offer: I keep my count in notches, and I've notched clean through three spears this season. The stingers come up out of the low pans at dusk, thick as they've ever been -- something down there's stirring them. Cut me thirty and I'll believe the fen's fightable another year. More than thirty and I start packing.
salt_fens_brine_lurkers.progress: The salt-stingers rise off the low pans, west by the salt-works, when the light goes. Thin them. I'll be re-hafting.
salt_fens_brine_lurkers.complete: Thirty and two. Good. That's a season I can notch. You'd think a man tires of killing the same fen over and over -- you don't, you just get quieter about it. Take the skinner. Blackglass holds an edge in wet no iron will. Mind you keep the salt-side clean.

objectives.text: Thin the salt-stingers off the low pans

salt_fens_brine_lurkers.note: Something below the low pans stirs the stingers up thicker every dusk. Nica notches his count and re-hafts his spear and does not ask what does the stirring. The fen is fightable one more year. He said it like a man reading his own sentence.

salt_fens_terrible_salt.title: The Worst Salt in Draconia
salt_fens_terrible_salt.summary: The salt-farmer's pans yield the greyest, bitterest salt on the coast, and he is unaccountably proud of it. He wants a fair boxful carried to the market stall before the tide-damp gets into it.
salt_fens_terrible_salt.offer: You'll not find worse salt anywhere between here and the drowned city, and I say that with my chest out. Grey as a gull, bitter as a bailiff -- but it keeps a fish through winter when the pretty white stuff lets it rot. Carry a box up to the stall for me before the damp finds it? My knees are salt themselves these days.
salt_fens_terrible_salt.progress: The stall's up by the cottages, north of the pans. Get the box under cover before the tide-damp gets in it. Grey salt hates a wet morning.
salt_fens_terrible_salt.complete: Under cover and dry -- bless you. Folk turn their noses up till the first cold snap, then it's Radu, Radu, have you any of the grey left. Every year the same. Here, a jar of the marsh-light -- it burns steady when the fog eats the lamps. You'll want it out here after dark. Everyone does.

objectives.label: the market stall
objectives.text: Carry the grey salt to the market stall

salt_fens_terrible_salt.note: Radu's salt is the worst on the coast and he loves it the way a man loves a stubborn dog. It keeps a fish through winter when the fine white rots. The fens make one honest thing, and he makes it, and asks nothing back but that you keep it dry.

salt_fens_the_fog_that_reads.title: The Fog That Reads
salt_fens_the_fog_that_reads.summary: Mâl's grandmother left him rules for the fog, one word each, the reasons long worn off. One rule is for a corridor that opens clear and straight from your own feet to the far stones. The rule is: Don't. He needs to know if the old word still holds.
salt_fens_the_fog_that_reads.offer: My grandmother gave me the fog's rules and never a why to a one of them. Salt at the sill. Never whistle on the causeway. And this: when the brack opens a clean corridor, straight as a ruled line, from your own boots off to the reading-stone -- you don't walk it. One word, that rule. Don't. But the corridors come oftener now, and I've lost three who wouldn't listen. Walk out to the stone the corridor points at. Don't walk the corridor. Tell me what stands at the end of a thing my grandmother had a word for.
salt_fens_the_fog_that_reads.progress: The corridor points at the old reading-stone, east and high, past the standing farmer. Go by the causeway, not the clear lane. Reach the stone from the side, the long way. Never the straight way.
salt_fens_the_fog_that_reads.complete: You came back by the causeway. Good. Most of what the fog opens a road to, it opens the road for a reason -- and the reason is never the walker's. A stone that reads shifting orange in the haze, you said, and the lane laid straight to it like a finger. That's the whole of my grandmother's word, then, with the why grown back. Don't. Take the counteragent. Sarea brewed it. If the quiet ever comes on you out here, drink, and remember your own name first.

objectives.label: the reading-stone above the fens
objectives.text: Reach the reading-stone the long way
Bring Mâl the word for the corridor

salt_fens_the_fog_that_reads.note: The fog opens corridors now, ruler-straight, from a walker's feet to a stone that reads orange in the haze. Mâl's grandmother had one word for it and no why. The why has grown back. The word was Don't.

salt_fens_five_editions.title: Five Editions Corrupted
salt_fens_five_editions.summary: The brine-witch brews from Hessik's method, five copies down the line and every copy wronger than the last. She wants a clean reading-draught proved against the fen-lamp's records before she trusts her own hand again.
salt_fens_five_editions.offer: I learned my brewing from a book that learned it from a book that learned it from a book, and somewhere down that road a scribe with a cold got a measure wrong and no one ever caught it. Five editions, each a little more corrupt, and mine's the sixth. My blue reads violet some nights. I don't know if it's the fen or the recipe. Take a fresh-brewed draught to Alba at the fog-lamp -- she logs the nights the light goes orange. If my blue matches her orange nights, my hand's the fault. If it doesn't, the fen is.
salt_fens_five_editions.progress: Alba keeps the fog-lamp south of the causeway hut, and she logs every night it turns. Bring her the draught. Let her tell you whether my method lies or the marsh does.
salt_fens_five_editions.complete: Neither, she says -- and both. My blue is true, but the fen's gone so loud my true blue can't tell a live stone from a waking one anymore. Hessik never brewed in a place this far gone. Five editions corrupted, and it turns out the world was the sixth correction. Here. The first-edition draught, uncorrupted -- I keep one bottle for the day it matters. Today it matters. Read carefully out there.

objectives.text: Take the reading-draught to Fog-Lamp Keeper Alba
Bring Iosif what Alba's log says

salt_fens_five_editions.note: Hessik's method came to the fens five copies corrupt, each scribe's error compounding the last. The brine-witch feared her hand. The fault was older and larger: the fen has grown so loud that a true reading can no longer tell a live stone from a waking one. The world was the sixth correction.

salt_fens_half_stamped.title: Half-Stamped
salt_fens_half_stamped.summary: Files walked out of the city mid-processing and stopped in the fens, half-collected, half-stamped, standing in the reeds like men who forgot an errand. The Wolfarin clerk pays to bring them back. She does not say back to what.
salt_fens_half_stamped.offer: A file should not have legs, and yet. Twelve of ours walked out of Records mid-processing -- collected on the one page, pending on the next -- and got this far into the brack before the stamp caught up with their feet. Now they stand out there. Half-collected. Half a man, if I'm honest, and I'm paid not to be. Put down the ones that won't queue and I'll credit your tablet. The house wants its paperwork. Even the paperwork that walks.
salt_fens_half_stamped.progress: The half-stamped stand among the drowned fences, north-east where the reeds are highest. The ones that turn on you are the ones the stamp's still deciding about. Put down eight.
salt_fens_half_stamped.complete: Eight settled. Credited to your tablet, cleanly. -- You want to know what happens to the ones that queue peaceable, don't you. They go back. Processing resumes. The stamp comes down the rest of the way. I file it as retrieval because retrieval is a word with no screaming in it. Take your credit. Don't watch the ones still standing. It makes them turn to look at who's watching.

objectives.text: Put down the half-stamped that will not queue

salt_fens_half_stamped.note: Twelve files walked out of Records mid-processing and stopped in the fens, collected on one page and pending on the next. The clerk calls it retrieval, a word with no screaming in it. The ones that queue peaceably are marched back, and the stamp comes down the rest of the way. Eight would not queue. They are settled now. The clerk does not use that word either.

salt_fens_the_priest_pays_opposite.title: What the Priest Pays For
salt_fens_the_priest_pays_opposite.summary: The Wolfarin house pays to march the half-stamped back to the city. The drowned-quarter priest pays for the opposite: leave them standing, and mark the reeds so no retrieval-man finds them. The runner asks which mercy the fens can afford.
salt_fens_the_priest_pays_opposite.offer: You've taken the clerk's coin -- I saw. She pays you to bring the half-stamped in. The priest pays me to keep them out. Not to save them, mind. There's no saving a file half-closed. Only to let them stand in the reeds a while longer as themselves, before the stamp finishes them into rows. He can't pay much. A drowned parish never can. But he pays in the one thing the house won't credit: he lets them keep their names till the water takes them. Ward the reed-paths for me. Turn the retrieval-men back at the fences.
salt_fens_the_priest_pays_opposite.progress: The retrieval-men work the fence-line north-east, hunting the half-stamped for the house. Turn six of them back before they reach the standing ones. Then mark the reeds so the paths read empty.
salt_fens_the_priest_pays_opposite.complete: Six turned back, and the reeds read empty behind you. The priest will sleep tonight, which is more than the priest deserves and exactly what he prays for anyway. -- No, I won't tell you it was the right ledger to have chosen. Both ledgers end the same page. One just lets a man stand in the fog as himself a season longer. That's the whole of what mercy costs out here: a season, and the name that goes into the water with him. Take the pouch. It's a warding thing. It works because it means nothing.

objectives.text: Turn back the Wolfarin retrieval-men

objectives.label: the drowned fences
objectives.text: Ward the reed-paths at the drowned fences

salt_fens_the_priest_pays_opposite.note: The house pays to march the half-stamped back into rows; the drowned parish pays to leave them standing in the reeds as themselves a season longer. Both ledgers end on the same page. The priest buys only the delay, and the name that goes into the water. Ileana warded the paths. She would not call it the right choice. There was no right choice to call it.

salt_fens_rustmaw_pans.title: The Thing Under the Pans
salt_fens_rustmaw_pans.summary: Something big and iron-jawed has denned under the flooded salt-pans, and it has ruined a season's work fouling the brine. Radu can lose the salt. He cannot lose the pans. Kill the rustmaw before it takes the last of them.
salt_fens_rustmaw_pans.offer: I told you my salt was the worst in Draconia and I meant it kindly. Now there's a thing under the low pans -- a rustmaw, big as a boat, jaws like a wreck's ribs -- and it's fouled three pans black. Salt I can lose. A pan's my grandfather's digging. Can't dig another at my age, and no one'll come dig here who isn't already trapped here. Put the brute down. I'll not watch, but I'll thank you loud after.
salt_fens_rustmaw_pans.progress: The rustmaw dens where the low pans flood deepest, west and south. Go at low light -- it surfaces to foul the brine when the tide's out. One's enough, if it's the big one.
salt_fens_rustmaw_pans.complete: Heard it die from the cottage. Ugly noise, an ugly thing making it, and now the quietest the pans have been all year. I'll re-cut the fouled ones come the dry. Grandfather's pans, dug by grandfather's boy's boy's hire. Take this -- a pot-hook, bog-iron, my own forging. Won't win you a war. Keeps a kettle over a fire, which out here is most of what a body needs kept.

objectives.text: Kill the rustmaw under the salt-pans

salt_fens_rustmaw_pans.note: A rustmaw denned under the salt-farmer's oldest pans and fouled them black. He could lose the salt. He could not lose his grandfather's digging. The brute is dead and the pans are the quietest they have been all year. Radu did not watch. He thanked the hollow air, loudly, the way a man thanks a thing he cannot repay.

salt_fens_lamp_goes_orange.title: The Nights It Turns
salt_fens_lamp_goes_orange.summary: The fog-lamp keeper logs every night the light 'goes orange for a bit' and cannot say why she bothers. Her log lines up with something. She wants a watcher's eyes on the lamp the next time it turns.
salt_fens_lamp_goes_orange.offer: I tend the fen-light so folk on the causeway have a mark to walk to. Steady blue-white flame, honest oil. But some nights -- I've kept the count -- it goes orange for a bit. Not the flame. The fog around it. Like the light's shining on something the fog's reading. I write down every night it happens and I couldn't tell you why except that writing it down feels like not being alone with it. Stand a watch with me. Tell me I imagine the orange. Or tell me I don't.
salt_fens_lamp_goes_orange.progress: The fog-lamp stands south of the causeway hut, on the low ground. Keep the watch through the dark hours with me. It turns near the deep of the night, if it turns at all.
salt_fens_lamp_goes_orange.complete: You saw it. I watched your face and you saw it -- the fog went the colour of a coal that shouldn't be there and then it didn't and you couldn't have said how long. Neither can I, ever. That's the worst of it. Time goes soft when it turns. My log says a quarter-hour. My knees say all night. Take the jar of marsh-light. It doesn't turn. Whatever's out there, it's got no use for a light that won't read.

objectives.label: the fog-lamp
objectives.text: Keep the night-watch at the fog-lamp
Tell Nica what the fog did

salt_fens_lamp_goes_orange.note: The fog-lamp keeper logs the nights the fog around her light goes the colour of a coal. Time runs soft while it turns; her log says a quarter-hour, her knees say all night, and both are honest. She writes it down because writing it down feels like not being alone with it. It is not the same as not being alone with it.

salt_fens_standing_farmer.title: The One Who Stopped
salt_fens_standing_farmer.summary: A salt-farmer walked out to the high reeds a fortnight ago and stopped, and stands there still, facing the reading-stone, listening. The chronicler wants him named in the record before the fens finish taking his name.
salt_fens_standing_farmer.offer: There's a man in the high reeds, standing. Been there a fortnight. Faces the old stone, head tilted, like a fellow leaning to catch the end of a joke. I knew him -- salt-farmer, three pans, a wife who's stopped calling him in for supper because he stopped answering. I can't fetch him back; no one comes back from the listening, not whole. But I can set his name in the record while it's still his. Go to him. Don't call his name -- names are the door out there. Just stand near enough to read the one on his belt-tag, and bring it to me.
salt_fens_standing_farmer.progress: He stands in the high reeds, north-east, by the standing-farmer's ground below the reading-stone. Go near. Read his tag. Do not say his name, and do not stay when the fog opens a lane.
salt_fens_standing_farmer.complete: That's the name. I'll set it in the book proper -- what he was, three pans, the wife, the whole of him, while the whole of him's still a thing worth six lines. In a season there'll be nothing to write but a date and a direction he faced. The fens take a man slow and keep the standing part longest, like it's the part they wanted. Here -- for a chronicler's work. A bound keepsake. Put a name in it that matters to you. Carry it. Names carried are names kept.

objectives.label: the standing farmer in the high reeds
objectives.text: Read the standing farmer's belt-tag
Bring Ozun the name for the record

salt_fens_standing_farmer.note: A salt-farmer walked to the high reeds and stopped, facing the reading-stone, listening. In a fortnight he has answered no one. The chronicler set his name in the book while it was still his: three pans, a wife who no longer calls him in. Soon the entry will be a date and a direction he faced. The fens keep the standing part of a man longest. It is the part they wanted.

salt_fens_ledger_in_reeds.title: The Tablet in the Reeds
salt_fens_ledger_in_reeds.summary: A debt-tablet washed up in the reeds by the salt-road, warm and hairline-cracked, its name unreadable but the first letter clear. Three parties want it. The salt-road master wants it gone before any of them learns it exists.
salt_fens_ledger_in_reeds.offer: There's a tablet out in the reeds by the old ledger-post -- washed up, cracked fine as a hair, and warm. Tablets aren't warm. I've had three quiet askings after it already: the house wants it filed, the Morven want it read, and a fellow with no house at all wants it dropped back in the black water where the tide found it. Me, I want it gone and my salt-road out of it. Fetch it. Bring it to me and no one else, and we'll decide together which asking we never answer.
salt_fens_ledger_in_reeds.progress: The tablet lies where the reeds meet the ledger-post, south by the low water. Bring it to me sealed -- don't hold it warm-side to your palm too long, and for the saints' sake don't read the letter.
salt_fens_ledger_in_reeds.complete: One letter. That's all that's readable, and it's enough to keep me up. -- No. We drop it back. The house files it, someone's finished; the Morven read it, it spreads; I keep it, it's my name up next. The black water's the only party that doesn't grow the thing by holding it. Take the forgery -- a toll-receipt, good as real, gets you past the checkpoints north without a tablet of your own to itemize. Out here, a life without a receipt is the safest kind there is.

objectives.label: the ledger-post in the low reeds
objectives.text: Recover the warm cracked tablet
Bring the tablet to Costel and no one else

salt_fens_ledger_in_reeds.note: A debt-tablet washed into the reeds, warm and hairline-cracked, one letter of a name still legible. The house would file it, the Morven would read it, a nameless man would drown it again. Costel chose the water: it is the only party that does not grow the thing by holding it. One letter kept him up all the same. It was not the same letter as his. He checked.

salt_fens_cold_camp.title: Three Bedrolls
salt_fens_cold_camp.summary: Pilgrims camped at the west edge of the fens: three bedrolls, a cold pot, footprints leading in and none leading out. The warden needs the camp searched for who they were before he reports it, or reports nothing.
salt_fens_cold_camp.offer: Found a camp at the west edge on my round. Three bedrolls, laid neat. Pot of something gone cold and grey, spoons in it, never touched. Footprints into the camp, plain as day. None leading out. I've stood this post nine years and I know a thing I ought to walk away from -- but there's three someones owed a name in a report, and I can't write what I can't read. Go through it. Find who they were. I'll decide after whether the report's worth the ink.
salt_fens_cold_camp.progress: The cold camp sits west, past the salt-pans, on the low dry ground by the cairn road. Search the bedrolls and the pot. Find a name, a token, anything. Then bring it back before dark.
salt_fens_cold_camp.complete: A letter, half-writ, in the top bedroll. 'To whoever reads this.' No name signed, no name to send it to. They came north to listen, same as they all do, and the fen answered by opening a road they walked out on and never walked back. -- I'll not report it. A report needs an ending and this has none, only three spoons in a cold pot. Take the striker. Fire's the one honest thing left out here. Light your own; don't trust a fen-lamp that's learned to turn.

objectives.label: the cold camp at the west edge
objectives.text: Search the pilgrims' cold camp
Tell Manea what the camp held

salt_fens_cold_camp.note: Three bedrolls at the west edge, a cold pot with the spoons still in it, footprints in and none out. In the top bedroll a letter, half-written: To whoever reads this. No name signed. They came north to listen and the fen opened them a road. Manea will not report it. A report needs an ending, and this has only three spoons in a cold pot.

salt_fens_wardsmith_brine.title: Salt-Cured Steel
salt_fens_wardsmith_brine.summary: The fen-smith has cracked how to cure a blade in brine so the salt-damp never rusts it, but he needs bog-iron dredged clean from the deep pans to prove the trick before the season's steel goes red.
salt_fens_wardsmith_brine.offer: Everything rusts out here. Everything. Nail, hinge, a man's good humour. But I've a notion -- cure the iron in the grey brine itself, fight salt with salt, and the damp gives it up as a bad job. Only I need clean bog-iron, dredged from the deep pans, not the fouled muck by the fences. Bring me a good load and I'll prove the trick or eat my own anvil. It'll be the trick. I can feel it in the ache of my hands, which is where I keep my sense these days.
salt_fens_wardsmith_brine.progress: Clean bog-iron lies in the deep pans, west and south, below where the stingers nest. Dredge me a good load -- eight lumps, no less, and shake the fouled muck off before you haul it.
salt_fens_wardsmith_brine.complete: Look at that. Cured it in the brine last night and this morning it's dry as a sermon and bright as a lie. Fight salt with salt -- I told you. First blade out of the batch is a nail-hook, humble work, but it'll hang your gear off a wet wall and never weep a fleck of rust. Yours. A smith that keeps a trick to himself is just a man with a hot rock.

objectives.text: Dredge clean bog-iron from the deep pans

salt_fens_wardsmith_brine.note: The fen-smith cured a blade in the same grey brine that rusts everything else, and it came out bright and dry: fight salt with salt. He keeps no trick to himself, being, he says, a man who does not care to be a man with a hot rock. In the drowned country a thing that will not rust is very nearly hope. Nearly.

salt_fens_bitter_water_stall.title: Bitter Water
salt_fens_bitter_water_stall.summary: The causeway host runs the only warm room in the fens and his one sweet-water spring has gone brackish overnight. He needs it read, warded, and drawn clean, or the last dry hearth on the coast goes dark.
salt_fens_bitter_water_stall.offer: I keep the only room in the fens with a fire that stays lit, and folk crawl in off that causeway grey to the lips and grateful for it. But my spring's gone bitter overnight -- brack where sweet was, and a fire's no comfort if there's naught clean to boil. Draw me a sample, take it to Sarea for a reading, and bring back whatever ward she says. I'll not lose this hearth. It's the last one before the drowned city, and the drowned city's no place to arrive thirsty.
salt_fens_bitter_water_stall.progress: Draw from the spring behind the causeway hut, then carry it to the brine-witch at the market. Bring back her ward and we'll set the salt-lines proper.
salt_fens_bitter_water_stall.complete: Warded and drawn and sweet again -- or sweet enough, out here sweet is a matter of opinion and low standards. Salt-lines at the spring-head, cedar in the water, and Sarea swears the brack'll hold off a season. A season's a long time in the fens. Sit a while by my fire before you go on. It stays lit. That's the whole of my boast, and it's a better boast than the drowned city can make.

objectives.label: the spring behind the causeway hut
objectives.text: Draw a sample from the failing spring
Take the sample to Brine-Witch Iosif for a reading

salt_fens_bitter_water_stall.note: The last warm hearth before the drowned city nearly went dark when its one sweet spring turned brack overnight. Salt-lines and cedar hold the bitterness off, the brine-witch swears, for a season. A season is a long time in the fens, and the shortest thing they have. Fane keeps his fire lit. It is a better boast than the drowned city can make.

salt_fens_stinger_hive.title: The Deep Pan Nest
salt_fens_stinger_hive.summary: The lurker-hunter traced the stingers back to a nest in the deepest flooded pan, and it is a nest built in rows. He wants it burned out before the rows finish whatever they are counting toward.
salt_fens_stinger_hive.offer: Followed the stingers home. Found their nest in the deep pan and wished I hadn't. It's not a nest, not the way a wasp builds. It's -- rows. Even rows, twelve to a line, and every line pointed the same way. Salt-stingers don't count. Something's counting for them. I'll not go in there alone and I'll not leave it to grow. Come burn it with me. Whatever's teaching the fen to line up, I'd sooner it lost this lesson.
salt_fens_stinger_hive.progress: The deep-pan nest sits at the flooded low ground, south-west, past where you thinned them before. Burn out the brood -- twenty-five should break the rows before they close.
salt_fens_stinger_hive.complete: Broke the rows. For now. You saw it too, though -- how they lined up even as they died, like dying was the last part of the count they'd been set. I've hunted this fen nine years and I never saw a thing line up to die before this season. Take the counteragent. If your head ever starts wanting to stand still and listen out here, drink it, and run. Running's the whole of the wisdom I've got left to teach.

objectives.text: Burn out the rowed nest in the deep pan

salt_fens_stinger_hive.note: The stingers nested in the deepest pan, and the nest was built in rows: twelve to a line, every line aimed one way. Stingers do not count. Something counts for them, and taught them to die still lining up. Nica broke the rows for a season. Nine years he has hunted the fen. He never saw a thing line up to die before this one.

salt_fens_cairn_watch.title: The Watch at the Cairn
salt_fens_cairn_watch.summary: An old cairn on the north rise marks graves older than the drowned city, and something has been re-stacking its stones by night. The chronicler asks for one silent watch kept over it, to see what hand does the tidying.
salt_fens_cairn_watch.offer: There's a cairn on the north rise older than Greyhollow, older than the port, likely older than the salt. Someone's been re-stacking it. Stones I logged fallen last month stand squared again, and no boot-print near it. I'll not send a warden with a spear; a spear frightens off a mystery and learns you nothing. I'll send you to stand still and watch. One night. Move for nothing. See what hand tidies a grave no living soul remembers the name in.
salt_fens_cairn_watch.progress: The cairn stands on the north-west rise, above the salt-pans. Keep the watch through the dark. Don't touch the stones. Don't help. Only watch, and remember what you see.
salt_fens_cairn_watch.complete: So. No hand at all. The stones settled themselves square while you watched, dust aligning under them in those fine straight lines, the ground going warm enough to melt the night's frost off the top rock. Nobody tidies the cairn. The cairn is being read, and reading squares it, the way a page lies flat when a thumb holds it open. Set this down in your own book, traveller: the dead here are kept neater than the living. Somebody prefers them that way. Take the keepsake-cord. Tie it to a thing you'd not want kept.

objectives.label: the old cairn on the north rise
objectives.text: Keep the silent watch over the cairn
Tell Ozun what tidies the cairn

salt_fens_cairn_watch.note: A cairn older than the port re-squares itself in the night: dust aligning under it in fine straight lines, the ground warming enough to melt the frost. No hand tidies it. It is being read, and reading squares it, the way a page lies flat under a held thumb. The dead of the fens are kept neater than the living. Something prefers them so.

salt_fens_trunk_hollow_hoard.title: What the Hollow Kept
salt_fens_trunk_hollow_hoard.summary: A reed-cutter found a dead tree's hollow stuffed with keepsakes -- rings, tokens, a child's shoe -- left by folk before they walked out to listen. He wants it emptied and the names returned to whoever's left to want them.
salt_fens_trunk_hollow_hoard.offer: Cutting reeds down the south end I found the old trunk-hollow packed full. Keepsakes -- rings, a cord or two, a little shoe. Folk leave them there, I reckon, before they go stand in the reeds. Like a man empties his pockets before deep water. Somebody ought to fetch them out and find who's owed them back, before the damp rots the lot. I'd do it myself but I can't read the tags, and truth told the hollow gives me the shivers. Bring the keepsakes to me and we'll sort the names.
salt_fens_trunk_hollow_hoard.progress: The trunk-hollow's at the south-west, in the dead tree by the low water. Empty it -- gently, mind, they're all somebody's. Bring the keepsakes back to me to sort.
salt_fens_trunk_hollow_hoard.complete: Look at all of it. Every one tagged in a shaking hand, and every hand that tagged it standing out in the reeds now or gone under. -- The little shoe's got no tag. That's the one I can't put down. Somebody's child emptied their pockets at a dead tree and walked into the fog light. We'll find the ones we can. The rest I'll keep dry till there's a name to give them to. Take the soothing-knot. You'll not sleep easy after the hollow. This helps. A little. It's honest about being only a little.

objectives.label: the trunk-hollow by the low water
objectives.text: Empty the keepsake hollow
Bring the keepsakes to Anton to sort

salt_fens_trunk_hollow_hoard.note: A dead tree's hollow, packed with keepsakes: rings, cords, a child's shoe with no tag. Folk empty their pockets there before they walk out to listen, the way a man empties them before deep water. Anton will return the ones he can and keep the rest dry against a name that may never come. The shoe he cannot put down. Nor, after, could anyone he showed it to.

salt_fens_white_on_white.title: White on White
salt_fens_white_on_white.summary: A salt-farmer was found dead in his own drying-yard, salt-white and salt-cold, no wound, no struggle -- the body itself the only clue against a white ground. The warden needs it read before the tide-crew hauls it as salvage.
salt_fens_white_on_white.offer: There's a body in the drying-yard by the causeway and I need a second pair of eyes before the tide-crew comes and files it as salvage, which is what a corpse is out here -- salvage with a name. Salt-farmer. No wound. No fight. White as his own crop and cold as it, laid down neat in the middle of a white yard where every mark shows, and there's not a mark. Come read it with me. A man doesn't die of nothing. Even here. Especially here.
salt_fens_white_on_white.progress: The drying-yard's by the causeway, where the murder-ground shows white. Read the body -- the hands, the eyes, the ground around him. Then tell me what killed a man in a yard that keeps no secrets.
salt_fens_white_on_white.complete: Clean fingernails. Eyes open, looking up the causeway toward the stone. And in the salt around him, so fine you'd miss it, the crystals lay in lines. Parallel. Combed. He didn't die of nothing, Warden -- he died of understanding something, and it left no more mark than a thought leaves. I've read a hundred deaths on this coast. This is the kind I don't write down, because writing it down is how it spreads. Take the razor. Blackglass. If it comes to it, a fast edge is kinder than what took him.

objectives.label: the salt-farmer in the drying-yard
objectives.text: Read the body in the white yard
Tell Manea what killed the salt-farmer

salt_fens_white_on_white.note: A salt-farmer dead in his own drying-yard, white on white, no wound in a place that keeps no secrets. Clean fingernails; eyes open toward the stone; the salt-crystals combed into parallel lines around him. He died of understanding something, which leaves no more mark than a thought. Manea did not write it down. Writing it down is how it spreads.

salt_fens_graves_facing.title: Which Way They Face
salt_fens_graves_facing.summary: The old fen graves were dug facing the sea, as fen graves always were. Someone has been turning the stones. The runner wants the graveyard counted and the turned ones marked before the priest is told how many.
salt_fens_graves_facing.offer: Fen folk bury facing the water -- toward the drowned city, toward the tide that'll have us all in the end. It's how it's always been done, and there's a comfort in a thing always done. But the graves are turning. Not all. Some. Stones that faced the sea now face the high reeds, toward that stone. I need them counted honest before I tell the priest, because the priest will want to know the number and the number is the whole of the fear. Walk the graveyard. Count which way they face. Bring me the tally.
salt_fens_graves_facing.progress: The graves lie east, on the rise above the drowned fences. Count them -- how many still face the sea, how many turned to the reeds. Bring me the true number. Don't round it kind.
salt_fens_graves_facing.complete: Nineteen face the sea still. Twelve have turned. Twelve. Of course twelve. It's always twelve, or a count of them, the whole rotten grammar of this country -- the dead lined up in twelves like a page of a ledger read aloud. The priest will pray. Praying's what he has. I'll set the salt-lines and mark the turned ones and next season count again, and the sea-facing number will be smaller. It always is. Take the ward-cord. Tie it where you sleep. The turned graves face inland now. Inland is where the living are.

objectives.label: the fen graveyard on the rise
objectives.text: Count which way the graves face
Bring Ileana the tally of turned graves

salt_fens_graves_facing.note: Fen graves were always dug facing the sea. Twelve have turned to face the reeds and the stone above them -- twelve, the country's whole grammar, the dead read out in twelves like a ledger. The runner will pray and mark and count again next season, when the sea-facing number is smaller. It always is. The turned graves face inland now. Inland is where the living sleep.

salt_fens_reading_stone_ward.title: The Long Way Round
salt_fens_reading_stone_ward.summary: Now that Mâl knows the corridors point at the reading-stone, he wants a salt-line laid at the stone's foot -- not to silence it, nothing silences it, but to break the ruled lanes the fog keeps drawing to it, so his walkers stop finding the straight road.
salt_fens_reading_stone_ward.offer: You brought me the word. Now help me spend it. The stone up on the high ground draws the corridors -- lays them straight from a walker's feet to its foot, and folk who've walked the causeway safe their whole lives take the straight road because straight looks kind. I can't silence the stone. No one can. But a salt-line at its foot breaks the lanes -- fouls the ruling, makes the fog draw crooked -- and a crooked road, a body distrusts. Come with me. Lay the line. Kill what stands guard over a thing that wants to be read. And don't, whatever it does, read it.
salt_fens_reading_stone_ward.progress: The reading-stone's on the high ground, north-east, past the standing farmer. Reach it the long way, clear the guardians, lay the salt-line at its foot. Keep your eyes on your boots at the stone. On your boots.
salt_fens_reading_stone_ward.complete: It's done. Line's laid, and already the fog's drawing crooked to the stone -- see, the lanes bend now, they don't rule straight. Won't save everyone. A man determined to listen will find a way to a thing that wants listening to. But it'll save the ones who only wandered, the ones who took the straight road because straight looked like mercy. That's most of them. Most is what I can save. Take the deep-bed lantern. It burns a light the fog can't turn. Go careful to the city, and don't come back but by the causeway.

objectives.text: Clear the guardians at the reading-stone

objectives.label: the foot of the reading-stone
objectives.text: Lay the salt-line at the stone's foot
Return to Mâl by the causeway

salt_fens_reading_stone_ward.note: A salt-line laid at the reading-stone's foot cannot silence it -- nothing silences it -- but it fouls the ruling, and the fog now draws its corridors crooked. A crooked road a body distrusts. It will not save the determined; nothing saves the determined. It saves the ones who only wandered, who took the straight road because straight looked like mercy. Mâl calls that most of them. Most is what he can save.

salt_fens_map_the_safe.title: A Map of What's Left Dry
salt_fens_map_the_safe.summary: The salt-road master needs the fens' remaining dry ground surveyed and marked so his carters stop drowning in shortcuts. But an honest map of the safe paths is worth killing for out here, and he knows it.
salt_fens_map_the_safe.offer: I run salt north to the Ledger Roads, and I've lost two carters this season to the brack -- not to lurkers, to shortcuts. There's no true map of what's dry anymore because what's dry keeps changing. I need one made honest: walk the causeway spurs, mark the ground that holds, and I'll draw it fair. Understand me, though -- a real map of the safe paths is a thing men have knifed for out here. Bring it to my hand and no other. Half of keeping folk alive is knowing who not to give the living to.
salt_fens_map_the_safe.progress: Walk the causeway and its spurs, out to the northern crossing where my carts turn. Mark the dry ground that holds a loaded wheel. Then bring the survey to me sealed.
salt_fens_map_the_safe.complete: Fair work. I'll draw it clean and my carters'll live another season on it -- till the dry moves again and I send someone out to do it over. That's the fens: you don't finish a map, you rent one. Here's your due, and the marked border-map besides -- it's the old country's edges, drawn true, worth having when you finally leave this coast. And you will leave it. Nobody's from the fens. We're all just carters who lost the road.

objectives.label: the northern causeway crossing
objectives.text: Survey the dry ground the carts can hold
Bring the sealed survey to Costel

salt_fens_map_the_safe.note: The fens' dry ground shifts faster than a map can be drawn, so the salt-road master does not finish maps -- he rents them, sending a walker out to redo the survey each season as the safe paths drown. Two carters were lost this year to shortcuts, not to monsters. Nobody is from the fens, Costel says. They are all carters who lost the road.

salt_fens_brine_witch_reagents.title: The First Editions
salt_fens_brine_witch_reagents.summary: The brine-witch wants to brew back to Hessik's uncorrupted first edition, but the reagents she needs -- clean marsh-lichen ungrown by any live stone -- are getting harder to find each season. She sends for a gathering from ground the fen hasn't reached.
salt_fens_brine_witch_reagents.offer: I told you my method came down to me five copies corrupt. I mean to brew back past the errors -- to Hessik's own first hand -- but that needs reagents as clean as his were, and clean is a thing the fen's running out of. Marsh-lichen ungrown by a live stone. I know one bank of it left, out by the cairn road, ground the orange hasn't reached yet. Gather me a good clutch before it does. Every season the clean ground shrinks and my reach with it. Beat the fen to the lichen. Just this once, be faster than the rot.
salt_fens_brine_witch_reagents.progress: The clean lichen grows on the old cairn road, west, on ground the fen hasn't turned. Gather a good clutch -- and check it doesn't read orange before you pull it. Clean means clean.
salt_fens_brine_witch_reagents.complete: Clean, all of it -- I checked twice, old hands, older fear. Now I can brew Hessik as Hessik meant, one honest bottle before the ground that grows it goes the way of everything. Here's the first pour: a poultice, cultured proper, closes a wound the salt would sour. -- Keep the receipt of this in your head, traveller.
