They're attending something. I won't have stone soldiers in my house that answer to a floor. Break the turned ones. I'd rather a gap in the watch than a watch that's changed sides.
the_archive_sentinel_dust.progress: The turned sentinels face the deep foundation, west and down. Break them before you learn what they attend.
the_archive_sentinel_dust.complete: Rubble, and good. But you saw the dust. You saw which way it pointed even after they fell. Whatever's in my cellar, it doesn't need the sentinels -- it just borrowed them to show me it could. Four hundred years I trusted stone to stay loyal. Take the striker. Iron's honest, at least, till the day it isn't. Ask me again next season.

objectives.text: Break the sentinels that turned to face the foundation

objectives.label: the west stair-head
objectives.text: See which way the dust still points

the_archive_sentinel_dust.note: The Archive's sentinels turned in one night to face the cellar, and the dust turned with them, pointing down and west in parallel lines. Serban broke the turned ones. The dust still points. Something in the foundation did not need the sentinels. It only wanted the captain to know it could take them.

the_archive_golem_quarry.title: The Tomb-Golems in the Sub-Stacks
the_archive_golem_quarry.summary: The tomb-golems that haul the heavy anchor-tablets have stopped hauling and started digging -- downward, toward the live node. The smith who maintains their bindings wants them broken before they reach whatever is calling them.
the_archive_golem_quarry.offer: I've kept the tomb-golems' bindings sound for thirty years -- they're just haulers, walking cranes for the anchor-tablets no man can lift. But they've stopped hauling. They're digging now, down through the sub-stack floor, patient as clerks, toward the west foundation. My bindings won't turn them. Something with a louder claim has them. Break the diggers before they open whatever they're so keen to reach. I've seen what patience gets you in this town. Nothing good, and always on schedule.
the_archive_golem_quarry.progress: The tomb-golems are digging down through the sub-stacks toward the node. Break them before they reach it.
the_archive_golem_quarry.complete: Down they go, back to rubble and rest. But look at the hole they'd sunk -- straight, plumb, aimed. No golem digs plumb on its own. Something drew them a line and they followed it like good clerks follow a ledger. I'll wall the pit and pretend that fixes it. Take the reaver -- dragonbone, from the old stock, before the ore stopped coming honest. It doesn't much care what's calling. Nor should you, if you want to keep your name your own.

objectives.text: Break the golems digging toward the node
Recover the focus-stone drawing them down

the_archive_golem_quarry.note: The tomb-golems stopped hauling and began digging plumb toward the west foundation, drawn by a focus-stone no smith placed. Vasile broke them and walled the pit. The hole they'd sunk was straight and aimed. Something in the Archive is drawing lines now, and the stone follows them like a good clerk follows a ledger.

the_archive_marked_border.title: The Map That Filed Itself
the_archive_marked_border.summary: A merchant's map of the trade-roads has grown a border of tiny collection-marks overnight -- every town he trades with, stamped in violet in a hand that isn't his. He wants to know who is filing his customers, and why the stamps match a schedule.
the_archive_marked_border.offer: I trade the roads between here and the piers, and I keep a good map. This morning it had a border it didn't have last night: little violet marks round the edge, one for every town I sell to, in a clerk's hand, precise as a ledger. Someone's filing my customers -- and the marks aren't random, they follow a route, a schedule, town by town in an order. I want to know whose schedule my whole living is on. Take the map to the watcher. He reads borders for a trade. Don't read the middle of it yourself. Just the edge.
the_archive_marked_border.progress: The merchant's map has a border of violet collection-marks. Take it to the watcher and learn whose schedule it follows.
the_archive_marked_border.complete: A collection route, he says. Town by town, the order a lender would work them, and every town I trade in is on it. So I've been feeding a supply-line without knowing -- selling grain to places already marked for finishing, keeping them fed and countable till it's their turn. I didn't do a wrong thing. I did a scheduled thing. That's worse, somehow. Take the map-border. Burn it or study it. I've done neither. I just can't stop looking at the order.

objectives.text: Take the self-marked map from the merchant's stall
Have the watcher read the collection-route in the border

the_archive_marked_border.note: A merchant's map grew a violet border overnight -- his every customer stamped for collection, in a route, on a schedule. Bogdan had been feeding a supply-line and never knew. He did nothing wrong. He did something scheduled, which is worse. He cannot stop looking at the order the towns are marked in.

the_archive_the_watchers_walls.title: The Walls That Kept a Voice
the_archive_the_watchers_walls.summary: The Archive inherited its listening-walls from an older spy-city, and one gallery has begun playing back a voice recorded a thousand years ago -- a coordinator's, giving an order nobody living gave. The watcher needs it silenced before the sentinels start obeying it.
the_archive_the_watchers_walls.offer: These walls carry sound. Always have -- the house was built on the bones of an older listening-city, and it kept the trick: whisper in the east gallery, it surfaces in the west, a beat late. Lately one gallery's surfacing something older. A voice, a woman's, cold and professional, giving a filing-order in a tongue that went quiet a thousand years back. Nobody's speaking it. The wall remembers it, and the sentinels have started drifting toward the sound like they mean to comply. Find the stone that's holding her voice and break it. Before the stone decides it's still on duty.
the_archive_the_watchers_walls.progress: A thousand-year-old order plays in the listening-gallery. Find and break the stone that kept the coordinator's voice.
the_archive_the_watchers_walls.complete: Silenced -- for now. You heard her, though. Not the words, saints keep you from the words, but the CALM. A woman filing the world tidy, a thousand years dead, and the walls kept her because the walls were built to keep exactly that: a voice that recognizes marks that shouldn't exist. She founded our whole listening trade, they say. Turned a spy's ear into a mercy, once. The wall only remembered the ear. Take the lens. Read borders, not middles. That's the whole of surviving here.

objectives.label: the listening-gallery
objectives.text: Trace the played-back coordinator's voice
Put down the sentinels drifting to obey it

the_archive_the_watchers_walls.note: The Archive's listening-walls kept a voice a thousand years -- a coordinator filing the world calm, in a tongue gone quiet. The sentinels began drifting to obey her. Vlaicu broke the stone that held her. The walls remembered her ear and forgot the one mercy she'd once done with it. So did the Archive. So did the world.

the_archive_the_forgers_writ.title: The Forger of Unfilings
the_archive_the_forgers_writ.summary: Someone is selling forged writs of unfiling -- documents that pretend to reopen a collected account, sold to the grieving for everything they have. The dignitary wants the forger's stock recovered, though whether to destroy it or sell it himself remains, as ever, an open question.
the_archive_the_forgers_writ.offer: There is a man selling hope, which is the most profitable and most despicable trade in this house. Forged writs of unfiling -- papers that claim to reopen a collected soul, sold to widows and orphans for the last coin they own. They don't work. Nothing reopens a closed account; that's the whole terror of collection. But the grieving will pay anything for the lie. Find his stock. Bring it to me. I'll decide whether to burn it or -- there are buyers for good forgeries, you understand. There always are. That's the second despicable trade.
the_archive_the_forgers_writ.progress: The forger's stock of false unfiling-writs is hidden in the toll-office. Recover it and bring it to the dignitary.
the_archive_the_forgers_writ.complete: His whole stock, and finely done -- these would fool a grieving mother, which is the point of them. Now. The clean thing is the fire. The profitable thing is a Wolfarin buyer who'd pay well for papers that forge the record's own hand. You're waiting for me to choose the fire. ...I'll tell you when I've decided. Take your fee. In this house, a man who hasn't yet chosen is a man with something to sell. I intend to stay that man as long as I can.

objectives.text: Recover the forger's stock of false writs

objectives.label: the toll-office
objectives.text: Search the toll-office for the forgery-stock

the_archive_the_forgers_writ.note: A man sold the grieving forged writs to reopen their collected dead -- papers that work on nothing but hope. Ilie holds the stock, undecided between the fire and a Wolfarin buyer. In the Archive, a man who has not yet chosen is a man with something to sell. He means to stay that man.

the_archive_the_couriers_satchel.title: The Satchel That Came Sealed
the_archive_the_couriers_satchel.summary: A courier reached the Archive gate and died at it -- no wound, clean hands, satchel still sealed. Inside is a message the Archive must file but no one dares read. The guard needs it carried to the sealed-intake, unopened, whatever it costs to not look.
the_archive_the_couriers_satchel.offer: A courier made the gate at dawn and died standing up. No wound. Clean hands, clean nails, boots muddy, satchel sealed -- he carried a thing all the way here and it killed him for carrying it, not for reading it, mind, the seal's unbroken. That's how the Courier died, the old one, in the stories. Whatever's in the satchel wants reading, and reading it is the ending. Carry it to the sealed-intake and put it in the drawer that's made for things you mustn't understand. Don't stop at the reading-desks. Don't set it down anywhere quiet. And don't, for your name's sake, break the seal.
the_archive_the_couriers_satchel.progress: The dead courier's sealed satchel must reach the sealed-intake unread. Carry it. Do not break the seal.
the_archive_the_couriers_satchel.complete: In the drawer, sealed, filed as 'received, unread' -- which is the only safe verdict for a thing like that. You felt it, didn't you. The pull. Every quiet corner you passed, it wanted you to stop and look, and looking would've been the last thing you ever understood. You walked past every one. Good. That's the whole skill of living here: carrying what the stone routes to you, and refusing to read the address. Take the ward. You've earned an incurious night.

objectives.text: Take the dead courier's sealed satchel

objectives.label: the sealed-intake
objectives.text: File the satchel unread in the sealed-intake

the_archive_the_couriers_satchel.note: A courier reached the gate and died of what he carried -- no wound, clean hands, satchel still sealed. You carried it to the sealed-intake unread, past every quiet corner that begged you to look. Comprehension is the ending. You refused the address. That is the only skill the Archive teaches that keeps a person theirs.

the_archive_the_thinning_veil.title: Where the Reading-Room Goes Thin
the_archive_the_thinning_veil.summary: In the oldest reading-room, the air has begun to thin -- the boundary between the record and the room wearing through where too many tablets have been understood in one place. The apothecary needs the thin patches mapped before a reader falls clean through into the ledger.
the_archive_the_thinning_veil.offer: There's a reading-room, the oldest one, where a thousand years of clerks have understood a thousand years of tablets, and all that comprehension has worn the place thin. Not the floor. The BOUNDARY -- between the room and the record it reads. Stand in the wrong patch and the room goes quiet and the ledger goes loud, and a reader can slip through into being filed without a stamp ever falling. I need the thin places mapped and marked before someone reads themselves out of existence. Take my reagent. Where it reads orange, the veil's worn through. Mark those. Stand in none.
the_archive_the_thinning_veil.progress: The oldest reading-room has worn thin between room and record. Map the orange patches where the veil is gone.
the_archive_the_thinning_veil.complete: Three patches, all by the founder's-lectern -- of course, where the deepest reading was always done. I'll rope them and post the ward. You'll think a boundary can't wear out. It's the most worn thing in this building. Every act of understanding is a small erosion, and we've been understanding here for ten centuries. Take the veil-charm. It thickens the air round a mind that's read too long. Wear it, and stop reading before it's interesting. It gets interesting right before the end.

objectives.label: the oldest reading-room
objectives.text: Map the worn-thin patches by the founder's-lectern
Break the sentinels drawn to the thinning

the_archive_the_thinning_veil.note: Ten centuries of understanding wore the oldest reading-room thin -- the boundary between room and record eroded to nothing by the low patient friction of comprehension. Toma mapped the worn places and roped them off. A reader can slip through into filing with no stamp falling at all. In the Archive, learning is a kind of erosion. It always was.

the_archive_dossier_of_a_habit.title: The Habit They Filed On You
the_archive_dossier_of_a_habit.summary: The watcher has found a dossier of habits that shouldn't exist -- one kept on the player, tracking every choice they've made since the border. He wants to know who ordered it, and whether the answer is the Morven, the Archive, or the stone itself.
the_archive_dossier_of_a_habit.offer: I read files for a living, and I recognize a mark that shouldn't exist. There's a dossier in the reading-room with your habits in it, traveler -- not your name, your DEEDS. The one who walked the miller's girl home. The one who chose the fire over the sale, or didn't. It's been kept since the border, updated clean, and no living hand I can find placed the orders. Fetch it before it thickens further. I'd know whether you're being watched by the Morven, by this house, or by the thing this house grew in its cellar. The answers get worse in that order.
the_archive_dossier_of_a_habit.progress: A dossier of your own habits is kept in the reading-room. Recover it and learn who ordered the watching.
the_archive_dossier_of_a_habit.complete: No hand ordered it. That's the answer, and it's the worst one. Not the Morven, not the Archive -- the file writes itself, updating each time you choose something specific and warm and hard. Something's keeping a record of exactly the thing the founders swore to protect, for exactly the reason they feared. It likes you, traveler. It files what it means to keep. Take the dossier. Read your own deeds if you dare. I'd sooner read a stranger's. Yours, you'd have to live with knowing were noticed.

objectives.text: Recover the self-writing dossier of your habits

objectives.label: the reading-room
objectives.text: Search the reading-room for the ordering hand

the_archive_dossier_of_a_habit.note: A dossier of the traveller's own deeds is kept in the Archive -- the one who walked her home, the one who chose the fire. No hand ordered it. The file writes itself, keeping exactly what the founders swore to protect, for exactly the reason they feared. Something in the house files what it means to keep. It has begun to keep you.

the_archive_the_blind_seers_word.title: Already Collected
the_archive_the_blind_seers_word.summary: A blind seer sits in the west gallery repeating one sentence at a stone-faced clerk who keeps walking the halls: 'Already collected -- why is it still walking?' The chronicler wants to know what the seer sees, and what the clerk is, before the clerk notices being seen.
the_archive_the_blind_seers_word.offer: There's a blind man in the west gallery, a seer, eyeless and sightless and seeing more than any of us. He sits and says one thing, over and over, whenever a certain clerk passes -- a grey-faced fellow in a worn coat who's been walking these halls longer than the register admits. 'Already collected,' the seer says. 'Why is it still walking?' I need to know what he means. Go and hear it from him proper. Mind the clerk doesn't take an interest in you. Men who are already collected have nothing left to lose by taking one.
the_archive_the_blind_seers_word.progress: The blind seer sits in the west gallery. Hear what he sees in the grey-faced clerk who will not stay filed.
the_archive_the_blind_seers_word.complete: So. The clerk's a closed account that refuses to be closed -- a debt that keeps walking, filed and finished and here anyway, the one discrepancy the whole machine can't settle. The seer's right to scream it. A record that finalized itself and then declined the verdict, wearing a coat, carrying no weapon, only solutions. ...I've filed ten thousand endings. I never once considered one might say no. Take the token. A kept name, in case you ever need to decline a verdict of your own.

objectives.text: Hear the blind seer in the west gallery

objectives.label: the west gallery
objectives.text: Watch the grey-faced clerk who will not stay filed

the_archive_the_blind_seers_word.note: A blind seer screams one truth at a grey-faced clerk: 'Already collected -- why is it still walking?' The clerk is a closed account that declined its own verdict, a debt that keeps walking, the one discrepancy the machine cannot settle. Lupu has filed ten thousand endings. He never once considered one might say no.

the_archive_the_kept_names_daily.title: The Day's Empty Chairs
the_archive_the_kept_names_daily.summary: Every dawn the night's collection-list is posted, and Mircea sets one empty chair for each new name. He asks a steady hand to read the list, set the chairs, and speak each name once aloud -- so the record isn't the only thing that kept it.
the_archive_the_kept_names_daily.offer: New list posted at the gate, same as every dawn -- the names collected in the night. I set a chair for each and speak them once over the first bowl, so the ledger isn't the last mouth to hold them. My voice goes some mornings; the list is long some mornings. Read it for me today. Set the chairs. Say each name the once, plain, no grief on it -- grief they can file, a plain saying they can't. Then the room's warm and the day can start. It's a small thing. Small things are the only ones that hold here.
the_archive_the_kept_names_daily.progress: The night's collection-list is posted at the gate. Read it, set a chair for each name, and speak each once.
the_archive_the_kept_names_daily.complete: That's them held, then -- once, warm, out loud, by a mouth and not a stamp. The record's got them closed. We've got them said. Doesn't undo the closing. Nothing undoes the closing. But there's a difference between a name that's only filed and a name that's also been missed, and the difference is this chair, this bowl, this morning. Come again tomorrow. There's always a tomorrow list. That's the one thing this town never runs short of.

objectives.label: the gate-post list
objectives.text: Read the night's collection-list at the gate
Set the empty chairs and speak each name once

the_archive_the_kept_names_daily.note: Each dawn Mircea sets a chair for every name the night collected, and speaks it once aloud. The record holds them closed; the saying holds them missed. It undoes nothing. But a name only filed and a name also missed are not the same name, and the difference is one warm chair each morning. There is always a tomorrow list.

the_archive_sentinel_patrol_daily.title: The Turned Watch
the_archive_sentinel_patrol_daily.summary: Every night a few more sentinels turn to face the cellar, and every morning the captain needs them broken before the day-clerks notice their guardians have changed sides. Grim work, done with a soldier's gallows humour. A standing job, and steady coin.
the_archive_sentinel_patrol_daily.offer: Morning inspection, same as ever: some of my stone lads turned in the night to face the cellar again, and I'll not have the day-clerks arriving to find the watch gazing lovingly at the floor. Bad for morale, and mine's thin enough. Walk the galleries, break the ones that turned -- you'll know them, they're the reverent ones -- and let me post honest gaps where there used to be treasonous statues. Pays daily. It's the only reliably fresh work in a house that mostly deals in old finished things.
the_archive_sentinel_patrol_daily.progress: The sentinels that turned overnight face the cellar again. Break the reverent ones before the day-clerks arrive.
the_archive_sentinel_patrol_daily.complete: Gaps where the traitors stood, and the clerks none the wiser -- good. You'd think breaking the same statues every dawn would wear on a man. It does. But there's a clean pleasure in it too: whatever's down that cellar recruits my watch every night, and every morning I fire them. It'll win eventually. Everything down there wins eventually. Till then, it's a day's work and a day's coin, and the coin spends. Same time tomorrow. Bring the striker warm.

objectives.text: Break the sentinels that turned overnight

the_archive_sentinel_patrol_daily.note: Every night the cellar recruits the captain's watch; every dawn he fires them with a hammer. He knows it wins eventually -- everything down there wins eventually. Until then it is honest work and daily coin in a house that otherwise deals only in old finished things. Serban takes a clean pleasure in the one job that stays fresh.

the_archive_riddlers_lens.title: Reading the Border, Never the Middle
the_archive_riddlers_lens.summary: A riddler's lens -- ground to read the edges of a document while blurring its dangerous centre -- has been stolen from the reading-room. The merchant who commissioned it wants it back before the thief reads a centre and stops being a thief.
the_archive_riddlers_lens.offer: I paid a Blestem grinder good coin for a riddler's lens -- clever glass, reads the border of a tablet sharp and blurs the middle soft, so a clerk can catalogue a dangerous document without understanding it and getting filed for the privilege. Some fool stole it. Which means some fool will use it wrong, read the middle for the thrill, and understand a thing that ends people. I want it back before there's a corpse with clean hands and my lens beside it. Ask the guard where the thief holed up. Then get me my glass.
the_archive_riddlers_lens.progress: The stolen riddler's lens is with a thief who means to read the middle. Recover it before he understands something fatal.
the_archive_riddlers_lens.complete: My glass, unbroken -- and the thief? Sat very still, eyes tracking nothing, hands flat and even on the table. Read the middle, then. Understood one line of one tablet and it finished him mid-theft, tidy as you like. The lens works; that's the awful proof of it. Take it. It'll let you read the edge of any dreadful thing and leave the heart of it soft. Read borders, traveler. The middle of every document in this house is a way to die politely.

objectives.text: Ask the guard where the lens-thief holed up
Recover the riddler's lens from the thief

the_archive_riddlers_lens.note: A thief stole a lens made to read a document's edge and blur its fatal centre -- then read the centre for the thrill. Bogdan recovered his glass from beside a corpse with clean hands and still eyes. The lens works; that is the awful proof of it. In the Archive, the middle of every document is a way to die politely.

the_archive_the_intake_door.title: The Door Made for Not Understanding
the_archive_the_intake_door.summary: The sealed-intake door -- where things too dangerous to read are filed unread -- has been left ajar, and something inside has begun reading itself aloud to the empty hall. The guard needs it shut and warded before a passing clerk answers.
the_archive_the_intake_door.offer: The sealed-intake -- you know it, the drawer-hall for things we file without ever reading, courier-satchels and the like -- its door's been left ajar. And with the door open, one of the things inside has started reading ITSELF, out loud, patient, to nobody, in that flat clerk's cadence that isn't a voice. A clerk walked past this morning and stopped to listen and I had to drag him off by the collar. Get in there, find the thing that's reading, don't listen to a word of it, and get the door shut and warded. A sealed-intake with an open door is just a mouth.
the_archive_the_intake_door.progress: The sealed-intake stands ajar and something inside reads itself aloud. Silence it and ward the door -- without listening.
the_archive_the_intake_door.complete: Shut, warded, and my ears still ringing with a sound I refused to make into words -- which is the trick, refusing to let it be words. It wasn't threatening. It never threatens. It was just reading, patient as a tide, offering to be understood, and understanding it is the whole of the harm. A house of things too dangerous to read, and we left the mouth open. Take the striker and the ward both. And if you ever hear reading in an empty hall -- walk the other way whistling. Loudly.

objectives.label: the sealed-intake hall
objectives.text: Silence the thing reading itself aloud
Ward the sealed-intake door shut

the_archive_the_intake_door.note: The sealed-intake -- the drawer-hall for things too dangerous to read -- was left ajar, and a thing inside began reading itself aloud to the empty hall, patient as a tide, offering to be understood. A clerk stopped to listen. Stelian dragged him off and shut the mouth. A house of unreadable things, and someone left the door open. Someone always does.

the_archive_the_first_edition_folly.title: The Clerk Who Read to the End
the_archive_the_first_edition_folly.summary: A junior clerk, ambitious and bright, has taken an un-dulled draught and gone into the deep stacks to finally understand the refusing tablet -- to make his name by closing what no one could close. Nedelcu needs him stopped before he succeeds.
the_archive_the_first_edition_folly.offer: One of mine's gone down to be a hero. Bright lad, brighter than's safe, and tired of being kept dull -- he's dosed himself clear-headed and gone into the deep stacks to settle the refusing tablet, the one that's closed and un-closed a thousand years. Thinks he'll make his name closing the uncloseable. He doesn't understand what closing it would mean, and if he gets close to understanding, the understanding will be the last thing he does. Go down. Bring him up. Break whatever's between him and that tablet. And pour this dulling-draught down his throat whether he thanks you or not.
the_archive_the_first_edition_folly.progress: The ambitious clerk has gone to close the refusing tablet. Reach him in the deep stacks before he understands it.
the_archive_the_first_edition_folly.complete: You got him. Half-collected already -- eyes going flat, hands going even -- and one line short of the ending he was so proud to reach. He'll hate me for the dulling. He'll be alive to hate me, which is the trade. His name'd have been made, right enough: 'the clerk who closed the uncloseable,' carved on nothing, because there'd have been no clerk left to carve for. Take the draught. Ambition's the vector in this house. It always points you at exactly the thing that ends you.

objectives.label: the refusing tablet's aisle
objectives.text: Reach the clerk before he closes the tablet
Break the golems between him and the tablet

the_archive_the_first_edition_folly.note: A bright clerk dosed himself clear and went to close the uncloseable tablet, to make his name. He got one line short of the ending. Nedelcu poured the dulling-draught down him and saved a man who will hate him for it, and be alive to. Ambition is the vector here. It points you at exactly the thing that ends you, and calls it your name.

the_archive_the_first_debt_of_the_house.title: The First Debt of the House
the_archive_the_first_debt_of_the_house.summary: The oldest anchor in the vault is the Archive's own founding tablet -- the first testimony it ever took, and the first it ever closed. The dignitary wants it read for a loophole. The reading will teach only that there was never a loophole, and never a founder who meant this.
the_archive_the_first_debt_of_the_house.offer: In the lowest vault sits the first anchor -- the founding tablet, the Archive's own first testimony, taken warm a thousand years ago by a man who wept doing it. It is also the first account this house ever closed. I have a use for it: if the founding was flawed, every collection since inherits the flaw, and a clever party might unfile the lot. Read the anchor. Find me the crack in the first stone. And if there's no crack -- tell me that too, though I warn you, men who learn there was never a loophole tend to stop arguing about anything.
the_archive_the_first_debt_of_the_house.progress: The founding anchor sits in the lowest vault. Read the first debt the house ever closed, and look for its flaw.
the_archive_the_first_debt_of_the_house.complete: No crack. You're certain. The first hand did it right -- took a warm life, held it whole, and then, for the best of reasons, closed it, and meant the closing as love. The rot was in the mercy from the first stone. There's no flaw to unfile, because the flaw was the founding, done perfectly. ...I'll not sell that reading. Some truths lose their value the moment a second person holds them. Take your fee, and the thing itself. Neither of us can put it down now.

objectives.label: the lowest vault
objectives.text: Read the Archive's founding anchor
Break the golems anchored to the first tablet

the_archive_the_first_debt_of_the_house.note: The Archive's first anchor was read for a loophole and yielded none. The founding was done perfectly, in love, and the closing was the flaw -- there from the first warm stone. There is nothing to unfile, because the rot was the mercy, done right. Ilie will not sell the reading. Some truths lose their worth the moment a second person holds them.

the_archive_a_ward_against_listening.title: A Ward Against the Listening
the_archive_a_ward_against_listening.summary: Petru's neighbour has started listening -- head tilted, still, attending something in the walls -- the first symptom before a person stops being a person. He begs a folk-ward hung and a hearth-hex sign cut before his neighbour finishes hearing whatever's speaking.
the_archive_a_ward_against_listening.offer: My neighbour's gone to listening. You know the look -- head on one side, hands gone quiet, eyes on the middle distance like the wall's telling him something worth more than his supper. It's the first sign. After the listening comes the stillness and after the stillness they don't come back. The old folk hung a ward and cut a hearth-hex over the door and it held the listening off, my gran swore by it. I've the sign to cut but no ward-stuff. Fetch me a folk-warding and a hex-sign from the apothecary. Quick, while he's still answering to his name.
the_archive_a_ward_against_listening.progress: Petru's neighbour has begun to listen. Fetch a folk-ward and a hearth-hex sign before the stillness takes him.
the_archive_a_ward_against_listening.complete: Hung and cut and blessed the old way, and -- there, look -- he blinked. Turned his head from the wall and asked was there soup. There's always soup. He's back, for now, for a while, for as long as a bit of chalk and a bent nail can argue with whatever's under this town. Won't hold forever. Nothing holds forever here. But it held tonight, and he ate, and he knew his own name saying grace. Take a ward for yourself. You'll feel the listening start one day. Everyone does.

objectives.text: Fetch a folk-ward from the apothecary
Fetch a hearth-hex sign to cut over the door

the_archive_a_ward_against_listening.note: Petru's neighbour began to listen -- head tilted, still, attending the walls, the first symptom before a person ends. A folk-ward and a cut hex-sign argued him back to his own name for one supper. It will not hold forever. Nothing holds forever here. But he ate tonight, and knew himself saying grace, and that was the whole of the win.

coldharbor_deep_the_water_that_kept_them.title: The Water That Kept Them
coldharbor_deep_the_water_that_kept_them.summary: Coldharbor drowned slow, quay by quay, and the harbor never let its dead float up. Dorica keeps the tally-book of who the water kept. She wants one more count, honest, before the ledger takes it.
coldharbor_deep_the_water_that_kept_them.offer: The harbor here does not give its dead back. Six hundred families went under with the low town, quay by quay, and not one washed up to be waked proper. I keep the tally still -- a name, a slip, a year. Walk the drowned steps and count for me the wights that answer to their own names. I would know how many are still theirs before someone files them otherwise.
coldharbor_deep_the_water_that_kept_them.progress: The Deep-Wights hold the flooded steps below the old customs quay. Put down the ones that no longer answer to a name, and count as you go.
coldharbor_deep_the_water_that_kept_them.complete: Eleven, and every one turned its face to me before it went down -- knew me, I think. Or knew the book. My grandmother's book. Whatever kept them down there kept them tidy, and tidy is the word that scares me. Here -- salt keeps a line honest. Take it.

objectives.text: Lay the nameless Deep-Wights down
Bring Dorica the count

coldharbor_deep_the_water_that_kept_them.note: Eleven Deep-Wights, and all knew the tally-book by sight. Dorica keeps counting names the water kept. Someone else, she fears, keeps a longer book -- and a different word for full.

coldharbor_deep_low_tide_lanterns.title: Low-Tide Lanterns
coldharbor_deep_low_tide_lanterns.summary: The Deep-Bed lanterns lit the drowned wards once, so folk could find the stair up before the water rose. They burn cold-blue now, and Nica wants them relit -- or wants to know why they light themselves.
coldharbor_deep_low_tide_lanterns.offer: Time was, a lantern in a Coldharbor window meant a warm floor above the tide. My father hung one every dusk of his life. They light now on their own, blue and cold, along the sunk arcade -- nobody carrying, nobody tending. I have stopped sleeping over it. Go down and gather the drowned ones the crawlers nest in, and bring me a lantern that a living hand still lit. I need to hold one that means what it used to.
coldharbor_deep_low_tide_lanterns.progress: The Cave-Crawlers nest in the flooded arcade among the drowned lanterns. Gather the Deep-Bed lanterns from the wrack.
coldharbor_deep_low_tide_lanterns.complete: Cold to the touch, all of them, and not a drop of oil left -- yet they held their light down there, blue as a well gone copper's cousin. My father's lantern meant come home. These mean stay. I will not hang another. Take this one; it may light your way better than it lit mine.

objectives.text: Gather cold-lit lanterns from the arcade
Clear the crawlers from the nests

coldharbor_deep_low_tide_lanterns.note: The Deep-Bed lanterns burn with no oil and no hand. Nica stopped hanging hers. Down in the arcade, something prefers the word stay to the word home.

coldharbor_deep_the_customs_house_stair.title: The Customs-House Stair
coldharbor_deep_the_customs_house_stair.summary: The old customs house sank with its ledgers sealed, and the water read them anyway. Sanda wants a look at the deepest stair, where the entries are said to be updating themselves.
coldharbor_deep_the_customs_house_stair.offer: I hold the customs seal now, for whatever a seal is worth under six fathoms of harbor. The old house sank with every ledger locked -- goods in, goods out, nothing owed. Dockmen swear the entries have kept writing down there. New lines. Fresh ink under salt water, which is not a thing ink does. Go to the sunk customs stair and see. I would send myself, but a clerk who reads too far in this town is a clerk who stops coming to work.
coldharbor_deep_the_customs_house_stair.progress: The sunk customs stair lies at the low quay, west of the tide-mark. Reach it and see what the ledgers have written since they drowned.
coldharbor_deep_the_customs_house_stair.complete: You saw the lines, then. Names entered against no goods, and a column I do not have a heading for, and every hand the same hand. That is not book-keeping. That is somebody keeping book on us. Say nothing at the inn. Take this -- a forger's craft, and the only honest thing in that house was the forgery.

objectives.label: the sunk customs stair
objectives.text: Reach the drowned customs stair
Report what the ledgers wrote

coldharbor_deep_the_customs_house_stair.note: The drowned ledgers keep writing in one hand. New names, no goods, a column with no heading. Sanda locked the door she cannot lock and went home. The house keeps her account current.

coldharbor_deep_crawlers_in_the_cistern.title: Crawlers in the Cistern
coldharbor_deep_crawlers_in_the_cistern.summary: The upper cistern is the one clean water left to the living of Coldharbor. Cave-Crawlers have come up the overflow into it. Ileana asks the plain, hard thing: clear them before the last clean water learns the harbor's taste.
coldharbor_deep_crawlers_in_the_cistern.offer: There is one cistern above the tide that still gives sweet water, and it is the reason anyone lives up here at all. The crawlers found the overflow. They are in it now, in the dark of it, and I have three children who drink from that pipe. I cannot pay what you are worth. I can pay what I have. Clear the cistern. Please. Before the sweet water goes the way of the harbor.
coldharbor_deep_crawlers_in_the_cistern.progress: The Cave-Crawlers have come up into the upper cistern by the overflow. Kill them and clear the clean water.
coldharbor_deep_crawlers_in_the_cistern.complete: Sweet. Still sweet. I ran a cup before I said a word to you, and I am not ashamed of it. My children will drink another season. You will not remember their names and that is all right -- I will remember yours. Take my mother's hex-sign. It kept our door. Let it keep yours.

objectives.text: Clear the crawlers from the upper cistern

coldharbor_deep_crawlers_in_the_cistern.note: The cistern runs sweet another season. Ileana's children will drink. She ran the first cup herself before thanks -- and was not ashamed. In Coldharbor, that is not greed. That is faith.

coldharbor_deep_a_name_the_wights_keep.title: A Name the Wights Keep
coldharbor_deep_a_name_the_wights_keep.summary: Ana chronicles a drowned town by asking its dead who they were. She has heard the Deep-Wights answer -- one name, over and over, that belongs to none of them. She wants it collected, before it collects a body.
coldharbor_deep_a_name_the_wights_keep.offer: I write the history of a place the sea already finished. My method is simple and my method is dreadful: I go down to the tide-line and I ask the Deep-Wights their names, and I write what they say. Lately they answer the same name. Not one of theirs -- I have the rolls, I checked. A living name. Go among them, take the tokens they clutch, and bring me back the one that carries a kept name. I must know whose it is before they do.
coldharbor_deep_a_name_the_wights_keep.progress: The Deep-Wights carry name-tokens at the tide-line below the chapel. Gather the kept-name tokens they hold.
coldharbor_deep_a_name_the_wights_keep.complete: It is a Coldharbor name. A living one -- a woman up at the inn who has never once gone below the tide. The wights have been keeping her name warm in the water, the way you keep a seat for someone expected. She is not expected yet. I have not told her. What would I say? Here. A shroud, for the named. You may have more use for it than she will.

objectives.text: Gather kept-name tokens from the wights
Give Ana the tokens

coldharbor_deep_a_name_the_wights_keep.note: The Deep-Wights keep a living woman's name warm in the water, a seat held for one not yet expected. Ana wrote it down and told no one. The harbor is patient. It has kept worse seats longer.

coldharbor_deep_the_watchers_vigil.title: The Watcher's Vigil
coldharbor_deep_the_watchers_vigil.summary: Voica stands the tide-watch alone since her partner went down and did not stop working. She asks a stranger to stand one turn of the glass at the low mark and simply witness the thing she can no longer trust her own eyes to.
coldharbor_deep_the_watchers_vigil.offer: I stand the tide-watch. Somebody must, in a town the tide already took -- to mark how high it comes, how fast, whether it means to finish us this year or the next. My partner stood it before me. He is down there still, and he stands it still, in his way, at his post, working. I cannot watch that alone another night. Stand one turn at the low mark with me. Only watch. Tell me tomorrow that you saw what I see, and that I am not the one who has gone wrong.
coldharbor_deep_the_watchers_vigil.progress: Keep the tide-watch with Voica at the low water mark, below the ruined mole. Stand the turn and witness.
coldharbor_deep_the_watchers_vigil.complete: You saw him. At his post. Marking the tide the way I taught him, the way he taught me, working a watch nobody set him. And you saw that the water did not rise the whole turn -- held flat, held still, the way a room holds still when someone is listening. Thank you for your eyes. Mine were right, and I am sorrier for it than I can say. Take the knot. It has soothed better watches than this one.

objectives.label: the low water mark
objectives.text: Stand the tide-watch at the low mark
Tell Voica what you witnessed

coldharbor_deep_the_watchers_vigil.note: Voica's partner keeps a watch no one set him, at a post the water took. The tide held flat the whole turn, the way a room holds still when it is being listened to. She was not the one gone wrong. She wishes she had been.

coldharbor_deep_forge_below_the_waterlin.title: The Forge Below the Waterline
coldharbor_deep_forge_below_the_waterlin.summary: Ozun works the last dry forge in Coldharbor and has run out of good iron. The only iron left is bog-iron the crawlers pack into their nests -- iron that drowned with the low town. He needs it, and he needs it not to remember where it came from.
coldharbor_deep_forge_below_the_waterlin.offer: A smith without iron is a man beating water. I have beaten a deal of water this season. The last iron in Coldharbor is down in the sunk wards -- bog-iron, harbor-iron, whatever you want to call metal that went under with the houses it was nailed into. The crawlers hoard it in their nests, saints know why. Bring me a wagon's worth. I will work it honest, and I will try very hard not to think about which door each nail came out of.
coldharbor_deep_forge_below_the_waterlin.progress: The Cave-Crawlers hoard drowned bog-iron in their nests below the tide. Gather it and clear the crawlers guarding it.
coldharbor_deep_forge_below_the_waterlin.complete: Good iron, once you scrape the harbor off it. Heavier than it should be -- iron that has been under a while comes up heavy, like it took on the weight of where it sat. I will make you something honest of it. This reaver first. Do not ask what the last owner did with the same iron. I did not ask either, and I sleep. Mostly.

objectives.text: Gather drowned bog-iron from the nests
Clear the crawlers hoarding it

coldharbor_deep_forge_below_the_waterlin.note: The bog-iron came up heavy, weighted with the weight of where it sat. Ozun works it honest and does not ask which drowned door each nail once held shut. Not asking is the last dry forge in Coldharbor's oldest craft.

coldharbor_deep_marsh_light_in_the_wards.title: Marsh-Light in the Drowned Wards
coldharbor_deep_marsh_light_in_the_wards.summary: Radu buys and sells what the tide gives up, and the tide has started giving up its own light. He wants marshlight jars bottled from the sunk wards -- and does not quite want to admit why the buyers upriver pay so well for cold light.
coldharbor_deep_marsh_light_in_the_wards.offer: I deal in what the harbor coughs back. Timber, brass fittings, the odd sealed jar of something the low town bottled before it drowned. Now the water itself glows down there -- a cold marsh-light, off no lamp, off no living thing. Upriver they pay a king's coin for jars of it. I do not ask what for. You will go down, you will bottle it clean, and you will not, at any point, look too long into a jar. That last part is not for the price. That part is for you.
coldharbor_deep_marsh_light_in_the_wards.progress: Bottle the cold marsh-light from the sunk wards. Do not look too long into the jars.
coldharbor_deep_marsh_light_in_the_wards.complete: Full jars, and you kept your eyes on your hands -- I can tell, because you are still talking sense. The buyers upriver will be pleased, and I will be paid, and I will spend it on lamp-oil like a sane man and burn honest fire in my own window. This one is yours. Keep it capped. Whatever wants to be read out of a light like that, let it stay unread in the glass.

objectives.text: Bottle cold marsh-light from the wards

coldharbor_deep_marsh_light_in_the_wards.note: The harbor gives up its own cold light now, and buyers upriver pay well to read it. Radu sells the jars and burns honest oil in his own window. What wants to be read out of a light like that, he keeps capped in glass -- and calls that caution, not cowardice.

coldharbor_deep_the_guards_own_dead.title: The Guard's Own Dead
coldharbor_deep_the_guards_own_dead.summary: Anton lost half his watch when the last ward went under. They rise now as Deep-Wights and keep patrolling the drowned street they died holding. He cannot ask his own men to lie down. He asks a stranger to.
coldharbor_deep_the_guards_own_dead.offer: I gave the order to fall back when the last ward flooded. Some did not hear it in time. Some did and stayed. They are down there yet -- my watch, my men, walking the sunk street in the same round I set them, holding a post the water took years ago. I have gone down twice to stand them down and twice I have come back up having done nothing but salute. A stranger can do what a captain cannot. Lay my men to rest. Bring their watch-tokens up so I can name them proper.
coldharbor_deep_the_guards_own_dead.progress: Anton's drowned watch rise as Deep-Wights on the sunk street. Lay them down and gather their watch-tokens.
coldharbor_deep_the_guards_own_dead.complete: Ten tokens. Ten names I can carve now, above the tide, where a name still means the man and not the debt. You did what I could not, and I will not insult you by calling it a kindness -- it was a hard thing and you did it hard. Take the ward. Folk-work, my mother's making. It turned the harbor from our door one winter longer than it had any right to.

objectives.text: Lay Anton's drowned watch to rest
Recover a watch-token from the wights

coldharbor_deep_the_guards_own_dead.note: Anton's watch held a post the tide took, walking the round he set them, dead and dutiful. He carved ten names above the water, where a name still means the man. Below, the harbor keeps a different roll, and a different word than rest.

coldharbor_deep_the_second_guard_who_won.title: The One Who Will Not Go Down
coldharbor_deep_the_second_guard_who_won.summary: Costel is the town's honest coward, and proud of it. He will guard any door above the waterline and none below it. He asks a stranger to fetch him a warding-charm from the deep so he can guard his own door with a clean conscience and drier boots.
coldharbor_deep_the_second_guard_who_won.offer: Anton will tell you I am a coward. Anton is right, and Anton is also down there every other night saluting corpses, so between the two of us I am the one who still sleeps. Here is my honest ask: there is warding-work sunk in the old chapel, chalk-ward, the kind that turns the drowned from a threshold. I want it for my door. I will guard the living end of every street in this town, gladly, all night -- but I will not go past the tide-line, and I have made my peace with the exact size of that no. Fetch it up. I will name my firstborn after your boots.
coldharbor_deep_the_second_guard_who_won.progress: A chalk handprint ward lies in the sunk chapel below the tide-line. Fetch it up for Costel -- he will not go himself.
coldharbor_deep_the_second_guard_who_won.complete: You are dripping and I am dry, and that, my friend, is the whole of my life philosophy in one wet moment. Bless you. This goes on my door tonight, and I will sleep the sleep of a man who knows exactly what he is not brave enough for. There is honor in a small no held firm. Take a second one -- I found a pair. One for your door. Everybody needs a threshold the harbor respects.

objectives.text: Recover the chalk wards from the sunk chapel

coldharbor_deep_the_second_guard_who_won.note: Costel guards every door above the tide and none below it, and calls that wisdom. He is not wrong. In a town the water is reading house by house, a small firm no may be the last honest ward left -- drier, at least, than the brave ones.

coldharbor_deep_what_the_second_commoner.title: What Marta Saw From the Roof
coldharbor_deep_what_the_second_commoner.summary: Marta lives in the last high garret and watches the tide from her roof, because from up there you can see the pattern the water makes going out. She has seen the drowned wards arrange themselves. She wants someone to walk the line she traced and tell her it is nothing.
coldharbor_deep_what_the_second_commoner.offer: I have the last high room in Coldharbor, and a window that faces the drowned wards, and too much time. When the tide draws out you can see the sunk roofs, and here is the thing I cannot un-see: they line up. Not how a town lines up -- how a page lines up. Rows. I chalked the line on my sill so I would know I did not dream it. Walk it. Reach the far end where the rows point, and come tell me it is just old streets under old water. Lie to me kindly if you have to.
coldharbor_deep_what_the_second_commoner.progress: Follow the line Marta traced from her sill: reach the far point where the drowned rows converge, past the sunk chapel.
coldharbor_deep_what_the_second_commoner.complete: You will not lie to me. I can see that on you. So it is rows, then, and they point somewhere, and something down there is keeping the streets of a drowned town straighter than the living ever kept them. I chalked my sill for proof and now I have proof and I do not want it. Take the map-border. I marked where the rows aim. I would rather it live in your pocket than on my sill.

objectives.label: where the drowned rows converge
objectives.text: Walk the line to where the rows point
Tell Marta what you found

coldharbor_deep_what_the_second_commoner.note: The drowned wards of Coldharbor lie in rows, straighter than the living ever kept them, aimed at a single point below the tide. Marta chalked her sill for proof and got it. She gave the proof away. The rows do not need her to keep them.

coldharbor_deep_salt_against_the_tide.title: Salt Against the Tide
coldharbor_deep_salt_against_the_tide.summary: Nica lays a salt line at the inn threshold every dusk, the way her grandmother did, to keep the drowned on their side of the water. The brine has gone thin and the crawlers cross it now. She needs proper salt-brine drawn from the deep springs to lay the line again.
coldharbor_deep_salt_against_the_tide.offer: My grandmother laid a salt line at this door every dusk of her life and told me the drowned respect three things: a name, a threshold, and salt. The salt has gone weak -- market brine, thin stuff, and the crawlers walk right over it into my taproom of a night. There are brine springs down in the sunk wards, old and strong, the real salt of the deep water. Draw me a tonic of it, proper brine, and I will lay a line at this door the drowned will read as no.
coldharbor_deep_salt_against_the_tide.progress: Draw proper salt-brine from the deep springs in the sunk wards, and clear the crawlers crossing the weak line.
coldharbor_deep_salt_against_the_tide.complete: Now that is salt. Bites the tongue, bites the air, bites whatever comes to the door meaning to come in. I laid the line while you watched -- see how it lies, even, unbroken, a sentence that ends in no. Grandmother would nod. She would also tell me a line only holds till the tide learns to read past it. Take this brine. Lay your own lines. Learn which ones the water still respects.

objectives.text: Draw salt-brine from the deep springs
Clear the crawlers crossing the line

coldharbor_deep_salt_against_the_tide.note: Nica laid the salt line even and unbroken, a sentence the drowned read as no. Her grandmother's word holds -- till the tide learns to read past it. In Coldharbor the salt is faith and the faith is thinning, one dusk at a time.

coldharbor_deep_the_incurious_charm.title: For Those Who Read Too Far
coldharbor_deep_the_incurious_charm.summary: Ana has watched three careful people go down to read the drowned inscriptions and come up wrong -- calm, tidy, incurious, gone. She is making charms to blunt the pull, and needs blackglass from the deep to grind the lenses. She will not go herself. She has read enough.
coldharbor_deep_the_incurious_charm.offer: I am a chronicler who has learned to stop reading, and it is the hardest craft I own. Three people went down to copy the drowned inscriptions this year. Careful people. They came up calm -- too calm, tidy, done. Incurious, is the word. As if a question had been lifted out of them clean. I am grinding charms against it -- something to keep the itch of a living mind alive in the presence of that pull. I need blackglass from the deep wards for the lenses. Bring it. And when you are down there, look at your boots.
coldharbor_deep_the_incurious_charm.progress: Gather blackglass from the drowned wards for Ana's charms. Read nothing. Look at your boots on the way out.
coldharbor_deep_the_incurious_charm.complete: Good glass, and you still have your questions -- I can hear them in how you breathe. That is what I am trying to keep alive in people. Not knowledge. Curiosity is the vector, they say; but the incurious dead are worse than the curious dead, because at least the curious ones wanted something once. Here. I ground one already. Wear it when you go deep. Let it itch. The itch is you.

objectives.text: Gather blackglass from the drowned wards
Bring Ana the blackglass

coldharbor_deep_the_incurious_charm.note: Three careful readers went down and came up incurious -- calm, tidy, a question lifted clean out of each. Ana grinds charms to keep the itch of a living mind alive. The incurious dead, she says, are worse. At least the curious ones wanted something once.

coldharbor_deep_the_dockmasters_ledger.title: The Dockmaster's Other Ledger
coldharbor_deep_the_dockmasters_ledger.summary: Dorica keeps the honest tally of the drowned. Something keeps another -- a Ledger of the Drowned Quarter, filed in the deepest cistern-vault -- that lists her townsfolk as accounts. She wants it recovered before it is completed, and does not want the clerk to know it exists.
coldharbor_deep_the_dockmasters_ledger.offer: You did well by my count, so I will trust you with what I trust no one at this inn. My tally names the dead: a mercy, a remembrance. There is another ledger down there. Deeper. In the flooded vault under the cistern. It names the living -- every soul in Coldharbor, filed like freight, with a column I will not describe because I do not have the stomach to say the word twice. It is being finished, line by line. Recover it. Bring it up before the last name goes in. And say nothing to Sanda -- a clerk who touches that book starts reading it, and reading it is the whole trap.
coldharbor_deep_the_dockmasters_ledger.progress: The Ledger of the Drowned Quarter lies in the flooded vault beneath the cistern. Recover it past the Deep-Wights that guard the entries.
coldharbor_deep_the_dockmasters_ledger.complete: You have it. Cold as the harbor and heavier than any book has cause to be, and every hand in it the same hand -- the one from the customs house, the one from the sunk chapel, the hand that has been writing this town down since before the water came. I will not read it. I will weight it with stone and sink it in the sweetwater cistern where nothing down there thinks to look for a thing hidden in the clean. Take my thanks, and the writ. A writ of un-filing is a small stubborn no. This town is going to need a great many small stubborn noes.

objectives.text: Clear the wights guarding the vault
Recover the drowned ledger
Bring Dorica the ledger unread

coldharbor_deep_the_dockmasters_ledger.note: The drowned quarter keeps a ledger of the living, filed like freight, in one patient hand. Dorica sank it unread in the sweetwater, a thing hidden in the clean. The book is not finished. Coldharbor will need a great many small stubborn noes -- and knows it now.

coldharbor_deep_the_reading_of_the_deep.title: The Reading of the Deep
coldharbor_deep_the_reading_of_the_deep.summary: Ana has the alchemist's sight and reads the drowned wards by color -- blue for the dead, violet for the deep places. In the lowest ward the color has begun to shift toward orange. She needs a witness to confirm the color she prays she is imagining.
coldharbor_deep_the_reading_of_the_deep.offer: You have proven you can go deep and come back yourself, which is rarer than you know. So I will ask the thing I cannot ask anyone born in this town. I have the reading-sight -- I see the grammar of the dead in color. Blue where the dead lie honest. Violet in the deep places, the sub-earth wards. In the lowest ward, at the drowned bell, the color has begun to move. Toward orange. Orange is a live signal writing itself, and orange is not supposed to be a thing the harbor can do. Go down to the drowned bell. Read it with this. Tell me the color. Tell me I am wrong.
coldharbor_deep_the_reading_of_the_deep.progress: Descend to the drowned bell in the lowest ward and read the color of the deep. Take the reading-draught.
coldharbor_deep_the_reading_of_the_deep.complete: You saw it shift. I watched your face and I have my answer and I did not want it. Blue to violet to that slow crawling orange, at the drowned bell, in the deepest ward of a town the sea already finished -- which means it is not finished. Something down there is writing itself awake, patient as tide, in a place no living hand can be blamed for waking it. I have written it down. That is all a chronicler can do. Take the draught back; you will need to read what comes. And walk out looking at your boots.

objectives.label: the drowned bell
objectives.text: Reach the drowned bell in the lowest ward
Tell Ana the color you read

coldharbor_deep_the_reading_of_the_deep.note: At the drowned bell in Coldharbor's lowest ward, the color of the deep shifts blue to violet to a slow crawling orange -- a live signal writing itself awake in a town the sea supposedly finished. It is not finished. Ana wrote it down. That is all a chronicler can do.

coldharbor_deep_the_bell_that_answers.title: The Bell That Answers
coldharbor_deep_the_bell_that_answers.summary: The drowned bell tolls on its own, under the water, in a rhythm that skips -- the same skip the whole town keeps in its sleep. Ana wants it silenced. The dead crowd it, keeping time. This is the closest anyone has come to touching the thing writing them down.
coldharbor_deep_the_bell_that_answers.offer: The bell tolls now. Under six fathoms, off no rope, in a rhythm that skips a beat and holds the skip -- and here is what has stopped my pen: the town keeps it. In their sleep. Costel hums it. The children clap it. A rhythm nobody taught them, off a bell nobody rings, and every day more of us keep better time. Go down to the drowned bell. The Deep-Wights crowd it, keeping the count. Break the count. Silence the bell. I do not know if it can be done. I know that if it cannot, we will all be keeping perfect time by winter, and perfect is the word that damns us.
coldharbor_deep_the_bell_that_answers.progress: Descend to the drowned bell and break the count: put down the Deep-Wights keeping its rhythm, and recover the reading-still from the bell-cage.
coldharbor_deep_the_bell_that_answers.complete: Silence. For now. You broke the count and the wights went still and the bell hangs quiet in the black water, and up here the humming stopped mid-note -- Costel looked at his own mouth like it had betrayed him. But I will tell you true, because you earned true: a skipped beat is not a broken rhythm. It is a rhythm waiting. The bell will find the count again. It has time, and time is the only thing it has always had. Take the still. Read what it catches. And forgive me -- I have made you a reader now, and reading is the wound.

objectives.text: Break the count at the drowned bell
Recover the reading-still from the bell-cage
Return the still to Ana

coldharbor_deep_the_bell_that_answers.note: The drowned bell tolled off no rope, in a rhythm the whole town kept in its sleep. The count is broken -- for now. A skipped beat is not a broken rhythm; it is a rhythm waiting, and the bell has only ever had time. Ana silenced it and made a reader of the one who helped. Reading is the wound.

coldharbor_deep_heart_of_the_deep_bed.title: The Heart of the Deep Bed
coldharbor_deep_heart_of_the_deep_bed.summary: Voica has stood the tide-watch long enough to feel where the harbor's cold comes from -- a single point in the deepest bed where the tide itself seems to listen. She asks for it to be found, and named. It cannot be ended. But it can be named.
coldharbor_deep_heart_of_the_deep_bed.offer: I have stood this watch longer than sense allows, and a watcher learns the water like a face. There is a cold in the deep bed that is not the cold of the sea. It has a center. I have felt the tide bend around it the way a room bends around the one person listening. My partner walks his post above it. The bell hangs over it. The rows point at it. Whatever is writing this town down, that is where the pen is. Go to the heart of the deep bed. Take the coldlight the deep grows around it -- a crown of it, if you can. I do not ask you to end the thing. I have watched too long to believe it ends. I ask you to name it, so that when it takes us we at least go named, and not merely filed.
coldharbor_deep_heart_of_the_deep_bed.progress: Descend to the heart of the deep bed, where the harbor's cold has a center. Face the Deep-Wights that keep it and bring up the coldlight crown that grows around the listening point.
coldharbor_deep_heart_of_the_deep_bed.complete: You brought it up. Cold enough to burn -- coldlight, the deep's own crown, grown around the place where the tide goes to listen. And you named it, in the reading, before you came up. That is what I asked and that is what matters. Not that we stopped it -- we did not, and I will not pretend. But we go named now. A named thing is a specific thing, and a specific thing is the one shape the perfect record was never built to hold. Wear the crown. It is cold, and it is ours, and it is the shape of our stubbornness. Let the deep read that.

objectives.label: the heart of the deep bed
objectives.text: Reach the heart of the deep bed
Face the wights that keep the listening point
Bring up the coldlight crown

coldharbor_deep_heart_of_the_deep_bed.note: At the heart of Coldharbor's deep bed the tide goes to listen, and something writes the town down from there. It was not ended -- Voica never believed it could be. It was named. A named thing is a specific thing, the one shape the perfect record cannot hold. The town goes named now, and not merely filed. It is the shape of a stubborn no, worn as a crown of cold light.

coldharbor_deep_the_merchants_last_manif.title: The Merchant's Last Manifest
coldharbor_deep_the_merchants_last_manif.summary: Radu's father shipped the last honest cargo out of Coldharbor the night the low town drowned, and the manifest went down with the customs house. Radu wants it recovered -- to clear his father's name, and because a debt-receipt from that night sells well upriver.
coldharbor_deep_the_merchants_last_manif.offer: My father was an honest man, which in this trade is a kind of miracle and a kind of curse. His last cargo went out the night the low town went under, and the manifest sank with the customs house -- proof he paid every toll, cleared every line. Folk up the coast whisper my family ran the harbor's last debt out unpaid. I will not have his name filed as a cheat. Go down to the sunk customs floor and bring me his manifest. A receipt from that night is worth coin to certain buyers besides. But the name matters more than the coin. This once.
coldharbor_deep_the_merchants_last_manif.progress: Recover the drowned manifest from the sunk customs floor, past the crawlers nesting in the wreck.
coldharbor_deep_the_merchants_last_manif.complete: There -- his mark, his hand, every toll paid and stamped, clean as the day the water took it. My father died owing this harbor nothing, and now I can prove it above the tide. The receipt I will sell; a man must eat. But this stamp I keep. Take the striker -- veinflint, off his own kit. He would have wanted it in a hand that goes down and comes back. Mine does neither anymore.

objectives.text: Clear the crawlers from the customs wreck
Recover the drowned manifest

coldharbor_deep_the_merchants_last_manif.note: Radu's father died owing the harbor nothing, and the drowned manifest proves it above the tide. Radu keeps the stamp and sells the receipt, because a man must eat. In a town being filed as debt, a paid account is a small defiance -- and worth coin to buyers who trade in who owes what.

coldharbor_deep_the_keepsake_cord.title: The Keepsake Cord
coldharbor_deep_the_keepsake_cord.summary: Ileana's mother went under with the low town and left nothing above the tide but a habit: she braided a keepsake cord for each child, and never finished the youngest's. Ileana wants the unfinished cord recovered, to finish it herself.
coldharbor_deep_the_keepsake_cord.offer: My mother braided a keepsake cord for each of us -- a knot for every year, a small warm nothing you wore at the wrist to say you were somebody's. She was braiding my youngest's the night the water came. Never finished it. It is down there still, in our house, on the peg by the hearth where she always worked. I cannot go -- I have living children who need me above the tide. But my youngest deserves her cord. Bring it up. I will finish the knots she started. It is the only inheritance the harbor left us, and I mean to have it.
coldharbor_deep_the_keepsake_cord.progress: Recover the unfinished keepsake cord from Ileana's drowned house, on the hearth-peg where her mother worked.
coldharbor_deep_the_keepsake_cord.complete: This is it. Her knots -- I would know them blind, the way she pulled them tight and then eased them, tight and eased, like she was arguing with the string. Seven years' worth, and I will braid the rest tonight and clasp it on my youngest and tell her whose hands began it. A small warm thing. That is what the deep cannot copy, my mother used to say -- it can file a life but it cannot braid one. Take a cord of mine. Wear it. Be somebody's.

objectives.text: Recover the unfinished keepsake cord
Bring Ileana her mother's cord

coldharbor_deep_the_keepsake_cord.note: Ileana's mother left one inheritance above the tide: an unfinished keepsake cord, a knot for every year. Ileana braided the rest by her mother's hand. The deep can file a life, her mother used to say, but it cannot braid one. A small warm thing is the one shape the perfect record cannot hold.

coldharbor_deep_bound_to_the_drowned.title: Bound to the Drowned
coldharbor_deep_bound_to_the_drowned.summary: The Deep-Wights have begun carrying bound keepsakes down from the living town -- lockets, rings, small treasured things that vanish from above and turn up clutched below. Voica wants them recovered, and wants to know how the dead are reaching up.
coldharbor_deep_bound_to_the_drowned.offer: Things are going missing above the tide. Small things -- a locket off a sleeping child's neck, a ring from a dead husband's box, the treasured nothings folk keep. They turn up below. The wights carry them. Clutched, cradled, kept. I have seen a Deep-Wight hold a locket the way a mother holds a locket, and I do not know how the dead are reaching up into the living town to take them, and the not-knowing is a cold in me the watch cannot warm. Go down. Take the bound keepsakes back off them. Every one you bring up is one thing the harbor does not get to keep.
coldharbor_deep_bound_to_the_drowned.progress: The Deep-Wights clutch bound keepsakes stolen from the living town. Recover them and put the wights down.
coldharbor_deep_bound_to_the_drowned.complete: You brought them up. A locket, a ring, a child's clay bird -- and I will walk each one back to its door myself, above the tide, and set it where it belongs, and tell no one how it wandered. Because here is the cold of it: the wights were not stealing. They were collecting. Keeping. Filing the small warm things away where nothing rots and nothing is lost -- and nothing is loved, either. Take the keepsake. It is bound, and it is back among the living. Keep it that way.

objectives.text: Put down the wights holding the keepsakes
Recover the stolen keepsakes

coldharbor_deep_bound_to_the_drowned.note: The Deep-Wights carry the town's small treasured things down and keep them where nothing rots -- and nothing is loved. Not stealing, Voica saw. Collecting. Filing the warm things away. She walked each one home above the tide. Every keepsake back among the living is one thing the harbor does not get to keep.

coldharbor_deep_the_dossier_below.title: The Dossier Below
coldharbor_deep_the_dossier_below.summary: Sanda has found the drowned ledgers do not merely list the dead -- they record the living in unnerving detail: their habits, their routes, the hour they pass each door. She wants a master dossier recovered before she is the one described all the way.
coldharbor_deep_the_dossier_below.offer: I told you the customs entries write themselves. I did not tell you the worst of it, because I was still deciding whether I had lost my mind. They do not only name us. They describe us. My route to the well. The hour I pass the chapel. The stair I favor, the hand I write with. A dossier of habits, filed against each living soul, and I have watched mine grow more exact by the week -- as if being known that well is the first half of being collected. There is a master dossier below, in the vault. Bring it up. I would see how well I am known before it knows me all the way.
coldharbor_deep_the_dossier_below.progress: Recover the dossier of habits from the drowned vault, past the wights that guard the files.
coldharbor_deep_the_dossier_below.complete: It is all here. Everything. The way I hold a pen. The song I hum without knowing -- the bell's song, Ana was right. Filed, dated, kept current in a hand that never sleeps. To be known this well is not to be seen; it is to be finished in advance. I will burn my page. It will only be re-copied, but I will burn it anyway, because a small stubborn no is the only pen I have left. Take the dossier. Learn from it how the deep reads a life. Then be a life it cannot finish reading.

objectives.text: Clear the wights guarding the files
Recover the master dossier
Bring Sanda the dossier

coldharbor_deep_the_dossier_below.note: The drowned vault keeps a dossier of the living -- routes, hours, the songs they hum unknowing. To be known that well, Sanda saw, is to be finished in advance. She burned her page knowing it would be re-copied, and burned it anyway. A small stubborn no is the only pen the living have left in Coldharbor.

coldharbor_deep_daily_the_tide_line_hold.title: Holding the Tide-Line
coldharbor_deep_daily_the_tide_line_hold.summary: Every night the crawlers and the drowned test the tide-line where the living town meets the water. Anton holds it with too few hands, and pays a standing coin to any who will thin the deep's nightly push.
coldharbor_deep_daily_the_tide_line_hold.offer: Same ask as last night, and it will be the same ask tomorrow, because the deep does not tire and my watch does. The crawlers come up the tide-line after dark, and the wights press behind them, testing the wards, counting the gaps. Thin them. I cannot hold Coldharbor's edge with the hands I have, and every night held is a night the living sleep dry. There is a coin in it, and my honest thanks, which by now you know is not nothing.
coldharbor_deep_daily_the_tide_line_hold.progress: Thin the deep's nightly push at the tide-line: the crawlers and the drowned that test the living edge.
coldharbor_deep_daily_the_tide_line_hold.complete: Held. Another night the water does not gain. It is not winning, holding -- I know that. The tide is patient and I am fifty-one. But my mother held this line before me and hers before her, and the water is still on its side of the salt this morning, and that is the whole of what a border hearth can promise. Same time tomorrow. The deep keeps its hours. So do we.

objectives.text: Thin the crawlers at the tide-line
Press the drowned back from the wards

coldharbor_deep_daily_the_tide_line_hold.note: The deep pushes the tide-line nightly and never tires. Anton holds it with too few hands, as his mother did, and hers. It is not winning. The water is still on its side of the salt this morning, and in Coldharbor that is the whole of what a border hearth can promise.

coldharbor_deep_daily_the_wardens_readin.title: The Warden's Reading
coldharbor_deep_daily_the_wardens_readin.summary: Ana takes a reading of the deep's color each day, to chart how fast the orange is spreading through the drowned wards. She needs fresh blackglass ground for the lenses, and a scout to walk the near wards and report the color.
coldharbor_deep_daily_the_wardens_readin.offer: Each day I read the wards, and each day I chart the orange a hand-span further than the day before. It is slow. It is not stopping. I need fresh glass -- the reading clouds a lens in a day, the way looking at it clouds a mind -- and I need a steadier hand than mine to walk the near wards and tell me the color, plain, before it reaches the stair we still use. Bring me blackglass, walk me the near wards, and read me the deep. It is a grim errand and I will not pretend otherwise. Charting a thing does not slow it. But an unwatched thing is a thing that gets to surprise you, and I am done being surprised.
coldharbor_deep_daily_the_wardens_readin.progress: Gather blackglass for the lenses and read the color of the near wards for Ana's chart.
coldharbor_deep_daily_the_wardens_readin.complete: A hand-span more of orange, same as yesterday, same as it will be tomorrow -- slow, sure, patient as the thing that writes it. Thank you for the glass and for the plain word. I will mark the chart and sleep the little a chronicler sleeps. We do not win by charting it. We win, if we win, by refusing to look away and refusing to be surprised. Come back tomorrow. It will have moved. So must we.

objectives.text: Gather blackglass for the lenses

objectives.label: the near drowned wards
objectives.text: Read the color of the near wards

coldharbor_deep_daily_the_wardens_readin.note: The orange spreads a hand-span a day through Coldharbor's drowned wards -- slow, sure, patient as the thing that writes it. Ana charts it and knows charting will not slow it. The living win, if they win, by refusing to look away, and refusing to be surprised.

coldharbor_deep_the_quiet_that_follows.title: The Quiet That Follows
coldharbor_deep_the_quiet_that_follows.summary: Those who come up from the deep too calm need a counteragent to bring the noise of a living mind back before the quiet sets for good. Nica brews it at the inn from lichen the crawlers foul, and needs a clean culture drawn from the deep springs.
coldharbor_deep_the_quiet_that_follows.offer: You have gone down more than any soul in this town and come up yourself each time, and I have started to think you might be the only one who can help me help the others. Some come up from the deep too quiet. Too settled. The counteragent of quiet, my grandmother's recipe -- it puts the racket of a living mind back into a person before the settled sets for good. I brew it here, but I need a clean lichen culture from the deep springs, and the crawlers foul every bed they touch. Draw me a clean culture. Clear the crawlers. There are three folk upstairs going quiet as I speak, and I mean to make them loud again.
coldharbor_deep_the_quiet_that_follows.progress: Draw a clean lichen culture from the deep springs and clear the crawlers fouling the beds.
coldharbor_deep_the_quiet_that_follows.complete: Clean culture -- I can brew a full batch off this. Two of the three upstairs took it and blinked and asked me a question, saints be thanked, a real question, the itch of a mind still lit. The third... the third I was too slow for. He is calm now, and tidy, and gone, and I will lay him out proper and not pretend the counteragent is a cure. It is a delay. But a delay is a day, and a day is a life, and I will brew delays until the harbor takes my hands. Take a dose. Keep the noise of yourself alive.

objectives.text: Draw a clean lichen culture
Clear the crawlers fouling the beds

coldharbor_deep_the_quiet_that_follows.note: The counteragent of quiet puts the racket of a living mind back into those the deep has settled too calm. It is not a cure; it is a delay. Nica brews delays and does not pretend otherwise. A delay is a day, and a day is a life, and she will brew them until the harbor takes her hands.

anchorfall_cracked_tablet.title: The Tablet That Came Back
anchorfall_cracked_tablet.summary: A debt-tablet washed up on the low pier at dawn, hairline-cracked and warm to the touch though the water is cold. The dockmaster wants it off his boards before the fog reads it. Fetch it, and do not read it.
anchorfall_cracked_tablet.offer: Something came in on the tide this morning that shouldn't have. A tablet -- an anchor-stone, the Archive kind -- lying on the low pier, cracked down the middle and warm as a loaf. Cold water, warm stone. I've hauled deposits out of this canal for thirty winters and I know when a thing wants to be picked up. Go and get it. Bring it to me sealed in your hands. And traveler -- keep your eyes on your boots the whole walk back.
anchorfall_cracked_tablet.progress: The cracked tablet lies on the low pier at the water's edge, west along the moorings. Walk out and take it -- face-down.
anchorfall_cracked_tablet.complete: You didn't read it. Good. Look at the crack -- it's stamped closed, and it isn't closing. Someone's whole life, out with the tide, and the ledger swears they're finished. The stone disagrees. I'll weight it and sink it deep. That's all we can do here: give the finished ones a heavier water to lie in.

objectives.label: the low pier
objectives.text: Reach the cracked tablet on the low pier
Take up the cracked tablet, face-down
