anchorfall_cracked_tablet.note: A closed account, still warm, washed back against the verdict. Radomir sank it deep. The dead do not argue with the math, they say. This one is still arguing.

anchorfall_the_unfiling.title: A Writ of Unfiling
anchorfall_the_unfiling.summary: The clerk keeps a drawer of accounts the Archive marked collected -- people who went quiet in a single night. She has drafted a writ to un-file one of them, and needs it carried to the Pit rim, where the anchors are cast down.
anchorfall_the_unfiling.offer: I file the finished, that's my office. I stamp the tablet, the line goes still, the district gets quieter by one. I've done it a thousand times without thinking, which is how they wanted it done. But there's one drawer I keep for the ones who went in a single night -- a whole household, collected between supper and morning, unlooted, undisturbed. I wrote a writ to un-file one. It won't undo anything. Carry it to the Pit rim anyway. Let there be a paper, somewhere, that says no.
anchorfall_the_unfiling.progress: Take the writ Sava sealed to the Pit rim, north where the anchors are cast down into the black water.
anchorfall_the_unfiling.complete: You laid it on the rim. It won't reopen a single account -- I know that, I've always known that. But the ground up there was warm under your boots, wasn't it. It's warm because this is arranged, all of it, filing itself whether I stamp or not. My writ changes nothing. I'll keep writing them. A no that changes nothing is still a no.

objectives.text: Take Sava's sealed writ
Carry the writ of unfiling

objectives.label: the Pit rim
objectives.text: Lay the writ at the Pit rim

anchorfall_the_unfiling.note: The ground at the rim lay warm, and the dust upon it ran in fine even lines, all pointing inward. The queue files itself. Sava's writ was a candle held against a tide. She will light another tomorrow.

anchorfall_orange_on_the_water.title: What the Fog Reads
anchorfall_orange_on_the_water.summary: The watcher keeps a vial of the old alchemist's reading-draught and swears the fog over the deep canal now answers it in shifting orange. Go to the water's turn, look through the glass, and come back before you understand what you see.
anchorfall_orange_on_the_water.offer: You've seen fog go blue over a graveyard, maybe. Violet where the deep stone runs close. Green when something's about to wake. Those you can stand and watch. But the water's turn, past the third bridge -- I put the glass to my eye there last night and the fog came up shifting, sliding, orange. That's the color of a thing writing itself. Go and look, just once, just long enough to be sure. Then walk away. Don't stand there working out what it's saying. The ones who work it out don't come back to say so.
anchorfall_orange_on_the_water.progress: Take the reading-glass to the water's turn past the third bridge, north canal. Look once. Then leave.
anchorfall_orange_on_the_water.complete: Orange, then. I hoped I was an old man's eyes gone bad. It's writing itself into the water now, not just the stones -- the whole canal a page, and the tide turning it. You looked and you left, and that's the only right answer there's ever been. There's no ward against this. There's only the discipline of not reading. Keep it. You'll need it downriver.

objectives.label: the water's turn
objectives.text: Look through the glass at the water's turn

anchorfall_orange_on_the_water.note: The fog read orange over the deep canal, the color of a signal writing itself into whatever will hold ink. You looked once and looked away. Tudor calls that the only ward there is. It is not a ward. It is a delay.

anchorfall_last_clean_barrel.title: The Last Clean Barrel
anchorfall_last_clean_barrel.summary: Every well in the district tastes of salt and worse, but Dorin's grandmother left him a trick: salt-brine drawn right, at the turn of the tide, keeps a barrel sweet. He's too gouty to walk the moorings. Gather good brine and the taproom stays warm one more week.
anchorfall_last_clean_barrel.offer: Don't drink the wells here, whatever a thirsty man tells you. But I'll tell you a secret my grandmother swore by, and she outlived three plagues to swear it: brine drawn clean at the turn of the tide, off the far moorings where the black water hasn't reached, will keep a barrel of ale sweet and a room full of tired folk sweeter. My knees won't carry me past the door. Fetch me good brine, four measures, and there'll be a bowl and a fire in it for you.
anchorfall_last_clean_barrel.progress: Draw clean salt-brine from the far moorings, at the tide's turn, four measures. The far end, where the black water hasn't reached.
anchorfall_last_clean_barrel.complete: Clean brine, and drawn right -- I can smell it. That's a week of warm nights bought, maybe two. Sit. There's stew, and it was walking this morning, which is more than most meat in this town can say. For one evening this room forgets what's out on the water. That's the whole of my trade, traveler: I sell people one hour of forgetting. Here's your bowl.

objectives.text: Draw clean brine at the far moorings

anchorfall_last_clean_barrel.note: Dorin sells one hour of forgetting, and the brine keeps the barrel sweet, and the room was warm and loud for an evening. Everything good here is temporary. That is what makes it good.

anchorfall_the_third_name.title: The Third Name on the List
anchorfall_the_third_name.summary: Matei keeps the district's honest register against the Archive's official one, and the two lists no longer agree. Three households the Archive calls collected, he calls simply gone. Ask the two who saw them last.
anchorfall_the_third_name.offer: I keep a register the Archive doesn't know about -- births, deaths, who owes whom a kindness. Small book, honest book. And lately it fights with the official ledger. The Archive stamps a name collected; my book says that man was drinking in Dorin's taproom the week after. Three names so far. The third one troubles me most. Ask Oprea and Pavel -- they lived nearest. Find me the difference between a man who died and a man who was finalized. I have a terrible feeling it's spelled differently.
anchorfall_the_third_name.progress: Ask Oprea and Pavel, who lived nearest the third household, what they saw the night it went quiet.
anchorfall_the_third_name.complete: So. No cart came. No fever, no thief, no water taken. They ate their supper, and by morning the house was still set for it, and nobody in it. The Archive's ink says collected, dated clean. My book will say: went quiet, cause unknown, the night the fog came up the canal. Two words for the same empty house. One of them has a receipt. That's the whole horror of this place, traveler -- it does its killing with a stamp.

objectives.text: Ask Oprea what she saw
Ask Pavel about the quiet house

anchorfall_the_third_name.note: Three households the ledger calls collected, and Matei's honest book calls simply gone. The difference between the words is a receipt. He wrote both down. Someone should keep the count. Someone always does.

anchorfall_deposits.title: Deposits
anchorfall_deposits.summary: The canal is the district's drain, and lately it carries more than salvage. Radomir needs the weighted keepsakes hauled up before they foul the low sluice -- the small bound things the finalized let fall as they went under.
anchorfall_deposits.offer: In an honest port a canal is a road for boats. Here it's a road for nothing that floats. Bodies are deposits, we say, quiet, so the young ones don't ask. But it isn't the bodies I need hauled -- it's what they carried under. Little bound things, cords, keepsakes, weighting them down. They're catching in the low sluice and I can't run the gate. Pull them up. Handle them gentle. Every one was somebody's whole reason to keep a name.
anchorfall_deposits.progress: Haul the weighted keepsakes from the low sluice, and reach the sluice-gate to clear it. Six should free the gate.
anchorfall_deposits.complete: Six. That'll run the gate again. Look at them, spread on the boards -- a cord, a carved thing, a bit of ribbon gone black. People go under this canal owing the Archive their whole existence, and the last thing their hands hold isn't a coin. It's this. The small warm thing. I keep a box of them. Nobody comes to claim it. I keep it anyway. Go on -- there's a fire at Dorin's, and you've earned the sitting.

objectives.text: Haul weighted keepsakes from the low sluice

objectives.label: the sluice-gate
objectives.text: Clear the low sluice-gate

anchorfall_deposits.note: The finalized go under owing everything, and the last thing their hands hold is the small warm thing, not the debt. Radomir keeps a box of them nobody comes to claim. He keeps it anyway. That is the whole of mercy in Anchorfall.

anchorfall_ash_on_the_moorings.title: Ash on the Moorings
anchorfall_ash_on_the_moorings.summary: Grey shapes have started drifting off the burned eastern moorings after dark -- ash-wraiths, the old ones call them, the smoke of finalized fires that never quite let go. They gather where the water reads orange. Break them before they thicken.
anchorfall_ash_on_the_moorings.offer: You know how a fire dies and the smoke hangs over the ash like it can't believe it's over? That's what walks the eastern moorings now. Ash-wraiths. Grey, cold, quiet -- the last breath of pyres we lit for the collected, standing up and drifting where the water goes bright. My spearmen won't work that stretch after dark, and I don't blame them. You've the look of someone the fog hasn't got a page for yet. Go break them apart before more of them thicken up out of the smoke.
anchorfall_ash_on_the_moorings.progress: The ash-wraiths gather along the burned eastern moorings after dark. Scatter them -- seven should thin the drift.
anchorfall_ash_on_the_moorings.complete: Seven, and the moorings breathe easier. You felt it, though -- they don't fight like a thing that wants to live. They drift. They gather where the water's brightest, like moths at a lantern, if the lantern were the end of the world. We burn the collected to be sure they stay finished. Turns out the smoke has opinions. Take the striker. It throws a spark even soaked, and a spark's the one thing they flinch from.

objectives.text: Scatter the ash-wraiths on the eastern moorings

anchorfall_ash_on_the_moorings.note: The pyres of the finalized send up a smoke that will not lie down, and it gathers where the water reads orange, like moths at the world's last lantern. Horia's spearmen will not work that stretch. Ash remembers being fire.

anchorfall_ledger_that_wont_close.title: The Ledger That Won't Close
anchorfall_ledger_that_wont_close.summary: One account in Sava's office refuses to stay stamped. Every night the violet 'collected' mark is smeared and the line stands open again, as if a dead hand kept refusing the verdict. She wants the ledger fetched and read -- carefully -- to learn whose account will not close.
anchorfall_ledger_that_wont_close.offer: There's a book in the records annex I've stopped being able to work. Every account in it stamped collected, violet ink, clean columns. Except one line, near the bottom. I stamp it. I lock the drawer. I come back at dawn and the stamp is smeared and the line stands open, and there's grave-dirt on the page that I did not put there. Fetch me the ledger. Read the open line -- only that line, mind, nothing above it, nothing carved in the margins. I need to know whose hand keeps saying no from under the ground.
anchorfall_ledger_that_wont_close.progress: Retrieve the ledger from the records annex and read only the open line. Nothing above it. Nothing in the margins.
anchorfall_ledger_that_wont_close.complete: Stamped, un-stamped, stamped again -- and the ink smeared each time toward the same corner, the way a hand moves when it's pushing something away. Whoever this was, the Archive keeps closing them and they keep declining. I've filed the finished for twenty years and told myself it was tidy. A tidy thing doesn't argue back. Take the writ-craft. And burn the ledger's margins unread -- I saw you not looking at them, and I thank you for it.

objectives.text: Take Sava's key to the records annex
Read only the open line in the ledger

anchorfall_ledger_that_wont_close.note: Stamped, un-stamped, stamped again, the ink smeared each time by a hand refusing the verdict from under the ground. The Archive closes the account nightly. Nightly, it declines. A tidy system does not argue back with itself.

anchorfall_saltline.title: A Line of Salt
anchorfall_saltline.summary: Oprea lays a line of salt across her threshold every night, the way her mother did against the fog. Her pouch is empty and the tide is coming high. Fetch her salt from the drying-flats -- and mind the flat where the dust lies in rows.
anchorfall_saltline.offer: My mother laid salt across the door every night of her life and I called her a superstitious old woman for it, right up until the night the fog came under our neighbor's door and their house went quiet by morning. Salt and fire, she said. The old preservations. The signal hates a thing that's already kept. My pouch is empty and the tide's running high tonight. Fetch me salt off the drying-flats -- but stay off the flat where the dust lies in rows. That one's listening.
anchorfall_saltline.progress: Gather salt from the drying-flats for Oprea's threshold. Stay clear of the flat where the dust lies in aligned rows.
anchorfall_saltline.complete: Bless you. That's a night's line, and maybe two. I don't know if it works -- how would I ever know, until the night it doesn't? But my mother laid the line, and her mother, and this district's a graveyard of houses whose folk stopped laying it. So I lay my salt and I mind the flat where the dust runs in rows, and I sleep the way you sleep when you've done the one small thing you can. That's not nothing. Down here, it's most of everything.

objectives.text: Gather threshold salt from the drying-flats

objectives.label: the drying-flats
objectives.text: Reach Oprea's door with the salt

anchorfall_saltline.note: Salt across the threshold, the way her mother did, against a fog that hates what is already kept. Oprea does not know if it works. The houses of those who stopped laying it stand open and quiet. She lays her line.

anchorfall_keepsake_cord.title: The Keepsake Cord
anchorfall_keepsake_cord.summary: Pavel's daughter went quiet in the night with the rest of her household, but he never found her keepsake cord among the deposits. He believes if he holds the small warm thing she carried, the Archive cannot say she is finished. Help him search the sluice.
anchorfall_keepsake_cord.offer: They tell me my girl is collected. Stamped, dated, filed. A clerk read it to me kindly, which was the cruelest part. But I've been down the sluice every dawn since, because I never found her cord -- the plaited thing she wore, that her mother made, that meant nothing to anyone but us. And I've got a notion, a mad old man's notion: the tablet closes a name, but it can't close a thing. If I hold her cord, they haven't finished her. Not all of her. Help me find it. I can't read the water anymore, my eyes are gone with weeping.
anchorfall_keepsake_cord.progress: Search the sluice for Pavel's daughter's plaited keepsake cord, among the deposits.
anchorfall_keepsake_cord.complete: That's -- yes. That's it. The knot her mother tied, that I could never learn. It's warm. It shouldn't be warm, should it, but it is. They can stamp her name a thousand times. Here's the thing they can't stamp, in my hand, and it's still warm. Thank you. You've given an old man the one argument left to him against the whole cold machine: a warm cord, and my refusal. It isn't much. It's what I have.

objectives.text: Find the daughter's keepsake cord in the sluice

anchorfall_keepsake_cord.note: The tablet closes a name; it cannot close a thing. Pavel holds his daughter's warm cord against the whole cold machine, and calls it his one remaining argument. It is not much. It is what he has. It is the thesis of this drowned world in one plaited knot.

anchorfall_forge_gone_cold.title: A Forge Gone Cold
anchorfall_forge_gone_cold.summary: Florea can't keep a fire lit -- the ash-wraiths drift in off the water and smother her coals, and the bog-iron she needs lies in the flooded eastern forge she can no longer reach. Clear the wraiths, gather the iron, and the district gets its one working anvil back.
anchorfall_forge_gone_cold.offer: A port needs one honest forge, and I mean to be it. But I can't hold a fire. The grey ones drift in off the moorings and settle on my coals like they're cold and want warming, and my flame just -- gives up. Sympathetic, my apprentice says, the fire recognizing kin. I say the fire's afraid. And the good bog-iron's all in the old eastern forge, flooded to the knee and thick with the things. Clear them out. Bring me iron. Let there be one place in this town where something's still being made and not just filed.
anchorfall_forge_gone_cold.progress: Clear the ash-wraiths from the flooded eastern forge and gather bog-iron. Four wraiths down, and iron enough for a season.
anchorfall_forge_gone_cold.complete: Iron, and the forge quiet. My fire's caught -- look at it, honest orange, the good kind, the kind that means work and not the water. In a town that files people, hammering a nail is nearly a prayer. I'll make you something worth the swinging. This edge -- I quenched it in brine and salt, the old way, the way that outlasts. It won't close an account. It'll open a wraith right up.

objectives.text: Clear the ash-wraiths from the eastern forge
Gather bog-iron from the flooded forge

anchorfall_forge_gone_cold.note: The ash-wraiths settle on live coals like cold kin wanting warmth, and Florea's fire gives up. She cleared them and lit it anyway. In a town that files people, hammering a nail is nearly a prayer. She means to be the one honest forge.

anchorfall_toll_forgery.title: The Forged Receipt
anchorfall_toll_forgery.summary: The Archive tolls every crossing, and a family that can't pay is a family whose debt matures toward collection. Gavril forges receipts that keep poor households off the ledger. He needs the blank stock recovered from a customs house before the auditor arrives.
anchorfall_toll_forgery.offer: I'll be plain, since you don't wear an Archive coat: I forge toll-receipts. A crossing here costs coin, and a family that can't pay gets their debt noted, and a noted debt matures, and a matured debt gets collected some quiet night. So I stamp them paper that says they paid. It's a crime. It's also the only mercy I know how to make in bulk. There's a bundle of blank receipt-stock in the old customs house, and an auditor due to count it. Fetch it before he does. Save me a printing, and I'll have saved a dozen households a very bad morning.
anchorfall_toll_forgery.progress: Recover the blank receipt-stock from the old customs house before the auditor's count.
anchorfall_toll_forgery.complete: Beautiful. Enough stock for a season of small lies. You look at me like you're deciding whether I'm a good man or a criminal, and the honest answer is I'm a criminal, and down here that's the same thing as a good man, and I've made my peace with the arithmetic. Every receipt I forge is a name that doesn't ripen on the ledger. The Archive collects the finished. I just keep people usefully unfinished. Here -- for the risk. Tell no one I said a word.

objectives.text: Recover the blank receipt-stock from the customs house

anchorfall_toll_forgery.note: Gavril forges toll-receipts to keep poor households off the ledger, because a noted debt matures and a matured debt is collected. A crime, and the only mercy he can make in bulk. He keeps people usefully unfinished. He has made his peace with the arithmetic.

anchorfall_chalk_handprint.title: The Chalk Handprint
anchorfall_chalk_handprint.summary: On a canal wall, a child's chalk handprint -- the same emblem folk pressed a thousand years gone, in a kinder land. Fresh survey-marks are drawn carefully around it, protecting or cataloguing it, no one is sure which. Tudor wants it warded before the fog decides.
anchorfall_chalk_handprint.offer: Come look at this before the tide takes it. A child's handprint, chalked on the canal wall down by the third bridge. Small. And around it, fresh survey-marks -- the careful kind, the kind that catalogues. Now here's what turns my stomach: that emblem's old. Older than this whole rotten port. They say children pressed it on a warm wall in a warm country a thousand years back, for luck, for love, for nothing but the joy of a hand in chalk. And now something's drawn a fence around it and I cannot tell if it means to keep it safe or keep it filed. Ward it. Choose for us.
anchorfall_chalk_handprint.progress: Reach the child's chalk handprint on the canal wall by the third bridge, and lay a ward upon it.
anchorfall_chalk_handprint.complete: You warded it. Good. Maybe it holds, maybe the fog reads it anyway. But look -- one small warm mark, a child's hand, unbroken through a thousand years of everything going cold. The last honest echo of a country that loved its children out loud. Whoever drew the survey-fence around it didn't smudge it. Couldn't, maybe. Even the machine that files us hesitated at a handprint. That hesitation is the only hope I've got left. Keep the ward's twin. Press it somewhere warm.

objectives.label: the canal wall
objectives.text: Reach the chalk handprint on the canal wall
Lay a ward on the handprint

anchorfall_chalk_handprint.note: A child's chalk handprint, unbroken through a thousand years, the last honest echo of a country that loved its children out loud. Something drew a careful fence around it and did not smudge it. Even the machine that files the living hesitated at a handprint. Tudor calls that hesitation his only hope.

anchorfall_deepbed_lantern.title: The Deepbed Lantern
anchorfall_deepbed_lantern.summary: A lantern that burns underwater once hung at the deep pier to warn boats off the sinking-ground. It went dark the night the deep district was finalized. Radomir wants it relit -- and its old wick recovered from the drowned mooring where the ash-wraiths thicken.
anchorfall_deepbed_lantern.offer: There was a lantern once, out at the deep pier -- a proper deepbed lantern, the kind that burns underwater on marsh-oil, to warn the barges off the sinking-ground. It went dark the same night the deep district went quiet, all at once, collected. Nobody relit it. Nobody wanted to stand where its light would fall. But barges still run that reach blind, and I've buried two crews to the sinking-ground since. Fetch the old wick off the drowned mooring and I'll light her again. The grey ones are thick out there. Bring a spark and a strong stomach.
anchorfall_deepbed_lantern.progress: Recover the deepbed lantern's old wick from the drowned mooring, out past the deep pier where the ash-wraiths thicken.
anchorfall_deepbed_lantern.complete: There. She catches, even wet -- marsh-oil doesn't care about water, never did. First light on that reach since the district went. It won't bring back a soul. It'll keep the next barge crew off the sinking-ground, and that's a smaller mercy but a real one, and real ones are all I traffic in. Funny thing -- the grey ones drew back from the flame. Even the ash of the finished remembers being afraid of the dark. Take her twin. Light it where you're going. It's darker there.

objectives.label: the drowned mooring
objectives.text: Reach the drowned mooring past the deep pier
Recover the deepbed lantern's old wick

anchorfall_deepbed_lantern.note: The deepbed lantern went dark the night the deep district was finalized, and no one wanted to stand in its light. Radomir relit it, not to bring a soul back but to keep the next crew off the sinking-ground. A smaller mercy. A real one. Real ones are all he traffics in.

anchorfall_the_quiet_district.title: The Quiet District
anchorfall_the_quiet_district.summary: A whole quarter of Anchorfall went silent in one night -- finalized, its doors still set for supper, its people simply closed. Lazar must post the boundary but can't walk the streets, where the finalized drift as ash. Clear a path and see for him what a collected district looks like.
anchorfall_the_quiet_district.offer: There's a quarter past the north bridge I have to fence off, by order, and I can't make my legs carry me across it. It went in one night -- the whole district, collected, closed. Not burned. Not sacked. The doors are shut, the tables are laid, the washing's still on the lines. Just nobody. And where the people were, the grey ones drift now, thick as fog with faces. I need the boundary posted and I need to know what's in there. Walk it. Clear a path. Come back and tell me what a filed district looks like from the inside, so I can lie to the young ones about it convincingly.
anchorfall_the_quiet_district.progress: Walk the finalized quarter past the north bridge, clear the drifting ash-wraiths, and reach its central square.
anchorfall_the_quiet_district.complete: Say it plain, I can take it plain. ...Tables laid. Washing on the lines. Not one soul, and the dust in every room lying in the same direction, like the whole quarter turned to face something as it went. That's what collected looks like. No blood, no ash but the grey ones, no scream anybody heard. A district balanced, closed, and filed, one quiet night, by paperwork. I'll post the fence. I won't sleep. Take the ward-plate. It's plate you can hold, which is more than most wards in this town.

objectives.text: Clear the drifting ash-wraiths from the quarter

objectives.label: the finalized square
objectives.text: Reach the central square of the quiet district

anchorfall_the_quiet_district.note: A whole quarter finalized in one night: doors shut, tables laid, washing on the lines, and in every room the dust lying one direction, as if the district turned to face something as it went. No blood. No scream. Just paperwork maturing into an empty street. Lazar posted the fence. He did not sleep.

anchorfall_a_warm_bowl.title: A Warm Bowl for the Watch
anchorfall_a_warm_bowl.summary: Dorin means to send hot stew out to the guards freezing on the night-boundary -- a small warm thing against a cold job. He needs a hand carrying it, and a word had with prickly old Horia, who insists he doesn't want the fuss.
anchorfall_a_warm_bowl.offer: The guards on the night-boundary are freezing out there watching a quarter full of nothing, and a man watches nothing better with something hot in him. I've a pot of good stew and a notion to send it out, but my knees, again, always my knees. Carry it for me? And talk sense into Horia first -- he'll bluster that he doesn't want the fuss, that a guard doesn't eat on watch. He's wrong. A guard who eats on watch is a guard who's still a person. Tell him I said so. Tell him it's an order from the only kitchen that matters.
anchorfall_a_warm_bowl.progress: Talk prickly Horia into accepting the stew, then carry Dorin's pot out to the night-boundary.
anchorfall_a_warm_bowl.complete: Ate it, did he. Every drop, and grumbled the whole time, which is how you know he was glad of it. Good. That's my whole rebellion against this town, traveler: a warm bowl carried to a cold post, on purpose, for no profit. The Archive files. I feed. Let them count which one keeps a man himself longer. Sit -- there's a bowl left, and it's got your name on it, the warm kind of name, the kind nobody stamps.

objectives.text: Talk Horia into accepting the stew

objectives.label: the night-boundary
objectives.text: Carry the stew to the night-boundary

anchorfall_a_warm_bowl.note: A warm bowl carried to a cold post, for no profit, on purpose: Dorin's whole rebellion against a town that files people. The Archive files. He feeds. He means to keep men themselves a little longer than the ledger keeps its accounts. Horia ate every drop and grumbled, which is how you know he was glad.

anchorfall_dossier.title: A Dossier of Habits
anchorfall_dossier.summary: The Morven still listen in Anchorfall, and their old surveillance-marks catalogue who is safe to save. Gavril wants a dropped Morven dossier recovered before a rival broker sells it to the Archive -- a book of habits that decides who ripens on the ledger.
anchorfall_dossier.offer: The Morven watch this port the way they've watched everything for a thousand years -- quietly, in chalk, in little marks around doors. Their old trade was listening to save people. It's rotted into listening to price them. There's a dossier gone astray, a book of habits -- who drinks where, who lays salt, who's already half-finished on the inside. In the right hands it saves a dozen. In the wrong hands it's a shopping list for the collectors. A rival of mine means to sell it up the chain by nightfall. Get it first. I'll see it burns, or serves the burning kind of mercy.
anchorfall_dossier.progress: Recover the dropped Morven dossier before Gavril's rival sells it to the Archive.
anchorfall_dossier.complete: Got it, and got it clean. Listen to this -- 'subject lays salt nightly, keeps a warm keepsake, sings under her breath; assess as un-finalizable, low collection value.' They meant it as a mark against her. It reads like a blessing. That's the whole secret of surviving here: be too warm, too specific, too stubbornly yourself to file cleanly. The Archive collects the finished. Stay unfinished and you stay. I'll use this to keep folk off the list. Same book, opposite hands.

objectives.text: Recover the Morven dossier before the sale

anchorfall_dossier.note: The Morven's old craft was listening to save people; it rotted into listening to price them. Gavril took their shopping-list and read it as a blessing: be too warm, too specific, too stubbornly yourself to file cleanly. Same book, opposite hands. The Archive collects the finished. Stay unfinished and you stay.

anchorfall_shroud_of_named.title: The Shroud of the Named
anchorfall_shroud_of_named.summary: Matei sews shrouds embroidered with the true names of the finalized -- names the Archive erased into account-numbers. He believes a named dead cannot be fully closed. He needs the drowned name-tokens recovered from the canal, and a word with the clerk who knows the old names.
anchorfall_shroud_of_named.offer: The Archive turns a person into a number when it collects them. The name goes in the fire, the number goes on the tablet, and the tablet closes clean because a number closes easier than a name. So I sew shrouds -- and on each one I embroider the true name, the whole warm awkward human name, of one of the finished. A named dead is a harder dead to file. It's stubbornness in thread. But I've run out of names I know. The old ones are on tokens sunk in the canal, and only Sava still remembers how they read. Bring me the tokens. Ask her the names. Let me sew the finished back into being someone.
anchorfall_shroud_of_named.progress: Recover drowned name-tokens from the canal, and ask Sava the clerk to read the old names.
anchorfall_shroud_of_named.complete: Six names. Six people the ledger reduced to a sum, given their whole clumsy human names back in blue thread. It won't reopen a tablet -- I'm not a fool, I know what I can't do. But when I lay these shrouds, there'll be a name on the finished again, and a name is a claim, and a claim is an argument, and the machine hates an argument even when it wins. This is my whole craft now: I sew the dead back into being someone. Take a shroud. It's yours if it comes to that. I hope it doesn't. I sewed your name in warm.

objectives.text: Recover drowned name-tokens from the canal
Ask Sava to read the old names

anchorfall_shroud_of_named.note: The Archive turns a person into a number, because a number closes easier than a name. Matei sews the true names back onto the finished in blue thread -- stubbornness in cloth. A named dead is a harder dead to file. It reopens nothing. It is an argument, and the machine hates an argument even when it wins.

anchorfall_marshlight.title: Marshlight in a Jar
anchorfall_marshlight.summary: The gaslight of Anchorfall doesn't reach the ground, and the dark between the piers is where the fog does its quiet work. Oprea's neighbors want jars of caught marshlight to hang in their doorways -- a cold small glow against a colder thing. Gather it from the fen-edge after dark.
anchorfall_marshlight.offer: You'll have noticed the lamps here hang high and their light dies before it touches the cobbles. That dark at the bottom, between the piers -- that's where the fog works. So we catch marshlight, the cold blue kind that comes off the fen after dark, and we seal it in jars and hang it low, in the doorways, at the knees, where the real dark lives. It's not much of a light. But the fog seems to dislike a lit floor. Gather me marshlight from the fen-edge -- four jars' worth. Half the lane's asking for one now, and I'm the only fool who'll go.
anchorfall_marshlight.progress: Catch cold marshlight from the fen-edge after dark, four jars' worth, and bring it to Oprea's lane.
anchorfall_marshlight.complete: Four jars, and glowing steady -- good, that's the cold kind, the kind the fen gives and nothing living needs. We'll hang them low tonight, all down the lane, a little blue at every threshold. I don't say it stops anything. I say the fog goes slower down a lit lane, and slower is more nights, and more nights is more mornings, and a morning is the whole of what we're fighting for down here. Take a jar. Hang it at your feet where you sleep. Mind the floor. The floor's where they come.

objectives.text: Catch cold marshlight from the fen-edge

objectives.label: Oprea's lane
objectives.text: Bring the jars to Oprea's lane

anchorfall_marshlight.note: The gaslight of Anchorfall dies before it reaches the ground, and the dark at the bottom is where the fog does its quiet work. So they hang cold marshlight low, at the knees, at the thresholds. It stops nothing. It slows the fog down a lit lane, and slower is more mornings, and a morning is the whole of what they fight for.

anchorfall_watch_the_water.title: Watch the Water
anchorfall_watch_the_water.summary: Tudor keeps the night-watch alone, and every night the ash-wraiths gather thicker where the canal reads brightest. He asks a steady hand to thin them before dawn, and to note nothing of what the water is trying to spell.
anchorfall_watch_the_water.offer: Same ask as most nights, if you've the stomach: the grey ones are gathering on the water again, thick where it reads brightest. They don't do much -- they drift, they thicken, they wait. But a thing that waits is a thing that's counting on you not being there. Break up tonight's drift for me. Don't linger reading the water while you do it. Kill them and come back. The reading's my burden. Yours is the breaking.
anchorfall_watch_the_water.progress: Break up tonight's gathering of ash-wraiths on the bright water before dawn.
anchorfall_watch_the_water.complete: Broken up, and dawn coming grey but coming. That's another night the water didn't finish whatever it's spelling. You didn't read it -- I watched you not read it, and I'm grateful, and I'll be grateful again tomorrow when they've gathered back. That's the shape of the watch here. You don't win it. You hold it, one night, and then you hold it again. Come back at dusk. There's always a dusk.

objectives.text: Break up tonight's ash-wraith drift on the water

anchorfall_watch_the_water.note: You don't win the night-watch. You hold it, one night, and then you hold it again. Another night the water didn't finish whatever it was spelling. Tudor will be grateful again tomorrow, when the grey ones have gathered back. There is always a dusk.

anchorfall_salvage_run.title: The Salvage Run
anchorfall_salvage_run.summary: The tide leaves a little of everything on the low boards each dawn -- rope, iron, and now and then a debt-tablet warm enough to sink deep. Radomir pays for a morning's salvage, honest work in a dishonest port, and asks that the warm ones be brought to him, not opened.
anchorfall_salvage_run.offer: Tide's been generous, which in this port means it left more than it took. Rope, iron, blackglass off the deep wrecks -- good salvage, and I pay fair for it, coin you don't have to forge. Bring me a morning's haul. And if you turn up a tablet gone warm among the driftwood -- and you might, they come in on every third tide now -- bring it to me sealed. Don't thumb it open to read the name. The warm ones are why I still keep a weighted line by the door.
anchorfall_salvage_run.progress: Work the low boards for a morning's salvage, and bring Radomir any warm tablet sealed.
anchorfall_salvage_run.complete: Fair haul. There's your coin, and no forging needed to spend it. One warm tablet in the lot -- I'll weight it and sink it with the others, deep where the tide can't argue it back before nightfall. Honest work in a port that's forgotten the word. Come back tomorrow; the tide keeps its own hours and it always leaves something. Some of it's even just rope.

objectives.text: Work the low boards for a morning's salvage

anchorfall_salvage_run.note: Honest work in a port that has forgotten the word: a morning's salvage off the low boards, rope and iron and blackglass, and now and then a warm tablet to weight and sink deep before the tide argues it back. The tide keeps its own hours. It always leaves something. Some of it is even just rope.

anchorfall_pier_repair.title: Nails Against the Tide
anchorfall_pier_repair.summary: The piers of Anchorfall rot faster than any crew can mend them, but Florea mends them anyway, plank by plank, because a town that stops repairing itself has agreed to be finished. She needs bog-iron for the day's nails. Bring her a load and swing a hammer that builds.
anchorfall_pier_repair.offer: The tide eats these piers faster than I can nail them and I nail them anyway, every morning, and I'll tell you why: the day this town stops mending itself is the day it's agreed to be filed. Rot's just finalization with a slower stamp. So I fight it with nails. Bring me bog-iron for the day's batch -- there's a rich seam in the low flats, and it's honest black iron, the kind that wants to be a nail. Swing a hammer with me that builds something for once, instead of the hammers this town's known for.
anchorfall_pier_repair.progress: Gather bog-iron from the low flats for the day's pier-nails.
anchorfall_pier_repair.complete: Good iron, and a good morning's nails out of it. There -- that plank'll hold another season, or another month, or another week, and every one of those is a spit in the tide's eye. You think it's small work. It's the biggest work there is down here: proof that somebody still means to stay. A town that keeps its piers is a town that hasn't given the Archive its answer yet. Come swing with me tomorrow. There's always another plank.

objectives.text: Gather bog-iron from the low flats

anchorfall_pier_repair.note: Rot is finalization with a slower stamp, so Florea fights it with nails, plank by plank, every morning. A town that keeps its piers is a town that has not given the Archive its answer yet. Small work, she says, and the biggest there is: proof that somebody still means to stay.

anchorfall_counting_the_silent.title: Counting the Silent
anchorfall_counting_the_silent.summary: Each dawn Horia counts the doors gone quiet in the night and walks the boundary of the finalized quarter to keep it from creeping. He needs the ash-wraiths pushed back from the fence-line and the count carried out. A grim daily arithmetic, done because someone must.
anchorfall_counting_the_silent.offer: Every dawn I count the doors that went quiet overnight, and every dawn the number's not zero, and every dawn I walk the boundary of the finished quarter to make sure it hasn't crept a house closer. Grim arithmetic. But an uncounted loss is a loss that gets forgotten, and a forgotten one gets no name and no shroud and no salt on the threshold. So we count. Push the grey ones back off the fence-line for me, then walk to the marker and note where the quiet ends today. Do it with me. It's lighter split two ways, the counting.
anchorfall_counting_the_silent.progress: Push the ash-wraiths back from the fence-line, then reach the boundary marker and note the count.
anchorfall_counting_the_silent.complete: Same as yesterday, thank whatever's left to thank -- the quiet didn't creep. Doors gone: three. I've their names for Matei's needle and salt for their thresholds too late and a mark on the fence that says the finished got no further today. That's a win, in Anchorfall. A day the map of the dead didn't grow. Take your share of the count off my shoulders, and come count again tomorrow. There's always a tomorrow to count. That's the trouble and the mercy both.

objectives.text: Push the ash-wraiths back from the fence-line

objectives.label: the boundary marker
objectives.text: Reach the marker and note the count

anchorfall_counting_the_silent.note: Grim dawn arithmetic: count the doors gone quiet, walk the boundary, make sure the finished quarter has not crept a house closer. A day the map of the dead does not grow is a win in Anchorfall. There is always a tomorrow to count. Horia calls that the trouble and the mercy both.

anchorfall_kept_name.title: A Name Worth Keeping
anchorfall_kept_name.summary: Sava has spent twenty years filing the finished; now she means to save one. A name she stamped is not yet fully closed, and if its token is recovered and kept warm by a living hand before dawn, the account may stall. She sends you for the token, and asks you to hold it, not read it.
anchorfall_kept_name.offer: I'll tell you a thing I've never told a soul with an Archive coat. Twenty years I've stamped the finished, clean columns, no questions. And I want to save one. Just one, before I'm done. There's a name I closed last week that isn't settled -- the tablet's warm, the token's loose in the canal, and the old craft says a name kept warm by a living hand before dawn doesn't finish closing. Fetch the token. Don't read it -- a living reader feeds the very thing we're stalling. Just hold it warm and bring it back, and let one account, one single account, stay open on my watch.
anchorfall_kept_name.progress: Recover the loose name-token from the canal before dawn. Hold it warm. Do not read it.
anchorfall_kept_name.complete: Warm. Still warm, and you didn't read it -- I can see the seal unbroken. Then it's not finished. One name, out of twenty years of names, that I closed and then declined to finish closing. It's not redemption. Twenty years doesn't unfile with one held token. But there'll be an account on the Archive's books that says collected and lies, because a living hand kept it warm and a clerk finally said no. Keep this token's twin. It's a name I embroidered myself. It's yours, if you ever need proof you were someone.

objectives.text: Recover the loose name-token, unread
Return the warm token to Sava

anchorfall_kept_name.note: Twenty years of filing the finished, and Sava saved one: a name kept warm by a living hand before dawn, its account stalled open. Not redemption. Twenty years does not unfile with one held token. But one line on the Archive's books now says collected and lies, because a clerk finally said no.

anchorfall_the_broker.title: The Broker Who Won't Close
anchorfall_the_broker.summary: A dead broker trades favors and grave-debts out of a back room in Anchorfall -- collected on the ledgers, yet still doing business. Gavril owes him, and the debt is coming due. Recover the broker's own crooked ledger before the man's tablet finally closes and takes Gavril's name with it.
anchorfall_the_broker.offer: There's a broker works the back rooms here, and I'll tell you the joke first so it doesn't spoil later: he's dead. Collected. Closed on the ledgers years ago, and here he sits, tapping his own tablet when he's nervous, trading favors and grave-debts with the patience of a man who's stopped arguing with the math. I did business with him -- everyone does -- and now his tablet's finally cracking closed, and when it does, every debt in his crooked ledger settles at once. Including mine. Get me that ledger. If his account closes with my name in it, I close too. Same overdue notice, he'd say. I'd rather stay overdue.
anchorfall_the_broker.progress: Recover the dead broker's crooked ledger before his tablet closes and settles every debt in it.
anchorfall_the_broker.complete: You got it, and the man let you -- he knew. 'Take it,' he'd have said, 'you and me and half this port, we're all the same overdue notice; difference is you're still arguing.' There -- my name, and a dozen others, in a dead hand's honest crooked columns. I'll burn the page we're on. Not the whole book -- there's debts in here the Archive should never collect and a burned book collects nothing. Same as always, down here: you don't win free. You stay usefully overdue, and you keep the ledger warm, and you argue with the math one more day.

objectives.text: Hear which back room the broker keeps
Recover the dead broker's crooked ledger

anchorfall_the_broker.note: A broker collected years ago, still trading, tapping his own tablet to check he is still allowed to exist. His crooked ledger held a dozen names the Archive should never collect. Gavril burned one page and kept the book warm. You do not win free in Anchorfall. You stay usefully overdue, and argue with the math one more day.

anchorfall_counteragent.title: The Counteragent of Quiet
anchorfall_counteragent.summary: The old alchemist's last recipe was a draught that briefly returns feeling to those going smooth and quiet near the fog -- restoring grief, anger, the stubborn warmth the signal flattens first. Matei needs its ingredients gathered from the fen and delivered to a neighbor already half gone still.
anchorfall_counteragent.offer: The smooth ones frighten me worse than the ash. You'll have seen them -- folk who stopped grieving, stopped raging, gone agreeable and empty by the water. That flatness is the door the signal comes in by; a person with no fury left is a person half-filed already. There was an alchemist here who brewed a counteragent -- a bitter draught that gives you back your grief for an hour, your anger, the stubborn warm mess of being a person. I have the recipe. I need the fen-herbs, and a strong stomach, because you'll be dosing my neighbor, and she's gone very quiet, and it is not gentle, giving someone back their pain.
anchorfall_counteragent.progress: Gather the fen-herbs for the counteragent and carry the draught to Matei's quiet neighbor.
anchorfall_counteragent.complete: She wept. Saints, she wept, and cursed, and threw the cup, and it was the best sound I've heard in a month -- a person, back, furious and grieving and hers again for an hour. It won't last. It never lasts; the flatness comes back the way the tide comes back. But for an hour she was too much herself to file, and she used the hour to hold her grandchild and swear at the fog, which is exactly the right use of an hour. Keep a dose. If you ever go smooth and agreeable near the water -- and you might -- something should be able to hand you your fury back.

objectives.text: Gather fen-herbs and brew the counteragent

objectives.label: the quiet neighbor's door
objectives.text: Carry the draught to the quiet neighbor

anchorfall_counteragent.note: The signal flattens grief and fury first, because a person with no rage left is half-filed already. Matei's draught gives the pain back for an hour. She wept and cursed and threw the cup, too much herself to file, and used the hour to hold her grandchild and swear at the fog. Exactly the right use of an hour. It never lasts. The tide comes back.

last_hearth_the_hearth_that_holds.title: The Hearth That Holds
last_hearth_the_hearth_that_holds.summary: A thousand years downriver from Raven Hollow, one common-room still keeps a fire and a stranger's chair. Ioana asks only that a newcomer eat, warm through, and be counted among the living before the fog decides otherwise.
last_hearth_the_hearth_that_holds.offer: Sit. You've the look of someone who walked the canal roads to get here, and nobody arrives at the Last Hearth by mistake -- they arrive because everywhere else stopped being warm. There's a stool by the fire that's held stranger folk than you. Sit in it. Eat. Let me hear you breathe a while, so I can tell you're still the sort that does.
last_hearth_the_hearth_that_holds.progress: The fire's in the common-room, and the stool's the low one by the hearthstone. Warm through before you do anything else, traveler.
last_hearth_the_hearth_that_holds.complete: There. Colour in you again. You'd be surprised how many come off that road grey to the lips and never get it back. We keep a fire and a chair and a name for everyone who sits -- it's a small thing to keep, but out here small warm things are the only ones worth the keeping. Take this. And come back to the fire when the fog gets loud.

objectives.label: the common-room hearth
objectives.text: Warm through at the common fire
Let Ioana count you among the living

last_hearth_the_hearth_that_holds.note: Ioana keeps a fire, a chair, and a name for every stranger who sits. She did not say why the last part matters most. In Greyhollow, a name is the thing they close first.

last_hearth_bread_that_rises.title: Bread That Still Rises
last_hearth_bread_that_rises.summary: Ioana's mother's yeast has risen bread here since before the canals took the old streets. It has never once failed. She wants it kept alive the honest way -- flour, water, warmth -- while the flour lasts.
last_hearth_bread_that_rises.offer: This crock's older than the port, dear. My mother's mother caught the wild yeast in it and it has never gone quiet -- not through the tide-years, not through the collections, not through any of it. Yeast dies where the ground goes warm, you know. So long as this one's still bubbling, the ground under us is still cold and honest. Feed it for me -- I want mill-flour, the coarse grey, four sacks off Neaga before her stock spoils.
last_hearth_bread_that_rises.progress: Neaga the merchant keeps the coarse grey mill-flour. Four sacks will feed the crock through the wet months.
last_hearth_bread_that_rises.complete: Listen to it. Still working, still alive, still telling us the truth about the ground. My mother said a house that can raise bread is a house the enemy hasn't reached yet. Warm loaf's yours for the fetching, and the coin. And thank you -- you've no idea what it steadies in me, hearing that crock breathe.

objectives.text: Bring coarse grey mill-flour to Ioana

last_hearth_bread_that_rises.note: The crock still breathes. As long as it does, the ground beneath the Last Hearth stays cold. Ioana feeds it every morning, the way you feed a thing you cannot afford to lose.

last_hearth_the_ledger_of_the_saved.title: The Ledger of the Saved
last_hearth_the_ledger_of_the_saved.summary: Tinca keeps a book that is the opposite of the Archive's: not who was collected, but who was kept -- every name a stranger gave the fire before the road took them on. She wants three names walked to their graves and read aloud, so the book stays a promise and not just paper.
last_hearth_the_ledger_of_the_saved.offer: The Archive keeps a ledger of the closed. I keep the other one -- the names of everyone who sat at that fire and stayed a person while they did. Most walk on. Some don't walk far. There are three kerbs in our little yard whose folk I wrote down myself, and a name unread is a name half-collected. Go and read them where they lie. Say them out loud. It's the whole of the work, and it's more than you'd think.
last_hearth_the_ledger_of_the_saved.progress: The kept graves lie in the yard behind the common-room. Read each name aloud where it's cut in the kerb.
last_hearth_the_ledger_of_the_saved.complete: You said all three. Good. I heard you from the door. A name read is a name that happened, and a thing that happened can't be filed into never-was, no matter how the fog leans on it. That's not superstition, whatever the clerks say -- it's the only counter I've got, and I've had a thousand years to test it. Here. You're in the book now, too. Try to stay the kind of name worth reading.

objectives.label: the kept graves
objectives.text: Read the three kept names aloud
Return the read names to Tinca

last_hearth_the_ledger_of_the_saved.note: Tinca keeps a ledger of the kept -- the anti-Archive, one warm name at a time. A name read aloud, she says, cannot be filed into never-was. She has had a thousand years to be wrong about it, and she is still reading.

last_hearth_peat_below_the_planks.title: What Rises From the Peat
last_hearth_peat_below_the_planks.summary: The Last Hearth stands on drained peat-bog, and lately the bog gives its dead back -- brown, patient, standing figures that come up through the reed-beds at the water's edge. Casandra needs the landing cleared before the barges won't berth.
last_hearth_peat_below_the_planks.offer: You'll have felt the ground give under your boots -- that's peat, and peat keeps what falls into it. Kept it kind, for centuries. Now it's giving them back. They come up through the reeds at the south landing, slow as you like, brown as the bog, and they walk straight for the warm windows. The bargemen won't tie up while they're about. Put them back down. Six of them, and mind your footing -- the ground that made them will try to make you, too.
last_hearth_peat_below_the_planks.progress: The peat-shamblers rise through the reed-beds at the south landing. Six put down should clear the berth.
last_hearth_peat_below_the_planks.complete: Six down, and the reeds quiet. You saw how they faced the windows -- every one. Not hungry. Drawn. Something warm's in short supply out here and the bog's learned to want it same as everything else. The barges'll berth tonight. Take this off the wharf-chest -- you earned dry boots and better.

objectives.text: Put down the peat-shamblers at the south landing

last_hearth_peat_below_the_planks.note: The peat gives its dead back now, and every one rises facing the warm windows. Not hunger, Casandra said. Wanting. In the drowned future, even the ground is short of warmth and has learned to come looking.

last_hearth_dry_boots_for_the_watch.title: Dry Boots for the Watch
last_hearth_dry_boots_for_the_watch.summary: Paraschiva stands the night wall in boots the canal-damp has eaten through. Maricica can cut new soles from good bog-iron rivets -- if someone will haul the iron up from the flooded forge-store.
last_hearth_dry_boots_for_the_watch.offer: Look at these. Two winters on the wall and the wet's had the soles clean off. You try standing a watch with the canal coming up through your toes -- turns a body mean, and a mean watcher misses things, and out here missing things gets folk finalized. Maricica says she'll nail me new soles if she's iron to do it with. There's a chest of bog-iron down in the old forge-store, half under water. Fetch me some. My feet and I will bless your name.
last_hearth_dry_boots_for_the_watch.progress: The bog-iron sits in the flooded forge-store below the smithy. Bring Paraschiva enough to sole a pair of boots.
last_hearth_dry_boots_for_the_watch.complete: Dry. Saints and small mercies, dry. You'd think after a thousand years of this I'd not still grin like a girl over dry socks, but there it is -- the little warm things keep you human, and staying human's the entire job out here. Take a few coins and my thanks. I'll see clearer on the wall tonight, and that's worth more than either of us lets on.

objectives.text: Haul bog-iron from the flooded forge-store

last_hearth_dry_boots_for_the_watch.note: Paraschiva got dry boots and grinned like a girl over it. A thousand years on the wall and the small warm things still keep her human. Staying human, she says, is the entire job out here.

last_hearth_the_warm_tablet.title: The Tablet That Stayed Warm
last_hearth_the_warm_tablet.summary: A debt-tablet washed up under the piers, hairline-cracked and still faintly warm -- an account not quite closed. Despina, who once filed such things for the Archive, cannot bring herself to finalize it and cannot bring herself to keep it. She needs it read by alchemical sight at the open piers before she decides.
last_hearth_the_warm_tablet.offer: I was Archive once. I stamped a great many people into closed and slept fine, and that's the confession, not the fog. This came up under the third pier at dawn. A debt-tablet, cracked along the top, and -- feel it -- warm. They're not supposed to be warm. Warm means the account's still running, still someone, still refusing the verdict. I can't finalize it. I can't hold it. Take it out to the open piers where the fog reads honest, and read it under sight-light. Tell me what colour it burns.
last_hearth_the_warm_tablet.progress: Carry the warm tablet to the open end of the piers and read it under alchemical sight where the fog runs clear.
last_hearth_the_warm_tablet.complete: Shifting orange. You needn't say it twice -- I saw your face come back. The signal's writing itself into the stone, out here, this far. It's not just Greyhollow's disease anymore; it's reaching for our warm little corner because warm is exactly what it hunts. Don't read the marks. Don't copy them. I'll seal it in lead and drop it where the peat's deep. Some accounts you keep open by refusing to close them. Go warm up. You've earned the fire tonight.

objectives.label: the open piers under sight-light
objectives.text: Read the warm tablet where the fog runs clear
Tell Despina what colour it burned

last_hearth_the_warm_tablet.note: A debt-tablet washed up warm, and under sight it burned shifting orange -- the signal writing itself, this far downriver, reaching for the last warm corner because warm is what it hunts. Despina sealed it in lead and did not close it. Some accounts, she said, you keep open by refusing.

last_hearth_the_child_of_chalk.title: The Handprint on the Wall
last_hearth_the_child_of_chalk.summary: On a canal wall, children have pressed chalk handprints for generations -- the same emblem as an orphanage a thousand years gone. One print has gone faintly copper and warm. Zoe wants it warded before a child touches it and starts to listen.
last_hearth_the_child_of_chalk.offer: The little ones chalk their hands on the wall by the water-stair -- always have, it's older than any of us, older than the port, a wall of small hands going back and back. My gran said the very same wall stood at a children's house upriver when the world was younger. There's a new print gone wrong. Copper-stained. Warm when you lay a palm near it. A child pressed that, and if another lays their hand in it and lingers, they'll start to listen, and you know what listening becomes. Ward it. I've the chalk-and-salt for it, only I can't make myself go near.
last_hearth_the_child_of_chalk.progress: The coppered handprint is on the canal wall at the water-stair. Set the warding, then bring word to Zoe.
last_hearth_the_child_of_chalk.complete: It's done? The salt-line holds? Bless you. I'll paint a new white hand over it come morning, a clean one, so the little ones have somewhere honest to press. A copper hand on a children's wall -- that's the whole of what's wrong with this age, that even that gets reached for. Here, take this. My gran carried one just like it, and she kept her wits to the very end, which out here is a kind of victory nobody names.

objectives.label: the water-stair wall
objectives.text: Ward the coppered handprint
Tell Zoe the ward is set

last_hearth_the_child_of_chalk.note: A copper handprint on a wall of children's chalk -- the same wall, Zoe's gran swore, that stood at an orphanage a thousand years upriver. It was warded and painted white again. Even a children's wall gets reached for now. That is the whole of what is wrong with this age.

last_hearth_salt_for_the_thresholds.title: Salt for the Thresholds
last_hearth_salt_for_the_thresholds.summary: The old folk of the Last Hearth lay a salt-line at every doorstep each dusk -- a small warding older than the reasons for it. Aglaia is short of brine-salt and short of knees. She sends for a fresh pouch daily.
last_hearth_salt_for_the_thresholds.offer: Every door in the Hearth gets its salt-line at dusk, that's how we've always done, and don't ask me the why -- my mother did it, and hers, and we're all still ourselves, which is more than the finalized districts can say. My knees have quit the stooping, though. Fetch me a pouch of good brine-salt from the fen-boilers, would you? Enough to line the doors before dark. There's a copper in it and a warm word, same as yesterday, same as tomorrow.
last_hearth_salt_for_the_thresholds.progress: The fen-boilers sell brine-salt by the pouch. Aglaia needs one before dusk to line the thresholds.
last_hearth_salt_for_the_thresholds.complete: There's my salt. The doors'll hold another night, then. Folk laugh at an old woman and her lines -- until the year the lines are the only thing between a house and the listening. You come back tomorrow, dear. There's always another dusk, and the salt runs out same as the light does.

objectives.text: Fetch brine-salt for the thresholds

last_hearth_salt_for_the_thresholds.note: The salt-lines go down every dusk, and no one remembers the reason -- only that the houses that keep the habit are still themselves. Aglaia's mother did it, and hers. It runs out same as the light does.

last_hearth_the_incurious_watch.title: The Incurious Watch
last_hearth_the_incurious_watch.summary: Ilinca watches the fog-line where the finalized district meets the warm town, and asks the strangest of favours: keep her watch a while at the pier-head, and do not try to understand anything you see. Understanding, out here, is how the fog gets in.
last_hearth_the_incurious_watch.offer: I'll ask you a thing that sounds like madness and isn't. Keep my watch on the pier-head an hour. Look at the fog where it meets the lamps. And whatever it seems to be spelling out there in the dark -- don't work at it. Don't lean in. Don't get curious. Out here the ones who go quiet aren't taken, they figure it out, and figuring it out is the taking. Watch like an old dog watches: alert, and utterly incurious. Can you do that? Most can't. It's why I ask a stranger.
last_hearth_the_incurious_watch.progress: Take the pier-head watch and hold it. Watch the fog-line -- and, whatever it seems to say, do not try to read it.
last_hearth_the_incurious_watch.complete: You held. And you didn't lean in when it started arranging itself, I could tell -- you kept your eyes soft and your mind on your boots. Good. That's a rarer talent than a sword, out here. The fog says things to keep you reading, because a reader seeds the next one. You gave it nothing. Take this charm -- it's for the itch, the pull to understand. Wear it when the fog talks. It won't stop the pull. It'll just remind you to look at your boots.

objectives.label: the pier-head watch
objectives.text: Keep the pier-head watch -- do not read the fog
Report the incurious watch to Ilinca

last_hearth_the_incurious_watch.note: Ilinca watches the fog-line and asks her watchers to stay incurious -- for out here the quiet ones are not taken, they figure it out, and figuring it out is the taking. The charm does not stop the pull to understand. It reminds you to look at your boots.

last_hearth_the_broker_who_didnt_close.title: The Broker Who Wouldn't Close
last_hearth_the_broker_who_didnt_close.summary: In the Greyhollow annex there is a broker, a debt still walking upright, who has kept one account open for a thousand years by refusing to file it. Despina wants him warned that the warm tablet surfaced at the Last Hearth -- and wants a read on whether he is ally or auditor.
last_hearth_the_broker_who_didnt_close.offer: There's a broker in the annex up the canal -- Marrow Pell, keeps books that should've closed and haven't. A dead man doing the one honest thing a dead man can: not filing. He needs to know a warm tablet came up on our piers, because if one surfaced here, more are curing, and he's the only one who reads the cracks. But Despina's-warning-you: he's Archive marrow to the bone. He might help. He might audit us instead. Take my word to him. Come back and tell me which face he wore.
last_hearth_the_broker_who_didnt_close.progress: Take word of the warm tablet toward the Greyhollow annex, reach Marrow Pell's canal-gate, and read whether he is help or auditor.
last_hearth_the_broker_who_didnt_close.complete: So he asked after the crack, not the name. That tells me plenty -- he still reads them as accounts, even the ones he won't close. But he didn't send a collector back down the canal with you, and in Greyhollow that's as near to friendship as the dead get. We'll trade him what we learn, warily. A refusal to close is the only politics left worth having out here. Rest by the fire. The annex chills a body worse than the fog.

objectives.text: Take Despina's warning toward the annex

objectives.label: the annex canal-gate
objectives.text: Reach the annex where Marrow Pell keeps his books
Report to Despina which face the broker wore

last_hearth_the_broker_who_didnt_close.note: Marrow Pell asked after the crack, not the name -- still an accountant, even in his refusal to close. But he sent no collector back down the canal, and in Greyhollow that is as near to friendship as the dead get. A refusal to close is the only politics left worth having.

last_hearth_the_morven_mark.title: The Mark Around the Kindness
last_hearth_the_morven_mark.summary: A fresh Morven surveillance-mark has been chalked carefully around the warded handprint -- protecting it, or cataloguing it, no one can say. Ilinca wants the Morven contact found and the intent read before the town decides the spies are enemies.
last_hearth_the_morven_mark.offer: Someone chalked a Morven mark round Zoe's warded handprint. Careful work -- the sort that either guards a thing or files it, and with the Morven you never know which, because they never know which, that's the whole horror of them. They're Sabira's grandchildren a thousand times removed, the listening-house, and they've a coordinator working our canals quiet-like. Find them at the canal-post. Read the mark's meaning off their own face. If they're guarding the child's wall, they're near enough to friends. If they're cataloguing it -- I want to know before the town does.
last_hearth_the_morven_mark.progress: Find the Morven coordinator at the canal-post, and read whether the chalked mark guards or catalogues.
last_hearth_the_morven_mark.complete: Guarding. You're sure? ...I believe you -- your face doesn't sell things. Then the Morven decided a wall of children's hands is worth their protection, and that's the closest this drowned age comes to hope: even the spies drew a line around the one warm thing and dared the fog to cross it. Don't mistake it for softness. The Morven guard what they intend to keep, and keeping's a colder word than saving. But I'll take it. Out here you take the warm you're given.

objectives.label: the Morven canal-post
objectives.text: Find the Morven coordinator
Tell Ilinca whether the mark guards or catalogues

last_hearth_the_morven_mark.note: The Morven chalked their mark to guard the children's wall, not to file it -- the closest this drowned age comes to hope. But keeping is a colder word than saving, and the Morven guard only what they intend to keep. Out here you take the warm you are given.

last_hearth_forge_that_still_rings.title: The Forge That Still Rings
last_hearth_forge_that_still_rings.summary: Maricica keeps the only ringing anvil for fifty leagues of drowned canal, and refuses to let it go quiet -- a silent forge, she says, is a district that's given up. She needs bog-iron and a warm pair of hands at the bellows to keep the ring alive one more season.
last_hearth_forge_that_still_rings.offer: Hear that? Ring of iron on iron. There's not another anvil sings between here and the salt fens -- they've all gone quiet, and a quiet forge is a district that's stopped arguing with the fog. Not mine. Not while I've breath. But I'm short of iron and shorter of help, and an old woman can't work the bellows and the hammer both. Bring me bog-iron, good clean lumps from the peat, and I'll keep the ring going another season. It's a small defiance. Small defiance is what we've got.
last_hearth_forge_that_still_rings.progress: Maricica needs bog-iron lumps from the peat to keep the forge ringing. Bring her a good haul.
last_hearth_forge_that_still_rings.complete: Now that's iron worth striking. Listen to it sing under the hammer. Every ring's a word in the argument, you understand -- the argument being that we're still here, still making, still warming metal instead of being warmed by the wrong ground. Take this -- I struck it from the last good billet, folded it warm. Carry something the fog didn't make. It matters more than steel usually does.

objectives.text: Bring bog-iron to keep the forge ringing

last_hearth_forge_that_still_rings.note: Maricica keeps the only ringing anvil for fifty leagues, and every strike is a word in one long argument: that the Last Hearth is still here, still making, still warming metal instead of being warmed by the wrong ground. Small defiance is what they have.

last_hearth_the_finalized_that_walk.title: The Finalized That Walk
last_hearth_the_finalized_that_walk.summary: A district up the canal was finalized in the night -- doors closed, faces gone -- and now the peat below it gives up figures that walk toward the Last Hearth's warmth in rows. Safta needs them stopped at the boundary before the town gets counted with them.
last_hearth_the_finalized_that_walk.offer: The Millrace district went quiet three nights back. Not raided -- finalized. Doors shut, lamps out, and by morning the folk in it were just... not folk anymore, the way it happens now. And the peat under them's giving up shamblers, and they don't wander -- they come, in rows, toward our lamps, toward the warm. The bog wants what the district lost. Hold the boundary with me. Put down what comes up the towpath. If they reach the Hearth in numbers, the fog reads us next, and I've buried too many warm windows already.
last_hearth_the_finalized_that_walk.progress: The peat-shamblers come in rows up the Millrace towpath toward the Hearth. Hold the boundary and put them down.
last_hearth_the_finalized_that_walk.complete: Held. You held the line. Look at them -- they were folk three nights gone, Millrace folk, and now they're just the shape the peat gave back, walking at our lamps because our lamps are the last warm thing on the water. That's not a battle, what we did. It's a mercy and a bookkeeping both, and I hate that those are the same act now. Take this. And say nothing warm to me tonight -- I've not the stomach for it. Tomorrow, maybe.

objectives.text: Hold the boundary against the Millrace rows
Stand down with Safta

last_hearth_the_finalized_that_walk.note: The Millrace district was finalized in the night, and the peat gave its people back as things that walk at warm lamps. Holding the towpath was a mercy and a bookkeeping both, and Safta hates that those are the same act now. She wanted no warm words after. Tomorrow, maybe.

last_hearth_a_trout_she_cant_cook.title: A Trout She Cannot Cook
last_hearth_a_trout_she_cant_cook.summary: Zoe has never been able to cook a river-trout -- the last thing her drowned brother ever said to her was about the river, and she cannot bear the smell of one over her own fire. She asks a stranger to catch, cook, and share one, so she can finally sit at a trout supper again.
last_hearth_a_trout_she_cant_cook.offer: This'll sound foolish. I can't cook a trout. Never could, not since -- my brother went into the canal the year of the great tide, and the last thing he ever said to me was about the river, about how the trout run at dawn, and now the smell of one over my fire and I'm nine years old and screaming at the water. But I miss trout. I miss sitting to one like an ordinary evening. Would you catch one and cook it? At the common fire, not mine? I'll sit if someone else's hands do it. Just this once, I'd like to eat like it didn't cost me a brother.
last_hearth_a_trout_she_cant_cook.progress: Catch a dawn-run river-trout, cook it at the common fire, and sit Zoe down to a trout supper at last.
last_hearth_a_trout_she_cant_cook.complete: I ate it. First trout in nine years, and I didn't scream, and I didn't cry until it was finished, which is progress, my gran would've said. Thank you. You didn't fix anything -- there's no fixing it -- you just made a supper someone else's hands cooked, and let me be at the table for it. That's all grief ever needed and never gets. Take this. It was his. He'd want it walking around warm instead of sitting in my drawer being sad.

objectives.text: Catch a dawn-run river-trout

objectives.label: the common fire
objectives.text: Cook the trout at the common fire for Zoe

last_hearth_a_trout_she_cant_cook.note: Zoe ate her first trout in nine years, cooked by another's hands, and only cried when it was finished. Nothing was fixed -- grief does not fix. She simply got to be at the table for once. That is all grief ever needed and never gets.

last_hearth_the_keepsake_run.title: The Keepsake Run
last_hearth_the_keepsake_run.summary: A sealed keepsake-box must go up the canal to the annex without being opened -- a dead woman's kept name, worth more to the Morven and the Archive than any tablet. The temptation to look inside is the point. Casandra needs it carried by someone who can refuse.
last_hearth_the_keepsake_run.offer: This box is sealed and it stays sealed. Inside's a keepsake -- a name, a lock of hair, some small warm proof that a woman lived and was specific and was loved. Worth nothing. Worth everything. The Morven want it because it's leverage; the Archive wants it because an unfiled name is a loose end; and you'll want to open it, because everyone does, and looking is exactly how the fog gets its foot in the door. Carry it to the annex gate up the canal. Don't read the seal. Don't lift the lid. Deliver it whole. Can you carry a thing and not need to know it?
last_hearth_the_keepsake_run.progress: Carry the sealed keepsake up the canal to the annex gate. Do not open it -- looking is how the fog gets in.
last_hearth_the_keepsake_run.complete: Sealed still. You've a rare set of hands, you know that? Most folk are curious by the second bridge and finalized by the fourth. That box held a woman named -- well. It held her, and now it's safe in a keeping that won't file her, and she gets to stay specific a while longer. That's the work out here. Not saving. Keeping. Carrying warm things past the pull to open them. Take this for it. You refused the lid. Most can't.

objectives.text: Take up the sealed keepsake from Casandra

objectives.label: the annex gate up the canal
objectives.text: Carry the keepsake to the annex, unopened

last_hearth_the_keepsake_run.note: A dead woman's kept name traveled the canal sealed, and the carrier refused the lid -- most are curious by the second bridge and finalized by the fourth. She stays specific a while longer now. The work out here is not saving. It is keeping, and carrying warm things past the pull to open them.

last_hearth_the_counter_taught.title: A Living Touch Receives
last_hearth_the_counter_taught.summary: A blind seer in the annex knows the counter -- that a living touch on a stone only feeds the machine, and a dead touch can transmit against it. Ilinca sends for the doctrine before the seer's own erasure finishes taking him, but every telling of it costs the teller.
last_hearth_the_counter_taught.offer: There's a seer in the annex, blind, with the eyeless sight, and he's dissolving -- every day less of him, because he understands too much and understanding erases. But he knows the one thing worth knowing: the counter. That a living hand on a stone only feeds it -- gives the machine the warm-to-cold it hungers for. And that a dead touch, a stubborn dead thing that keeps getting up, can push the living present into the record instead. Go and take the telling from him. It'll cost him some of what's left. He'll give it anyway. That's the kind of man erasure is taking.
last_hearth_the_counter_taught.progress: Reach the blind seer in the annex and take the counter from him -- knowing the telling costs him what little remains.
last_hearth_the_counter_taught.complete: You have it, then. A living touch receives; a dead touch transmits; the weapon is specificity and stubbornness, small imperfect warm things carried by something that won't stay down. And it cost him a hand's-breadth of self to say it -- I can hear it in how you're quiet. That's the whole tragedy in one transaction: the truth that could save us is priced in the teller. Carry it carefully. Don't waste what he spent. And when you finally stand where the stone is -- remember which touch is which.

objectives.label: the annex where the seer dissolves
objectives.text: Take the counter from the blind seer
Bring the counter back to Ilinca

last_hearth_the_counter_taught.note: The blind seer gave up the counter -- living touch receives, dead touch transmits, the weapon is specificity and stubbornness -- and it cost him a hand's-breadth of the self he had left. The truth that could save the world is priced in its teller. Do not waste what he spent.

last_hearth_lamps_against_the_fog.title: Lamps Against the Fog
last_hearth_lamps_against_the_fog.summary: Neaga trades in deepbed-lantern oil, the only light that burns steady in the wet fog, and keeps the Hearth's lamps lit against the dark that creeps a little closer each year. She needs oil hauled up daily to keep every warm window glowing.
last_hearth_lamps_against_the_fog.offer: Come in out of it, come in. Fog's up early tonight and every year it's up earlier -- creeps a lane closer while nobody's measuring, and the only answer I've got is more lamps burning longer. Deepbed oil, that's the stuff, burns steady where cheaper light drowns. There's a run of it needs hauling from the wharf before the barge shoves off. Fetch it and I'll keep the windows lit another night. A lit window says someone's home and human. The fog hates a lit window. Good enough reason to keep them all burning.
last_hearth_lamps_against_the_fog.progress: Haul deepbed-lantern oil from the wharf so Neaga can keep the Hearth's windows lit tonight.
last_hearth_lamps_against_the_fog.complete: Every window lit. Good. Let the fog creep -- it'll find no dark corner in the Hearth tonight. You'll come again tomorrow, I expect; the oil burns down and the fog comes back, that's the arrangement, and it's a fair one so long as we've hands to haul and coin to trade. Here's your coin. Warm yourself before you go back out in it.

objectives.text: Haul deepbed oil to keep the lamps lit

last_hearth_lamps_against_the_fog.note: The fog creeps a lane closer each year while no one measures it, and Neaga's only answer is more lamps burning longer. A lit window says someone is home and human, and the fog hates a lit window. Good enough reason to keep them all burning.

last_hearth_the_reader_who_refused.title: The Reader Who Refused
