finalized_fields_rows_of_twelve.progress: The moor-wails gather in their rows along the canal-edge below the piers. Break the count -- eight should scatter the arrangement.
finalized_fields_rows_of_twelve.complete: Eight down and the rest lost their rows -- scattered, ragged, blessedly uncountable. For a while. They will re-form; things out here always re-form, it is the one industry that never slows. But tonight the water spells nothing, and a dockmaster who can count his cargo and get the same number twice sleeps like a paid man. Take the coin off the manifest. It was never anyone's.

objectives.text: Break the rows of wails on the canal-edge

finalized_fields_rows_of_twelve.note: The wails hung in rows of twelve for twelve nights, arranging like papers toward the Archive. Gherghe broke the count before it finished its sentence. Out here nothing scatters for long -- but a night that spells nothing is a night, and he took it.

finalized_fields_the_stamp_and_the_thumb.title: The Stamp and the Thumb
finalized_fields_the_stamp_and_the_thumb.summary: A dossier came back stamped, un-stamped, then stamped again -- three impressions where the writ allows one. The clerk wants it read against the merchant's and the watcher's memories before he files a thing that has been decided twice.
finalized_fields_the_stamp_and_the_thumb.offer: Look at this line. Stamped. Then un-stamped -- see the thumb-smear where someone wet it and lifted it. Then stamped again, harder, angry. Three impressions. The writ allows one. Someone up the chain closed this account, changed their mind, and closed it once more, and no clerk out here has the standing to ask why. Ask for me. Zaharia sold to this man. Manea watched him walk the rows. Hear them both before I file the third stamp.
finalized_fields_the_stamp_and_the_thumb.progress: Merchant Zaharia dealt with the twice-closed man; Watcher Manea saw his last walk. Ask them both what the ledger left out.
finalized_fields_the_stamp_and_the_thumb.complete: So he paid a debt, was released, and someone with a longer reach decided release was an error. The un-stamp was mercy. The re-stamp was correction. And the correction wins, because it always wins -- the ledger keeps whichever impression is last. I will file it. What else is there. But I know now which of the three was true, and the true one is the one the record threw away.

objectives.text: Ask Merchant Zaharia about the twice-closed man
Ask Watcher Manea what he saw on the rows

finalized_fields_the_stamp_and_the_thumb.note: Stamped, un-stamped, stamped: three impressions, and the ledger keeps only the last. The un-stamp was mercy and the re-stamp overruled it. Iosif filed the correction because there was nothing else to do -- and kept, in his own head, the impression the record threw away.

finalized_fields_a_shoe_a_cup_a_name.title: A Shoe, a Cup, a Name
finalized_fields_a_shoe_a_cup_a_name.summary: Emilian's daughter was filed a year ago, and the account gave her back as a number in a row. He does not want her un-filed -- he knows better. He wants one true, useless, particular thing of hers spoken over the ground so the record cannot generalize her.
finalized_fields_a_shoe_a_cup_a_name.offer: They filed my girl a year past. I am not asking the impossible -- I know what closed means, I have lived out here long enough to stop asking for mornings. But the account made her tidy. A row, a number, a clean line. She was not tidy. She hid trout in her apron and lied about it badly. Go to her row and say one true small wrong thing about her over the ground, out loud, where the record can hear it refuse to fit. That is all. That is everything.
finalized_fields_a_shoe_a_cup_a_name.progress: The girl's row lies in the eastern filing-plots. Stand over it and speak one particular, useless, true thing the ledger could never hold.
finalized_fields_a_shoe_a_cup_a_name.complete: You told the ground she lied about the trout. Out loud, the specific of it, the exact bad lie. It does not un-close her. Nothing does. But the account is perfect and perfect means general, and now there is one detail in the air over her row that the master file cannot copy. A splinter in a smooth thing. She would have laughed at being a splinter. Take her cord. She tied it. Badly. Keep the bad knot.

objectives.label: the daughter's row in the eastern plots
objectives.text: Speak one true, particular thing over her row

finalized_fields_a_shoe_a_cup_a_name.note: Over the girl's row you said the one true useless thing: she lied about the trout, and badly. The account is perfect, and perfect is general, and now a splinter of the particular hangs where the master file cannot reach. Emilian kept the bad knot she tied. It will not hold. That is why he keeps it.

finalized_fields_the_incurious_trade.title: The Incurious Trade
finalized_fields_the_incurious_trade.summary: The merchant sells charms that mean nothing on purpose -- protection that works because it refuses to be read. His curing-batch spoiled in the owed water, and he needs peat-shambler tallow rendered to seal the next, before a customer gets clever and reads one.
finalized_fields_the_incurious_trade.offer: Best-selling item I stock is a charm that means absolutely nothing, and I will thank you not to look too closely at why that sells out here. The whole defense is that it refuses to signify -- a thing the record cannot read, it cannot file. My sealing-batch curdled in the well-water, which now, wonderful, tastes owed. I need tallow off the shamblers in the rows to reseal. Six should do. Do not, whatever you do, find the charms interesting.
finalized_fields_the_incurious_trade.progress: The peat-shamblers render good tallow, and they walk the near rows by the sheds. Take six, and keep your questions to yourself.
finalized_fields_the_incurious_trade.complete: Sealed and stupid, the whole batch, meaning nothing beautifully. A woman came in yesterday and asked what the glyph meant. I told her it means nothing and she looked disappointed and safe, which is the correct order of feelings out here. Here -- one for you. Wear it. Be bored by it. Boredom is armor in the Fields, and the curious are always the first entry.

objectives.text: Render tallow and seal the incurious charms
Take shambler tallow from the near rows

finalized_fields_the_incurious_trade.note: Zaharia sealed a batch of charms that mean nothing, beautifully. Out here a thing the record cannot read is a thing it cannot file -- so his best defense is boredom worn as an amulet. The curious are always the first entry. He stays in business by being the least interesting man in the Fields.

finalized_fields_the_toll_no_one_paid.title: The Toll No One Paid
finalized_fields_the_toll_no_one_paid.summary: A filing-barge arrived with its toll unpaid and no crew aboard -- just cargo, correctly manifested, and one receipt in a hand the dockmaster half-knows. He wants the roads walked to the toll-post to learn who signed for a crossing that killed the signer.
finalized_fields_the_toll_no_one_paid.offer: Barge came in at dawn, toll unpaid, crew gone -- not overboard, gone, the way accounts go. Cargo all present and correctly listed, which is the part that turns my stomach. And a toll-receipt in the tray, signed. I half-know the hand. Every crossing has a price and this one collected it and kept the change. Walk out to the ledger-road post where it launched. Find who signs for a crossing that files the signer. Quietly. The Morven pay for quiet.
finalized_fields_the_toll_no_one_paid.progress: The barge launched from the toll-post out on the ledger-road edge of the Fields. Walk there and read the crossing's record.
finalized_fields_the_toll_no_one_paid.complete: The signature is the tollkeeper's own. He signed his own crossing -- released his own account by paying it forward with himself, one clean line, cargo delivered, keeper collected. A man made himself the toll so his passengers went free and unlisted. There is a forged receipt in it for you; use it and pay no toll, the way he made sure they never would. Some men balance a ledger by subtracting themselves.

objectives.label: the toll-post on the ledger-road edge
objectives.text: Read the crossing's record at the toll-post

finalized_fields_the_toll_no_one_paid.note: The tollkeeper signed his own crossing -- made himself the fare so his passengers crossed free and unlisted. A man can balance a ledger by subtracting himself. The Fields recorded the crossing and lost the mercy in it. Gherghe kept the forged receipt, and did not use it lightly.

finalized_fields_the_shroud_weaver.title: The Shroud-Weaver
finalized_fields_the_shroud_weaver.summary: Fane weaves shrouds for the rows, one particular flaw into each so the wearer stays too specific to file cleanly. Her loom-thread ran out and the shed-wails took the last spool. She needs it back before a plain shroud goes out.
finalized_fields_the_shroud_weaver.offer: I weave the shrouds that go over the rows. Everyone thinks it is decency. It is defense. I work one flaw into every weave -- a dropped stitch, a wrong-turned knot, a thread that lies where no thread should -- because everything worn is woven, and everything woven can be told again, and a shroud with a stubborn flaw in it keeps the wearer too particular to file smooth. The wails dragged off my last spool. Get it back. A plain shroud went out this morning and I have not slept.
finalized_fields_the_shroud_weaver.progress: The moor-wails haunt the weaving-sheds and took the spool into the near rows. Retrieve it, and thin them if they will not give it up.
finalized_fields_the_shroud_weaver.complete: Got it. Good. The plain shroud -- I will go out tonight and work a flaw into it by lantern, a bad stitch where the account expects a clean edge. It is not much against what they are. A dropped stitch against a whole institution of closing. But the record wants everyone the same, and I make everyone a little wrong, and a little wrong is the only prayer that has ever worked out here. Take a bound one. I put your flaw in already.

objectives.text: Recover the spool from the weaving-shed wails
Retrieve the flawed-weave spool

finalized_fields_the_shroud_weaver.note: Fane weaves one deliberate flaw into every shroud -- a dropped stitch against a whole institution of closing. The record wants everyone the same; she makes everyone a little wrong. A little wrong is the only prayer that works in the Fields. She sleeps only after the flaw is in.

finalized_fields_the_ground_that_wont_co.title: The Ground That Won't Cool
finalized_fields_the_ground_that_wont_co.summary: The smith cannot temper -- the filing-yard ground never grows cold, so nothing he quenches sets true. He wants the reading taken at the warm patch to learn which symptom it is, and the shamblers cleared so he can work the far forge instead.
finalized_fields_the_ground_that_wont_co.offer: A smith needs cold to make hard. Heat the steel, quench it, let the ground pull the warmth down -- that is tempering, that is the whole craft. My yard never cools. The dirt sits fever-warm day and night, and every blade I quench comes up soft, comes up wrong, comes up owed. That is a node under my floor, I would bet my hands on it. Take the reading at the warm patch so I know what colour I am fighting. Then clear the far forge of shamblers so I can work ground that still knows how to be cold.
finalized_fields_the_ground_that_wont_co.progress: The warm patch sits under the filing-yard; the far forge stands choked with shamblers. Read the one, clear the other.
finalized_fields_the_ground_that_wont_co.complete: Green, you say. Thermal. A stone under my floor waking slow -- not orange yet, thank whatever still listens, not signalling, only warming. The far forge is cold enough to work and I made a true blade there this morning, first in a month. Dragonbone, old stock, kept for the day the near forge failed for good. That day is coming. Take it before it does. It will not go soft on you.

objectives.label: the warm patch under the filing-yard
objectives.text: Take the reading at the warm patch
Clear the shamblers from the far forge

finalized_fields_the_ground_that_wont_co.note: The reading came back green -- thermal, a stone under the smithy warming slow toward waking. Not orange, not yet signalling. Vintila works the far forge while it still knows how to be cold, and keeps one true blade against the day the near ground finishes its patient rising.

finalized_fields_no_fresh_starts.title: No Fresh Starts
finalized_fields_no_fresh_starts.summary: The innkeeper's yeast will not rise -- nothing new begins near a node, only settlements. He wants a live culture carried from the one shed the warmth hasn't reached, before the last of his mother's starter dies for good.
finalized_fields_no_fresh_starts.offer: My mother's bread-starter has risen in this house sixty years, hand to hand, and this month it lies flat and grey in the crock. It is not the flour. Nothing new begins near one of their nodes -- no starts, only settlements, only closings. There is a culture keeps alive in the cold-shed past the last filing-row, the one place the warmth has not reached. Carry me a live spoon of it, gently, warm against you. If the starter dies, so does the one thing in this house older than the wrongness.
finalized_fields_no_fresh_starts.progress: The living culture keeps in the cold-shed past the last filing-row. Carry a live spoon back, warm and unspilled, before it takes the chill.
finalized_fields_no_fresh_starts.complete: It took. Look -- it is rising, it is arguing with the crock the way live things argue. Sixty years and I nearly lost it to a fever in the ground. A node kills beginnings, and a rising loaf is nothing but a beginning that refuses to know better. My guests will smell bread tomorrow and forget for an hour where they are. That is worth more than gold out here. But take the gold too. And the poultice -- it is grown, not mixed, so it is a beginning as well. Keep beginning things.

objectives.label: the cold-shed past the last filing-row
objectives.text: Fetch the live culture from the cold-shed
Carry the living culture back warm

finalized_fields_no_fresh_starts.note: A node kills beginnings; a rising loaf is a beginning that refuses to know better. Ursu carried the last live culture from the one shed the warmth had not reached, and his mother's sixty-year starter argued with the crock again. The guests forgot, for an hour, where they were. Out here that is the whole of mercy.

finalized_fields_the_wail_that_kept_a_na.title: The Wail That Kept a Name
finalized_fields_the_wail_that_kept_a_na.summary: One moor-wail on the far rows cries a single word all night, and the watcher swears it is a name -- his aunt's, filed twenty years back. He wants six of the mute wails silenced so he can stand close enough to be sure of the one that speaks.
finalized_fields_the_wail_that_kept_a_na.offer: The wails out here do not say anything -- that is the point of them, sound with the meaning filed off. But one on the far rows cries the same shape all night, and I have stood near enough on three nights to be almost certain it is a name. My aunt's. Closed twenty years. The mute ones crowd it and drown it. Thin them -- six, the silent kind -- so I can stand at the speaking one and know. Do not silence that one. I have to hear it out.
finalized_fields_the_wail_that_kept_a_na.progress: Six mute wails crowd the far rows around the one that speaks. Silence the mute, and leave the speaker.
finalized_fields_the_wail_that_kept_a_na.complete: It is her name. I stood a foot from it and it wailed her name at me all the way to dawn, the same word her whole life reduced to one cried syllable. The account took everything else -- kept only the shape of what she was called, worn down to noise, and even that it could not make mean nothing. She is closed. But a closed thing that still cries a name is a receipt with a smudge on it, and a smudge is where you start. Take the token. Choose whose name it keeps. Choose out loud.

objectives.text: Silence the mute wails on the far rows

finalized_fields_the_wail_that_kept_a_na.note: One wail on the far rows still cries a name -- his aunt's, filed twenty years past, her whole life worn to one syllable the account could not empty. A closed thing that still speaks a name is a receipt with a smudge. A smudge is where you start.

finalized_fields_papers_point_home.title: The Papers Point Home
finalized_fields_papers_point_home.summary: Every loose paper in the filing-shed has turned to face one wall overnight, aligned like dust, pointing the way papers point toward the Archive. The clerk wants the alignment read and the far corner reached before the sheets decide he should follow them.
finalized_fields_papers_point_home.offer: Come look, and keep your feet on the floor. Every loose sheet in my shed lay where I left it last night. This morning they all face the north wall. Aligned. Same edge, same lean, like iron filings, like dust when the ground has made up its mind. Papers out here orient toward the Archive without being told, the way the drowned lean downstream. I want the corner they point to walked and read, because I have started wanting to walk there myself, and a clerk who follows his own papers does not come back to sign the day out.
finalized_fields_papers_point_home.progress: The aligned papers point to the far north corner of the filing-shed. Reach it and read what draws them, then come away.
finalized_fields_papers_point_home.complete: A blank tablet in the wall, cold as the rest is warm, humming so low you feel it in the fillings of your teeth. Not signalling -- receiving, drinking the direction of every paper in the district toward itself. I nailed a shroud over it and the sheets fell slack, went back to being only paper. It will pull again. Everything here points home eventually. But my shed is a shed tonight and not an arrow, and I signed the day out with my own hand. Take the lantern. Its light belongs to the dark; it will not wake anything by shining.

objectives.label: the far corner the papers point to
objectives.text: Read what draws the aligned papers

finalized_fields_papers_point_home.note: Every loose sheet in the shed turned north overnight, pointing the way the drowned lean downstream -- toward a blank tablet in the wall, cold and drinking direction. Iosif nailed a shroud over it and signed the day out by his own hand. Everything here points home eventually. He bought a night of being only a clerk.

finalized_fields_the_accord_survey.title: An Accord Survey of the Rows
finalized_fields_the_accord_survey.summary: The Accord wants the Fields surveyed -- how far the rows have grown since the last count -- and the last three surveyors were filed. The guard needs the boundary stone at the horizon-edge reached and read, and the wardens along the way put down.
finalized_fields_the_accord_survey.offer: The Accord signed the pause a thousand years back and has been maintaining it with paperwork ever since, which out here means surveys, which out here means dead surveyors. Three of mine walked to count how far the rows have crept and got counted instead. The boundary stone at the horizon-edge holds the last true figure. Reach it, read the figure, come back breathing. Put down the wardens between -- they file surveyors on sight, professional courtesy, one ledger to another. I will not send a fourth to die of arithmetic.
finalized_fields_the_accord_survey.progress: The boundary stone stands at the horizon-edge of the rows. Reach it and read the last count -- and put down the wardens that guard the arithmetic.
finalized_fields_the_accord_survey.complete: The rows have grown a full league since the last honest count. A league of new-filed, quietly, while the Accord filed reports about filing reports. That is the figure I send back, and they will read it, and they will schedule a committee, and the rows will grow a league more while the committee sits. But at least the number is true now, and true numbers are rare enough out here to be a kind of weapon. Take the plate. Riverset steel -- it was made where water still ran clean.

objectives.label: the boundary stone at the horizon-edge
objectives.text: Read the last true count at the boundary stone
Put down the wardens guarding the arithmetic

finalized_fields_the_accord_survey.note: The rows have crept a full league since the last honest count -- filed quietly while the Accord filed reports about filing. Aurel sent back a true figure, which out here is rare enough to be a weapon. A committee will convene. The rows will grow another league while it sits.

finalized_fields_the_morven_dossier.title: A Habit Worth Knowing
finalized_fields_the_morven_dossier.summary: A Morven agent works the Fields, and Cornel needs to know his rounds before the man decides Cornel is a loose end. He wants the watcher and the clerk pressed for the agent's habits -- for the Morven do not collect assumptions.
finalized_fields_the_morven_dossier.offer: There is a Morven man walking my district and I do not know his rounds, which means he knows mine, which means I am the one being surveyed. I will not be a loose end tied off in the rows. I need his habits -- when he moves, where he rests, when he drops his guard, if he ever does. Manea watches everything and admits to watching nothing. Iosif files everyone. Press them both, gently, and build me a true dossier. The Morven do not collect assumptions, and neither, tonight, will I.
finalized_fields_the_morven_dossier.progress: Watcher Manea and Clerk Iosif each hold a piece of the agent's pattern. Ask them both and build the dossier of his habits.
finalized_fields_the_morven_dossier.complete: Now that is a dossier. Rests at the third bell, always facing a door, never with his back to the water. Drops his guard once a night, when he thinks the shift has changed -- it has not; I changed the roster and told no one. I know when he is soft now, and a man who knows that does not need to use it, only to have it. That is the Morven trade: information is the only wall that holds out here. Take the dossier. Knowing is armor. Assuming is how they file you.

objectives.text: Press Watcher Manea for the agent's movements
Ask Clerk Iosif for the agent's filed pattern

finalized_fields_the_morven_dossier.note: Cornel built a true dossier on the Morven man walking his district -- rounds, rests, the one soft hour a night. A man who knows that need not use it, only hold it. Information is the only wall that holds in the Fields. Assuming is how they file you. He keeps the dossier, and the agent, unknowing, keeps walking.

finalized_fields_grown_not_mixed.title: Grown, Not Mixed
finalized_fields_grown_not_mixed.summary: The smith's apprentice took a warden's claw across the arm and the wound will not close -- it reads owed, like the water. Vintila needs the living poultice grown, not mixed, from culture at the cold-shed, because out here only a beginning can cure a closing.
finalized_fields_grown_not_mixed.offer: My apprentice caught a warden's claw and the cut sits there owed -- will not scab, will not close, sulks like a debt. Salve does nothing; a mixed thing is a dead thing and the wound wants no dead things in it. You need the poultice that is grown, not stirred -- a live culture off the cold-shed, alive and knowing it is needed. Fetch me the culture. The boy is nineteen and lies about his age to sound older and I will not have him filed before he is even done being wrong about that.
finalized_fields_grown_not_mixed.progress: The live culture keeps at the cold-shed cultures past the sheds. Bring a growing spoon back before it dies to the warmth.
finalized_fields_grown_not_mixed.complete: It took to the wound like it was glad of the work -- grown things want to grow, that is the whole medicine. Scabbed by evening, the owed-taste gone out of it. Only a beginning cures a closing out here; a live cure against a debt. The boy is at the bellows again, lying about his age to the new girl, and I have never been so glad to hear a stupid lie. Take a poultice for the road. You will meet worse than a claw before the rows are done with you.

objectives.text: Grow the living poultice for the wound

finalized_fields_grown_not_mixed.note: A warden's claw leaves a wound that reads owed and will not close. Only a beginning cures a closing -- a live culture, grown not mixed, against a debt in the flesh. The apprentice healed and went back to lying about his age. Vintila had never been so glad of a stupid lie.

finalized_fields_the_first_edition.title: The First Edition
finalized_fields_the_first_edition.summary: The chronicler keeps the Fields' history in editions, and each new one is quietly wrong where the last was true -- corrected toward the Archive's version. He wants the first, unrevised edition recovered from the drowned shed before the correcting hand reaches it.
finalized_fields_the_first_edition.offer: History out here comes in editions, and each edition is paid to forget a little more than the last. I have watched my own true pages replaced with tidier lies between one binding and the next -- the record correcting itself toward the version the Archive prefers, which is the version with no one in it. The first edition still exists. Unrevised. Drowned in the old shed past the piers, which is the only reason it survived correction. Get it to me before the hand that edits reaches that far. Everything the later editions were paid to forget is in that one book.
finalized_fields_the_first_edition.progress: The first edition lies in the drowned shed past the piers. Recover it before the correcting hand reaches that far.
finalized_fields_the_first_edition.complete: It is all here. The names the later editions rounded off. The famine that was engineered and the edition that called it weather. The girl who lied about trout, written down while she still lived, a whole warm useless paragraph the corrected editions cut to a line and then to a number and then to nothing. I will copy it by hand, wrong on purpose in one place so no clean copy can be made from mine. Read the draught -- it tastes of every edition at once. Drink it and remember what you are not supposed to.

objectives.label: the drowned shed past the piers
objectives.text: Recover the first, unrevised edition
Carry off the first edition

finalized_fields_the_first_edition.note: Each edition of the Fields' history is paid to forget more than the last, correcting toward the version with no one in it. Danciu recovered the first, drowned and unrevised. He copies it by hand, wrong in one place on purpose. No clean copy can be made from a stubborn one.

finalized_fields_the_chalk_ward.title: The Chalk Ward
finalized_fields_the_chalk_ward.summary: The children of the Fields press chalk handprints on the shed-walls, one warm among the cold -- the old Hearth mark that keeps a child too particular to file. The wardens have been smearing them out. Emilian wants the prints re-laid and the smearing wardens stopped.
finalized_fields_the_chalk_ward.offer: You have seen the chalk handprints on the walls, maybe, and thought them children playing. They are the oldest ward there is -- brought from the Last Hearth by the ones who still remember hearths. One print in every row is warm; today it is a living child's, and a warm particular print is a child the record cannot smooth into a number. The wardens have been walking the walls and smearing them out. Re-lay the prints where they were rubbed. Put down the wardens doing the rubbing. A child's hand is not much of a wall. It is the only one they have.
finalized_fields_the_chalk_ward.progress: The smeared handprints line the shed-walls of the near rows. Re-lay the chalk, and put down the wardens rubbing them out.
finalized_fields_the_chalk_ward.complete: Warm again, all along the wall, small hands in chalk where the cold hands tried to file them off. One print is warmer than the others; it always is, and today it is little Petru's, three years old and afraid of the wardens the way he ought to be. A ward that means one particular child cannot be generalized into a row. It is not much. But my girl's row is out east, unwarded and smooth, and I will not lose a second child to a clean line. Take the ward. It is warm today. Tomorrow it is someone else's. That is how it keeps working.

objectives.label: the smeared handprint wall
objectives.text: Re-lay the chalk handprints on the wall
Stop the wardens smearing the prints

finalized_fields_the_chalk_ward.note: The chalk handprints are the oldest ward there is, carried from the Last Hearth: one warm among the cold, a particular child the record cannot smooth into a number. The wardens smear them; Emilian re-lays them. A child's hand is not much of a wall. In the Fields it is the only one there is.

finalized_fields_the_veil_of_thinning.title: The Veil of the Thinning
finalized_fields_the_veil_of_thinning.summary: Where the rows meet the Orange Fog the veil between filed and unfiled thins, and things cross that should stay closed. The merchant needs the thinning-place reached and read, and the wails that gather at the seam scattered before the fog learns the way in.
finalized_fields_the_veil_of_thinning.offer: West edge of the rows, where the Fields run up against the Orange Fog, the veil goes thin -- the wall between what is filed and what is still drifting wears through like a coat at the elbow. Things cross there that should stay closed, and the fog past it reads shifting orange, which is the one colour you never want a place to be. I stock a veil against exactly this -- wards fear, wards attention, wards the notice of closed things. But I need the seam read to charge it true, and the wails scattered from the crossing. Go to the thinning. Do not look into the orange. It looks back, and it takes notes.
finalized_fields_the_veil_of_thinning.progress: The thinning-place lies at the west seam where the rows meet the Orange Fog. Read the seam and scatter the wails that gather there.
finalized_fields_the_veil_of_thinning.complete: Read and warded. The veil is charged against the thinning now -- it wards fear and entrancement and the attention of the closed, and it does it by refusing to mean anything the fog can read. The orange did look at me while you worked. I felt it take a note. But a note is not an entry, not yet, and a veil that signifies nothing gives it nothing to file. Wear it west of here, if you are fool enough to go west of here. Everyone is, eventually. The fog is patient and the Fields lean toward it.

objectives.label: the thinning-place at the fog-seam
objectives.text: Read the seam where the veil wears thin
Scatter the wails at the crossing

finalized_fields_the_veil_of_thinning.note: West of the rows the veil wears thin where the Fields meet the Orange Fog, and things cross that should stay closed. The fog reads shifting orange, and it took a note while the seam was read. A note is not yet an entry. The veil wards by meaning nothing. The fog is patient.

finalized_fields_the_hearth_sign.title: The Hearth Hex-Sign
finalized_fields_the_hearth_sign.summary: The innkeeper wants a hex-sign painted over his door the old Hearth way -- slowly, in the rain, so the wet can teach it patience. He needs the pigment ground from row-clay and the shambler that guards the good clay-bank put down.
finalized_fields_the_hearth_sign.offer: I want a hex-sign over my door like my grandmother kept -- not for luck, she would have cuffed me for saying luck. For manners. A sign painted slow, in the rain, so the wet can teach it patience, tells the wrongness this is a threshold and not a column to be totted up. The good painting-clay is in the bank by the near rows, and a shambler has taken to standing on it the way they stand on everything worth having. Put it down and dig me a measure of clay. I will paint the sign tonight if the sky is kind enough to weep.
finalized_fields_the_hearth_sign.progress: The clay-bank sits by the near rows under a standing shambler. Put it down and dig a measure of good row-clay.
finalized_fields_the_hearth_sign.complete: Painted it slow in last night's drizzle, the way she taught me, and it came up patient and plain over the door. It means nothing anyone can read, which is the entire point -- a threshold that refuses to be a line-item. The rain will keep teaching it as long as it hangs there. Guests touched it going in without knowing why and slept the better for it. Take the spare sign. Hang it wherever you rest. A door that knows it is a door is worth a night's peace out here, and nights are all we sell.

objectives.text: Clear the shambler off the clay-bank
Dig row-clay and paint the hex-sign

finalized_fields_the_hearth_sign.note: Ursu painted the hex-sign slow in the drizzle, the way his grandmother taught -- a threshold that refuses to be a line-item, patient because the rain keeps teaching it. Guests touched it going in and slept the better. A door that knows it is a door is worth a night. Nights are all the Fields sell.

finalized_fields_the_soothing_knot.title: The Soothing Knot
finalized_fields_the_soothing_knot.summary: Fane ties a soothing knot for every warden she can name before it is put down -- a small forbidden decency the regulations forbid twice over. She wants the naming-cord's thread gathered from the wails and one row of wardens named and laid to rest gently.
finalized_fields_the_soothing_knot.offer: There is a woman down south, Ilka, ties a soothing knot for every shell she can put a name to before it is laid down. Regulations forbid the naming and forbid the knot -- both, separately, they thought of it twice. I do it anyway, out here, quietly. A warden was someone once; the account took the someone and left the walking. If I can name even one before it is put down, and tie the knot, then for a breath it is a person again and not a line. Gather the cord-thread off the wails -- they hoard it -- and help me name and lay one row to rest. Gently. It is the whole point that it is gentle.
finalized_fields_the_soothing_knot.progress: The wails on the near rows hoard the cord-thread; a row of wardens waits to be named and laid down. Gather the thread, and give them the gentle end.
finalized_fields_the_soothing_knot.complete: We named four of the six -- an ostler, a barge-girl, a man who mended nets, a name I did not finish and will not guess at, because a guessed name is a lie and the record is made of lies enough. Tied a knot for each named one. It changes nothing the ledger would recognise. But for the breath before I laid them down they were people with trades and not entries, and the account cannot get that breath back now, it is spent, it is spent on them. Take the knot. Tie it for someone. Name them first, out loud, or do not tie it at all.

objectives.text: Gather the cord-thread the wails hoard
Name and lay the row of wardens to rest

finalized_fields_the_soothing_knot.note: Fane named four of six wardens before laying them down -- an ostler, a barge-girl, a net-mender, one name unfinished because a guessed name is a lie. For the breath before the end each was a person and not an entry, and the account cannot get that breath back. It is spent on them.

finalized_fields_the_marked_map.title: One Road Drawn Wrong
finalized_fields_the_marked_map.summary: The dockmaster keeps a map of the barge-routes with one road drawn wrong on purpose, so no clean copy can be lifted from it. Today's decoy needs walking to confirm the true way still runs, before the tide re-files the channels.
finalized_fields_the_marked_map.offer: Every map I hand out has one road drawn wrong on purpose -- an old border trick, Vlaicu's own, keeps anyone from lifting a clean copy off my desk. Trouble is I must walk the true route now and again to be sure the wrong one is still the only lie on the page. The channels re-file with the tide out here; a right road can go wrong on its own overnight, and then the lie is telling the truth and I have no lie left. Walk today's marked way to the pier-head and back. Tell me the true road still runs where I say it does.
finalized_fields_the_marked_map.progress: Today's marked route runs out to the pier-head along the canal. Walk it and confirm the true way still holds.
finalized_fields_the_marked_map.complete: Still true, good. The lie is still a lie and the road is still a road, which out here is a fine morning's news. Tide will have another go tomorrow -- it always does, the channels never stop re-filing themselves. Here is a map for your own use, with the wrong road already marked. Learn which one it is. A man who knows his own lie is a man nobody can copy, and out here being uncopyable is the closest thing to being safe.

objectives.label: the pier-head at the canal mouth
objectives.text: Walk today's marked route to the pier-head

finalized_fields_the_marked_map.note: Gherghe walks the marked route to keep his one deliberate lie the only lie on the page -- the channels re-file with the tide, and a right road can turn wrong overnight. A man who knows his own lie cannot be copied. Out here, uncopyable is as close to safe as the ground allows.

finalized_fields_a_night_on_the_rows.title: A Night's Watch on the Rows
finalized_fields_a_night_on_the_rows.summary: Someone must stand the night-watch on the filing-rows and thin whatever wanders off its line, so the town wakes to the same number of rows it slept beside. The watcher shares the vigil nightly with whoever is brave or fool enough.
finalized_fields_a_night_on_the_rows.offer: I stand the rows every night so the town does not have to know how the rows behave at night. They wander, some of them -- drift off their filed line and go looking, and a warden that goes looking is a warden building a new row somewhere it was not meant to be. Take a lantern and the far length with me tonight. Thin what wanders. Keep the count. If the town wakes to the same number of rows it slept beside, that is the whole of the job, and it is never once been an easy one.
finalized_fields_a_night_on_the_rows.progress: The far length of the filing-rows is tonight's watch. Thin the wanderers -- wardens and wails both -- and keep the count true till dawn.
finalized_fields_a_night_on_the_rows.complete: Same number of rows this morning as last night. That is a win out here, the only kind on offer: nothing got worse while you watched. You held the count and the count held the town, and neither of us had to learn what the wanderers were looking for. Do not think about it. That is advice, not idle talk -- the ones who think about what the rows want are the ones the rows want. Come again tomorrow if you can bear it. The rows are patient. So must we be.

objectives.text: Thin the wardens that wander off their line
Scatter the wails drifting the far length

finalized_fields_a_night_on_the_rows.note: The rows wander at night, drifting off their filed lines to go looking -- and a warden that goes looking builds a new row. Manea keeps the count so the town wakes to the same number it slept beside. Nothing got worse while he watched. Out here that is the only victory on offer, won again each dark.

finalized_fields_the_ledger_of_the_drown.title: The Ledger of the Drowned Quarter
finalized_fields_the_ledger_of_the_drown.summary: A cracked debt-tablet washed into the Fields still warm, and the ledger it belongs to sits half-closed in the drowned rows -- an account that cannot finish closing. The chronicler asks it be reached and read, for a debt that cannot close is the one shape the counter takes.
finalized_fields_the_ledger_of_the_drown.offer: A tablet came up the canal into the rows last week, cracked hairline down the middle, and warm -- still warm, the way only an account not yet closed stays warm. It belongs to a ledger out in the drowned rows that has been trying to finish closing for a year and cannot. Every night it stamps the last line and every morning the line is open again. I have read enough of the world to be afraid of what that means, and to need to know it anyway. Reach the ledger. Read why it will not close. A debt that cannot be finalized is either a flaw in the machine -- or the flaw is a door.
finalized_fields_the_ledger_of_the_drown.progress: The half-closed ledger sits in the drowned rows where the tablet washed from. Reach it and read why the account will not close.
finalized_fields_the_ledger_of_the_drown.complete: It will not close because the name on it is a dead man's who keeps signing. A grave-debt, recorded collected, observably still writing -- an entry the ledger cannot balance because he crossed his own definition out and will not stay defined. Stamped, un-stamped, stamped, forever, a smudge that will not resolve. I copied the crack, not the seal. The counter is not a weapon you swing; it is a thing you refuse to finish being. Take the ledger. Every crossing has a price. This one collected it, and kept the change, and is still, somehow, walking.

objectives.label: the half-closed ledger in the drowned rows
objectives.text: Read why the account will not close

finalized_fields_the_ledger_of_the_drown.note: The tablet washed up warm because its account never finished closing -- a dead man's debt, recorded collected, observably still signing his own line. He crossed out his own definition and will not stay defined. The counter is not a weapon you swing; it is a thing you refuse to finish being. Danciu copied the crack, and not the seal.

finalized_fields_the_writ_and_the_wall.title: The Writ and the Wall
finalized_fields_the_writ_and_the_wall.summary: Iosif means to serve the Writ of Un-Filing on his own account before the correcting hand closes it, and needs the blank tablet in the far rows -- the receiving-wall itself -- reached and shrouded so his stamp has somewhere true to land.
finalized_fields_the_writ_and_the_wall.offer: You brought me the writ, and I have carried it in my coat and not used it, because a writ served wrong is worse than none. To un-file an account you must serve it where the filing happens -- at the receiving-wall, the blank tablet out in the far rows that drinks direction and closes lines. It is warded now; I mean to shroud it, serve my own writ against my own closing, and walk away an open account on purpose. Come with me. If it takes me mid-service, you finish the stamp. Stamped, un-stamped -- there is no third impression on this one. Make sure of that.
finalized_fields_the_writ_and_the_wall.progress: The receiving-wall stands in the far rows, drinking direction. Reach it, shroud it, and see the writ served -- one impression, no third.
finalized_fields_the_writ_and_the_wall.complete: Served. The wall took the shroud and the writ went down un-stamped over my line, and I felt the account let go of me the way a hook lets go of a lip -- not gently, but it let go. I am open now. Pending, on purpose, un-filed by my own hand, a clerk who used the system's one honest form against the system. It will try to close me again; it always tries. But there is no third impression on this one, and I made sure. Keep the writ. There will be other open accounts worth keeping open. You know where I file mine now. Nowhere. On purpose.

objectives.label: the receiving-wall in the far rows
objectives.text: Serve the writ at the receiving-wall
Hold off the wardens during the service

finalized_fields_the_writ_and_the_wall.note: Iosif served the Writ of Un-Filing on his own account -- shrouded the receiving-wall, stamped his line un-stamped, and felt the hook let go of the lip. He is open now, pending on purpose, a clerk who turned the system's one honest form against it. It will try to close him again. There is no third impression. He made sure.

angel_wings_romance_ioana_1.title: A Letter the Reeve Will Read
angel_wings_romance_ioana_1.summary: Ioana keeps the stones of Angel Wings; Tinca the Reeve keeps the debts. Across a courtyard neither can cross without the other minding, a chronicler decides to write to a woman who only reads ledgers.
angel_wings_romance_ioana_1.offer: The Reeve and I have shared one wall for eleven years and not one word that wasn't business. She won't come to me -- her post is her post, she'd sooner die than leave the ledger unwatched. And I won't be seen crossing to her. So I've written her a letter. Carry it, would you. Put it in her hand, not the box. The box, she reads. A hand, she has to answer.
angel_wings_romance_ioana_1.progress: Tinca stands her post by the tithe-table, as she has every day since the old queen. Give her the letter yourself.
angel_wings_romance_ioana_1.complete: She read it twice. I watched from here -- she read it twice, and then she folded it small, the way you fold a thing you mean to keep. Eleven years of ledger between us and I've finally written something she can't record. Tell me what her face did. No -- don't. Let me have it wrong and kinder.

objectives.text: Fetch oak-gall ink for the fair copy
Put the letter in Tinca's hand

angel_wings_romance_ioana_1.note: Ioana writes what the town forgets. She has never once written to be answered. The Reeve folded the page small, and kept it. Angel Wings records debts. This one it will not.

angel_wings_romance_ioana_2.title: What the Ledger Cannot Hold
angel_wings_romance_ioana_2.summary: The Reeve answered -- in the only hand she owns, dry as a tithe-line. Ioana means to answer the dryness with warmth and needs a page clean enough to be worth Tinca's careful eye.
angel_wings_romance_ioana_2.offer: She wrote back. In her ledger-hand, mind -- 'received, and noted, and not unwelcome.' Not unwelcome. From Tinca that's a bonfire, traveler. I mean to write past her guard this time. But the rats have got into the record-house again, and I can't compose a love-line with them chewing the town's memory behind me. Clear them. Then I'll write her something she can't file.
angel_wings_romance_ioana_2.progress: The plague-rats nest in the record-house cellar, under the shelves where the old rolls are kept. Thin them, and bring me clean linen for the page.
angel_wings_romance_ioana_2.complete: There -- the record-house holds another year, and so do I. I've written it plain: that a woman who guards a ledger against the whole hungering world might let one person past the table. She'll read it at her post. She reads everything at her post. Maybe this once she'll look up.

objectives.text: Clear the rats from the record-house
Gather clean linen for the letter

angel_wings_romance_ioana_2.note: Not unwelcome, the Reeve wrote, and thought she had given nothing away. Ioana has spent her life reading what people meant to keep. She read that.

angel_wings_romance_ioana_3.title: Two Hands, One Page
angel_wings_romance_ioana_3.summary: The Reeve will not leave her post; the chronicler will not stop writing to her. So Ioana proposes the only union two women wedded to their duties can keep -- a shared table where the ledger and the chronicle lie open side by side.
angel_wings_romance_ioana_3.offer: She'll never leave that table. And I'd never ask her to -- the town needs its debts watched as much as it needs remembering. So I asked her the only thing I could: let me bring my chronicle to your post. Let the ledger and the record sit one page apart, and us behind them. She sent no answer. She sent a second chair. Fetch me candles, friend -- good ones, tallow that burns clean. We'll be reading late.
angel_wings_romance_ioana_3.progress: Buy clean tallow candles from Anca at the market, and carry Ioana's writing-desk across to the Reeve's table by the tithe-hall.
angel_wings_romance_ioana_3.complete: The chair fit. That's the whole of it and it's everything. Her ledger on her side, my chronicle on mine, one candle between so neither of us wastes wax being proud. She still writes 'noted' in the margin of my sentences. I've started writing 'kept' in hers. Angel Wings will outlast the stone by exactly this -- two stubborn women and a table they won't leave.

objectives.text: Buy clean-burning tallow candles
Set the second chair at the Reeve's table

objectives.label: the shared table
objectives.text: Carry the desk to the tithe-hall

angel_wings_romance_ioana_3.note: The Reeve records debts and waits. She has begun, in her margins, to record something that is not owed. Ioana writes 'kept' beside it. The candle burns clean, and low, and does not go out.

angel_wings_romance_safta_1.title: The Innkeeper's Standing Order
angel_wings_romance_safta_1.summary: Downriver at Riverfork, an innkeeper named Profira orders wormwood by the letter and pays in words she has no business including. Safta, who smells of tallow and wormwood, has started answering in kind.
angel_wings_romance_safta_1.offer: There's an innkeeper down at Riverfork -- Profira -- orders my wormwood and marsh-lichen by the season, always has. Only lately her orders have grown. A line about the river. A line about her knees in the cold. A line, last month, about how the man who carries my parcels has a kind face and she hopes I do too. I am forty-six and I smell of wormwood, traveler. Nobody flirts with me by ledger. Gather the lichen. I'll send her more than she paid for.
angel_wings_romance_safta_1.progress: Marsh-lichen grows where the Vein slows, on the wet stones east of the wall. Bring me enough to fill Profira's order -- and a little over.
angel_wings_romance_safta_1.complete: There. The order, and a twist of dried mint she didn't ask for, and a line of my own -- that a woman who writes about her knees to her apothecary is either very ill or very lonely, and I'd treat either. Let's see which she admits. Carry it careful. It's a long way down the water, and I've put more in that parcel than lichen.

objectives.text: Gather marsh-lichen by the slow Vein
Buy clean salt to pack the parcel

angel_wings_romance_safta_1.note: Safta trades in poultice, purge, and poison, all the same to her. She has never before packed a parcel with a line of her own in it. The river runs down to Riverfork, slow, and carries it.

angel_wings_romance_safta_2.title: Answered by the Same Water
angel_wings_romance_safta_2.summary: Profira wrote back, and did not talk about her knees. The apothecary means to send a remedy no innkeeper truly needs -- a cordial for sleeping easy -- and it wants a river-pearl the delta only grudgingly gives.
angel_wings_romance_safta_2.offer: She wrote back. Not one word about her knees -- all about the inn, the fog on the water, the empty chair by her good fire that used to be her husband's. Three husbands buried, that one. I know the kind. I'm going to send her a sleeping-cordial, the good one, the one that costs me a river-pearl I can't spare. She sleeps badly, she said, in a house full of other people's rest. Bring me a pearl from the delta shallows. I'll make her a night that's hers.
angel_wings_romance_safta_2.progress: River-pearls sit in the delta shallows where the drowners nest. Take what you can from them, and gather luminous-lichen to seal the vial so it keeps the long way down.
angel_wings_romance_safta_2.complete: A pearl. Good. Half a life's savings dissolved into a bottle for a woman I've never stood in a room with. My mother would call me a fool. My mother married a man she'd met twice and stayed happy forty years, so I don't rate her counsel highly. Here -- the cordial, and a note: sleep well, Profira, in the chair that's yours. Carry it dry.

objectives.text: Take river-pearls from the delta drowners
Gather luminous-lichen to seal the vial

angel_wings_romance_safta_2.note: Three husbands under the Riverfork stones, and the chair still empty. Safta spent a river-pearl on a stranger's sleep. The delta gives its pearls grudgingly. She gave hers gladly.

angel_wings_romance_safta_3.title: The Meeting at the Ferry
angel_wings_romance_safta_3.summary: A season of parcels, and neither woman has seen the other's face. Profira has named a day at the grey ferry-landing between their towns. Safta, who has not left Angel Wings in nine years, means to be there.
angel_wings_romance_safta_3.offer: She's named a day. The ferry-landing, where the water divides -- halfway, so neither of us has to be the one who traveled further. Nine years I've not passed that wall, traveler. Nine years of other people's ailments and my own door. And now a woman I know only by her hand is standing at a landing in three days' time, and I find I'd walk to the sea for her. Help me close the shop right. And -- there's a thing I'd carry to her. Not medicine. Bring me river-pearls enough to string.
angel_wings_romance_safta_3.progress: Gather what pearls you can for the string, then walk with Safta down the water road to the ferry-landing between the towns.
angel_wings_romance_safta_3.complete: She was shorter than her letters. That's the first thing I thought -- all that warmth in a woman I could tuck under my arm. She'd strung her own pearls too. We stood there holding two strings and laughing like fools while the ferryman waited. I've a shop in Angel Wings and she's an inn in Riverfork and neither of us is young. But there's a ferry, traveler. It runs both ways. We've decided the water is not so wide.

objectives.text: Gather river-pearls to string
Meet Profira at the ferry-landing

objectives.label: the grey ferry-landing
objectives.text: Reach the landing between the towns

angel_wings_romance_safta_3.note: Two women who knew each other only by carried water met halfway on it, each holding pearls the other could not see them string. The ferry runs both ways. They have decided the river is a road, not a wall.

riverfork_romance_dragan_1.title: A Letter the Smith Won't Send
riverfork_romance_dragan_1.summary: Dragan of Riverfork and Zamfira of Angel Wings learned the forge from the same dead master and have quarreled by letter for thirty years. Now word comes upriver that Zamfira's lungs are failing. Dragan has written a letter he cannot make himself send.
riverfork_romance_dragan_1.offer: You'll think me a hard old fool. There's a smith up in Angel Wings -- Zamfira. We learned the fire from the same master, her and me, forty years back, and we've argued the right way to draw steel by letter ever since. Cruel letters. Fond ones. Same thing, with smiths. Now they tell me her lungs have gone, the way ours all go -- coal and years. I've written to her. I've written and torn it up eleven times. I can't send it. My hands shake and it isn't the cold. Take it. Before there's no one to take it to.
riverfork_romance_dragan_1.progress: Zamfira keeps the Angel Wings forge, or keeps to her bed beside it now. Carry Dragan's letter up the water road and put it in her hand.
riverfork_romance_dragan_1.complete: You gave it to her. Good. That's -- good. Thirty years I've had the last word in every argument by making her wait a week for my answer. Now the week is the enemy. Did she read it herself, or did someone read it to her? ...Don't tell me. If someone had to read it to her, I'd rather carry that not-knowing than the knowing. Come back when she's answered. If she answers.

objectives.text: Gather iron-scrap to weight the seal
Carry Dragan's letter to Zamfira

riverfork_romance_dragan_1.note: Two smiths, one dead master, thirty years of arguing the fire by letter. Dragan wrote it eleven times and burned ten. The eleventh went up the water. His hands shook, and it was not the cold.

riverfork_romance_dragan_2.title: What the Fire Kept
riverfork_romance_dragan_2.summary: Zamfira answered -- weaker in the hand, sharper in the words. She has a last thing to argue and a last thing to ask. Dragan cannot leave his fire; the delta needs its only forge. So the letters carry what the smiths cannot.
riverfork_romance_dragan_2.offer: She wrote back. The hand's gone shaky as mine but the tongue's still a file -- told me I've drawn a tang wrong my whole life and she'd not die letting me think otherwise. Then, under it, small: she asked what our master's grave looked like now. She's not seen it in twenty years. Can't travel. Nor can I -- Riverfork's got one forge and it's mine, and a delta with no smith is a delta that starves. So. Go to the old master's grave. Tell me what grows there. I'll write it to her true.
riverfork_romance_dragan_2.progress: The old master's grave lies in the famine ground between the towns, where the field-work turns dangerous. See it, and gather grave-silk from the stones to send her a true token of it.
riverfork_romance_dragan_2.complete: Moss on the west face, you say, and the iron marker she forged him rusted through but standing. She'll want it exactly so -- she forged half that marker herself, the halves that meet still. I've written it all down for her, every rust-spot, honest. And I told her the tang thing -- she's right. Forty years I knew she was right and argued anyway, because the arguing was the having of her. Take it up. Quickly now.

objectives.text: Clear the wights from the grave-ground
Gather grave-silk from the master's stone

riverfork_romance_dragan_2.note: The marker they forged him together still stands, the joined halves holding where the rest gave way. Dragan wrote her the grave true, rust-spot by rust-spot. Then he told her she was right. Forty years he had argued only to keep arguing with her.

riverfork_romance_dragan_3.title: The Last Answer
riverfork_romance_dragan_3.summary: Zamfira's last letter asks for one thing: not that Dragan come, but that he finish, at his own fire, the blade she began and cannot. It is to lie with her, forged by two hands across a river neither could cross in time.
riverfork_romance_dragan_3.offer: Her hand's failed. Someone writes for her now, and she'll only send what she'd say plain. She's asked one thing. Not that I come -- she forbade it, said a delta with no smith kills more than a river between two old fools ever could. She sent me a blade half-drawn. Her half. She hasn't the breath to finish it, and she'll not have it finished by a stranger. Bring me good fuel, the clean-burning coal -- ember-dust, not slag. I'll draw her half into mine. It's to go in the ground with her. My hands are steady for this. For this, they're steady.
riverfork_romance_dragan_3.progress: Gather clean ember-dust for a true fire, then stand with Dragan at his forge while he finishes Zamfira's blade -- the last answer he will send her.
riverfork_romance_dragan_3.complete: It's done. Her taper into my point, the weld true -- you'd not find the seam, and she'd hate that, she'd want to see exactly where her work ended and mine began. Too late to argue it now. The courier leaves at first light for Angel Wings. She'll not read this letter; she'll hold it. That's more than the Courier in the old chamber ever managed -- his satchel's still sealed, they say, forty years, undelivered. Mine gets there. Mine gets there. Go on, then. I want to bank the fire alone.

objectives.text: Gather clean ember-dust for the fire

objectives.label: Dragan's forge
objectives.text: Stand at the forge for the last welding
See the finished blade sent upriver

riverfork_romance_dragan_3.note: Two smiths, one river, one blade -- her taper welded into his point, the seam invisible she'd have wanted to see. She will not read the last letter. She will hold it. The old Courier's satchel is sealed still, undelivered. This one arrived. That was the whole of the love: it arrived in time.

iron_vein_romance_dragan_1.title: The Bowl She Keeps Warm
iron_vein_romance_dragan_1.summary: For nine winters Dragan has kept one bowl warm past close for Lupu the peat-cutter, and never once told him why. She would rather send a stranger to fetch him in from the cold than say it herself.
iron_vein_romance_dragan_1.offer: You'll have noticed I talk to keep from saying things. Here's a thing I won't say: there's a peat-cutter, Lupu, works the black bog dark to dark, and every dusk for nine winters I've had a bowl warm for him whether he came or not. He's late tonight. Cold's getting into the valley. Go tell him the Bent Oar's still open -- and don't tell him I sent you fretting. Tell him it's just the last bowl.
iron_vein_romance_dragan_1.progress: Lupu cuts peat out past the marker on the black bog. Carry him word the hearth is lit -- and, if the beds are fouled, clear him a path home.
iron_vein_romance_dragan_1.complete: He came in. Sat where he always sits. Ate the bowl and said the bread was better than it's been, which is a kind lie, and I let him keep it. Nine winters of the last bowl, and tonight a stranger walked it out to him for me. That's further than I've carried it myself. Take this -- the house's own luck. It's kept the door warm this long.

objectives.text: Carry word to Lupu on the black bog
Clear the peat-beds on his way in

iron_vein_romance_dragan_1.note: Nine winters of a bowl kept warm and never named. Tonight it was carried the last stretch by a stranger's hand. Dragan talks to keep from saying things. She said nothing, and set two bowls out.

iron_vein_romance_dragan_2.title: A Door That Sticks
iron_vein_romance_dragan_2.summary: The Bent Oar's back door has hung crooked for years. Lupu keeps meaning to mend it; Dragan keeps meaning to let him. Neither has managed the asking. She would like the makings gathered, so the offer is easier to make.
iron_vein_romance_dragan_2.offer: The back door's hung crooked since the flood-year and every dusk Lupu eyes it like it owes him money. He'd fix it in an hour. He won't offer and I won't ask, and so it sticks, and we both go on pretending the wind did it. Bring me warm bog-iron for a new hinge-pin and a fistful of grave-lichen to pack the frame -- give a body the makings and the asking gets small enough to do.
iron_vein_romance_dragan_2.progress: Warm bog-iron comes from the seeps past the marker; the packing-lichen grows on the cold hearth-stones downriver. Gather both, and put down what fouls the beds.
iron_vein_romance_dragan_2.complete: I set the iron and the lichen on the workbench where he'd see it, and said not one word. He looked at it a long while. Then he took off his coat and hung it on the peg -- his peg, nine winters and I never called it that aloud -- and mended the door before his bowl went cold. It shuts true now. First quiet it's kept in years.

objectives.text: Gather warm bog-iron for the hinge-pin
Scrape grave-lichen to pack the frame
Clear the seeps of bog-widows

iron_vein_romance_dragan_2.note: He hung his coat on his own peg and mended the door that had stuck since the flood-year. Dragan said nothing, which for her is a confession. The door shuts true. Everything good is temporary; that is what makes it good.

iron_vein_romance_dragan_3.title: The Long Bog Night
iron_vein_romance_dragan_3.summary: Lupu has not come in. The ground past the marker has gone warm in patches, and the dead of the black bog are walking earlier each dusk. For the first time in nine winters, Dragan puts the fear into words -- to a stranger, because she cannot to him.
iron_vein_romance_dragan_3.offer: He's not come in. Bowl's gone cold twice over and the candle's shorter than the night, and I -- listen. The ground out past the marker's warm now, warm where it's got no business being, and folk who go stand in it stop coming back for supper. I have kept a bowl for that man nine winters and never once said the word for what he is to me, and if the bog takes him tonight I never will. Find him. Bring him in. I am done being careful.
iron_vein_romance_dragan_3.progress: Lupu works the far cuttings where the ground runs warm. Reach him before the bog's dead do, and bring down whatever walks between him and the hearth.
iron_vein_romance_dragan_3.complete: You found him standing in the warm patch, head tilting, the way the listeners stand -- and you brought him back before it finished with him. He's by the fire now, shivering the wrong kind of cold out of him. I took his hand. Nine winters and I took his hand and said the word. He said he'd known since the second winter and was only waiting on me. Fool man. Fool me. Take the charm -- it kept the rooks off this door. Let it keep something off you.

objectives.label: the far cuttings
objectives.text: Reach Lupu in the warm ground
Put down the bog's dead between him and home
Bring Lupu back to the hearth

iron_vein_romance_dragan_3.note: She took his hand and said the word she had kept nine winters, and the warm ground did not get to keep him. He had known since the second winter. The bog counts what it takes in straight even lines. It has no line for two old fools by a fire.

iron_vein_romance_dragan_4.title: Two Bowls, One Hearth
iron_vein_romance_dragan_4.summary: There is to be no wedding -- Dragan says at their age a wedding is just an excuse for other people. There is only the moving of Lupu's few things under one roof, and a plain supper for the handful who ever kept them warm in return.
iron_vein_romance_dragan_4.offer: No wedding. At our age a wedding's just an excuse for other people to drink your ale and cry about their own lives. What there is, is Lupu's cot and his kettle and his one good coat coming under this roof for good, and a supper after -- flat grey bread and all -- for the few who ever set a bowl out for us. Fetch the makings for the pot from Vasile, and word to Lupu it's tonight. He'll pretend he forgot. He forgets nothing.
iron_vein_romance_dragan_4.progress: Vasile keeps the provisions on the back-roads; Lupu waits by the cuttings, pretending. Bring the makings and the word, and the hearth is theirs.
iron_vein_romance_dragan_4.complete: It's done. His coat hangs beside mine, his kettle's found the hook it was always going to find, and we ate flat grey bread by a fire that's kept the same three of us warm for years. No stone carved, no vow shouted -- just two bowls, one hearth, and a door that shuts true. Let the ground stay warm out there. In here it's warm the right way, and I earned every inch of it slow. Sit. There's a bowl for you too. There always was.

objectives.text: Bring the makings for the pot from Vasile
Carry Lupu word it is tonight

iron_vein_romance_dragan_4.note: No vow was shouted and no stone was carved. His coat hangs beside hers; his kettle found its hook. The Bloodstone keeps its ledger in straight even lines. There is no entry for two bowls kept warm at one hearth, slow, across nine winters, on purpose.

vetka_romance_toma_1.title: The Names That Grow Shorter
vetka_romance_toma_1.summary: Toma keeps the Vetka stones and watches the graven names shorten year by year. One name he re-cuts by hand every spring: the wife of Iancu the smith. For eleven years he has done it, and never told Iancu who keeps her name whole.
vetka_romance_toma_1.offer: You know the trouble with this town? The names on the stones grow shorter every year -- letter by letter, as if the ground were forgetting on purpose. There's one I won't let it have. El:na, Iancu's wife, eleven years under the kerb, and every spring I re-cut her name deep before the season eats another letter. He doesn't know it's me. He thinks the stone just wears slow. Bring me fresh oak-gall ink and a good marsh-lichen to grind, and I'll cut her whole again before the thaw.
vetka_romance_toma_1.progress: The ink-galls hang on the alders past the moor-marker; the grinding-lichen grows on the cold kerb-stones. Gather both, and clear the graveyard path of what the moor sends up.
vetka_romance_toma_1.complete: Deep-cut and whole for another year -- ELENA, every letter, the way the ground would rather I didn't. Eleven springs I've done this for a man who argues with me across the square about everything under heaven and doesn't know I keep his grief legible. I don't do it for thanks. I do it because a name is the last true thing, and this town eats them. There. That's my confession for the year, and I've made it to a stranger. Fitting.

objectives.text: Gather oak-gall ink for the re-cutting
Grind marsh-lichen for the graving-paste
Clear the graveyard path of the moor's dead

vetka_romance_toma_1.note: Eleven springs Toma has re-cut Elena's name deep before the ground could eat another letter -- and the man it is for does not know whose hand keeps his grief legible. A name is the last true thing. This town eats them. He does not.

vetka_romance_toma_2.title: What the Square Argues About
vetka_romance_toma_2.summary: Iancu the smith has found out who has been re-cutting his wife's name. Rather than thank Toma -- neither man knows how -- he has started leaving the chronicler mended tools on his step. The whole square pretends not to notice two old men courting by way of an argument.
vetka_romance_toma_2.offer: Iancu knows. Found chisel-marks fresh on Elena's stone this spring and followed them to me, and did the smith's version of gratitude -- which is to leave a re-hung chronicle-hinge on my step, unasked, and pick a fight about the weather so I can't mention it. Two lost fingers and no patience and he mends my things in the dark like a boy leaving flowers. I'd like to answer in kind. Bind me these keepsake-cords the way he binds a hilt, so my thanks is a thing his hands will know.
vetka_romance_toma_2.progress: The binding-cord and its makings are gathered from the herd-camps on the open moor. Iancu keeps his forge by the square. Bring the makings, then take Toma's answer to the smith.
vetka_romance_toma_2.complete: I bound the cords his way -- his own knot, the one he ties without looking -- and left them on the forge-stone with no note, because he leaves no notes. He turned them over a long while. Then he said the weather looked to hold, which from Iancu is a sonnet, and I said it did, which from me is another. Forty years this town has watched us argue across the square. It's just now working out we were doing something else the whole time.

objectives.text: Bind keepsake-cords the smith's own way
Leave Toma's answer on Iancu's forge-stone

vetka_romance_toma_2.note: Iancu mends the chronicler's things in the dark and picks fights so no thanks can be spoken; Toma answers in the smith's own knot. Forty years the square watched two men argue. It is only now working out they were doing something else the whole time.

vetka_romance_toma_3.title: One Roof Before Winter
vetka_romance_toma_3.summary: The cold comes hard to Vetka, and two old men heating two cold cottages is a waste neither will admit is really about the cold. Toma proposes the practical thing at last: one roof, one fire, before winter -- and lets the practicality carry what he cannot say plainly.
vetka_romance_toma_3.offer: Winter's coming down off the moor and I'll say the practical thing, because the true thing won't fit in my mouth: two old men heating two cold cottages is a waste, and my chimney draws better than his, and Elena's stone is a short walk from my door where we can both keep it whole. He'll pretend it's only sense. Let him. Warding-signs for the new lintel is what I need, and word carried to Iancu -- through Marcu, who's herded past both our doors forty years and earned the telling.
vetka_romance_toma_3.progress: The warding-signs are painted with materials from the heath-camps; Marcu herds the open moor and will carry the word kindly. Gather the makings, then send the telling through him.
vetka_romance_toma_3.complete: It's done, and done plainly. His anvil-tools hang in my back room, his boots are by my better-drawing fire, and Elena's stone is a short walk we take together now, both of us keeping her name whole against the hungry ground. No vow. Iancu would sooner re-forge his own hand than stand up and speak one. But he sleeps under my roof and argues with me over breakfast, and after forty years across a square, that argument is the nearest thing to a wedding-song this town will get. Good. It's ours.

objectives.text: Paint warding-signs for the shared lintel
Send the telling to Iancu through Marcu

vetka_romance_toma_3.note: No vow was spoken; Iancu would sooner re-forge his own hand. But his tools hang in Toma's back room, and they keep Elena's name whole together now. Forty years of arguing across a square, and it turns out that was the wedding-song. The ground eats names. It gets neither of theirs.

iron_vein_romance_grigore_1.title: The Letters Across the Water
iron_vein_romance_grigore_1.summary: Grigore keeps the ledger of the Iron Vein's dead, and for twelve years has kept something warmer besides: letters to Ilinca, Petru's sister across the water in Vetka, whom he has loved slowly and at a distance since before the river ran this wrong.
iron_vein_romance_grigore_1.offer: I keep the ledger of the dead, and it never balances -- but there's one column I've kept twelve years that balances fine. Letters. To Ilinca, the innkeeper Petru's sister, over in Vetka. We were young by this river once and neither of us ever said the whole of it, so we've said it slow, a page a season, twelve years of pages. My knees won't cross the bog anymore. Carry this one to Petru; he'll see it reaches her. And bring me fresh linen and ink -- a letter that far deserves good cloth to travel in.
iron_vein_romance_grigore_1.progress: Petru keeps the Vetka inn and sends word to his sister; the good linen and ink are gathered from the birch-flats between the towns. Carry the letter, and bring the makings for the next.
iron_vein_romance_grigore_1.complete: Petru took it careful, the way you hold a thing that matters to someone you're fond of. Twelve years of pages across a slow poisoned river, and every one of them still says the same thing under the weather-talk and the harvest-counts. I've never once written the word love. She's never once written it back. We both know what the letters are. Some things you keep so long the keeping is the saying. Thank you. It's a good cloth. She'll notice.

objectives.text: Carry Grigore's letter to Petru in Vetka
Gather good linen from the birch-flats
Gather oak-gall ink for the next page

iron_vein_romance_grigore_1.note: Twelve years of letters across a poisoned river, and neither has once written the word for it. Some things are kept so long the keeping becomes the saying. Grigore keeps the ledger of the dead. This is the one column that ever balanced.

iron_vein_romance_grigore_2.title: The Three Notes She Hums
iron_vein_romance_grigore_2.summary: Ilinca's latest letter is wrong. The hand wanders; she writes of three notes she cannot place and of the warm ground calling her to rest. Grigore knows the symptoms from his own ledger. He will not lose her to the stone without a fight -- an incurious charm, carried across the water.
iron_vein_romance_grigore_2.offer: Her letter came back changed. The hand wanders off the line, and she writes of three notes she keeps humming that nobody taught her, and of ground gone warm that she says feels like rest. I have written those symptoms in my ledger beside a hundred names. I will not write hers. There's a charm the herb-women make -- an incurious charm, it keeps a mind from leaning toward what wants reading. Bring me one, and a warding for the letter itself, and put down whatever's been walking the crossing. She is not becoming a number. Not while I have ink.
iron_vein_romance_grigore_2.progress: The incurious charm is gathered from the folk-healers; the letter-warding from the birch-flats. Clear the crossing of the changed dead, then bring both makings to Grigore.
iron_vein_romance_grigore_2.complete: It's sewn into the fold where she'll find it, with a page that for once does say the word -- because a man doesn't stay careful when the ground is calling the one he loves to rest. Twelve years I hummed no tune. Now I've caught the three notes too, from reading her pages too close. I burned that page unread past the first line. Ash tells no one anything. I'll keep writing her the ordinary weather. Ordinary is a rope. She'll hold it if I keep throwing.

objectives.text: Fetch an incurious charm from the healers
Gather wardings for the letter, face-down
Put down the changed dead at the crossing

iron_vein_romance_grigore_2.note: Her letters have caught the three notes; reading them too close, so has he. He burned the worst page unread past the first line -- ash tells no one anything -- and sewed an incurious charm into the fold. He keeps writing her the ordinary weather. Ordinary is a rope. He keeps throwing it.

iron_vein_romance_grigore_3.title: The Last Page He Will Not Read Aloud
iron_vein_romance_grigore_3.summary: Word comes from Vetka with Petru: Ilinca went out to the warm ground at dusk and did not come back for supper. There is a last letter, sealed, addressed to Grigore. He asks the player to carry a keepsake to her kerb, and to help him do the one thing left -- keep her a name the stone cannot shorten.
iron_vein_romance_grigore_3.offer: Petru came himself. He doesn't come to the Iron Vein for anyone. So I knew before he spoke. She went out to the warm ground at dusk, the way the listeners do, and she did not come back for supper, and they will not find a mark on her. There's a last letter. Sealed. To me. I have not opened it and I will not read it aloud, not ever -- some pages a man keeps unread so the tenant stays lit in them. Carry her keepsake-cord to Petru for the kerb. And bring me kept-name tokens, so I can grave her whole where the ground can't eat her.
iron_vein_romance_grigore_3.progress: Petru waits with the kerb in Vetka; the kept-name tokens are recovered from the warm ground where the listeners fell. Carry the keepsake, gather the tokens, and clear the way.
iron_vein_romance_grigore_3.complete: Her name is cut deep now on a token no ground can reach, ILINCA, every letter, laid in my ledger's one warm column beside twelve years of weather. The sealed letter sits under it, and there it stays -- if I never read the last page, the last page never ends. That's not sense. Grief isn't sense. She loved me slow across a dying river for twelve years and never wrote the word, and neither did I, and we both spent every page saying it. Keep the totem. It knows how to hold a name against forgetting. So do I. It's the only trade I ever learned.

objectives.text: Carry Ilinca's keepsake to Petru for the kerb
Gather kept-name tokens from the warm ground
Clear the listeners' ground of its dead

iron_vein_romance_grigore_3.note: Ilinca went to the warm ground and did not come back for supper. Grigore graved her name deep where no ground can eat it and left her last letter sealed forever -- if the last page is never read, it never ends. Twelve years, and neither once wrote the word. Every page was the word. The stone has no entry for that.

stonepath_romance_sava_1.title: The Cheaper Nail
stonepath_romance_sava_1.summary: Sava sells the same nails as her cousin Radomir the smith, for less, and the crossroads has taken sides. She wants proof his edge is no better than her price.
stonepath_romance_sava_1.offer: You want the truth of it? Radomir up at the forge charges double for a nail and calls it honesty. I charge half and call it Tuesday. Folk here can't buy a shoe without picking a side. Go stand at his anvil, hear him run me down -- I know he does it -- and bring me back exactly what the man said. Word for word. I like to know my enemy's temper before I sharpen mine.
stonepath_romance_sava_1.progress: Radomir keeps the forge on the north edge of the yard. Let him talk. He always does.
stonepath_romance_sava_1.complete: That's him, all right. 'The only honest thing left in The Stonepath.' He rehearses it, you know. Twenty years we've traded across the same square and he still practices insulting me. ...Say that back to me again. The bit about my eyes. He said that?

objectives.text: Hear out Radomir at the forge

stonepath_romance_sava_1.note: Sava wanted her rival's insults carried like coin. She counted them twice. One of them, she kept.
