stonepath_romance_sava_2.title: A Fair Weight
stonepath_romance_sava_2.summary: Radomir has told the yard Sava's iron is soft. She means to shame him with the truth: bring good scrap so he must forge with her stock and swallow his own tongue.
stonepath_romance_sava_2.offer: He's telling everyone my iron's soft. Soft. My grandmother's iron. So here's the game, traveler -- gather good scrap off the wild wood dead, the real stuff, and haul it to his fire. Make him forge with metal I sourced. If it holds an edge, he says so in front of the yard. If it doesn't... well. I'll owe you a drink and him an apology, and one of those has never once happened.
stonepath_romance_sava_2.progress: Salvage iron scrap from the raider-dead on the wild wood, then set it at Radomir's anvil.
stonepath_romance_sava_2.complete: He held it to the light a long while. Didn't say soft. Didn't say anything, which for Radomir is a sonnet. Then he asked who'd sourced it, and I said me, and he -- he almost smiled, traveler. I've made men weep over a price. I've never once made that man almost smile. It's unsettling. Take the ring; it's overstock.

objectives.text: Salvage good iron from the wild-wood dead
Set Sava's iron at Radomir's forge

stonepath_romance_sava_2.note: Her iron held the edge. He would not say soft again. She noticed the not-saying more than any praise.

stonepath_romance_sava_3.title: Two Stalls, One Roof
stonepath_romance_sava_3.summary: The autumn rains are coming and both their stalls stand open to the crossroads wind. Pavel proposes they share one roof for the season. Neither will say yes first.
stonepath_romance_sava_3.offer: Pavel at the inn had a notion, and I hate that it's a good one. One awning over both our stalls before the rains. Shared. His and mine. I said I'd rather freeze, which is a lie, and he knew it. Go to Pavel and hear the terms -- then tell Radomir it was Pavel's idea and not mine. It has to not be mine. I have a reputation for winning and this feels dangerously like the other thing.
stonepath_romance_sava_3.progress: Ask Pavel at the inn for the terms, then carry them, carefully, to Radomir.
stonepath_romance_sava_3.complete: So we're sharing an awning. Under the same roof, out of the same rain, arguing over the same square, for a whole season. He said only that his side stays tidy. I said mine outsells his. We both know neither thing is the point. ...Twenty years, traveler. It took a stranger and a leaking roof. Don't you dare look pleased.

objectives.text: Get the awning terms from Pavel
Carry the terms to Radomir

stonepath_romance_sava_3.note: One awning, two stalls, a season of shared rain. They kept the arguing. It was only ever the arguing they loved.

stonepath_romance_sava_4.title: What the Cold Doesn't Take
stonepath_romance_sava_4.summary: Radomir has gone quiet and cold at his forge, sitting too still, the way folk do before they walk east. Sava will not lose him to the stone. She sends warmth, and steel.
stonepath_romance_sava_4.offer: He's gone still. Sitting at a cold anvil, staring at the eastern rows, not arguing. Radomir not arguing is a corpse that hasn't finished. It's the stone in him -- the quiet that takes the ones who listen. I won't have it. Clear the wolves off the west spur so I can reach him, then bring the hearth-wax the old women burn against it. I am going to make that man too warm to leave.
stonepath_romance_sava_4.progress: Break the wolf-pack on the west spur, gather warding hearth-wax, and get both to Radomir's cold forge.
stonepath_romance_sava_4.complete: I lit every candle I own and put my hand on his and told him he still owes me an apology from a Tuesday in autumn, and he can't leave until he pays it. And he came back. Slow. Like a fire catching. He said 'your iron was never soft.' Twenty years to say it. We'll take the rest slow, the two of us, out of the same rain. Everything good is temporary. That's what makes it his.

objectives.text: Clear the wolves off the west spur
Gather warding hearth-wax
Bring warmth to Radomir's cold forge

stonepath_romance_sava_4.note: She warmed him back from the eastern quiet with candles and an old argument. He said her iron was never soft. It was the nearest thing to a vow either would speak.

grey_marches_romance_radu_1.title: The Undercut
grey_marches_romance_radu_1.summary: Anton the merchant sells cheap blades to the charcoal-burners who face the dead wood. Radu, who says little, wants the player to see how the cheap steel fails.
grey_marches_romance_radu_1.offer: Anton sells them soft blades cheap. Charcoal-folk go into the dead wood with iron that folds on a bark-revenant's hide, and they don't come back, and he counts the coin. I've said my piece to him. Words slide off Anton. Go into the grey trees, put down what walks there, and see what a folded edge does to a working man. Then you'll know why I can't stand the sight of him. I don't say much. I'll say that.
grey_marches_romance_radu_1.progress: Face the bark-revenants in the dead wood and mark how a cheap edge fails against them.
grey_marches_romance_radu_1.complete: Now you've seen it. Steel that folds when a life's on it. He knows. That's the part that -- no. Never mind. Bring me a poor man's grey silk off those things and I'll show you what a real edge is. It's the only argument I win with Anton. He's louder. My blades come home.

objectives.text: Put down the bark-revenants in the dead wood
Recover grave-silk from the dead wood

grey_marches_romance_radu_1.note: Radu let the dead wood argue his case for him. He is a smith of few words. The count of folded blades was the whole of his grief.

grey_marches_romance_radu_2.title: A Fire Worth Sharing
grey_marches_romance_radu_2.summary: Anton's cart broke on the east path and his stock lies in the dead wood where the hungering walk. Radu will not let even his rival be taken. He sends help, and says nothing of why.
grey_marches_romance_radu_2.offer: Anton's cart went over on the east path. His stock's scattered in the dead wood and the man's fool enough to go back for it alone, where the hungering walk. I can't -- look. I don't like him. I've never liked him. But I'll not have Grey Marches short one loud fool because I stood at a cold forge and let it happen. Go with him. Bring back his crates. Don't tell him I sent you. Tell him you found the wreck.
grey_marches_romance_radu_2.progress: Recover Anton's scattered crates from the east path and see him safe from the hungering.
grey_marches_romance_radu_2.complete: He's back, then. And his stock. Good. I -- that's good. He asked who sent word. You said you found the wreck. Right. Right. ...He's not a bad hand at a bargain, Anton. I'll say that once and never again. The forge stays warm tonight. If a loud man wanted to warm his hands at it, on his way past, I'd not bar the door. That's all. That's all I'm saying.

objectives.text: Recover Anton's scattered stock
Ward off the grave-moths on the east path
See Anton safe back to the yard

grey_marches_romance_radu_2.note: Radu sent help to the rival he swore he hated and asked that his name be kept out of it. The forge stayed warm that night. He left the door unbarred.

grey_marches_romance_radu_3.title: The Thing He Won't Say
grey_marches_romance_radu_3.summary: Anton is dying by inches, the grey creeping into him from the failing forest. Radu forges him a blade he will never charge for, and asks the player to carry it, and the words he cannot.
grey_marches_romance_radu_3.offer: The grey's in Anton. The forest's dying and it's taking him needle by needle, the way it takes the trees. He's still bargaining, still loud, but slower. I made him a blade. Best edge I've ever drawn. I won't charge him -- can't, the coin would make it a trade and it isn't a trade. Carry it to him. And tell him... tell him the smith says the blade comes home. He'll know what I can't get my mouth to shape. Ileana can put the words in the ledger if I can't put them in the air.
grey_marches_romance_radu_3.progress: Take Radu's blade to Anton on the dead wood's edge, then have Ileana keep the record of it.
grey_marches_romance_radu_3.complete: He took it. Ran his thumb down the edge, the way he prices a thing, and then he stopped pricing. Said 'tell the smith I know.' That's -- that's all either of us has ever managed. Twenty years of undercutting and one blade given free at the end of it. I never said the word. He never made me. Some fires you keep by not naming them, so the naming can't cost you. Ileana has it kept now. The two of us. Held. Unsaid. It's enough. It was always going to have to be enough.

objectives.text: Carry Radu's free blade to Anton
Have Ileana keep the record

grey_marches_romance_radu_3.note: The smith gave the merchant the finest blade he ever drew and charged him nothing, and named nothing. Anton said only, I know. Some fires are kept by never speaking them. This one was kept.

stonepath_romance_vintila_1.title: Rumor and Record
stonepath_romance_vintila_1.summary: Vintila keeps the true chronicle; Sava the merchant sells whatever version pays better. They have feuded for years over which account of the crossroads is real.
stonepath_romance_vintila_1.offer: Sava sells stories by the ounce and calls it trade. I keep the ledger of the dead and it never balances, but at least it's true. She's been telling travelers the shortening stone is a swindle, a chisel-trick to sell charms. It is not a trick. I need the count to prove it. Walk the standing stones at the crossroads heart, read what's carved, and bring me the true measure. Let her sell rumor. I'll set the record against her.
stonepath_romance_vintila_1.progress: Read the three standing stones at the crossroads and bring Vintila the true carved count.
stonepath_romance_vintila_1.complete: There. The eastern stone's lost another line since spring -- I have it in my own hand from spring, so I know. It is getting shorter. Sava will hate that it's real; she can't sell a truth that frightens people. She'll come argue. She always comes to argue. I've kept a chair for it. Don't tell her the chair's for her.

objectives.label: the eastern standing stone
objectives.text: Read the eastern stone

objectives.label: the western standing stone
objectives.text: Read the western stone

objectives.label: the shortening stone
objectives.text: Measure the shortening stone

stonepath_romance_vintila_1.note: The chronicler set true count against a merchant's convenient lie. The stone was shorter than spring. He kept a chair for the argument he hoped was coming.

stonepath_romance_vintila_2.title: The Chair Kept for Arguing
stonepath_romance_vintila_2.summary: The feud has warmed into something neither will name. Vintila wants Sava's own rumor-network to save lives, not sell them, and needs the player to broker a truce over lamp-oil and grave-silk.
stonepath_romance_vintila_2.offer: I have decided -- and this pains me -- that Sava's web of gossip is the fastest warning-bell in four kingdoms, and I am wasting it hating her. If she'd carry my true count to travelers instead of her pretty lie, fewer would linger at the shortening stone and go quiet. Gather chalk-marker chips from the kerbs and good grave-silk for a warded lamp, and take them to her stall with an offer of truce. She'll say no. Bring back exactly how she says it. I collect her refusals.
stonepath_romance_vintila_2.progress: Bring kerbstone chips and grave-silk to Sava's stall and put Vintila's truce to her.
stonepath_romance_vintila_2.complete: She said -- and I want this word for word in the ledger -- she said, 'Fine, chronicler, I'll sell your grim truth, but only because it frightens people into buying my charms, so we both win.' Which is a yes dressed as a swindle, which is the most romantic thing that woman has ever said to me, and I am forty years too dignified to tell her so. Keep the band. I've no use for a thing that means what she just meant.

objectives.text: Gather chalk-marker chips from the kerbs
Gather grave-silk for a warded lamp
Put the truce to Sava

stonepath_romance_vintila_2.note: The chronicler's truth would ride the merchant's gossip now, to warn instead of to sell. She said yes in the shape of a swindle. He wrote it down and kept the page apart from the rest.

stonepath_romance_vintila_3.title: The Inscription That Wants You
stonepath_romance_vintila_3.summary: Copying the shrinking stone has bound Vintila to it; the marks are surfacing on his own ledger, unbidden. He is going quiet. The player must break the pull before it takes him.
stonepath_romance_vintila_3.offer: I read the shortening stone too closely, too long. The marks are on my ledger now, on the blank facing page, in no hand but the stone's. It writes itself while I sleep. Sava found me at dawn just staring east, and for once she did not sell it as a story -- she cried, which neither of us will ever mention. I need blackglass from the wild wood to seal the pages, and the thing at the crossroads root put down, before the quiet in me finishes. Hurry. I am becoming an entry.
stonepath_romance_vintila_3.progress: Slay the boars fouling the crossroads root, gather blackglass shard to seal the ledger, and get both to Vintila.
stonepath_romance_vintila_3.complete: You sealed it. The facing page is blank again -- truly blank, my blank, not the stone's. Sava sat with me the whole while and did not once try to profit from my fear, which is how I finally understood what she is to me. Forty years of arguing across a square. I nearly missed it by reading a stone instead of her face. Thank you. Go tell her the ledger's clean. She'll pretend not to care. Watch her hands.

objectives.text: Clear the boars at the crossroads root
Gather blackglass to seal the ledger
Seal Vintila's haunted ledger

stonepath_romance_vintila_3.note: The stone had begun to write him into itself. They sealed the ledger and he understood, too late to be early, what the arguing had always been. The facing page was his own blank again.

stonepath_romance_vintila_4.title: A Ledger Cannot Follow
stonepath_romance_vintila_4.summary: Vintila will not leave the crossroads; someone must keep the shortening stone's true count, and only he can, at cost. He sends Sava away to live, and asks the player to carry the goodbye.
stonepath_romance_vintila_4.offer: Sava wants us to leave together. West, past the reach of the stone, a stall and a chair and a quiet old age. I want it more than I have wanted anything in forty years of wanting nothing. But if I go, no one keeps the true count, and the stone shortens in the dark and no one warns the ones who linger. I am the last chronicler who can read it without being read. Take her the coach fare and this: my word that it is love, and that love is exactly why she must go and I must stay.
stonepath_romance_vintila_4.progress: Carry Vintila's parting to Sava, then see her to the crossroads coach that leaves west.
stonepath_romance_vintila_4.complete: She took the fare. She did not take it quietly -- Sava has never done one thing quietly -- but she took it, and she went west, and she'll live, loud and unswindled, into an old age I bought her with the price of staying. That is the whole of what I have. A stone to read until it reads me, and the knowledge that somewhere west a woman is arguing with a stranger about the price of nails, and living. Everything good is temporary. She was good. She is living. I kept the count. It was a fair trade. It was the only trade I'll never regret.

objectives.text: Carry Vintila's parting to Sava

objectives.label: the crossroads coach yard
objectives.text: See Sava to the westbound coach

stonepath_romance_vintila_4.note: He loved her, and so he sent her west to live, and stayed to keep the count that would one day keep him. She went loud, arguing to the last. The chair stays empty by the ledger now. He does not move it.

greyhollow_romance_marioara_1.title: Green Bottle, Black Bottle
greyhollow_romance_marioara_1.summary: Marioara keeps Hessik's old sight alive over her counter, and the harbor fog has started reading her back. She needs marshlight jars gathered from the drowned lanes to steady her still -- and someone standing near who does not flinch from what she is.
greyhollow_romance_marioara_1.offer: Green bottle mends, black bottle ends -- and I keep a third my grandmother never named, the one you look through instead of drinking. The trouble is the fog's begun looking back through it at me, and I can't read a page while it's reading my hand. I need marshlight, fresh from the wet lanes, to hold the flame honest. Fetch me a dozen jars. And traveler -- when you bring them, don't do the thing the others do, where you set them down and leave quick. Set them down and stay a moment. I'll pour you something from the green bottle. On the house, which I never say twice.
greyhollow_romance_marioara_1.progress: Marshlight gathers in the flooded lanes where the drowned clerks drift. Fill a dozen jars, then bring them to Marioara's counter -- and stay for the pouring.
greyhollow_romance_marioara_1.complete: Twelve jars, and you stayed for the twelfth. Most set them on the sill and are gone before the door swings. You sat where the light could reach you, and the still didn't so much as shiver -- it reads rot, my sight, and it found none in you to fret over. Do you know how rare that is, to have a room's worth of wrongness and one clean thing sitting in it. Drink the green. It's only tea. I put nothing in it. That's the point.

objectives.text: Gather marshlight from the wet lanes
Scatter the clerks that drift the flooded lanes
Bring the jars and stay for the pouring

greyhollow_romance_marioara_1.note: Marioara reads rot for a living, and her sight found none in the one who stayed. She poured the green bottle and named the rareness plainly: a room of wrongness, and one clean thing sitting in it. She did not say what she hoped it stayed.

greyhollow_romance_marioara_2.title: The Handprint at the Sixth Lock
greyhollow_romance_marioara_2.summary: There is a chalk handprint at the sixth lock that Marioara has walked past for thirty years and never once touched -- a child's, from before the sight took her. She has decided, this once, not to walk past it alone.
greyhollow_romance_marioara_2.offer: I'll tell you a thing I've told no one who could still leave. When my grandmother's sight came into me, I was small, and I put my hand on the wet wall at the sixth lock to steady the dizziness of it -- the first time the world went transparent and awful. The chalk's still there. Thirty years I've crossed the far bank to avoid the near one. I want to go and look at it. Not read it -- look. I find I can't make my feet do it while I'm the only one on the towpath. Walk the sixth lock with me. Bring a folk warding, the plain kind, in case my hands shake and want something to hold that isn't the wall.
greyhollow_romance_marioara_2.progress: The sixth lock lies west along the canal. Carry a folk warding for her hands, and go to the handprint together -- the one she has crossed the water to avoid for thirty years.
greyhollow_romance_marioara_2.complete: It's a small hand. That's all. A frightened girl's, pressed once and left. I built thirty years of not-looking on top of it and it turns out to be just a handprint on a wet wall, no worse than the rest of me. You held the warding out and I took your wrist instead. I hope that was allowed. It was the only clean thing on the whole towpath and I wanted to hold the clean thing. There. Now you know the wound and you're still standing on the near bank. I'll stop expecting you to cross to the far one.

objectives.text: Carry a plain folk warding for her hands

objectives.label: the handprint at the sixth lock
objectives.text: Go to the sixth lock with Marioara
Stand with her at the handprint

greyhollow_romance_marioara_2.note: She crossed the water to the near bank, thirty years late, and found only a small frightened handprint waiting -- no worse than the rest of her. She took a wrist instead of the warding. The one who came did not let go, and did not cross to the far side, and that was the whole of the answer.

greyhollow_romance_marioara_3.title: What Hessik Meant As a Figure of Speech
greyhollow_romance_marioara_3.summary: Marioara wants to read the fog off the Pit rim through the orange-still one more time -- but this time to test a hope, not a dread. She thinks the sight might read what a person loves as clearly as what a person rots from. She would like to see whether it reads the two of you the same color.
greyhollow_romance_marioara_3.offer: Hessik wrote that a live stone reads a person the way a wet page reads ink, and I spent thirty years pretending he meant it prettily. I've a crueller experiment and a kinder question both. Take the orange-still up to the Pit rim, where the fog reads truest, and hold it while I stand beside you. If the sight only reads wrongness, we'll come back grim and I'll never mention it again. But I have a notion it reads whatever's strongest in a body -- and lately, traveler, the strongest thing in mine isn't the dread. Clear the drowned clerks off the rim first. I won't have us interrupted for something so small as the risen dead while I'm working up my nerve.
greyhollow_romance_marioara_3.progress: The Pit rim reads the fog truest. Clear the clerks that gather there, then hold the orange-still while Marioara stands beside you -- and see what color the two of you read.
greyhollow_romance_marioara_3.complete: Not blue. Not violet. Not the orange that means run. It read a color I've no name for and my grandmother had no name for, warm at the edge like a banked hearth, and it was brightest exactly in the span of air between where you stood and where I did. The sight reads the strongest thing. I've spent my whole life certain the strongest thing in me was the seeing of rot. It appears I was wrong, and I have never in my life been so glad to be wrong about the color of anything. Keep the method. And keep close to the still. It likes you near.

objectives.text: Clear the clerks off the Pit rim

objectives.label: the Pit rim
objectives.text: Read the fog at the rim, beside her

greyhollow_romance_marioara_3.note: The fog read a color neither the living nor the dead had a name for, warm as a banked hearth, and it burned brightest in the arm's length of air between the two who stood to read it. The sight reads the strongest thing in a body. For the first time in thirty years, hers was not the dread.

greyhollow_romance_marioara_4.title: A Candle in Wet Weather
greyhollow_romance_marioara_4.summary: Marioara has kept her craft the way one keeps a candle in wet weather -- guarded, alone, hands cupped. She would like, for once, a second pair of hands cupped around the same small flame. She asks for a keepsake cord, and for you to help her hang a new sign over the door.
greyhollow_romance_marioara_4.offer: My grandmother taught me to keep the sight like a candle in a downpour -- both hands round it, back to the wind, and no one close enough to gust it out. I've kept it that way forty years and the keeping is the loneliest craft there is. I don't want to keep it alone anymore. Fetch me a keepsake cord from the merchant -- the sort couples knot when they mean it -- and come help me hang the new sign I've had painted for the door. It still says green bottle mends, black bottle ends. But I've had a third line added, under, small. You'll see. Then, if you'll have a rot-reader with a wound she's stopped hiding, we'll cup our hands round the same candle and let the weather do its worst.
greyhollow_romance_marioara_4.progress: Fetch a keepsake cord from Tinca the merchant, then help Marioara hang the new sign above her door -- and read the third line she had painted under the old one.
greyhollow_romance_marioara_4.complete: There. It hangs straight. Green bottle mends, black bottle ends -- and, small, under: and the third light we keep between us. You read it and didn't leave and didn't do the flinch. Forty years I read every soul that crossed my counter for the rot in them, and I never once thought to let a soul read me back and stay. The candle's yours to guard now, half of it. The weather's foul and getting fouler and the Pit is patient and none of that has changed. But my hands aren't the only ones cupped round the flame tonight, and that has. Come in out of the wet. I've the green bottle on.

objectives.text: Fetch a keepsake cord from the merchant
Ask Tinca for the cord couples knot when they mean it
Help Marioara hang the new sign

greyhollow_romance_marioara_4.note: The new sign hangs straight over the apothecary door, the old line and a third one under it, small: and the third light we keep between us. Forty years she read every soul for its rot and let none read her back. The weather off the Pit has not eased. But two pairs of hands cup the candle now, and it has not gone out.

orange_fog_romance_floarea_1.title: The List She Reads Aloud
orange_fog_romance_floarea_1.summary: Floarea keeps a list of the finalized whose names she has never been able to fit -- closed files walking the fog, unnamed and unrested. She reads the list aloud sometimes, guessing, so the guessing is at least warm. She asks you to gather the kept-name tokens the wisps carry, and to sit with her while she reads.
orange_fog_romance_floarea_1.offer: You've watched me long enough to have questions, so here's the answer to the one you haven't asked. I keep a list. The finalized who walk out there -- I can rest most of them, if I know the name and enough of the small true weight of who they were. But some I can't fit. Closed files with the name worn off, walking their old errands forever. I read my list to them at dusk, guessing, because a wrong name said warm is kinder than no name said at all. Bring me the tokens the wisps carry at the thinning; each holds a name the ledger left unbalanced. And then -- this is the strange part of the asking -- sit with me while I read. It's a thing I've only ever done alone. I'd like, once, to not.
orange_fog_romance_floarea_1.progress: The kept-name tokens fall from the wisps at the thinning. Gather them for Floarea's list, then sit with her at dusk while she reads the names aloud -- the thing she has only ever done alone.
orange_fog_romance_floarea_1.complete: You sat the whole reading. Nobody sits the whole reading; it's long and it doesn't help and the fog gets in your teeth. You stayed to the last unfittable name and then you asked me mine. Mine. Floarea. Cenusa, if it matters, though the Cenusa is half the fog by now. Do you know, in all the years of reading names into the grey, no living soul ever thought to ask me back for one of my own. It's on my list, you understand. Near the bottom. I keep it there so I don't forget I'm not finished yet. You asking has moved it up a line.

objectives.text: Gather the names the ledger left unbalanced
Thin the wisps for the tokens they carry
Sit with Floarea while she reads the list

orange_fog_romance_floarea_1.note: Floarea reads the unfittable names into the fog at dusk, guessing, so the guessing is warm. One living soul sat the whole reading and then asked her own name back. It is on her list, near the bottom, so she remembers she is not finished. The asking moved it up a line.

orange_fog_romance_floarea_2.title: Brightest Where I Stand
orange_fog_romance_floarea_2.summary: Floarea confesses the reason she keeps the list: the fog reads brightest where she stands, and she believes she is half-finalized already -- a file the ledger has begun to close. She wants you to walk the reach with her and learn every small true thing about her, while there is still a her to learn.
orange_fog_romance_floarea_2.offer: Now the hard telling, since you've earned it and I've no habit of gentleness to lose. The fog goes orange brightest where I stand. It's been reading me a long while -- I stayed on the Coast too long, watched the thinning too close, and something began writing me into an entry. Half a file, walking. That's why I learned the trick of names: I was practicing on the dead what I'll need done for me. So walk the reach with me to the true edge, and don't gather anything, and don't kill anything if you can help it. Just -- learn me. The tune I hum without hearing it. That I take my tea scalding. That I hate the color blue. The specific undoes the finalized, you'll recall. If I'm to be a closed file, I'd have one living soul carry the open version. Bring a bound keepsake. Whatever you learn, knot it in.
orange_fog_romance_floarea_2.progress: Walk the reach to its true edge beside Floarea. Carry a bound keepsake and knot into it the small true things she tells you along the way -- while there is still a her to learn.
orange_fog_romance_floarea_2.complete: You knotted them all in. The tune -- it's one my mother hummed and I never knew the words to. The scalding tea. The hated blue. The thing I've never told a soul, that I'm afraid not of being collected but of being collected wrong, filed as someone I never was. You have the open version now, knotted and carried, and the fog can write whatever entry it likes. It won't be the true one. The true one walks around in your pocket. I find I'm not as frightened as I was this morning. Isn't that a thing. A woman half-filed, and less afraid, because somebody bothered to learn she takes her tea scalding.

objectives.text: Carry a bound keepsake for her small true things

objectives.label: the true edge of the reach
objectives.text: Walk the reach to its edge beside Floarea
Knot in every small true thing she tells you

orange_fog_romance_floarea_2.note: The fog reads brightest where Floarea stands; she believes she is half a file already, closing. So she gave the open version away, knotted into a keepsake -- the hummed tune, the scalding tea, the fear of being filed as someone she never was. A woman half-collected, and less afraid, because one soul bothered to learn the small true weight of her.

orange_fog_romance_floarea_3.title: The Turn Toward the Last Hearth
orange_fog_romance_floarea_3.summary: The reach has moved two hands closer, and Floarea has decided to walk into it on her own terms rather than wait for the file to close over her wrong. She asks you to walk her to the turn toward the Last Hearth -- not to save her, which cannot be done, but to be the one who knew her, and let her go known.
orange_fog_romance_floarea_3.offer: The reach came two hands west this season. I've read enough entries to know my own is nearly written, and I won't sit in my chair and let it finish me into a stranger. So I'm going to walk out to it myself, at the turn toward the Last Hearth, while the name I answer to is still mine and not the fog's. Here is what I want from you, and it's the largest thing I've ever asked anyone. Don't stop me -- there's no stopping it, only the manner of the going. Walk me to the turn. Say my name at the edge, the true one, with all you knotted in the keepsake. Send the wisps off gentle before I step past them; I'd not have them crowding. And then let me go, known. That's the whole of it. To be undone by my own name, said right, by someone who stayed. It's more than this place sells anyone. It's more than I read for the ones on my list.
orange_fog_romance_floarea_3.progress: Send the wisps off gentle at the turn toward the Last Hearth, then walk Floarea to the edge and say her true name -- the manner of a going that cannot be stopped, only kept.
orange_fog_romance_floarea_3.complete: You said it right. Everything knotted in the keepsake, in the order I told you, my mother's tune under it. I felt the entry try to close over me and find it couldn't -- there was too much true and specific in the way you said Floarea for the fog to file me as anyone else. So I go as myself. Undone by my own name, in a warm mouth, at a turn in the road, which is a better death than the Coast writes for anyone and far better than I read for my list. Don't grieve it as a loss. Grieve it as a keeping. You have the open version still. Take it up the road to the Last Hearth. Read it aloud sometimes, guessing at the parts you missed, so the guessing stays warm. That's the trade, love. That was always the trade. Go on. Don't watch the last of it. Look at your boots -- you know the drill. And thank you. For the asking, back at the start. It moved the whole list.

objectives.text: Send the wisps off gentle at the turn

objectives.label: the turn toward the Last Hearth
objectives.text: Walk Floarea to the turn and say her true name
Let her go, known

orange_fog_romance_floarea_3.note: Floarea walked into the reach on her own terms, undone by her own name said right, in a warm mouth, at a turn in the road. Not saved -- there was no saving -- but kept. The open version of her rides up the road to the Last Hearth now, in a pocket, to be read aloud at dusk, guessing at the missed parts so the guessing stays warm. That was always the trade.

listening_steppe_romance_petru_1.title: The Man Who Says Little
listening_steppe_romance_petru_1.summary: Petru beats iron like it owes him money and speaks in the fewest words that will do. The forge needs good bog iron off the near grass, and Petru -- who never asks for company -- finds a roundabout way of asking you to bring it yourself, and to stay while he works it.
listening_steppe_romance_petru_1.offer: Steel's steel, and mine's honest, which is more than the steppe manages most days. I'm short of stock. Bog iron, off the near grass -- the reddish nodules, not the black, the black's no good. Bring me eight and I'll cut you an edge worth the walk. And. Hm. The merchant could fetch it. I know that. I'm asking you. Some folk you don't mind having stand by the fire while the hammer's going. You're one. That's a longer speech than I've made all week, so take the iron and don't make me improve on it.
listening_steppe_romance_petru_1.progress: The reddish bog iron lies in the near grass, west of the forge. Bring Petru eight good nodules -- and stand by the fire while he works them, since he went to the trouble of asking you and not the merchant.
listening_steppe_romance_petru_1.complete: Good iron. Reddish, like I said, not that black rubbish. You stood the whole heat and didn't fill the quiet with talk, which is the thing I can't abide in most and the thing I noticed you don't do. Here's your edge. I made it a hair finer than the walk was worth. Don't read into that. ...Read a little into it. Not much. Come back when the stock's low. It's low often, if I'm honest, and I mean to be honest with you, which is new.

objectives.text: Gather reddish bog iron from the near grass

objectives.label: the near grass
objectives.text: Work the near grass for good iron
Stand by the fire while Petru works the iron

listening_steppe_romance_petru_1.note: Petru speaks in the fewest words that will do, and he spent several of them asking the wrong-priced favor of the right person. The one who came stood the whole heat and did not fill the quiet. He made the edge a hair finer than the walk was worth, and told them to read a little into it. Not much.

listening_steppe_romance_petru_2.title: Knotted to the Body
listening_steppe_romance_petru_2.summary: Petru tells you, in the fewest words that will carry it, why he keeps a keepsake cord knotted at his wrist: he went out to the long grass once and came back one of the listening, and the note has never fully left him. He asks you to walk to the Kneeling Stone with him, and to be the reason he turns back.
listening_steppe_romance_petru_2.offer: I'll say this once and plain, because you've earned plain and I've no other kind. Two winters back I walked out to the long grass. Don't know why. Nobody does; that's the whole horror of it. I stood in the field with the standing ones a day and a night, faced the way they faced, and it was Nedelcu dragged me back by the collar. I hear the note still. Low. Under the hammer, under the wind. Most days the forge is loud enough to drown it. This cord at my wrist -- keeps a name knotted to a body, the old folk say. Mine's held two years by iron and stubbornness. Walk me up to the Kneeling Stone. I go once a season to prove I still can turn around. It's easier to turn if there's a reason standing behind me. Be the reason. Bring a keepsake cord; if mine's fraying I'd not know, being the one it's holding.
listening_steppe_romance_petru_2.progress: The Kneeling Stone sits up the north track where the road stops pretending to be a road. Carry a keepsake cord in case his is fraying, and walk Petru to the stone -- and be the reason he turns back.
listening_steppe_romance_petru_2.complete: I turned around. Faced the field, heard the note pull, and turned around -- because you were behind me and turning toward you was easier than turning toward it. That's the trick nobody tells you about the listening. It's not that you're called. It's that nothing's calling you back the other way. Now there is. I felt the cord hold and I felt something better than the cord hold, and I came down off that track lighter than I've been in two winters. Keep the cord you brought. Knot it to mine. Two names, one body's worth of stubbornness, and a reason at my back. Let the grass wait. It's good at waiting. I'm learning to be good at staying.

objectives.text: Carry a keepsake cord in case his is fraying

objectives.label: the Kneeling Stone
objectives.text: Walk Petru up to the Kneeling Stone
Be the reason he turns back

listening_steppe_romance_petru_2.note: Petru went to the long grass once and came back one of the listening; the note has never fully left him. He turned back from the Kneeling Stone because someone stood behind him to turn toward. The listening, he learned, is not being called -- it is nothing calling you the other way. Now something does. He knotted two cords into one.

listening_steppe_romance_petru_3.title: Something Better Than the Cord
listening_steppe_romance_petru_3.summary: The prowlers that pace the flocks have learned to walk the field's edge where the listening gather, and Petru will not let you go alone to thin them -- the field pulls at him worst there, and he means to prove, at the field's edge itself, that he can stand in the pull and still choose to stay.
listening_steppe_romance_petru_3.offer: The prowlers work the field's edge now, right where the standing ones gather -- Toma's ewes lamb soon and the flock won't run from them, same wrongness as ever. Somebody has to thin them. And I know you'd go alone to spare me the edge, and I'm telling you no. The edge is exactly where I need to stand. Two winters I've turned back a season at a time and called it staying. That's not staying. That's a man who hasn't been tested. Take me to the field's edge, thin the prowlers with me, and let me feel the full pull of that grass with a blade in my hand and you beside me and choose -- properly choose, in the worst of it -- to walk home after. If I can do it there, the turning-back's not a trick anymore. It's just my life. Bring a soothing knot. Not for me. For after, when my hands won't stop shaking and I don't want to hide it from you.
listening_steppe_romance_petru_3.progress: The prowlers pace the field's edge where the listening gather -- the worst of the pull. Thin them beside Petru, carry a soothing knot for the shaking after, and let him choose to walk home from the very edge.
listening_steppe_romance_petru_3.complete: I stood at the edge with the note screaming under everything and I killed prowlers and I heard the field say stay and I said no. Out loud. Felt a fool. Best fool I ever was. My hands are shaking -- there, I'm not hiding it, I said I wouldn't -- so hold the knot to them a minute and don't say anything, you're good at that. It's done. The listening's still in me; it doesn't leave; the old folk are right about that much. But it's not the strongest thing in me anymore, and it hasn't been since the day you stood the whole heat and didn't fill the quiet. Walk me home. Slow. The grass can watch us go. I want it to see a man it didn't get.

objectives.text: Thin the prowlers at the field's edge with Petru

objectives.label: the field's edge
objectives.text: Stand the worst of the pull beside him
Carry a soothing knot for the shaking after

listening_steppe_romance_petru_3.note: Petru stood at the field's edge in the full pull of the grass, a blade in his hand and someone beside him, and chose out loud to walk home. The listening does not leave; the old folk are right. But it is no longer the strongest thing in him. The grass watched a man it did not get walk home slow, and unhidden.

listening_steppe_romance_petru_4.title: Good at Staying
listening_steppe_romance_petru_4.summary: Petru means to make the plainest possible declaration in the plainest possible way: two rings off his own anvil, and a hearth hex-sign for the door of a house he wants to share. He asks you to fetch the sign from the merchant and to help him hang it -- and to hear the longest speech he will ever make.
listening_steppe_romance_petru_4.offer: I've made a thing at the anvil these three nights and I'll not be coy about it, coyness being another word for cowardice and I've spent my quota of that on the grass. Two rings. Plain iron, no stones -- stones are for men who need the ring to do the talking, and I mean to do my own. But a house wants a sign as well as rings. Fetch me a hearth hex-sign off Iancu -- the kind the old folk hang to keep a name knotted to a home, not just a body. Help me nail it over my door. And then stand still, because I'm about to make the longest speech of my life and I've only the one in me. It's this: I went to the grass and it kept a piece of me and gave it back short. You never once treated the short piece like something to pity. You treated it like a man. I'd like to be your man, short piece and all, for as long as the staying holds -- and it's held since the day you didn't fill the quiet. That's the speech. Say yes or say no, but say it plain. I've earned plain, and so have you.
listening_steppe_romance_petru_4.progress: Fetch a hearth hex-sign from Iancu the merchant, then help Petru hang it over his door -- and hear the longest, plainest speech the quiet man has in him.
listening_steppe_romance_petru_4.complete: You said yes, plain, the way I asked, and I'll not pretend my eyes stayed dry -- a smith's allowed the one weakness and I'll spend it on you and no one else. The sign's hung straight. The rings fit; I sized the second one off the cord at your wrist while you slept in the chair by the forge, which I'll admit was a liberty. Here's how it is now. The grass is still out there. The note's still under the wind on the bad nights. But I've a house with a sign on the door and a name knotted to more than a body, and a reason at my back that doesn't leave when the season turns. I was a man the field nearly got. I'm a man who stays instead. You made the difference, and I'm plain-spoken enough to say so, and stubborn enough to keep saying it every dark-to-dark I've got left.

objectives.text: Fetch a hearth hex-sign from the merchant
Ask Iancu for the sign that keeps a name knotted to a home
Help Petru hang the sign and hear his speech

listening_steppe_romance_petru_4.note: Petru forged two plain rings and made the longest speech of his life, which was short. The grass is still out there; the note still comes on bad nights. But there is a sign over the door and a name knotted to more than a body now. He was a man the field nearly got. He is a man who stays instead, and plain-spoken enough to keep saying so.

blestem_romance_aglaia_1.title: A Chair Left Empty
blestem_romance_aglaia_1.summary: Aglaia has kept the warm door of Blestem through three husbands and every winter the fog can throw. She has set a fourth chair by her fire, and has decided, late, whom she wants to fill it.
blestem_romance_aglaia_1.offer: Sit. No — that chair. That one's been empty too long and I'm tired of looking at it. There's a woman keeps the apothecary two lanes over. Profira. Smells of tallow and knows more about the dead than the living, and I have decided, at my age, that I would like to know her better. I can't very well cross the market carrying that on my face. Not in this city. Carry a soothing knot to her — a widow's gift, nothing anyone can read into. And tell me what her hands do when she takes it.
blestem_romance_aglaia_1.progress: Profira keeps her shelves of tallow and wormwood two lanes east, where the lichen glows on the wet stone. Take the knot. Watch her hands, not her mouth.
blestem_romance_aglaia_1.complete: Both hands, you say. She took it in both hands and didn't ask who sent it. ...Good. A woman who's buried people learns to hold a thing gently. I buried three. I know the grip. Here — for the walk, and for looking where I told you and nowhere else. This city has ears. Keep this one between the two of us and the fire.

objectives.text: Gather a soothing knot for Profira
Bring the knot to Profira Vulpescu

blestem_romance_aglaia_1.note: Aglaia has outlasted three husbands and forty winters of fog, and she has set out a fourth chair. Profira took the knot in both hands. Old hands know how to hold what is temporary.

blestem_romance_aglaia_2.title: Where No Wall Hears
blestem_romance_aglaia_2.summary: In the Listening City a tender word is a coin, and every stall-keeper is someone's ear. Aglaia will not court a woman where Cazimir can file it. She wants one corner of Blestem the walls do not reach.
blestem_romance_aglaia_2.offer: You'd think at my age I could say a kind thing out loud. Not here. In Blestem a fondness is a lever, and I'll not hand Cazimir a lever over Profira — she's the only thing new I've wanted in twenty years. There's a dead-end alley off the Transcub churches where the old god used to be worshipped, back when there was a god who minded cruelty. The walls there don't carry. Lay a folk-warding at its mouth — it means nothing, that's the whole point of it, nothing to read, nothing to file. Then bring her old dossier back from that market-rat Despina before it's sold. I'll not have her past for sale while I'm learning her present.
blestem_romance_aglaia_2.progress: The disused alley sits off the ivy-eaten Transcub churches. Ward its mouth so no meaning leaks out. Then buy back whatever Despina holds on Profira before it goes to another ear.
blestem_romance_aglaia_2.complete: Warded, and the dossier in the fire — I watched it burn, thank you. Ash tells Cazimir nothing. Now there's one square of this whole listening city where I can say a foolish thing and only Profira hears it. That's more than most marriages ever managed, and I'd know. Come — I mean to use it tonight.

objectives.text: Ward the silent alley off the churches
Buy back Profira's dossier from Despina
Settle the price with Despina Vulpescu

blestem_romance_aglaia_2.note: In a city that owns its people through their secrets, Aglaia bought back Profira's and burned it. She warded one dead-end alley into silence. It is the smallest free ground in Blestem, and it is enough.

blestem_romance_aglaia_3.title: The Long Illness
blestem_romance_aglaia_3.summary: Winter comes hard to the cleft where the fog pools, and the cough that comes with it has taken Aglaia to her bed. Profira, who has spent a life learning the dead, now bends every hour of it to keeping one woman among the living.
blestem_romance_aglaia_3.offer: She won't tell you she's ill, so I will. The wet-lung. It comes for the old ones every winter this fog pools, and Aglaia's been outrunning it forty years and this year it caught her. I can hold it back. I have the trade for it — I've just never once wanted the trade to matter this much. Bring me luminous lichen off the true-dark stone, the lichen that glows because it was asked to, not the market strain. And clear the basalt golems from the old quarry road so I can reach the deep beds where it grows. I am not losing her to a cough. Not now. Not when I've only just been let in.
blestem_romance_aglaia_3.progress: The true-dark lichen grows on wet stone past the old quarry, where the basalt golems stand their slow watch. Break them off the road and gather the living light.
blestem_romance_aglaia_3.complete: It's enough. It's enough to hold her. She'll cough through spring and complain the whole way and I will be glad of every sour word of it. You know the strange thing? I've spent my whole life not minding the dead. They're patient, they ask for nothing. And then an innkeeper sets a chair by her fire and suddenly I mind a great deal whether one particular woman keeps breathing. That's her fault. I'll tell her so when she's well.

objectives.text: Clear the quarry road to the deep beds
Gather living lichen from the true dark
Bring the lichen back to Profira

blestem_romance_aglaia_3.note: Profira spent sixty years unafraid of the dead because the dead ask for nothing. Then Aglaia asked for a chair filled, and the apothecary learned fear at last — the ordinary kind, that a cough could take a person. The lichen held. Winter did not.

blestem_romance_aglaia_4.title: The Fourth Chair
blestem_romance_aglaia_4.summary: Aglaia is out of her bed and has made up her mind, the way she made it up about the chair. Two old women in the Listening City mean to bind their two households into one hearth, quietly, and dare the walls to file it.
blestem_romance_aglaia_4.offer: Up, well, and done waiting — I'm too old to waste a spring being coy. Profira's moving her shelves into my back room and I'm not asking the city's leave. But I'll do it right. Fetch the chronicler Varvara to stand witness — a Vulpescu wedding two Vulpescus, she'll enjoy the paperwork. And bring a keepsake cord from the old churches, the ones to Transcub who minded cruelty to the self. Seems fitting. Two women who spent a lifetime being careful, choosing at the end to be kind to themselves. Come stand with us. The alley's warded. For one hour the walls hear nothing but us, and I've waited long enough to say a foolish thing out loud.
blestem_romance_aglaia_4.progress: Varvara keeps the stones and the record. Fetch her to witness, and bring a keepsake cord from the Transcub churches to bind the two households.
blestem_romance_aglaia_4.complete: There. Witnessed, corded, and the walls none the wiser for one warded hour. Profira says she's minded the dead her whole life and it's high time she minded a living woman across the breakfast table, and I told her she'll mind me a long while yet — I've outlasted three and I've no plans to stop. It won't last forever, none of it does. That's what makes it worth the fire. Sit. That chair's yours whenever the road brings you back. You filled the empty one.

objectives.text: Bring a keepsake cord from the churches
Fetch Varvara to witness the binding

blestem_romance_aglaia_4.note: Two women who had each buried more than most, bound one hearth from two in the city that owns its people by their secrets — and kept this one. Everything good is temporary. They chose it anyway, and dared the walls to file the choice.

whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_1.title: Forty Years of the Same Weather
whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_1.summary: For forty years Ruxandra has poured the ale and Ecaterina has beaten the iron, three doors apart, and neither has said the one thing. Ruxandra will not say it either. But he will send a warm meal up the cold lane, if someone else carries it.
whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_1.offer: You'll think me a fool, and you'll be right, so let's have that settled. There's a woman at the forge — Ecaterina. Been three doors down forty years. Says little, means all of it, and every winter I watch her work through the cold with no one to bank her fire at night. I've meant to say something for four decades. I'm sixty-one. I've run out of decades to mean it in. I can't carry it myself — she'd read my face before I reached the anvil, and then we'd both have to stand there having faces. Take her a hot bowl and a warm knot for her hands. Don't say it's from me. Just... let her be warm one evening.
whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_1.progress: Ecaterina keeps the forge three doors down the cold lane, working late as she always has. Bring her a soothing knot for her hands and a warm bowl. Say nothing of who sent it.
whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_1.complete: She ate it? Good. That's — good. Forty years and the most I've managed is one warm bowl she doesn't know is mine. My father would've called me a coward. He'd have been right, too. But she was warm one evening, and there's a whole winter of them I let go cold, so I'll count the one. Here. For the carrying, and for not telling her. She'd only make that face.

objectives.text: Warm a knot for the smith's cold hands
Bring the meal to Ecaterina, unsigned

whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_1.note: Forty years three doors apart, and Ruxandra's whole confession was a hot bowl carried by another hand and left unsigned. She was warm one evening. He counted the one and let himself grieve the winters.

whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_2.title: The Thing the Forge Kept
whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_2.summary: Ecaterina says little and means all of it, and she has known who sends the bowls for forty years. She keeps her own unsaid thing hammered flat in a drawer, and she has decided the player will carry it, because she never learned another way.
whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_2.offer: You brought the bowl. Don't. I know whose hand it's from — known forty years, since before your parents. A smith reads heat. I read his the first week he opened that inn and I've been reading it ever since, and I never once said, because saying is a thing my family beat out of me the way you beat slag from iron. But I'm as old as he is. The cliff-stalkers are down off the high passes again, and one of them nearly had me on the ridge road last week, and I lay there thinking: forty years, and I never gave him the one thing. Clear the ridge road. Then bring me keepsake cord — good, strong, the kind that holds. There's a thing I made him at the forge the year he came and never had the courage to hand over. It's time it left the drawer.
whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_2.progress: The cliff-stalkers have come down onto the ridge road where Ecaterina nearly fell. Clear them, then bring her strong keepsake cord to bind the thing she has kept forty years in the forge drawer.
whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_2.complete: It's an iron rose. I made it the winter he came, when I still had the fool in me to make such a thing, and I've kept it in the drawer forty years because handing it over meant saying the thing, and I couldn't. I still can't. That hasn't changed and it won't — I'm too old and too made-of-iron to change now. But you can carry it. You carried the bowls both ways without either of us losing face. Carry this. Bind it with the cord. He'll know the hand. He always knew the hand. Just — let him have it before one of us goes cold for good.

objectives.text: Clear the ridge road that nearly took her
Gather strong cord to bind the kept thing
Take the iron rose from the drawer

whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_2.note: Ecaterina forged an iron rose the winter Ruxandra came, and kept it in a drawer for forty years because handing it over meant speaking, and speaking was beaten out of her young. A cliff-stalker on the ridge changed the arithmetic. She still could not say it. She let the rose say it.

whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_3.title: One Hearth, Two Houses
whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_3.summary: The iron rose is delivered, and forty years of the unsaid finally has a shape — though neither will ever say the word aloud. Two people too old and too proud to change agree instead to share the one thing that matters: a warm fire, and the other beside it.
whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_3.offer: She sent me a rose made of iron. Forty years in a drawer, she said through your mouth, because you're the only one either of us can talk through — a fine pair of old fools we are. I'll not ask her to marry me. She'd say no, not from want of caring but from want of the words, and I'd not put her through hunting for them. But I'll ask her to sup at my fire every evening and forge by day and keep her own bed and her own name and her own iron rose. Bring her a hearth hex-sign — one for her forge, one for my hearth, the same sign on both. Not a wedding. Just... one fire, warm at both ends of the lane. Ask her. She'll not say yes out loud. Watch her hands.
whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_3.progress: Bring a hearth hex-sign for each threshold — the forge and the inn, marked the same. Take one to Ecaterina and ask her, knowing she will answer with her hands, not her mouth.
whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_3.complete: She hung it over the forge door. Didn't say a word — set down her hammer, took the sign, and hung it, and looked at me across forty years of lane, and that was the yes. We'll not wed. We'll not say the word. We're too old and too set to start being different people now. But she sups at my fire nightly and I bank hers by night and neither of us is cold anymore, and that's the whole of what I ever wanted and never had the nerve to reach for. You reached for it, for us. An old man's thanks, traveler. Take the rose's twin — she made two. She'd want you to have the other.

objectives.text: Mark forge and hearth with the same sign
Ask Ecaterina to share the fire

whisper_passes_romance_ruxandra_3.note: They never said the word, and never will. Ecaterina hung the sign and set down her hammer, and that was the whole vow. Two houses, two names, two beds — and one fire, warm at both ends of the lane at last. Some love is a thing you keep, not a thing you say.

angel_wings_romance_otilia_1.title: The Grave She Still Tends
angel_wings_romance_otilia_1.summary: Otilia has buried three husbands and outlasted every winter Angel Wings can throw — but she still walks to the last one's grave each dusk and stays too long in the cold. Safta the apothecary, who smells of tallow and knows too much about the dead, has begun walking with her.
angel_wings_romance_otilia_1.offer: You lodge at Otilia's inn, so you'll have seen her go out at dusk. She walks to the far grave-rows and stands at the third husband's kerb till the cold's in her bones, and she's done it two winters now. I'm the apothecary — I know what the cold does to the old, and I know what grief does worse. I'll not tell her to stop mourning; a body mourns as long as it mourns. But I'd have her mourn warm. Take her a warm knot for her hands and sit with her at the grave a while. Don't hurry her off it. Just be a second body in the cold. She's stood there alone two winters. That's two too many.
angel_wings_romance_otilia_1.progress: Otilia walks to the far grave-rows of Angel Wings at dusk and stands at the third husband's kerb. Bring her a soothing knot, and sit the cold hour with her. Do not hurry her.
angel_wings_romance_otilia_1.complete: She let you sit. That's more than she lets most — she waves me off some evenings, says the dead don't need a crowd. But she let you stay, and she was warm, and she talked a little, you said. About him. Good. Grief that gets spoken is grief that's leaving. I've spent my life with the dead; I know the difference between tending a grave and living in one. She's been living in that one. It's time somebody walked her home before full dark. Here — for sitting the cold hour. Not everyone can.

objectives.text: Warm a knot for Otilia's cold hands
Sit the dusk hour at the grave with Otilia

angel_wings_romance_otilia_1.note: Otilia buried three husbands and outlasts every winter, yet still stands the dusk hour at the last kerb till the cold takes her hands. Safta will not tell her to stop. She means only that Otilia mourn warm, and not alone. She let a second body stay. It was a beginning.

angel_wings_romance_otilia_2.title: What the Dead Don't Need
angel_wings_romance_otilia_2.summary: The grave-rows have gone bad — plague-rats in the kerbstones, the tired fields fouling the resting-ground — and Otilia grieves that she cannot even keep his stone clean. Safta means to give her that much, and a little more: the beginning of a reason to come home from the cold.
angel_wings_romance_otilia_2.offer: The far rows have gone foul — plague-rats in the kerbstones since the fields turned tired, and Otilia found his stone gnawed and wept over it like a fresh grave. Three years he's been down and she wept like a fresh grave. That's not tending anymore. That's a woman who won't come in from the cold because coming in feels like leaving him. Clear the vermin from the rows so his kerb's clean. Then bring me a kept-name token — I'll cut his name in it proper, and she can carry him warm in a pocket instead of standing cold at a stone. The dead don't need her frozen at a kerb. I've handled enough of the dead to know: they'd want her by a fire. He would.
angel_wings_romance_otilia_2.progress: Clear the plague-rats fouling the far grave-rows, then bring Safta a kept-name token so the third husband's name can be carried, not stood over in the cold.
angel_wings_romance_otilia_2.complete: She took the token. Held it, read the name, and — this is the thing — she smiled. First smile I've had off her in two winters. Said she could carry him in her apron now and bank the fire at the same time, and wasn't that a thing he'd have liked, her warm and busy instead of froze at a stone. I've spent my whole life being the one who's easy with the dead, the one nobody wants to sit beside at supper for it. She sits beside me now. I find I don't mind being known for once, instead of just for the trade.

objectives.text: Clear the vermin fouling the grave-rows
Bring a token to carry his name warm
Give Safta the token to cut his name

angel_wings_romance_otilia_2.note: Otilia wept at a three-year grave like a fresh one, because coming in from the cold felt like leaving him. Safta cut his name into a token she could carry warm — and Otilia smiled, and sat beside the woman the town keeps at arm's length for knowing the dead too well. Grief, spoken, was leaving at last.

angel_wings_romance_otilia_3.title: A Place Set at the Fire
angel_wings_romance_otilia_3.summary: Otilia carries the third husband warm in her apron now instead of standing frozen at his stone, and she has stopped walking to the grave at dusk. What she walks toward instead is the apothecary's chair by her own fire — not a fourth husband, but a companion for the winters that are left.
angel_wings_romance_otilia_3.offer: You've watched me a while now, so I'll say it plain, the way I say everything. I loved three men and buried three men and I'll not go looking for a fourth to bury — I'm too old to break my heart on schedule. But Safta walks me home from the rows every dusk, and knows the dead and isn't frightened of mine, and sits at my fire, and when did I stop being cold of an evening? Somewhere between her token and her chair. I'll not wed her. I'll not call it what folk call it. I'll call it this: a place set at my fire that's hers, for however many winters are left. Bring me a hearth hex-sign for the second chair, and fetch Ioana off the stones to see it done — not a wedding, just a witness that Otilia Corbeanu chose to be warm again before the end.
angel_wings_romance_otilia_3.progress: Bring a hearth hex-sign to mark the second chair at Otilia's fire as Safta's own, and fetch the chronicler Ioana to witness — not a wedding, but a choosing.
angel_wings_romance_otilia_3.complete: Done, and Ioana's got it in the stones — not as a wedding, I made her write it plain: two women who decided not to spend what's left of their winters cold. Safta says the dead don't need us frozen and she's right, she's always right, it's a maddening quality in a woman. He'd not begrudge me this. None of the three would. They knew me warm; they'd want me warm going out. Everything good runs out, dear — three husbands taught me that, and it's true, and it's exactly why you set the second chair while there's still a fire to set it by. You gave an old widow back her evenings. Sit in the third chair whenever the road's cold. There'll be a bowl.

objectives.text: Mark the second chair at Otilia's fire
Fetch Ioana to witness the choosing

angel_wings_romance_otilia_3.note: Otilia buried three and swore off a fourth — then set a second chair at her fire for the apothecary who was not afraid of her dead. Not a wedding: a choosing, witnessed in the stones. She laid the last grief down warm, and let the winters left be warm too. Everything good runs out. That is why she set the chair.

the_gift_romance_sanda_1.title: The Loaf That Rose Anyway
the_gift_romance_sanda_1.summary: In the Gift, where the red soil is grown from the dead and the yeast dies house by house, Sanda Lupei's starter still rises. She would like it known she noticed you first.
the_gift_romance_sanda_1.offer: You. Yes, you, standing there like the door bit you. Every bakehouse from here to the pit's gone flat -- dead yeast, grey dough, the whole trade in a pinch of ash -- and mine's still breathing. Come feel the crock, it's warm as a cat. I'll cut you a slice if you fetch me two things for the next bake. And if you come back... well. I'll cut you two slices.
the_gift_romance_sanda_1.progress: Clean salt off the fen-line, and a scrape of luminous lichen from the old stones -- Sanda swears the second is her grandmother's secret and won't say more with that face on.
the_gift_romance_sanda_1.complete: Look at it. LOOK at it. Rose like it never heard the bad news. Everything good is temporary, they keep telling me -- so I make more of it, faster than the world can eat it. Sit. Eat. You've got flour on your cheek and I'm not going to tell you where.

objectives.text: Bring clean salt off the fen-line
Scrape lichen from the old stones

the_gift_romance_sanda_1.note: Sanda's bread rose in a town where bread has stopped rising. She called it luck and cut a second slice. The starter is older than she is, and it is warm.

the_gift_romance_sanda_2.title: Two Chairs by the Oven
the_gift_romance_sanda_2.summary: Sanda wants to keep the warm door open past dark for once -- not for coin, for company. But the brazier-imps have taken the wood-store, and a warm oven is nothing without wood to feed it.
the_gift_romance_sanda_2.offer: So here's the thing nobody asks a baker: what do you do at night, when the bread's proofing and there's nobody to talk to but the crock? I'll tell you what I do. I make two mugs and drink both, which is a sad little habit and I'd like to break it. Clear the imps off my wood-store and I'll keep the oven lit late. You bring the stories. I've got flour enough for both of us.
the_gift_romance_sanda_2.progress: The brazier-imps have nested in the Gift's wood-store, and cold wood makes a cold oven. Burn them out, then bring back a bundle of cedar to keep the fire honest.
the_gift_romance_sanda_2.complete: Two chairs, one oven, and a night that doesn't have to end at the last loaf. I set the second mug down in front of you on purpose this time -- did you notice? Of course you noticed. You notice everything but the flour on your own face. Sit closer. The fire's better here.

objectives.text: Burn the imps from the wood-store
Gather cedar to feed the oven

the_gift_romance_sanda_2.note: The oven stayed lit past dark, and there were two chairs by it. Sanda set down a second mug and did not drink from it. She let it go cold on purpose, and neither of them mentioned the mug again.

the_gift_romance_sanda_3.title: Everything Good Is Temporary
the_gift_romance_sanda_3.summary: Sanda has decided to bake a proper festival loaf for the Gift-Tide -- braided, sweet, and unafraid -- and to say a thing out loud she has practiced saying to the crock. She wants you standing there when she does.
the_gift_romance_sanda_3.offer: Alright. I've rehearsed this on the starter forty times and the starter has no notes, so you're getting the finished version. I want to braid a real loaf for the Gift-Tide -- river-pearl sugar in it, the whole show -- and I want your name on the empty chair at my table, not some stranger's. Fetch me what I need for the braid. And I'll say the rest when your hands are full and you can't run.
the_gift_romance_sanda_3.progress: River-pearl for the sweet crumb, a raven feather to bind the grain-sack seal the old way, and Sanda swears the loaf won't braid right without both. She is not looking at you while she says any of it.
the_gift_romance_sanda_3.complete: There. It's said, it's braided, and it rose -- of course it rose, everything I make for you rises. Everything good is temporary, love. That's the whole point of it. So I'm not going to waste a single warm hour pretending otherwise. Set the empty chair. Then sit in it yourself, and stop being empty.

objectives.text: Gather river-pearl for the sweet crumb
Bind the grain-sack seal the old way

objectives.label: the festival table
objectives.text: Set the empty chair at the Gift-Tide table

the_gift_romance_sanda_3.note: The festival loaf rose golden in a town built on the dead, and Sanda set the empty chair and then filled it. She said the terrible true thing plainly -- everything good is temporary -- and chose, out loud, to keep it anyway.

last_hearth_romance_tinca_1.title: The Line the Ledger Won't Hold
last_hearth_romance_tinca_1.summary: Maricica the smith plays a fiddle after the forge cools, and has written a tune with no name -- for Tinca, the town's chronicler, who records every death and has never once recorded a joy. Maricica is too shy to hand it over herself.
last_hearth_romance_tinca_1.offer: You keep the ledger of the dead, I hear. I keep a different sort. I'm Maricica -- the forge, third door. Here's my trouble: I made a tune. It's got no words and no name and it's for Tinca Cenusa, and every time I get within ten feet of her the fiddle goes silent and so do I. You've an honest face. Carry it to her. Don't tell her it's a love song -- just say a smith wrote it and couldn't keep it.
last_hearth_romance_tinca_1.progress: Take Maricica's untitled tune to Tinca in the chronicle-house. She writes only what the town would rather forget; a song may be the first thing she has ever been handed to keep, not to bury.
last_hearth_romance_tinca_1.complete: She read the notes like they were a death-date -- careful, that flat little face -- and then she asked who wrote it. I told her a smith. She said, and I quote, 'Tell the smith the ledger has no line for this.' Traveler. That's not a no. I've spent my whole life reading Tinca's no's. That's the sound of one being written down for the first time.

objectives.text: Carry Maricica's tune to Tinca
Bring Maricica what Tinca said

last_hearth_romance_tinca_1.note: A smith wrote a song with no name and could not carry it herself. Tinca read it like an obituary and said the ledger had no line for it. Everyone in the room understood that this was a beginning.

last_hearth_romance_tinca_2.title: A Fiddle Wants New Strings
last_hearth_romance_tinca_2.summary: Emboldened, Maricica wants to play the tune where Tinca can hear it -- but her old fiddle strings snapped on the high note that matters most, and the moor-wails have driven the string-gut trade off the road.
last_hearth_romance_tinca_2.offer: She sent a note back. A NOTE. Folded like a court summons and it just said 'the second verse is wrong.' She's right, curse her, she's always right, and I can't fix it -- my top string went on the exact note where the song turns kind. I need gut for new strings, and the fen's crawling with moor-wails between here and the string-maker. Clear the road. I'll have the fiddle ready by the time you're back, and my nerve, maybe, if I'm lucky.
last_hearth_romance_tinca_2.progress: Clear the moor-wails from the fen road so the string-trade can pass, and bring back a length of soothing-reed -- Maricica binds the new strings with it, the way her mother did.
last_hearth_romance_tinca_2.complete: Strung, tuned, and the high note holds -- listen. That's the one she said was wrong, and she WAS right, and I'm not too proud to say so. I'll play it under the chronicle-house window at the Table of Strangers. If she opens it, she opens it. If she doesn't, at least the moor heard a smith play something that wasn't a hammer for once.

objectives.text: Clear moor-wails from the fen road
Gather soothing-reed for the new strings

last_hearth_romance_tinca_2.note: Maricica restrung the fiddle and the high note held -- the note where the song turns kind. She meant to play it under Tinca's window. The moor, at least, would hear a smith make something soft.

last_hearth_romance_tinca_3.title: What the Chronicle Left Out
last_hearth_romance_tinca_3.summary: Tinca has heard the fiddle under her window three nights running and has not opened it. She asks you -- not Maricica -- to help her find out whether a chronicler of the dead is allowed a page of her own.
last_hearth_romance_tinca_3.offer: Sit. You've been the road between that smith and me, so you'll hear this straight. I have written down four hundred and eleven names of the dead in this town. Not one line of anything that gladdened me. I told myself the ledger has no room. Then a smith played the same eight bars outside my window for three nights and did not knock -- which is the most patient argument anyone has ever made against me. Fetch me clean paper-stock and good ink. I have a page to start that isn't an ending.
last_hearth_romance_tinca_3.progress: Linen-scrap for a page fit to last, and oak-gall ink that does not fade -- Tinca will not write this in the cheap ink she uses for the dead. She is starting a different book, and she wants it to hold.
last_hearth_romance_tinca_3.complete: I have begun it. One line, dated today: 'A smith played, and I listened.' That is the whole entry and it is the truest thing in the house. Do not tell Maricica I wrote it in the good ink. Let her find out at the festival, when I open the window. I intend to open the window. Write THAT down, if you keep such things -- I no longer trust myself to keep only endings.

objectives.text: Bring paper-stock fit to last
Bring ink that does not fade

last_hearth_romance_tinca_3.note: Tinca began a new book in the good ink, the kind she never wastes on the dead. Its first line was four words long and dated the day she stopped keeping only endings.

last_hearth_romance_tinca_4.title: The First Dance and No Promises
last_hearth_romance_tinca_4.summary: At the Table of Strangers -- the feast where every outdoor table must feed whoever sits down -- Tinca will open her window and hear the whole song for the first time. She wants the square set right, and she wants you there. She promises nothing. She is finally allowing herself that.
last_hearth_romance_tinca_4.offer: The Table of Strangers, tonight. The one night this town feeds anyone who sits, no names asked -- and I have decided that is a fine night to stop asking for names and just let someone in. Help me set the square. I won't call it a wedding, and I won't call it a promise; I've filed enough certainties to distrust them. I'll call it a dance, and a window I open, and a smith I let finish her song. That will do. That will more than do.
last_hearth_romance_tinca_4.progress: Set the great-table for strangers by the chronicle-house, then take Tinca's own hand-copied verse to Maricica at the forge -- the second verse, the one Tinca finally got right herself.
last_hearth_romance_tinca_4.complete: She opened with the wrong verse, the stubborn thing, so I opened the window and sang the right one over her, and she laughed -- Maricica laughed, in front of the whole square, dropped the bow and laughed. We danced once. I made no promise and she asked for none. Some happy things you leave unfinished on purpose, so the world can't collect on them. This one, we'll keep unfinished a long, long time.

objectives.label: the chronicle-house square
objectives.text: Set the Table of Strangers by the chronicle-house
Carry Tinca's finished verse to Maricica

last_hearth_romance_tinca_4.note: They danced once at the Table of Strangers and made no promises to each other, which was the point. Tinca closed the ledger of the dead for one whole night. She left the new book open, and did not date the end of anything.

sangeroasa_romance_gherghe_1.title: A Smith With Something to Say
sangeroasa_romance_gherghe_1.summary: Gherghe the smith has loved Ana the apothecary for two years and eleven months, by his own count, and has never managed one clean sentence to her face. He has decided the coward's route: to court her through her trade instead.
sangeroasa_romance_gherghe_1.offer: Two fingers I've lost to this forge and not one word I can keep in my head when Ana Lupei's in the room. Tallow and wormwood, she smells of -- I could pick it out of a fire. Here's my plan, and it's a bad one, so don't laugh: I'll not TELL her anything. I'll make myself useful to her work till she can't do without me. She needs nightshade off the red rows and something to bind a poultice. Fetch it in my name. Let the trade say what my mouth won't.
sangeroasa_romance_gherghe_1.progress: Gather marsh-lichen from the threaded ground for Ana's poultices -- harvesting it is trespass on the dead, and Gherghe knows it, and asks you to be gentle about it -- and a clean binding of linen.
sangeroasa_romance_gherghe_1.complete: She took it. Said 'tell the smith this is good stock' and went back to her mortar, and I have been turning that sentence over on the anvil all afternoon like it's a blade I can't get straight. 'Good stock.' From Ana. You'd think she'd handed me the whole Gift. ...She sort of did, traveler. She sort of did.

objectives.text: Gather marsh-lichen from the threaded ground
Bring clean linen to bind the poultice

sangeroasa_romance_gherghe_1.note: Gherghe courted Ana through her own trade, being a coward about the words. She called his stock good and turned back to her mortar. He carried the two words home like an ingot and could not get them straight.

sangeroasa_romance_gherghe_2.title: The Grave She Tends
sangeroasa_romance_gherghe_2.summary: Gherghe learns why Ana knows too much about the dead: she buried someone in the Gift's red soil and walks out to the grave-rows each dusk. He wants to help her tend it -- but the mourners of the Gift rise where grief is thickest, and the walk is no longer safe.
sangeroasa_romance_gherghe_2.offer: I found out why she smells of wormwood, traveler. It's grave-herb. She's got someone under the red rows -- won't say who, and I'll not ask -- and she walks out at dusk to tend it, alone, past the mourners. That's not courting business anymore. That's just a person I can't stand to see walk into the dark by herself. Clear the grave-row so she can tend it in peace. I'll not even tell her it was me. She's carried enough without owing me.
sangeroasa_romance_gherghe_2.progress: The mourners of the Gift gather where grief runs deepest. Clear them from the grave-rows so Ana can walk safely, and bring a handful of clean salt to lay on the threshold of the grave, the old warding.
sangeroasa_romance_gherghe_2.complete: It's done, and she doesn't know it was us, and it's going to stay that way. I watched from the cinder slope -- didn't follow, just watched the road stay clear for her. She knelt a long time. When she stood, she wasn't crying. That's not nothing, out here. Sometimes love's just making sure the road to the grave is safe and never once asking to be thanked for it.

objectives.text: Clear the mourners from the grave-rows
Bring salt to ward the grave's threshold

objectives.label: the red grave-rows
