sangeroasa_debt_murder_what_the_ledger_r.note: Grosch was murdered by a word -- 'collected,' printed a thousand years too early on a labor-ledger, that finishes any honest man who reads a dead-man's row to its end. The forger who wrote the row will hang for forgery; the word that killed the foreman cannot be arrested. The captain wrote it a heart-fit. Some murder weapons you can only bury the victim under.

the_archive_selfedit_the_changed_card.title: The Card That Changed
the_archive_selfedit_the_changed_card.summary: A catalog card that read 'open, pending' at dusk reads 'collected, closed' by dawn -- in fresh violet ink, in no clerk's roster. Lupu wants a witness who will read the card twice and swear to the change before the Archive decides he imagined it.
the_archive_selfedit_the_changed_card.offer: I need you to read a card, and then read it again in the morning, and be a witness that isn't me -- because when it's only me, the Archive files my report under error and I start to doubt my own eyes. Shelf forty, the pending-accounts drawer. There's a card there that says open. It has said open for forty years. Last week it started saying closed by morning and open again by the time I've fetched my spectacles -- fresh violet ink each dawn, and not one clerk on the night-roster to have wet a pen. Read it at dusk. Note the words. We'll read it together at first light. And keep your voice down near the sentinels; they've grown particular about who reads what.
the_archive_selfedit_the_changed_card.progress: Read the pending card at shelf forty by dusk and note its words, then confirm the dawn change with Lupu.
the_archive_selfedit_the_changed_card.complete: You read 'open, pending' at dusk. I read 'collected, closed' beside you at dawn. Same card. Same drawer. Fresh violet, still tacky. And no hand on the roster to have written it. Forty years I've catalogued a house that keeps the world's memory, and this week the house started editing it -- quietly, at night, closing the open ones. I've stopped telling myself it's a clerk playing tricks. A clerk gets tired. Whatever wets this pen every dawn does not. Take the lens. We're going to find whose ink it is, and I'm afraid the answer is no one's.

objectives.label: shelf forty, the pending drawer
objectives.text: Read the changing card at dusk and note its words
Confirm the dawn change with Lupu

the_archive_selfedit_the_changed_card.note: A card that read 'open, pending' for forty years read 'collected, closed' by dawn -- fresh violet ink, no clerk on the roster to have written it. The house built to keep the world's memory has begun, at night, to edit it. Lupu stopped calling it a clerk's trick. A clerk gets tired.

the_archive_selfedit_the_ink_of_no_one.title: The Ink of No One
the_archive_selfedit_the_ink_of_no_one.summary: The overnight ink matches no inkwell in the Archive -- it reads shifting orange under the still, the same colour as a live stone writing itself. Toma sends you into the deep stacks to find where the changed cards cluster, and what stands at their center.
the_archive_selfedit_the_ink_of_no_one.offer: Lupu brought me the card. I put my still to the ink and I have not slept since. Blue is the dead, violet the sub-earth, green a carving waking -- I read those the way you read a kettle. This ink reads shifting orange. Orange is the signal writing itself into a medium, and it is writing itself into our catalogue in the shape of the word 'collected.' The changed cards aren't scattered -- they cluster, drawer by drawer, closing outward from one point in the deep stacks like frost from a nail. Walk the stacks. Find the center. Read the depth of the colour where it's brightest -- and read none of the words, or you'll die the Courier's death, clean-nailed and satchel sealed, having understood exactly one thing too many.
the_archive_selfedit_the_ink_of_no_one.progress: Walk the deep stacks, find where the closed cards cluster tightest, and read the depth of the orange at their center. Clear the sentinels and tomb-golems that guard it.
the_archive_selfedit_the_ink_of_no_one.complete: Brightest at the foundation-stone under shelf nine, exactly where the oldest tablets are shelved -- the ones the histories call 'matured past legibility.' Matured. What a word for it. Those tablets didn't fade, traveler. They finished reading themselves. And now the reading's spread up into the paper catalogue, closing our open accounts in the world's own hand, because the Archive was built on a live piece of the very thing it was meant to file. The house is the disease wearing the cure's coat. Take the counteragent -- it quiets the pull of the words. There is nothing in my shop that quiets the fact of them.

objectives.text: Clear the sentinels warding the deep stacks
Break the tomb-golems at the cluster's center

objectives.label: the foundation-stone under shelf nine
objectives.text: Read the orange at its brightest center

the_archive_selfedit_the_ink_of_no_one.note: The overnight ink read shifting orange -- the signal writing itself, in the shape of the word 'collected.' The changed cards closed outward from the foundation-stone under shelf nine, where the oldest tablets were shelved: not faded past legibility, but finished reading themselves. The Archive was built on a live stone. The house is the disease in the cure's coat.

the_archive_selfedit_the_night_clerks_ha.title: The Night-Clerk's Hand
the_archive_selfedit_the_night_clerks_ha.summary: The Watch has a suspect -- a night-clerk whose ink is on the changed cards and whose lamp burns in the deep stacks past every curfew. But the man is not closing the accounts. He is copying them the instant before they close, racing a hand that is not his.
the_archive_selfedit_the_night_clerks_ha.offer: Before you side with the chronicler and his ghost-ink, hear the Watch's version. There's a night-clerk, Nedelcu, whose lamp burns in the deep stacks every night the cards change, and whose ink -- his own, mixed at his own desk -- is on every altered drawer. That's a man at a scene with a pen in his hand, and I'm minded to close him the easy way. But I'm not a fool, and neither, I think, are you. Go into the stacks tonight. Find him at his lamp. Watch what his pen actually does before I put him in irons for a crime that might be the opposite of one.
the_archive_selfedit_the_night_clerks_ha.progress: Find Nedelcu the night-clerk at his lamp in the deep stacks and watch what his pen is truly doing to the cards.
the_archive_selfedit_the_night_clerks_ha.complete: You watched him, and you'll tell me what I half-hoped. He's not closing the cards. He's copying them -- open, pending, the true reading -- onto his own paper, faster than the house can close them, the instant before the orange takes the word. His ink's on the drawers because he's leaned over every one, transcribing the open version before it dies. He's not the hand editing the Archive. He's the one man keeping a record the Archive can't reach: a shadow-catalogue of every account the house has quietly finished. The Watch wanted a culprit. What we've got is a witness nobody licensed. Harder to hang. Take his dossier -- and don't let the sentinels learn what he keeps under the ninth shelf.

objectives.label: the night-clerk's lamp
objectives.text: Find Nedelcu copying cards in the deep stacks
Learn what Nedelcu's pen is truly doing

the_archive_selfedit_the_night_clerks_ha.note: The night-clerk's ink was on every changed drawer -- because he leaned over each one to copy the true reading before the orange closed it. Nedelcu was not editing the Archive. He was racing it, keeping a shadow-catalogue of every account the house quietly finished. The Watch wanted a culprit. It got a witness no one had licensed.

the_archive_selfedit_too_specific_to_fil.title: Too Specific to File
the_archive_selfedit_too_specific_to_fil.summary: The Archive edits itself and no hand can stop it -- a closed entry is a finished one, and the house closes everything it can define. Nedelcu knows the one card it cannot touch: a caption too specific, too warm, too wrong to file. Help him write it, and keep one open thing open forever.
the_archive_selfedit_too_specific_to_fil.offer: You know now that I can't stop it. Nobody can arrest a house for remembering the wrong way. The orange closes what it can define, and it can define almost everything -- a name, a date, a debt, all of it filing itself shut while we sleep. But it choked on one card. Forty years ago some clerk enshrined an herb-woman's walking stick and wrote the caption wrong -- 'she fed six winters of strangers and asked one question,' and never wrote the question down. The house can't close it. It has no word for an unanswered question. That's the crack, traveler. Not a weapon -- a splinter too specific to file. Come to the Founders' Hall. Help me write one caption so warm and so exact and so unfinished that the orange can't find a place to stamp it shut. We won't save the Archive. We'll keep one card open. It's the only kind of winning this house allows.
the_archive_selfedit_too_specific_to_fil.progress: Go to the Founders' Hall, hold off the sentinels, and help Nedelcu inscribe a caption too specific for the house to close.
the_archive_selfedit_too_specific_to_fil.complete: It's done. 'She asked one question, and it was never written down, and that is the point of her.' Read it and you can feel the house recoil -- there's no ledger-word for a deliberate blank, no way to stamp 'collected' on an ending someone chose to leave open. The orange washed up to the card and stopped, the way tide stops at a salt-line. One card. Open forever, because it's too warm and too exact and too unfinished to finish. The Archive will close ten thousand others by the next dawn and I'll copy what I can and lose most of it. But this one it can't have. Take the token -- a kept name, unclosed. Carry it out where the house's reach ends. Some memory has to leave the building alive.

objectives.text: Hold off the sentinels at the Founders' Hall

objectives.label: the Founders' Hall
objectives.text: Inscribe the caption too specific to file

the_archive_selfedit_too_specific_to_fil.note: No hand could stop the Archive editing itself shut -- a closed entry is a finished one, and the house finishes all it can define. Nedelcu found the crack: a caption too warm, too exact, too deliberately unfinished for any ledger-word to close. One card kept open forever against ten thousand quietly finished by dawn. Not a victory. A splinter the machine can't swallow, carried out of the building alive.
